Document 1 of 356

Raising bonds in Kensington Market

Author: Slofstra, Martin

Publication info: Slofstra, Martin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/raising-bonds-kensington-market/docview/3053514549/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Raising+bonds+in+Kensington+Market&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Slofstra%2C+Martin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: KMCLT plans to raise community-sourced funding rather than relying on an external lender for a mortgage
It was an event aimed at not developers, but a neighbourhood organization, and it was all about an attempt to stop or at least slow down the gentrification of Kensington Market.
Late last month, local residents politicians and members of the Kensington Market Community Land Trust (KMCLT), a non-profit organization, gathered at Café Pamenar on Augusta Ave. to celebrate the March 25 launch of a fund-raising initiative called Kensington Community Bonds.
The goal: Raise up to $2 million through the bonds in order for the KMCLT to acquire a second property in Kensington that it said will help it "preserve affordable housing and prevent further displacement of legacy businesses by taking more property off the not-for-profit real estate market."
Incorporated in 2017, it stated in in a release issued in March that "it has sought to keep housing affordable and accessible for everyone in the community. The organization aims to provide stability to longtime renters especially those who face gentrification and renoviction."
It notes that KMCLT's "current portfolio consists of one property in Kensington Market, which was purchased after a resident-led resistance to the previous landlord's illegal eviction process."
That move resulted in the city of Toronto giving the group $3 million to buy, build and renovate the property at 54-56 Kensington Ave. as affordable housing for a 99-year term. The building has 12 residential units as well as five retailers.
"The community land trust has kept its 12 residential units affordable and provided five diverse businesses with long-term lease stability. 54-56 Kensington Avenue's current residential rental rates average well under 80 per cent of Average Market Rent ($1,142 for a bachelor apartment, and $1,594 for a 2-bedroom) set by the Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC)," the organization says.
"The land trust will be using $200,000 raised from the bonds to pay off a portion of the mortgage on this existing property."
Dominique Russell, co-chair of the land trust, points out that the use of bonds with investment minimums starting at $500 and interest rates of up to 4.5 per cent, allows the group to use "community-sourced funding rather than relying on an external lender for a mortgage. The community bond model gives us control over our financing terms. And instead of paying interest to banks, we will be putting this money directly into the community we serve."
At the April launch event, speakers included Toronto mayor Olivia Chow, Russell, University-Rosedale city councillor Dr. Dianne Saxe, the last environmental commissioner of Ontario - the Ford government abolished her office in 2019, MPP for University-Rosedale Jessica Bell and Kevin Barrett, co-chair of the KMCLT.
"This community bond campaign is part of a larger vision for the community land trust, of investing in our community, and trapping resources in our community," Barrett said. "We spend an awful lot of time seeing an awful lot of mechanisms by which people put money into this community to take profit out. And that is what is creating a crisis in terms of the lack of affordable places to have a shop, and the lack of affordable places to live. We are looking to restructure that market completely, and the community bond campaign, I think, is a great way to put those pieces together."
Bell, the Ontario NDP critic for housing, said the Ford government, has not fixed the housing crisis because, "it has never been more expensive to rent, or own a home in Toronto or Ontario. And we need real solutions.
"Now it is my job as a provincial representative to stand up for strong rent control, to invest and build thousands of affordable homes and non-market homes on public land. And to make it make it easier for people to buy a home, which means making it harder for people to buy their 12th home or their 30th home. It is about human rights. And it is about making sure that housing is for people."
Bell added that Kensington residents and the KCMLT mean "business. They are not just going to rely on politicians to solve an issue. These folks are saying we are going to solve it here by taking action and by buying up property and turning it into affordable housing one building at a time. It is practical. It is brilliant."
Saxe noted that as the environmental commissioner, she traveled all over Ontario and "what I heard over and over again, is people want to invest in their community, see improvements in places that they love, and keep the money at home. And that is what this will do. (The bond) is such a great innovation. and I'm delighted to see it. We are working with you to get more improvements in Kensington."
Chow, meanwhile, urged local residents to "please invest because not only are you going to put a little bit more money in your pocket - not too much, but a little bit - you will feel good about it, because you are enhancing the livelihood of your neighborhood."

Subject: Affordable housing; Gentrification

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing

Identifier / keyword: kmclt; plans; raise; funding; relying; external

Document 2 of 356

Need more housing? Relief from HST a good place to start

Author: Slofstra, Martin

Publication info: Slofstra, Martin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/need-more-housing-relief-hst-good-place-start/docview/3053511640/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Need+more+housing%3F+Relief+from+HST+a+good+place+to+start&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Slofstra%2C+Martin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Interest rate increases, along with excessive taxes, fees and levies, along with red tape and bureaucracy are a big reason that housing has become unaffordable
Political leaders at all levels of government are now waking to the fact that building more housing - affordable housing in particular - is critical to keeping up with our rapidly growing population.
With 1.3 million new immigrants to Canada in the last three years and plans to welcome up to 542,000 in 2024 and 550,000 in 2025, it's clear that we need to ratchet up the number of new affordable housing units that are being built. It must be deemed a key priority going forward.
However, affordable housing has become a pipe dream for many. Young people are leaving our cities and seeking accommodation elsewhere - sometimes out of the country - due to lack of supply.
The crux of the matter is that interest rate increases, along with excessive taxes, fees and levies, along with red tape and bureaucracy are a big reason that housing has become unaffordable.
Change must be made.
The federal and provincial governments have opted to exempt all forms of purpose-built rental, whether affordable or market, from the HST/GST. However, affordable ownership homes delivered by non-profit organizations like Habitat for Humanity continue to be subject to the taxes. This will ultimately lead to fewer new affordable ownership units being built in Canada.
It is disappointing that, in their recent budgets, neither the provincial nor federal governments made a commitment to any HST/GST relief for non-profit affordable ownership homes.
In the Greater Toronto Area, the HST/GST is adding an average of $80,000 per home to the price that Habitat for Humanity incurs to deliver desperately needed new affordable ownership homes. For every 100 homes the organization delivers, they could provide 13 more if they weren't burdened with the additional expense.
Exempting or deferring these taxes on non-profit affordable ownership homes would represent a loss of only 0.1 to 0.5 per cent of total revenues from HST/GST on new homes. Seems there is plenty to gain in the way of affordable housing from making such a small sacrifice.
Both RESCON and Habitat for Humanity have raised the issue with the federal and provincial governments. We have written to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford in hopes of convincing them to provide HST/GST relief for non-profit affordable ownership homes.
The economic value of an HST/GST exemption could be retained as part of the second mortgage in non-profit affordable homeownership programs, thus avoiding any financial windfall at time of sale for homeowners.
Moreover, this form of relief could be provided as a deferral rather than a straight exemption, with the requirement that it be paid back if and when an affordable ownership unit is sold to the market.
The Blueprint for More and Better Housing report released in March 2024 by the Task Force for Housing and Climate provided more support, recommending that the federal and provincial governments provide a "full HST/GST exemption for charitable non-profit (housing) organizations."
We wholeheartedly agree and have urged both governments to move quickly to expand the HST/GST relief that's now in place for purpose-built rental to also include non-profit affordable ownership housing. It seems to be an entirely logical step to take such important action.
We would also encourage specific action to provide a full rebate of the HST/GST for first-time buyers of new homes. They're being taxed on new housing at rates which would've crushed their parents and grandparents. Governments must do more to help them get a foothold in the market.
A commentary on LinkedIn co-authored by Ene Underwood, CEO at Habitat for Humanity GTA, and Julie Deans, president and CEO of Habitat for Humanity Canada, noted that Canada has extraordinary potential but to fully realize its possibilities, the country must get housing right.
The executives note that increasing numbers of developers are having to delay or abandon new builds due to negative economics.
Disturbingly, a survey by Habitat for Humanity demonstrated that the level of despair, particularly among young people, has increased and growing numbers of immigrants are considering leaving or not coming to Canada at all.
The need for affordable housing has reached a tipping point. We must do something to turn the tide. Inaction is not an option.
Richard Lyall is president of the Residential Construction Council of Ontario (RESCON). He has represented the building industry in Ontario since 1991. Contact him at media@rescon.com.

Subject: Interest rates; Bureaucracy; Affordable housing; Profits; Nonprofit organizations

Business indexing term: Subject: Interest rates Affordable housing Profits

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Habitat for Humanity; NAICS: 6242 29, 813319

Document 3 of 356

End goal on interference was Beijing-friendly MPs

Author: Gunter, Lorne

Publication info: Gunter, Lorne. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/end-goal-on-interference-was-beijing-friendly-mps/docview/3052354377/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=End+goal+on+interference+was+Beijing-friendly+MPs&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=Gunter%2C+Lorne&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: China's Communist government was never trying to rig the nationwide outcome of either the 2019 or 2021 elections. And anyone who was hoping the Commission on Foreign Interference led by Justice Marie-Josée Hogue was going to uncover a widespread plot to keep Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals in office was always going to be unsatisfied.
Instead, what Chinese agents and their surrogates were always attempting to do was ensure a handful or more of Beijing-friendly MPs ended up sitting in the caucus of the winning party.
Mission accomplished. Hogue asked in her 194-page report, released last Friday: "Was there foreign interference targeting the 2019 and '21 general elections? Yes, I have no difficulty concluding that there was."
"People are right to be worried and to want to shine a light on what is going on," Hogue wrote. The level of interference was "troubling," even if it did not swing the whole of the election in favour of the Liberals.
And she concluded that while other foreign governments were actively interfering, too - notably Iran and India - China was far and away the most active.
In its conclusions, Hogue's public inquiry was vastly more sensible than the two previous inquiries the Liberals put in place. In February 2023, the committee of bureaucrats the Liberals had appointed to watch out for interference in the 2021 election concluded there was "no evidence to indicate foreign state actors" had meddled in the campaign.
And, of course, last May, former governor general and "special rapporteur," David Johnston, a longtime Trudeau family friend, found there had been interference, but held cabinet entirely blameless. "I have not found examples of ministers, the Prime Minister or their offices knowingly ignoring intelligence, advice or recommendations on foreign interference or being driven by partisan considerations in dealing with these issues."
What a stain that naïve conclusion will always be on the legacy of an otherwise eminent Canadian.
Having twice been critical of Hogue's inquiry, I now feel she deserves praise for showing convincingly what seemed obvious, but which the Trudeau government and earlier inquiries were apparently wilfully blind to.
One thing Hogue appears to have uncovered that has gone largely unsaid, even in her report, is that Trudeau knew of the foreign interference early on in events (meaning before the 2019 election), but ignored or downplayed Chinese meddling solely to improve Liberal election chances.
Since interference became an issue a year and a half ago, both the PM and his staff have insisted he was never told by CSIS or the RCMP that the Chinese government was conniving to get friendly Liberals elected to the House of Commons.
However, one of the most glaring examples of such a Communist conspiracy was the Liberal nomination contest in the Ontario riding of Don Valley North before the 2019 election. Since this was a safe Liberal seat, controlling the outcome of the Liberal nomination would have been equivalent to manipulating the outcome of the election itself.
Trudeau and his chief of staff, Katie Telford, have repeatedly denied knowing about efforts by Chinese diplomats, posted in Toronto, to pack the nomination meeting in favour of eventual winner, Han Dong, who has a long history of contacts with Chinese officials.
But, Hogue writes, in his closeddoor testimony, Trudeau told her "un-endorsing Mr. Dong would have (had) direct electoral consequences" for the Liberals'chances in the riding, as well as having "a devastating impact on Mr. Dong personally."
Not only does this show Trudeau had in fact been tipped to the possibility of Chinese interference in at least one riding as early as 2019, it also shows he puts Liberal electoral goals and the feelings of Liberal candidates above national security.
This indirect revelation about what Trudeau knew and when may be the most damaging finding of Hogue's inquiry. lgunter@postmedia.com @sunlornegunter

Subject: Nominations; State elections; Prime ministers

Location: China; Beijing China

Identifier / keyword: communist; government; never; trying; nationwide; outcome

Title: End goal on interference was Beijing-friendly MPs

GUNTER: End goal on interference was Beijing friendly MPs

Author: Chau, Eddie

Publication info: Chau, Eddie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/gunter-end-goal-on-interference-was-beijing/docview/3052179131/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=GUNTER%3A+End+goal+on+interference+was+Beijing+friendly+MPs&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Chau%2C+Eddie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: China's Communist government was never trying to rig the nationwide outcome of either the 2019 or 2021 elections. And anyone who was hoping the Commission on Foreign Interference led by Justice Marie-Josée Hogue was going to uncover a widespread plot to keep Justin Trudeau's Liberals in office was always going to be unsatisfied.

"I have not found examples of ministers, the Prime Minister or their offices knowingly ignoring intelligence, advice or recommendations on foreign interference or being driven by partisan considerations in dealing with these issues."

However, one of the most glaring examples of such a Communist conspiracy was the Liberal nomination contest in the Ontario riding of Don Valley North before the 2019 election. This indirect revelation about what Trudeau knew and when may be the most damaging finding of Hogue's inquiry.

Document 5 of 356

No PC Police for micro aggressions against Jews

Author: Agar, Jerry

Publication info: Agar, Jerry. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-pc-police-micro-aggressions-against-jews/docview/3051773166/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+PC+Police+for+micro+aggressions+against+Jews&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=Agar%2C+Jerry&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Apparently protection from micro-aggressions, triggering, and hostile language doesn't extend to Jews at Canada's universities.
You remember microaggressions? At the University of Toronto the explanation is contained in a document quoting Dr. Derald Wing Sue, "Microaggressions are the everyday verbal, nonverbal, and environmental slights, snubs, or insults, etc., whether intentional or unintentional, which communicate hostile, derogatory, or negative messages to target people based solely upon their marginalized group membership."
At McGill University the description of a training session at McGill's Teacher Support Union tells us, "Microaggressions happen so quickly, whether they be racial, gender-based, about sexual orientation, ableism, or another form of cultural oppression. The subsequent impacts of microaggressions disrupt the learning and safety of our classrooms."
Workshops, training, language and general lecturing of the student body and the public by universities has served to make some people tiptoe around controversial social subjects, lest those who self-appoint themselves to police the rest of us point the bony finger of blame in accusations of hate.
Both U of T and McGill are currently the sites of supposedly Pro-Palestinian rallies.
Supposedly, because as Joe Warmington has reported in the Sun, "Anti-Jewish segregation is happening on the campus of the University of Toronto."
Jews are not allowed to enter the encampment. "At least non-approved Jews. These unofficial border guards do ask for papers at the border crossing there.
Or, at least, proof of religion, affiliation or ideology."
Video exists of those same "border guards" refusing a professor entry. He insists it is because he is Jewish.
Those border guards have no official status from any proper authority. They are self-appointed, masked and "protecting" an illegal encampment.
They also appear anti-Semitic.
That's macro-aggression. It should set off all sorts of triggers at the U of T, but leadership there has always been accused of weakness on Jew hatred.
The Jerusalem Center for Public Affairs wrote, "The University of Toronto is one of the most highly regarded academic institutions in Canada. A coalition of anti-Israel groups initially used the university campus as a launching pad for what has become an annual series of events that take place in dozens of cities around the world."
They are referencing the annual Anti-Israeli Apartheid Week, seen by many as permission to show as anti-Semitic under cover of a supposed concern for Palestinians.
The Canadian Press reports on McGill: "Both McGill and Quebec Premier François Legault have described the Montreal encampment as illegal, and asked police to help dismantle the tents - though as of Sunday the force had not complied with the request. Opponents of the camp, including counter-protesters who showed up Thursday, have said it makes some Jewish students feel unsafe."
One would think Jewish students feeling unsafe would unleash the PC Police with claims of micro-aggressions, triggering, and a need for workshops and healing for those students.
But no. Protesters on campus and in the streets in Canada claim they are not anti-Jewish, but against what the Israeli government is doing in reaction to the Hamas attack of last October 7th.
How much more believable would those claims be if at least some demanding and sign waving showed condemnation of the terrorist group that attacked innocent Jews? How about demands for the return of hostages taken by Hamas? Or does all the anguish over micro and macro-aggressions not apply to Jews?

Subject: Antisemitism; Jewish people; Colleges & universities; Microaggressions; College campuses

Business indexing term: Subject: Colleges & universities

Document 6 of 356

Parrish ahead in Mississauga mayor race: Poll

Author: Connor, Kevin

Publication info: Connor, Kevin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/parrish-ahead-mississauga-mayor-race-poll/docview/3051772539/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Parrish+ahead+in+Mississauga+mayor+race%3A+Poll&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Connor%2C+Kevin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mississauga mayoral candidate Carolyn Parrish has extended her lead in the race.
A new Liaison Strategies poll for the National Ethnic Press and Media Council of Canada finds Parrish is now at 28% among all voters, up from 18% in March.
"Just on candidate filings and withdrawals alone a lot has taken place in the Mississauga byelection," said David Valentin, principal at Liaison Strategies, in a release on Monday.
"Brian Crombie, ex-husband of former mayor and current Ontario Party Leader Bonnie Crombie, has filed. Peter McCallion, son of the longest-serving mayor of Mississauga, Hazel McCallion, has dropped out and endorsed the front runner, Carolyn Parrish.
"In the midst of all these comings and goings, Brian Crombie has debuted with 7% support among all voters, and Councillor Stephen Dasko has gained 3% and is now at 11%.
"However, the real story is former councillor (and former MP) Carolyn Parrish has grown her support by 8 points and now enjoys a 17-point lead," Valentin said.
Support for Parrish is strongest with seniors, who are considered the most reliable voters.
Councillor Dipika Damerla has 20% of the vote putting him in second place.

Subject: Mayors; Voters

Identifier / keyword: mississauga; mayoral; candidate; carolyn; parrish; extended

Document 7 of 356

Encampment 'dangerous'; Marshals wearing keffiyehs at U of T tell Jews they can't enter

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/encampment-dangerous-marshals-wearing-keffiyehs/docview/3051772535/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Encampment+%27dangerous%27%3B+Marshals+wearing+keffiyehs+at+U+of+T+tell+Jews+they+can%27t+enter&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: No Jews allowed! That's the situation when it comes to the "Little Gaza" encampment on the University of Toronto campus.
At least non-approved Jews. These unofficial border guards do ask for papers at the border crossing there.
Or, at least, proof of religion, affiliation or ideology.
It's not 1930s Germany, but looks like it. It's Toronto 2024.
There's no spinning or downplaying it. Anti-Jewish segregation is happening on the campus of the University of Toronto. The videos out of the "Little Gaza" tent encampment protesting Israel are both abhorrent and astonishing.
Premier Doug Ford has certainly seen enough.
"We need to remove those people," Ford said at the Holocaust Remembrance commemoration on Monday. "These universities have to make a move."
One video shows a Jewish professor not being allowed inside the fenced-in anti-Israel encampment that is monitored by protesters who cover their faces.
While not trying to get into the compound, another video shows independent journalist and lawyer Caryma Sa'd's fearless videographer referred to as "a Jew."
Counter-protesters were blocked not just from entering the fenced-in area of King's College Circle but from freely moving about the walkway outside of it.
And there are several videos showing a Jewish counter-protester having his Star of David Israeli flag taken from him and being physically assaulted by a completely masked person.
It got pretty tense there. On one side of the sidewalk you had "pro-Palestinian" protesters in a line while on the other side you had Israel Now counter-protesters. In the middle were just seven U of T special constables who did an outstanding job of preventing this from blowing up into an all-out brawl.
Toronto For Palestine in their X postings over the weekend called it a largely peaceful gathering and made the point that it had the support of labour unions - something CUPE president Fred Hahn reposted.
The University of Toronto stated Monday, "We remain concerned about large numbers of the broader public coming to campus for rallies and marches" and particularly "reported assaults and hate speech."
Saying it is "not clear how many of these involve individuals inside the encampment or members of the public," Prof. Sandy Welsh, vice-provost students, said they have "forwarded four reports to Project Resolute, a Toronto Police Service initiative to fight anti-Semitism and Islamophobia, for further investigation."
Welsh also said, "Members of the U of T administration met in good faith with U of T student representatives of the individuals in the encampment" and they are working "to address our concerns about health and safety, sanitation, emergency and public access, and language that is threatening, hateful and discriminatory."
Also, "we hope to implement temporary sanitation improvements and additional changes to the fence this week to create even safer surroundings for all community members and minimize health risks."
Toronto Police spokesperson Stephanie Sayer said its hate crimes unit is investigating Saturday incidents in which "two men attended King's College Circle to counter-protest" and after "people from the encampment began yelling at the two men" they "were assaulted, and had their flags and scarves taken." Later in a separate incident after an argument, police said "one person was assaulted from behind, was pulled by the hair and punched."
Charges have not been laid at this point and every person is considered innocent until proven guilty.
But video shows a student being assaulted. PhD student Joshua Loewen-Samuels shared with the Toronto Sun a letter he sent to the University of Toronto in which he wrote that he went to King's College Circle to "peacefully raise awareness about the genocide in Darfur and to pro protest the oppression of Gaza by Hamas" when he and his friend "were both physically assaulted outside the encampment near Convocation Hall" in what was a "hate-related incident."
Both Joshua, who is Christian, but a self-described "Zionist," and his friend Mykhailo, who is Jewish, but not a student at U of T, were assaulted, he said. Toronto Police took their statements and drove them home, he added.
Inside the encampment, Loewen-Samuels said he heard children being told to say "f---Jews" and calling them "baby killers kikes, evil Zionists" who should "go back to Europe."
Said Loewen-Samuels: "The encampment is more dangerous than you may realize" and "I recommend you putting yourself in the shoes of a Jewish student," because "there was so much anti-Semitic Jewish hatred expressed that I don't have the time or the space to relate everything."
Things are out of control there.
It's just a matter of time before someone is hurt or killed. And every fair-minded person knows if such acts of discrimination were directed toward any minority group it would not be tolerated. It is being tolerated and managed here. At this encampment, people in masks, wearing black and white checkered keffiyehs, are in control of several acres of lawn surrounded by a fence that was erected to keep people out. It has turned out to instead allow occupiers to control the area and decide who they will permit to enter.
Authorities seem to have no answer for it or even much interest in it. Outside of a few U of T special constables who did a remarkable job of not letting this situation blow to extremes, there has been no police intervention so far - although they are not far away and are on standby.
These are times of rising anti-Semitism, as B'nai Brith brought out in its annual report which shows university campus are cesspools of Jew hatred - including 5,791 anti-Semitic incidents.
Certainly, the situation is untenable at U of T and all people squatting illegally should be removed immediately. Hate crime charges can be laid where appropriate. Canada from top to bottom should not condone anti-Semitism, or any form of discrimination, like this.
Any messages coming from government that suggests they don't are not getting to the people who are in charge of Little Gaza on the campus of U of T, where they have to the power to exclude Jews from their fenced-off city. jwarmington@postmedia.com @joe_warmington

Subject: Hate crimes; Sanitation; Assaults; College campuses; Antisemitism; Jewish people; Hate speech; Zionism; Criminal investigations; Demonstrations & protests; Constables

Location: Israel; Gaza Strip

Company / organization: Name: University of Toronto; NAICS: 611310

Identifier / keyword: allowed; situation; comes; little; encampment; university

Document 8 of 356

AGAR: No PC Police for microaggressions against Jews

Author: Chau, Eddie

Publication info: Chau, Eddie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/agar-no-pc-police-microaggressions-against-jews/docview/3051639864/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=AGAR%3A+No+PC+Police+for+microaggressions+against+Jews&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Chau%2C+Eddie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Apparently protection from micro-aggressions, triggering, and hostile language doesn't extend to Jews at Canada's universities.
You remember microaggressions?
At the University of Toronto the explanation is contained in a document quoting Dr. Derald Wing Sue, "Microaggressions are the everyday verbal, nonverbal, and environmental slights, snubs, or insults, etc., whether intentional or unintentional, which communicate hostile, derogatory, or negative messages to target people based solely upon their marginalized group membership."
At McGill University the description of a training session at McGill's Teacher Support Union tells us, "Microaggressions happen so quickly, whether they be racial, gender-based, about sexual orientation, ableism, or another form of cultural oppression. The subsequent impacts of microaggressions disrupt the learning and safety of our classrooms."
Workshops, training, language and general lecturing of the student body and the public by universities have served to make some people tiptoe around controversial social subjects, lest those who self-appoint themselves to police the rest of us point the bony finger of blame in accusations of hate.
Both U of T and McGill are currently the sites of supposedly Pro-Palestinian rallies.
Supposedly, because as Joe Warmington has reported in the Sun, "Anti-Jewish segregation is happening on the campus of the University of Toronto."
Jews are not allowed to enter the encampment. "At least non-approved Jews. These unofficial border guards do ask for papers at the border crossing there.
Or, at least, proof of religion, affiliation or ideology."
Video exists of those same "border guards" refusing a professor entry. He insists it is because he is Jewish.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
Those border guards have no official status from any proper authority. They are self-appointed, masked and "protecting" an illegal encampment.
They also appear anti-Semitic.
That's macro-aggression.
It should set off all sorts of triggers at the U of T, but leadership there has always been accused of weakness on Jew hatred.
The Jerusalem Center for Public Affairs wrote, "The University of Toronto is one of the most highly regarded academic institutions in Canada. A coalition of anti-Israel groups initially used the university campus as a launching pad for what has become an annual series of events that take place in dozens of cities around the world."
They are referencing the annual Anti-Israeli Apartheid Week, seen by many as permission to show as anti-Semitic under cover of a supposed concern for Palestinians.
Canadian Press reports on McGill. "Both McGill and Quebec Premier François Legault have described the Montreal encampment as illegal, and asked police to help dismantle the tents - though as of Sunday the force had not complied with the request. Opponents of the camp, including counter-protesters who showed up Thursday, have said it makes some Jewish students feel unsafe."
One would think Jewish students feeling unsafe would unleash the PC Police with claims of micro-aggressions, triggering, and a need for workshops and healing for those students.
But no.
Protestors on campus and in the streets in Canada claim they are not anti-Jewish, but against what the Israeli government is doing in reaction to the Hamas attack of last October 7th.
How much more believable would those claims be if at least some demanding and sign waving showed condemnation of the terrorist group that attacked innocent Jews?
How about demands for the return of hostages taken by Hamas?
Or does all the anguish over micro and macro aggressions not apply to Jews?

Subject: Antisemitism; Jewish people; Colleges & universities; Microaggressions; College campuses

Business indexing term: Subject: Colleges & universities

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: University of Toronto; NAICS: 611310; Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Document 10 of 356

Carolyn Parrish surges ahead in race for Mississauga mayor: Poll

Author: Connor, Kevin

Publication info: Connor, Kevin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/carolyn-parrish-surges-ahead-race-mississauga/docview/3051437851/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Carolyn+Parrish+surges+ahead+in+race+for+Mississauga+mayor%3A+Poll&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Connor%2C+Kevin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mississauga mayoral candidate Carolyn Parrish has extended her lead in the race.
A new Liaison Strategies poll for the National Ethnic Press and Media Council of Canada finds Parrish is now at 28% among all voters, up from 18% in March.
"Just on candidate filings and withdrawals alone a lot has taken place in the Mississauga byelection," said David Valentin, principal at Liaison Strategies, in a release on Monday.
"Brian Crombie, ex-husband of former mayor and current Ontario Party Leader Bonnie Crombie, has filed. Peter McCallion, son of the longest-serving mayor of Mississauga, Hazel McCallion, has dropped out and endorsed the front runner, Carolyn Parrish.
"In the midst of all these comings and goings, Brian Crombie has debuted with 7% support among all voters, and Councillor Stephen Dasko has gained 3% and is now at 11%.
"However, the real story is former councillor (and former MP) Carolyn Parrish has grown her support by 8 points and now enjoys a 17-point lead," Valentin said.
Support for Parrish is strongest with seniors, who are considered the most reliable voters.
Councillor Dipika Damerla has 20% of the vote putting him in second place and Alvin Tedjo at 17%.
"Damerla does best among seniors while Tedjo does best with the 18-34 cohort," the pollster noted.
Valentin added: "The race in Mississauga has had plenty of developments and name recognition is almost certainly playing a large factor."
The survey was done April 29 and 30 using interactive voice response technology, and the poll surveyed 907 Mississauga residents. The margin of error for the poll is plus or minus 3.25%, 19 times out of 20.
The mayoral byelection, being held after Bonnie Crombie quit to take the leadership of the provincial Liberal Party, will be held on June 10. The winner will serve for the remainder of the 2022-26 city council term.
AMONG ALL VOTERS
28% - Carolyn Parrish (+10 from March 21-22 poll)
15% - Dipika Damerla (No Change)
13% - Alvin Tedjo (No Change)
8% - Stephen Dasko (+3)
5% - Brian Crombie (New Candidate)
4% - George Tavares (No Change)
2% - David Shaw (No Change)
25% Undecided (-12)
DECIDED VOTERS ONLY
37% - Carolyn Parrish (+8)
20% - Dipika Damerla (-4)
17% - Alvin Tedjo (-4)
11% - Stephen Dasko (+3)
7% - Brian Crombie (New Candidate)
5% - George Tavares (-1)
3% - David Shaw (No Change)

Subject: Voice response technology; Polls & surveys; Mayors; Voters

Identifier / keyword: mississauga; mayoral; candidate; carolyn; parrish; extended

Document 11 of 356

Honour day; Remembrance ceremony 'stark reminder'of cost to keep communities safe

Author: Connor, Kevin

Publication info: Connor, Kevin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/honour-day-remembrance-ceremony-stark-reminderof/docview/3051255160/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Honour+day%3B+Remembrance+ceremony+%27stark+reminder%27of+cost+to+keep+communities+safe&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Connor%2C+Kevin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Heroes in life, not death, was the message.
Four police officers had their names added to the Ontario Police Memorial at Queen's Park on Sunday.
Thousands of officers attended the 25th annual Ceremony of Remembrance, organized by the Ontario Police Memorial Foundation.
The annual Ceremony of Remembrance pays tribute to Ontario's fallen police officers who have died in the line of duty.
"Today marks the 25th anniversary of the Ontario Police Memorial Foundation Ceremony of Remembrance," said Jason Tomlinson, president of the memorial foundation. "Our annual gathering at Queen's Park is a stark reminder of the cost of safer communities. Although it is a time of reverence and reflection, it is also a time of support and love.
"It is my privilege to honour the 277 officers lost in the line of duty while sadly adding four more names to the Wall of Honour. This year, we are engraving the names of two historic officers and two active duty officers to the honour role."
The names added to the Ontario Police Memorial include Sgt. Eric Mueller of the Russell County OPP detachment and Det.-Const. Steven Tourangeau of the Perth County OPP detachment.
Mueller died in May 2023 while responding to a call outside of Ottawa. The 42-year-old father of two was shot and killed responding to reports of gunfire.
Tourangeau also died in May 2023 after being involved in a collision with a school bus.
Other additions included Const.
Chief Edward Lavery of the Prescott police, who died in 1928, and Det.-Sgt. Thomas A Whitelaw of Metro Toronto (1938).
"Today, we pay tribute to the heroic police officers who lost their lives in the line of duty," said Premier Doug Ford. "We owe a debt to these officers that can never be repaid. My deepest personal condolences to the families, friends and colleagues of the fallen. We will never forget their service and sacrifice.
"Our government will always stand with the brave and selfless men and women who put their lives at risk every day for the safety and protection of the people of Ontario. I extend my gratitude to the Ontario Police Memorial Foundation for ensuring that no fallen officer in Ontario is forgotten."

Subject: Rites & ceremonies; Police

Remembrance ceremony 'stark reminder' of cost to keep communities safe, police say

Author: Connor, Kevin

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Remembrance+ceremony+%27stark+reminder%27+of+cost+to+keep+communities+safe%2C+police+say&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Connor%2C+Kevin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Heroes in life, not death, was the message.
Four police officers had their names added to the Ontario Police Memorial at Queen's Park on Sunday.
Today, I stood with thousands of Ontario police service members in honouring the service and sacrifice of those who have died in the line of duty at the 25th annual @HeroesInLife Ceremony of Remembrance. They will never be forgotten. #HeroesInLife
- Chief Myron Demkiw (@TPSMyronDemkiw) May 5, 2024

The annual Ceremony of Remembrance pays tribute to Ontario's fallen police officers who have died in the line of duty.

Document 13 of 356

Put money where mouth is; Trudeau, Ford should attach personal fortunes to EV corporate welfare

Author: Hill, Tegan; Clemens, Jason

Publication info: Hill, Tegan; Clemens, Jason. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/put-money-where-mouth-is-trudeau-ford-should/docview/3050755893/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Put+money+where+mouth+is%3B+Trudeau%2C+Ford+should+attach+personal+fortunes+to+EV+corporate+welfare&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Hill%2C+Tegan%3BClemens%2C+Jason&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last week, with their latest tranche of corporate welfare for the electric vehicle (EV) sector, the Trudeau and Ford governments announced a $5 billion subsidy for Honda to help build an EV battery plant and ultimately manufacture EVs in Ontario. Here's a challenge: If politicians in both governments truly believe these measures are in the public interest, they should tie their personal fortunes with the outcomes of these subsidies (a.k.a. corporate welfare).
One of the major challenges with corporate welfare is the horrendous economic incentives. The politicians and bureaucrats who distribute corporate welfare have no vested financial interest in the outcome of the program. Whether these programs are spectacularly successful - or more likely spectacular failures - the politicians and bureaucrats experience no direct financial gain or loss. Simply put, they're investing taxpayer money, not their own.
Put differently, the discipline imposed on investors in private markets, such as the risk of losing money or even going out of business, is wholly absent in the government sector. Indeed, the history of corporate welfare in Canada, at both the federal and provincial levels, is rife with abject failures due in large measure to the absence of this investing discipline.
In the last 12 months in Ontario, automakers have been major beneficiaries of corporate welfare.
The $5 billion for Honda is on top of $13.2 billion to Volkswagen and $15 billion to Stellantis.
That equates to roughly $979 per taxpayer nationally for federal subsidies and an additional $1,372 for Ontario taxpayers. And these figures don't include the debt interest costs that will be incurred as both governments are borrowing money to finance the subsidies.
And there's legitimate reason to be skeptical already of the potential success of these large-scale industrial interventions by the federal (Liberal) and Ontario (Conservative) governments. EV sales in both Canada and the United States haven't grown as expected by governments despite purchase subsidies.
Disappointing EV sales have led several auto manufacturers, including Toyota and Ford, to scale back their EV production plans.
There are also real concerns about the practical ability of EV manufacturers to secure required materials. Consider the minerals needed for EV batteries. According to a recent study, 388 new mines- including 50 lithium mines, 60 nickel mines and 17 cobalt mines- would be required by 2030 to meet EV adoption commitments by various governments. For perspective, there were a total of 340 metal mines operating across Canada and the U.S. in 2021. The massive task of finding, constructing and developing this level of new mines seems impractical and unattainable, meaning that EV plants being built now will struggle to secure needed inputs. Indeed, depending on the type of mine, it takes anywhere from six to 18 years to develop.
Which brings us back to the Trudeau and Ford governments.
Given the economic incentive problems and practical challenges to a large-scale transition to EVs, would members of the Trudeau and Ford governments - including the Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford - want to attach a portion of their personal pensions to the success of these corporate welfare programs? More specifically, assume an arrangement whereby those politicians would share the benefits of the program's success but also share any losses through the value of their pensions. If the programs work as marketed, the politicians would enjoy higher valued pensions.
But if the programs disappoint or even fail, their pensions would be reduced or even cancelled.
Would these politicians still support billions in corporate handouts if their personal financial well-being was tied to the outcomes? As the funding of private companies to develop the EV sector in Ontario continues with the support of taxpayer subsidies, Ontarians and all Canadians should consider the misalignment of economic incentives underpinning these subsidies and the practical challenges to the success of this industrial intervention.
Clemens and Hill are economists with the Fraser Institute

Subject: Bureaucrats; Mines; Manufacturers; Success; Welfare; Subsidies; Politicians

Business indexing term: Subject: Manufacturers Subsidies

Location: United States--US; Canada

Identifier / keyword: latest; tranche; corporate; welfare; electric; vehicle

Title: Put money where mouth is; Trudeau, Ford should attach personal fortunes to EV corporate welfare

Publication title: The Toronto Sun; Toronto

First page: A.6

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 5, 2024

column: Guest Opinion

Section: News

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 08373175

Document 14 of 356

Fergus the partisan speaker needs to go

Author: Gunter, Lorne

Publication info: Gunter, Lorne. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fergus-partisan-speaker-needs-go/docview/3050755647/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Fergus+the+partisan+speaker+needs+to+go&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=Gunter%2C+Lorne&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Speaker Greg Fergus may not mean to be biased in favour of the Liberals in the House of Commons.
Perhaps his partisanship is just drilled so deep into his marrow that he doesn't even realize how biased he's being.
That doesn't matter. Fergus acts in a partisan, pro-Liberal way too often. So, therefore he must go.
He has put his thumb on the Liberal scale too many times. And in his all-powerful role - where objectivity and impartiality are key - whether his bias is conscious or unconscious doesn't matter. He's too tainted to be trusted as neutral arbiter of Commons business.
Ever since Fergus expelled Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre from question period on Tuesday, there have been accusations against the Speaker that he was deliberately trying to protect Prime Minister Justin Trudeau at a time when Poilievre was scoring political points for hammering at the Liberals'stubborn devotion drug decriminalization, which has been an unmitigated disaster at ending addiction and overdose deaths.
The policy has increased street crime and deaths, not lowered them.
Trudeau started the fracas with Poilievre by ignoring the opposition leader's questions about the drug policy and instead asking the Conservative why he consorts with white nationalists - a reference to an impromptu visit Poilievre made two weeks ago to a Maritimes tax-protest camp where one of the RVs sported the symbol of a white supremist group on its door.
Misdirection and false accusations are all the Liberals have right now, since every policy initiative they take, including last week's budget, sinks them further and further in the polls.
Moments later, Poilievre called both Trudeau and his drug policy "wacko." (Apparently, the truth is not a defence in Parliament the way it would be in a libel suit.) Trudeau responded by calling Poilievre "spineless." Yet, Speaker Fergus only saw fit to throw Poilievre out.
Fergus was right about Poilievre's language being "unparliamentary," but no more unparliamentary than Trudeau's.
And Poilievre was guilty, as Fergus said, of "calling into question the character of an individual member of parliament."
But so, too, was Trudeau. Indeed, I would argue it is worse character assassination for Trudeau to claim Poilievre is "actively courting the support of groups with white nationalist views," than it is for Poilievre to label Trudeau and his policies wacko.
Where Fergus displayed his bias was in seeing and acting only on Poilievre's infractions of the Commons'rules and not Trudeau's.
Compounding Fergus's one-sidedness was the fact the Speaker didn't go through all the steps necessary to warn an MP, particularly one who is also a party leader, that he was in danger of expulsion. Fergus acted arbitrarily and without regard for Poilievre's due process.
Fergus also expelled Alberta Conservative MP Rachael Thomas for challenging his uneven application of House rules.
Thomas Mulcair, the former NDP and Opposition Leader, told CTV that Fergus had acted in a manner that "was manifestly, obviously partisan." Before becoming NDP leader, Mulcair has been his party's House leader and is very familiar with Parliamentary rules.
Fergus clearly exercised a double standard, coming down harder and faster on the Conservatives than the Liberal. Such poor judgement is completely unacceptable in someone with has much unchallenged power as the Speaker of the House of Commons.
It is also part of a pattern of partisan bias on Fergus's part since becoming Speaker. In December, Fergus landed in hot water for making a video tribute, dressed in his Speaker's robes, to mark the retirement of the outgoing leader of the Ontario Liberals.
Even if you believe none of this rises to a level of bias that disqualifies Fergus from his job, in his post even the perception of bias is enough. And there is enough perception of bias to force Fergus to resign to preserve public trust that the House of Commons if not merely a Liberal institution. lgunter@postmedia.com @sunlornegunter

Subject: Members of Parliament; Partisanship; Drug policy; Bias

Identifier / keyword: speaker; fergus; biased; favour; liberals; house

Title: Fergus the partisan speaker needs to go

Publication title: The Toronto Sun; Toronto

First page: A.15

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 5, 2024

Section: Comment

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 08373175

Source type: Newspaper

GUNTER: Fergus the partisan speaker needs to go

Author: Pollard, Dave

Publication info: Pollard, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/gunter-fergus-partisan-speaker-needs-go/docview/3050688580/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=GUNTER%3A+Fergus+the+partisan+speaker+needs+to+go&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Pollard%2C+Dave&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 16 of 356

Parliament's referee must be impartial

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/parliaments-referee-must-be-impartial/docview/3050616107/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Parliament%27s+referee+must+be+impartial&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We've seen the best and the worst examples of the exercise of the Speaker's authority in Parliament recently.
The best was Ontario Speaker Ted Arnott, who showed courage and a true understanding of his role as independent referee in the Legislature by banning keffiyehs from the chamber.
Keffiyehs, the checkered scarves worn by supporters of Hamas, are now seen as a political statement. In today's highly charged atmosphere, where protesters wearing that garment have taken to the streets and university campuses with chaotic results, banning it from the legislative precinct was the right thing to do. Arnott did not flinch in his decision, even though he was urged to back down by his own party leader, Premier Doug Ford.
If only his counterpart in the federal House had shown as much bravery and class.
Instead, Greg Fergus demonstrated once again why he doesn't seem to grasp that as Speaker he represents the voice of all the people of Canada - not just Liberals. He must cast off his party affiliation, set his personal feelings aside and rise above partisan politics.
In tossing Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre this week, Fergus showed he's not even-handed.
Poilievre was thrown out of the chamber for calling Prime Minister Justin Trudeau "wacko," in a question about B.C.'s drug policy. While unparliamentary, that's a mild epithet. Trudeau once called a cabinet minister, "a piece of s---." His father infamously uttered "Fuddle duddle." Put in context, Justin Trudeau had earlier provocatively called Poilievre "spineless," and tried to associate him with white nationalists. Trudeau was not scolded, although Fergus told him not to make comments that call into question the character of an MP.
Poilievre, on the other hand, was told to withdraw his "wacko" comment. He immediately said he would replace it with "extremist," but was told to leave the chamber anyway.
This is a one-sided approach to enforcing parliamentary rules. Fergus will lose the confidence of MPs if he's not careful. He recently failed to censure MPs who gloatingly draped themselves in keffiyehs after a divisive vote on Palestine.
This country has a great history of legendary Speakers who have upheld parliamentary tradition. We're a better country because of them. Fergus should remember when you don the Speaker's tricorn hat, you're also wearing a great tradition of impartiality.

People: Trudeau, Justin

Letters to the Editor, May 4, 2024

Full text: TRUDEAU HYPERVENTILATING
Observed with interest the heated exchange between our woke prime minister and Pierre Poilievre in the House on April 30. Unfortunately, Justin Trudeau seems to hold the view that lurking around each corner is a white supremacist. All too often, his desperation to cling to power is on full display as he hyperventilates while lecturing those who don't hold his views. A wise man once pointed out to me that when small men cast long shadows, the sun is about to set!
Dennis Tschirhart
Simcoe
(A wise saying indeed. We foresee the sun setting on Trudeau as PM)
FERGUS THE FAILED SPEAKER
It is hard to believe that question period could deteriorate any lower than it has, which is an insult slugfest with the Speaker being very pro Liberal. Justin Trudeau never answers a direct question, but he will throw as much shade as possible in an insulting manner at Pierre Poilievre, and then get offended when responded to in kind. It is time for all the Parliamentarians to try to be more dignified in question period. If Trudeau cannot answer a question properly, he should just keep quiet.
Vivienne Edwards
Peterborough
(The majority of Speakers who have served in the role have done a decent job remaining non partisan - Fergus has no such talent)
OUT OF ORDER
The actions of the Speaker in the House of Commons during question period on April 30 were disgracefully biased. He allowed the prime minister to make defamatory remarks about the leader of the Opposition while not responding to the question that was asked. After he ordered the leader of the Opposition to leave the House, he continued to allow disparaging remarks from other MPs about the Opposition leader in his absence. He lost control of the House by allowing unbridled PM remarks.
Ken Amesbury
Ottawa
(The Conservatives were correct in demanding he resign. Of course that was never going to go anywhere but at least it forced the discussion)
NOT THE MAJORITY VIEW
With Marit Stiles and Sarah Jama pushing to get the keffiyeh into the Ontario legislature, will their next power play be a push to have the Hamas flag flown over the legislature? Stiles and Jama do not represent the majority of Canadians. The Speaker of the house is correct - no political symbols should be on display in the legislature.
Mary Bronson
(Exactly)

Document 18 of 356

EDITORIAL: Parliament's referee must be impartial

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/editorial-parliaments-referee-must-be-impartial/docview/3050558825/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=EDITORIAL%3A+Parliament%27s+referee+must+be+impartial&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We've seen the best and the worst examples of the exercise of the Speaker's authority in Parliament recently.
The best was Ontario Speaker Ted Arnott, who showed courage and a true understanding of his role as independent referee in the Legislature by banning keffiyehs from the chamber.
Keffiyehs, the checkered scarves worn by supporters of Hamas, are now seen as a political statement. In today's highly-charged atmosphere, where protesters wearing that garment have taken to the streets and university campuses with chaotic results, banning it from the legislative precinct was the right thing to do. Arnott did not flinch in his decision, even though he was urged to back down by his own party leader, Premier Doug Ford.
If only his counterpart in the federal House had shown and much bravery and class.
Instead, Greg Fergus demonstrated once again why he doesn't seem to grasp that as Speaker he represents the voice of all the people of Canada - not just Liberals. He must cast off his party affiliation, set his personal feelings aside and rise above partisan politics.
In tossing Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre this week, Fergus showed he's not even-handed.
Poilievre was thrown out of the chamber for calling Prime Minister Justin Trudeau "wacko," in a question about B.C.'s drug policy. While unparliamentary, that's a mild epithet. Trudeau once called a cabinet minister, "a piece of s- - -." His father infamously uttered, "fuddle duddle." Put in context, Trudeau had earlier provocatively called Poilievre "spineless," and tried to associate him with white nationalists. Trudeau was not scolded, although Fergus told him not to make comments that call into question the character of an MP.
Poilievre, on the other hand, was told to withdraw his "wacko" comment. He immediately said he would replace it with "extremist," but was told to leave the chamber anyway.
This is a one-sided approach to enforcing parliamentary rules. Fergus will lose the confidence of MPs if he's not careful. He recently failed to censure MPs who gloatingly draped themselves in keffiyehs after a divisive vote on Palestine.
This country has a great history of legendary Speakers who have upheld parliamentary tradition. We're a better country because of them. Fergus should remember when you don the Speaker's tricorn hat, you're also wearing a great tradition of impartiality.

Identifier / keyword: worst; examples; exercise; authority; parliament; recently

Title: EDITORIAL: Parliament's referee must be impartial

Document 19 of 356

Number crunched; Poll after poll shows Canadians done with Trudeau

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/number-crunched-poll-after-shows-canadians-done/docview/3050406652/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Number+crunched%3B+Poll+after+poll+shows+Canadians+done+with+Trudeau&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It might be time to call in a priest to perform last rites on the corpse that is Justin Trudeau's Liberal movement.
If something isn't done soon, the stench coming off the corpse of what was once the most successful political party in the western world will be unbearable, much like listening to Trudeau.
On Wednesday, two different polling firms released results showing a 21-point gap between Trudeau's movement and the Conservative Party of Canada under Pierre Poilievre.
Leger, the most accurate polling firm in the last two federal elections, released numbers in a poll conducted for Postmedia showing the Conservatives at 44% support nationally to 23% for the Liberals. With the NDP polling at just 17% nationally, the Tories have a four-point lead over the combined Liberal and NDP vote.
That means if Trudeau and NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh finally consummated their coalition agreement and merged, they still wouldn't beat the Conservatives.
Hours later, Abacus Data, in a poll for the Toronto Star, released its poll showing the same numbers on the national front: 44% CPC, 23% LPC, 17% NDP.
Two polls from separate firms, for distinct media companies, and the same result.
Since the latest budget from the Trudeau Liberals was released on April 16, there have been seven different polls from five different firms showing a lead of 18-21 points for the Conservatives.
In the Leger poll, Poilievre's Tories hold a commanding lead in every region of the country except Quebec: a 10-point lead in Atlantic Canada, a 22-point lead in Ontario, and west of there it simply gets embarrassing.
Poilievre's Conservatives hold a 25-point lead over the Liberals among men, 47% to 22%, and an 18-point lead among women, 42% to 24%. Among voters aged 18-34, the very people Trudeau aimed the budget at, the Tories have 46% support, the NDP 20% and the Liberals 17%.
Among voters 35-54, the Conservatives are at 43%, the NDP at 21% and the Liberals at 20%.
Budget 2024 was an utter failure for the Trudeau Liberals. They promised billions in new spending, they cravenly attempted to attract younger voters, and the results speak for themselves.
Among their most coveted demographics, they're in third place.
Part of this is that voters are simply done with Trudeau after nearly nine years of him being in power. The problem for the party is it has transformed itself from a successful party into a movement built around Trudeau himself.
It refers to itself as a movement, but few Canadians still want to move with Trudeau.
Asked by Leger who'd make the best prime minister, 32% of Canadians said Poilievre. That's lower than his party numbers, but still double Trudeau. Just 16% chose Trudeau and only 11% chose Singh - below the 14% who said none of the above and the 14% who said they didn't know.
It's not just the polling numbers that show Trudeau's Liberal movement is slipping away into the dark abyss. The fundraising numbers tell their own story. The latest fundraising figures show the Tories raising three times as much money as the Liberals in the first three months of the year.
Between January and March, the Conservatives raised $10.7 million, the Liberals $3.1 million and the NDP $1.3 million.
If an election were held today, it's clear Trudeau would be thrown out of office and the Liberal party - make that "movement" - would be decimated. The only thing keeping Trudeau in power is Singh and the other MPs waiting to secure their six-year pensions in 2025.
The current government needs to be defeated and Canadians should be headed to the polls, but a collection of MPs will hang on for dear life to ensure they qualify for their pensions next year.
Our democracy isn't well served when people are voting for their own personal interests instead of the country's best interests.
The smell of death hangs over Trudeau's Liberals. Let's pray we can exorcise them from our national life sooner rather than later. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Polls & surveys; Canadians; Voters

Identifier / keyword: priest; perform; rites; corpse; justin; liberal

Title: Number crunched; Poll after poll shows Canadians done with Trudeau

LILLEY: Polls continue to show Canadians are done with Trudeau

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-polls-continue-show-canadians-are-done/docview/3050136165/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Polls+continue+to+show+Canadians+are+done+with+Trudeau&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 21 of 356

Come On, Eileen!; De Villa not telling truth about drug decriminalization

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/come-on-eileen-de-villa-not-telling-truth-about/docview/3050010520/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Come+On%2C+Eileen%21%3B+De+Villa+not+telling+truth+about+drug+decriminalization&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Dr. Eileen de Villa wants you to believe that if Toronto follows British Columbia's lead and decriminalizes all hard drugs, everything will be fine.
In a video statement released Tuesday, de Villa said if hard drugs are decriminalized, people won't be using drugs in public willy-nilly.
"On open public drug use, let me be perfectly clear," de Villa said. "Lighting up a crack pipe in a playground or injecting drugs on the subway, these are not acceptable and should not be allowed."
Except under the proposal the city put forward to the federal government, those activities would be allowed.
The city was very clear in its proposal, dubbed the Toronto Model, that drug use would only be prohibited in child-care facilities, K-12 schools and airports.
That means police would be powerless to stop someone sitting down at your local community playground and lighting up a crack pipe as children played all around. Officers would have no tools at their disposal to deal with someone who was injecting drugs on the subway.
The proponents of this plan continually say drug addiction is a health issue and not a law enforcement issue and people shouldn't be charged for small amounts of drugs for personal use. According to the city's own proposal for decriminalization, there were 617 people charged with simple possession in 2021, but in 581 of those instances, possession was not the only charge, it was an add on to other charges such as assault.
That means just 36 people were charged with simple possession as the sole offence in 2021.
This proves the activists, like de Villa, are making a false argument about policing and charges.
Taking away the ability of police to deal with people who are causing public disorder through their open drug use in public places will lead to chaos. This is part of the reason B.C. Premier David Eby has asked the federal government to recriminalize hard drugs in most instances.
These drugs would still be legal in private dwellings, even shelters and some other locations, but be illegal in most public settings. B.C. saw the chaos and disorder caused by the wholesale decriminalization of drugs and they want to walk it back but so far, the Trudeau government has been noncommittal.
Trudeau's addictions minister Ya'ara Saks would only say on Wednesday that she is awaiting information from B.C. before making a decision. Those comments are more open than earlier in the week when she would only say that this was the first year of a three-year pilot project.
At that time, Saks also refused to rule out granting Toronto their exemption.
On Wednesday, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre sent Trudeau a letter demanding he reject Toronto's proposal "to legalize crack, cocaine, heroin, meth and other hard drugs."
Poilievre pointed to the massive increase in drug overdose deaths since the Trudeau Liberals took office and began liberalizing Canada's drug policies and laws. Those stats alone should be all that is needed for Saks to realize the decriminalization in B.C., the kind Toronto wants, is a mess and not worth standing by. B.C. saw a record 2,546 overdose deaths in 2023. That's more than Ontario in a province with one-third of our population.
That is not a record worth following.
Is there an overdose problem? Absolutely, but decriminalization is not the answer.
The four pillars of drug policy have long been prevention, treatment, enforcement and harm reduction. The activists who now control our policy from the public health side focus almost exclusively on harm reduction and making drugs easier to get.
That's not a compassionate answer when we aren't also acting on the other pillars.
Toronto's policy is deeply flawed, de Villa is trying to mislead the entire city and Toronto's application for decriminalization should be rejected.

Subject: Decriminalization; Drug overdose; Drug policy; Harm reduction; Drug use

Identifier / keyword: eileen; villa; believe; toronto; follows; british

Title: Come On, Eileen!; De Villa not telling truth about drug decriminalization

Document 22 of 356

Magical power of a 'piece of cloth'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/magical-power-piece-cloth/docview/3050010146/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Magical+power+of+a+%27piece+of+cloth%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the furor about the ruling by Speaker Ted Arnott to ban keffiyehs from the Ontario legislature, MPP Sarah Jama claimed it's "just a piece of cloth."
Oh, a keffiyeh is so much more than that. It's a magical piece of clothing that allows protesters to avoid arrest if they express views that might be considered hateful. It gives them the freedom to say and do anything they want - whether it's to call for the death of Jews or to praise rape and murder by Hamas on Oct. 7.
It gives immunity to students camping out on university campuses, denying others their right to attend classes.
Protest is legal. Occupation is not. They're prepared to trample Western democratic rights and freedoms in support of Hamas - a terrorist organization that doesn't support freedom or democracy in Gaza.
Any non-keffiyeh-wearing student behaving that way would be summarily tossed from the property or expelled from the university.
Those wearing the magical checkered scarf are given a free pass.
Parliamentary rules, enforced by the Speaker, forbid partisan attire so there's a neutral place for debate. But if you don the magical keffiyeh, you can ignore the rules.
Shockingly, Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles has turned this into a political football, blaming Premier Doug Ford because some of his MPs have balked at giving unanimous consent to wear the keffiyeh, as is their right.
Stiles tossed Jama from the NDP caucus last year over incendiary comments on Israel.
Now she's supporting her right to break long-held rules of parliament.
Jama refused to remove the keffiyeh and refused to leave the Legislature when asked to last week. Because, well, it's a magical "piece of cloth," so the rules don't apply.
If MPPs were wearing symbols in support of Israel, no doubt the NDP would ask they be removed.
Jama couldn't live by the rules of party politics. Now she can't live by parliamentary rules. It seems she wants to reshape Canada into her own image - one in which Jewish Canadians apparently don't exist and where the rules protecting democratic freedoms can be tossed if they don't suit you.
This isn't about her freedom to wear a keffiyeh.
She can wear one anywhere else she wants, just not in the legislature, where parliamentary rules of fairness and democracy are paramount.

Subject: Legislatures; Democracy

Location: Israel
