Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+to+the+Editor%2C+April+27%2C+2024&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: POLITICAL CULTURE
Re "Jama won't play ball" (The Canadian Press, April 26): Was Sarah Jama wearing the kaffiyeh regularly prior to October 7, 2023? If yes, it is cultural to her. If not, it is clearly political. End of discussion.
Wayne D. Crawford
Markham
(From what we recall, no she wasn't)
DUTY TO CHALLENGE
I get it that the official opposition, namely NDP Leader Marit Stiles, has a duty to challenge the official Conservative Party on all matters. But to challenge the longstanding legislature rules on political symbols she has sold her soul. Unbelievable that a seasoned politician would ruin her reputation on a timeless position upheld by all parties over the years.
Wayne Smith
Scarborough
(Ford shouldn't have stepped into it either)
SHAME ON YOU
It is disgusting that politicians, and the riff-raff of our society here in Ontario, are demanding the rules of the legislature be changed to allow the keffiyeh be worn in provincial parliament. Is it not enough that our Jewish friends be subjected to hate mongering. Shame on the young generation of haters.
Bill Wagstaff
Mississauga
(A lost generation of clueless youth who have no regard for history. It's terrifying)
EARN RESPECT
I was raised to believe that people in suits automatically commanded respect. As I got older, I learned that nothing could be further from the truth. Along with decent people, many of the biggest criminals and cruel, heartless, overly judgmental people wear them as well. Respect must be earned, not automatically granted. Case in point with respect to the latest provincial caucus controversy. What people wear in the legislature or anywhere else should not matter. What they represent and accomplish should. And time wasted on issues like this mean important work does not get done expediently. Earn respect don't automatically expect it.
Rick Lockman
Orillia
(But specifically on the issue of political symbols most accept the rules and have for a long time)
SOCK SALESMAN
Some people are saying Trudeau won't quit because he has nothing to fall back on. I'm sure he could always get a job as a sock salesman for one of the Chinese businesses he so admires.
G. Maguire
Belleville
(He's have to work hard. That's not his thing)

Document 51 of 356

EV industry huge gamble for taxpayers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ev-industry-huge-gamble-taxpayers/docview/3047033422/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=EV+industry+huge+gamble+for+taxpayers&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Someone should tell Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Ontario Premier Doug Ford that creating a successful electric vehicle industry in Canada will require a lot more than throwing taxpayers'money at it.
That's the easy part, which Trudeau and Ford have been announcing over the past year, jointly bestowing mega-billions of dollars in public support for EV battery plants.
Parliamentary Budget Officer Yves Giroux has said it will take 20 years for the two governments to break even in recovering up to $28.2 billion in subsidies awarded to two companies - $15 billion to Stellantis-LG Energy Solutions to build a battery production plant in Windsor and $13.2 billion to Volkswagen to do the same in St. Thomas.
On Thursday, Trudeau and Ford announced another $5 billion deal in support of a $15-billion project by Honda to build another battery plant in Alliston, along with the construction of other facilities to create a supply chain for EVs.
They said it will create 1,000 jobs, secure 4,200 existing ones and lead to spinoff employment of more than 28,000 related jobs.
What they didn't say is that creating an EV supply chain from scratch involves multiple massive projects that will have to be simultaneously developed and co-ordinated.
These include mining and transporting the raw materials for EV batteries to production facilities, manufacturing EV batteries, manufacturing EV vehicles, cathode and anode manufacturing, and EV battery recycling.
The good news is Canada, has an abundance of the metals, minerals and other raw materials needed to build EV batteries and compete with the global market leader, which is China.
But getting those raw materials out of the ground will require an unprecedented mining boom in ecologically sensitive parts of Canada, including Ontario's Ring of Fire, with governments needing to dramatically streamline their existing environmental processes for getting mines and roads approved.
While some Indigenous groups favour these developments, others, who will have to be meaningfully consulted, are opposed.
The auto sector is vital to the Canadian economy so it's hardly surprising Trudeau's Liberals and Ford's Progressive Conservatives are boasting about their support of it.
But we won't know whether the multi-billiondollar public investments they are making into Canada's EV sector today will give taxpayers good value for money spent, until years down the road.

Subject: Supply chains; Raw materials; Manufacturing

Business indexing term: Subject: Supply chains Manufacturing

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: someone; prime; minister; justin; trudeau; ontario

Document 52 of 356

EDITORIAL: EV industry huge gamble for taxpayers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/editorial-ev-industry-huge-gamble-taxpayers/docview/3046905445/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=EDITORIAL%3A+EV+industry+huge+gamble+for+taxpayers&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Someone should tell Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Ontario Premier Doug Ford that creating a successful electric vehicle industry in Canada will require a lot more than throwing taxpayers' money at it.
That's the easy part, which Trudeau and Ford have been announcing over the past year, jointly bestowing mega-billions of dollars in public support for EV battery plants.
Parliamentary Budget Officer Yves Giroux has said it will take 20 years for the two governments to break even in recovering up to $28.2 billion in subsidies awarded to two companies - $15 billion to Stellantis-LG Energy Solutions to build a battery production plant in Windsor and $13.2 billion to Volkswagen to do the same in St. Thomas.
On Thursday, Trudeau and Ford announced another $5 billion deal in support of a $15-billion project by Honda to build another battery plant in Alliston, along with the construction of other facilities to create a supply chain for EVs.
They said it will create 1,000 jobs, secure 4,200 existing ones and lead to spinoff employment of more than 28,000 related jobs.
What they didn't say is that creating an EV supply chain from scratch involves multiple massive projects that will have to be simultaneously developed and co-ordinated.
These include mining and transporting the raw materials for EV batteries to production facilities, manufacturing EV batteries, manufacturing EV vehicles, cathode and anode manufacturing, and EV battery recycling.
The good news is Canada, has an abundance of the metals, minerals and other raw materials needed to build EV batteries and compete with the global market leader, which is China.
But getting those raw materials out of the ground will require an unprecedented mining boom in ecologically sensitive parts of Canada, including Ontario's Ring of Fire, with governments needing to dramatically streamline their existing environmental processes for getting mines and roads approved.
While some Indigenous groups favour these developments, others, who will have to be meaningfully consulted, are opposed.
The auto sector is vital to the Canadian economy so it's hardly surprising Trudeau's Liberals and Ford's Progressive Conservatives are boasting about their support of it.
But we won't know whether the multi-billion-dollar public investments they are making into Canada's EV sector today will give taxpayers good value for money spent, until years down the road.

Subject: Supply chains; Raw materials; Manufacturing

Business indexing term: Subject: Supply chains Manufacturing

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: someone; prime; minister; justin; trudeau; ontario

Document 53 of 356

Just Go Find More Docs!; Economically illiterate budget will chase 'em away, but Grits don't care

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/just-go-find-more-docs-economically-illiterate/docview/3046139583/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Just+Go+Find+More+Docs%21%3B+Economically+illiterate+budget+will+chase+%27em+away%2C+but+Grits+don%27t+care&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Trudeau government is trying to assure Canadians that if its tax changes scare away family doctors, it will just recruit new ones from other countries.
It's both an admission that its policy is a problem and it's clueless on how to fix it.
In last week's budget, the Trudeau government announced it would increase the inclusion rate on capital gains from 50% to 66%.
Admittedly, this change doesn't affect the lives of most Canadians, but if you want to have doctors, plumbers, real estate agents, small business owners - or if you dream of owning a family cottage - you should care about this change.
The Trudeau Liberals are trying to claim, falsely, that their changes will only affect the top 0.13% of income tax earners - you know, the rich folks with the monocle and top hat like the Monopoly man.
That's far from the truth, and one of the main targets of this change are family doctors, the kind of physician in short supply in Canada.
Despite what many people think, your family doctor doesn't work for the government. They run their practice as a small business and bill the government for seeing you.
They have no workplace pension, so most save and invest through their corporation, something established doctors have done for decades.
The tax changes in this latest budget will hurt them, make it harder for established doctors to retire, and make it less lucrative for new med school graduates to set up practice in Canada. As provincial governments work hard to attract more family doctors, the Trudeau government in Ottawa is making it less attractive to set up a practice but telling Canadians, don't worry.
"We recognize that, but one of the things that we often need will continue to do is encourage foreign credential recognition," Rechie Valdez, Trudeau's small business minister, told the CBC last week.
This is one of the dumbest things I've heard a cabinet minister say in more than 25 years of covering politicians and government officials saying dumb things.
In her single statement, Valdez acknowledged their tax change policies might chase away the already rare commodity that is a family doctor, but they'll just recruit more family doctors.
"This is not something Canada can recruit our way out of," the Ontario Medical Association said in response to the clip of Valdez circulating on social media.
The OMA said doctors are already facing financial strain and these tax changes will make it worse.
"This could threaten physician practices and patient access," the OMA said.
Some Canadians may say that doctors are wealthy so who cares, tax them more. That sounds good until you can't get a family doctor because they've all moved to Florida, North Carolina or Arizona.
It's not just doctors being hit.
It's anyone in small business who has been successful. The Trudeau government wants to tax success to the point there's no more success.
What do you expect from a dilettante whose family hasn't had to break a sweat to earn a dollar since his grandfather built a chain of gas stations across Montreal in the 1930s? This is emblematic for the entire Trudeau government. It's economically illiterate.
These tax changes won't just hit doctors, it will hit many others. The family that bought an apartment block or a multiplex home to offer a second income or retirement fund will also be hit.
Sure, Trudeau says he wants more family doctors and more small landlords, but his tax policy discourages them.
Increasingly, I'm hearing from people who have a family cottage and who want to leave it to their kids or grandkids, but the capital gains tax changes will make it impossible.
These people aren't the super wealthy the Liberals claim they are taxing; they are the middle class the Liberals claims to support.
This budget is a mess in more ways than one, and the pain will be felt for generations. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Small business; Canadians; Illiteracy; Decades; Capital gains

Business indexing term: Subject: Small business Capital gains

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: trudeau; government; trying; assure; canadians; changes

Document 54 of 356

Ford plugs into EV deal; Secures Honda investment, sets sights on Toyota pact

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-plugs-into-ev-deal-secures-honda-investment/docview/3046139524/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+plugs+into+EV+deal%3B+Secures+Honda+investment%2C+sets+sights+on+Toyota+pact&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The expansion of Honda Canada's production facilities north of Toronto will be a bigger investment than the EV battery facilities of Volkswagen and Stellantis combined. Honda is set to announce their plans to produce electric vehicles and EV batteries at their campus in Alliston and further production of battery parts with a partner.
The announcement, scheduled for Thursday morning, almost didn't happen as the Trudeau government tried to steer part of the investment toward Quebec. Industry Minister Francois-Philippe Champagne has been trying to direct investment in the EV battery supply chain toward Quebec.
In this instance, Premier Doug Ford pushed hard for the full investment to be in Ontario, and he won. Honda, through its partnership with Asahi Kasei - a Japanese firm that specializes in chemicals - will establish parts production in eastern and southwestern Ontario with the assembly happening at Honda's plant in Alliston.
The facility, which has two assembly plants that date to 1986, produces Honda's CRV and Civic models.
One government source, speaking on background, said the total investment by Honda in their facilities will top $13 billion.
The announcement comes as the EV sector is hitting a bumpy patch and the Trudeau and Ford governments are under criticism for being too generous with the VW and Stellantis deals. This deal with Honda will follow a much different structure in terms of how the federal and provincial governments will support it.
With both VW and Stellantis, the Trudeau government offered production tax credits that mirrored what the Biden administration was offering through the U.S. Inflation Reduction Act.
Under those terms, the companies weren't given any funds up front but were offered tax breaks if employment and production quotas were met.
Due to timelines, the competition for critical mineral inputs and other factors, it's doubtful that either company will ever see the full tax breaks, reportedly valued at $13.2 billion for VW and $15 billion for Stellantis.
Canada's tax breaks in those deals only exist if the U.S. legislation remains in place and, with an American election this fall, the unpopular measure could be killed stateside. Honda's deal is being described as less generous but with more stability than those agreed to in the Stellantis and VW deals.
The feds are offering two different tax credits to secure the Honda investment.
The clean technology investment tax credit offers a refundable credit equal to 30% of the cost of investments in new machinery and equipment for EV production. The electric vehicle supply chain investment tax credit offers a further 10% for companies that invest in all three areas the government is looking to land: EV assembly, EV battery production and cathode development.
The Ford government's contribution to securing the deal, which it took the lead on months ago, is focused on supporting Honda's capital investment in its new plants in Alliston. That generally means assisting with the infrastructure needed to get the plant running.
The Alliston plant employs 4,200 people, according to Honda. This expansion for both vehicle assembly and battery production will add well over 1,000 additional jobs while securing the existing employment and manufacturing output.
Watching this deal from the wings will be Toyota Canada.
It found out, via the media last fall, that the Trudeau government was rejecting its proposal for an EV battery facility as too expensive. It was an odd moment for a government that has courted the industry and asked for proposals.
While the feds walked away, the Ford government in Ontario has tried to keep Toyota at the table.
Toyota opened its first Canadian assembly plant in 1988 and now operates three plants - two in Cambridge and one in Woodstock - employing more than 8,000 people. The company has been less aggressive in the EV market than others, saying it believes hybrids are needed at the moment with a more gradual move toward full electrification.
That said, it invested in the EV space with an $8-billion announcement in October for a facility in North Carolina and $1.3 billion this year in Kentucky.
With Honda now secure, landing Toyota becomes the next big task for the Ford government.
"It's coming. Don't worry," Premier Doug Ford said when asked by the Toronto Sun about Toyota.
With close to $40 billion in auto investments landed over the past three years, Ontario is on a streak. Don't count Toyota out. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Supply chains; Tax cuts; Tax credits; Decades; Electric vehicles; Factories

Business indexing term: Subject: Supply chains Tax cuts Tax credits Factories

Location: United States--US; Canada; Quebec Canada

Company / organization: Name: Stellantis NV; NAICS: 336110, 551112

Identifier / keyword: expansion; honda; production; facilities; north; toronto

Document 57 of 356

'Can't cancel us'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cant-cancel-us/docview/3044887238/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Can%27t+cancel+us%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The keffiyeh took centre stage at the Ontario legislature Tuesday as protesters unfurling the scarves were ejected from the public galleries and an Independent member draped one over her shoulders in open defiance of a Speaker's ban on the attire.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles had tried for a second time to get the Legislature to pass a unanimous consent motion to overturn the Speaker's ban on keffiyehs, which he says are being worn to make a political statement, contrary to the rules of the assembly.
But a few Ontario members of the governing Progressive Conservatives voted against Stiles'motion, and shortly after it failed a group of four people watching question period stood up, waved and put on keffiyehs, shouting "free, free Palestine" and "you can't cancel us."
Legislative security ejected them and around the same time Sarah Jama - who sits as an Independent after being kicked out of the NDP caucus last year amid accusations of anti-Semitism - put on a keffiyeh and wasn't asked to remove it for the remainder of the hour-long question period.
"This is the least I could do, is stand up in solidarity," Jama said outside the chamber. "It's completely ridiculous and theatrical that we're not allowed to wear this cultural cloth in this place."
Speaker Ted Arnott said since Jama sits at the opposite end of the chamber from him, he couldn't definitively see the pattern on her scarf.
Progressive Conservative backbencher Lisa MacLeod was one of the people who voted against the motion, calling it anti-Semitic because it came during the Jewish holiday of Passover.
- The Canadian Press, with Sun files

Subject: Antisemitism

Identifier / keyword: keffiyeh; centre; stage; ontario; legislature; protesters

Title: 'Can't cancel us'

Publication title: The Toronto Sun; Toronto

First page: A.3

Document 58 of 356

Going after premier, chief after jury's decision just ugly politics

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/going-after-premier-chief-jurys-decision-just/docview/3044887174/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Going+after+premier%2C+chief+after+jury%27s+decision+just+ugly+politics&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By the way some are trying to look for scapegoats, you would swear it was the police and politicians who were on trial instead of a man who was acquitted of all charges for running over and killing an undercover cop.
Too many critics are getting caught up in a blame game frenzy, and for the good of public safety, it has got to stop.
Umar Zameer, 31, had been charged with first-degree murder in the July 2, 2021 death of Det. Const. Jeffrey Northrup, 55, in the underground garage at Toronto City Hall. He walked from the courtroom a free man after a jury found him not guilty Sunday.
This was a result of the system doing its work and finding the appropriate outcome. Now others are pointing fingers at people who had nothing to do with any of what transpired that night.
It's just a political witch hunt. So it was nice to see Premier Doug Ford stand up to the pack trying to score political points.
For the safety of every police officer in the province who sees that no one was held responsible for one of them dying in the line of duty, he should continue to do so. He should fend off the mob's demands that he buckle and apologize for comments he made about the case before the trial.
Time will tell if Ford can withstand the pressure from the Mondaymorning quarterbacks who insist he bend a knee and provide a mea culpa for his comments about the decision to grant bail to the man who was accused of first-degree murder in the death of a Toronto police officer.
But so far, he has. Ford had posted to social media: "It's completely unacceptable that the person charged for this heinous crime is now out on bail. Our justice system needs to get its act together and start putting victims and their families ahead of criminals."
Fast-forward three years later, and the suspect he was referring to was acquitted. Now people are out for blood.
"It's a very sad situation that happened," Ford told reporters at Queen's Park Tuesday. "I respect the court's decision. My heart goes out to (Northrup's widow) Margaret and her family."
When pressed about his bail comments, the premier added: "You know at that time, I had limited information. The courts have decided, and the jury has decided, and you have to respect the justice system."
Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown took it a step further.
"Everyone is celebrating the not guilty verdict of first-degree murder, and they forget we have a dead father, husband and police officer," Brown told the Toronto Sun Tuesday night.
"Jeffrey was from Brampton. He ran our local lacrosse programs and Boy Scouts. He was a champion for Special Olympics. He was loved. This is the real tragedy that everyone forgets. He was killed in the line of duty."
Brown added, "Umar Zameer shouldn't have been charged with first-degree murder, but it doesn't change the fact he was responsible for Jeffrey Northup's death. The Crown failed him. They failed the Northrop family. It also doesn't change my belief that we need bail reform in Canada."
Saying "the Crown should never have proceeded with first-degree murder charges," Brown insisted the "Crown owes an apology to not only Zameer, but more importantly to the Northrop family."
Brown added: "As for my comments, former mayor (John) Tory and the premier's comments on bail reform," he is still of the belief "we need bail reform in Canada. My tweet at the time made reference to the case of Henderson Bellman. She was killed by someone released on bail for the fifth time. I continue to believe we need bail reform in Canada."
Brown and Ford are looking at the big picture. Same goes for Toronto Police Chief Myron Demkiw, who should not have to apologize, either. A police officer died in the line of duty and a person was charged with first-degree murder as per what the Criminal Code demands.
Demkiw bringing in outside eyes to review the case and how undercover policing is done going forward were the right moves.
While there are flaws in the system and changes could come as result, that same justice system also got things right thanks to the jury.
To come back and focus on leaders'comments three years ago to make political hay is unfair - especially since they are the ones who had to deal with the grieving family and shaken up police officers. After all, it's up to them to convince officers they'll be safe out there.
It's horrible what happened, but looking to blame others is nothing more than political opportunism. jwarmington@postmedia.com

Subject: Police; Acquittals & mistrials; Murders & murder attempts; Trials

Location: Canada

Document 59 of 356

Polls: Budget a big bust; Voters shrug off Trudeau government's money geyser shtick

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/polls-budget-big-bust-voters-shrug-off-trudeau/docview/3044887094/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Polls%3A+Budget+a+big+bust%3B+Voters+shrug+off+Trudeau+government%27s+money+geyser+shtick&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If the Trudeau government's latest budget was an attempt to boost Liberal fortunes, it was a bust - a big, expensive, divisive bust that will ruin our economy for years to come while getting Justin Trudeau none of the support he hoped for.
Those are the findings of the latest poll from Ipsos.
OK, Ipsos found most people didn't like the budget or didn't care and Trudeau isn't getting a boost in support. The rest is my commentary.
Just 10% of respondents who knew anything about the budget said it would help them personally compared with 37% who felt the budget would hurt them and 53% who were neutral.
Asked if the budget would make them more or less likely to vote Liberal, 8% said more likely, 34% said less likely and 58% said there was no change.
Ipsos, in a poll for Global News, asked Canadians to rate the budget with two thumbs up, two thumbs down or a shrug of the shoulders and the results aren't good for the Trudeau Liberals. Just 17% gave the budget two thumbs up, 40% gave it two thumbs down and 43% shrugged their shoulders.
Even among the younger voters this budget was meant to appeal to, the reaction was far more negative than positive.
This shows up in party support numbers as well. Ipsos is now showing a 19-point lead for Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives over Trudeau's Liberals. The Conservatives would take 43% of the vote nationally if an election were held today, the Liberals 24% and the NDP 19%.
Trudeau has been frantically courting Gen Z and millennials, his budget mentioning each of them multiple times, but it isn't working.
Among Gen Z, 38% would vote Conservative, 28% would vote for the NDP and the Liberals are third at 27%. Millennial voters are also not impressed with Trudeau with 40% saying they would vote Conservative, 24% Liberal and 23% NDP.
Regionally, Poilievre's Conservatives hold a significant lead in every part of the country except Quebec, where they are tied for second with the Liberals.
In Ontario, where the Liberals hold 75 seats, the Conservatives hold an 11-point lead, 41% to 30% support. The Liberals won all those Ontario seats taking just 39% of the vote in the last election to the Conservatives at 35%, meaning outside of Toronto, there wouldn't be very many Liberals in Ontario if these numbers hold.
The Ipsos numbers are hardly outliers in showing sustained support for the Conservatives and the lack of a bounce for the budget. Last week, Abacus Data released a poll showing very similar numbers. It was released before the budget, but after all the good news spending announcements had been made.
Among major polling firms, the smallest gap between the Liberals and Conservatives over the past six months was an Ipsos poll released in January showing just a nine-point lead. Beyond that, for months, the Conservatives have consistently shown a double-digit lead over the Liberals.
This budget was supposed to be Trudeau's attempt to claw his way back to relevance. The Liberal party, according to the Globe and Mail, has set a goal of narrowing the gap with the Conservatives to just five points by July, but results like this won't help.
Trudeau increased program spending by more than $30 billion compared with last year. He's promising to take care of every aspect of your life using tax dollars and it isn't resonating with voters.
Maybe, just maybe, the problem isn't a lack of spending or government announcements. Maybe the problem is Justin Trudeau. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Document 60 of 356

'Very Sensitive Topic'; Keffiyeh debate rages on as motion fails again

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/very-sensitive-topic-keffiyeh-debate-rages-on-as/docview/3044887090/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Very+Sensitive+Topic%27%3B+Keffiyeh+debate+rages+on+as+motion+fails+again&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Things must be going well in Ontario right now because the biggest issue being debated is whether you can wear a keffiyeh in the Legislature. Speaker Ted Arnott ruled last week that the keffiyeh, a checkered scarf, sometimes black and white and sometimes coloured, is a political symbol and therefore not allowed in the Legislature.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles put forward a motion for the second time in a week asking that the Legislature allow the kaffiyeh to be worn.
"I seek unanimous consent that this house acknowledge that the keffiyeh is a culturally significant clothing item to many in Ontario's Palestinian, Muslim and Arab communities, and should neither be considered an expression of a political message or an accessory likely to cause disorder and should therefore be permitted to be born in this house," Stiles said as she read her motion just before question period.
About half a dozen MPPs from the PC side of the aisle called out no and that was the end of the debate.
In a unanimous consent motion, any MPP, for any reason, can say no and that ends the debate.
Stiles isn't being truthful when she says the keffiyeh isn't a political symbol. It clearly is. She also knows political symbols are banned from the Legislature, something she acknowledged when speaking to reporters about her motion.
The people who've been organizing the protests that have taken over our streets for the past six months encourage people to wear their keffiyehs to the protest. Those at the protests, like one of the men on the loudspeaker in Ottawa this weekend, call the scarf a political statement.
"They banned the keffiyeh in the Parliament," the man said as fellow protesters called out "shame."
"They banned the keffiyeh, the symbol of resistance, the Palestinian symbol of resistance in the Parliament. They are afraid, but they cannot ban it in the streets of our country."
That's a pretty blunt statement that the keffiyeh is in fact a political symbol. So, too, is how it is used in posters for the protest movement supporting the Palestinian cause.
In fact, a group of protesters who interrupted and temporarily shut down the Legislature during question period while waving keffiyehs and yelling "Free Palestine" also made the point that it was a political garment at this point.
Right now, for MPPs to wear a daffodil lapel pin in support of the Canadian Cancer Society during their April campaign, special permission is needed, unanimous consent granted, because support for this cause is seen as political. Stiles, Premier Doug Ford and others who've supported allowing the keffiyeh into the Legislature have accused others of being divisive, but by pushing this instead of following long-standing rules, it's they who've caused unnecessary division.
Ford emerged from a long PC Caucus meeting with his MPPs and cabinet ministers shortly after 3 p.m. saying he wouldn't be punishing any of the MPPs who said no to allowing the keffiyeh.
"No, this is a very sensitive topic here and around the world," Ford said, adding that on sensitive topics he believes in free votes.
Ford likely didn't have many options on that front. His caucus is clearly divided on this issue. Those willing to speak about the meeting said it was tense, it was uncomfortable, and it wasn't clear the majority were on Ford's side on this issue.
While only a handful said no to Stiles'motion, there hasn't been a rush of PC MPPs rushing to call for allowing the keffiyeh.
Not only backbench MPPs but also those within his own cabinet have been pushing back at the premier. Several cabinet ministers have let it be known they think the premier went too far on this issue.
That doesn't mean this is the end of the matter, despite Ford seeming to want to move on. The NDP seems determined to bring this back to the Legislature again and again, at least until the Milton byelection on May 2.
Global politics, in the Ontario legislature, driven by a local byelection. Welcome to 2024.

Subject: Bans; Legislatures; Demonstrations & protests; Debates; Consent

Identifier / keyword: things; going; ontario; right; biggest; issue

Title: 'Very Sensitive Topic'; Keffiyeh debate rages on as motion fails again

column: Opinion

Document 61 of 356

New seat maps or not, Tories will win next vote

Author: Gunter, Lorne

Publication info: Gunter, Lorne. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-seat-maps-not-tories-will-win-next-vote/docview/3044887047/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+seat+maps+or+not%2C+Tories+will+win+next+vote&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=Gunter%2C+Lorne&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last week, there was a (smallish) chance we could have seen a federal election called.
That's because any election called from here on out will be fought using the new 343-seat map, instead of the old 338-seat map used for the past three general elections.
Why would adding five seats make a difference to when the election was called? Because three of the five new ridings are in Canada's fast-growing province, Alberta, which isn't exactly friendly turf for the Trudeau Liberals. And the other two - one each in B.C. and suburban Toronto - aren't looking good for the Grits, either.
Every riding counts in a Parliament where the margins between parties are ultra-thin.
So Alberta receiving three more seats (increasing from 34 to 37) could play a disproportionately important role in whether the Trudeau Liberals can retain even a minority government.
Ontario gets one new riding in the Greater Toronto Area.
Since this area votes predominantly Liberal, even in the suburbs, the addition of a new seat might blunt the advantage the Conservatives get in Alberta.
Or it might not. According to most polls in the last three months, the Liberals are behind the Conservatives by 12 to 15 points in the ring of suburbs around Toronto and Mississauga (the famous (905) where Grits currently hold nearly all of the seats.
Now every suburban Toronto riding has to be said to be at risk of going Conservative.
In the entire country, the only places that seem to be clinging tenaciously to Justin Trudeau and his party, are the cores of Montreal, Ottawa, Toronto and Vancouver.
Not the suburbs of those cities, just the cores, which together have nowhere near enough seats to re-elect the Grits.
British Columbia is the other province to receive an extra seat.
Liberals there are facing stiff challenges from New Democrats in Vancouver or Conservatives everywhere else.
Or at least they would be if the riding boundaries weren't being redrawn deliberately to give Liberals candidates the best possible chances of being elected or re-elected.
Areas that poll strongly Liberal are being kept together on the new map in Vancouver proper.
Suburban Vancouver, which typically splits three ways between the three main parties, gets the new B.C. seat (and probably a new Conservative MP given the way the polls stand), while any ridings that have Vancouver in their names have been gerrymandered to keep as many as possible Liberal red.
Outside of Vancouver proper, the Libs tend to be running a distant third behind the Conservatives and NDP. There would appear to be little chance of a single Liberal winning in B.C.'s vast interior, for instance.
In Alberta, currently 30 of 34 seats are Conservative. Even in Edmonton (which has 20 out of 20 provincial MLAs who are New Democrats) the majority of seats are Conservative.
There is a solid chance that after the next election, 36 of 37 will be painted blue.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh is in Edmonton this week door-knocking in core Edmonton ridings in the belief that if all of the city's provincial constituencies vote New Democrat, than more of its eight federal ridings should be competitive for his party, too.
It's a nice, but foolish, theory.
Nowhere in the country are the Liberals hated more than in Alberta, were some ridings vote nearly 80% Conservative. And with Singh's party seen by Alberta voters as lackies to the Liberals who are keeping a very unpopular government in power, it's hard to see how the New Dems grow their seat total.
Indeed, I suspect they will lose one of their two current seats, leaving Heather McPherson in Edmonton-Strathcona as the only non-Conservative from Alberta.
According to the polling analysis site, 338Canada.com (which hasn't changed its name yet), all of these machinations mean the Conservatives should win 207 of 343 seats, and perhaps as many as 231. lgunter@postmedia.com

Subject: Conservatism; Elections

Identifier / keyword: chance; federal; election; called; fought; using

Title: New seat maps or not, Tories will win next vote

WARMINGTON: Going after Premier, police chief after Zameer verdict just ugly politics

Author: Warmington, Joe

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/warmington-going-after-premier-police-chief/docview/3044671253/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: By the way some are trying to look for scapegoats, you would swear it was the police and politicians who were on trial instead of a man who was acquitted of all charges for running over and killing an undercover cop.
Too many critics are getting caught up in a blame game frenzy, and for the good of public safety, it has got to stop.
Umar Zameer, 31, had been charged with first-degree murder in the July 2, 2021 death of Det.-Const. Jeffrey Northrup, 55, in the underground garage at Toronto City Hall. He walked away from the courtroom a free man after a jury found him not guilty on Sunday.
Premier Doug Ford is asked about the Zameer verdict and whether he should have made statements when he was granted bail.
- Brian Lilley (@brianlilley) April 23, 2024

It's just a political witch-hunt.

But so far he has.
Ford had posted to social media: "It's completely unacceptable that the person charged for this heinous crime is now out on bail. Our justice system needs to get its act together and start putting victims and their families ahead of criminals."
Fast-forward three years later, and the suspect he was referring to was acquitted. Now people are out for blood.
"It's a very sad situation that happened," Ford told reporters at Queen's Park Tuesday. "I respect the court's decision. My heart goes out to (Northrup's widow) Margaret and her family."
When pressed about his bail comments, the premier added, "you know at that time, I had limited information. The courts have decided, and the jury has decided, and you have to respect the justice system."
Flashback to September 2021, when Umar Zameer was granted bail, and Premier Doug Ford weighed in on Twitter.
- Mike Crawley (@CBCQueensPark) April 22, 2024
Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown took it a step further.
"Everyone is celebrating the not guilty verdict of first-degree murder, and they forget we have a dead father, husband and police officer," Brown told The Toronto Sun Tuesday night. "Jeffrey was from Brampton. He ran our local lacrosse programs and boy scouts. He was a champion for Special Olympics. He was loved. This is the real tragedy that everyone forgets. He was killed in the line of duty."
Brown added, "Umar Zameer shouldn't have been charged with first-degree murder, but it doesn't change the fact he was responsible for Jeffrey Northup's death. The Crown failed him. They failed the Northrop family. It also doesn't change my belief that we need bail reform in Canada."
Umar Zameer is pictured leaving the Toronto courthouse - at 361 University Ave. - on April 2, 2024.
Saying "the Crown should never have proceeded with first-degree murder charges," Brown insisted the "Crown owes an apology to not only Zameer, but more importantly to the Northrop family."
Brown added "as for my comments, former mayor (John) Tory and the premier's comments on bail reform" he is still of the belief "we need bail reform in Canada. My tweet at the time made reference to the case of Henderson Bellman. She was killed by someone released on bail for the fifth time. I continue to believe we need bail reform in Canada. The justice system is too much of a revolving door."
Brown and Ford are looking at the big picture. Same goes for Toronto Police Chief Myron Demkiw, who should not have to apologize, either. A police officer died in the line of duty and a person was charged with first-degree murder as per what the Criminal Code demands.
This exhibit from the Umar Zameer trial shows Toronto Police Const. Jeffrey Northrup outside City Hall. (Ontario Superior Court of Justice)
Demkiw bringing in outside eyes to review the case and how undercover policing is done going forward were the right moves.
While there are flaws in the system and changes could come as result, that same justice system also got things right thanks to a jury which made its ruling.
To come back and focus on leaders' comments three years ago to make political hay is unfair - especially since they are the ones who had to deal with the grieving family and shaken up police officers. After all, it's up to them to convince officers they'll be safe out there.
It's already horrible what happened. But looking to blame others is nothing more than political opportunism.

LILLEY: Keffiyeh debate rages on at Queen's Park even as motion fails

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-keffiyeh-debate-rages-on-at-queens-park/docview/3044652042/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Keffiyeh+debate+rages+on+at+Queen%27s+Park+even+as+motion+fails&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Things must be going well in Ontario right now because the biggest issue being debated is whether you can wear a keffiyeh in the legislature. Speaker Ted Arnott ruled last week that the keffiyeh, a checkered scarf, sometimes black and white and sometimes coloured, is a political symbol and therefore not allowed in the legislature.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles put forward a motion for the second time in a week asking that the legislature allow the keffiyeh to be worn.
"I seek unanimous consent that this house acknowledge that the keffiyeh is a culturally significant clothing item to many in Ontario's Palestinian, Muslim and Arab communities, and should neither be considered an expression of a political message or an accessory likely to cause disorder and should therefore be permitted to be born in this house," Stiles said as she read her motion just prior to Question Period.
About half a dozen MPPs from the PC side of the aisle called out no and that was the end of the debate. In a unanimous consent motion, any MPP, for any reason can say no and that ends the debate.
Stiles isn't being truthful when she says that the keffiyeh isn't a political symbol. It clearly is. She also knows that political symbols are banned from the legislature, something she acknowledged when speaking to reporters about her motion.
The people who have been organizing the protests that have taken over our streets for the last six months encourage people to wear their keffiyehs to the protest. Those at the protests, like one of the men on the loudspeaker in Ottawa this weekend, call the scarf a political statement.
"They banned the keffiyeh in the Parliament," the man said as fellow protesters called out "shame."
"They banned the keffiyeh, the symbol of resistance, the Palestinian symbol of resistance in the Parliament. They are afraid but they cannot ban in in the streets of our country."
That's a pretty blunt statement that the keffiyeh is in fact a political symbol. So, too, is how it is used in posters and artwork for the protest movement to support the Palestinian cause.
In fact, a group of protesters who interrupted and temporarily shut down the legislature during Question Period while waving keffiyehs and yelling "Free Palestine" also made the point that it was a political garment at this point.
Right now, for MPPs to wear a daffodil lapel pin support of the Canadian Cancer Society during their April campaign, special permission is needed, unanimous consent granted, because support for this cause is seen as political. Stiles, Premier Doug Ford and others who have supported allowing the keffiyeh into the legislature have accused others of being divisive but by pushing this, instead of following long standing rules, it's they who have caused unnecessary division.
LILLEY: Ford jumps on contentious keffiyeh issue with both feet
LILLEY: Rules on clothing in legislatures are in place to stop division
Ford emerged from a long PC Caucus meeting with his MPPs and cabinet ministers shortly after 3 p.m. saying he wouldn't be punishing any of the MPPs who said no to allowing the keffiyeh.
"No, this is a very sensitive topic here and around the world," Ford said, adding that on sensitive topics he believes in free votes.
Ford likely didn't have many options on that front, his caucus is clearly divided on this issue. Those willing to speak about the meeting said it was tense, it was uncomfortable, and it wasn't clear that the majority were on Ford's side on this issue.
While only a handful said no to Stiles' motion, there hasn't been a rush of PC MPPs rushing to call for allowing the keffiyeh.
The premier has been facing pushback not only from backbench MPPs but also from within his own cabinet. Several cabinet ministers have let it be known they think the premier went too far on this issue.
That doesn't mean this is the end of the matter, despite Ford seeming to want to move on. The NDP seems determined to bring this back to the legislature again and again, at least until the Milton by-election on May 2.
Global politics, in the Ontario legislature, driven by a local byelection.
Welcome to 2024.

GUNTER: New seat maps or not, Conservatives will win next federal election

Author: Shantz, Ian

Publication info: Shantz, Ian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/gunter-new-seat-maps-not-conservatives-will-win/docview/3044628504/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=GUNTER%3A+New+seat+maps+or+not%2C+Conservatives+will+win+next+federal+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Shantz%2C+Ian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last week, there was a (smallish) chance we could have seen a federal election called. That's because any election called from here on out will be fought using the new 343-seat map, instead of the old 338-seat map used for the past three general elections.
Why would adding five seats make a difference to when the election was called? Because three of the five new ridings are in Canada's fast-growing province, Alberta, which isn't exactly friendly turf for the Trudeau Liberals. And the other two - one each in B.C. and suburban Toronto - aren't looking good for the Grits, either.
Every riding counts in a Parliament where the margins between parties are ultra-thin. So Alberta receiving three more seats (increasing from 34 to 37) could play a disproportionately important role in whether the Trudeau Liberals can retain even a minority government.
Ontario gets one new riding in the Greater Toronto Area. Since this area votes predominantly Liberal, even in the suburbs, the addition of a new seat might blunt the advantage the Conservatives get in Alberta.
Or it might not. According to most polls in the last three months, the Liberals are behind the Conservatives by 12 to 15 points in the ring of suburbs around Toronto and Mississauga (the famous (905) where Libs currently hold nearly all of the seats.
Now every suburban Toronto riding has to be said to be at risk of going Conservative.
In the entire country, the only places that seem to be clinging tenaciously to Justin Trudeau and his party, are the cores of Montreal, Ottawa, Toronto and Vancouver. Not the suburbs of those cities, just the cores, which together have nowhere near enough seats to re-elect the Grits.
British Columbia is the other province to receive an extra seat. Liberals there are facing stiff challenges from New Democrats in Vancouver or Conservatives everywhere else. Or at least they would be if the riding boundaries weren't being redrawn deliberately to give Liberals candidates the best possible chances of being elected or re-elected. Areas that poll strongly Liberal are being kept together on the new map in Vancouver proper.
Suburban Vancouver, which typically splits three ways between the three main parties, gets the new B.C. seat (and probably a new Conservative MP given the way the polls stand), while any ridings that have Vancouver in their names have been gerrymandered to keep as many as possible Liberal red.
Outside of Vancouver proper, the Libs tend to be running a distant third behind the Conservatives and NDP. There would appear to be little chance of a single Liberal winning in B.C.'s vast interior, for instance.
GUNTER: The never-ending incompetence of the Trudeau Liberals
GUNTER: Voters likely to see through budget's attempts to win back affections
In Alberta, currently 30 of 34 seats are Conservative. Even in Edmonton (which has 20 out of 20 provincial MLAs who are New Democrats) the majority of seats are Conservative.
There is a solid chance that after the next election, 36 of 37 will be painted blue.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh is in Edmonton this week door-knocking in core Edmonton ridings in the belief that if all of the city's provincial constituencies vote New Democrat, than more of its eight federal ridings should be competitive for his party, too.
It's a nice, but foolish, theory. Nowhere in the country are the Liberals hated more than in Alberta, were some ridings vote nearly 80% Conservative. And with Singh's party seen by Alberta voters as lackies to the Liberals who are keeping a very unpopular government in power, it's hard to see how the New Dems grow their seat total.
Indeed, I suspect they will lose one of their two current seats, leaving Heather McPherson in Edmonton-Strathcona as the only non-Conservative from Alberta.
According to the polling analysis site, 338Canada.com (which hasn't changed its name yet), all of these machinations mean the Conservatives should win 207 of 343 seats, and perhaps as many as 231.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO

HUNTER: America's most spoiled kids say YAY! for Hamas

Full text: And when the revolution finally comes, we'll all have servants.
Among the hordes cheering on Hamas from university campuses and the streets of America are among the most privileged kids in the world. There's the pricey North Face coats, keffiyeh, of course, and a slew of other luxe goods.
Of the 114 anti-Israel protesters slammed behind bars in the Big Apple following protests at Columbia University, many emerged from behind the gilded gates of the country's richest families.
Kids of executives were there along with the politically well-connected. Here are some of the brat pack who grew up in multimillion-dollar palaces, attended private schools and have spent their whole lives being spoiled rotten.
Many of those arrested attended elite Barnard College. Cost? $90,000 per annum.
No doubt, when storm clouds blotted out their sunny cosplay work in the form of the NYPD, they were quickly on the phone with mommy and daddy.
JUST LIKE MOM! Isra Hirsi shares same fanatical views as her mom. INSTAGRAM
ISRA HIRSI
411: Daughter of "Squad" member of Minnesota congresswoman Ilhan Omar who is notoriously anti-Israel. On Monday, the New York Post reported that Hirsi now claims she's homeless and can't get food after she was booted from her - GASP! - $90,000 Barnard College dorm. She's so uptight and nauseatingly self-righteous that even her friends call her the PC Police.
She whined to Teen Vogue (where else?): "I was a little bit frantic, like, where am I going to sleep? Where am I gonna go?"
Mom said: "I'm enormously proud."

In 2020, Seward crossed a double yellow line in her Toyota Tacoma pickup truck near trendy Montpelier, Vermont, killing an elderly couple. But wait! She pleaded no contest to a traffic ticket called "driving on roadways laned for traffic." $220 fine. Mom paid it.
"The only reason she wasn't charged with murder is because she has a rich daddy. She should be behind bars," the couple's niece Eve Taylor told the Post. "She has no remorse, she received no punishment. She's just prancing around Columbia with her Ivy League privilege. After basically getting away with murder, she's now promoting murder, with no understanding of what she's promoting. It's outrageous they haven't thrown her off campus."
Home is a $2.2-million mansion near Atlanta. She was busted last Thursday at an anti-Israel encampment at Columbia.

LILLEY: Poll shows budget a bust for Trudeau government as voters shrug

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-poll-shows-budget-bust-trudeau-government/docview/3044355193/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Poll+shows+budget+a+bust+for+Trudeau+government+as+voters+shrug&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If the Trudeau government's latest budget was an attempt to boost Liberal fortunes, then it was a bust.
A big, expensive, divisive bust that will ruin our economy for years to come while getting Justin Trudeau none of the support he had hoped for.
Those are the findings of the latest poll from Ipsos.
OK, Ipsos found most people didn't like the budget or didn't care and that Trudeau isn't getting a boost in support, the rest is my commentary.
Just 10% of respondents who knew anything about the budget said it would help them personally compared to 37% who felt the budget would hurt them and 53% who were neutral. Asked if the budget would make them more or less likely to vote Liberal, 8% said more likely, 34% said less likely and 58% said there was no change.
Ipsos asked Canadians to rate the budget with two thumbs up, two thumbs down or a shrug of the shoulders and the results aren't good for the Trudeau Liberals. Just 17% gave the budget two thumbs up, 40% gave it two thumbs down and 43% shrugged their shoulders.
Even among the younger voters this budget was meant to appeal to, Gen Z and Millennials, the reaction was far more negative than positive.
This shows up in party support numbers as well - Ipsos is now showing a 19-point lead for Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives over Trudeau's Liberals. The Conservatives would take 43% of the vote nationally if an election were held today, the Liberals 24% and the NDP 19%.
Trudeau has been frantically courting Gen Z and Millennials, his budget mentioning each of them multiple times, but it isn't working.
Among Gen Z, 38% would vote Conservative, 28% would vote for the NDP and the Liberals are in third at 27%. Millennial voters are also not impressed with Trudeau with 40% saying they would vote Conservative, 24% Liberal and 23% NDP.
EDITORIAL: Is Trudeau confused by his climate plan?
EDITORIAL: One-way ticket to bankrupt health care
LILLEY: Trudeau's budget nothing more than Trump-style populist campaign
Regionally, Poilievre's Conservatives hold a significant lead in every part of the country except Quebec, where they are tied for second with the Liberals.
In Ontario, where the Liberals hold 75 seats, the Conservatives hold an 11-point lead, 41% to 30% support. The Liberals won all those Ontario seats taking just 39% of the vote in the last election to the Conservatives at 35%, meaning, outside of Toronto, there wouldn't be very many Liberals in Ontario if these numbers hold.
The Ipsos numbers are hardly outliers in showing sustained support for the Conservatives and the lack of a bounce for the budget. Last week, Abacus Data released a poll showing very similar numbers for party support. It was released before the budget, but after all the good-news spending announcements had been made.
Among major polling firms, the smallest gap between the Liberals and Conservatives over the past six months was an Ipsos poll released in January showing just a nine-point lead. Beyond that, for months, the Conservatives have consistently shown a double-digit lead over the Liberals.
This budget was supposed to be Trudeau's attempt to claw his way back to relevancy. The Liberal Party, according to the Globe and Mail, has set a goal of narrowing the gap with the Conservatives to just 5 points by July but results like this won't help.
Trudeau increased program spending by more than $30 billion compared to last year. He's promising to take care of every aspect of your life using tax dollars and it isn't resonating with voters.
Maybe, just maybe, the problem isn't a lack of spending or government announcements. Maybe the problem is Justin Trudeau.
blilley@postmedia.com

Document 67 of 356

Pandering to hate; In Milton's byelection, Gaza is dominating, not local issues

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pandering-hate-miltons-byelection-gaza-is/docview/3044075747/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pandering+to+hate%3B+In+Milton%27s+byelection%2C+Gaza+is+dominating%2C+not+local+issues&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The dominant issue in the Milton byelection doesn't seem to be expanded GO train service or building Hwy. 413 - it's Gaza. That's based on what is being said and what is not being said by candidates and leaders.
This weekend, Liberal candidate Galen Naidoo Harris posted and quickly deleted a video saying that it's wrong to stand with Israel and that he will represent those concerned about Gaza.
"For those folks that are advocating for Gaza, know that I have your back, and I'll be your voice at Queen's Park," Naidoo Harris said.
While recording the video, Naidoo Harris was wearing a kaffiyeh, a scarf long associated with Palestinian political struggles. He's been campaigning on this issue as well, falsely blaming Ontario Premier Doug Ford for banning the kaffiyeh from the Ontario legislature and making it a partisan issue.
The truth is that Ted Arnott, the Speaker of the Ontario legislative assembly, ruled last week that the kaffiyeh, in today's context, is a political statement and following long-standing rules for the legislature, it cannot be worn. Ford opposed the move and joined NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie in calling for Arnott to reverse course.
It's not the government that sets the rules for the legislature, it's the Speaker, and Arnott has ruled the kaffiyeh is a political symbol and statement.
As if to hammer home that point, Naidoo Harris spent the weekend campaigning in one. The scarf has no cultural or religious connection for him. He's making a political statement and trying to make it partisan, which is why Arnott said it's not appropriate in the legislature.
The rules have long been in place to ensure people aren't wearing anything that can inflame passions or tensions. It's not about dividing people, as so many politicians claimed last week, it's about calming those divisions.
That didn't stop Ford from jumping on the issue, though, and denouncing the Speaker's ruling, not something a premier normally does.
Ford didn't do this for reasons of principle, though. He did it because he's worried about the result in the Milton byelection.
He also did this after telling his caucus not to say or post anything to social media about the Middle East until after the byelection.
Milton has a significant Muslim population, and all the parties, candidates and leaders are courting, even pandering to that voting bloc. It's why you haven't, as of this writing, seen a single Ontario political party leader denounce the vile chants in favour of Hamas this weekend in Ottawa. "Oct. 7 is proof that we are almost free," one of the protest leaders called out as the march in Ottawa went by the Parliament buildings. He followed up with "Long live Oct. 7, long live the resistance," and with that, the crowd of thousands cheered.
It's despicable that anyone would cheer the barbaric attacks of Oct. 7. The attacks were carried out by a group that has been listed as a banned terrorist entity in Canada for more than 20 years.
Crickets from Ontario's political leaders. They are all cowards, too afraid to say anything lest they lose the votes of radicals in Milton who support terrorism, which I doubt most Muslim Canadians do. They all posted on social media about Passover, but they haven't denounced the cheering of a terrorist attack that killed more than 1,200 Jews six months ago.
Now, compare that to a Nazi flag and Confederate flag showing up however briefly at the Freedom Convoy in Ottawa, on that same stretch of road in 2022. The political leaders couldn't issue their condemnations fast enough, and opposition parties called on Ford to condemn the flags and intolerance even harder.
Here, with open cheers of support for terrorism, not only is Ford silent - which is shameful - but so are Stiles and Crombie.
If you can't condemn people cheering on terrorism and murder on our own streets, then you aren't fit for public office.
The people of Ontario deserve better. Every single leader is failing us at the moment. Do better, or move aside for someone with moral clarity and a backbone. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Terrorism; Legislatures; Political leadership; Social networks; Flags

Document 68 of 356

Is Trudeau confused by his climate plan?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-trudeau-confused-his-climate-plan/docview/3044074945/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Trudeau+confused+by+his+climate+plan%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: News that the federal and Ontario governments will announce a deal this week to subsidize yet another electric vehicle battery plant in the province raises an obvious question.
That is, does Prime Minister Justin Trudeau understand the contents of his own climate change plan? This time, according to a report by The Canadian Press based on government sources, "Honda Canada is set to build an electric vehicle battery plant near its auto manufacturing facility in Alliston, Ont., where it also plans to produce fully electric vehicles.
"The Honda facility will be the third electric vehicle battery plant in Ontario, following in the footsteps of Volkswagen and a Stellantis LG plant in Windsor, and while those two deals involved billions of dollars in production subsidies as a way of competing with the United States'Inflation Reduction Act subsidies, Honda's is expected to involve capital commitments and tax credits."
But just last month, Trudeau said he established his national carbon tax because "I prefer a cleaner ... market-based solution" to addressing climate change, as opposed to "the heavy hand of regulations" and "incentives ... subsidies and rewards."
To be blunt, what was he talking about? Trudeau's own environment minister, Steven Guilbeault, boasted after the Liberals unveiled their 2023 budget last year, that the government's $200-billion climate change plan being financed by Canadian taxpayers includes, in addition to Trudeau's carbon tax, more than 100 government programs involving both, "the heavy hand of regulations" and "incentives ... subsidies and rewards?" The subsidies are being announced all the time. Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland said last year that part of the reason for that - and the reason the federal government is handing out more subsidies for so-called green technology than it originally anticipated - is the need to compete with the U.S. because of the massive subsidies President Joe Biden introduced in his Inflation Reduction Act.
In terms of government regulations, Trudeau's plan includes, just for starters, "clean fuel" and "clean electricity" regulations, a "Regulatory Framework for an Oil and Gas Sector Greenhouse Gas Emissions Cap" and "regulated targets for zero-emission vehicles."
Contrary to Trudeau's claim he introduced his carbon tax as an alternative to public subsidies and government regulations, he in fact loaded subsidies and regulations on top of his carbon tax.

Subject: Automobiles; Inflation Reduction Act 2022-US; Emission standards; Emissions; Carbon; Subsidies; Climate change; Electric vehicles; Competition; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Inflation Reduction Act 2022-US Subsidies Environmental tax

Location: United States--US

Identifier / keyword: federal; ontario; governments; announce; subsidize; another

Document 69 of 356

LILLEY: In Milton's byelection Gaza is dominating, not local issues

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-miltons-byelection-gaza-is-dominating-not/docview/3043939543/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+In+Milton%27s+byelection+Gaza+is+dominating%2C+not+local+issues&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The dominant issue in the Milton byelection doesn't seem to be expanded Go-Train service or building Hwy. 13 - it's Gaza. That's based on what is being said and what is not being said by candidates and leaders.
This weekend, Liberal candidate Galen Naidoo Harris posted and quickly deleted a video saying that it's wrong to stand with Israel and that he will be the voice for those concerned about Gaza at Queen's Park.
"For those folks that are advocating for Gaza, know that I have your back, and I'll be your voice at Queen's Park," Naidoo Harris said.
While recording the video, Naidoo Harris was wearing a keffiyeh, a scarf long associated with Palestinian political struggles. He's been campaigning on this issue as well, falsely blaming Ontario Premier Doug Ford for banning the keffiyeh from the Ontario legislature and making it a partisan issue.
The truth is that Ted Arnott, the speaker of the Ontario Legislative Assembly, ruled last week that the keffiyeh, in today's context, is a political statement and following longstanding rules for the legislature, it cannot be worn. Ford opposed the move and joined NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie in calling for Arnott to reverse course.
It's not the government that sets the rules for the legislature, it's the speaker, and Arnott has ruled that the keffiyeh is a political symbol and statement.
Recommended video
As if to hammer home that point, Naidoo Harris spent the weekend campaigning in one. The scarf has no cultural or religious connection for him, he's making a political statement, and trying to make it partisan, which is why Arnott said it's not appropriate in the legislature.
The rules have long been in place to ensure that people aren't wearing anything that can enflame passions or tensions. It's not about dividing people as so many politicians claimed last week, it's about calming those divisions.
The candidate @BonnieCrombie picked to carry the @OntLiberal banner in Milton posted and then deleted a video. In it @GalenNHarris says he wants to be the voice at Queen's Park for those who care about Gaza.
The whole Milton by-election is apparently about Gaza. All parties.
- Brian Lilley (@brianlilley) April 22, 2024
That didn't stop Ford from jumping on the issue, though, and denouncing the speaker's ruling, not something a premier normally does. Ford didn't do this for reasons of principle, though, he did it because he's worried about the result in the Milton byelection.
He also did this after telling his caucus not to say or post anything to social media about the Middle East until after the byelection.
Milton has a significant Muslim population, and all the parties, candidates and leaders are courting, even pandering to that voting bloc. It's why you haven't, as of this writing, seen a single Ontario political party leader denounce the vile chants in favour of Hamas this weekend in Ottawa.
Chrystia Freeland is part of a government which has put forward a bill, that if passed, could put you under house arrest for things you might say in the future.
Asked about chants and cheers for "Long live October 7" and Freeland says she can't comment.
- Brian Lilley (@brianlilley) April 22, 2024
"Oct. 7 is proof that we are almost free," one of the protest leaders called out as the march in Ottawa went by the Parliament buildings. He followed up with "Long live Oct. 7, long live the resistance," and with that, the crowd of thousands cheered.
It's despicable that anyone would cheer the barbaric attacks of Oct. 7. The attacks were carried out by a group that has been listed as a banned terrorist entity in Canada for more than 20 years.
Crickets from Ontario's political leaders, they are all cowards, too afraid to say anything lest they lose the votes of radicals in Milton who support terrorism, which I doubt most Muslim Canadians do. They all posted on social media about Passover, but they haven't denounced the cheering of a terrorist attack that killed more than 1,200 Jews six months ago.
Now, compare that to a Nazi flag and Confederate flag showing up however briefly at the Freedom Convoy in Ottawa, on that same stretch of road in 2022. The political leaders couldn't issue their condemnations fast enough, and opposition parties called on Ford to condemn the flags and intolerance even harder.
Here, with open cheers of support for terrorism, not only is Ford silent - which is shameful - so are Stiles and Crombie.
If you can't condemn people cheering on terrorism and murder on our own streets, then you aren't fit for public office.
The people of Ontario deserve better; every single leader is failing us at the moment. Do better, or move aside for someone with moral clarity and a backbone.
LILLEY: They are openly cheering Hamas and their terrorist attacks now
LILLEY: Ford jumps on contentious keffiyeh issue with both feet

Subject: Terrorism; Legislatures; Political leadership; Social networks; Elections; Flags

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks

Location: Gaza Strip

EDITORIAL: Is Trudeau confused by his climate plan?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/editorial-is-trudeau-confused-his-climate-plan/docview/3043937279/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=EDITORIAL%3A+Is+Trudeau+confused+by+his+climate+plan%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/
