Document 71 of 356

Renaming Highway 400 after Gordon Lightfoot involves a long 'process'

Author: Stevenson, Jane

Publication info: Stevenson, Jane. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/renaming-highway-400-after-gordon-lightfoot/docview/3043256293/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Renaming+Highway+400+after+Gordon+Lightfoot+involves+a+long+%27process%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Stevenson%2C+Jane&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Carefree Highway, you're facing a long road ahead.
More specifically, thechange.org petition to change the name of Hwy. 400 to the Gordon Lightfoot Memorial Highway, currently has more than 19,000 signatures. But those in favour of renaming the highway - which heads from Toronto past Gordon Lighfoot's birthplace in Orillia - face a lengthy process.
The petition was started on March 4, 2024 by Douglas Walkinshaw, who, while a few years younger than the musical icon, went to high school with Lightfoot at the now torn-down Orillia District Collegiate and Vocational Institute.
Lightfoot died at the age of 84 last May 1 of natural causes.
"My thinking is the 400 leads up to Orillia, leads up to Parry Sound, and Georgian Bay, too," said Walkinshaw, 82, an engineer who now lives in Ottawa.
"He lived all his life in Toronto, so people are thinking that from Toronto and Hamilton; they have to head up 400 to get to Orillia, to get to Georgian Bay. He loved Georgian Bay, by the way, and wrote many songs there. So I think 400 is the appropriate one."
Recommended video
Walkinshaw has been given some guidance from Ontario's transportation ministry "on what's involved in the highway dedication process" but when asked, government officials didn't provide a statement on what the province thinks of the idea or how likely it is to happen.
"I took it to the ministry right after he died," said Walkinshaw. "And they said, 'Well, we have a process. You have to go to every urban centre along the highway and get them to support it, and you have to get any First Nations groups along the highway to support it. And then if you get them all supporting it, and we agree to it, then you'll have to find any signage etc.' And I thought, 'Well, that would be perfect for naming a back lane after your great uncle,' where you're not talking about all of these municipalities along the way. Ontario needs a better process. It's a problem."
Lightfoot's legacy - he penned over 425 songs - is already being remembered musically.
The Lightfoot Band with their newest member, lead singer and rhythm guitarist Andy Mauck, has been touring since January and will be the house band, along with Blue Rodeo, at a memorial concert on May 23 at a Massey Hall called Celebrating Gordon Lightfoot.
So far Burton Cummings, Sylvia Tyson, Tom Cochrane, City and Colour, Kathleen Edwards, and Serena Ryder, are among those confirmed to perform.
Walkinshaw said he plans on submitting a request for action on Monday to Premier Doug Ford.
"I'm just doing it because I think it's good for Ontario and Canada and the world to keep his music alive," he said.

Subject: Musical performances

Location: Georgian Bay

People: Lightfoot, Gordon

Document 72 of 356

'Halal mortgage'gets budget nod; Move slammed on social media as 'pandering'

Author: Daniell, Mark

Publication info: Daniell, Mark. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/halal-mortgagegets-budget-nod-move-slammed-on/docview/3042710104/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Halal+mortgage%27gets+budget+nod%3B+Move+slammed+on+social+media+as+%27pandering%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Daniell%2C+Mark&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the federal government unveiled its latest budget, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals revealed their plans to explore new ways to expand home ownership, including the adoption of "halal mortgages."
According to this week's federal budget, the Liberal government revealed that it "is exploring new measures to expand access to alternative financing products, like halal mortgages."
The charging of interest goes against the Islamic faith and financial gains made through interest are viewed as unjust in the eyes of Sharia law.
A "halal mortgage" still lets lenders earn a profit with a different set of terms that can include charging a higher overall price for a person's home - essentially including future growth gains in the selling price - engaging in a rentto-own model or establishing a partnership between a lender and home owner that slowly reverts to the latter over time as payments are made.
Mohamad Sawwaf, co-founder and CEO of Manzil, an Islamic financial institution based in Toronto, says that "halal mortgages" are the same as traditional ones.
"The process and documentation is what makes the transaction halal," he told CTV News in 2022.
"People that get Islamic mortgages still pay a comparable amount that you would pay if you got a conventional mortgage. It's just that the structure of these mortgages are different," Walid Hejazi, an associate professor of economic analysis and policy at the University of Toronto's Rotman School of Management, told the CBC.
The Liberals said changes could see adjustments "in the tax treatment of these products or anew regulatory sandbox for financial service providers."
Muslims represent almost 5% of the Canadian population, more than doubling in size since 2001.
In 2022, a group urging Canadian-Muslims to vote in the Ontario election predicted they would affect the outcome in almost 50 provincial ridings.
The feds began consulting with month "to understand how policies can better support the needs of all Canadians seeking to become homeowners."
An update on the scheme is expected later this year in the 2024 fall economic statement.
As the news made headlines in recent days, Canadians reacted to the plan with a mixture of confusion and anger on social media.
Many called the proposition a "desperate ploy" by the Liberals to secure more votes and calls for religion to be kept out of politics.
"Bigotry aside, these are just fixed rate mortgages with mental gymnastics to fool God," one person wrote on Reddit.
"I'm a Muslim myself but I don't understand why Canadians are entertaining this aspect of Sharia law. The Muslims who immigrated here did so with full knowledge of how the western finance works," another added. "Nothing more than pandering by the Liberal government."
"If someone wants to move to Canada, they can adjust to the way we operate in our country, not the other way," a third wrote. mdaniell@postmedia.com

Subject: Mortgages; Islamic law; Muslims; Social networks

Document 73 of 356

Why Justin Trudeau's Liberals are introducing 'halal mortgages'

Author: Daniell, Mark

Publication info: Daniell, Mark. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-justin-trudeaus-liberals-are-introducing/docview/3042513304/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Living+Arrangements+of+Children+of+Immigrants&amp;title=The+Future+of+Children&amp;issn=10548289&amp;date=2011-04-01&amp;volume=21&amp;issue=1&amp;spage=&amp;au=Landale%2C+Nancy+S%3BThomas%2C+Kevin+J+A%3BJennifer+Van+Hook&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Future+of+Children&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/10.1353%2Ffoc.2011.0003

Full text: As the federal government unveiled its latest budget, Justin Trudeau's Liberals revealed its plans to explore new ways to expand home ownership, including the adoption of "halal mortgages."
According to this week's federal budget, the Liberal government revealed it "is exploring new measures to expand access to alternative financing products, like halal mortgages."
The charging of interest goes against the Islamic faith and financial gains made through interest are viewed as unjust in the eyes of Sharia law. A "halal mortgage" still lets lenders earn a profit with a different set of terms that can include charging a higher overall price for a person's home (essentially including future growth gains in the selling price), engaging in a rent-to-own model or establishing a partnership between a lender and home owner that slowly reverts to the latter over time as payments are made.
Mohamad Sawwaf, the co-founder and CEO of Manzil, an Islamic financial institution based in Toronto, says that "halal mortgages" are the same as traditional ones.
"The process and documentation is what makes the transaction halal," he told CTV News in 2022
"People that get Islamic mortgages still pay a comparable amount that you would pay if you got a conventional mortgage. It's just that the structure of these mortgages are different," Walid Hejazi, an associate professor of economic analysis and policy at the University of Toronto's Rotman School of Management, tells CBC
The Liberals said that changes could see adjustments "in the tax treatment of these products or a new regulatory sandbox for financial service providers."
Muslims represent almost 5% of the Canadian population, more than doubling in size since 2001. In 2022, a group urging Canadian-Muslims to vote in the Ontario election predicted they would affect the outcome in almost 50 provincial ridings.
The feds began consulting with financial service providers last month "to understand how policies can better support the needs of all Canadians seeking to become homeowners."
An update on the scheme is expected later this year in the 2024 fall economic statement.
As the news made headlines in recent days, Canadians reacted to the plan with a mixture of confusion and anger on social media. Many called the proposition a "desperate ploy" by the Liberals to secure more votes and calls for religion to be kept out of politics.
"Bigotry aside, these are just fixed rate mortgages with mental gymnastics to fool God," one person wrote on Reddit
"I'm a Muslim myself but I don't understand why Canadians are entertaining this aspect of Sharia law. The Muslims who immigrated here did so with full knowledge of how the western finance work," another added. "Nothing more than pandering by the Liberal government."
"If someone wants to move to Canada, they can adjust to the way we operate in our country, not the other way," a third wrote.
The measure also drew criticism in the House of Commons on Friday after Liberal MP Iqra Khalid insisted the feds are looking "to understand how federal policies can protect Canadians from abuses."
Bloc Quebecois MP Marilene Gill questioned why "halal mortgages" are "included in the budget" and accused the Liberals of coming up with a "tailored solution" after a Muslim group threatened to cut off a $680,000 donation to the party.
BLOC MP: "Does the subject of 'expanding access to halal mortgages' being in the budget have anything to do with a Muslim lobbying group threatening to pull $680,000 from the Liberal Party?"
- Amazing Zoltan (@AmazingZoltan) April 20, 2024
"Why do they want to introduce elements of sharia law into the mortgage rules of this so-called secular country?" Gill's fellow Bloc MP Martin Champoux asked
But advocates say that intervention is needed because Muslims partaking in "halal mortgages" can be forced to pay higher rates than conventional mortgages. Wider access and more competition could see those rates drop.
"Canada is about 20 years behind on Islamic finance compared to other developed countries like the U.S. and U.K.," says Zuhair Naqvi, who founded Oakville, Ont.-based Eqraz.
Battered in the polls and facing a home ownership crunch, Trudeau has pledged to build nearly four million homes by 2031.
"It's a plan to build housing, including for renters, on a scale not seen in generations. We're talking about almost 3.9 million homes by 2031," the prime minster said earlier this month.
Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland also announced the feds will allow 30-year amortization periods on insured mortgages for first-time homebuyers purchasing newly built homes.
mdaniell@postmedia.com

Document 74 of 356

Keffiyeh ban upholds democratic values

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/keffiyeh-ban-upholds-democratic-values/docview/3042363888/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Keffiyeh+ban+upholds+democratic+values&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Speaker Ted Arnott showed remarkable courage and integrity in banning keffiyehs from the Ontario legislature this week.
The checkered scarf, which has become politicized of late and associated with the pro-Hamas movement, is inappropriate attire in a place where politicians should be able to discuss controversial issues in a place of neutrality.
House of Commons Speaker Greg Fergus should be brave by following suit and banning it from the House of Commons. Had he done so earlier, he could have avoided the unseemly picture of several MPs draping themselves in keffiyehs as they gloated over the outcome of a recent divisive pro-Palestinian motion in the House.
This is not an unusual parliamentary measure.
Props, signs, flags, slogans and inappropriate attire are routinely banned and people asked to leave for attempting to display them.
The reason is obvious. Politicians must feel free to discuss divisive issues in an atmosphere of safety and security. They should not be intimidated by passive-aggressive displays.
Leaders of all four parties in the Ontario legislature professed dismay at Arnott's decision. That's performative poppycock - and they know it. They know the rules better than anyone and they're playing to the peanut gallery in asking Arnott to reconsider.
MPP Sarah Jama called the ban part of this country's "legacy of colonialism." Jama was earlier dumped from the NDP caucus for statements deemed controversial on the Hamas-Israel war.
The "legacy of colonialism" is the Westminster model of Parliament and we should be thankful for it. It has provided us with a tried and tested mature democracy where people can freely express their views without brawling in the aisles.
Federal Justice Minister Arif Virani poured gas on the fire, saying on X that the keffiyeh "should be welcomed in all Canadian institutions. An expression of pride in one's heritage should be celebrated, not stifled."
Celebrate your pride outside the House, minister. Don't flout the rules.
Comparing the keffiyeh to a yarmulke, hijab or crucifix or other outward expression of religion is a false analogy. Those are religious obligations. The keffiyeh is a cultural item that has, since Oct. 7, been used by demonstrators of all faiths to express support for Hamas, deemed a terrorist organization by Canada.
Arnott's ban is fair and should be respected by all legislatures.

Subject: Bans; Colonialism

Company / organization: Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Identifier / keyword: speaker; arnott; showed; remarkable; courage; integrity

Re "French hero gains Australian residency for confronting killer in Sydney mall attack" (The Associated Press, April 18): Well done that the Aussie government recognized this act of bravery. Kevin Moran
Oakville

UNAFFORDABLE HOMES
There is a perfect storm of pressures brewing and it is not just interest rates, job loss, illness, credit debt or rising cost of living, One, is that Investors and developers will want assurances that the homes they build can sell and not below their costs. I just got my insurance renewal policy and they are asking for an additional $1,000 per year and I have a never made a claim. This adds another $90 per month to home ownership. Carbon tax added hundreds of dollars to the costs of ownership as well. Everything that is put into building a home from sewer connections, paving the roads, sidewalks, digging ditches and making curbs went up on April Fools' Day. Home affordability is just getting harder, one drip at a time.
Brian Mellor
Midland
(It's one thing trying to even get into the housing market, then all of these additional fines, levies and fees make it even more punitive)
REST IN PEACE MR. FRENCH
Re "Kristen's dad dies at 92" (Jane Stevenson, April 18): My condolences to the French family who have lost a husband and father. I'm sure Doug French's life was filled with sadness at the loss of his daughter Kristen. I hope he is finally at peace.
Karen Benz
(It is difficult to imagine just how hard it would have been to move in from losing his daughter the way he did. May he rest in peace)
WHAT ARE THE PRIORITIES?
Jews in Toronto and all over Ontario - on the streets, in the malls, at graffitied and firebombed synagogues, while shopping or eating, working, inside high school and university classrooms and campuses - are needing to defend themselves against verbal and physical threats, altercations and intimidation, constantly. But the highest and more immediate priority is 'banning the banning' of the keffiyeh; the conflagrating, ongoing egregious symbol of 'from the river to the sea' and Mein Kampf? How about that Premier Ford?
Victor Redlick
Toronto
(Our Jewish community needs to be protected and feel safe, that should be the priority. Ford should have stayed out of it and simply disagree with - but respect the Speaker's decision)

'GETTING WORSE': Trudeau Liberals blamed for latest line of desperate workers in Toronto

Author: Wilford, Denette

Publication info: Wilford, Denette. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/getting-worse-trudeau-liberals-blamed-latest-line/docview/3042015876/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27GETTING+WORSE%27%3A+Trudeau+Liberals+blamed+for+latest+line+of+desperate+workers+in+Toronto&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Wilford%2C+Denette&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Videos of massive lineups for job fairs and open calls in the GTA are commonplace on social media.

However, most blamed the federal government - namely Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Housing Minister Sean Fraser - and the Liberals' immigration policies.

Document 78 of 356

LILLEY: Rules on clothing in legislatures are in place to stop division

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-rules-on-clothing-legislatures-are-place/docview/3041835103/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Rules+on+clothing+in+legislatures+are+in+place+to+stop+division&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The rules around what is acceptable to wear in parliament come from a time when political divisions were deeper than they are now.
It's too bad that so many people, especially in the media or political establishment, don't understand this before they pronounce on an issue, like the keffiyeh.
The decision of Ted Arnott, Speaker of the Ontario legislature, to say that the wearing of a keffiyeh is not allowed has been described as a ban. In reality, it is the upholding of the rules that were put in place to allow parliament to function instead of boiling over into partisan and sectarian battles.
In Canada's early days we were bringing together a collection of communities - English, French, Irish, Catholic, Protestant. Those divisions may seem small now but our first political assassination in Canada was Thomas D'Arcy McGee, an Irish immigrant, MP and member of Sir John A. Macdonald's government, who was killed for being on the wrong side of an issue, according to the gunman.
So, we have rules in parliament, be it the House of Commons or provincial legislatures, on how to behave in order to pave over differences enough to allow for civilized debate. This includes not being allowed to call your political opponent names - like not being allowed to call them a liar even when they are lying through their teeth.
It's all an attempt to promote civil discourse, this also extends to how members of a legislature are allowed to dress.
Roman Catholic priests elected to the House of Commons have had to seek permission to wear their clerical collar rather than a shirt and tie. Those of Scottish heritage are normally granted permission, if requested, to wear a kilt on Robbie Burns Day, but it's not a given.
Generally, though, wearing anything that can be construed as political in any way is forbidden.
This includes MPs or MPPs wearing a daffodil to support the Canadian Cancer Society in April or a member of the legislature wearing a local junior hockey team jersey during a playoff run. Each of those requires the consent of the legislature before it can happen.
In fact, when you see MPs or MPPs wearing a particular ribbon on their lapel during question period for a specific cause, even as wholesome as cancer research, it's because the members of the legislature agreed to this ahead of time.
These rules, in place over generations, were instituted to ensure the debate in the legislature remained focused on the task at hand and wasn't enflamed by divisive symbols. An MP or MPP wearing a Freemason's apron or Orangeman's sash and bowler hat would have not only been offensive to Irish and French Catholics years ago, it could have led to violence inside and outside of the legislature.
Wearing a keffiyeh post Oct. 7, as many MPs in Ottawa have, or as some have done at Queen's Park, is purely a political statement and should not be allowed under the rules. This is especially true because the people wearing them are not Palestinian, they are not Arabs, they are wearing them to make a political statement of solidarity.
That is what the rules are designed to prevent.
LILLEY: Doug Ford needs to take a time out after a rough week
LILLEY: Ford jumps on contentious keffiyeh issue with both feet
Speaker of Ontario legislature stands by keffiyeh ban
This isn't an attack on Palestinians or Muslims, this is the equal application of rules that have been applied to other cultures and religions beforehand.
We shouldn't upend our system over false claims of bigotry.
If these rules are changed, or if they continue to be flaunted in Ottawa under Speaker Greg Fergus, then there will be no ability to enforce the rules at all. All political statements using clothing or props will be allowed.
That isn't a road we want to go down. Speaker Arnott's ruling isn't divisive, it's the opposite of that.
blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Legislatures; Assassinations & assassination attempts

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: rules; around; acceptable; parliament; political; divisions

Title: LILLEY: Rules on clothing in legislatures are in place to stop division

Document 79 of 356

Unplug Ford; After one rough week, premier needs all the cottage time he can get

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/unplug-ford-after-one-rough-week-premier-needs/docview/3041686695/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Unplug+Ford%3B+After+one+rough+week%2C+premier+needs+all+the+cottage+time+he+can+get&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford looks like he needs a week off, or at least a long weekend at the cottage with his phone turned off.
This hasn't been a good week for the premier with policy reversals, jumping into a controversy he didn't need and announcing he opposed his own minister's bill before saying he supported it.
Answering questions from reporters on Thursday morning, Ford seemed tired, like he needed time at the lake.
It started Monday morning with a good-news announcement of expanded GO train service for Milton, where the announcement was made and where a byelection is underway. The announcement included more GO train trips to Kitchener and making every other train on the UP Express from Union Station to Pearson Airport an express.
The plan was that commuters who wanted to use the Bloor or Weston stops serviced by UP Express could take the more frequent GO train, which runs on the same line and services the same stops. But some people complained, they called the local city councillor, they called the local MPP - Ford's nephew Michael - and by late Tuesday afternoon the UP Express decision was being walked back.
A few angry phone calls and an online petition and Ford was backing up his new policy faster than a runaway train.
On Wednesday night, after going a whole day without a policy reversal, Ford and his team decided to reverse themselves on the advice given to the PC caucus and brought Middle East politics into Ontario politics. It's ironic that this happened because Ford and his team had just spent time telling the PC caucus, in less than gentle terms, that he did not want them speaking or posting to social media about anything related to the war between Israel and Hamas until after the May 2 byelection in Milton.
The next day, Ford not only not only commented on a ruling from Ted Arnott, Speaker of the Ontario legislature, that wearing a kaffiyeh was against the rules, he insisted that Arnott act like the premier and reverse himself.
The kaffiyeh isn't something people were wearing in the Ontario legislature before the Oct. 7 terrorist attacks by Hamas and even if someone tried, it would have been against the rules.
There are long-standing rules in Canada's parliamentary system that dictate what can be worn and anything deemed being political or supportive of a cause is forbidden unless permission is sought and granted. Every spring, MPPs representing cities or towns with an OHL team enjoying a playoff run need to ask permission to wear something supporting their local team in the legislature.
Even something as benign as wearing a daffodil lapel pin for the Canadian Cancer Society cannot happen with permission being sought and granted.
If hockey and cancer are too political, what do you think the rules would say about wearing a kaffiyeh in solidarity with the Palestinian people? It is clearly a political act and against the rules.
Speaker Arnott didn't ban the kaffiyeh, he upheld long-standing rules - and Ford, along with the two opposition leaders, want that changed because of the large Muslim vote at stake in the Milton byelection. We shouldn't be changing long-standing rules just because politicians want to pander for votes, which is exactly what each of them are doing.
Ford's final misstep this week, at least so far, was when he was asked a very clear question about Bill 166. A group of academics, some with a history of anti-Israel and pro-Palestinian activism, had complained that this bill was an infringement on academic independence.
This bill calls for post-secondary institutions to have anti-racism, anti-Islamophobia and anti-Semitic policies in place. There is no way these academics would be protesting this legislation if it didn't include anti-Semitism.
When asked about the bill, Ford said he didn't think it was the way to go and that universities should govern themselves and ministers should use other tools. It was a government bill, a bill brought forward by his minister, and he was speaking against it.
Hours later, Ford's office "clarified" he was still in support of the bill.
Anyone can have a bad week.
Ford has had a particularly bad one.
Doug, it's time to go to the cottage and unplug for a while. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Antisemitism; Legislatures; Politics

Location: Israel

Company / organization: Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Identifier / keyword: looks; needs; weekend; cottage; phone; turned

Title: Unplug Ford; After one rough week, premier needs all the cottage time he can get

Publication title: The Toronto Sun; Toronto

First page: A.5

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Apr 19, 2024

column: Opinion

Section: News

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 08373175

Source type: Newspaper

Language of publication: English

Document 80 of 356

LILLEY: Doug Ford needs to take a time out after a rough week

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-doug-ford-needs-take-time-out-after-rough/docview/3041499259/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Doug+Ford+needs+to+take+a+time+out+after+a+rough+week&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford looks like he needs a week off, or at least a long weekend at the cottage with his phone turned off.
This hasn't been a good week for the Premier with policy reversals, jumping into a controversy he didn't need to and announcing he opposed his own minister's bill before saying he supported it.
Answering questions from reporters on Thursday morning, Ford seemed tired, like he needed time at the lake.
It started Monday morning with a good news announcement of expanded GO Train service for Milton, where the announcement was made and where a by-election is currently underway. The announcement included more GO Train trips to Kitchener and making every other train on the UP Express from Union Station to Pearson Airport an express.
The plan was that commuters who wanted to use the Bloor or Weston stops currently serviced by UP Express could take the more frequent Go Train, which runs on the same line and services the same stops. But ... some people complained, they called the local City Councillor, they called the local MPP - Ford's Nephew Michael - and by late Tuesday afternoon the UP Express decision was being walked back.
A few angry phone calls and an online petition and Ford was backing up his new policy faster than a runaway train
On Wednesday night, after going a whole day without a policy reversal, Ford and his team decided to reverse themselves on the advice given to the PC Caucus and brought Middle East politics into Ontario politics. It's ironic that this happened because Ford and his team had just spent time telling the PC caucus, in less than gentle terms, that he did not want them speaking or posting to social media about anything related to the war between Israel and Hamas until after the May 2 byelection in Milton.
The next day, Ford not only commented on a ruling from Ted Arnott, speaker of the Ontario Legislature, that wearing a keffiyeh was against the rules, he insisted that Arnott act like the Premier and reverse himself
The keffiyeh isn't something people were wearing in the Ontario Legislature before the Oct. 7 terrorist attacks by Hamas and even if someone tried, it would have been against the rules.
There are longstanding rules in Canada's parliamentary system that dictate what can be worn and anything deemed being political or supportive of a cause is forbidden unless permission is sought and granted. Every spring, MPPs representing cities or towns with an OHL team enjoying a playoff run need to ask permission to wear something supporting their local team in the legislature.
LILLEY: Ford jumps on contentious keffiyeh issue with both feet
LILLEY: Ford's PCs hold commanding lead, Liberals steal NDP support
LILLEY: Ford orders LCBO to bring back paper bags. It's about time!
Even something as benign as wearing a daffodil lapel pin for the Canadian Cancer Society cannot happen with permission being sought and granted.
If hockey and cancer are too political, what do you think the rules would say about wearing a keffiyeh in solidarity with the Palestinian people? It is clearly a political act and against the rules.
Speaker Arnott didn't ban the keffiyeh, he upheld longstanding rules and Ford, along with the two opposition leaders, want that changed because of the large Muslim vote at stake in the Milton byelection. We shouldn't be changing longstanding rules just because politicians want to pander for votes, which is exactly what each of them are doing.
Ford's final misstep this week, at least so far, was when he was asked a very clear question about Bill 166. A group of academics, some with a history of anti-Israel and pro-Palestinian activism, had complained that this bill was an infringement on academic independence.
This bill calls for post-secondary institutions to have anti-racism, anti-Islamophobia and anti-Semitic policies in place. There is no way these academics would be protesting this legislation if it didn't include anti-Semitism.
When asked about the bill, Ford said he didn't think it was the way to go and that universities should govern themselves and ministers should use other tools. It was a government bill he was speaking against, a bill brought forward by his minister, and he was speaking against it.
Hours later, Ford's office "clarified" that he was still in support of the bill.
Anyone can have a bad week, Ford has had a particularly bad one.
Doug, it's time to go to the cottage and unplug for a while.
blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Antisemitism; Legislatures; Politics

Location: Israel

Company / organization: Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Document 81 of 356

LILLEY: Ford jumps on contentious keffiyeh issue with both feet

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-ford-jumps-on-contentious-keffiyeh-issue/docview/3041219423/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Ford+jumps+on+contentious+keffiyeh+issue+with+both+feet&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's hard to know what Doug Ford was thinking on Wednesday night, or if he was thinking at all.
Just around 9 p.m., Ford issued a statement calling on Ted Arnott, the Speaker of the Legislature, to reverse his decision that bans the wearing of keffiyehs at Queen's Park.
"I do not support his decision as it needlessly divides the people of our province. I call on the Speaker to reverse his decision immediately," Ford said.
Ford, with much pressure from his top advisors, has been trying not to comment much lately on anything to do with the war in the Middle East or its ramifications here. With that statement, he jumped in with both feet and picked a side, something that is getting noticed.
The reason that Arnott issued the ban is to uphold long-standing rules against the use of props, including clothing or anything added to your clothing that makes a political statement. In the current context, wearing a keffiyeh is a political statement, something those wearing them to rallies across the country, or in legislatures, would certainly agree with.
They are being worn to show solidarity with the Palestinian people.
"The Speaker cannot be aware of the meaning of every symbol or pattern but when items are drawn to my attention, there is a responsibility to respond," Arnott said in an email to MPPs on Wednesday. "After extensive research, I concluded that the wearing of keffiyehs at the present time in our Assembly is intended to be a political statement. So, as Speaker, I cannot authorize the wearing of keffiyehs based on our longstanding conventions."
These are the same rules that require MPPs to seek permission of the House to wear the hockey jersey of their local junior team to show support during a playoff run. If hockey jerseys are deemed too political, surely the same can be said of the keffiyeh in the current context.
There can be no doubt that keffiyehs are an overt political statement, used to show solidarity with the Palestinian people, especially since Oct. 7. The organizers of the rallies that have overtaken the streets of Toronto since the start of the war have often encouraged participants to ensure they bring their keffiyehs.
Why?
Because it is a political statement.
The Speaker would not allow a member to take their seat if they were wearing a MAGA hat or a shirt that said "Bring them home now" in relation to Israeli hostages.
The rules may seem unfair to some, bigoted to others but they have been put in place and upheld to encourage civil debate in contentious times. While we think our politics is divisive now, it's important to remember that traditionally government and opposition benches are kept at least 13 feet - or two sword lengths - apart, though in Ontario the distance is much wider.
If Ford and the opposition leaders want to allow people to wear a keffiyeh then they can change the rules. That would also end up allowing other overtly political statements to be made, something I doubt they want to happen.
All Ford had to do on this matter was correctly and plainly say that this was not his decision or one of his government and that rules and procedures of the legislature are handled by the Speaker. By taking the stance that he has, Ford is alienating all sides and not making anyone happy, a tactic that has failed for Justin Trudeau in Ottawa.
Muslim voters who have this as their driving issue won't be swayed by Ford's statement to come to his side. Meanwhile, Jewish voters who feel Ford has been slow, or even silent after incidents of anti-Semitism, are noting how quick he was to speak out on this issue.
Ford and his team likely thought they had a winner of a statement on Wednesday night. Things look much different in the light of day.
blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Voters; Palestinian people

Identifier / keyword: thinking; night; around; issued; statement; calling

Document 82 of 356

Everyone, let's GO!; 300 more train trips added to GTA service

Author: Wilford, Denette

Publication info: Wilford, Denette. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/everyone-lets-go-300-more-train-trips-added-gta/docview/3039681935/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Everyone%2C+let%27s+GO%21%3B+300+more+train+trips+added+to+GTA+service&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Wilford%2C+Denette&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government unveiled the largest GO Transit train service expansion in more than a decade Monday.
More than 300 trips per week will be added to the Milton, Lakeshore West, Lakeshore East, Kitchener and Stouffville lines.
"As part of our work to get it done on the largest public transit expansion in North America, our government is adding hundreds of additional GO train trips each week for communities across the GTA," Premier Doug Ford said in a statement.
Ford said the added trips will save the average transit rider $1,600 a year.
Beginning April 28, travellers will see weekend train service increase from every 30 minutes to every 15 minutes in the afternoon and evening on the Lakeshore West and East corridors between Oakville GO station, Union Station and Durham College Oshawa GO station.
For the first time, riders on the Kitchener line will have a 30-minute weekday service during midday and evenings between Bramalea and Union Station.
The province is also adding evening train service seven days a week on the Stouffville line, as well as an additional morning rushhour trip to Union for commuters in Milton and an afternoon rush-hour trip from Union to Milton GO.
For those heading to and from Pearson airport, every second UP Express train seven days a week will be non-stop between Union and Pearson, providing a more direct option for airport transfers.
"As Ontario's population continues to grow, our government is investing in a world-class transit network that connects communities and people to good jobs and affordable housing," Minister of Transportation Prabmeet Sarkaria said.
"We're delivering on our plan to bring more reliable, convenient twoway all-day GO train service to commuters in the Greater Golden Horseshoe."

Document 83 of 356

'Extreme wokeness'; calls Canada 'cautionary tale'for America Bill Maher

Author: Daniell, Mark

Publication info: Daniell, Mark. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/extreme-wokeness-calls-canada-cautionary-talefor/docview/3039681634/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Extreme+wokeness%27%3B+calls+Canada+%27cautionary+tale%27for+America+Bill+Maher&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.21&amp;au=Daniell%2C+Mark&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bill Maher took aim at Canada, calling the country a "cautionary tale" and warning that its embrace of "extreme wokeness" is forcing voters to the right.
During a monologue last week on Real Time With Bill Maher, the latenight personality compared Canada to America and carefully demonstrated how high unemployment, unchecked immigration, skyrocketing debt and lack of productivity are seeing the country falling further and further behind its G7 counterparts.
"If we want to save our country, we should learn from other progressive nations and pump the brakes on extreme wokeness," Maher wrote in a caption that accompanied a clip posted to YouTube.
"If we want to save our country, we should follow the advice good liberals have given for decades and learn from other countries. Especially those beacons of progressivism like Canada, England and Scandinavia, and I agree we should, as long as we're honest about the lessons we're learning and as long as we're up to date on the current data," Maher began his eight-minute warning.
Maher then compared the unemployment rate in America (3.8%) and Canada (6.1%), and revealed that his neighbours to the north has more cities with worse air pollution than in the U.S.
He was careful to say he wasn't trying to pick on Canada. "I love Canada and its people," Maher said. "But I hate zombie lies. That's when things change, but what people say about them doesn't."
For decades, Vancouver "seemed idyllic," he said. But that's no longer the case.
"Canada was where all the treasured goals of liberalism worked perfectly. It was like NPR come to life, but with poutine," he quipped.
"Canada was the Statue of Liberty with a low-maintenance haircut and cross-country skis, a giant idealized blue state with single-payer health care, gun control and abortion on polite demand. Canada was where every woke White college kid wearing pajama pants outdoors who had it up to here with America's racist patriarchy dreamt of living someday. I mean, besides Gaza," Maher joked as his audience broke into laughter.
"There's only one problem with thinking everything's better in Canada: It's not. Not anymore, anyway. Last year, Canada added 1.3 million people, which is a lot in one year - the equivalent of the U.S. adding 11 million migrants in one year. And, now, they're experiencing a housing crisis even worse than ours and we're sleeping in tents. The median price of a home here is $346,000. In Canada converted to U.S.
dollars, it's $487,000. If Barbie moved to Winnipeg, she wouldn't be able to afford her dream house and Ken would be working at Tim Hortons."
Last month, the Canadian Press reported that people in Ontario and British Columbia increasingly missed payments on mortgages and credit cards in the fourth quarter of 2023, citing stats from Equifax Canada.
In Ontario, the mortgage delinquency rate was up 135.2% compared with a year earlier, while B.C.'s rate rose by 62.2%.
"It definitely is a worrying trend," Rebecca Oakes, vice-president of advanced analytics at Equifax Canada, told CP.
Maher noted that because of mortgage debt, "Canada has the highest debt to GDP ratio of any G7 nation."
He then took aim at the country's "vaunted health-care system."
"(It) ranks dead last among high income countries, and access to primary health care, and the ability to see a doctor in a day or two. And it's not for lack of spending. Of the 30 countries with universal coverage, Canada spends over 13% of its economy on it, which is a lot of money for free health care. Look, I'm not saying Canada still isn't a great country, it is, but those aren't paradise numbers. If Canada was an apartment, the lead feature might be: 'America adjacent.'" With his audience breaking into gales of laughter, and one of his guests, Piers Morgan, also started to grin, Maher continued.
"Honestly Canada, I'm not saying any of this because I enjoy it. I don't because I've always enjoyed you. But I need to cite you as a cautionary tale to help my country. And the moral of that tale is yes, you can move too far left," Maher warned. "And when you do, you wind up pushing the people in the middle to the right. At its worst, Canada is what American voters think happens when there's no one putting a check on extreme wokeness," he said, citing Ontario trans teacher Kayla Lemieux, who went viral worldwide after images circulated of the instructor wearing massive prosthetic breasts in the classroom.
"What about the children?" Maher asked. "Who says, 'When it comes to huge, ridiculous t---, let's save that for the kids?' ... This is why people vote for Trump."
"They say in politics, liberals are the gas pedal, and conservatives are the brakes, and I'm generally with the gas pedal, but not if we're driving off a cliff," he said.
Jumping to immigration, he cited Sweden opening its borders to "over a million and a half immigrants since 2010" which led to "20%" of its citizens being foreign born, its education system "tanking," and Europe's leader in "gangland killings."
"One result is that the far-right parties are in the government now there for the first time. To which liberals say blaming immigrants for the rising crime rate is racist. Yeah, but is it true? Of course it's true. It's not a coincidence the quality of life went down after the Somali gangs started a drug turf war using hand grenades. Calling it racist doesn't solve the problem. It hands future elections to someone who will solve the problem and who I promise you're not going to like," he said, flashing a photo of presumptive Republican presidential nominee Donald Trump.
"I'm a college educated Canadian, who works 40 hours per week in a white collar job, and I can't afford to live in the country I was born in. Tough to date from your mom's basement. America, do not go down the road we went ... Bill is not wrong," one person commented on YouTube.
"Eight years of Trudeau destroyed this country," another added.
On X, Maher's comments went viral, drawing nearly 42 million views nearly 5,000 comments with many calling the TV personality's assessment spot on.

Document 85 of 356

'EXTREME WOKENESS': Bill Maher calls Canada 'a cautionary tale' for America

Author: Daniell, Mark

Publication info: Daniell, Mark. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/extreme-wokeness-bill-maher-calls-canada/docview/3039263834/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27EXTREME+WOKENESS%27%3A+Bill+Maher+calls+Canada+%27a+cautionary+tale%27+for+America&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Daniell%2C+Mark&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bill Maher took aim at Canada calling the country a "cautionary tale" and warning that its embrace of "extreme wokeness" is forcing voters to the right.
During a monologue last week on Real Time With Bill Maher, the late-night personality compared Canada to America and carefully demonstrated how high unemployment, unchecked immigration, skyrocketing debt and lack of productivity are seeing the country falling further and further behind its G7 counterparts.
"If we want to save our country, we should learn from other progressive nations and pump the brakes on extreme wokeness," Maher wrote in a caption that accompanied a clip posted to YouTube
"If we want to save our country, we should follow the advice good liberals have given for decades and learn from other countries. Especially those beacons of progressivism like Canada, England and Scandinavia, and I agree we should, as long as we're honest about the lessons we're learning and as long as we're up to date on the current data," Maher began his eight-minute warning.
Maher then compared the unemployment rate in America (3.8%) and Canada (6.1%), and revealed that his neighbours to the north has more cities with worse air pollution than in the U.S.
He was careful to say he wasn't trying to pick on Canada. "I love Canada and its people," Maher said. "But I hate zombie lies. That's when things change, but what people say about them doesn't."
For decades, Vancouver "seemed idyllic," he said. But that's no longer the case.
"Canada was where all the treasured goals of liberalism worked perfectly. It was like NPR come to life, but with poutine," he quipped.
"Canada was the Statue of Liberty with a low-maintenance haircut and cross-country skis, a giant idealized blue state with single-payer health care, gun control and abortion on polite demand. Canada was where every woke White college kid wearing pajama pants outdoors who had it up to here with America's racist patriarchy dreamt of living someday. I mean, besides Gaza," Maher joked as his audience broke into laughter.
"There's only one problem with thinking everything's better in Canada: It's not. Not anymore, anyway. Last year, Canada added 1.3 million people, which is a lot in one year - the equivalent of the U.S. adding 11 million migrants in one year. And now, they're experiencing a housing crisis even worse than ours and we're sleeping in tents. The median price of a home here is $346,000. In Canada converted to U.S. dollars, it's $487,000. If Barbie moved to Winnipeg, she wouldn't be able to afford her dream house and Ken would be working at Tim Hortons."
They say in politics, liberals are the gas pedal, and conservatives are the brakes, and I'm generally with the gas pedal, but not if we're driving off a cliff.
- Bill Maher (@billmaher) April 13, 2024
Last month, the Canadian Press reported that people in Ontario and British Columbia increasingly missed payments on mortgages and credit cards in the fourth quarter of 2023, citing stats from Equifax Canada.
In Ontario, the mortgage delinquency rate was up 135.2% compared with a year earlier, while B.C.'s rate rose by 62.2%.
"It definitely is a worrying trend," Rebecca Oakes, vice-president of advanced analytics at Equifax Canada, told CP.
Maher noted that because of mortgage debt, "Canada has the highest debt to GDP ratio of any G7 nation."
He then took aim at the country's "vaunted health-care system."
"(It) ranks dead last among high income countries, and access to primary health care, and the ability to see a doctor in a day or two. And it's not for lack of spending. Of the 30 countries with universal coverage, Canada spends over 13% of its economy on it, which is a lot of money for free health care. Look, I'm not saying Canada still isn't a great country, it is, but those aren't paradise numbers. If Canada was an apartment, the lead feature might be: 'America adjacent.'"
With his audience breaking into gales of laughter, and one of his guests, Piers Morgan, also started to grin, Maher continued.
"Honestly Canada, I'm not saying any of this because I enjoy it. I don't because I've always enjoyed you. But I need to cite you as a cautionary tale to help my country. And the moral of that tale is yes, you can move too far left," Maher warned. "And when you do, you wind up pushing the people in the middle to the right. At its worst, Canada is what American voters think happens when there's no one putting a check on extreme wokeness," he said, citing Ontario trans teacher Kayla Lemieux, who went viral worldwide after images circulated of the instructor wearing massive prosthetic breasts in the classroom.
Oakville Trafalgar High School teacher Kayla Lemieux in shop class.
"What about the children?" Maher asked. "Who says, 'When it comes to huge, ridiculous t-, let's save that for the kids?' ... This is why people vote for Trump."
"They say in politics, liberals are the gas pedal, and conservatives are the brakes, and I'm generally with the gas pedal, but not if we're driving off a cliff," he said.
Jumping to immigration, he cited Sweden opening its borders to "over a million and a half immigrants since 2010" which led to "20%" of its citizens being foreign born, its education system "tanking," and Europe's leader in "gangland killings."
"One result is that the far-right parties are in the government now there for the first time. To which liberals say blaming immigrants for the rising crime rate is racist. Yeah, but is it true? Of course it's true. It's not a coincidence the quality of life went down after the Somali gangs started a drug turf war using hand grenades. Calling it racist doesn't solve the problem. It hands future elections to someone who will solve the problem and who I promise you're not going to like," he said, flashing a photo of presumptive Republican presidential nominee Donald Trump.
"I'm a college educated Canadian, who works 40 hours per week in a white collar job, and I can't afford to live in the country I was born in. Tough to date from your mom's basement. America, do not go down the road we went... Bill is not wrong," one person commented on YouTube.
"Eight years of Trudeau destroyed this country," another added.
On X, Maher's comments went viral, drawing nearly 42 million views nearly 5,000 comments with many calling the TV personality's assessment spot on.
"He's 100% correct. This current government is running our country into the ground," Canadian human rights campaigner Yasmine Mohammed wrote "When I left four years ago, everyone abroad was so surprised 'but Canada is so great' ya, no. It's great at propaganda. It's hanging on to 'zombie lies'. We were great. I hope we will be again."
mdaniell@postmedia.com
Joe Rogan urges Canada to vote for Pierre Poilievre: 'Get your s*** together!'
'Charming anti-Semite': Bill Maher reveals why he axed Kanye West interview

A lot of people from Ontario go on holidays to cottages and other countries for months at a time. CLIMATE CRISIS
Re "Poilievre demands first ministers meeting as Ford ties PM's future to carbon price" (The Canadian Press, April 2): I long for a day when a Conservative leader calls for an emergency meeting on the climate change crisis, rather than one to take a rebate out of our pockets. We are one of dozens of countries worldwide with a form of carbon pricing in place, but are unique in giving the cash raised back directly to citizens. How is it that we are also the ones whining most about it? We are not alone in facing a cost of living crisis. We are just alone in thinking that letting the richest industries and individuals pollute to their hearts' content will resolve it.
Mark Taylor Calgary

Document 89 of 356

Ford's PCs forty-fied; Poll numbers good, but Grits growing

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-pcs-forty-fied-poll-numbers-good-grits/docview/3037733604/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+PCs+forty-fied%3B+Poll+numbers+good%2C+but+Grits+growing&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If an election were held today, Doug Ford's PC party would win another big majority government. The latest poll from Liaison Strategies also shows Bonnie Crombie's Liberals would form the opposition and regain official party status at Queen's Park.
Oh, and the NDP, they'd be relegated to third place once again.
The telephone poll of 1,280 eligible Ontario voters finds that 40% would back Ford's PCs, 30% would back Crombie's Liberals but just 18% would pick the NDP under Marit Stiles.
Ford's support is pretty much where it was in the last election when his party won 83 seats. Crombie has been able to move the Liberals up since taking over as leader in December, but only at the expense of the NDP.
In the last two elections, the Liberals haven't been able to even win enough seats to be considered a recognized party in the legislature. Under the official rules, the nine Liberal MPPs are considered Independents and given limited resources and time in question period compared with the NDP.
But like they did under former leader Andrea Horwath and continue to do under Stiles, the NDP has squandered their opportunity to cement themselves as the left-of-centre choice in Ontario. In every province west of Ontario, there is essentially no Liberal party to speak of and elections are between the NDP and parties representing what is often called a free market coalition.
Had the Ontario NDP played their cards right starting in 2018, they could have ensured the same in Ontario. Instead, the Ontario Liberal Party is showing signs of coming back to life.
Not that the Crombie's Liberals should be popping champagne corks yet.
When Steven Del Duca took over as leader of the Liberal party in March 2020, they saw a bounce in the polls. There were periods in 2020, 2021 and even early 2022 when the Del Duca Liberals were leading Ford and his PC party, and then the campaign happened and the Liberals ended up at 24%, 16 points behind Ford.
It's always important for political types to remember that voters are fickle, polls can change and campaigns matter. Those three rules apply to every party, including Ford, who appears to be sitting pretty at the moment.
Beyond the 40% voter support, there are some other good numbers for Ford in this poll.
Just shy of half of voters, 49%, say the federal carbon tax should be scrapped. The environment isn't the issue it was before for the Liberals or NDP, with 62% saying pollution has decreased in the province compared with five years ago.
In a head-to-head question on which issue is more important, 54% said affordability while just 25% the environment with 21% saying they didn't know.
That makes Bonnie Crombie's recent comments to an Empire Club luncheon in Toronto problematic.
During a fireside chat after her speech last week, Crombie was asked about her promises to put more money into health care and education and how she would make sure the government, if she were running it, could pay for these promises.
"Well, it's very interesting, because I've been thinking about what exactly the government is spending money on other than gimmicks and freezing the gas tax and giving you a rebate for your stickers and making them so you don't have to pay for them," Crombie said.
"That's really reducing the fiscal capacity and I keep thinking, would people really miss that, or is that money that could be better invested in education and our health-care system?" Calling tax cuts and fee cuts a reduction in the fiscal capacity is a tell. Crombie thinks the government should have that money instead of voters. Given that her party is trying to win a byelection in the suburban seat of Milton, these comments will hurt the party.
Crombie has done well taking support from the NDP, but she's likely taken as much as she can there. To win, she'll need to peel voters away from Ford's PCs and she won't do it by promising to increase taxes and fees on commuters.
Promises like that are music to Doug Ford's ears. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Polls & surveys; Voters; Elections

Identifier / keyword: election; party; another; majority; government; latest

Title: Ford's PCs forty-fied; Poll numbers good, but Grits growing

Full text: WE ALL PAY
This latest carbon tax increase is just another tax grab by the Liberals. Trudeau says that the consumer will be the only one to have to pay this tax. However, he has forgotten the people who also pay this tax. There is the person who grows the product, the person who ships the product to the processing plant, the processing plant, another truck who takes it to the distribution centre, another truck to take it to the store, and finally, the store who all have to pay the carbon tax. It's a good thing that Trudeau was a drama teacher before he got into politics. If he was either a math or business economics teacher, he would definitely fail.
Scott McMaster
Peterborough
(He has miserably failed as our PM. It is hard to fathom just how much is going to have to be done economically to reverse just some of Trudeau's disastrous policies)
ALL ABOUT BOOZE
Re "Folding on paper" (Brian Lilley, April 8): Given the tough times, Doug Ford wants to give free paper bags at LCBO stores for the unfortunate people who forget to bring a reusable bag. He has some leverage with the LCBO, but would it not be more impactful to encourage grocery stores to provide free paper bags? Isn't food more important to struggling Ontarians than alcohol? Not in Ford's opinion. It's all about alcohol. It's a family value!
Peter Wilson
North York
(As you say, he can steer policy for a government controlled entity. The grocers can make the same move if they chose to)
STOP INTERFERING
Regardless of whether any riding outcomes were affected by foreign interference in recent federal elections, the fact that members of the commission investigating such have recognized and admitted that there was foreign interference should not simply shrug their collective shoulders and say, 'No harm, no foul.' This is akin to the courts saying that someone who discharges a firearm in a public place but whose bullets failed to hit anyone has not committed a crime. Action should be taken against the perpetrators and steps must be taken to prevent similar interference in the future.
Robert Laing
Toronto
(Agreed. But the Trudeau Liberals along with their governing partners the NDP, won't do a thing)

Subject: Tax increases; Environmental tax

Document 91 of 356

LILLEY: Ford's PC's hold commanding lead, Liberals steal NDP support

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-fords-pcs-hold-commanding-lead-liberals/docview/3037591766/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Ford%27s+PC%27s+hold+commanding+lead%2C+Liberals+steal+NDP+support&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If an election were held today, Doug Ford's PC Party would win another big majority government. The latest poll from Liaison Strategies also shows Bonnie Crombie's Liberals would form the opposition and regain official party status at Queen's Park.
Oh, and the NDP, they'd be relegated to third place once again.

In every province west of Ontario, there is essentially no Liberal Party to speak of and elections are between the NDP and parties representing what is often called a free market coalition.
Had the Ontario NDP played their cards right starting in 2018, they could have ensured the same in Ontario. Instead, the Ontario Liberal Party is showing signs of coming back to life.
Not that the Crombie's Liberals should be popping champagne corks yet.
When Steven Del Duca took over as leader of the Liberal Party in March 2020, they saw a bounce in the polls. There were periods in 2020, 2021 and even early 2022 where the Del Duca Liberals were leading Ford and his PC Party and then the campaign happened and the Liberals ended up at 24%, 16 points behind Ford.
It's always important for political types to remember that voters are fickle, polls can change and campaigns matter. Those are three rules that apply to every party, including Ford, who appears to be sitting pretty at the moment.
Beyond the 40% voter support there are some other good news numbers for Ford in this poll.
Just shy of half of voters, 49%, say the federal carbon tax should be scrapped. The environment isn't the issue it was before for the Liberals or NDP with 62% saying pollution has decreased in the province compared to five years ago.
In a head-to-head question on which issue is more important 54% said affordability while just 25% the environment with 21% saying they didn't know.
Which makes Bonnie Crombie's recent comments to an Empire Club luncheon in Toronto problematic.
During a fireside chat after her speech last week, Crombie was asked about her promises to put more money into health care and education and how she would make sure the government, if she were running it, could pay for these promises.
"Well, it's very interesting, because I've been thinking about what exactly the government is spending money on other than gimmicks and freezing the gas tax and giving you a rebate for your stickers and making them so you don't have to pay for them," Crombie said.
"That's really reducing the fiscal capacity and I keep thinking, would people really miss that or is that money that could be better invested in education and our health care system?"
LILLEY: Ford orders LCBO to bring back paper bags. It's about time!
WARMINGTON: Doug Ford wonders is 'out to lunch' Steven Guilbeault running Canada?
Calling tax cuts and fee cuts a reduction in the fiscal capacity is a tell, Crombie thinks government should have that money instead of voters. Given that her party is currently trying to win a by-election in the suburban seat of Milton, these comments will hurt the party.
Crombie has done well taking support from the NDP, but she's likely taken as much as she can there. To win, she'll need to peel voters away from Ford's PCs and she won't do it by promising to increase taxes and fees on commuters.
Promises like that are music to Doug Ford's ears.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO

Document 93 of 356

'Covered in blood'; Witness tells Hamilton murder trial how she found wounded woman

Author: Hunter, Brad

Publication info: Hunter, Brad. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/covered-blood-witness-tells-hamilton-murder-trial/docview/3037229305/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Covered+in+blood%27%3B+Witness+tells+Hamilton+murder+trial+how+she+found+wounded+woman&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Hunter%2C+Brad&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Annette Bright sensed something was wrong in the lonely Stoney Creek industrial park.
It was between 7:20 and 7:25 p.m. on Feb. 28, 2021.
"Something is not right," she told a Hamilton court Wednesday, adding that a "sickening" feeling came over her whole body.
Bright turned her vehicle around and realized a young woman was lying on the road.
It appeared she had crawled and was on her knees.
"I asked her, 'What's wrong?'and she said 'Help me,'" Bright said. "She wanted to get into my car, and said she was cold. I said, 'No'. I asked myself, 'Is this for real?'"It was real." The young woman - now identified as Jordyn Romano - told Bright she had been shot.
"She was frantic and cold and there was blood on her shirt. The middle of the shirt was covered in blood."
Bright was the first witness called in the murder trial of Oliver Karafa and Lucy Li.
It's a homicide case that has drawn worldwide attention.
The daughter of a wealthy businesswoman with deep ties to the Liberal party, Li and boyfriend Karafa are charged with first-degree murder and attempted murder in the Stoney Creek shooting of Tyler Pratt, 39, and Romano, then 26.
Romano had been shot in the heart. She survived. Her unborn baby did not.
Karafa and Li have pleaded not guilty.
On Wednesday, the alleged murderers arrived in matching black suits that looked as though they were off the rack. Karafa wore a black sweater under his suit jacket and a white button-down shirt.
Li wore a cream-coloured form-fitting sweater underneath her black blazer. He was attentive; she looked lost.
The court also heard testimony from two Hamilton paramedics who were among the first responders and a warehouse supervisor of the company where Pratt's body was found.
At times, Bright appeared poised to unleash a flood of tears. Even Li's defence lawyer, Liam O'Connor, offered heartfelt kudos to Bright for stopping to help the critically wounded Romano.
On Tuesday, assistant Crown attorney Steven Kim outlined the case against Karafa and Li in his opening address.
Karafa and Li had become friends with Pratt and his fiancee, Romano, in October 2021 after being introduced by a mutual acquaintance.
On the night of the shootings, the four were allegedly at 347 Arvin Ave. in Stoney Creek to discuss a marijuana-growing business at the Arven Ave. building Li allegedly claimed her mother owned.
Instead, Pratt was shot six times. "Jordyn Romano was also supposed to die that day," Kim told the court. "Miraculously, she survived being shot in the heart, and missed being killed by two other shots fired into the car where she was seated."
Karafa, the Crown said, was the triggerman.
Pratt had recently moved from B.C. to southern Ontario. He allegedly told his new pals that he had made a boatload of money in the dope trade.
According to prosecutors, Karafa allegedly convinced Pratt to invest $470,000 in a personal protection business in Europe. Pratt was expecting a payday the day following his slaying.
After the shootings, the Crown said, Karafa and Li bought an Audi for $10,000 and fled north, then to Montreal where they caught a flight to Frankfurt. Next in their odyssey was his home country of Slovakia, and finally Budapest, where they were captured six months later.
Romano is expected to spend the day testifying Thursday. bhunter@postmedia.com

HUNTER: Witness tells Hamilton murder trial how she found wounded victim

Author: Hunter, Brad

Publication info: Hunter, Brad. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hunter-witness-tells-hamilton-murder-trial-how/docview/3037096039/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=HUNTER%3A+Witness+tells+Hamilton+murder+trial+how+she+found+wounded+victim&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Hunter%2C+Brad&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: HAMILTON - Annette Bright sensed something wasn't right in a lonely Stoney Creek industrial park.
It was between 7:20 and 7:25 p.m. on Sunday, Feb. 28, 2021.
"[I thought to myself] something is not right," she told a Hamilton court on Wednesday, adding that a "sickening" feeling came over her whole body.
Bright turned her vehicle around and realized a young woman was lying on the road. It appeared she had crawled and was on her knees.
Oliver Karafa, 28, left, and Yun (Lucy) Lu Li, 25.
"I asked her, 'what's wrong?' and she said 'help me'," Bright said. "She wanted to get into my car, and said she was cold. I said 'no'. I asked myself, 'is this for real?'.
"It was real."
The young woman - now identified as Jordyn Romano - told Bright she had been shot.
"She was frantic and cold and there was blood on her shirt, the middle of the shirt was covered in blood."
Yun Lu "Lucy" Li, 25, is accused of first-degree murder in the Feb. 28, 2021 shooting in Stoney Creek that killed Tyler Pratt, 39, and wounded a 26-year-old woman. (lucy.lucy.lucy.li/Facebook)
Bright was the first witness called in the high-profile first-degree murder trial of Oliver Karafa and Lucy Li. It is a homicide case that has drawn worldwide attention.
The daughter of a wealthy businesswoman with deep ties to the Liberal Party, Li and boyfriend Karafa are charged with first-degree murder and attempted murder in the Stoney Creek shooting of Tyler Pratt, 39, and Romano, then 26.
Romano had been shot in the heart. She survived. Her unborn baby did not.
Karafa and Li have pleaded not guilty.
Tyler Pratt was shot and killed in Hamilton in 2021.
On Wednesday, the alleged murderers arrived in matching black suits that looked as though they were off the rack. Karafa wore a black sweater under his suit jacket and a white, button-down shirt.
Recommended video
Li wore a cream-coloured, form-fitting sweater underneath her black blazer. He was attentive; she looked lost.
The court also heard testimony from two Hamilton paramedics who were among the first responders and a warehouse supervisor of the company where Pratt's body was found.
At times, Bright appeared poised to unleash a flood of tears. Even Li's defence lawyer, Liam O'Connor, offered heartfelt kudos to Bright for stopping to help the critically injured Romano.
On Tuesday, assistant Crown attorney Steven Kim outlined the case against Karafa and Li in his opening address.
Karafa and Li had become friends with Pratt and his fiancee, Romano, in October 2021 after being introduced by a mutual acquaintance.
On the night of the shootings, the four were allegedly at 347 Arvin Ave. in Stoney Creek to discuss a marijuana-growing business at the Arven Ave. building that Li allegedly claimed her mother owned.
Instead, Pratt was shot six times.
"Jordyn Romano was also supposed to die that day," Kim told the court. "Miraculously, she survived being shot in the heart, and missed being killed by two other shots fired into the car where she was seated."
Karafa, the Crown said, was the trigger man.
Pratt had recently moved from British Columbia to southern Ontario. He allegedly told his new pals that he had made a boatload of money in the dope trade.
According to prosecutors, Karafa allegedly convinced Pratt to invest $470,000 in a personal protection business in Europe. Pratt was expecting a payday the day following his slaying.
Following the shootings, the Crown said, Karafa and Li bought an Audi for $10,000 and fled north, then to Montreal where they caught a flight to Frankfurt, Germany. Next in their odyssey was his home country of Slovakia, and finally Budapest, Hungary, where they were captured six months later.
On Thursday, Romano is expected to spend the day testifying.
bhunter@postmedia.com
@HunterTOSun
