Document 96 of 356

Letters to the Editor, April 10, 2024

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/letters-editor-april-10-2024/docview/3035265074/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+to+the+Editor%2C+April+10%2C+2024&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: PRODUCTIVITY PROBLEMS
As Dennis Miller used to say, " I don't want to go off on a rant here," but is it possible that our problem with low productivity coincides with the fact that our economy and governments - together with their institutions - are much more heavily unionized than our neighbours to the south. This isn't an opinion against the need to secure fair wages, but an opinion that questions the unionized bargaining structure to secure those wages. In addition to prolonged strikes that require political intervention, union bargaining strategies often put forward a demand for better wages for doing the same or less work. Is it possible that securing more days off, improved sick leave, emergency leave, and perhaps a four-day work week might impact our productivity and hence our standard of living negatively?
Doug Appt Niagara Falls, Ont.
(Certainly this would have an impact on productivity)
SPENDING PROBLEMS
Re "Get priorities right" (Brian Lilley, April 3): Brian Lilley has correctly defined this issue. This federal government is interfering in provincial affairs by spending Canadian taxpayer money to solve a problem. But the feds are aggravating the problem. Justin Trudeau's government is desperately looking for a response to Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's suggestion to create incentives and disincentives for gate-keeper municipal bureaucrats with existing federal funds. Trudeau wants to pour on more Canadian taxpayer cash because that's all he knows. When you are a hammer, every problem is a nail. But this plan is like bringing out a bigger bucket when all he needs to do is turn off the tap.
Larry Sylvester Acton
(Trudeau has put little to no thought to the finances of our country. He's only interested in spending our money recklessly to try to secure an election win)
BASED ON MERIT
I wish Ontario Premier Doug Ford would be as brave as Ron DeSantis, Greg Abbot and other U.S. governors. They have taken steps to ban the outright racist extremes of DEI (diversity, equity and inclusion). They have forced their schools to promote merit-based education and fire every DEI employee they can find. Would you rather have a surgeon or pilot who was hired based on merit or on DEI. I am all for merit. I hope the Conservatives wake up and realize this is a winning strategy.
Gordon Skinner Uxbridge
(Merit wins. But don't hold your breath that Ford will do something like this)

Document 97 of 356

Because it's (not) 2016!; Meeting with premiers is so last decade

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/because-not-2016-meeting-with-premiers-is-so-last/docview/3034911970/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Because+it%27s+%28not%29+2016%21%3B+Meeting+with+premiers+is+so+last+decade&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Six premiers have now written Prime Minister Justin Trudeau asking for a meeting to discuss the carbon tax. Trudeau's response has been to say there's no need for a meeting, since he already had one with premiers - in 2016.
Strange thing, though, not only was that meeting eight years ago, none of the people now serving as premier were in office at the time.
Brad Wall, who was premier of Saskatchewan in 2016, said in a social media post he remembers that meeting in March 2016 very well. He said Trudeau and the premiers agreed not to impose a consumer carbon tax until provinces were consulted.
Later that year, at a meeting of provincial, territorial and federal environment ministers, Wall's environment minister and premier, Scott Moe, walked out of the meeting in October 2016. So, too, did the Liberal environment ministers of both Nova Scotia and Newfoundland and Labrador.
Trudeau's unilateral decision to bring in the carbon tax while allegedly engaged in consultation with the provinces was also criticized by Alberta's then-NDP premier Rachel Notley.
Now we have six premiers who have written to Trudeau asking for a meeting, including Andrew Furey the Liberal premier of Newfoundland and Labrador, and Trudeau falls back on a meeting eight years ago at which he acted in bad faith. Trudeau is trying to claim it's only conservative premiers and conservative politicians who are pushing back against the carbon tax.
That claim discounts the letter from Furey and a request from a seventh premier, Manitoba's Wab Kinew, a New Democrat, who has asked that the carbon tax be removed in his province.
"Canadians expect us to work together, and it's time for you to come to the table," Moe said in his letter to Trudeau.
That was a sentiment echoed by Ontario Premier Doug Ford, who has worked well and closely with Trudeau over the past several years.
"It's time we came together as leaders to address the very real affordability concerns being expressed by Canadians and do everything we can to help them keep costs down," Ford wrote.
Trudeau is having none of it, though. He is trying hard to defend his carbon tax against premiers across the country and of all political stripes. He is pushing back at the 69% of Canadians who, according to a Leger poll, opposed Trudeau's decision to increase the carbon tax on April 1.
Trudeau continues to claim that eight out of 10 families get more money due to the carbon tax.
"If you have a giant mansion and an indoor swimming pool and three big personal cars, it might not cover all of that, but for regular families that are hardworking, it puts more money in their pockets," Trudeau said.
That statement ignores the report and the recent comments from the non-partisan Parliamentary Budget Officer, who has stated multiple times that when all factors are considered on the carbon tax, most families are worse off.
You will pay more, you will earn less, and Trudeau says that will make you better off.
In his 2016 meeting with the premiers, Trudeau made a promise he did not keep, saying he wouldn't impose a solution. He also told the country before the 2019 election that his government had no intention of raising the tax beyond $50 a tonne. It's now $80 and climbing.
Now to put off having another meeting with a new batch of premiers, he's telling the fib that we are better off. The only way we will be better off is when he leaves office. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Carbon; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax

Location: Canada; Newfoundland & Labrador Canada

Document 98 of 356

LILLEY: Trudeau refuses to meet premiers on carbon tax, they met in 2016

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-trudeau-refuses-meet-premiers-on-carbon/docview/3034822457/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Trudeau+refuses+to+meet+premiers+on+carbon+tax%2C+they+met+in+2016&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Six premiers have now written Prime Minister Justin Trudeau asking for a meeting to discuss the carbon tax. Trudeau's response has been to say there's no need for a meeting, since he already had one with premiers - in 2016.
Strange thing, though, not only was that meeting eight years ago, none of the people who are now serving as premier were in office at the time.
Brad Wall, who was premier of Saskatchewan in 2016, said in a social media post that he remembers that meeting in March 2016 very well. He said that Trudeau and the premiers agreed not to impose a consumer carbon tax until provinces were consulted.
Later that year, at a meeting of provincial, territorial and federal environment ministers, Wall's environment minister and premier, Scott Moe, walked out of the meeting in October 2016. So, too, did the Liberal environment ministers of both Nova Scotia and Newfoundland and Labrador.
Trudeau's unilateral decision to bring in the carbon tax while allegedly engaged in consultation with the provinces was also criticized by Alberta's then-NDP premier Rachel Notley.
Now we have six premiers who have written to Trudeau asking for a meeting, including Andrew Furey the Liberal premier of Newfoundland and Labrador, and Trudeau falls back on a meeting eight years ago where he acted in bad faith. Trudeau is trying to claim that it's only conservative premiers and conservative politicians who are pushing back against the carbon tax.
That claim discounts the letter from Furey and a request from a seventh premier, Manitoba's Wab Kinew, a New Democrat, who has asked that the carbon tax be removed in his province.
Recommended video
"Canadians expect us to work together, and it's time for you to come to the table," Moe said in his letter to Trudeau.
That was a sentiment echoed by Ontario Premier Doug Ford, a man who has worked well and closely with Trudeau over the past several years.
"It's time we came together as leaders to address the very real affordability concerns being expressed by Canadians and do everything we can to help them keep costs down," Ford wrote.
Trudeau is having none of it, though; he is trying hard to defend his carbon tax against premiers across the country and of all political stripes. He is pushing back at the 69% of Canadians who, according to a Leger poll, opposed Trudeau's decision to increase the carbon tax on April 1.
Trudeau continues to claim that eight out of 10 families get more money due to the carbon tax.
"If you have a giant mansion and an indoor swimming pool and three big personal cars, it might not cover all of that, but for regular families that are hardworking, it puts more money in their pockets," Trudeau said.
That statement ignores the report and the recent comments from the non-partisan Parliamentary Budget Officer, who has stated multiple times that when all factors are considered on the carbon tax, most families are worse off.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford implored Prime Minister Justin Trudeau not to institute the April 1 carbon tax increase on Canadians.
"When both fiscal and economic impacts of the federal fuel charge are considered, we estimate that most households will see a net loss," Parliamentary Budget Officer Yves Giroux said in a report released last March.
"Based on our analysis, most households will pay more in fuel charges and GST - as well as receiving slightly lower incomes - than they will receive in Climate Action Incentive payments."
You will pay more, you will earn less, and Trudeau says that will make you better off.
In his 2016 meeting with the premiers, Trudeau made a promise he did not keep, saying he wouldn't impose a solution. He also told the country before the 2019 election that his government had no intention of raising the tax beyond $50 a tonne, it's now $80 and climbing.
Now to put off having another meeting with a new batch of premiers, he's telling the fib that we are better off. The only way we will be better off is when he leaves office.
LILLEY: Economists' support for federal carbon tax seems like a lot of hot air
LILLEY: Trudeau Liberals double down on hiking carbon tax even higher

Subject: Canadians; Carbon; Prime ministers; Tax increases; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax increases Environmental tax

Document 99 of 356

LILLEY: Ford orders LCBO to bring back paper bags. It's about time!

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-ford-orders-lcbo-bring-back-paper-bags/docview/3034641113/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Ford+orders+LCBO+to+bring+back+paper+bags.+It%27s+about+time%21&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bring back the paper bags!
That's the message from Ontario Premier Doug Ford to the LCBO. Ford has written to LCBO President and CEO George Soleas, telling him to reverse course on his bag policy.
The LCBO announced they were phasing out paper bags last August, instead only offering customers the chance to buy recyclable bags or bring their own.
"This initiative will help to minimize our impact on the environment and create a more sustainable Ontario," the government agency said in a statement.
Environmental concerns or not, Ford wants the policy reversed.
"I'm requesting that you take immediate steps to reverse the decision to remove paper bags from the LCBO's retail locations," Ford wrote.
"At a time when many Ontario families are already struggling to make ends meet, every additional expense counts. That includes charging customers for reusable bags instead of the free paper bags that the LCBO previously offered."
Those reusable bags the LCBO sells go for $1.25 for a bag to carry two bottles, or $2.95 for one to carry six. They also sell a four-pocket organic cotton bag for $14.95 which is not overly affordable, nor is it environmentally friendly.
A man holds a paper LCBO bag on Aug. 22, 2023. (Postmedia News)
For Ford, his call for the LCBO to bring back paper bags is mostly about affordability.
"People rightly expect their government - and, by extension, crown corporations such as the LCBO - to be mindful of these costs and refrain from imposing additional and unnecessary burdens on them," Ford said.
Recommended video
He's right, I just wish it hadn't taken him seven months to tell the LCBO they were on the wrong track.
The bags the LCBO now sells are made from plastic, recycled water bottles, but aren't recyclable themselves unlike the old paper bags - a point Ford makes in his letter.
"Moreover, the environmental merits of this decision are questionable at best. Paper bags are an easily recyclable alternative to single-use plastic, which is why the LCBO adopted them in the first place," Ford wrote.
Friendly reminder that as of today we are no longer supplying paper bags at checkout. Don't forget to bring your own bag, or purchase one of our reusable bags. Learn more about our sustainability efforts here:
- LCBO (@LCBO) September 5, 2023
A study by Denmark's Ministry of the Environment and Food found the kind of plastic bags the LCBO is selling would have to be reused eight times to be better than a plastic shopping bag when it comes to climate change. However, the LCBO's plastic bags would need to be used 84 times to be better on all environmental indicators.
That organic cotton bag they are selling would need to be used a whopping 20,000 times to be better than plastic when all environmental factors are considered.
Except, that's comparing the reusable bags to plastic bags, whereas the LCBO was previously using paper - an easily recycled product. It's been well documented that we now have a glut of reusable plastic bags in the world, leading many to question whether this is helping or hurting
We all want a cleaner environment; I don't know a single person who wants to be polluting, but we aren't always smart about policy. This LCBO policy was an attempt by the government agency to look like a good corporate steward, a leader in environmental and sustainability goals.
It hasn't worked out that way and it has definitely inconvenienced consumers.
"As a government, we are focused on making life easier, more convenient, and more affordable for the people of Ontario. The decision to remove paper bags has had the opposite effect," Ford wrote to Soleas.
What will be interesting to find out is how much money the LCBO has been making off the plastic bags they have been selling. The paper bags were obviously a cost to them, the plastic ones a potential profit centre.
I'll still take my reusable bag with me when I plan a trip, but I'll also welcome the return of the paper bags for those times when I pop into the LCBO unexpectedly.
This is a common sense move, and a welcome one.
LILLEY: Trudeau's out-of-control immigration hurting jobs and housing
LILLEY UNLEASHED: Did Ontario's budget spend too much? Too little?

Subject: Environmental impact; Recycling; Plastics; Climate change

Folding on paper; Ford orders LCBO to bring back brown bags

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/folding-on-paper-ford-orders-lcbo-bring-back/docview/3034604885/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Folding+on+paper%3B+Ford+orders+LCBO+to+bring+back+brown+bags&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

This is a common-sense move, and a welcome one. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Document 101 of 356

Sinking Liberal fortunes prompting NDP MPs to jump ship

Author: Gunter, Lorne

Publication info: Gunter, Lorne. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sinking-liberal-fortunes-prompting-ndp-mps-jump/docview/3034088654/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sinking+Liberal+fortunes+prompting+NDP+MPs+to+jump+ship&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=Gunter%2C+Lorne&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In Alberta, 1993 was mockingly referred to as the 'Year of the Family'for Conservative MPs.
At the end of the Mulroney era, with Kim Campbell as their unpopular new leader, the province's incumbent Tory MPs could see the writing on the wall. With Preston Manning and the Reform party on the rise, many sitting Progressive Conservatives imagined it was time to leave politics before voters tossed them out.
In the months leading up to the October 1993 vote, one Tory after another announced they were leaving politics so they could spend more time with their families, hence the sarcastic nickname the 'Year of the Family.'Indeed, the Conservatives ended up losing every seat in the province.
Now it appears the federal NDP may be headed for its own 'Year of the Family.'Following a weekly caucus meeting in Ottawa on Wednesday, which people in attendance described as "chaotic," three veteran NDP MPs announced they would not be seeking re-election whenever the next election rolls around.
Two long-standing NDP MPs, Charlie Angus and Carol Hughes, both of whom represent northern Ontario ridings, are leaving, in part, because their ridings are being redistricted to make it harder for them to be re-elected and easier for the local Liberal candidates to unseat them.
Meanwhile, the third, Rachel Blaney from Vancouver Island, is leaving to - you guessed it - "focus on my family and stay closer to home."
A fourth New Democrat MP, Daniel Blaikie of Winnipeg, announced a week earlier that he was leaving office to take a job advising new Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew and because he's "been hoping to find a way to spend more time with my young kids and with my wife."
Being an MP, especially one who has to fly across the country every week to Ottawa and back, is tough on a family. Quitting to spend more time back home is a legitimate reason. However, it's also a convenient cover for, "I was going to be handed my electoral head on a platter anyway, so..."
The 'Year of the Family'announcements, resulting from this week's fractious NDP caucus meeting, may spell the beginning of the end of the NDP's cozy relationship with the Trudeau Liberals.
Apparently in caucus, frustrations came to a head over NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh's willingness to go along with anything and everything the Liberal government does.
There is no real pharmacare deal. The national daycare strategy created by the Trudeau government (at NDP urging) has been a flop. Housing costs continue to soar, whether rents or mortgages. And the Liberals have been plagued by one scandal after another.
In return for propping up the Grits and saving them from an election, an increasing number of NDP MPs have reportedly concluded their party is receiving too little in return.
It also doesn't help that poll after poll shows the NDP has gained nothing from its "confidence and supply" coalition with Trudeau. If an election were held today, the NDP would win just about the same number of seats as it currently holds (24).
Some members are said to be losing faith in Singh's leadership, especially because young voters, who are leaving the Liberals en masse, are shifting right to Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives and not left to the NDP. There is seen to be too little a difference between Singh and Trudeau on major issues.
One NDP strategist even accused Singh of lashing the NDP to the mast of a sinking Liberal ship.
When word seeped out of the NDP's heated caucus, even Trudeau's office was said to be worried about the implications.
Can it be too much longer before the NDP caucus finally forces Singh to pull the plug on the Liberal minority? lgunter@postmedia.com @sunlornegunter

Subject: Leadership; Voters

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Identifier / keyword: alberta; mockingly; referred; conservative; mulroney; campbell

Title: Sinking Liberal fortunes prompting NDP MPs to jump ship

Document 102 of 356

Letters to the Editor, April 7, 2024

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/letters-editor-april-7-2024/docview/3034069956/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+to+the+Editor%2C+April+7%2C+2024&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: AN ELECTION ISSUE
Justin Trudeau drastically raised immigrant intake numbers to the highest in decades, bringing in several million in just a few years, plus tens of thousands refugees. Housing was not Trudeau's concern at that time. Only now that Opposition Leader Poilievre has made it an election issue and Trudeau's poll numbers are in the dumper is he talking of throwing $6 billion at housing. Not only has housing been negatively impacted by Trudeau's mass immigration policy, but also our ailing healthcare system as millions of Canadians already were unable to get a family doctor and dealing with unhealthy wait times.
Larry Comeau
Ottawa
(It's a mess his government created. No one should believe the Liberals can or will fix it)
STOP THE HUNT
Last year, Ontario expanded dog hunting, a cruel bloodsport. Hunters train dogs to chase and maul foxes, coyotes and rabbits in enclosed areas. These animals have no chance of escaping and are often killed. Despite laws phasing out dog hunting pens since 1997, the province allowed their return. In "train and trial" competitions, terrified coyotes are kept in large, outdoor cages, forced to run for their lives while dogs pursue them. Participants replace coyotes killed by dogs. Ontarians rallied against this, but the fight continues. This is outright cruelty.
Alexandria Walker
(We will look into it)
SCANDALOUS DATE CHANGE
I am not fully aware of our Canadian election rules, but how can the federal government, a minority government, just decide to extend the next election date by a week so MPs can achieve their six-year requirement to take a pension on the backs of Canadians? Canada's national debt is approximately $1.2 trillion with annual interest payments of $47.1 billion thanks to the ever-spending liberals and the coalition-supporting NDP. I am a senior and I am tired of my taxes paying incompetent leaders to kill our economy and then take a fat pension.
Gord Peters
Chestermere, Alberta
(It's appalling what the the Liberals did with the help of the NDP)

Subject: Dogs

Identifier / keyword: election; issue; justin; trudeau; drastically; raised

Title: Letters to the Editor, April 7, 2024

Document 103 of 356

GUNTER: Sinking Liberal fortunes prompting NDP MPs to jump ship

Author: Chau, Eddie

Publication info: Chau, Eddie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/gunter-sinking-liberal-fortunes-prompting-ndp-mps/docview/3034043199/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=GUNTER%3A+Sinking+Liberal+fortunes+prompting+NDP+MPs+to+jump+ship&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Chau%2C+Eddie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

The 'Year of the Family' announcements, resulting from this week's fractious NDP caucus meeting, may spell the beginning of the end of the NDP's cozy relationship with the Trudeau Liberals.
Apparently in caucus, frustrations came to a head over NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh's willingness to go along with anything and everything the Liberal government does.
There is no real pharmacare deal. The national daycare strategy created by the Trudeau government (at NDP urging) has been a flop. Housing costs continue to soar, whether rents or mortgages. And the Liberals have been plagued by one scandal after another.
In return for propping up the Grits and saving them from an election, an increasing number of NDP MPs have reportedly concluded their party is receiving too little in return.
It also doesn't help that poll after poll shows the NDP has gained nothing from its "confidence and supply" coalition with Trudeau. If an election were held today, the NDP would win just about the same number of seats as it currently holds (24).
Some members are said to be losing faith in Singh's leadership, especially because young voters, who are leaving the Liberals en masse, are shifting right to Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives and not left to the NDP. There is seen to be too little of a difference between Singh and Trudeau on major issues.
One NDP strategist even accused Singh of lashing the NDP to the mast of a sinking Liberal ship.
When word seeped out of the NDP's heated caucus, even Trudeau's office was said to be worried about the implications.
Can it be too much longer before the NDP caucus finally forces Singh to pull the plug on the Liberal minority?

Subject: Leadership; Voters

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Identifier / keyword: alberta; mockingly; referred; conservative; mulroney; campbell

Document 104 of 356

Not enough housing, jobs; Immigration numbers balloon out of control as system overloads

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/not-enough-housing-jobs-immigration-numbers/docview/3033973375/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Not+enough+housing%2C+jobs%3B+Immigration+numbers+balloon+out+of+control+as+system+overloads&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's out-of-control immigration numbers are not only having a negative impact on housing, they're now hurting the employment situation. In the same week that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said we're bringing in people faster than we can absorb them in the housing market, Statistics Canada issued a warning on jobs.
The March unemployment rate stood at 6.1%, a full point higher than the 5.1% recorded in March 2023. According to StatsCan, we're bringing in people faster than we're creating jobs.
Over the past year, employment has grown by 1.6% or 324,000 jobs, while Canada's population has grown by 3.2% or more than a million people considered working age. There are now 247,000 more people listed as unemployed in Canada than there were a year ago.
In Ontario, where the largest population growth has happened and where most of the immigrant population settles, there are 576,000 people unemployed, an increase of 134,000 compared to March 2023. In that same time frame, the province's unemployment rate has risen from 5.3% to 6.7% despite Ontario adding 86,000 new jobs in that time period.
Simply put, we're bringing in people faster than we can house them and faster than the economy can create adequate jobs. It's madness and it needs to be dealt with, but while the man in charge, Trudeau, is acknowledging there is a problem, he won't take any responsibility.
"In 2017, temporary residents made up about 2% of Canada's population. Right now, they make up about 71/2% of Canada's population. That's where all the pressure on our housing, or a big part of the pressure on our housing markets, and other areas are coming from," he said.
Temporary residents are part of the immigration system, that would be something the federal government is responsible for. Even if a college, university or province wants to have an international student enter the country, the final say, and this includes on how many can come, rests with the feds.
Listening to Trudeau, you'd think he and his government were powerless to do anything as these numbers climbed over the past several years. Standing with Sean Fraser, the man who messed up immigration and is now in charge of housing, Trudeau blamed the provinces for the more than 3 million temporary residents that his government allowed into the country.
Trudeau's solution? Reduce the temporary resident portion of our population down to 5% from the current 7.5%.
That is still not likely enough to solve the problem.
Trudeau is only now admitting immigration levels are too high, but as recently as November was responding to questions about lower housing costs in the United States by suggesting that Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre was anti-immigration.
"As for the difference between Canada and the United States, one of the differences is that our population is growing much faster than the population in the U.S. I'm certain the leader of the opposition wasn't about to suggest he was anti-immigration, because we all know immigration creates jobs and prosperity," Trudeau said.
Well, it used to create jobs and prosperity, but then the Liberals broke the system. They ramped up immigration levels, on all fronts, to unsustainable levels.
Our permanent resident intake was 431,645 in 2022 and we're on our way to 500,000.
We took in nearly 900,000 foreign students last year.
On the refugee front, we've seen a spike from 16,000 people seeking asylum in 2015 when Trudeau took office to 137,000 last year.
We're in the middle of a housing crisis - we don't have enough homes for the people who live here already and that is creating an affordability crisis.
Now, we're adding on a growing level of unemployment, which could soon be headed for crisis levels.
In the middle of all this, no sane country would admit a million new people in the space of nine months.
Under Trudeau, though, we don't have policy based in sanity or reality but on his feelings. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Unemployment; Job creation; Employment; Economic conditions; Immigration

Business indexing term: Subject: Unemployment Job creation Employment Economic conditions

Document 105 of 356

De Matteo's teen an anti-Fans; Sopranos star reveals how she shut down son criticizing her raunchy new career

Author: Daniell, Mark

Publication info: Daniell, Mark. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/de-matteos-teen-anti-fans-sopranos-star-reveals/docview/3033972900/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=De+Matteo%27s+teen+an+anti-Fans%3B+Sopranos+star+reveals+how+she+shut+down+son+criticizing+her+raunchy+new+career&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.27&amp;au=Daniell%2C+Mark&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Drea de Matteo had some choice words for her 12-year-old son, Waylon, when he tried to take shots at her OnlyFans career.
"Do you like that jacket you just asked me to buy you?" De Matteo, 52, recalled asking him on a recent episode of Club Random's The Sage Steele Show. "You like all the things that you want that make you happy? All your little things. You like that new PC that you begged for Christmas? Because you wouldn't have that s---if mommy didn't show her a--!" De Matteo continued, "If he says one thing about OnlyFans to me, all I have to do is be like, 'You like the Moncler jacket? You like that?'" When Steele asked The Sopranos star how her boy took her response, de Matteo laughed. "That shuts him up because he's a greedy little bastard, too, sometimes. I mean, he's 12, you know? ... He's a label whore."
While Waylon tried to criticize her foray into adult entertainment, de Matteo revealed that her daughter Alabama, 16, "doesn't care" and helps her with content creation on the site.
But de Matteo also recounted the moment she told her two children about her raunchy photoshoot with fellow OnlyFans star Carmen Electra. "I had to sit them down the other day and be like, 'Look, I did a collaboration with Carmen Electra and these pictures are racy - I'm not gonna lie' ... They are a 100% holy s---, I've never seen myself in a public photo like that."
For $15 per month, subscribers get access to uncensored shots of the mother of two who played Adriana, the girlfriend of Michael Imperioli's Christopher Moltisanti, for six seasons on The Sopranos. Other celebrities who have embraced the platform include Iggy Azalea, Denise Richards, Cardi B, Bella Thorne, Tyga, and Chris Brown.
De Matteo told Steele she doesn't know how many subscribers she has, but admitted that she had a lot of signups initially because people thought she'd be doing pornography. "It's really just a fan page ... that's what it is," she said.
De Matteo previously hailed her work on the platform as a lifesaver, revealing she paid off her mortgage debt in "five minutes" after starting her adults-only subscription service.
The Sons of Anarchy alum told the Daily Mail earlier this year she was down to her last $1,000 and was close to losing her home before she made the decision to join the website.
"It saved us," she admitted.
"OnlyFans saved my life, 100%. I can't believe I'm saying that, but it really did save us."
The actress, who lost work over her refusal to get the COVID jab, said she didn't want to join the site but added that she was out of options after the bank threatened to take her home.
"They put me into foreclosure and my house had flooded, so I was trying to sell the house quickly," she told the outlet. "I wanted to try and sell it before they took it."
De Matteo decided to join OnlyFans as a way to help re-establish her financial footing.
In her conversation with Steele, de Matteo said joining OnlyFans was "the last trick up my sleeve" to try and take care of her family as she was down to her "last $1,000" with a home no one would buy.
"People say a lot of mean things, like 'desperate.'Damn right I was desperate - I was f---ing desperate and I hope you're never desperate," she said.
"I got a lotta heat for the OnlyFans thing," she continued. "But I'm 50 ... my life is over."
But OnlyFans has allowed de Matteo the ability to start a whole new version of her life. Her kids like to joke, "Those boobies fed me when I was a little baby and they're still feeding me now."

People: Electra, Carmen

Document 108 of 356

Tax evaporates; City does about-face on rainwater levy

Author: Kinsella, Warren

Publication info: Kinsella, Warren. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tax-evaporates-city-does-about-face-on-rainwater/docview/3032864117/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tax+evaporates%3B+City+does+about-face+on+rainwater+levy&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Kinsella%2C+Warren&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Zap! You're gone! Take that, Rain TaxT! Just days after the Toronto Sun revealed the city's plan to impose a Rain TaxT - honestly, they were planning on doing that - municipal bureaucrats have executed a whiplashinducing about-face, and sort-of scrapped it.
And who says the
Sun doesn't get results? And make no mistake: This was no late April Fools' joke. The City of Toronto was, indeed, planning on slapping a new tax on you - for how much rainwater falls on your property. Which, the last time we checked, you don't really have much control over.
And which is, you know, good for the environment they always profess to be concerned about.
To recap: In a little-noticed release that read like something out of Monty Python, city mavens claimed they were seeking input on what they called the "Stormwater Charge & Water Service Charge Consultation."
Key parts of their "consultation" included these whoppers: n "Stormwater is rain and melted snow. When not absorbed into the ground, stormwater runs off hard surfaces, onto streets, down storm drains and through a network of pipes that carry it into local waterways." You know: The storm drains that your tax dollars already pay for. n "In urbanized areas like Toronto, there are a lot of hard surfaces. When severe storms happen, more stormwater runs off hard surfaces and enters the city's sewer system. Too much stormwater can overwhelm the city's sewer system, which can lead to flooded basements and impacts to surface water quality in Toronto's rivers, streams and Lake Ontario's waterfront." Get that? Rain is bad for - yes, they actually said that - Lake Ontario. n "The city would undertake individual assessments of each property using aerial photography to determine the hard surface area (square metres) on a property." That is the best one - the city was planning on deploying a fleet of drones (all NDP orange, perhaps) that would hover over your property and guesstimate how much rooftop you've got.
City of Toronto: Rain and rooftops, bad. More taxes, good.
Anyway, after the truth, er, seeped out, and the Sun revealed the shocking facts to our fellow tax-weary plebes, apparatchiks at City Hall paddled away, fast.
They posted this on their website this week: "Thank you for your interest in this consultation. Consultation has been paused to allow city staff to do further work to align the possible implementation of a stormwater charge with the city's broader climate resilience strategy, as well as the commercial parking levy being considered and the city's long-term financial plan. Future updates will be provided once available."
"Paused" ain't killed, fellow
Waterworld citizens! Will city staff try again after they "do further work" on their waterlogged scheme? The Sun is watching - and we'll be reporting back to a water-weary Gotham!

Document 109 of 356

'We see it every day'; World Stray Animal Day shines important light

Author: Nelles, Laura Shantora

Publication info: Nelles, Laura Shantora. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-see-every-day-world-stray-animal-shines/docview/3032863654/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+see+it+every+day%27%3B+World+Stray+Animal+Day+shines+important+light&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=Nelles%2C+Laura+Shantora&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Many of us have a pet at home, whether it's furry, scaly or feathered.
Animal companions bring us laughter, joy and unconditional love.
Unfortunately, not all pets have a safe place to call home.
April 4 is World Stray Animal Day, launched in 2010, to draw attention to the plight of homeless pets.
According to the Toronto Humane Society, more than 40,000 animals are surrendered by their owners or found as strays in Ontario every year.
Preventing your pet from becoming a stray starts with the basics: Ensure your pet's microchip contact info is current with your address and phone number. It's also important to license your pet. In Toronto, it's the law, all cats and dogs must be licensed.
"I know a lot of people don't want to have to pay additional money to get a pet licence, but that really is the intent of the licence, is to make sure we have up-to-date ownership information, and that we're able to get pets home," said Mike Mulick, animal services manager in Brampton.
If your pet becomes lost, Mulick says the first step is to call your local shelter and file a report.
"The first thing our staff do, even before they bring the animal back to the shelter, while they're still in the field is they'll look for lost reports in the area and try to hopefully find one that matches," Mulick said.
Secondly, ask your neighbours, knock on doors, post flyers and on social media.
"We find that most animals don't actually venture that far from where they're lost," he said. "We find that the average person doesn't ever think that their pet will get lost or run away, and they don't need a pet licence or a microchip ... but we see it every day and we're very strong proponents of, at the very least, having the microchip in your pet so there's some form of identification."
(Last year, 225 dogs were reunited with their families in Brampton, out of 388 stray dogs.)
Anyone who finds a lost pet should also report it to animal services, including the time, location and description of the animal. DANGERS OF RELEASING A PET While some strays are little houdinis who snuck out the back door, others are intentionally abandoned. Left in parks or green spaces, unwanted pets don't have survival skills to live outside. Mulick cites the two biggest concerns for abandoned pets are interactions with wildlife and vehicles.
Sarah Simpson, public relations and communications officer with Rouge National Urban Park, said the park, in the eastern GTA, located only two stray animals in 2019 and '20, but there were 18 cases each in 2022 and '23.
"Pets are not equipped to survive in the wild, at all. So, suddenly, they have to compete for food sources and territories with the native species, and this is often with poor outcomes. Parks Canada has recovered some animals in the park that were in very poor health, emaciated, frost-bitten, experiencing a high degree of stress, so it's a cruel fate for them to be released like this. And, in some cases, we're too late," she said.
Not only is abandoning a pet illegal and detrimental to its well-being, animals, such as red-eared slider turtles, can become invasive, competing with native endangered species for food and resources.
Simpson said if any visitors to the park encounter an abandoned pet, don't approach it, as it's likely highly stressed and its behaviour may be unpredictable.
"Contact Rouge Visitors Services, let us know where they saw it, what kind of animal, as much information as possible."
Call 416-264-2020 or email rouge@pc.gc.ca to report any pet sightings within Rouge Park. I CAN NO LONGER KEEP MY PET Mulick says there has been a 41% increase in stray dogs at the Brampton shelter over the past year, citing affordability as a concern for pet owners.
"A lot of the people that we have spoken to are starting to have a more difficult time affording their dogs and, unfortunately, they can end up becoming strays or let loose, and then we end up having to take them in. So, things like the rising interest rates, inflation, just general cost of living and the cost of pet care going up - it can cause situations where people feel like their only option is to let their animal go, hoping that it eventually comes to the shelter."
While some owners may feel this is their only option, letting an animal loose contributes to shelter overcrowding.
"It kind of becomes a circle, unfortunately, where we can't necessarily take in every surrender because we don't have the space because we're housing stray animals. But then we can't take them in right away, and we're finding that it's also causing more stray animals." lnelles@postmedia.com

Subject: Parks & recreation areas; Semiconductors; Animals; Pets

Identifier / keyword: whether; furry; scaly; feathered; animal; companions

Title: 'We see it every day'; World Stray Animal Day shines important light

Publication title: The Toronto Sun; Toronto

Document 110 of 356

Doctor's orders; York U's new med school should help Ontario train, keep more health professionals

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/doctors-orders-york-us-new-med-school-should-help/docview/3032863596/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doctor%27s+orders%3B+York+U%27s+new+med+school+should+help+Ontario+train%2C+keep+more+health+professionals&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford admitted he was smiling big time as he walked to the podium on Wednesday morning. He was announcing the establishment of the second medical school in Ontario since he became premier.
"I can't even wipe the smile off my face," Ford said.
He had reason to be happy. His government was announcing the establishment of the York University school of medicine. It won't open until 2028, but when it does the school will focus on training the next generation of family physicians in Ontario.
Lest anyone think we have an abundance of medical schools in this province, we currently only have six and half of them - University of Toronto, Western University and Queen's University - opened in the 1800s. Since then, we added the University of Ottawa in 1945, McMaster University in 1965 and the Northern Ontario School of Medicine in 2005.
Once the York University and Toronto Metropolitan University schools of medicine open, we will have eight training doctors in this province. The unique feature of York is it will focus on training primary-care physicians - what most of us call our family doctor.
The big problem is, given the shortage of family doctors in Ontario and the large investment that both government and private donors are making in this school, how do we ensure those trained work here in this field? There is no way to force someone to work in a particular field of medicine short of signing them to a contract and paying for their tuition, much like the military. Short of that, we can't force someone to be a family doctor rather than going into a more lucrative specialization.
With the launch of the York University school of medicine, there will be an attempt to ensure students not only learn here but work here in this field.
"I want to support Ontario students," Ford said when asked about too many medical school spots going to international students.
Right now, about 18% of medical school spots in Ontario go to international students. Ford said he wants to make sure those spots go to Ontario students rather than forcing them to study at medical schools in Ireland, the Caribbean or Australia.
He said too often those students don't come back home to practise medicine after being forced to leave.
Health Minister Sylvia Jones said some of the keys to attracting and keeping more family doctors will be setting up more primarycare teams and establishing more opportunities to work in the field.
"As we expand primary care opportunities," Jones said, "we are seeing that there are many, many opportunities for students who want to practise and live in the province of Ontario."
Rhonda Lenton, president of York University, said the soon-tobe-established school of medicine would focus on recruiting those who want to work as family doctors.
"So you're attracting the students who want to be in primary care and then it's about the curriculum and our students will be getting extended opportunities to actually work in primary care," Lenton said.
She said due to specialization, other medical schools don't give much time for students to fully experience family medicine.
This school won't come online until 2028 and won't solve all of Ontario's problems, but it's a step in the right direction. After decades of being short doctors and importing them from all around the world, Ontario is on the path to sustainability.
In the grand scheme of things, the worst outcome is that we have too many doctors, which is better than too few, and start exporting them.
Ford had a reason to smile on Wednesday. Really, we all did. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Primary care; Foreign students; Medicine; Medical schools

Company / organization: Name: York University; NAICS: 611310

Identifier / keyword: premier; admitted; smiling; walked; podium; morning

'WE SEE IT EVERY DAY:' World Stray Animal Day shines important light on homeless pets

Author: Nelles, Laura Shantora

Publication info: Nelles, Laura Shantora. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-see-every-day-world-stray-animal-shines/docview/3031881929/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 113 of 356

KINSELLA: City of Toronto does about-face on rainwater tax

Author: McLeod, Cynthia

Publication info: McLeod, Cynthia. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/kinsella-city-toronto-does-about-face-on/docview/3031699173/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=KINSELLA%3A+City+of+Toronto+does+about-face+on+rainwater+tax&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=McLeod%2C+Cynthia&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Zap! You're gone!
Take that, Rain TaxT!
Just days after the Toronto Sun revealed the city's plan to impose a Rain TaxT - honestly, they were planning on doing that - municipal bureaucrats have executed a whiplash-inducing about-face, and sort-of scrapped it.
And who says the Sun doesn't get results?
And, make no mistake: This was no late April Fools' joke. The City of Toronto was, indeed, planning on slapping a new tax on you - for how much rainwater falls on your property. Which, the last time we checked, you don't really have much control over.
And which is, you know, good for the environment they always profess to be concerned about.
To recap: In a little-noticed release that read like something out of Monty Python, city mavens claimed they were seeking input on what they called the "Stormwater Charge & Water Service Charge Consultation."
Key parts of their "consultation" included these whoppers:
- "Stormwater is rain and melted snow. When not absorbed into the ground, stormwater runs off hard surfaces, on to streets, down storm drains and through a network of pipes that carry it into local waterways." You know: The storm drains that your tax dollars already pay for.
- "In urbanized areas like Toronto, there are a lot of hard surfaces. When severe storms happen, more stormwater runs off hard surfaces and enters the city's sewer system. Too much stormwater can overwhelm the city's sewer system, which can lead to flooded basements and impacts to surface water quality in Toronto's rivers, streams and Lake Ontario's waterfront." Get that? Rain is bad for - yes, they actually said that - Lake Ontario.
- "The city would undertake individual assessments of each property using aerial photography to determine the hard surface area (m2) on a property." That is the best one - the city was planning on deploying a fleet of drones (all NDP orange, perhaps) that would hover your property and guesstimate how much rooftop you've got.
City of Toronto: Rain and rooftops, bad. More taxes, good.
Anyway, after the truth, er, seeped out, and the Sun revealed the shocking facts to our fellow tax-weary plebes, apparatchiks at City Hall paddled away, fast.
They posted this on their website this week: "Thank you for your interest in this consultation. Consultation has been paused to allow city staff to do further work to align the possible implementation of a stormwater charge with the city's broader climate resilience strategy, as well as the commercial parking levy being considered and the city's long-term financial plan. Future updates will be provided once available."
"Paused" ain't killed, fellow Waterworld citizens! Will the city try again after they "do further work" on their waterlogged scheme?
The Sun is watching - and we will be reporting back to a water-weary Gotham!

Subject: Sewer systems; Stormwater management; Planning; Rain

Business indexing term: Subject: Sewer systems

Location: Lake Ontario

Identifier / keyword: toronto; revealed; https; impose; honestly; planning

Title: KINSELLA: City of Toronto does about-face on rainwater tax

Section: Columnists

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 3031699173

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/kinsella-city-toronto-does-about-face-on/docview/3031699173/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 114 of 356

Grits running for the exits; Amid abysmal polling, almost half of party caucus ruling out another run

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/grits-running-exits-amid-abysmal-polling-almost/docview/3030924143/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Grits+running+for+the+exits%3B+Amid+abysmal+polling%2C+almost+half+of+party+caucus+ruling+out+another+run&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-04-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This past week, the deputy governor of the Bank of Canada said, "You know those signs that say, 'In an emergency, break the glass?'Well, it's time to break the glass." Carolyn Rogers was talking about the economy, but easily could have been talking about the Liberal party and their leadership problems.
Surely, people in senior positions at Liberal HQ can read the polls - all of them - and assess that things are not good.
The latest Ipsos poll has Pierre Poilievre and his Conservatives leading Justin Trudeau's Liberal nationally by 18 points. The Conservatives are at 41% nationally, compared with the Liberals at 23%. In Ontario the lead is 19 points, in British Columbia it's 20 points, and it's the same in Atlantic Canada.
It's not just Ipsos. The latest Abacus poll also has an 18-point lead for the Conservatives, Nanos gives the Conservatives a 15-point lead, Mainstreet a 20-point lead and Leger a 16-point lead. All these polls show the Conservatives leading across different age categories and in every region of the country other than Quebec.
Someone must have told Trudeau that it's time to go.
Well, in fact, I've been told several people have told him that his time is up - even close family have raised the issue. Depending on who you talk to, Trudeau is either listening and will leave, or is determined to stay to fight Poilievre.
None of us are in the room, none of us are in his head, so we don't know what he will do. What is clear is that many Liberal MPs are going to head for the exits and seek greener pastures outside of the world of politics.
Reportedly, as many as 70 Liberal MPs have decided not to run in the next election. That's 44% of the current caucus! This is not the kind of normal turnover due to retirement that we see in all parties ahead of a pending election, this is a stampede for the exits - not before passing Go and collecting their gold-plated MP's pension after six years of service, though.
Yes, Canadians have noticed that in an attempt to stay in power until October 2025, the Trudeau government will move the election back by one week. If the minority Parliament lasts until then, it will mean 80 MPs who were elected in 2019 will qualify for their pension.
Among the 80 are 32 Conservatives, 22 Liberals, 20 Bloc and six NDP. The Conservatives aren't worried about losing their seats right now, but the others are, so they will do what they can and vote for whatever is put in front of them to get those pensions, ranging from $32,000 to $49,000 per year.
It may not be enough to get rich on, but it's a nice cushion as you look for other work - and plenty of Liberals especially will be looking for new work.
The seat models for the current polling show the Conservatives winning more than 200 seats, but the Liberals could drop as many as 100. One longtime Liberal worries his party will be reduced to their strongholds in Toronto and Montreal, with just a smattering of seats outside of these two cities.
It's happened before. In 1984, when Brian Mulroney won the biggest majority ever, the Liberals were reduced to 40 seats. In 2011, when Stephen Harper won government and the NDP the opposition, the Liberals only wound up with 34 seats.
If the line continues, Trudeau could be leading his party into the same kind of embarrassing territory.
Someone in the Liberal party needs to break the glass. blilley@postmedia.com

Company / organization: Name: Ipsos; NAICS: 541910

LILLEY: With Trudeau's polling, it's time for Libs to break the glass

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-with-trudeaus-polling-time-libs-break/docview/3028179762/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+With+Trudeau%27s+polling%2C+it%27s+time+for+Libs+to+break+the+glass&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This past week, the deputy governor of the Bank of Canada said, "You know those signs that say, 'In an emergency, break the glass?' Well, it's time to break the glass." Carolyn Rogers was talking about the economy, but easily could have been talking about the Liberal Party and their leadership problems.
Surely, people in senior positions at Liberal HQ can read the polls - all of them - and assess that things are not good.
The latest Ipsos poll has Pierre Poilievre and his Conservatives leading Justin Trudeau's Liberal nationally by 18 points. The Conservatives are at 41% nationally, compared to the Liberals are at 23%, while in Ontario, the lead is 19 points, in British Columbia, it's 20 points, and it's the same in Atlantic Canada.
It's not just Ipsos: The latest Abacus poll also has an 18 point lead for the Conservatives; Nanos gives the Conservatives a 15-point lead; Mainstreet, a 20-point lead; and Leger, a 16-point lead All these polls show the Conservatives leading across different age categories and in every region of the country other than Quebec.
Someone must have told Trudeau that it's time to go.
Well, in fact, I've been told several people have told him that his time is up, even close family have raised the issue. Depending on who you talk to, Trudeau is either listening and will leave, or is determined to stay to fight Poilievre.
None of us are in the room, none of us are in his head, so we don't know what he will do. What is clear is that many Liberal MPs are going to head for the exits and seek greener pastures outside of the world of politics.
Reportedly, as many as 70 Liberal MPs have decided not to run in the next election, that's 44% of the current caucus!
This is not the kind of normal turnover due to retirement that we see in all parties ahead of a pending election, this is a stampede for the exits. Not before passing Go and collecting their gold-plated MPs pension after six years of service, though.
Recommended video
Yes, Canadians have noticed that in an attempt to stay in power until October 2025, the Trudeau government will move the election back by one week. If the minority Parliament lasts until that point, it will mean 80 current MPs who were elected in 2019 will qualify for their pension.
Among the 80 are 32 Conservatives, 22 Liberal, 20 Bloc and six NDP. The Conservatives aren't worried about losing their seats right now, but the others are, so they will do what they can, and vote for whatever is put in front of them to get those annual pensions, ranging from $32,000 to $49,000 per year.
It may not be enough to get rich on, but it's a nice cushion as you look for other work. And plenty of Liberals especially will be looking for new work.
Trudeau cares about provincial issues like childcare, pharmacare, rent issues.
He doesn't care about the military, national unity, foreign affairs, guiding the economy.
It's time he stepped down as PM and ran to be Premier of Quebec.
Read & RT #cdnpoli
- Brian Lilley (@brianlilley) March 30, 2024
The seat models for the current polling show the Conservatives winning more than 200 seats, but the Liberals could drop as many as 100 seats. One longtime Liberal worries that his party will be reduced to their strongholds in Toronto and Montreal, with just a smattering of seats outside of these two cities.

Someone in the Liberal Party needs to break the glass.

Document 116 of 356

Trudeau isn't a good egg; PM acknowledges most religious holidays - but not Good Friday

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-isnt-good-egg-pm-acknowledges-most/docview/3020709126/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+isn%27t+a+good+egg%3B+PM+acknowledges+most+religious+holidays+-+but+not+Good+Friday&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was a snub of biblical proportions, and it didn't go unnoticed by Canada's 19 million Christians.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, after all, would never miss offering good wishes to any other religion at the start of its most sacred holidays.
For Ramadan, he posted on X: "This Ramadan comes at an anxious time for the Muslim and Arab community. When I joined them for their Iftar in Vancouver, I heard their worries first-hand - about family and friends in Gaza, and about feeling vulnerable here at home - and I let them know they're supported."
On the Hindu holiday, he wrote: "Diwali reminds us that truth, light and goodness will always prevail. To celebrate that hopeful message and mark this important festival, I joined a virtual celebration earlier this evening. Happy Diwali to everyone celebrating!" In the past for the Sikhs'holiest holiday, he posted: "Vaisakhi is a time to focus on what matters - family, friends and being there for our neighbours" and wishing "Sikh Canadians celebrating a very happy Vaisakhi."
So what did Canada's PM post to honour Good Friday? Nothing. The only X post on the national holiday was about $10-a-day child care.
However, in fairness, Trudeau wasn't alone in not publicly marking the solemn day in which Jesus Christ was crucified.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, whose agreement with Trudeau keeps the minority government in power, also didn't put out a Good Friday message on social media.
Nor did Bloc Quebecois Leader Yves-Francois Blanchette.
Accidental? Well, if you consider that Christians have seen more than 100 churches in Canada vandalized or burned and, as the Christian Post reports, Bill C-367 is under review in the House of Commons, which would repeal religious exemption from Section 319 of the Canadian Criminal Code, it's understandable why some don't think this is a coincidence.
Critics of this private member's bill say it could open up Christians and other religious groups to "hate speech" charges over any comments or criticisms of the LGBT or abortion movements.
Others say it will never pass and is nothing to worry about.
But the Christian Post says Bill C-367 specifically targets Paragraph 319 3B and Paragraph 319 3-1B of the Criminal Code, which prohibits any conviction on hate speech charges if, "in good faith, the person expressed or attempted to establish by an argument an opinion on a religious subject or an opinion based on a belief in a religious text."
Certainly in Canada there should be no tolerance for discrimination of any religion, or for Islamophobia, anti-Semitism or any other attacks on faith.
But this has to include Christianity.
Many feel there is an attack on Christianity, even though it's still the faith close to half of Canadians observe. This may change in the years ahead, but for now it's a fact, even if the federal government doesn't always acknowledge it.
A good example of that was an X post by Veterans Affairs that showed some artificially coloured eggs being prepared for sailors with the words: "We want to wish veterans, current members of the Canadian Armed Forces and their families a happy March holiday season."
Christians call it Easter. While three federal party leaders, who could agree on a coalition to form the next government, didn't publicly acknowledge Good Friday, Opposition leader Pierre Poilievre - the man polls suggest will be the next PM - certainly did.
On X, Poilievre wrote: "A solemn Good Friday to all Christians commemorating the death of Jesus Christ" and quoted from John 3:16 "for God so loved the world that He gave his only Son, so that everyone who believes in Him may not perish but may have eternal life."
Ontario Premier Doug Ford didn't just post about it but took the time to do a video to express warm wishes on X, saying: "As Christians in Ontario observe Good Friday, we mourn the death of Jesus who died on the cross and remember the many sacrifices he made for us. Whether you're observing this day with family or at a religious service, I wish you a day filled with peace and reflection."
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, who disgustingly was shouted down this week at a special Iftar dinner she held at city hall in which she even respectfully wore a hijab, waited until 6:30 p.m. Friday to post a message on X with images of her greeting people at Little Italy's Station of the Cross parade.
But to her credit, she did attend and she did send out a nice message as well.
"I was honoured to join the St. Francis of Assisi Good Friday Procession in Little Italy this afternoon to mark Jesus'love and sacrifice," Chow's message states. "Today is an opportunity to reflect on the power of forgiveness and redemption. I wish all of Toronto a solemn and blessed day."
Politicians are in a tough spot because there are so many religions and ethnic groups in Canada and there is an unofficial expectation to make sure they honour them all on social media - but there is no law saying they have to.
And Trudeau, Singh and Blanchette exercised that very freedom by not sending out Good Friday social media messages this year.
Perhaps they'll change course on Sunday to honour Christ's Resurrection? Either way, Happy Easter, everybody! jwarmington@postmedia.com @joe_warmington

Subject: Holidays & special occasions; Religion; Ramadan; Hate speech; Christians; Christianity; Social networks

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks

Location: Italy; Canada

Document 117 of 356

Top clicks

Author: Wilford, Denette

Publication info: Wilford, Denette. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/top-clicks/docview/3020707774/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Top+clicks&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=Wilford%2C+Denette&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: WHO CAN TAX THE RAIN? If you were laughing along as you read Warren Kinsella's column this past week on the City of Toronto's possible rain tax, you weren't alone.
But it's no laughing matter as bureaucrats are pushing to have "a stormwater charge for all property classes" - meaning, everyone will ultimately pay for the invasive wet stuff.
Why? Well, because those pesky raindrops don't just soak into the grass and plant life (which was a good thing, we thought), but because it also dribbles down into the sewers (which were built to accommodate water that occasionally falls from the sky, but we digress), then it's no longer such a good thing.
Apparently "too much stormwater can overwhelm the city's sewer system, which can lead to flooded basements and impacts to surface water quality in Toronto's rivers, streams and Lake Ontario's waterfront."
Enter the wishy-washy rain tax.
GREECE SOURS ON TRUDEAU Germany, Japan, Israel, the United States, Great Britain and now Greece - it's a long list of Canadian allies that have been let down by Justin Trudeau.
Greek Prime Minister Kyriakos Mitsotakis was recently in Canada, where he expressed an interest in buying our country's liquefied natural gas, but no deal was struck thanks to the Trudeau Liberals, who've shown they aren't interested in selling Canada's LNG resources - because it could increase our greenhouse gas emissions.
But Brian Lilley noted that exporting LNG, especially to places still burning fuels like coal for electrical generation, would lower global emissions even if Canada's rose slightly.
The Liberals love to say that emissions know no borders, but they're steadfast in not increasing Canada's emissions even if it means lowering global emissions. LIVID OVER TRANS PLAYERS An Australian women's soccer club with five transgender players sparked outrage from parents and online critics after the team dominated a recent women's tournament.
Flying Bats FC not only won the Beryl Ackroyd Cup final over the Macquarie Dragons 4-0 in pre-season play but also took home a $1,000 prize, according to Denette Wilford.
The club went undefeated over the four-week tourney, even notching a 10-0 victory against one team.
That's the kind of score you see in kids'beginner leagues.
One soccer club official called out the competition, accusing the league of not being transparent so players don't know "if they are going to be playing biological males or not."
Tennis legend and women's sports activist Martina Navratilova pleaded once again on X to "keep women's sports female. It is not the place for any failed male athletes." 'INSANELY RICH'TAKE NHL analyst and podcast host Andrew Berkshire got ripped for sharing his opinion on Edmonton Oilers - and former Toronto Maple Leafs - winger Zach Hyman, Dan Bilicki reported.
In the awful video, Berkshire attributed Hyman's success to growing up "insanely rich."
Yeah, because money buys you a spot in the NHL.
Please note the sarcasm because that's exactly what many on social media retorted, namely former Leafs defenceman turned NHL analyst Carlo Colaiacovo, who thought the whole take was ridiculous.
- Denette Wilford

Subject: Womens sports; Emissions; Professional hockey

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: laughing; along; warren; column; possible; alone

Letters to the Editor, March 31, 2024

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/letters-editor-march-31-2024/docview/3020548031/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+to+the+Editor%2C+March+31%2C+2024&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: BONNIE'S CARBON TAX?
To those who might consider giving Bonnie Crombie the benefit of the doubt when she proclaims that she will not implement a carbon tax if the Liberals are voted in to power in the next provincial election, I would like to remind them of a similar promise made by a previous Liberal leader, Dalton McGuinty, who promised that he would not raise taxes if he won the election. McGuinty won and almost immediately brought in a health tax, levy, which we all still pay today. A carbon levy, Bonnie?
Doug Bernard
Thornhill
(A levy is a another term for a tax)
PRISON PLAN
I think I have an excellent idea for the Liberal Trudeau government. With Trudeau's 'Catch & Release' failing miserably and our lack of a sufficient military, I believe Trudeau could continue to adapt the Communist Chinese government ideals while raising tax dollars, pocketing more money and taking more lavish trips. In my opinion, if Trudeau turned our prisons into military training centres and our convicts (murderers, car thieves, drug dealers) into soldiers, if/when war were to break out, our prisons would no longer be overpopulated and our military would be well stocked with men/women. Given Trudeau's love for the Chinese Communist Party, I am sure he has considered this and is currently coming up with the lies to sell the idea to his Liberal voters as well as his NDP supporters.
Guy Zebedee
Belleville
(We realize this is a tongue in cheek suggestion, besides this idea would never fly with the public. Being in the military requires discipline and a duty to serve)
HATE CRIMES
I enjoyed reading Warren Kinsella's column "Red-Letters Day" (March 25). Those misguided people who are protesting on behalf of Hamas do not understand that Hamas is a terrorist organization. They attacked Israel without provocation. Israel has no choice but to go after them and do not rest until they are defeated. Those protesters would not be able to act as they do were they to go to Gaza and behave as abominably as they can here in Canada. They should be arrested and tried for hate crimes.
Sybil Fretz
Pickering
(It is becoming increasingly obvious that it never going to happen)
