Document 120 of 356

TOP CLICKS: The week that was in viral stories

Author: Wilford, Denette

Publication info: Wilford, Denette. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/top-clicks-week-that-was-viral-stories/docview/3020347129/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TOP+CLICKS%3A+The+week+that+was+in+viral+stories&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Wilford%2C+Denette&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Toronto Sun takes you straight to the heart of the action.
Whether it's local news, provincial and national politics, or the worlds of celebrity and sports, we have you covered.
Some stories set the world on fire. And these ones are the most popular online stories from the past seven days, clicked on by Sun readers like you.
Here are our top stories:
KINSELLA: Toronto's overlords pondering implementation of a rain tax
If you were laughing along as you read Warren Kinsella's column this week on the City of Toronto's possible rain tax, you weren't alone.
But it's no laughing matter as bureaucrats are pushing to have "a stormwater charge for all property classes" - meaning, everyone will ultimately pay for the invasive wet stuff.
Why? Well, because those pesky raindrops don't just soak into the grass and plant life (which was a good thing, we thought), but because it also dribbles down into the sewers (which were built to accommodate water that occasionally falls from the sky, but we digress), then it's no longer such a good thing.
Apparently "too much stormwater can overwhelm the city's sewer system, which can lead to flooded basements and impacts to surface water quality in Toronto's rivers, streams and Lake Ontario's waterfront."
Enter the wishy-washy rain tax.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Greek Prime Minister Kyriakos Mitsotakis (R) take part in a Greek Independence Day parade on March 24, 2024 in Montreal. (Dave Chan/AFP via Getty Images)
LILLEY: Greece is the latest Canadian ally disappointed by Trudeau
Germany. Japan. Israel. The United States. Great Britain. And now, Greece. It's a long list of Canadian allies that have been let down by Justin Trudeau's actions.
Greek Prime Minister Kyriakos Mitsotakis was recently in Canada where he expressed an interest in buying our country's liquified natural gas, but no deal was struck thanks to the Trudeau Liberals, who have shown they aren't interested in selling Canada's LNG resources - because it could increase our greenhouse gas emissions.
But Brian Lilley noted that exporting LNG, especially to places still burning fuels such coal for electrical generation, would lower global emissions even if Canada's rose slightly.
The Liberals love to say that emissions know no borders, but they are steadfast in not increasing Canada's emissions even if it means lowering global emissions.
Backs of Flying Bats FC. (The Flying Bats Women's Soccer Club/Facebook)
Parents livid after girls' soccer teams get annihilated by squad with five trans players
An Australian women's soccer club with five transgender players sparked outrage from parents and online critics after the team dominated a recent women's tournament.
Flying Bats FC not only won the Beryl Ackroyd Cup final over the Macquarie Dragons 4-0 in preseason play, but also took home a $1,000 prize, according to Denette Wilford.
The club went undefeated over the four-week tourney, even notching a 10-0 victory against one team.
That's the kind of score you see in kids beginner leagues.
One soccer club official called out the competition, accusing the league of not being transparent so players don't know "if they are going to be playing biological males or not."
Tennis legend and women's sports activist Martina Navratilova pleaded once again on X to "keep women's sports female. It is not the place for any (failed) male athletes."
In this photo illustration, a gas flame burns on a gas stove. (Sean Gallup/Getty Images)
SWIFT: The war on natural gas in Ontario needs to stop
Late last year, the Ford government stated they would overturn a decision by the Ontario Energy Board (OEB) to effectively discourage the attachment of new housing to the natural gas grid.
(Typically, when a new housing development is planned, the units will be hooked up to natural gas when they are built, which requires a great deal of planning and building out of the natural gas infrastructure, guest columnist Catherine Swift, president of the Coalition of Concerned Manufacturers and Businesses of Canada, pointed out.)
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
At 60%, natural gas is currently the most common source of heating in Ontario households but environmental groups influenced the OEB decision to change this approach and now require the developer to pay the entire cost of the attachment of the new housing to the gas grid up front.
But Ontario Energy Minister Todd Smith reacted quickly and put a stop to that with the Ford government introducing legislation last month called the Keeping Energy Costs Down Act.
Ontarians should be pleased that, through Ontario government legislation, their future energy choices are being left for them to make, not some climate groups that do not have their best interests at heart.
Edmonton Oilers' Zach Hyman (18) and Ryan Nugent-Hopkins (93) celebrate Hyman's game-winning goal against the Winnipeg Jets. (The Canadian Press)
NHL analyst gets absolutely roasted for 'insanely rich' take on Zach Hyman
NHL analyst/podcast host Andrew Berkshire got ripped for sharing his opinion on Edmonton Oilers (and former Toronto Maple Leafs) winger Zach Hyman, Dan Bilicki reported.
In the awful video Berkshire attributed Hyman's success to growing up "insanely rich."
Yeah, because money buys you a spot in the NHL.
Please note the sarcasm because that's exactly what many on social media retorted, namely former Leafs defenceman-turned-NHL analyst Carlo Colaiacovo, who thought the whole take was ridiculous.

WARMINGTON: Prime Minister acknowledges most religious holidays - except Good Friday

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/warmington-prime-minister-acknowledges-most/docview/3020342486/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=WARMINGTON%3A+Prime+Minister+acknowledges+most+religious+holidays+-+except+Good+Friday&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was a snub of biblical proportions that did not go unnoticed by Canada's 19 million Christians.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, after all, would never miss offering good wishes to any other religion at the start of their most sacred holidays.
For Ramadan he posted on X: "This Ramadan comes at an anxious time for the Muslim and Arab community. When I joined them for their Iftar in Vancouver, I heard their worries firsthand - about family and friends in Gaza, and about feeling vulnerable here at home - and I let them know they're supported."
On the Hindu holiday he wrote: "Diwali reminds us that truth, light, and goodness will always prevail. To celebrate that hopeful message and mark this important festival, I joined a virtual celebration earlier this evening. Happy Diwali to everyone celebrating!"
In the past for the Sikh's holiest holiday he has posted: "Vaisakhi is a time to focus on what matters - family, friends, and being there for our neighbours" and wishing "Sikh Canadians celebrating a very happy Vaisakhi."
This Ramadan comes at an anxious time for the Muslim and Arab community.
When I joined them for their Iftar in Vancouver, I heard their worries firsthand - about family and friends in Gaza, and about feeling vulnerable here at home - and I let them know they're supported.
- Justin Trudeau (@JustinTrudeau) March 29, 2024
So what did Canada's prime minister post to honour Good Friday?
Nothing.
The only X post on the national holiday was about $10-a-day child care.
LILLEY: Trudeau explains away arson attacks on churches
WARMINGTON: Sometimes the river sees one's life journey go from dark place to bright spaces
Church groups accuse feds of continued summer job grant discrimination: Report
However, in fairness, Trudeau was not alone in not publicly marking the solemn day in which Jesus Christ was crucified on the cross.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, whose agreement with Trudeau keeps the minority government in power, also didn't put out a Good Friday message on social media.
Nor did Bloc Quebecois Leader Yves-Francois Blanchette.
Accidental?
CMS Ss beautifully reenacted Jesus's final steps in a moving performance of the Stations of the Cross. Their dedication and faith was truly inspiring. Ty Gr. 6 teachers & Ss for your heartfelt performance.@ycdsb@TheresaMcnicol1
- Canadian Martyrs CES (@CMS_ycdsb) March 29, 2024
Well, if you consider that Christians have seen more than 100 churches in Canada vandalized or burned and, as the Christian Post reports, there is "the proposed Bill C-367? currently "under review in the House of Commons, that would repeal "religious exemption" in Section 319 of the Canadian Criminal Code, it's understandable why some don't think this is a coincidence.
Critics of this private members bill say it could open up Christians, and other religious groups, to "hate speech" charges over any comments or criticisms of the LGBT or abortion movements. Others say it will never pass and is nothing to worry about it.
Canadian Bill C-367 could send Christian's to jail for just quoting the Bible. The only people that won't be arrested will be progressive and woke "Christians."
"If you try to develop a Christianity that's not offensive, it's not Christianity."
- John MacArthur @DrJoeBoot
- Tom Mammen (@TomMammen) March 28, 2024
But the Christian Post states Bill C-367 specifically targets Paragraph 319(3)?(b) and Paragraph 319(3.?1)?(b) of the Criminal Code, which prohibits any conviction on hate speech charges if "in good faith, the person expressed or attempted to establish by an argument an opinion on a religious subject or an opinion based on a belief in a religious text."
Certainly in Canada there should be no tolerance for discrimination of any religion, Islamophobia, anti-Semitism or any other attacks on faith.
But this has to included Christianity.
We want to wish Veterans, current members of the @CanadianForces and @rcmpgrcpolice and their families a happy March holiday season! ???#CanadaRemembers
- Veterans Affairs CA (@VeteransENG_CA) March 29, 2024
Many feel there is an attack on Christianity - even though it is still the faith close to 50% of Canadians observe. This may change in the years ahead but for now it's a fact, even if the federal government does not always acknowledge it.
A good example of that was an X post by Veteran's Affairs that showed some artificially coloured eggs being prepared for sailors with the message, "We want to wish veterans, current members of the Canadian Armed Forces and their families a happy March holiday season."
Christians call it Easter.
While the three federal party leaders, which could agree on a coalition to form the next government, did not publicly acknowledge Good Friday, Opposition Leader and poll leader to be the next prime minister Pierre Poilievre certainly did.
"For God so loved the world that He gave his only Son, so that everyone who believes in Him may not perish but may have eternal life."
-John 3:16
A solemn Good Friday to all Christians commemorating the death of Jesus Christ.
-
"Car Dieu a tant aimé le monde qu'il a donné son...
- Pierre Poilievre (@PierrePoilievre) March 29, 2024
On X Poilievre posted: " A solemn Good Friday to all Christians commemorating the death of Jesus Christ" and quoted from John 3:16 "for God so loved the world that He gave his only Son, so that everyone who believes in Him may not perish but may have eternal life."
Ontario Premier Doug Ford didn't just post about it but took the time to do a video to express warm wishes on X, saying "as Christians in Ontario observe Good Friday, we mourn the death of Jesus who died on the cross and remember the many sacrifices he made for us. Whether you're observing this day with family or at a religious service, I wish you a day filled with peace and reflection."
As Christians in Ontario observe Good Friday, we mourn the death of Jesus who died on the cross and remember the many sacrifices he made for us.
Whether you're observing this day with family or at a religious service, I wish you a day filled with peace and reflection.
- Doug Ford (@fordnation) March 29, 2024
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, who was grossly shouted down this week at a special Iftar dinner she held at City Hall in which she even respectfully wore a hijab, waited until 6:30 p.m. Friday to post a message on X with images of herself greeting people at little Italy's Station of the Cross parade.
But to her credit, she did attend and she did send out a nice message as well.
I was honoured to join the St. Francis of Assisi Good Friday Procession in Little Italy this afternoon to mark Jesus' love and sacrifice.
Today is an opportunity to reflect on the power of forgiveness and redemption. I wish all of Toronto a solemn and blessed day.
- Mayor Olivia Chow (@MayorOliviaChow) March 29, 2024
"I was honoured to join the St. Francis of Assisi Good Friday Procession in Little Italy this afternoon to mark Jesus' love and sacrifice," Chow's message states. "Today is an opportunity to reflect on the power of forgiveness and redemption. I wish all of Toronto a solemn and blessed day."
Politicians are in a tough spot because there are so many religions and ethnic groups in Canada and there is an unofficial expectation to make sure they honour them all on social media. But there is no law saying they have to do so.
And Trudeau, Singh and Blanchette exercised that very freedom by not sending out Good Friday social media messages this year.
Perhaps they will change course on Sunday to honour Christ's resurrection?
Either way, Happy Easter everybody.
jwarmington@postmedia.com

Document 122 of 356

Tapping taxpayers; Ford gives every caucus member - but one - a raise with latest shuffle

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tapping-taxpayers-ford-gives-every-caucus-member/docview/3020293679/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tapping+taxpayers%3B+Ford+gives+every+caucus+member+-+but+one+-+a+raise+with+latest+shuffle&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Call it jobs for the boys, and the girls, who make up Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative Party caucus.
Ontario's premier is responsible for a 78-member caucus and somehow has found a way to give all but one of his MPPs additional roles that come with extra money.
In addition to Ford's bloated 30-member cabinet, there are also 38 parliamentary assistants.
On Thursday, just before the Easter weekend, Ford shuffled the deck chairs for these assistants, which isn't something that would ever be of interest in the general public except for the extra cost.
Members of the Ontario Legislative Assembly are paid a base salary of $116,550, an amount that hasn't gone up since then-premier Dalton McGuinty froze MPP salaries in 2008 in the face of the financial crisis.
To get around this lack of pay increases, governments have increasingly provided their members with these plumb postings, but Ford's current situation is next level.
When MPPs are appointed to serve as parliamentary assistants, their pay is bumped up to roughly $140,000, meaning the 38 PAs serving under Ford cost taxpayers close to $900,000 more. Committee chairs earn an additional $16,000, the PCs currently chair six of the eight committees and are co-chairs of the other two.
Cabinet ministers earn a salary of $165,851 per year, meaning the 30 ministers in Ford's government are costing taxpayers almost $1.5 million extra per year compared to a basic MPP's salary.
Jay Goldberg, Ontario director for the Canadian Taxpayers'Federation, points out that Ford ran for office on a platform to control the size and cost of government.
"Instead, he's bloated it larger than ever and appointed a record number of parliamentary assistants to hand out raises to caucus members without being transparent with taxpayers," Goldberg said in response to the shuffle.
mentary assistants to actually deliver on smaller and more efficient government for taxpay-ers."
He should, but this game of smoke and mirrors happens because no one wants to increase pay for MPPs and they play these games to get around that while in office.
All of this pales in comparison to the high wages paid to MPs in Ottawa.
While MPPs at Queen's Park haven't had a raise in 16 years, their April 1. On Monday, Canadian leg-receive generous annual raises each ond highest paid federal islators will become the sec-according to a report politicians in the world, from the National Post.
Backbench MPs will receive a raise of $8,500, pushing their salaries to $203,100 per year.
Cabinet ministers will see their salaries increase by $12,500 to $299,000, plus perks like a car and driver.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau will see his salary increase by $17,000 to $406,200 annually.
By comparison, Ford has made $208,974 each year he has been in office.
While the size of the cabinet and the number of parliamentary secretaries in Ontario is out of control, there is an argument to be made to increase the base salaries of elected officials in the province.
Many of them make less than the civil servants and political staffers they work alongside.
There surely could be a happy medium between what MPPs are paid in Ontario, $116,000, and what their federal counterparts in Ottawa are paid, $203,000.
To attract good people, there needs to be good compensation, without having to resort to tricks like giving all but one member of the PC caucus a role that comes with extra cash.
That idea would never fly with a large slice of the electorate, who believe we have too many politicians and they're all overpaid.
If MPPs were given raises, not as crazy as we're seeing in Ottawa, we could stop the charade of everyone getting these extra jobs to ensure a pay raise.
We need smaller government, we need more transparency, but, let's be honest, MPPs also need a raise after 16 years. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Raises; Politics; Wages & salaries

Business indexing term: Subject: Raises Wages & salaries

Full text: AIR QUALITY
This just doesn't seem fair. According to the recently published World Air Quality Index, in 2023 Canada had worse air quality than the United States for the first time in history. Given that we've had a carbon tax for five years now, while the Americans don't have one at all, how much longer do we wait, and how much higher must it be before it finally starts to help? Oh right, I forgot. Our federal government doesn't measure that, it just calculates the extra funding it gets to redistribute.
Al Willey
Edmonton
(Canadians have recognized the little value they are getting for the carbon tax. Let's not forget it's increasing 23% on April 1)
CHECK THE RECORD
It sounds like the majority of Canadians are against the carbon tax. However, a majority of the Members of Parliament are voting to increase the carbon tax. I wonder why this is. These members are supposed to represent their constituents. It seems to me that they are representing the leaders of their party and the party whips, not their constituents. I would strongly recommend that all Canadians should have a serious look at how their MPs vote before they vote for them again.
James N. Rogers
Regina
(It is important to remember come election time exactly who voted to increase the cost of living for Canadians in the midst of an affordability crisis)
TRUMP TIME
Jonah Goldberg notes that Trump's MAGA movement is now the Republican "establishment" (March 17). Goldberg ends his otherwise excellent opinion piece by warning that, if Trump loses in November, they will have no one to blame but themselves since "they're the establishment now." Yet it is precisely Trump's anti-establishment approach that so many - including Democrats! - find so endearing. Cheer up conservatives!
Jake VanderKooy
Brampton
(There are many people who won't admit they are going to vote for Trump because of the backlash. It's a big number)
BONNIE'S CARBON TAX?
To those who might consider giving Bonnie Crombie the benefit of the doubt when she proclaims that she will not implement a carbon tax if the Liberals are voted in to power in the next provincial election, I would like to remind them of a similar promise made by a previous Liberal leader, Dalton McGuinty, who promised that he would not raise taxes if he won the election. McGuinty won and almost immediately brought in a health tax, levy, which we all still pay today. A carbon levy, Bonnie?
Doug Bernard
Thornhill
(A levy is a another term for a tax)

LILLEY: Ford's bloated government gives every PC MPP but one a raise

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-fords-bloated-government-gives-every-pc/docview/3018651010/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Ford%27s+bloated+government+gives+every+PC+MPP+but+one+a+raise&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Call it jobs for the boys, and the girls, who make up Doug Ford's PC Party caucus.
Ontario's Premier is responsible for a 78-member caucus and somehow has found a way to give all but one of his MPPs additional roles that come with extra money.
In addition to Ford's bloated 30-member cabinet, there are also 38 parliamentary assistants. On Thursday, just before the Easter weekend, Ford shuffled the deck chairs for these assistants, which isn't something that would ever be of interest in the general public except for the extra cost.
Members of the Ontario Legislative Assembly are paid a base salary of $116,550, an amount that hasn't gone up since then-premier Dalton McGuinty froze MPP salaries in 2008 in the face of the financial crisis.
To get around this lack of pay increases, governments have increasingly provided their members with these plumb postings, but Ford's current situation is next level.
When MPPs are appointed to serve as parliamentary assistants their pay is bumped up to roughly $140,000, meaning the 38 PAs serving under Ford cost taxpayers close to $900,000 more. Committee chairs earn an additional $16,000, the PCs currently chair six of the eight committees and are co-chairs of the other two.
LILLEY: Ontario budget includes bigger deficit, lower growth, modest spending increase
LILLEY: Supreme Court should've decided Ontario's wage restriction law after bizarre ruling
TERRAZZANO: MPs hike our taxes and stuff their own wallets
Cabinet ministers earn a salary of $165,851 per year, meaning the 30 ministers in Ford's government are costing taxpayers almost $1.5 million extra per year compared to a basic MPP's salary.
Jay Goldberg, Ontario director for the Canadian Taxpayers' Federation, points out Ford ran for office on a platform to control the size and cost of government.
"Instead, he's bloated it larger than ever and appointed a record number of parliamentary assistants to hand out raises to caucus members without being transparent with taxpayers," Goldberg said in response to the shuffle.
"Ford needs to show leadership and reduce the size of both the cabinet and the number of parliamentary assistants to actually deliver on smaller and more efficient government for taxpayers."
He should, but this game of smoke and mirrors happens because no one wants to increase pay for MPPs and they play these games to get around that while in office.
All of this pales in comparison to the high wages paid to MPs in Ottawa.
While MPPs at Queen's Park haven't had a raise in 16 years, their counterparts on Parliament Hill receive generous annual raises each April 1. On Monday, Canadian legislators will become the second highest paid federal politicians in the world according to a report from National Post
Backbench MPs will receive a raise of $8,500, pushing their salaries to $203,100 per year. Cabinet ministers will see their salaries increase by $12,500 to $299,000, plus perks like a car and driver.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau will see his salary increase by $17,000 to $406,200 annually.
By comparison, Ontario Premier Doug Ford has made $208,974 each year that he has been in office.
While the size of the cabinet and the number of parliamentary secretaries in Ontario is out of control, there is an argument to be made to increase the base salaries of elected officials in the province. Many of them make less than the civil servants and political staffers that they work alongside.
There surely could be a happy medium between what MPPs are paid in Ontario, $116,000, and what their federal counterparts in Ottawa are paid, $203,000.
To attract good people there needs to be good compensation, without having to resort to tricks like giving all but one member of the PC caucus a role that comes with extra cash. That idea would never fly with a large slice of the electorate who believe we have too many politicians and they are all overpaid.
If MPPs were given raises, not as crazy as we are seeing in Ottawa, we could stop the charade of everyone getting these extra jobs to ensure a pay raise.
We need smaller government, we need more transparency, but let's be honest, MPPs also need a raise after 16 years.
blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Raises; Politics; Wages & salaries

Business indexing term: Subject: Raises Wages & salaries

Identifier / keyword: girls; party; caucus; premier; responsible; somehow

Document 125 of 356

Small businesses 'unfairly punished' by upcoming carbon tax hike: CFIB

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/small-businesses-unfairly-punished-upcoming/docview/3014028107/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Small+businesses+%27unfairly+punished%27+by+upcoming+carbon+tax+hike%3A+CFIB&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Many small business owners are not looking forward to the upcoming carbon tax hike next week.
According to the Canadian Federation of Independent Business, 56% of small businesses will be forced to raise prices to accommodate the increase while 45% report they will need to freeze or reduce wages. One third say it will reduce their ability to invest in environmental initiatives.
The federal carbon tax jumps by 23% to $80 per tonne on April 1.
"The giant hike in carbon taxes further highlights how unfair this tax is for small businesses," CFIB president Dan Kelly said in a statement.
He is calling on the federal government to freeze the upcoming hike.
"The whole carbon tax system has become a shell game, and sadly, small firms are unfairly punished by it," he said.
Kelly said the Liberal government has not kept its promise to return some revenues from the carbon tax to small businesses.
"Ottawa is sitting on $2.5 billion in carbon tax rebates intended for small firms, calling into question the government's claim that the tax is revenue neutral," Kelly said.
According to CFIB estimates, businesses in Ontario, Manitoba, Saskatchewan and Alberta - provinces under the federal carbon tax system - that qualify for a rebate could receive a one-time payment between $2,600 and nearly $7,000.
In the four Atlantic provinces that came under the federal carbon tax last July, rebates would be between $630 and $1,060.
British Columbia, Quebec and Northwest Territories have their own carbon tax for all sources of emissions.
Kelly said he was happy to hear Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland's comments to a recent committee meeting that small businesses will soon have some good news on the billions owed to them since 2019.
"This can't come soon enough," Kelly added.
The CFIB is calling on Ottawa to drop the planned carbon tax hike and immediately return the billions owed to all small businesses.
"Ottawa has an opportunity to right the wrong and announce concrete plans to return the promised $2.5 billion to all small businesses, not just certain sectors," said Jasmin Guenette, CFIB's vice-president of national affairs.
"We hope the government listens to small business concerns and will announce details of a plan to keep its promise in the upcoming budget."
LILLEY: Economists' support for federal carbon tax seems like a lot of hot air
GOLDSTEIN: How the Liberals blew their credibility on the carbon tax
OPINION: Trudeau government should acknowledge impact of carbon tax hike

Document 126 of 356

How Liberals blew their credibility on carbon tax

Author: Goldstein, Lorrie

Publication info: Goldstein, Lorrie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-liberals-blew-their-credibility-on-carbon-tax/docview/3013891319/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Liberals+blew+their+credibility+on+carbon+tax&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=Goldstein%2C+Lorrie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Once a government loses its credibility on an issue, it's hard to recover it.
That's what has happened to the Trudeau Liberals on their carbon tax.
Their loss of credibility began when they claimed they had come up with a tax that, because of rebates, would leave 80% of Canadian households paying it better off, which never passed the smell test.
That's because what they didn't say is that this was only the fiscal impact of the carbon tax - dollars in, dollars out - not its negative impact on the Canadian economy.
That only happened when independent, non-partisan Parliamentary Budget Officer Yves Giroux examined the Trudeau carbon tax.
He said the government was right to say 80% of households end up better off when only the fiscal impact of the carbon tax is considered.
But when you factor in its negative impact on the economy, Giroux federal carbon tax are already worse off today, rising to 80% in Nova Scotia in 2025, in Ontario in 2026, in Manitoba in 2029 and in Alberta and P.E.I. in 2030.
Giroux reported that the carbon tax would lower Canada's GDP and reduce business investment and labour income for skilled and unskilled workers. He also noted the government doesn't remit the GST paid on top of the carbon tax back to those paying it.
Ever since, the Liberals and their Giroux, have been throwing up red herrings and irrelevant arguments, not to discredit his analysis of the fiscal impact of their carbon tax - because Giroux agrees with the Trudeau Liberals on that - but his added analysis of its economic impact.
They complained Giroux didn't factor in the cost of other things the Trudeau government might have done to lower emissions in place of a carbon tax, ignoring the fact the role of the PBO is to assess government They said the PBO didn't factor in future savings due to new technologies to fight climate change, nor the cost of doing nothing because of increasingly severe weather.
This ignored that what the PBO was assessing was the economic impact of Trudeau's carbon tax that began at $20 per tonne of industrial greenhouse gas emissions in 2019, is now $65 per tonne and will rise on April 1 to $80 per tonne, on its way to $170 per tonne in 2030.
We don't know what the Liberal plan is after 2030 and, as Giroux has noted, claiming the carbon tax will save money because of technological innovation or less severe weather in Canada by 2030 is unrealistic.
It's also absurd. Contrary to his critics'claims, Giroux has examined the long-term impact of climate change on the Canadian economy, estimating that, "recent increases in temperature and precipitation combined with future changes in weather patterns will reduce Canada's real GDP by 5.8% in 2100."
The issue, Giroux said, is that even if we were able to reduce our emissions to net zero by tomorrow, it would have no impact on the weather in Canada because our emissions, at 1.5% of the global total, aren't enough to materially impact climate change.
Reducing our emissions will give us the moral high ground in urging major emitters like China to do the same, but the idea that anything Canada does alone through Trudeau's carbon tax is going to mean fewer wildfires, floods and storms in Canada, is absurd. lgoldstein@postmedia.com @sunlorrie

Subject: Economic impact; Gross Domestic Product--GDP; Cost control; Credibility; Emissions; Carbon; Climate change; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Economic impact Gross Domestic Product--GDP Cost control Environmental tax

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: government; loses; credibility; issue; recover; happened

Document 127 of 356

Carbon copies; Economists do decent impression of Liberal MPs with support for tax hike

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/carbon-copies-economists-do-decent-impression/docview/3013891148/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Carbon+copies%3B+Economists+do+decent+impression+of+Liberal+MPs+with+support+for+tax+hike&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The headline made it sound pretty clear: "Economists defend Liberals'carbon price as political rhetoric heats up." A story under a headline like that might make you think there was a hefty defence laid out for the carbon tax being imposed and soon to be increased by the Trudeau Liberals.
If you read the story, you'd find out you were wrong - and you'd also be wrong to think there was a robust defence of the carbon tax in the open letter from economists that it was based upon.
"As economists from across Canada, we are concerned about the significant threats from climate change," the letter says. "Healthy public debate is good, but it should be based on sound evidence and facts."
The letter goes on to address what they said were five myths about the carbon tax. Given that this letter is from economists, it's the portion that discusses the impact of the carbon tax that caught my eye.
The letter claims the carbon tax is not affecting inflation, then cites government talking points about rebates and a thoroughly debunked claim from the Bank of Canada as proof.
"According to the Bank of Canada, carbon pricing has caused less than one-20th of Canada's inflation in the past two years," the letter says.
This claim was made by bank governor Tiff Macklem last fall and was widely reported, but never fact checked by the media. Instead, asking the bank how it got to that figure fell to academic Sylvain Charlebois.
Charlebois, the senior director of the Agri-Food Analytics Lab at Dalhousie University who is also known as the Food Professor, asked the bank to explain how it arrived at that claim. The Bank of Canada admitted it only looked at the effect of gasoline, heating oil and natural gas when applied directly to the consumer to arrive at that claim. That is not only a very narrow view of how to measure the effects of the carbon tax, but it also fails to take into account the pass-through effect of the tax on consumers.
A cucumber grown in one of southwestern Ontario's many greenhouses would have to pay the carbon tax on the CO2 used to grow the cucumber. The trucking company that picked up the cucumber would have to pay the carbon tax on the fuel used to transport the cucumber. The warehouse that stores the cucumber and the store that sells it will both pay the carbon tax on keeping that cuke cool.
You will only pay the carbon tax on the gas it takes for you to drive to the store, and that is all that the Bank of Canada uses to assess the effect on inflation, not all of the other steps that increase the price. It's a lazy method for the Bank of Canada and it's lazier still for these economists to use that as their measurement when claiming to refute myths about the carbon tax.
Yves Giroux, the parliamentary budget officer, looked at the full effect of the carbon tax - from what we all pay, the lower economic growth, the increase in prices, the increased amount of GST we pay - and arrived at a basic conclusion: Most of us are paying more.
"Once you factor in the rebate, but also the economic impacts, the majority of households will see a negative impact as a result of the carbon tax," Giroux told a House of Commons committee last week.
That's not something these economists mention. Instead, they spend more time repeating government talking points and sounding like Liberal MPs than they do in providing evidence for their claims.
"While Trudeau and his so-called 'experts'live comfortable lives, they are forcing a 23% tax hike on everyday Canadians who are already unable to make ends meet," the federal Conservatives said in a statement.
That tax hike, fully supported by the Liberals, Bloc Quebecois and NDP, takes effect on Monday and will surely push prices higher.
Who are you going to believe, the cash register or some outof-touch academics? @brianlilley blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Economists; Carbon; Environmental tax; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Economists Environmental tax Tax increases

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Bank of Canada; NAICS: 521110

Identifier / keyword: headline; sound; pretty; clear; economists; defend

They said the PBO didn't factor in future savings due to new technologies to fight climate change, nor the cost of doing nothing because of increasingly severe weather.
This ignored that what the PBO was assessing was the economic impact of Trudeau's carbon tax that began at $20 per tonne of industrial greenhouse gas emissions in 2019, is now $65 per tonne and will rise on April 1 to $80 per tonne, on its way to $170 per tonne in 2030.

Document 130 of 356

SWIFT: The war on natural gas in Ontario needs to stop

Author: Doucette, Chris

Publication info: Doucette, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/swift-war-on-natural-gas-ontario-needs-stop/docview/3004424030/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=SWIFT%3A+The+war+on+natural+gas+in+Ontario+needs+to+stop&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Doucette%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's a war being waged in Ontario against home heating sources and it's got to stop.
Late last year, the Ford government made the right call in stating they would overturn a decision by the Ontario Energy Board (OEB) to effectively discourage the attachment of new housing to the natural gas grid.
Typically, when a new housing development is planned, the units will be hooked up to natural gas when they are built. This requires a great deal of planning and building out of the natural gas infrastructure.
Payment for this infrastructure has customarily been made in small installments over a period of 40 years by the homeowner. This system worked well for decades and there was no reason to believe it would not work well for decades to come.
Natural gas is currently the most common source of heating in Ontario, used by 60% of households.
HUDAK: Decision by Ontario Energy Board threatens housing progress
TABUNS: Doug Ford wants to raise your gas bill
OPINION: Ford government again breaks promise to reduce taxes
Enter the green zealots. Their influence undoubtedly contributed to the OEB decision to change this approach and now require the developer to pay the entire cost of the attachment of the new housing to the gas grid up front. This increases the cost of a home by about $4,400.
This means the cost of homes will increase and there will be a disincentive to hook up the houses to natural gas. That's what the activists want.
The OEB based their decision on the belief that natural gas was being phased out and the future would be more reliant on "green" energy sources such as wind and solar power. The OEB also recommended the installation of heat pumps. These pumps are expensive, require more electricity to operate, are difficult to install and have not been reliable in cold weather, among other drawbacks.
Ontario Environment Minister Todd McCarthy reacted quickly after the decision was announced just before Christmas. He said the government planned to overturn it. In February, the Ford government introduced legislation called the Keeping Energy Costs Down Act to accomplish this.
The legislation is also intended to encourage the OEB to undertake "broader engagement" in its consultations and invite participation from a wider range of affected groups, such as homebuilders and construction companies.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
The climate extremists have been trying to get rid of natural gas for some time, despite the fact that natural gas is reliable, affordable and much cleaner than other fossil fuels.
What is ironic is that about 15 years ago, environmental groups were lauding clean natural gas as the ideal replacement for coal. Now that coal has been eliminated from Ontario's energy mix, natural gas is suddenly the new villain.
Reliability and energy security have not been sufficiently considered in this conversation. We have seen in other countries - and even in some Canadian provinces such as Alberta - that an over-reliance on energy sources such as wind and solar can lead to shortages of power when it is most needed, such as in extremely cold weather.
It is also true that Ontario is currently a world leader with respect to sustainable power generation. With energy generated from hydroelectric, nuclear, wind and solar, biomass and natural gas, the system is already 90% emissions-free. The notion of eliminating such a reliable energy source as natural gas to obtain miniscule, if any, progress in terms of emissions reduction seems pointless.
The ideal system for the climate crowd would be to have no fossil fuels used anywhere, and to force that scenario on us all whether we like it or not.
If natural gas infrastructure is not built up-front to serve new housing developments, it will be very difficult to unscramble the omelet later if these households decide they want to use natural gas after all because the alternatives don't work well.
Ontarians should be pleased that, through Ontario government legislation, their future energy choices are being left for them to make, not some climate groups that do not have their best interests at heart.
- Catherine Swift is the president of the Coalition of Concerned Manufacturers and Businesses of Canada. Learn more at www.ccmbc.ca

Subject: Legislation; Infrastructure; Energy resources; Fossil fuels; Natural gas

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure

Identifier / keyword: being; waged; ontario; against; heating; sources

Document 131 of 356

Ford talks like a Tory, spends like a Liberal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-talks-like-tory-spends-liberal/docview/3001524737/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+talks+like+a+Tory%2C+spends+like+a+Liberal&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Ontario Premier Doug Ford and the Progressive Conservatives came to power in 2018, they promised to end the spending excesses of the 15-year dynasty of Liberal premiers Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne they had just defeated.
Six years later, they are the McGuinty-Wynne Liberals when it comes to spending, deficits and debt accumulation.
In unveiling its first budget on April 11, 2019, the Ford government said: "Ontario's government for the people has made a commitment to balance the budget responsibly" by "restoring trust, transparency and accountability in the province's finances ..." In unveiling its sixth budget on Tuesday - the largest in Ontario history at $214.5 billion for the 2024 fiscal year - the Ford government has managed to balance the books once, in 2021, similar to the McGuinty-Wynne record of balancing the books only three times in 16 budgets.
A year ago, in its 2023 budget, the Ford government predicted it would balance the province's books and record a $200 million surplus in 2024.
On Tuesday, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said the Ford government will record a $9.8-billion deficit in 2024, more than triple the $3-billion deficit it recorded in 2023, and won't balance the province's books until the 2026 fiscal year.
Don't count on it. The Ford government's books are starting to resemble the train wreck the Wynne government's books were in its final years in power.
In its 2017 budget, the Liberals predicted three consecutive years of balanced budgets. A year later, they did a complete about-face and predicted six consecutive years of deficit budgets, until they would balance the books in 2024 - the same year the Ford government said last year that it would balance the books, while now saying there will be a $9.8-billion deficit.
When the Ford government came to power in 2018, it complained that it had "inherited the largest subnational (net) debt in the world" from the Liberals at $343 billion, an increase of more than $200 billion in the 15 years they had been in power.
Today, under the Ford government, Ontario's net debt is projected at $439 billion in 2024, up $96 billion in six years.
So don't hold your breath on the Ford government balancing the books anytime soon.

Subject: Budget deficits

Business indexing term: Subject: Budget deficits

Identifier / keyword: ontario; premier; progressive; conservatives; power; promised

Document 132 of 356

Inflation fixation; Budget attempts to balance need for growth with high cost of living

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/inflation-fixation-budget-attempts-balance-need/docview/3001523353/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Inflation+fixation%3B+Budget+attempts+to+balance+need+for+growth+with+high+cost+of+living&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Peter Bethlenfalvy stood in the Legislature to deliver the 2024 Ontario budget, he paid tribute to his father. He told the story of a 19-year-old leaving his native Hungary just after the Second World War and coming to Canada without much, but finding success.
Bethlenfalvy told the Legislature that his father is about to turn 94 and he wanted to dedicate this budget to him.
"He lived the Ontario dream, he lived the Canadian dream," Bethlenfalvy said, "and the hope and dream for all 16 million Ontarians is that they can have the same opportunity as my father had."
Given the economic conditions facing Ontario, Canada and the world, that's going to be more difficult. On top of the crushing inflation and high interest rates we've all been dealing with, economists predict Ontario is headed for a slowdown.
"These projections now show that while economic growth is expected to significantly slow in the coming year, private-sector forecasters are cautiously optimistic that it will not drop into negative territory before rebounding in subsequent years," Bethlenfalvy said.
While he and Premier Doug Ford may be optimistic that we won't hit a recession, hope isn't a plan. Many of the factors that will determine whether we bounce back quickly or sink into a recession are outside of the provincial government's control.
Inflation is still a global concern, though governments can make things worse through measures like raising taxes, for example. The Ford government used its 2024 budget to target the federal carbon tax, with Bethlenfalvy mentioning the tuition freeze for Ontario's colleges and universities. There had been a strong lobby campaign by colleges and universities to allow tuitions to increase for the first time since 2019.
tax and its looming increase 11 times during his speech.
By contrast, the Ford government tried to highlight that its goal was to make life more affordable. It pointed to the gas tax cut, the pause on the alcohol escalator tax, a 6.1% tax cut to help vineyards, the removal of tolls from Hwys. 412 and 418 and the removal of the licence plate sticker fee.
At times, it was like the Ford government was trying to roll out its greatest hits of fee and tax removals.
The budget also discussed the ongoing Behind the scenes, even Bethlenfalvy supported the tuition hike, but Ford was steadfast that costs not increase for students or their families during an affordability crisis. Like so much in this budget, it had already been announced; Ford made the decision public with an off-the-cuff remark during a news conference weeks ago.
There are vague promises about changing the auto insurance system to give consumers more choice, including by allowing drivers to decline some services that are part of mandatory coverage. There is, however, no massive overhaul of the system, an idea that has plagued past governments of all stripes.
In total, the Ford government forecast it will spend $214.5 billion this year, but it is predicting just $205.7 billion in revenue due to the slowing economy. That should mean a deficit of $8.8 billion for the coming fiscal year, but Bethlenfalvy is also including a $1-billion reserve fund, meaning a total predicted deficit of $9.8 billion for 2024-25.
Perhaps as a sign of the already slowing economy, the budget report shows a deficit for the closing fiscal year of $3 billion instead of the predicted $2.2 billion.
There are modest spending increases in such areas as health and education, but they won't be enough for the public-sector unions or their allies in the New Democratic and Liberal parties. Then again, those parties and unions would never find any level of spending increase to be enough.
When he was premier, the late Bill Davis was fond of saying: "Boring is good." In the context of a budget coming as the economy slows, Tuesday's budget was boring and that is good. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Budgets; Tax cuts; Colleges & universities; Economic conditions; Recessions

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets Tax cuts Colleges & universities Economic conditions Recessions

LILLEY: Ontario budget includes bigger deficit, lower growth, modest spending increase

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-ontario-budget-includes-bigger-deficit/docview/2991814640/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Ontario+budget+includes+bigger+deficit%2C+lower+growth%2C+modest+spending+increase&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 135 of 356

OPINION: Trudeau government should acknowledge impact of carbon tax hike

Author: Spiro Papuckoski

Publication info: Spiro Papuckoski. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/opinion-trudeau-government-should-acknowledge/docview/2987785687/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=OPINION%3A+Trudeau+government+should+acknowledge+impact+of+carbon+tax+hike&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Spiro+Papuckoski&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On April 1, the Trudeau government will raise the federal carbon tax from $65 to $80 per tonne of greenhouse gas emissions. Opposition is widespread. Seven provincial premiers want the government to scrap the hike, and the federal Conservatives tabled a motion (unsuccessfully) in Parliament to topple the government over the hike. In response, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau says the tax hike will put "more money in the pockets of the vast majority of Canadians."
But in reality, it will make life even more expensive in a country already experiencing declining living standards.
For example, the hike will add an additional 3 cents per litre of gasoline at the pumps. The price of gas will grow, as the federal carbon tax is scheduled to rise incrementally to $170 per tonne in 2030 - only six years from now.
Still, according to the Trudeau government, most Canadians will be better off because much of the proceeds of the carbon tax are refunded to eligible households. However, a recent report from the Parliamentary Budget Officer (PBO) found most households will experience a net loss of income from the federal carbon tax, even after rebates.
LILLEY: Trudeau Liberals double down on hiking carbon tax even higher
GOLDSTEIN: Trudeau's costly carbon tax just the tip of the iceberg
GREEN: Trudeau carbon tax hike will hurt future generations
Specifically, in fiscal year 2024-25, 60% of households in Alberta, Ontario, Saskatchewan, Newfoundland and Labrador, Nova Scotia, Prince Edward Island and Manitoba will pay more in carbon taxes than what they receive in rebates, after accounting for both direct and indirect costs of the carbon tax. By 2030, 80% of households in Alberta, Ontario, Manitoba, Nova Scotia and P.E.I. will be worse off, as will 60% of households in Saskatchewan and Newfoundland and Labrador.
Indeed, according to the PBO estimates, the carbon tax will cost the average Canadian household between $377 and $911 in 2024-25 - even after rebates, with Albertans being the most affected. As the carbon tax escalates annually, the financial burden will intensify. By 2030, the carbon tax's average net cost for Canadian households will rise to $1,490 in Manitoba, $1,723 in Saskatchewan, $1,820 in Ontario and $2,773 in Alberta.
More broadly, the carbon tax will negatively impact economic activity, which will also affect federal finances. According to the PBO report, due to the economic impact of the carbon tax, the federal deficit will increase by $2.3 billion in 2024-25 and by $7.1 billion in 2030-31, when the carbon tax reaches $170 per tonne.
The PBO's findings echo those of a 2021 study published by the Fraser Institute, which found that a $170-per-tonne carbon tax will cause the economy (i.e. GDP) to shrink by about 1.8%, cause a permanent loss of nearly 185,000 Canadian jobs, and reduce real income in every province. And that the contractionary effects from the carbon tax will cause combined federal and provincial government budgets to deteriorate by an estimated $22 billion annually.
Finally, despite claims by federal Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault that the carbon tax rebates will "help Canadians," according to the Canadian Federation of Independent Businesses, after five years into the rebate program, Ottawa still owes more than $2.5 billion in rebates to small and medium-sized firms. In other words, the government has been unable to fully implement the mechanism that mitigates some of the tax's harmful economic effects.
Clearly, despite any claims to the contrary, Monday's federal carbon tax hike will leave Canadian families financially worse off (now and in the future), damage government finances and negatively impact the economy. In light of this reality, many Canadians and elected representatives want the Trudeau government to change course. The government should acknowledge the real impact of the carbon tax on Canadians from coast to coast and act accordingly.
- Julio Mejia and Elmira Aliakbari are analysts at the Fraser Institute.

Subject: Net losses; Carbon; Decades; Environmental tax; Rebates; Canadians; Emission standards; Households; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Net losses Environmental tax Rebates Tax increases

Location: Manitoba Canada; Canada; Newfoundland & Labrador Canada; Saskatchewan Canada; Nova Scotia Canada

Company / organization: Name: Fraser Institute; NAICS: 541720

Document 136 of 356

LILLEY: Trudeau wastes $42 million on gun 'buy back' that doesn't work

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-trudeau-wastes-42-million-on-gun-buy-back/docview/2987117276/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Trudeau+wastes+%2442+million+on+gun+%27buy+back%27+that+doesn%27t+work&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Somehow, the Trudeau Liberals have figured out how to spend $42 million on a "gun buyback" program that hasn't seen the government take ownership of a single gun.
It takes a special kind of incompetence to spend four years and $42 million on a government priority and accomplish the square root of nothing.
According to a story broken by my National Post colleague Bryan Passifiume, that's where we are though, four years after Justin Trudeau said he was acting to make Canadians safer.
On May 1, 2020, just days after the Nova Scotia mass murder that left 22 people dead and injured many more, Trudeau said his government was banning "assault-style" guns. At first, he announced that 1,500 types of guns would be banned by a cabinet decree with legislation to follow.
He already knew when he made this announcement that guns used by the killer, Gabriel Wortman, were illegally smuggled in from the United States. Trudeau also knew by that point that police had been warned about Wortman's tendency towards violence and his illegal gun collection and had done nothing.
"Assault-style firearms designed for military use have no place in Canada. By removing them from our streets, we will limit the devastating effects of gun-related violence and help make our country safer," Trudeau said at the time.
Of course, what he was banning were not guns made for military use, they were for hunting and sport shooting. Trudeau also knew that the biggest problem for gun violence in Canada were the guns smuggled across the border, not the guns in the basements and gun safes of Canada's more than 2 million legal, licenced gun owners.
Still, he pushed ahead with plans for a so-called "gun buyback" but could never figure out how to make it work.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
Since the start of their efforts, the Trudeau Liberals have turned to the RCMP, the Canadian Armed Forces, provincial police forces in Ontario and Quebec, Canada Post and more to try and find partners to try and help collect these now banned forearms.
The national and provincial police services simply don't have the time or resources to deal with this and the military is not equipped. As for Canada Post, does it make sense to have gun owners take their AR-15s to the Canada Post outlet at their local pharmacy?
According to the Trudeau government's response to an order paper question from Senator Don Plett, there are currently 60 staff from Public Safety Canada working on devising the program and 15 RCMP officers also assisting. That's 75 staff, costing a lot of money and, thankfully, not delivering anything.
There are also two employees at Service Canada working full-time on the file and in what can only be described as the epitome of government speak, "the equivalent of 5.825 full-time employees" from Public Services and Procurement Canada.
"This is a boondoggle, and it hasn't even begun," Plett said Friday during the Senate question period."
"How can your government have spent $42 million on this, when not a single firearm has been bought back?"
The issues with Bill #C21 continue. I've learned in a response to an Order Paper question that the Trudeau govt's Buyback Program has ALREADY cost $42 M. Have they even bought back a single #firearm? I am flabbergasted by this boondoggle!
Trudeau is not worth the cost!
- Senator Don Plett (@DonPlett) March 22, 2024
Senator Marc Gold stood to defend the government's actions by saying, "it is hard to put a value on a life."
"Every gun that is not in circulation, that cannot be used to create and to cause injury to individuals and their families is worth the investment," Gold said.
Worth the investment?
This is $42 million spent trying to design a system to take guns away, not from criminals, but from law-abiding, legal, licenced gun owners. Imagine what that $42 million could have accomplished if spent on increasing capabilities at the border to stop gun smuggling.
This plan from Trudeau, to ban the 1,500 rifle variants, was always about politics and not results. Four years later, we have the proof, plenty of money spent, lots of heated rhetoric, no results.

Subject: Bans; Gun violence

Location: Canada

People: Plett, Don

Identifier / keyword: somehow; trudeau; liberals; figured; spend; million

Document 137 of 356

Political Spending; With upcoming Ontario budget, preem plots course for radical centre

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/political-spending-with-upcoming-ontario-budget/docview/2986629750/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Political+Spending%3B+With+upcoming+Ontario+budget%2C+preem+plots+course+for+radical+centre&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Expect the Ontario budget to take a "no surprises" route when it is released on Tuesday afternoon. According to those in the know, the government has spent the last several weeks announcing most of what is in the budget.
On Monday, Premier Doug Ford announced his government would extend the gas tax cut through the end of 2024.
"With the federal government about to increase its costly carbon tax, it's never been more important to provide relief at the pumps and put hundreds of dollars back into people's pockets. That is why we are extending our gas tax cut," Ford said in Mississauga on Monday.
He also launched into a broadside against the federal carbon tax, which is set to go up next week.
"On April 1, the federal government will be raising its terrible carbon tax by a staggering 23%," Ford said. "This increase is going to raise the price at the pumps by an additional three cents per litre of gas."
Ford said that as of next week, the carbon tax will amount to 17.6 cents for every litre of gas purchased in the province.
"It's going to increase the cost of everything. It's going to hurt every single person in Ontario. So once again, I'm calling on the federal government to not make life more expensive for Ontario families. I urge you to immediately scrap next week's tax hike."
He then went on to attack Bonnie Crombie, the leader of Ontario's third party, which lacks official standing in the legislature. Ford said despite Crombie's statement that her Ontario Liberal Party wouldn't bring in a provincial carbon tax, she remains the "Queen of the Carbon Tax" and her advisers have all endorsed the tax in the past.
It's telling that Ford remains focused on Crombie, a leader without a seat in the legislature. Not that Ford sees her as a threat to his power - at least not yet.
The latest Ontario polling puts the Ford PCs at 41% of voter support, which is the same as they received in the last election. The Ontario Liberals moved up to 27% of support when Crombie took over in early December, which was about three points higher than what they got in the 2022 election, but they've stayed there ever since.
The Ontario NDP, meanwhile, has dropped down to 21% of voter support under Leader Marit Stiles compared with the 24% it received in the last election.
Crombie was always going to be a bigger threat to Stiles, at least initially, than she would be to Ford, and that theory has been proven to be true. Whether Crombie can threaten Ford in the future remains to be seen.
Budgets, like the one being released Tuesday, are political documents as much as they are financial documents and we can expect a lot of politics to be at play.
The Ford government will play up that they have refused to increase tuition fees for colleges and universities, a move that will sit well with students and their parents. They will highlight their cut to the gas tax, their removal of tolls from highways 412 and 418 and the removal of the licence plate sticker fee.
Sure, those are old promises, announced and re-announced more times than we can count, but in an affordability crisis highlighting what you have done to make life cheaper will matter.
On that note, the Ford government will also highlight its investments in transit and the reduction in fares for people using multiple systems to get to work. We can expect some backslapping on Hwy. 413 and the Bradford Bypass.
On transit, their message will be: However you get around, we are there to make it easier for you.
Spending will increase, there is no doubt, but Ford will once again be able to say that while he has increased spending dramatically, he's never increased taxes.
Expect the government to deliver a budget that hugs the radical centre while the NDP and Liberals scream about non-existent cuts. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Budgets; Tax cuts; Carbon; Gasoline taxes; Tax increases; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets Tax cuts Gasoline taxes Tax increases Environmental tax

LILLEY: Premier plots course for radical centre in upcoming Ontario budget

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-premier-plots-course-radical-centre/docview/2986233896/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Premier+plots+course+for+radical+centre+in+upcoming+Ontario+budget&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Bonnie Crombie refuses to stand up against next week's Liberal carbon tax hike, but that's not a surprise.
She spent her whole career raising property taxes and backing carbon taxes, while our government lowered costs by cutting the gas tax and fighting the Liberal carbon tax.
- Doug Ford (@fordnation) March 25, 2024
It's telling that Ford remains focused on Crombie, a leader without a seat in the legislature and leading a party without official party status. Not that Ford sees her as a threat to his power, at least not yet.

Document 140 of 356

LILLEY: Trudeau Liberals double down on hiking carbon tax even higher

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-trudeau-liberals-double-down-on-hiking/docview/2976402541/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Trudeau+Liberals+double+down+on+hiking+carbon+tax+even+higher&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Trudeau Liberals just gave Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre another stick to beat them with. Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault won't rule out raising the carbon tax even further than already planned.
Maybe Guilbeault hasn't heard, but the carbon tax isn't popular and has been hurting his party in the polls for some time now.
"We haven't made a decision on that. We've started consultation to prepare the next phase of emission reductions," Guilbeault told CTV's Question Period in an interview that aired Sunday morning.
"There's no decision that has been that has been made yet other than we will continue increasing the price on pollution."
The carbon tax is currently set at $65 per tonne of CO2 emission and will rise to $80 per tonne, a 23% next Monday - April 1. Each year, on April 1, the tax will go up by $15 per tonne until it reaches $170 per tonne in 2030 and now the Liberals say it may go higher still.
The carbon tax, and the annual increases, have become lightning rods for both the general public and provincial governments protesting the increases. A recent poll showed that 69% of Canadians opposed the increase set to take place next week, and seven out of 10 provincial governments officially asked for the carbon tax hike to be paused as inflation and the cost of living remain top concerns.
The Trudeau government has tried to portray the carbon tax as not being a factor in inflation, and has even described their carbon tax rebates as an affordability measure. That would be news to Statistic Canada, which has noted that Saskatchewan has lower inflation than the national average after the provincial government decided not to apply the carbon tax on home heating for customers of the government-owned SaskEnergy.
Recommended video
"In Saskatchewan, the collection of the carbon levy ceased in January 2024, contributing to the province's year-over-year price decline of natural gas (-26.6%)," the federal agency said last month
For the last two months, inflation in Saskatchewan has been declining faster than in other parts of the country. When inflation dropped to 2.9% nationally in January, it was just 1.9% in Saskatchewan and when it dropped to 2.8% nationally in February, it was just 1.7% in the Prairie province.
As his carbon tax comes under full attack, @justintrudeau tries to sell his carbon tax rebates as an affordability measure to deal with rising prices while Ontario Premier @fordnation wonders what Trudeau is smoking.
Read & RT #cdnpoli #onpoli
- Brian Lilley (@brianlilley) March 19, 2024
A big part of that is dropping the carbon tax on home heating, even though Trudeau claims it doesn't increase prices and that most Canadians are better off due to the rebates provided under the tax scheme.
That's not what the Parliamentary Budget Officer said last week before a Commons committee.
"Once you factor in the rebate, but also the economic impacts, the majority of households will see a negative impact as a result of the carbon tax," Yves Giroux, Canada's non-partisan Parliamentary Budget Officer, told MPs last week.
So, you already pay more in total costs than you get back, the price increases next week, the Liberals won't rule out increasing the tax in perpetuity, and they want you to think you are better off for all of this. No wonder this policy has become such a successful line of attack for Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives.
Poilievre spent much of his time during an axe the tax rally in Ottawa on Sunday, denouncing the impacts of the carbon tax on Canadian industry and jobs.
"By driving up the cost of production, farming, forestry, fisheries, he's driving production to more dirty, polluting foreign dictatorships. We, in Canada, want to bring the production home which is why I keep saying 'Bring it home,'" Poilievre said.
Bringing production home appears to be the last thing Trudeau wants to do, based on how his policies are designed. Even his recent push for more EV vehicle sales will try to force consumers to buy electric vehicles made in China, a country with an abhorrent environmental record.
The latest national poll shows the Conservatives at 41% support, the Liberals at 23% and the NDP at 19%. As long as the Liberals and NDP keep pushing the ever-increasing carbon tax, don't expect that to change.
LILLEY: Trudeau in full defensive mode to save his carbon tax hike
LILLEY: Trudeau survives confidence motion as MPs vote against nation's mood

Subject: Rebates; Carbon; Environmental tax; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Rebates Environmental tax Tax increases

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Identifier / keyword: trudeau; liberals; conservative; leader; pierre; poilievre

Title: LILLEY: Trudeau Liberals double down on hiking carbon tax even higher
