Document 141 of 356

Crombie knows NIMBY; Liberal leader flip-flops on Ontario housing problem

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombie-knows-nimby-liberal-leader-flip-flops-on/docview/2973795531/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie+knows+NIMBY%3B+Liberal+leader+flip-flops+on+Ontario+housing+problem&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Is Bonnie Crombie's Ontario Liberal Party ready to take on NIMBYs or is she just playing a role in the wake of her housing policy release? It's a question worth asking based on her party's last election win and her own policy flip-flops on the how and where to build housing.
Last July, the Ontario Liberals won the riding of Kanata-Carleton, an area of the province they had not won in well over a century.
One of the main reasons for the victory was campaigning against more housing being built and attracting NIMBYs to their cause.
The big issue in the byelection last summer was whether a privately owned golf course could be developed into housing.
Locals, not just the well-heeled who had bought homes backing onto the course, were outraged that the golf course would soon host homes.
The golf course was part of the fabric of the community, it was a place local kids would toboggan in the winter, a pleasant green space even for those not swinging a club.
The Liberals, including their local candidate and now MPP Karen McCrimmon, leaned into the issue.
The Progressive Conservatives, saying they were doing everything they could to build housing, couldn't exactly come out against a privately owned golf course being turned into housing - it would seem elitist.
The Liberals saw an opportunity and pounced, making the issue a problem for Premier Doug Ford and his candidate Sean Webster.
"Kanata residents are concerned about losing access to the green space they care about. Are you going to give away the protected land at Kanata Lakes to your billionaire friends just like you did with the Greenbelt?" the Liberals asked in a news release still on their website.
Now, Crombie has come out with a housing policy that would allow anyone, in any built-up area of the province, to knock down an existing home and put up a fourplex or even a four-storey building.
There are areas of this province with entire neighbourhoods where one and two-storey homes as the tallest buildings.
Crombie's proposal, if adopted, would forbid local councils from saying no to a four-storey home going up in a neighbourhood of bungalows.
Advocates of the policy can argue that it's the right thing to do in a housing crisis, but so too is building homes on a golf course the owners no longer want.
That byelection last July took place at the same time as the Ford government was still pushing ahead with allowing homes to be built on a section of the Greenbelt surrounding the Greater Toronto Area.
That was a policy the Liberals generally opposed, though Crombie's flip-flopping on the issue is well documented.
By late August, Ford caved to public pressure and not only abandoned the idea of building on the Greenbelt but apologized for even attempting to do so.
Ford's policy flip-flop on the Greenbelt was bad public policy - this was not pristine farmland, and he should have stayed the course - but politically it was popular to reverse course.
In the same way, it doesn't matter how many housing experts say that Crombie's policy of allowing four-storey homes "as-of-right" in every corner of the province is the right thing to do, it's a vote loser.
She won't win votes in Orleans, Manotick, Ancaster, Burlington, Grimbsy, London, Guelph, Timmins, Sudbury or many other communities with a policy like this.
In fact, this kind of policy would also be unpopular in many of the Toronto ridings the Liberals should be contending in, or even ones they hold like Beaches East York.
A recent poll by Liaison Strategies found that while 55% thought not enough housing was being built right now in Ontario, 57% thought enough housing was being built in their community.
Translation, people know we need more housing built, but they don't necessarily want new homes built next to them.
Which is why Crombie opposed the policies she is now proposing when she was mayor of Mississauga. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

LILLEY: Crombie's Liberals fighting NIMBYism but they know it works

Document 143 of 356

That's not very neighbourly; Want a 4-storey building next to you?

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/thats-not-very-neighbourly-want-4-storey-building/docview/2973587087/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=That%27s+not+very+neighbourly%3B+Want+a+4-storey+building+next+to+you%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Should your neighbour be able to knock down their two-storey home in your suburban neighbourhood and put up a four-storey, multi-residential unit? That has been the argument in Ontario provincial politics over the past few days, though you might not know it.
The Ontario Liberals released their new housing plan earlier this week claiming to take bold steps to fix the province's housing affordability crisis. Bill 175, dubbed the "Building Universal and Inclusive Land Development in Ontario Act," makes sweeping changes that prevent municipalities from restricting home building in several ways.
The most sweeping though, as the Liberals highlighted is that the law would "allow up to four residential units and up to four stories to be built as-of-right provincewide on any parcel of urban land that is zoned as 'residential.'" There are also prohibitions on requiring parking for any fourplexes or four-storey buildings and that multi-unit, residential building on any lot can include detached, semi-detached or rowhouses on a lot.
Now, by "as-of-right" what this legislation means is that the owner of the property gets to make the decision and no local council can stop it. This is something that is pushed by housing advocates and planners, but it's not something that would be popular in many residential neighbourhoods - especially low-density areas.
Premier Doug Ford was asked on Thursday about Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie's plan to allow fourplexes.
It's not clear if he didn't hear or didn't understand the question, but he blasted her plan to allow four-storey buildings in any residential neighbourhood.
"I can assure you 1,000%, you go in the middle of communities and start putting up four-storey, six-storey, eight-storey buildings, and right deep in the communities, there's going to be ... a lot of shouting and screaming, that's a massive mistake," Ford said.
On Friday, he was in Hamilton and asked again about fourplexes and answered about four-storey buildings in neighbourhoods.
"If I put a four-storey tower beside you or your neighbours, they'd lose their minds," Ford said, noting that areas of the city like Ancaster, Waterdown or Stoney Creek wouldn't accept tall buildings in your regular suburban neighbourhoods.
While it's unclear if he has understood the questions given that he has been asked about fourplexes and answered by talking about four-storey buildings, it's also not clear that reporters covering Ford understand the difference.
The Liberal plan is clear, it allows fourplexes as-of-right and it allows four-storey buildings as-of-right. That's on any residential lot that isn't considered rural. Don't let the word "urban" fool you - this will apply in any built-up area of town.
Crombie has spent the past few days calling Ford a NIMBY (not in my backyard) housing opponent.
"You've shown your true NIMBY colours, Doug. If you won't build the housing Ontario needs, I will," she posted on X.
Interestingly, when Crombie was mayor of Mississauga, she railed against these same ideas when they were put forward by the province's Housing Affordability Task Force.
"A one-size-fits-all approach will not work," Crombie said in March 2022 in response to the task force's report.
Crombie held a news conference complete with graphics to scare Mississauga residents, warning that they could see four-storey buildings on their streets. Back then, she opposed the provincial task force recommendations - now she's demanding the province implement them.
Crombie is right to suggest that fourplexes as-of-right should go ahead, it's a sensible solution. Ford, though, is right that the public doesn't want a four-storey building going up next to their single or double-storey suburban home.
Both leaders want to build housing and to solve the crisis we need good ideas. That doesn't mean every suggestion put forward needs to be adopted.
Crombie's four-storey as-of-right idea being applied to every community in the province is a non-starter.
The housing advocates will tell her it's a winner, but voters will make her a loser at the ballot box if she pushes it. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Task forces; Neighborhoods; Buildings

LILLEY: Do you want a four-storey building next to your suburban home?

Document 145 of 356

WARMINGTON: Hard to believe it's eight years since we lost Rob Ford

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/warmington-hard-believe-eight-years-since-we-lost/docview/2973463555/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=WARMINGTON%3A+Hard+to+believe+it%27s+eight+years+since+we+lost+Rob+Ford&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: How soon we forget.
Yet, when it comes this bigger-than-life man, how could we? But, yes, it was on March 22, eight years ago, that Toronto lost its 64(th) mayor and one of its most famous ever citizens to cancer.
And while I usually remember the anniversary of the late great Rob Ford's death, this time I didn't.
Until I received a text.
"Just thinking of you today and wondering how you are considering we both lost a dear friend named Rob," said his loyal friend and campaign strategist Louise Boehler. "I know he is watching over us."
If he could look down from his office as mayor of heaven - as his daughter Stephanie so poignantly mentioned at his funeral - I doubt he would like what he sees. Food bank lineups out the door, crime, poverty, tent cities, people losing their homes because of inflation and, of course, carbon taxes.
WARMINGTON: Rob Ford lives, where did the mayoral candidates holiday and what's their fave flick?
WARMINGTON: Doug Ford's win also a victory for his late brother, Rob
The life and death of Rob Ford
It's not that problems didn't exist when he was mayor but what would drive him nuts is to see the move toward more taxation and government control with less focus on keeping the cost of living down.
"There is nothing I enjoy more than putting money back in taxpayer's pockets," Rob Ford told me.
And he backed that up.
On March 22, 2024 we take a moment to remember the legendary 64th mayor of Toronto and Argos fan Rob Ford who died 8 years ago today March 22, 2016. He wouldn't have liked the wasteful spending of today's politics but would have liked 2 see his brother as premier.
- Joe Warmington (@joe_warmington) March 22, 2024
He trimmed Toronto's budget, got rid of the auto-registration tax, and voted against taxpayer's money being spent on soccer stadiums and basketball practice facilities and endless trips.
His at-the-trough politician enemies hated for him for his hard line on wasteful spending.
The Gravy Trainers didn't want him to govern. They did everything they could do to stop him from governing - including having a judge vacate his position as elected mayor thanks to breaking the rule of using his councillor's City of Toronto letterhead to help raise $3,100 for a football foundation to help teens play football.
It was a petty thing to go after him for, but it worked it.
Rob told me it was in the weeks in between that decision and the appeal that reversed it that he hit rock bottom and experimented in a drunken state with crack cocaine - something that would dog him and the city for the next two years.
HOLD THE GRAVY: Tax freeze caps Rob Ford's frantic first day. Toronto Sun frontpage for Thursday November 2, 2010.
When he left himself open thanks to his addictions and demons, his opponents were in the driver's seat and they were relentless. They would follow him everywhere, pick on him, bully and belittle him. There were so many Toronto Sun front pages from the time he called to say how much he was enjoying rehab to his appearance on the Jimmy Kimmel show.
It was a wild time - for Rob Ford and for Toronto. And while he was able to successfully beat the habit, he was not able to beat cancer.
Dead at 46 on March 22, 2016.
It was a huge loss for Toronto and for his family. He was a good person whose heart was in the right place.
As Rex Murphy so rightly said, he was "a man bigger than his flaws," which a lot of people took stock in when the mayor who replaced him resigned from office in his third term after apologizing for having a relationship with a female staffer.
People make mistakes. But nobody chased John Tory around town on that scandal.
Perhaps that was in part because of the lessons learned from the Rob Ford circus. By then, citizens seemed to better realize those who run for public office are only human. They are going to have good days and bad days, but they are not bugs to be squashed. They are public servants who should be held to account but still respected.
Doug and Rob Ford celebrate at a Toronto Maple Leafs game in this undated file photo.
Eight years later, Rob Ford is finally getting the respect he didn't see in life because people now appreciate he had their back when it came to their day-to-day expenses. He didn't squander their money and that can't be said for many of the politicians who came along after him.
Current Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow and current Ontario Premier Doug Ford, Rob's brother, and even former mayor Tory all understand that Rob Ford was a human being but also a very special one who achieved a lot and cared a lot.
This spring they are going to name the football stadium at Centennial Park in his honour, and as long as it doesn't cost taxpayers much, he would have loved that.
In many ways, Rob Ford was like a big teenager who loved football, loved his family, his city, and most especially he loved the idea that government is run for the least amount of dollars possible.
"Have a smile and a laugh in his honour," said Boehler.
And on this eighth anniversary of Rob Ford's death, perhaps shed a tear too.
jwarmington@postmedia.com

Subject: Football; Mayors; Anniversaries

People: Ford, Rob

Identifier / keyword: forget; comes; march; eight; years; toronto

Title: WARMINGTON: Hard to believe it's eight years since we lost Rob Ford

Document 146 of 356

Grits' defence of carbon tax divorced from reality

Author: Goldstein, Lorrie

Publication info: Goldstein, Lorrie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/grits-defence-carbon-tax-divorced-reality/docview/2972993566/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Grits%27+defence+of+carbon+tax+divorced+from+reality&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=Goldstein%2C+Lorrie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Correcting the Trudeau government's political spin defending its carbon tax is like playing whack-amole.
You deal with one nonsensical claim and another pops up.
For example, the false impression the Liberals try to create that the carbon tax is mainly about the price of gasoline and home heating fuel.
In reality, the carbon tax increases the cost of 22 forms of fossil fuel energy.
That's why it increases the cost of almost everything, because almost all goods and services are created using fossil fuels.
On April1, the carbon tax increases by 23% to $80 per tonne of greenhouse gas emissions, up from $65, followed by annual hikes of $15 every April 1 until 2030, when it will be $170 per tonne.
The carbon tax will increase the cost of gasoline by 3 cents a litre to 17.61 cents on April 1, rising to 37.43 cents in 2030.
The cost of natural gas w ill increase by 2.86 cents per cubic metre, to 15.25 cents, rising to 32.40 cents in 2030.
Here's the added cost of the carbon tax on 20 other fuels on April 1, and in 2030: n Propane: 12.38 cents/litre; 26.31 cents. n Aviation gasoline: 19.59 cents/litre; 41.63 cents. n Aviation turbo fuel: 20.65 cents/litre; 43.89 cents. n Butane: 14.24 cents/litre; 30.26 cents. n Coke: $254.38/tonne; $540.55. n Coke oven gas: 5.6 cents/cubic metre; 11.9 cents. n Combustible waste: $159.78/tonne; $339.54. n Ethane: 8.15 cents/litre; 17.3 cents. n Gas liquids: 13.31 cents/litre; 28.28 cents. n Heavy fuel oil: 25.50 cents/litre; 54.19 cents. n High heat value coal: $178.48/tonne; $379.28.
* Kerosene: 20.65 cents/litre; 43.89 cents. n Light fuel oil (diesel): 21.39 cents/litre; 45.45 cents. n Low heat value coal: $141.80/tonne; $301.31. n Methanol: 8.78 cents/litre; 18.67 cents. n Naphtha: 18.03 cents/litre; 38.32 cents. n Non-marketable natural gas: 20.35 cents/cubic metre; 43.25 cents. n Petroleum coke: 30.18 cents/litre; 64.13 cents. n Pentanes plus: 14.24 cents/litre; 30.26 cents. n Still gas: 17.18 cents/cubic metre; 36.5 cents.
The Liberals say 80% of households in the eight provinces paying the carbon tax (Quebec and British Columbia have separate systems) receive more in rebates than they pay in carbon taxes.
But Parliamentary Budget Officer Yves Giroux says that, when the negative economic impact of the carbon tax is factored in, 60% pay more than they receive in rebates, rising to 80% in Nova Scotia in 2025, in Ontario in 2026, in Manitoba in 2029 and in Alberta and Prince Edward Island in 2030.
Here are the PBO's estimated annual net costs (after rebates) for average households paying the carbon tax as of April 1, and in 2030: Alberta $911, $2,773; Ontario $627, $1,820; Saskatchewan $525, $1,723; Manitoba $502, $1,490; Nova Scotia $537, $1,513; P.E.I. $550, $1,521; Newfoundland and Labrador $377, $1,316.
New Brunswick joined the federal system after these estimates were completed, and the carbon tax carve-out for households using home heating oil temporarily reduces their costs for three years.
The Trudeau government argues the PBO didn't factor in the economic costs of climate change.
But claiming the carbon tax will result in financial savings due to less severe weather in Canada by 2030, when our emissions at 1.5% of the global total don't materially impact climate change, as the PBO notes, is absurd.
The government says the carbon tax is revenue neutral.
It's not. The government keeps the GST imposed on top of the carbon tax and hasn't rebated billions of dollars promised to small businesses. lgoldstein@postmedia.com @sunlorrie

Subject: Aviation; Gasoline; Cost control; Emissions; Carbon; Households; Natural gas; Environmental tax; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Cost control Environmental tax Tax increases

Document 147 of 356

'Carbon tax election'; Poilievre challenges Trudeau to let Canadians have say

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/carbon-tax-election-poilievre-challenges-trudeau/docview/2972993526/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Carbon+tax+election%27%3B+Poilievre+challenges+Trudeau+to+let+Canadians+have+say&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pierre Poilievre is demanding a vote of non-confidence in the Trudeau Liberal government and that a carbon tax election be held. Sadly for the many Canadians wanting to see the end of the Trudeau government, we aren't likely to get that election even though the vote will happen Thursday.
Speaking to the Conservative caucus Wednesday morning, Poilievre said it's time for Canadians to have a say on the rising carbon tax.
"I'm announcing that if Trudeau does not declare today an end to his forthcoming tax increases on food, gas and heat, that we will introduce a motion of non-confidence in the prime minister," Poilievre said.
Well, with a vote that happened on Wednesday afternoon, right after question period, Trudeau showed that he won't back down on increasing the carbon tax by 23% on April 1.
An opposition day motion put forward by the Conservatives stated that with 70% of Canadians and 70% of provinces against the carbon tax increase, the government should stop the planned increase.
That vote went down 204-119, with just the Conservatives and Independent MP Kevin Vuong voting to "spike the hike," as Poilievre describes it.
The Liberals were backed by the Bloc Quebecois and Jagmeet Singh's NDP in supporting the carbon tax.
Now that the Liberals have said they won't stop the carbon tax hike, Poilievre has said that he'll table the non-confidence motion on Thursday and the vote will take place Thursday night. Barring some bizarre and unforeseen events, the vote tally on the non-confidence motion will most likely look like what the vote to "spike the hike" looked like.
The NDP is still sticking to their coalition agreement with the Liberals even if it hasn't helped them become more popular with voters. As for the Bloc Quebecois, they are likely to vote against the non-confidence motion, but even if they support it, the numbers will still favour the Trudeau government.
I'd be ecstatic if something weird happened on Thursday and we headed into an election, but that is extremely unlikely.
The Conservatives were right to point out that 70% - 69% if we are being completely accurate - are against the carbon tax hike, as was shown in a recent Leger poll. Seven out of 10 provincial governments have come out against raising the carbon tax, including the Liberal government of Newfoundland and Labrador.
In question period on Wednesday, Poilievre quizzed Trudeau time and again on the carbon tax and its negative effect on Canadians. He noted Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie has rejected the idea of a carbon tax and that the Liberal opposition in Nova Scotia voted with the government to call for the government to pause the hike.
Each time, Trudeau stood and raised the issue of the carbon tax rebate and framed it as an affordability measure.
"Which will it be: Will he spike the hike or will we have a carbon tax election?" Poilievre asked.
It will be that Canadians get more money with the Canada carbon rebate, as 80% of Canadian households in areas where the federal carbon tax applies get more money every year from the rebate than they pay, Trudeau claimed multiple times.
Poilievre's response was to note parliamentary budget officer Yves Giroux testified this week that once all factors were calculated, the carbon tax costs most families.
"Once you factor in the rebate, but also the economic impacts, the majority of households will see a negative impact as a result of the carbon tax," Poilievre quoted Giroux as telling Poilievre won't win the no-con-a Commons committee on Monday. fidence vote but is clearly hoping to continue to ride the wave of opposition to the carbon tax. Trudeau is hoping that Canadians have grown to like the carbon tax rebate and won't want to see it go. rebate, it's because you aren't pay-Of course, if you stop getting the ing the tax anymore, something Trudeau seems to hope you forget. after Thursday, but the campaign-There won't be an election soon ing from all sides will continue. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Document 148 of 356

New Brunswick premier shows the way; Editorial

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-brunswick-premier-shows-way-editorial/docview/2972993460/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+Brunswick+premier+shows+the+way%3B+Editorial&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New Brunswick's provincial government tabled its sixth consecutive balanced budget on Tuesday, which is virtually unheard of in Canada. This is a fiscal approach residents shouldn't take for granted, and policy-makers across the country should adopt.
Since taking office in 2018, Premier Blaine Higgs has been a light of responsible, centre-right governance. He is not a flashy leader; rather, he's measured, determined and disciplined. His Progressive Conservatives have focused on stabilizing the province's precarious finances - turning New Brunswick from a warning sign to a success story.
These are real accomplishments.
Perhaps the biggest indicator is the parliamentary budget officer's judgment that New Brunswick's finances are now sustainable over the long term - one of only five provinces to get this nod.
Change did not happen overnight. Instead, Higgs gradually improved the balance sheet and paid down debt - creating about $100 million of new fiscal room and reducing the impact of punishing debt-servicing charges. More than $2.3 billion in surpluses have accumulated since the PCs took power, according to Public Accounts. (For context, New Brunswick's entire budget comes in at about $13 billion.)
This is a stark contrast to the irresponsibility we've seen in Ottawa, where the federal government has moved its goal posts over and over again to justify ever-higher spending. This year's deficit is expected to reach nearly $50 billion.
It's the kind of policy-making that will saddle future generations with the effects of today's bad decisions. New Brunswick seems to get this point in a way few other provinces do.
Higgs is not without his critics on the left, who point out that surpluses could support higher social spending and that revenue increases owe to higher federal transfers.
Yet these ideas are exactly what got New Brunswick into trouble in the first place. The Liberals'"see a dollar, spend a dollar" routine sent the province careening toward a fiscal cliff, until cooler heads decided to break that cycle.
There have also been right-leaning critics who want Higgs to make substantive reforms. While the premier has delivered modest income tax relief, he hasn't slashed tax rates for a simple reason: it would be premature to do so with so much economic uncertainty, including low growth, high interest rates and inflation still running relatively high.
To be sure, deeper cuts will eventually be needed to make New Brunswick competitive. But the premier is putting his province on a fiscal footing to be able to afford them, without plunging back into deficit.
Others have criticized Higgs'social policy agenda, specifically around gender policies in public schools. But here, too, we find his approach to be the opposite of extreme.
His government's reforms have observed a common-sense distinction between older children aged 16 and up - who can choose to use a different name and pronoun in school - and those who are younger. The latter group needs parental consent to go ahead with such changes.
Far from being a revolution, this is a good balance between individual students'freedoms and parental rights.
It's a dose of maturity and reason in a highly emotional, sometimes hysterical culture war.
This is the kind of leadership we need: not only in Ottawa but in all our provincial capitals.
New Brunswick's example shows that even small, less-advantaged provinces can get ahead through good policy. It's an approach that deserves emulation to get Canada back on track.

Subject: Provinces; Budgets

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: provincial; government; tabled; sixth; consecutive; balanced

Title: New Brunswick premier shows the way; Editorial

GOLDSTEIN: Liberal defence of their carbon tax divorced from reality

Author: Goldstein, Lorrie

Publication info: Goldstein, Lorrie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/goldstein-liberal-defence-their-carbon-tax/docview/2972832466/se-2?accountid=15090

Aviation gasoline: 19.59 cents/litre; 41.63 cents.
Aviation turbo fuel: 20.65 cents/litre; 43.89 cents.
Butane: 14.24 cents/litre; 30.26 cents.
Coke: $254.38/tonne; $540.55.
Coke oven gas: 5.6 cents/cubic metre; 11.9 cents.
Combustible waste: $159.78/tonne; $339.54.
Ethane: 8.15 cents/litre; 17.3 cents.
Gas liquids: 13.31 cents/litre; 28.28 cents.
Heavy fuel oil: 25.50 cents/litre; 54.19 cents.
High heat value coal: $178.48/tonne; $379.28.
Kerosene: 20.65 cents/litre; 43.89 cents.
Light fuel oil (diesel): 21.39 cents/litre; 45.45 cents.
Low heat value coal: $141.80/tonne; $301.31.
Methanol: 8.78 cents/litre; 18.67 cents.
Naphtha: 18.03 cents/litre; 38.32 cents.
Non-marketable natural gas: 20.35 cents/cubic metre; 43.25 cents.
Petroleum coke: 30.18 cents/litre; 64.13 cents.
Pentanes plus: 14.24 cents/litre; 30.26 cents.
Still gas: 17.18 cents/cubic metre; 36.5 cents.

LILLEY: Poilievre challenges Trudeau to hold a carbon tax election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-poilievre-challenges-trudeau-hold-carbon/docview/2972822650/se-2?accountid=15090

That vote went down 204-119, with just the Conservatives and independent MP Kevin Vuong voting to "spike the hike" as Poilievre describes it.
The Liberals were backed by the Bloc Quebecois and Jagmeet Singh's NDP in supporting the carbon tax.
Recommended video
Now that the Liberals have said they won't stop the carbon tax hike, Poilievre has said that he'll table the non-confidence motion on Thursday and the vote will take place Thursday night. Barring some bizarre and unforeseen events, the vote tally on the non-confidence motion will most likely look like what the vote to "spike the hike" looked like.

- Toronto Sun (@TheTorontoSun) March 20, 2024
Each time, Trudeau stood and raised the issue of the carbon tax rebate and framed it as an affordability measure.
"Which will it be: Will he spike the hike or will we have a carbon tax election?" Poilievre asked.
It will be that Canadians get more money with the Canada Carbon Rebate - 80% of Canadian households in areas where the federal carbon tax applies get more money every year from the Canada Carbon Rebate than they pay, Trudeau claimed multiple times.
Poilievre's response was to note Parliamentary Budget Officer Yves Giroux testified this week that once all factors were calculated, the carbon tax costs most families.
"Once you factor in the rebate, but also the economic impacts the majority of households will see a negative impact as a result of the carbon tax," Poilievre quoted Giroux as telling a Commons committee on Monday.
Poilievre won't win the non-confidence vote but is clearly hoping to continue to ride the wave of opposition to the carbon tax. Trudeau is hoping that Canadians have grown to like the carbon tax rebate and won't want to see them go.
Of course, if you stop getting the rebate, it's because you aren't paying the tax anymore, something Trudeau seems to hope you forget.
There won't be an election after Thursday, but the campaigning from all sides will continue.

Title: LILLEY: Poilievre challenges Trudeau to hold a carbon tax election

Document 151 of 356

EDITORIAL: N.B. premier shows the way

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/editorial-n-b-premier-shows-way/docview/2972802315/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=EDITORIAL%3A+N.B.+premier+shows+the+way&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New Brunswick's provincial government tabled its sixth consecutive balanced budget on Tuesday, which is virtually unheard of in Canada. This is a fiscal approach residents shouldn't take for granted, and policy-makers across the country should adopt.
Since taking office in 2018, Premier Blaine Higgs has been a light of responsible, centre-right governance. He is not a flashy leader; rather, he's measured, determined and disciplined. His Progressive Conservatives have focused on stabilizing the province's precarious finances - turning New Brunswick from a warning sign to a success story.
These are real accomplishments. Perhaps the biggest indicator is the parliamentary budget officer's judgment that New Brunswick's finances are now sustainable over the long term - one of only five provinces to get this nod.
Change did not happen overnight. Instead, Higgs gradually improved the balance sheet and paid down debt - creating about $100 million of new fiscal room and reducing the impact of punishing debt-servicing charges. More than $2.3 billion in surpluses have accumulated since the PCs took power, according to Public Accounts. (For context, New Brunswick's entire budget comes in at about $13 billion.)
This is a stark contrast to the irresponsibility we've seen in Ottawa, where the federal government has moved its goal posts over and over again to justify ever-higher spending. This year's deficit is expected to reach nearly $50 billion.
It's the kind of policy-making that will saddle future generations with the effects of today's bad decisions. New Brunswick seems to get this point in a way few other provinces do.
Higgs is not without his critics on the left, who point out that surpluses could support higher social spending and that revenue increases owe to higher federal transfers.
Yet these ideas are exactly what got New Brunswick into trouble in the first place. The Liberals' "see a dollar, spend a dollar" routine sent the province careening toward a fiscal cliff, until cooler heads decided to break that cycle.
There have also been right-leaning critics who want Higgs to make substantive reforms. While the premier has delivered modest income tax relief, he hasn't slashed tax rates for a simple reason: it would be premature to do so with so much economic uncertainty, including low growth, high interest rates and inflation still running relatively high.
To be sure, deeper cuts will eventually be needed to make New Brunswick competitive. But the premier is putting his province on a fiscal footing to be able to afford them, without plunging back into deficit.
Others have criticized Higgs's social policy agenda, specifically around gender policies in public schools. But here, too, we find his approach to be the opposite of extreme.
His government's reforms have observed a common-sense distinction between older children aged 16 and up - who can choose to use a different name and pronoun in school - and those who are younger. The latter group needs parental consent to go ahead with such changes.
Far from being a revolution, this is a good balance between individual students' freedoms and parental rights. It's a dose of maturity and reason in a highly emotional, sometimes hysterical culture war.
This is the kind of leadership we need: not only in Ottawa, but in all our provincial capitals. New Brunswick's example shows that even small, less-advantaged provinces can get ahead through good policy. It's an approach that deserves emulation to get Canada back on track.

Subject: Provinces; Budgets

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets

Location: Canada

Premier 'smoking'mad over federal carbon tax increase

Author: Williams, Brian

Publication info: Williams, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-smokingmad-over-federal-carbon-tax/docview/2969264412/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+%27smoking%27mad+over+federal+carbon+tax+increase&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Williams%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford came out swinging Tuesday against the federal carbon tax in a speech to hundreds of grain farmers from across the province who are meeting in London.
Ford was a last-minute addition to speak at the 2024 March Classic, the largest grain-focused conference in Eastern Canada, which was held at RBC Place convention centre.
He declared his staunch opposition to the federal government's looming carbon tax increase.
"You know all it does is gouge people," Ford said. "I don't know what the guy is smoking up there, but it's not beneficia ... " It wasn't immediately clear whether Ford was referring to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau with the "smoking" remark.
The carbon tax is set to increase "by a staggering 23%" on April 1, Ford said, calling it detrimental to taxpayers and referring to it as a big "scheme or scam."
A carbon tax is designed to aid the environment by reducing greenhouse gas emissions.
Speaking to the audience, Ford said it takes money out of people's pockets and will "increase the cost of every product you produce."
Federal government officials have said a rebate system ensures the vast majority of Canadians get back more than they pay in carbon taxes, with the benefits weighted to aid lowerincome families.
To combat a future carbon tax increase, Ford said last month his government introduced new legislation to require future provincial governments to hold a referendum before "imposing a carbon tax scheme." bwilliams@postmedia.com The Local Journalism Initiative is funded by the Government of Canada

Trudeau'S Great 'Gouge'; PM in full defensive mode to save his carbon tax hike

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeaus-great-gouge-pm-full-defensive-mode-save/docview/2969263947/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau%27S+Great+%27Gouge%27%3B+PM+in+full+defensive+mode+to+save+his+carbon+tax+hike&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau wants you to know that life would be a lot worse without his carbon tax rebate.
The Liberals are now selling the carbon tax, and the rebate that comes with it, as an affordability message, even though the carbon tax is part of what is making everything more expensive.
Pierre Poilievre's Conservative Party has put forward a motion calling the Trudeau government to can-cel its 23% carbon tax increase scheduled for April 1.
"The leader of the Opposition wants to take away those Canada Carbon Rebate cheques from Canadian families," Trudeau thundered in the Commons.
He said those rebates benefit families, a claim he has made since he announced his carbon tax plan back in 2018 and a claim that has been disputed by anyone with open eyes.
There are two main problems with Trudeau's carbon tax rebate system and the claim that eight out of 10 families get back more than they pay. The rebate calculation is based on averages rather than what you pay, and as the Parliamentary Budget Office has shown, the claim doesn't include all the other economic impacts.
When calculating your rebate, the government uses averages from Statistics Canada for such measurements as driving. It uses the figure of roughly 15,000 km per year for the rebate, even though many of you reading this now, especially suburban and rural readers, drive far more than that.
When it calculates the cost of heating a home, it again uses averages and average emissions, mean-ing the 525-square-foot condo in downtown Toronto is thrown into the mix with the large suburban Anyone raising a family in the house for a family raising three kids. suburbs or rural areas of this country is most certainly paying more than they're getting back in the carbon tax.
Then there are other economic costs.
Still, I'll concede that the PBO found that in using the government's formula, including averages, most families will get back more than they pay. Yet, when looking at the whole economic pic-ture, the total cost of the tax means most families are worse off financially thanks "When both fiscal and to the carbon tax.

Document 155 of 356

LILLEY: Trudeau in full defensive mode to save his carbon tax hike

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-trudeau-full-defensive-mode-save-his/docview/2968977347/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Trudeau+in+full+defensive+mode+to+save+his+carbon+tax+hike&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau wants you to know that life would be a lot worse without his carbon tax rebate. The Liberals are now selling the carbon tax, and the rebate that comes with it, as an affordability message, even though the carbon tax is part of what is making everything more expensive.
Pierre Poilievre's Conservative Party has put forward a motion calling the Trudeau government to cancel their 23% carbon tax increase scheduled for April 1.
"The leader of the Opposition wants to take away those Canada Carbon Rebate cheques from Canadian families," Trudeau thundered in the Commons.
Trudeau said those rebates benefit families, a claim he's made since he announced his carbon tax plan back in 2018 and a claim that's been disputed by anyone with open eyes.
There are two main problems with Trudeau's carbon tax rebate system and the claim that eight out of 10 families get back more than they pay. The rebate calculation is based on averages rather than what you pay, and as the Parliamentary Budget Office has shown, the claim doesn't include all the other economic impacts.
When calculating your rebate, the government uses averages from Statistics Canada for measurements such as driving. They use of the figure of roughly 15,000 kilometres per year for the rebate, even though many of you reading this now, especially suburban and rural readers, drive far more than that.
When they calculate the cost of heating a home, they again use averages and average emissions, meaning the 525 square-foot condo in downtown Toronto is thrown into the mix with the large suburban house for a family raising three kids.
Anyone raising a family in the suburbs or rural areas of this country is most certainly paying more than they are getting back in the carbon tax.
Then there are other economic costs.
Still, I'll concede that the PBO found using the government's formula, including averages, that most families will get back more than they pay. Yet, when looking at the whole economic picture, the total cost of the tax means most families are worse off financially thanks to the carbon tax.
Recommended video
"When both fiscal and economic impacts of the federal fuel charge are considered, we estimate that most households will see a net loss," Parliamentary Budget Officer Yves Giroux said in a report released last March
"Based on our analysis, most households will pay more in fuel charges and GST - as well as receiving slightly lower incomes - than they will receive in Climate Action Incentive payments."
When Poilievre stands up in the House of Commons and tells Trudeau that his tax will cost Canadians more, this is what he is talking about - the total cost. When Trudeau stands up and replies only that eight out of 10 will get back more than they pay, he's talking about the tax without considering the economic impact on the whole country and on your finances.
"You know, all it does is gouge people," Ontario Premier Doug Ford said Tuesday in London, Ont. "I don't know what the guy is smoking up there, but it's not beneficial. It hurts people, everything from transporting goods from point A to point B, going to pick your kids up at a hockey game or sports facility."
And with that you have summing up the sentiment of most Canadians.
A Leger poll conducted for the Canadian Taxpayers' Federation asked the simple question, "Do you support or oppose the federal government increasing the carbon tax?" A very strong majority (69%) said they opposed the tax hike, while 31% said they supported it.
So what will Trudeau do come April 1?
He's going to ignore the popular will of the people, he's going to ignore the stated desires of the majority of provinces, and he will increase the tax by 23% - from $65 to $80 a tonne.
Trudeau said recently that it's not his job to be popular; he's doing well at that part.

Fed-up Reddit users planning boycott of all Loblaw stores

Author: Wilford, Denette

Publication info: Wilford, Denette. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/fed-up-reddit-users-planning-boycott-all-loblaw/docview/2968936755/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Fed-up+Reddit+users+planning+boycott+of+all+Loblaw+stores&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Wilford%2C+Denette&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Fed-up Reddit users are planning to boycott Loblaw stores and have already identified alternative places to shop.
Emily Johnson created a subreddit called Loblaws Is Out of Control last November, which was intended for people to vent about Loblaw grocery prices amid their increasing profits as Canadians continue to struggle.
"I think that there had been a lot of frustration and resentment that had been building already," Johnson said in an interview last month.
"And this was kind of the straw that broke the camel's back."
The group, which now has more than 25,000 members, a.k.a. "broke Canadians," has taken to sharing photos of overpriced items, tips on how to save, and which of Loblaw's competitors may offer more bang for your buck.
But the community has now decided to take action and physically boycott the store.
It's something many people threaten to do, but the group means business.
"Our community has taken the time to organize a movement which aims to boycott Loblaw stores until prices can be reduced," Johnson explained on the page
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
"Loblaw, and other major grocers in Canada enjoy the benefits of a monopoly on an essential service, and force us to pay utterly ridiculous prices," her message continued.
"Canadians are facing a cost of living crisis, and grocers are a major contributor to this. Vulnerable populations such as seniors, persons with disabilities, and those on fixed incomes are left further behind. Food banks across the country are seeing a drastic increase in demand."
As a result, the group has organized a boycott of Loblaw stores to "demand action in order to provide relief to Canadians."
Among the asks of the grocery giant, the group wants a 15% reduction in prices and for Loblaw to remove its member-only pricing associated with its PC Optimum card.
The community is also working on a petition that will be sent to the House of Commons.
"Be mindful in your shopping; try to avoid non-essential items being purchased at Loblaw stores wherever possible," the group suggested.
CHARLEBOIS: The Loblaw saga - corporate missteps in the aisles
'FELT LIKE A THIEF:' Loblaw stores sparks outrage as it tests receipt scanners
Loblaw to build more than 40 new stores as part of $2-billion investment plan
The affected banners are Loblaws, Real Canadian Superstore, No Frills, Zehrs, Fortinos, Valu-Mart, Wholesale Club, Your Independent Grocer, T&T Supermarket, PC Financial and Shoppers Drug Mart in Ontario.
Others stores across the country are Atlantic Superstore, Dominion, Extra Foods, Freshmart, Maxi, Provigo and SuperValu.
The group also provided dozens of other options for Canadians to shop, broken down by province and territory.
The boycott is set to take place for the month of May.
The Toronto Sun reached out to Loblaw for comment but did not receive a response back before publication.

Document 157 of 356

Trudeau should end undemocratic alcohol tax hikes

Author: Terrazzano, Franco

Publication info: Terrazzano, Franco. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-should-end-undemocratic-alcohol-tax-hikes/docview/2968634077/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+should+end+undemocratic+alcohol+tax+hikes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=Terrazzano%2C+Franco&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Getting hit in the face twice is better than getting hit four times. But you know what's even better? Not getting hit at all.
Canada's Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland recently announced she would increase alcohol taxes by 2% on April 1, instead of the planned 4.7% tax hike.
A smaller tax hike is better, but even at 2%, this year's alcohol tax hike will cost taxpayers $40 million.
The feds shouldn't be hiking alcohol taxes at all, especially when businesses and consumers are already struggling with higher costs.
And the government should end the undemocratic escalator that automatically increases alcohol taxes every year without a single vote in the House of Commons.
First passed in Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's 2017 federal budget, the alcohol escalator tax automatically increases excise taxes on beer, wine and spirits every year by the rate of inflation, without a vote in Parliament.
Since then, this undemocratic tax escalator has cost taxpayers more than $700 million across beer, wine and liquor, according to Beer Canada.
If members of Parliament think Canadians aren't paying enough tax, then they should at least have the spine to vote on the hike. They shouldn't hide behind an automatic increase that allows them to take more from Canadians every year.
Canadians already pay an arm and a leg in taxes every time they go to a bar or a liquor store.
Taxes already make up more than half the price of alcohol.
Let's say you're having friends and family over for a celebration. If you buy a couple of cases of beer, a couple of bottles of wine and a couple of bottles of spirits, you can expect to pay about $210. More than $110 of that is tax.
In fact, Canadians pay five times more tax on a case of beer than our friends south of the border. In Saskatchewan, Prince Edward Island and Newfoundland and Labrador, the tax on a case of beer costs more than the total price of a case in half of American states.
While Canadians are paying higher taxes, Americans are enjoying tax cuts. From 2017 to 2019, Canadian beer taxes went up $34 million for large brewers while American beer taxes went down by $31 million.
The Ontario government has a similar automatic tax escalator as the feds. But one big difference is that Premier Doug Ford has chosen to continually cancel the government's scheduled beer tax hikes.
In February, the Ford government announced it would stop its 4.6% beer tax hike scheduled for March. In fact, since 2018, the Ford government has consistently cancelled its scheduled beer tax hikes, providing about $200 million in relief.
"This pause on beer basic tax and LCBO mark-up rates will help make it easier for businesses by providing savings for consumers and helping brewers to reinvest in themselves and their workers," Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said when making the announcement.
To Freeland and Trudeau's credit, they are providing some tax relief. The government is cutting its excise tax in half for the first 15,000 hectolitres of beer brewed in Canada.
But that isn't enough. The government shouldn't be raising any alcohol taxes. And it should end its undemocratic automatic escalator that increases alcohol taxes every year without a vote in Parliament.
Franco Terrazzano is federal director of the Canadian Taxpayers Federation

Document 158 of 356

Crombie come lately; Responds to last month's tax tumult, but silent on today's hike worries

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombie-come-lately-responds-last-months-tax/docview/2968633770/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie+come+lately%3B+Responds+to+last+month%27s+tax+tumult%2C+but+silent+on+today%27s+hike+worries&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bonnie Crombie said that neither she nor the Liberal party will bring in a carbon tax if they win the next Ontario election. She still hasn't said if she supports a carbon tax though, and that still leaves her vulnerable to attacks from Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives.
After spending months being attacked by Ford's PCs as the "Queen of the Carbon Tax," Crombie showed up with answers to an old debate without realizing the conversation had changed.
A month ago, Crombie was asked during a news conference at Queen's Park whether she supported a carbon tax. She wouldn't answer then and she wouldn't answer on live TV when pressed at least nine times during CTV's Power Play. Crombie would only say that she would speak to experts and study the issue. Even when asked about the federal carbon tax, she pivoted back to that talking point.
Well, on Monday, she announced her expert panel that will study Ontario's climate response, but promised there would be no carbon tax.
"Let me be very clear, a carbon tax will not be part of my plan," Crombie said in a video released on social media.
Seems that perhaps polling - not her expert panel - informed her decision.
But since then, the conversation around the carbon tax has shifted.
Now the main issue is the looming federal carbon tax hike of 23% from $65 to $80 a tonne on April 1. That's an issue that Crombie's Ontario Liberals don't want to talk about, even if Liberals in other provinces are willing to speak out.
Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Andrew Furey wrote to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau last week asking him to pause the planned carbon tax hike. He was not alone on that front as the premiers of Nova Scotia, P.E.I., New Brunswick, Ontario, Saskatchewan and Alberta all asked Trudeau to stop the planned tax hike, pointing to affordability issues.
Even New Brunswick's Liberal opposition backed of spiking the hike, as federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre likes to put it.
"At a time when so many New Brunswickers are struggling to make ends meet, it's unreasonable to ask them to pay more for a federal carbon tax increase," Susan Holt, leader of the New Brunswick Liberals, said in a statement.
Those weren't words that Crombie or her MPPs at Queen's Park were willing to utter. Asked several times about the federal carbon tax, Liberal MPPs John Fraser and Mary-Margaret McMahon didn't offer much in the way of a response.
"We're not going to tell the federal government what to do. We're working on Ontario. That's a big enough chore in itself," McMahon said at Queen's Park.
Fraser added: "You know, it's this is the federal government's business. We've made it very clear that a Liberal climate change (plan) will not include a carbon tax."
Both MPPs said there is an affordability crisis in the province, but without addressing that the coming increase will only make that worse.
In response to questions put to her office, Crombie accused Ford of being obsessed with weighing in on federal politics.
"The prime minister is getting a lot of advice on this particular question and he doesn't need more advice from me," Crombie said.
"Instead, I am focused on delivering the most ambitious plan for climate action this province has ever seen - one that does not place any additional costs on the people of Ontario."
What Ford is obsessed with, along with the premiers of seven other provinces, is what the additional costs of the 23% carbon tax hike and the scheduled annual increases will mean for their constituents. Crombie should have been prepared to answer this question fully on a day when she was announcing her plan instead of dodging a major issue.
The Liberal opposition leader in New Brunswick has an opinion. The NDP premier of Manitoba wants the tax scrapped. Everyone, except Crombie, has an opinion. She's still giving answers to last month's debate. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Carbon; Prime ministers; Climate change; Environmental tax; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax Tax increases

Identifier / keyword: bonnie; crombie; liberal; party; bring; carbon

Gov't paid Tory-bashing T.O. TikToker $9Gs: Report

Author: Connor, Kevin

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/govt-paid-tory-bashing-t-o-tiktoker-9gs-report/docview/2968633278/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Gov%27t+paid+Tory-bashing+T.O.+TikToker+%249Gs%3A+Report&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Connor%2C+Kevin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Department of Canadian Heritage paid thousands of dollars to a Toronto TikTok personality who has disparaged Conservatives in a number of profane posts, access-to-information records show.
Documents show Dylan Horner was paid $9,000 by the government in 2022 for a brief appearance in Ottawa, according to contracts issued through Corus Sales Inc. of Toronto.
Horner did not comment. Corus Sales was contracted to "run a promotional campaign for activities related to Canada Day," said a statement of work obtained by Blacklock's Reporter.
"Dylan Horner will be required to come to Ottawa to create content on Canada Day programming," it said.
"Dylan Horner will have special backstage access to produce behind-the-scenes content, but will need to be accompanied by a member of Department of Canadian Heritage staff."
Expenses included "two roundtrip train tickets from Toronto to Ottawa with Via Rail Canada and three nights'stay in a hotel room at the Delta Ottawa."
Horner has trashed Conservative politicians in a series of posts.
"Oh my God, shut up," he said of Opposition leader Pierre Poilievre. "Like actually shut the f---up. Shut the f---up. This is exactly why you can't trust Conservatives."
Horner slammed Premier Doug Ford by posting he was "100% convinced we all lose brain cells listening to this man speak."
On Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, Horner wrote, "It is always the b------who look like they would tear up at a Sears'clearance rack who have their nose in the business of trans youth, always."
Horner, in a post about a 2021 federal election TV debate, called then-Opposition leader Erin O'Toole a "pile of crap" and questioned why anyone would vote Conservative.

Document 161 of 356

TERRAZZANO: Trudeau should end undemocratic alcohol tax hikes

Author: Chau, Eddie

Publication info: Chau, Eddie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/terrazzano-trudeau-should-end-undemocratic/docview/2967585327/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TERRAZZANO%3A+Trudeau+should+end+undemocratic+alcohol+tax+hikes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Chau%2C+Eddie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Getting hit in the face twice is better than getting hit four times. But you know what's even better? Not getting hit at all.
Canada's Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland recently announced she would increase alcohol taxes by 2% on April 1, instead of the planned 4.7% tax hike.
A smaller tax hike is better, but even at 2%, this year's alcohol tax hike will cost taxpayers $40 million.
The feds shouldn't be hiking alcohol taxes at all, especially when businesses and consumers are already struggling with higher costs.
And the government should end the undemocratic escalator that automatically increases alcohol taxes every year without a single vote in the House of Commons.
First passed in Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's 2017 federal budget, the alcohol escalator tax automatically increases excise taxes on beer, wine and spirits every year by the rate of inflation, without a vote in Parliament.
Since then, this undemocratic tax escalator has cost taxpayers more than $700 million across beer, wine and liquor, according to Beer Canada.
If members of Parliament think Canadians aren't paying enough tax, then they should at least have the spine to vote on the hike. They shouldn't hide behind an automatic increase that allows them to take more from Canadians every year.
Canadians already pay an arm and a leg in taxes every time they go to a bar or a liquor store.
Taxes already make up more than half the price of alcohol.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
Let's say you're having friends and family over for a celebration. If you buy a couple of cases of beer, a couple of bottles of wine and a couple of bottles of spirits, you can expect to pay about $210. More than $110 of that is tax.
In fact, Canadians pay five times more tax on a case of beer than our friends south of the border. In Saskatchewan, Prince Edward Island and Newfoundland and Labrador, the tax on a case of beer costs more than the total price of a case in half of American states.
While Canadians are paying higher taxes, Americans are enjoying tax cuts. From 2017 to 2019, Canadian beer taxes went up $34 million for large brewers while American beer taxes went down by $31 million.
The Ontario government has a similar automatic tax escalator as the feds. But one big difference is that Premier Doug Ford has chosen to continually cancel the government's scheduled beer tax hikes.
In February, the Ford government announced it would stop its 4.6% beer tax hike scheduled for March. In fact, since 2018, the Ford government has consistently cancelled its scheduled beer tax hikes, providing about $200 million in relief.
"This pause on beer basic tax and LCBO mark-up rates will help make it easier for businesses by providing savings for consumers and helping brewers to reinvest in themselves and their workers," Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said when making the announcement.
To Freeland and Trudeau's credit, they are providing some tax relief. The government is cutting its excise tax in half for the first 15,000 hectolitres of beer brewed in Canada.
But that isn't enough.
The government shouldn't be raising any alcohol taxes. And it should end its undemocratic automatic escalator that increases alcohol taxes every year without a vote in Parliament.
Franco Terrazzano is federal director of the Canadian Taxpayers Federation

Document 163 of 356

Time for common sense to replace Trudeau's wokester era

Author: Snobelen, John

Publication info: Snobelen, John. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/time-common-sense-replace-trudeaus-wokester-era/docview/2957812847/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Time+for+common+sense+to+replace+Trudeau%27s+wokester+era&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=Snobelen%2C+John&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: They say leadership is causing something's time to come. Starting a wave is way better than trying to catch one or, worse, swimming against the current.
Justin Trudeau caught a wave back in 2015. He was woke when woke was cool. He and his fellow travellers were all over global warming. Heck, just tax that nasty carbon.
Fiscal policy was boring stuff.
The budget would balance itself.
Indigenous re l ations just needed a guy in a fringed jacket paddling a canoe.
It was as easy as being cool. Or, following in a family tradition, simply being cooler than Michael Ignatieff and Stephen Harper.
But that was then. It seems these days, Justin Trudeau, much like skinny jeans, has fallen out of fashion. Mr. Sunny Ways is heading into storm clouds.
It was b ound to happen.
Trudeau and his happy climate warriors were in fine shape when all it took to be cool was taking a meeting with Greta Thunberg. But, when the rubber hit the road, the climate scheming imploded under the weight of its hubris.
It seems no matter how the Liberals package it, Canadians are just not buying the notion that taxing their food and heating will have any impact on the climate.
Go figure.
The same goes for the Aboriginal file. Great rhetoric and significant paddling skills can't cover up the kind of leadership that tossed Jody Wilson-Raybould from the Liberal party.
The biggest blow was probably that business about the budget balancing itself. Trudeau is eight budgets into governing. He has grown the debt and deficit at a record pace and that legacy is chasing him hard.
Next year, the government will spend twice as much on paying interest as it does on defence. And that debt just keeps growing as the annual deficit adds more burden on the backs of Canadian taxpayers. That is decidedly uncool.
Those policy failures may be easier to fix than the eight-year decline in the once mighty Canadian public service. These days, the public service doesn't seem to be able to do much of anything and it doesn't seem to notice that it can't.
Can't make payroll. Can't buy an app. Can't buy ships, or planes or vaccines. Heck, it can't even run an inquiry into buying an app or foreign interference or Trudeau's assorted vacations.
Turns out governing is serious business. Who knew? Wokesters might be many things, but they are decidedly not serious. Take, for instance, the recent decision of Supreme Court Justice Sheilah Martin. She ruled that the word 'woman'was confusing, preferring the term "person with a vagina."
Seriously? I can assure the learned Justice that, after many years on the planet, I can with reasonable certainty identify a woman. But I am totally baffled how I would, without a significant violation of privacy, determine who has a vagina.
Woke is over. It was in fashion for a moment in a decidedly unserious time.
Justin Trudeau has exhausted his hour on the stage. Serious times call for something more than a faded rockstar from the woke era.
Now is a great time to get serious about climate mitigation, public service that serves the public, budgets balanced by governments with priorities, investment in a modern and focused military, and above all else, a government that knows when to leave people alone.
All of that might sound revolutionary - and it is. Think of it as a Common Sense Revolution.
Snobelen was a cabinet minister in the Conservative government of Ontario premier Mike Harris from 1995 to 2002

Subject: Vagina

People: Trudeau, Justin

Identifier / keyword: leadership; causing; starting; better; trying; catch

Title: Time for common sense to replace Trudeau's wokester era

Document 164 of 356

Can't shut down Trudeau; Hateful pro-Hamas protesters don't deter PM

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cant-shut-down-trudeau-hateful-pro-hamas/docview/2957812758/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Can%27t+shut+down+Trudeau%3B+Hateful+pro-Hamas+protesters+don%27t+deter+PM&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: They came with their flags, their placards, their keffiyehs and their chants of there being "only one solution" for Israel.
The mob that shut down Justin Trudeau's reception with the Italian prime minister two weeks ago tried to also shut down his party fundraiser on Friday night.
But this time Toronto Police showed up and did their job. Or, perhaps more correctly, they were allowed to do their job.
There were barricades in the streets stopping protesters from blocking the entrances to the King Edward Hotel on King St. E. - east of Yonge St. - where Trudeau was hosting his event. Police had proper control of the side streets and side entrances of the hotel, something sorely missing two weeks ago when police obviously failed to plan for a demonstration at the Art Gallery of Ontario that they should have known about.
A more cynical person might say the police were ensuring Trudeau could go ahead with collecting money from donors. The event billed at $1,725 per person to hear the PM speak about issues like the affordability crisis according to Liberal Party promotional material.
Regardless of the reason for the event, the Prime Minister, and even average citizens, should be able to go about their daily lives without having it shut down by the mob.
"We will not allow the Canadian government to proceed with business as usual and we will amplify our demands," the Palestinian Youth Movement wrote in a social media post.
That post included a call to shut down Trudeau's fundraiser for Palestine - something they weren't able to do this time. Not that they didn't try, and not that they didn't show up to chant the most despicable things.
"There is only one solution," a slight young woman chanted into a microphone.
After the audience repeated the chant, she followed up with the second half of it.
"Intifada revolution," she called out for the audience to repeat.
Only one solution sounds a little ominous to Jewish ears, like a hint of the Holocaust, but that is the point. The intifada revolution, that's a call for a never-ending war against Israel and the Jewish people until they are no more, or at least subservient in the Middle East.
Just like there is no way to explain away the real meaning of the genocidal chant "from the river to the sea," there is no way to explain away what these other chants mean.
The protesters had words for Prime Minister Trudeau as well, calling him Genocide Justin and claiming he has blood on his hands.
I will say as a staunch critic of Trudeau that these claims have no merit and never did, but especially not after the past several weeks. Trudeau has called for a ceasefire, he has restored funding to UNRWA, he has blocked military sales to Israel and the mob outside the King Eddy hotel still wasn't satisfied.
The Trudeau government has been slowly but surely abandoning Israel, Canada's long-standing ally, and moving towards the Hamas side of the equation. It still isn't enough for this mob who will never be happy, never be satisfied until they hear Trudeau or a future Canadian leader also chanting "from the river to the sea" or another genocidal chant against Israel.
The protesters actually stood outside the hotel and chanted, "Justin Trudeau what do you say? How many kids have you killed today?" The answer is obviously none, but this movement is so blinded by their hatred of Israel, and Jews, that they will make false claims against someone who is mostly on their side but also speaks up against hatred and anti-Semitism.
"We are the intifada," they chanted as the sun set.
It's a lovely thought that people who support the intifada, the random killing of people just because they are Jewish, are currently roaming our streets. Not only that, but they are also proudly proclaiming their views in the open.
Canada has changed these last several years - and not for the better. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Prime ministers; Genocide; Art galleries & museums

Business indexing term: Subject: Art galleries & museums

Location: Canada; Israel
