MANDEL: Career criminal pleads guilty to manslaughter in deadly hit-and-run

Author: Mandel, Michele

Publication info: Mandel, Michele. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mandel-career-criminal-pleads-guilty-manslaughter/docview/2957761176/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MANDEL%3A+Career+criminal+pleads+guilty+to+manslaughter+in+deadly+hit-and-run&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Mandel%2C+Michele&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Jamil Nazarali wanted to celebrate his girlfriend's birthday in style, so he and his friends rented a boat to cruise Lake Ontario.
How could they ever expect that summer evening of fun would end in tragedy when Nazarali returned to Polson Pier and crossed paths with a "monster?"
That man is Robert Cada, 34, a career criminal who first made headlines in 2012 - let out from jail due to a clerical error, he was in the food court with his Regent Park buddies when he narrowly missed being shot by Christopher Husbands in the melee that killed two of his childhood friends.
You'd think that being given a second chance at life would mend your ways. But you'd be wrong.
On July 19, 2021, this time on bail from drug trafficking charges in London, Ont. and wearing a GPS ankle bracelet, Cada admits he killed Nazarali and injured his friend when he ran them down following an argument in the Polson Pier parking lot.
Originally charged with first-degree murder, he's pleaded guilty to manslaughter and aggravated assault.
Jamil Nazarali and his girlfriend Lily.
"I want to be clear - I was not out looking to harm or hurt anyone," Cada said Friday from Beaver Creek Institution, his empty words offering no comfort to the dead man's grieving family. "This tragedy keeps me up at night and I wish with all my heart it did not occur."
Crown attorney Bev Richards and defence lawyer Nabeel Sheiban have jointly proposed a 15-year sentence to run consecutively to the seven-year term Cada is currently serving for possession of 144 grams of fentanyl for the purposes of trafficking.
Just 25, Nazarali co-owned a burrito franchise in Whitby with his brother Alim and had purchased a pre-construction condo with dreams of getting married and starting a family.
"His future was bright and he was so excited," Alim wrote in his victim impact statement that was read into court. "We would say, 'What a time to be alive.'"
Jamil Nazarali, right, who was killed in a deliberate hit and run on Polson St. on July 19, 2021, is seen here with his brother Alim.
On that summer evening, Nazarali and his group had disembarked from the boat at Polson Pier just before 9 p.m. and were arranging to get an Uber to take them home when an argument broke out at the roundabout with a group of males.
According to the agreed statement of facts, Nazarali and his friends were looking at his green matte F-150 when Cada and his passenger suddenly got out of his truck in the middle of Polson St. and a loud dispute erupted.
"The deceased was heard yelling 'Get me my shank, get me my shank' multiple times by independent witnesses."
At 9:05 p.m., the statement says, Cada got back into his F-150 and drove at the pedestrians, running over Nazarali and sending his friend Thisanth Kulasingham flying into the air. Cada then fled the scene, leaving Nazarali dead on the pavement and Kulasingham injured.
An image released by Toronto Police shows Robert Cada, 32, of Toronto, after an alleged deliberate fatal hit-run on Polson St. on July 19, 2021.
Nazarali was the youngest child; the baby they all doted on.
"Our family is broken because we have lost our precious son," his father Alnoor wrote in his victim impact statement. "It has been impossible to continue to live without him."
His sister Rosemina told the court she's been diagnosed with PTSD.
"I feared every stranger I crossed paths with because my brother was killed by a complete and total stranger; I've constantly feared since then that I would cross paths with another monster capable of the same thing," she said.
"On July 19, 2021, Jamil Nazarali went to celebrate his girlfriend's birthday and he never came home. He should have been able to come home. I wish every day that I could go back and bring him home to his family, where he belongs."
WARMINGTON: It started as a special waterfront birthday party but ended in murder
WARMINGTON: Night out for fun ends in senseless violence outside Toronto club
WARMINGTON: Man wanted in Polson St. murder dodged Eaton Centre bullets in 2012
Cada's lawyer told the court that he is now getting in touch with his Aboriginal heritage and he's had a "difficult life" - born to a 15-year-old mother who was incarcerated until he was three, with an "unstable childhood" in a family that struggles with alcoholism.
According to his London sentencing, Cada's long criminal record includes trafficking, robbery, aggravated assault and assault with a weapon.
What a contrast with a young man who was working hard to realize his dreams.
"The question to the person who is responsible for his death is to look at what he has taken from us," said Nazarali's dad. "What will he do in his remaining days of his life on this earth to make up for this unimaginable loss?"
Superior Court Justice Maureen Forestell will sentence Cada next week.
mmandel@postmedia.com

Subject: Birthdays; Robbery; Murders & murder attempts; Criminal pleas; Manslaughter

Kitchener residents escape armed carjacking in viral video

Author: Wilford, Denette

Publication info: Wilford, Denette. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/kitchener-residents-escape-armed-carjacking-viral/docview/2957746761/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Kitchener+residents+escape+armed+carjacking+in+viral+video&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Wilford%2C+Denette&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In Toronto, home invasions and break-and-enters for auto thefts rose 400% in 2023.
And it's just as bad in the rest of Ontario, with a vehicle stolen every 14 minutes in the province.
Things don't look to be changing anytime soon, especially when Toronto Police suggest making things easier for thieves to steal your vehicles by leaving your keys by the front door.
The latest carjacking caught on camera occurred in Kitchener earlier this week.
On Monday at about 10 p.m., Waterloo Regional Police responded to a report of a carjacking in the area of Elmsdale Dr. and Huntsworth Ave.
The victim was in the parking lot of their residence when they were approached by multiple carjackers, according to police.
Carjacking in Kitchener. ??
- Justice_Queen ?? ???? (@RE_MarketWatch) March 14, 2024
The victim was forced out of their vehicle when a firearm was brandished towards them, cops said.
As seen on the video, the victim and others - one of whom appeared to be holding an infant carrier - were able to escape unharmed and the suspects got away with the vehicle (after a painful multi-point turn in the parking lot) before police arrived on scene.
The vehicle was later located in the area of Marina Rd. and Belmont Ave., about five kilometres away.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
Last month, the federal government earmarked $121 million to combat car theft and gang activity in Ontario, along with $28 million to help stop the export of stolen vehicles.
But that wasn't enough for people on social media who were unsurprised by yet another carjacking as well as the government's role in the increase in crime.
"Welcome to Canada the world's newest 3rd world development," one person wrote. "Congratulations Liberals, this is the utopia you voted for."
Another added: "I can't wait for the Conservatives to win a super majority and all of Trudeau's bulls*** will hopefully get reversed and crimes will actually be punished and drugs might actually be illegal again."

Document 168 of 356

Willing to hit the road; Public still favours building new highways in Ontario: Poll

Author: Spiro Papuckoski

Publication info: Spiro Papuckoski. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/willing-hit-road-public-still-favours-building/docview/2957647474/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Willing+to+hit+the+road%3B+Public+still+favours+building+new+highways+in+Ontario%3A+Poll&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Spiro+Papuckoski&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A new survey suggests Ontario residents continue to favour investments in new highways and roads.
The same poll found respondents don't agree with recent comments from federal Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault, who told a Montreal audience last month that his Liberal government should "stop investing in new road infrastructure."
The poll, commissioned by the Residential and Civil Construction Alliance of Ontario and the Ontario Road Builders Association, showed a majority of respondents - 55% - support building Hwy. 413, while 28% were opposed.
The results were more evenly split among women over 55 years of age.
The proposed 52-km route would connect Hwy. 401 and the 407 ETR interchange at the Halton-Peel boundary to Hwy. 400 north of Vaughan.
The Bradford Bypass, a 16-km link between the 400 and Hwy. 404 north of Newmarket, had similar support in the poll, with 56% of respondents in favour of the route being built, while 24% were opposed.
Support for the proposed highways were virtually unchanged from a poll done in June 2021, while opposition has grown somewhat.
Meanwhile, 55% disagreed with the statement that the federal government should stop investing in new roads, while 33% agreed.
"Ontarians have made it clear that they want all levels of government to invest in new and existing road infrastructure to alleviate congestion and open up more economic opportunities," Nadia Todorova, executive director of RCCAO, said in a news release.
"It is time for the federal government to accept the will of the people and support transformational infrastructure. Projects like Hwy. 413 and the Bradford Bypass, and their supporting transit pairings, will better enable the movement of people and goods throughout one of North America's fastest growing regions."
Support for the projects cut across political lines, including those who don't identify with any one party.
Opponents point to the loss of fertile farmland and negative effects on waterways and woodlands, and say the highways won't help in cutting carbon emissions.
"At a time when we are facing a climate emergency, when it is becoming increasingly clear that our inability to address it is leading us towards a worst-case scenario, continuing to base our communities around a reliance on cars as the primary mode of transportation is extremely irresponsible," the Simcoe County Greenbelt Coalition said on its website.
The study was conducted from Feb. 28 to March 1 among a sample of 1,959 Ontario residents who are members of Maru Blue's online panel.

Document 169 of 356

Dancing around issue; Trudeau needs to explain his concerns about TikTok app

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/dancing-around-issue-trudeau-needs-explain-his/docview/2957647455/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dancing+around+issue%3B+Trudeau+needs+to+explain+his+concerns+about+TikTok+app&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The American House of Representatives has voted to ban TikTok from American app stores unless its Chinese-based parent company sells it within 180 days.
Here in Canada, the Trudeau government ordered a national security review of TikTok last September, but it won't tell you anything about it.
It's a typical Canadian answer: Sorry, to protect you, we can't tell you what we're doing.
The review was confirmed by Industry Minister Francois-Philippe Champagne on Thursday morning.
Later in the day, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau spoke to the issue but wouldn't say much.
"As you know, Canada made the determination that no government phones or devices can have the TikTok app. That's a matter of security and safety. We're watching, of course, the debate going on in the United States, and I can't comment on national security reviews," Trudeau said Thursday in Windsor.
It's a typical Canadian response: Something is a matter of national security, but we can't tell you any details to keep you safe.
There is obviously something up with TikTok and Canadians should be briefed on why this app on the phones of teens and young people across the country is such a concern.
It's not just the Trudeau government in Ottawa that has banned the app on government devices. Ontario, Quebec, Alberta, British Columbia, Saskatchewan, Nova Scotia, Prince Edward Island and several municipalities have banned the app on government phones.
India banned the app outright in 2020, while other governments have either banned it or placed restrictions on it, including Afghanistan, Australia, Belgium, Denmark, the Netherlands, New Zealand, Taiwan, the U.K. and the EU.
Tech giant Amazon asked workers to delete TikTok from their phones in 2020 but reversed course after a strong backlash.
Wednesday's vote in the U.S.
House of Representatives passed with a strong bipartisan majority, 352-65, with 50 Democrats and 15 Republicans opposing it. That should tell us this isn't a matter of free speech being curtailed by government but something larger.
The bill's future in the Senate is unclear but it has the possibility of passing and, if it does, U.S. President Joe Biden has said he'd sign the bill into law.
Whether the bill would survive court challenges is also unclear, but the simple act of it passing would put incredible pressure on TikTok's parent company ByteDance.
The accusation against TikTok, via ByteDance, is that the company shares user data with the Chinese government. There are also allegations that the algorithm is manipulated to present pro-Beijing or other political agendas.
The Israeli government has made claims that TikTok promotes an anti-Israel and anti-Semitic agenda. A recent study found just 30 minutes a day on the app increased the likelihood of users to hold anti-Semitic views by 17%.
Last fall, TikTok faced a barrage of negative headlines after video posts of people praising Osama bin Laden's "Letter to America," his manifesto on why he attacked the United States. Its popularity in the days after the Hamas terror attacks was one of the key reasons TikTok was accused of manipulating its system to push political messaging.
Freedom of speech and freedom of expression are key pillars of western democratic values and need to be upheld. Yet, if ByteDance is using TikTok to funnel information or intelligence to Beijing, that is a problem that goes beyond freedom of speech.
If there are reasons for governments to consider banning TikTok, including our own government in Canada, they should be upfront about what they know rather than hiding the information. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Antisemitism; Bans; National security; Freedom of speech

Location: China; Beijing China; United States--US; Canada

Company / organization: Name: TikTok Inc; NAICS: 518210; Name: Bytedance Ltd; NAICS: 513210, 518210; Name: House of Representatives; NAICS: 921120

Public still favours building new highways and roads in Ontario: Poll

Author: Spiro Papuckoski

Publication info: Spiro Papuckoski. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/public-still-favours-building-new-highways-roads/docview/2957382142/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Public+still+favours+building+new+highways+and+roads+in+Ontario%3A+Poll&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Spiro+Papuckoski&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A new survey suggests Ontario residents continue to be in favour of investments in new highways and roads.
And the same poll found respondents don't agree with recent comments from Steven Guilbeault, the federal environment minister, who told a Montreal audience last month that his Liberal government should "stop investing in new road infrastructure."
The poll, commissioned by the Residential and Civil Construction Alliance of Ontario (RCCAO) and the Ontario Road Builders Association (ORBA), showed a majority of respondents (55%) were in support of building Hwy. 413, while 28% were opposed.
The results were more evenly split among women over 55 years of age.
The proposed 52-kilometre route would connect the existing Hwy. 401 and the 407 ETR interchange at the Halton-Peel boundary to Hwy. 400 north of Vaughan.
The Bradford Bypass, a 16-kilometre link between Hwys. 400 and 404 north of Newmarket, had similar support in the poll, with 56% of respondents in favour of the route being built while 24% were opposed.
Support for the proposed highways were virtually unchanged from a previous poll done in June 2021, while opposition has increased 5% and 9% respectively.
Meanwhile, more than half of respondents (55%) disagreed with the statement that the federal government should stop investing in new roads, while 33% agreed.
"Ontarians have made it clear that they want all levels of government to invest in new and existing road infrastructure to alleviate congestion and open up more economic opportunities," Nadia Todorova, executive director of RCCAO, said in a news release.
"It is time for the federal government to accept the will of the people and support transformational infrastructure. Projects like Hwy. 413 and the Bradford Bypass, and their supporting transit pairings, will better enable the movement of people and goods throughout one of North America's fastest growing regions."
Support for the future projects cut across all political lines, including respondents who didn't identify with a provincial party.
The Ontario government argues the new highways will connect residents to major employment centres and help ease traffic congestion by reducing travel times.

Opponents point to the loss of fertile farmland and negative impacts on waterways and woodlands, while arguing single-vehicle use would not help cut down carbon emissions.
"At a time when we are facing a climate emergency, when it is becoming increasingly clear that our inability to address it is leading us towards a worst case scenario, continuing to base our communities around a reliance on cars as the primary mode of transportation is extremely irresponsible," says the Simcoe County Greenbelt Coalition
The study was conducted from Feb. 28 to March 1 among a sample of 1,959 Ontario residents who are members of Maru Blue's online panel.

Document 172 of 356

Anti-hike preem team; Provincial leaders, including a Liberal, plead for no carbon tax increase

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/anti-hike-preem-team-provincial-leaders-including/docview/2956945799/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Anti-hike+preem+team%3B+Provincial+leaders%2C+including+a+Liberal%2C+plead+for+no+carbon+tax+increase&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Well, this is getting awkward. It's not just Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre calling on Justin Trudeau to stop the carbon tax hike on April 1 - there are a growing number of premiers, too. Andrew Furey, the Liberal premier of Newfoundland and Labrador, has written to Trudeau asking him not to raise the tax in just over two weeks' time.
"The coming almost 25% increase (from $65 to $80 per tonne) in the federal carbon tax on April 1 is causing understandable worry and people consider how they will manage the mounting financial strain," Furey wrote in a letter sent on Tuesday.
"We ask for the collaboration of the federal government to address the ramifications of the current challenges families face and not to compound them."
Furey now joins Ontario's Doug Ford, Alberta's Danielle Smith, Saskatchewan's Scott Moe and New Brunswick's Blaine Higgs in calling for the feds to call off their planned tax increase. Or, to put it in the language of Poilievre, they all want Trudeau to "spike the hike."
Poilievre held a rally in Toronto on Sunday with nearly 3,000 people in attendance. While he's been railing against the carbon tax since he launched his Conservative leadership bid, he launched a campaign Sunday to get Canadians to call their Liberal and NDP MPs to tell them to cancel the 23% tax increase scheduled for April 1.
"April 1, April Fools'Day, and with Justin Trudeau and the NDP the joke is on you," Poilievre said.
He described how the carbon tax, only charged to Canadian businesses, makes everything from farm food to steel in this country less competitive than imports, to which no tax is applied. He said that competitive disadvantage puts Canadian jobs at risk.
Ford has been calling on Trudeau for months to cancel the planned tax increase and took aim again as recently as Monday, calling it the worst tax ever for making everything more expensive.
"The federal government cannot hike up this carbon tax by 23%," Ontario's premier said.
"When the No. 1 issue on any poll you see is affordability - affordable homes, affordable gas, affordable groceries - and these guys are going to raise it on April 1?" Ford said he didn't understand what the Trudeau Liberals are up to with this move.
Higgs, New Brunswick's premier, put out a statement Tuesday saying the carbon tax has been brutal on the people of his province, driving up costs of everyday essentials.
"Even if he won't cancel the tax outright, he can stop his plan to impose even more harm on New Brunswickers and Canadians by halting the planned tax increase," Higgs said.
"Justin Trudeau is putting ideology above individuals and politics over people. The urgency is real and Canadians deserve better than this."
Smith chimed in on social media, saying she backs Furey's request.
"Alberta stands shoulder to shoulder with people of Newfoundland and Labrador in their opposition to the federal carbon tax, which makes life more unaffordable and hurts families," Alberta's premier posted to X.
Saskatchewan's Moe simply shared the online posts of Furey and Higgs, saying he agreed with them. Moe is in the middle of a fight with the federal government over whether to charge the carbon tax on home heating fuels in his province.
After Trudeau "paused" the carbon tax on home heating oil - used predominantly in Atlantic Canada, where the Liberals have a lot of MPs - Moe refused to charge and remit the carbon tax via provincially owned natural gas provider SaskEnergy.
While several premiers opposed to the carbon tax hike can be described as conservative, even Manitoba's NDP Premier Wab Kinew has raised concerns about the impact of the carbon tax on family budgets in his province.
There is no doubt that Trudeau will face tremendous pressure on this file next week when the House of Commons gathers for their only sitting before the April 1 tax hike. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Carbon; Environmental tax; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax Tax increases

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Canada; Newfoundland & Labrador Canada

Document 173 of 356

The reality is, Trudeau hasn't offered a real pharmacare plan

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/reality-is-trudeau-hasnt-offered-real-pharmacare/docview/2956945244/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+reality+is%2C+Trudeau+hasn%27t+offered+a+real+pharmacare+plan&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's not universal, it's not national and it's not pharmacare. In fact, the Liberal proposal - plan is too generous- is barely a proposal.
Sure, there is legislation that has been introduced, but have you read it? The pharmacare plan unveiled two weeks ago looks like it was written on the back of a cocktail napkin. Once you strip away the fancy formatting, the title page, the pages of definitions, the actual meat and potatoes of the bill is about a page and a half long.
Last week, Ontario Premier Doug Ford was asked what more detail he wanted before signing onto the deal - his answer should have been any detail.
What the Trudeau Liberals are counting on is that most people, including most journalists who ask people like Ford questions, won't bother reading the bill and seeing the complete lack of detail.
The first line of the summary of the bill gives away that this is not what the Liberals are selling it as.
"This enactment sets out the principles that the Minister of Health is to consider when working towards the implementation of national universal pharmacare," the bill summary reads.
It's right there in black and white - this legislation is about how the minister should work towards a pharmacare system.
If you listen to the rhetoric of the Liberals and their junior coalition partners in the NDP, pharmacare has been delivered and the Conservatives are just waiting to destroy it.
The truth is that under the bestcase scenario, there won't be any deals with the provinces to start this so-called universal, national pharmacare plan until just before the scheduled election in 2025. It's just as likely, if not more likely, that we won't see this plan enacted until after the next election.
Which makes all of this nothing more than political posturing by Trudeau, which is what he's been doing since the announcement.
So, back to what was promised in the deal between the Liberals and the NDP that established their dysfunctional coalition. The deal states clearly that the NDP demanded "progress towards a universal national pharmacare program."
It also demanded the passage of the "Canada Pharmacare Act" by the end of 2023.
The NDP got none of what it asked for but it continues to prop up the Liberals.
Let's break down the elements then. The Merriam-Webster Dictionary defines universal as "including or covering all or a whole collectively or distributively without limit or exception." This plan will only cover contraceptives and diabetes medication to start, nothing else.
That means a woman who is past menopause, who doesn't have diabetes but has high blood pressure, for example, will get nothing.
This plan isn't national because Quebec has already rejected it but demanded funding compensation.
Alberta has said it isn't interested in the federal plan given it has its own. Meanwhile, British Columbia already covers contraceptives and therefore doesn't need to join that part of the federal plan.
That leaves the rest of the provinces wondering if this helps them or hurts them. Most have prescription drug programs for low-income individuals and while those could be improved, this proposal, such as it is, doesn't seem to be it.
As for this being pharmacare, go back to the part of this being universal. This program will cover two classes of drugs, that is it. There isn't even a full promise of more down the line. Cancer drugs? Not yet. Drugs for liver disease? Not anytime soon.
Kids with ADHD? Not unless they want contraceptives.
And yet Trudeau will spend from now until the next election acting like he is a hero for providing birth control to people who are mostly covered or can afford it. He will fight an election campaign as if it is the 1950s and women are oppressed simply because he has nothing left to give.
Justin Trudeau hasn't proposed a national pharmacare plan, he has proposed a national save Justin's political career plan and that is something we should all reject.

'WILL NEVER, EVER SELL': Premier accuses union leader of lying about LCBO's future

Full text: Premier Doug Ford accused the union leader representing LCBO workers of lying about the future of the alcohol retailer after his MPPs were targeted by provincewide protests this week.
LCBO workers targeted Progressive Conservative MPPs during a day of action on Tuesday, accusing the Ford government of planning to sell off the Crown corporation and distributing petitions to the offices of 11 government officials, including the premier's constituency office in Etobicoke.

Document 175 of 356

LILLEY: The reality is, Trudeau hasn't offered a real pharmacare plan

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-reality-is-trudeau-hasnt-offered-real/docview/2956661450/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+The+reality+is%2C+Trudeau+hasn%27t+offered+a+real+pharmacare+plan&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's not universal, it's not national and it's not pharmacare. In fact, the Liberal proposal - plan is too generous - is barely a proposal.
Sure, there is legislation that has been introduced, but have you read it?
The pharmacare plan unveiled two weeks ago looks like it was written on the back of a cocktail napkin. Once you strip away the fancy formatting, the title page, the pages of definitions, the actual meat and potatoes of the bill is about a page and a half long.
Last week, Ontario Premier Doug Ford was asked what more detail he wanted before signing onto the deal - his answer should have been any detail.
What the Trudeau Liberals are counting on is that most people, including most journalists who ask people like Ford questions, won't bother reading the bill and seeing the complete lack of detail. The first line of the summary of the bill gives away that this is not what the Liberals are selling it as.
"This enactment sets out the principles that the Minister of Health is to consider when working towards the implementation of national universal pharmacare," the bill summary reads.
It's right there in black and white - this legislation is about how the minister should work towards a pharmacare system. If you listen to the rhetoric of the Liberals and their junior coalition partners in the NDP, pharmacare has been delivered and the Conservatives are just waiting to destroy it.
The truth is that under the best-case scenario, there won't be any deals with the provinces to start this so-called universal, national pharmacare plan until just before the scheduled election in 2025. It's just as likely, if not more likely, that we won't see this plan enacted until after the next election.
Which makes all of this nothing more than political posturing by Trudeau, which is what he's been doing since the announcement.
So, back to what was promised in the deal between the Liberals and the NDP that established their dysfunctional coalition. The deal states clearly that the NDP demanded "progress towards a universal national pharmacare program."
They also demanded the passage of the "Canada Pharmacare Act" by the end of 2023.
The NDP got none of what they asked for but they continue to prop up the Liberals.
Let's break down the elements then.
The Merriam-Webster Dictionary defines universal as "including or covering all or a whole collectively or distributively without limit or exception." This plan will only cover contraceptives and diabetes medication to start, nothing else.
That means a woman who is past menopause, who doesn't have diabetes but has high blood pressure, for example, will get nothing.
This plan isn't national because Quebec has already rejected it but demanded funding compensation. Alberta has said they aren't interested in the federal plan given they have their own. Meanwhile, British Columbia already covers contraceptives and therefore doesn't need to join that part of the federal plan.
That leaves the rest of the provinces wondering if this helps them or hurts them. Most provinces have prescription drug programs for low-income individuals and while those could be improved, this proposal, such as it is, doesn't seem to be it.
As for this being pharmacare, go back to the part of this being universal.
This program will cover two classes of drugs, that is it. There isn't even a full promise of more down the line.
Cancer drugs? Not yet.
Drugs for liver disease? Not anytime soon.
Kids with ADHD? Not unless they want contraceptives.
And yet, Trudeau will spend from now until the next election acting like he is a hero for providing birth control to people who are mostly covered or can afford it. He will fight an election campaign as if it is the 1950s and women are oppressed simply because he has nothing left to give.
Justin Trudeau hasn't proposed a national pharmacare plan, he's proposed a national save Justin's political career plan and that is something we should all reject.
blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Diabetes; Prescription drugs; Birth control

Identifier / keyword: universal; national; pharmacare; liberal; proposal; generous

Title: LILLEY: The reality is, Trudeau hasn't offered a real pharmacare plan

Publication title: The Toronto Sun (Online); Toronto

LILLEY: Even Liberal premiers are calling on Trudeau to spike planned carbon tax hike

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-even-liberal-premiers-are-calling-on/docview/2956615823/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Even+Liberal+premiers+are+calling+on+Trudeau+to+spike+planned+carbon+tax+hike&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Well, this is getting awkward.
It's not just Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre calling on Justin Trudeau to stop the carbon tax hike on April 1, there are a growing number of premiers, too. Andrew Furey, the Liberal premier of Newfoundland and Labrador has written to Trudeau asking him not to raise the tax in just over two weeks' time.
"The coming almost 25% increase (from $65 to $80 per tonne) in the federal carbon tax on April 1 is causing understandable worry and people consider how they will manage the mounting financial strain," Furey wrote in a letter sent on Tuesday.
"We ask for the collaboration of the federal government to address the ramifications of the current challenges families face and not to compound them."
I continue to stand up for Newfoundlanders and Labradorians against the federal carbon tax.
I am now asking Ottawa to pause its planned increase to the carbon tax, set for April 1st, as the high cost of living is enough of a burden on families.
The full letter is here:
- Andrew Furey (@FureyAndrew) March 12, 2024
Furey now joins Ontario's Doug Ford, Alberta's Danielle Smith, Saskatchewan's Scott Moe and New Brunswick's Blaine Higgs in calling for the feds to call off their planned tax hike. Or, to put it in the language of Poilievre, they all want Trudeau to "spike the hike."
Poilievre held a massive rally in Toronto on Sunday with nearly 3,000 people in attendance. While he's been railing against the carbon tax since he launched his Conservative leadership bid, he launched a campaign on Sunday to get Canadians to call their Liberal and NDP MPs to tell them to cancel the 23% tax increase scheduled for April 1.
"April 1, April Fools' Day, and with Justin Trudeau and the NDP the joke is on you," Poilievre said
He described how the carbon tax, only charged to Canadian businesses, makes everything from farmers to steel mills in this country less competitive than imports, which pay no tax. He said that competitive disadvantage puts Canadian jobs at risk.
LILLEY: Thousands turn out for Poilievre's message of hope and change
LILLEY: Ford warns Trudeau, stop carbon tax hike or be crushed by voters
GOLDSTEIN: The carbon tax is going up and so are emissions
Ford has been calling on Trudeau for months to cancel the planned tax increase and took aim at it again as recently as Monday, calling it the worst tax ever for making everything more expensive.
"The federal government cannot hike up this carbon tax by 23%," Ontario's premier said
"When the No. 1 issue on any poll you see is affordability - affordable homes, affordable gas, affordable groceries - and these guys are going to raise it on April 1?"
Ford said he didn't understand what the Trudeau Liberals are up to with this move.
Higgs, New Brunswick's premier, put out a statement on Tuesday saying that Trudeau's carbon tax has been brutal on the people of his province, driving up costs of everyday essentials.
"Even if he won't cancel the tax outright, he can stop his plan to impose even more harm on New Brunswickers and Canadians by halting the planned tax increase," Higgs said
"Justin Trudeau is putting ideology above individuals and politics over people. The urgency is real and Canadians deserve better than this."
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
Smith chimed in on social media, saying that she backs Furey's request.
"Alberta stands shoulder to shoulder with people of Newfoundland and Labrador in their opposition to the federal carbon tax, which makes life more unaffordable and hurts families," Alberta's premier posted to X
Saskatchewan's Moe simply shared the online posts of Furey and Higgs, saying he agreed with them. Moe is in the middle of a fight with the federal government over whether to charge the carbon tax on home heating fuels in his province.
After Trudeau "paused" the carbon tax on home heating oil - which is predominantly used in Atlantic Canada, where the Liberals have a lot of MPs - Moe refused to charge and remit the carbon tax via provincially owned natural gas provider SaskEnergy.
While several of the premiers opposed to the carbon tax hike can be described as conservative, even Manitoba's NDP Premier Wab Kinew has raised concerns about the impact of the carbon tax on family budgets in his province.
There is no doubt that Trudeau will face tremendous pressure on this file next week when the House of Commons gathers for their only sitting before the April 1 tax hike.

Document 177 of 356

Corporate welfare costing taxpayers over $50 billion a year: Report

Author: Goldstein, Lorrie

Publication info: Goldstein, Lorrie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/corporate-welfare-costing-taxpayers-over-50/docview/2956054650/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Corporate+welfare+costing+taxpayers+over+%2450+billion+a+year%3A+Report&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Goldstein%2C+Lorrie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's federal, provincial and municipal governments spent $52 billion on corporate welfare in 2022 - the latest data available -according to a new study by the Fraser Institute released Tuesday.
"These subsidies for businesses - also known as corporate welfare - come with huge costs to government budgets and taxpayers, while doing little if anything to stimulate economic growth," said Tegan Hill, co-author of "The Cost of Business Subsidies in Canada: Updated Edition," by the fiscally conservative think tank.
The study found annual government spending on corporate welfare has more than doubled from $24.5 billion in 2007 to $52 billion in 2022, in inflation-adjusted dollars.
Between 2007 and 2019 - the year before the pandemic hit - federal, provincial, and local government spending on business subsidies totalled $352.1 billion.
Federal business subsidies alone skyrocketed to $88.5 billion in the first year of the pandemic in 2020, decreasing to $47 billion in 2021.
Recommended video
They decreased again to $11.2 billion in 2022, but that was still almost double the $6.5 billion the feds spent in 2019, the year before the pandemic hit.
Meanwhile, provincial corporate welfare increased the fastest, from $13.2 billion in 2007 to $35.4 billion in 2022.
Between 2007 and 2019, two provinces spent more on corporate welfare, on average, than they received in corporate tax revenue (Prince Edward Island at 160.8% and Quebec at 100.8%), while in Manitoba, corporate welfare ate up 97.5% of all corporate tax revenues.
The total cost per taxpayer of financing federal, provincial and municipal corporate welfare between 2007 and 2019, adjusted for inflation, was highest in Quebec at $30,579, followed in descending order by Saskatchewan ($29,413); Prince Edward Island ($21,298); Alberta ($19,662); Ontario ($18,898); British Columbia ($17,898); Manitoba ($17,224); Nova Scotia ($12,987); Newfoundland and Labrador ($11,090) and New Brunswick ($9,484).
The study notes these estimates are conservative because it only includes direct government transfers to private businesses and government business enterprises.
"To be clear, the report does not provide a comprehensive measure of government support to businesses, which would include all tax expenditures, loan guarantees, direct investment and regulatory privileges extended to particular firms or industries," the study says.
The reason is due to a lack of transparency about total government financial support to businesses and an ongoing failure to produce "a comprehensive accounting detailing such support in all forms."
Canadian politicians across the political spectrum have criticized government subsidies to businesses for decades - from former Conservative prime minister Stephen Harper, to former NDP leader David Lewis, who coined the phrase "corporate welfare" in 1972, when he criticized "corporate welfare bums" for demanding and getting millions of dollars in government subsidies.
Despite widespread agreement corporate welfare - also known as crony capitalism - is a costly and inefficient way to encourage economic growth, corporate welfare today is a fact of life throughout the Canadian economy, from the auto and aerospace sectors, to the energy and aviation industries, to the media and more.
The Fraser Institute report concludes these business subsidies impose a significant financial burden on Canadian taxpayers and increases government deficits, while failing to foster widespread economic growth because governments have a dismal record of trying to pick winners and losers in the economy, instead of letting the marketplace decide.
"Rather than give preferential treatment to certain companies and industries, it's high time Canada reduced business taxes and helped to foster a pro-economic growth environment that gives all businesses the opportunity and incentives to succeed," said Jake Fuss, co-author of the study.
Report card ranks Ontario elementary schools on reading, writing and math
Make the rich pay? They already do, Fraser Institute study says

Subject: Corporate taxes; Economic growth; Government spending; Government subsidies; Pandemics

Business indexing term: Subject: Corporate taxes Economic growth Government spending Government subsidies

Location: Prince Edward Island Canada; Manitoba Canada; Canada; Quebec Canada

Company / organization: Name: Fraser Institute; NAICS: 541720

Identifier / keyword: federal; provincial; municipal; governments; spent; billion

Title: Corporate welfare costing taxpayers over $50 billion a year: Report

Publication title: The Toronto Sun (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Mar 12, 2024

Section: News

Publisher: Post media Network Inc.

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Document type: News

Politicians silent on anti-Semitism

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/politicians-silent-on-anti-semitism/docview/2955962995/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Politicians+silent+on+anti-Semitism&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Remember at the start of the pandemic in early 2020 when politicians at all levels of government united to combat racism against Canadians of Chinese origin? Remember when then-federal Liberal health minister Patty Hajdu, then-Ontario PC health minister Christine Elliott and then-Toronto mayor John Tory met at a dim sum restaurant in Toronto's Chinatown, encouraging people to continue to patronize Chinese restaurants and Asian-run businesses?
While they would soon close all restaurants because of COVID, remember how they stood shoulder to shoulder with Canada's Asian community to show solidarity with a community they perceived as being under attack? Why then, are politicians regardless of party, at all levels of government not standing shoulder to shoulder now in public displays of solidarity with Canada's Jewish community, which has been under attack for more than five months, experiencing a wave of Jew hatred not seen in Canada since the 1930s? We've seen Jewish community centres firebombed, shots fired at Jewish day schools, Jewish synagogues defaced, Jewish businesses and homes vandalized, Jewish students threatened, Jewish neighbourhoods targeted by demonstrators describing them as "Zionist-infested areas," and demonstrations outside a Jewish hospital in Toronto and Montreal's Holocaust Museum.
Why the silence - with a handful of honourable exceptions - from politicians at all levels of government who, instead of publicly and physically demonstrating support for Canada's Jewish community, utter mindless, useless platitudes on X about opposing anti-Semitism and Islamophobia? Of course, all decent people oppose anti-Semitism and Islamophobia, but it is not "pro-Israel" demonstrators who are targeting mosques, it's "pro-Palestinian" demonstrators who are targeting synagogues.
Blaming Jews in Canada for the military actions of the Israeli government in Gaza is classic anti-Semitism, just as it would be Islamophobic for "pro-Israel" demonstrators to target mosques in Canada for Hamas' terrorism. The Jewish community itself is divided on the military campaign of Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu in Gaza, which the "pro-Palestinian" mobs know full well because they're constantly quoting Jews who oppose the war.
The thundering silence from most Canadian politicians about these attacks on Jews is not only a disgrace, it's also fuelling the hatred aimed at Jews because the haters now know they can get away with it.
They won't stop until they're stopped.

Subject: Antisemitism; Synagogues; Jewish people; Jewish life & ethics; Islamophobia; Politics; Xenophobia; Politicians

Location: Gaza Strip; Canada; Israel

Winners & losers; Corporate welfare costs taxpayers over $50 billion a year: Report

Publication info: Goldstein, Lorrie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/winners-amp-losers-corporate-welfare-costs/docview/2955962978/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Winners+%26amp%3B+losers%3B+Corporate+welfare+costs+taxpayers+over+%2450+billion+a+year%3A+Report&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Goldstein%2C+Lorrie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's federal, provincial and municipal governments spent $52 billion on corporate welfare in 2022, the latest data available, according to a study by the Fraser Institute released Tuesday.
"These subsidies for businesses - also known as corporate welfare - come with huge costs to government budgets and taxpayers, while doing little if anything to stimulate economic growth," said Tegan Hill, co-author of the study by the fiscally conservative think tank.
It found annual government spending on corporate welfare has more than doubled, from $24.5 billion in 2007 to $52 billion in 2022, in inflation-adjusted dollars.
Between 2007 and 2019 - the year before the pandemic hit - federal, provincial and local government spending on business subsidies totalled $352.1 billion.
Federal business subsidies alone skyrocketed to $88.5 billion in the first year of the pandemic in 2020, decreasing to $47 billion in 2021.
They decreased again to $11.2 billion in 2022, but that was still almost double the $6.5 billion the feds spent in 2019, the year before the pandemic hit.
Meanwhile, provincial corporate welfare increased the fastest, from $13.2 billion in 2007 to $35.4 billion in 2022.
Between 2007 and 2019, two provinces spent more on corporate welfare, on average, than they received in corporate tax revenue (Prince Edward Island at 160.8% and Quebec at 100.8%), while in Manitoba, corporate welfare ate up 97.5% of all corporate tax revenues.
The total cost per taxpayer of financing federal, provincial and municipal corporate welfare between 2007 and 2019, adjusted for inflation, was highest in Quebec at $30,579, followed in descending order by Saskatchewan ($29,413), Prince Edward Island ($21,298), Alberta ($19,662), Ontario ($18,898), British Columbia ($17,898), Manitoba ($17,224), Nova Scotia ($12,987), Newfoundland and Labrador ($11,090) and New Brunswick ($9,484).
The study said these estimates are conservative because it only includes direct government transfers to private businesses and government business enterprises.
"To be clear, the report does not provide a comprehensive measure of government support to businesses, which would include all tax expenditures, loan guarantees, direct investment and regulatory privileges extended to particular firms or industries," the study says.
The reason is due to a lack of transparency about total government financial support to businesses and an ongoing failure to produce "a comprehensive accounting detailing such support in all forms."
Canadian politicians across the political spectrum have criticized government subsidies to businesses for decades - from former Conservative prime minister Stephen Harper to former NDP leader David Lewis, who coined the phrase "corporate welfare" in 1972, when he criticized "corporate welfare bums" for demanding and getting millions of dollars in government subsidies.
Despite widespread agreement that corporate welfare - also known as crony capitalism - is a costly and inefficient way to encourage economic growth, it's a fact of life today throughout the Canadian economy, from the auto and aerospace sectors to the energy and aviation industries, to the media and more.
The Fraser Institute report concludes these business subsidies impose a significant financial burden on Canadian taxpayers and increases government deficits, while failing to foster widespread economic growth because governments have a dismal record of trying to pick winners and losers in the economy, instead of letting the marketplace decide.
"Rather than give preferential treatment to certain companies and industries, it's high time Canada reduced business taxes and helped to foster a pro-economic growth environment that gives all businesses the opportunity and incentives to succeed," said Jake Fuss, co-author of the study. lgoldstein@postmedia.com @sunlorrie

Subject: Corporate taxes; Economic growth; Government spending; Government subsidies; Pandemics

EDITORIAL: Politicians silent on anti-Semitism

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/editorial-politicians-silent-on-anti-semitism/docview/2955548781/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=EDITORIAL%3A+Politicians+silent+on+anti-Semitism&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Remember at the start of the pandemic in early 2020 when politicians at all levels of government united to combat racism against Canadians of Chinese origin?
Remember when then-federal Liberal health minister Patty Hajdu, then-Ontario Progressive Conservative health minister Christine Elliott and then-Toronto mayor John Tory met at a dim sum restaurant in Toronto's Chinatown, encouraging people to continue to patronize Chinese restaurants and Asian-run businesses?
While they would soon close all restaurants because of COVID, remember how they stood shoulder-to-shoulder with Canada's Asian community to show solidarity with a community they perceived as being under attack?
Why then, are politicians regardless of party, at all levels of government - federal, provincial and municipal - not standing shoulder-to-shoulder now in public displays of solidarity with Canada's Jewish community now, which has been under attack for more than five months, experiencing a wave of Jew hatred not seen in Canada since the 1930s?
Jewish community centres firebombed, shots fired at Jewish day schools, Jewish synagogues defaced, Jewish businesses and homes vandalized, Jewish students threatened, Jewish neighbourhoods targeted by demonstrators describing them as "Zionist-infested areas," demonstrations outside a Jewish hospital in Toronto and Montreal's Holocaust Museum.
Why the silence - with a handful of honourable exceptions - from politicians at all levels of government who, instead of publicly and physically demonstrating support for Canada's Jewish community, utter mindless, useless platitudes on 'X" about opposing anti-Semitism and Islamophobia?
Of course, all decent people oppose anti-Semitism and Islamophobia, but it is not "pro-Israel" demonstrators who are targeting mosques, it's "pro-Palestinian" demonstrators who are targeting synagogues.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
Blaming Jews in Canada for the military actions of the Israeli government in Gaza is classic anti-Semitism, just as it would be Islamophobic for "pro-Israel" demonstrators to target mosques in Canada for Hamas' terrorism.
The Jewish community itself is divided on the military campaign of Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu in Gaza, which the "pro-Palestinian" mobs know full well because they're constantly quoting Jews who oppose the war.
The thundering silence from most Canadian politicians - at all levels of government - about these ongoing attacks on Jews is not only a disgrace, it's also fuelling the hatred aimed at Jews because the haters now know they can get away with it.
They won't stop until they're stopped.

Document 183 of 356

Watch out for scams; Threats growing across Internet

Author: Saltzman, Marc

Publication info: Saltzman, Marc. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/watch-out-scams-threats-growing-across-internet/docview/2954671565/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Watch+out+for+scams%3B+Threats+growing+across+Internet&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=Saltzman%2C+Marc&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Considering March is Fraud Prevention Month, it's a good time for a plain-English cybersecurity refresher.
Let's take a quick look at three typical Canadians: n Jane, a Toronto-based movie lover, is accustomed to seeing messages from Netflix about newly added films to watch. And so, it wouldn't be odd to get an email, with the familiar red logo, saying her account needs updating. n Meanwhile, Mo in Vancouver received a text message from someone at CRA about filing his taxes on time. Given that it's March, tax season, he doesn't second-guess it. n And then there's Stéphane in Montreal, who answers a call from his internet service provider's tech support department claiming they detected a virus on his PC.
Though it may not be painfully obvious to some, all three scenarios are attempts to scam Canadians.
In fact, they're becoming more frequent, costly and harder to spot than the abovementioned examples.
"Fraud is on the rise and continuously evolving with new advances in technology, with losses reported to the Canadian Anti-Fraud Centre reaching an all-time high of $554 million last year alone," says Leigh Tynan, cybersecurity expert and director of TELUS Online Security.
"And that's with only an estimated 5 to 10% of victims filing a report, so the losses are likely much higher," Tynan adds.
Advancements in artificial intelligence (AI) are making it more difficult to detect, confirms Maryam Saeed, head of risk at Visa Canada.
"Fraudsters are becoming increasingly sophisticated, using AI to create convincing scams to ensnare even the savviest of consumers," she says.
For example, AI can help fraudsters write more professional-looking messages, with fewer spelling and grammatical mistakes, as well as provide the ability to "deep fake" a familiar voice on the phone.
"We carried out a report, and what we realized is that more than 1 in 3, almost 35% of Canadians, admit to falling for a scam - on more than one occasion," Saeed says.
So, it's time to put an end to these scammers once and for all.
The good news is you don't need to be tech savvy to safeguard your data and devices.
What better time than in March - Fraud Prevention Month - to go over the basics. The following is a look at how to up your game in only a few minutes. KNOWLEDGE IS POWER The first tip is to be vigilant against the fraudsters.
Exercise common sense when reading emails and text messages. No respectful company will ask you to urgently confirm your identity by filling out forms.
Never click on suspicious attachments or links. Don't trust anyone calling you claiming to be from your bank or credit card issuer, the CRA, Microsoft, your ISP, and so on.
When in doubt, contact the company - like your bank - to ask if it really was them. Chances are, it wasn't.
And be sure to use a phone number you already have for them or look it up outside of any number provided inside the message you received.
"Whenever something seems too urgent or sounds too good to be true, my advice is to take a pause," Saeed suggests.
"There's no harm missing a call or calling back later. Just be very careful around the information you're sharing."
Resist free public WiFi, as you're more likely to be hacked than browsing on a secure connection at home.
Or use your smartphone as a personal hotspot instead.
When shopping online, stick with reputable retailers, and before you type in your credit card look for indicators that the site is secure, such as a little lock icon on the browser's status bar or a URL for a website that begins "https:" (the "s" stands for "secure").
"Also, keep a record of all your transactions and check your statements" in case something looks off, advises Saeed. "This is an effective way to stay on top of everything, in case your credit information has been compromised unknowingly." OTHER CYBERSECURITY TIPS A few other ways to remain cybersafe during Fraud Prevention Month, and beyond: n Be aware of social media scams, such as a legitimate-looking direct message from a "friend" over Facebook Messenger or Instagram, that say they're stuck in some city and need money right away.
Your real friend's account was biometric such as Apple are a good protect your devices. compromised and cloned. n Remember to have a strong password for all your accounts, which is at least seven characters long and a combination of letters, numbers and symbols (bonus points for adding upper and lowercase characters).
Never use the same password for all your online activity, because if a site or app is breached, then the crooks have access to all your accounts.
Password manager apps, like Dashlane and Last-Pass, aren't a bad idea. n For online banking and shopping apps, opt for "multifactor authentication," which not only requires your password to log in but also a one-time code sent to your mobile device, to prove it's really you. n Use the "private" or "incognito" mode of your browser, which deletes your history and cookies after your session.
Better yet, consider a Virtual Private Network (VPN) app to remain anonymous when online. n Make sure you lock your device, in case it's stolen or lost, by adding a PIN, password or biometrics option (like fingerprint or facial scan).
Set up the "Find my phone" feature, which lets you remotely wipe your phone's data clean if lost or stolen, and see where you left it on a map (when logged into another device with the same login credentials). If it's stolen, never try to retrieve it on your own - instead, contact the authorities. n Install and update reputable antimalware on all your devices, such as McAfee Ultimate Family, which can identify, quarantine, delete, and report any suspicious activity.
The most robust software offers a suite of services, including a firewall and encryption options. n Finally, set up automatic updates on all your tech, including your operating system, browser, plug-ins, and other software.
You only need to do this once. For software that doesn't allow for automatic updates, check them regularly. SMARTER THAN YOU THINK While aging Canadians are disproportionately targeted by fraudsters, they are "a lot more vigilant than other generations about online safety," says Vivianne Gauci, senior vice president of Customer Experience at HomeEquity Bank.
It's younger generations, like Millennials and Gen Z, who are opening themselves up more to scams by engaging in "risky online behaviour," perhaps because of comfort and familiarity that breeds a false sense of security.
Despite this finding, two thirds (66%) of Canadians polled think Boomers are the most likely to fall victim to online scams, according to the results of a recent survey by Ipsos and HomeEquity Bank, which services Canadian homeowners ages 55 and older, and is bestknown as the provider of the CHIP Reverse Mortgage.
"It's not just because (younger generations) are using technology more, but it's things like connecting to public WiFi and using autofill for passwords or the same one for multiple accounts," Gauci explains.
Boomers lead all other demographics when it comes to being constantly concerned about fraud when using the internet - 85% versus a 76% average for all other generations.
@marc_saltzman

Subject: Generations; Software; Canadians; Reverse mortgages; Artificial intelligence; Virtual private networks; Internet service providers; Fraud prevention; Social networks; Cybersecurity

Business indexing term: Subject: Reverse mortgages Artificial intelligence Internet service providers Fraud prevention Social networks

Identifier / keyword: considering; march; fraud; prevention; month; cybersecurity

Document 184 of 356

GOLDSTEIN: Today's Jew hatred comes from the left

Author: Goldstein, Lorrie

Publication info: Goldstein, Lorrie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/goldstein-todays-jew-hatred-comes-left/docview/2954488051/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=GOLDSTEIN%3A+Today%27s+Jew+hatred+comes+from+the+left&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Goldstein%2C+Lorrie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For those wondering about the dismal response of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau - and most Liberal and NDP MPs - to the worst outbreak of Jew hatred in Canada since the 1930s, the answer is simple.
Their self-proclaimed "progressive" ideologies compel them to believe, for the sake of their constant virtue-signalling, that Jew hatred only comes from skinheads, white supremacists and neo-Nazis.
Denouncing that they are comfortable with - indeed obsessed with - whether its Conservative MPs meeting with a far-right German politician, or Trudeau accusing Conservatives of standing with "people who wave swastikas" when they criticized his use of the Emergencies Act against the Freedom Convoy
But when the hatred is coming from within their own house - from the left's chattering classes, from so-called public intellectuals, from university professors and student associations, from doctors in university medical schools and lawyers and labour leaders - they're paralyzed.
That is, if they don't already agree with the hateful views being expressed.
GOLDSTEIN: First they came for the Jews - now they're targeting everyone
GOLDSTEIN: The left's alliance with Islamofascism ignited today's anti-Semitism
GOLDSTEIN: The Israel-Hamas war will end - the anti-Semitism won't
Last week, former B.C. NDP cabinet minister Selina Robinson, who is Jewish, resigned from the NDP caucus, saying it was rife with anti-Semitism.
This after she was drummed out of cabinet for saying Israel was created on "a crappy piece of land," which angered pro-Palestinian groups and for which she apologized.
In response to her letter explaining her reasons for resigning, Nathan Rotman, who is Jewish, former national director of the NDP and former chief of staff to then NDP Alberta premier Rachel Notley, wrote on X that, "The letter by Robinson reflects the experiences of many in the NDP. This is not a BC problem, it's a national one. It's become more uncomfortable to be Jewish in the party and that's an understatement. I haven't left the party but it's certainly leaving me."
The "pro-Palestinian" demonstrations that have been targeting Jews across Canada for months, are not filled with neo-Nazis chanting "the Jews will not replace us", marching under Confederate flags or burning Swastikas the way the KKK used to burn crosses.
These protesters are fuelled by the vilification of Jews coming from the left.
From the Boycott, Divestment and Sanctions movement, which the House of Commons - on a Conservative motion - condemned in 2016 by a vote of 229-51 for promoting "the demonization and delegitimization of the State of Israel."
From the Israel Apartheid Week campaigns, which have the same goal and which began at the University of Toronto in 2005, before infecting university campuses around the world.
Liberal and NDP politicians have lectured Canadians for years that white supremacy and neo-Nazism don't happen in a vacuum - that they are fuelled by hate-filled, right-wing political rhetoric which they constantly condemn.
But most have gone silent since Oct. 7 about the hate-filled, left-wing political rhetoric on university campuses that provides the intellectual justification for Hamas-loving protesters to target Jewish neighbourhoods in what they call "Zionist-infested" areas.
Trudeau said last week he's heard from many Jews that they feel intimidated by protests targeting them and that while everyone is upset about the "horrible things we are seeing" in Gaza, "hateful or harassing behaviour, particularly against neighbours, is not what we do here in Canada."
Well, if it's not what we do in Canada, what does Trudeau plan to do about it other than issue periodic, ineffective, motherhood expressions of concern, because the issue is about what's been happening in Canada for more than five months?
These protesters are targeting Jews - they always have an excuse for targeting Jews - blaming them for the actions of the Israeli military in Gaza, which is classic Jew hatred, to say nothing of the fact many Jews in Canada oppose the military policies of Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu in Gaza, which have led to the death of tens of thousands of Palestinians.
But in Canada today, even arguing in favour of a ceasefire, contingent on Hamas releasing the surviving hostages from the 253 it kidnapped during its terrorist attack on Israel on Oct. 7 - when it also raped, tortured and murdered 1,200 innocent people - is apt to get you labelled a genocide enabler and baby-killer by the "pro-Palestinian" crowd.
The actions of these demonstrators targeting Jews where they live, work and worship - the latest excuse for targeting synagogues is that they promote the sale of Israeli land stolen from the Palestinians - are as outrageous as it would be if "pro-Israel" demonstrators were targeting mosques in "Muslim-infested" areas, to protest the Islamofascist ravings of those imams in Canada who have been calling for the death of Jews in their prayers for years.
Of course if that was happening, Canada's "progressive" politicians would be going berserk.
lgoldstein@postmedia.com
