Document 185 of 356

SALTZMAN: Watch out for these growing scams

Author: Doucette, Chris

Publication info: Doucette, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/saltzman-watch-out-these-growing-scams/docview/2954461063/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=SALTZMAN%3A+Watch+out+for+these+growing+scams&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Doucette%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Considering March is Fraud Prevention Month, it's a good time for a plain-English cybersecurity refresher.
Let's take a quick look at three typical Canadians:
- Jane, a Toronto-based movie lover, is accustomed to seeing messages from Netflix about newly added films to watch. And so, it wouldn't be odd to get an email, with the familiar red logo, saying her account needs updating.
- Meanwhile, Mo in Vancouver received a text message from someone at CRA about filing his taxes on time. Given that it's March, tax season, he doesn't second-guess it.
- And then there's Stéphane in Montreal, who answers a call from his internet service provider's tech support department claiming they detected a virus on his PC.
Though it may not be painfully obvious to some, all three scenarios are attempts to scam Canadians.
In fact, they're becoming more frequent, costly and harder to spot than the abovementioned examples.
"Fraud is on the rise and continuously evolving with new advances in technology, with losses reported to the Canadian Anti-Fraud Centre reaching an all-time high of $554 million last year alone," says Leigh Tynan, cybersecurity expert and director of TELUS Online Security.
"And that's with only an estimated 5 to 10% of victims filing a report, so the losses are likely much higher," Tynan adds.
SALTZMAN: Putting the 'smart' back into smartphones
SALTZMAN: Testing tomorrow's tech today at the 2024 CES
SALTZMAN: How to stream TV shows, movies - for free
Advancements in artificial intelligence (AI) are making it more difficult to detect, confirms Maryam Saeed, head of risk at Visa Canada.
"Fraudsters are becoming increasingly sophisticated, using AI to create convincing scams to ensnare even the savviest of consumers," she says.
For example, AI can help fraudsters write more professional-looking messages, with fewer spelling and grammatical mistakes, as well as provide the ability to "deep fake" a familiar voice on the phone.
"We carried out a report, and what we realized is that more than 1 in 3, almost 35% of Canadians, admit to falling for a scam - on more than one occasion," Saeed says.
So, it's time to put an end to these scammers once and for all.
The good news is you don't need to be tech savvy to safeguard your data and devices.
What better time than in March - Fraud Prevention Month - to go over the basics. The following is a look at how to up your game in only a few minutes.
Keep track of all your banking transactions to watch out for fraudulent activity.
KNOWLEDGE IS POWER
The first tip is to be vigilant against the fraudsters.
Exercise common sense when reading emails and text messages. No respectful company will ask you to urgently confirm your identity by filling out forms. Never click on suspicious attachments or links. Don't trust anyone calling you claiming to be from your bank or credit card issuer, the CRA, Microsoft, your ISP, and so on.
When in doubt, contact the company - like your bank - to ask if it really was them. Chances are, it wasn't. And be sure to use a phone number you already have for them or look it up outside of any number provided inside the message you received.
"Whenever something seems too urgent or sounds too good to be true, my advice is to take a pause," Saeed suggests. "There's no harm missing a call or calling back later. Just be very careful around the information you're sharing."
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
Resist free public WiFi, as you're more likely to be hacked than browsing on a secure connection at home. Or use your smartphone as a personal hotspot instead.
When shopping online, stick with reputable retailers, and before you type in your credit card look for indicators that the site is secure, such as a little lock icon on the browser's status bar or a URL for a website that begins "https:" (the "s" stands for "secure").
"Also, keep a record of all your transactions and check your statements" in case something looks off, advises Saeed. "This is an effective way to stay on top of everything, in case your credit information has been compromised unknowingly."
Using biometric options such as Apple FaceID are a good way to protect your electronic devices.
OTHER CYBERSECURITY TIPS
A few other ways to remain cybersafe during Fraud Prevention Month, and beyond:
- Be aware of social media scams, such as a legitimate-looking direct message from a "friend" over Facebook Messenger or Instagram, that say they're stuck in some city and need money right away. Your real friend's account was compromised and cloned.
- Remember to have a strong password for all your accounts, which is at least seven characters long and a combination of letters, numbers and symbols (bonus points for adding upper and lowercase characters). Never use the same password for all your online activity, because if a site or app is breached, then the crooks have access to all your accounts. Password manager apps, like Dashlane and LastPass, aren't a bad idea.
- For online banking and shopping apps, opt for "multifactor authentication," which not only requires your password to log in but also a one-time code sent to your mobile device, to prove it's really you.
- Use the "private" or "incognito" mode of your browser, which deletes your history and cookies after your session. Better yet, consider a Virtual Private Network (VPN) app to remain anonymous when online.
- Make sure you lock your device, in case it's stolen or lost, by adding a PIN, password or biometrics option (like fingerprint or facial scan). Set up the "Find my phone" feature, which lets you remotely wipe your phone's data clean if lost or stolen, and see where you left it on a map (when logged into another device with the same login credentials). If it's stolen, never try to retrieve it on your own - instead, contact the authorities.
- Install and update reputable antimalware on all your devices, such as McAfee Ultimate Family, which can identify, quarantine, delete, and report any suspicious activity. The most robust software offers a suite of services, including a firewall and encryption options.
- Finally, set up automatic updates on all your tech, including your operating system, browser, plug-ins, and other software. You only need to do this once. For software that doesn't allow for automatic updates, check them regularly.
Younger people are more likely to connect to public WiFi.
BOOMERS ARE SMARTER THAN YOU THINK
While aging Canadians are disproportionately targeted by fraudsters, they are "a lot more vigilant than other generations about online safety," says Vivianne Gauci, senior vice president of Customer Experience at HomeEquity Bank.
It's younger generations, like Millennials and Gen Z, who are opening themselves up more to scams by engaging in "risky online behaviour," perhaps because of comfort and familiarity that breeds a false sense of security.
Vivianne Gauci is the senior vice president of Customer Experience at HomeEquity Bank.
Despite this finding, two thirds (66%) of Canadians polled think Boomers are the most likely to fall victim to online scams, according to the results of a recent survey by Ipsos and HomeEquity Bank, which services Canadian homeowners ages 55 and older, and is best-known as the provider of the CHIP Reverse Mortgage.
"It's not just because (younger generations) are using technology more, but it's things like connecting to public WiFi and using autofill for passwords or the same one for multiple accounts," Gauci explains.
Boomers lead all other demographics when it comes to being constantly concerned about fraud when using the internet - 85% versus a 76% average for all other generations.

Subject: Generations; Software; Canadians; Smartphones; Reverse mortgages; Artificial intelligence; Virtual private networks; Internet service providers; Fraud prevention; Social networks; Banking; Wireless access points; Cybersecurity

Business indexing term: Subject: Smartphones Reverse mortgages Artificial intelligence Internet service providers Fraud prevention Social networks Banking

Identifier / keyword: considering; march; fraud; prevention; month; cybersecurity

Document 186 of 356

'Affront to our values'; There's nothing appropriate about protesting outside a synagogue

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/affront-our-values-theres-nothing-appropriate/docview/2954376089/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Affront+to+our+values%27%3B+There%27s+nothing+appropriate+about+protesting+outside+a+synagogue&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: THORNHILL - Fights, threats, hate, intimidation and police.
Lots and lots of police. Not just York Regional Police, whose jurisdiction this was. Peel Regional Police and Toronto Police sent troops over too, including Toronto's mounted unit.
Surreal to witness. The war isn't just happening in Gaza but here in the GTA - all happening in a usually quiet and lawful Vaughan neighbourhood Thursday.
It was mayhem there and a glimpse into what anarchy across the country would look like. This was a powder keg waiting to blow. While there were a few flare-ups, police managed to keep it from becoming a full explosion. But, man, this was close.
This is Canada now. "The truly appalling scene permitted to unfold yesterday in Thornhill is a stain on the moral fabric of the entire country," said Richard Robertson, B'nai Brith's research and advocacy director. "Canadians do not harass and intimidate their fellow citizens on the precipice of their houses of worship and in front of their own homes.
"To do so is an affront to our national values and an abuse of our hard-earned freedoms. To travel to a Jewish neighbourhood to villainize its residents is not exercising your rights. It is an act of heinous anti-Semitism."
Here's a question for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford: What would happen if Jewish people from the GTA descended on an Islamic centre and mosque like pro-Hamas supporters did at a synagogue here Thursday? Would there be a tweet denouncing bringing the protest to a place of worship? It's doubtful it would ever happen. Hopefully it never does.
The Jewish people I spoke with at this unfortunate event said that, no matter what, they'd never take any grievance to a mosque. That hundreds of pro-Palestinian Canadians would go to the front of Beth Avraham Yoseph of Toronto on Clark Ave. W., near Bathurst St., in Thornhill and stay all day and into the night is disturbing.
It's nothing more than anti-Semitism, unlawful assembly and intimidation.
Their excuse that they're protesting a real estate seminar offering information about how to purchase a property in the Holy Land is a smokescreen. If they have a grievance about that or anything else, they can legally express their concerns outside of Vaughan's city hall or Toronto's or at Queen's Park or Parliament Hill or outside the Israeli consulate.
"It is unacceptable that a place of worship, or nearby schools and daycares, be targeted in this way," Vaughan Mayor Steven Del Duca wrote in a joint statement also signed by Councillor Gila Marlow, Thornhill MP Melissa Lantsman and Thornhill MPP Laura Smith. "The right to peaceful protest is an important freedom afforded to all Canadians. But the freedom does not give people the right to incite hate, make threats, or engage in acts of violence."
There was nothing peaceful about what happened here Thursday. There were plenty of calls for intefadeh and driving Jews out of Israel. York Regional Police made three arrests after about four major fights broke out during the day.
Interestingly, all three of those arrested were pro-Israel demonstrators.
In one incident, police said officers allegedly spotted a man in the crowd carrying a knife and arrested him.
Kevin Haas, 40, of Markham, is charged with possession of a weapon.
In a second incident, a protester was arrested for allegedly assaulting a woman, police said.
Meir Gerichter, 32, of Vaughan, is charged with assault.
In a third incident, police said an argument between a man and a woman escalated, with the woman allegedly threatening to spray coyote repellent - similar to pepper spray - at the man, so she was taken into custody.
Ina Sandler, 48, of Vaughan, is charged with possessing a weapon and assault with a weapon.
York cops said their Hate Crime Prevention Unit "has been engaged" in the investigations.
York Regional Police, with the assistance of Toronto and Peel cops, deserve a lot of credit for keeping this from going completely out of control and resulting in death or injuries. The elements were there for this to be a lot worse.
But it's not a two-sides story. This is all on the pro-Palestinian organizers who continually take the provocative action of taking their grievances to synagogues, hospitals, Jewish-owned businesses, schools, restaurants, daycare centres, neighbourhoods, comedy clubs, roadways and even shutting down the prime minister's gala dinner at the Art Gallery of Ontario.
They do this because political leaders like Trudeau, Ford, Mayor Olivia Chow and others don't strongly denounce it and throw the book at the leaders who organize this - some of whom are paid disrupters who have no affiliation with the Israel-Gaza war but are anti-capitalist anarchists. The PM, premier and Toronto mayor can learn a lesson about leadership from Del Duca, who isn't just thinking about elections but thinking about basic decency.
That said, there were many peaceful protesters at this event on both sides. You always have your hotheads but you also have people who are worried about the human side of it - whether it be the horrors of Oct. 7 or the humanitarian concerns of starvation of children in Gaza who don't deserve this plight. You see people with their flags of support but remaining silent and not causing any trouble.
The police can't be blamed for any of this as much as that is an easy thing to do. Their hands are tied. Fatigued, they're facing protests every day and know if they do one thing that is perceived wrong, they'll lose their careers or be a scapegoat for the politicians, as happened to then-Ottawa police chief Peter Sloly over the Freedom Convoy.
In Ottawa, the trucker protesters were benign compared to anything we're seeing here since the Oct. 7 slaughter in Israel. But the management of these protests show a double standard coming from the political leadership.
There's nothing wrong with protesting. But there is something wrong with taking that protest to a synagogue. jwarmington@postmedia.com @joe_warmington

Subject: Antisemitism; Synagogues; Jewish people; Leadership; Criminal investigations; Arrests; Crime prevention; Assaults; Demonstrations & protests; Prime ministers; Art galleries & museums

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Art galleries & museums

Location: Gaza Strip; Israel

Identifier / keyword: thornhill; fights; threats; intimidation; police; regional

Document 187 of 356

Ford tells PM to nix tax hike; Federal Liberals will suffer in next election if increase goes ahead

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-tells-pm-nix-tax-hike-federal-liberals-will/docview/2954376087/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+tells+PM+to+nix+tax+hike%3B+Federal+Liberals+will+suffer+in+next+election+if+increase+goes+ahead&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford is offering Justin Trudeau a bit of friendly advice: Cancel the carbon tax hike on April 1 or voters will punish you next election.
Saying that affordability is the No. 1 issue he hears about from voters, Ford said he doesn't understand why the Trudeau Liberals are going ahead with the tax hike.
"You know the carbon tax is the worst tax you can ever put on the backs of people, and I can't believe the federal government is actually going to hike the gas tax by 23%. Like, what don't you get?" Ford said Friday.
He had been asked if he'd follow Saskatchewan's lead and not collect the carbon tax on home heating.
The premier said Saskatchewan can do that because it owns the biggest supplier of natural gas in the province, but in Ontario those supplying home heating are private companies and he can't ask them to break the law.
"The No. 1 issue with people's affordability," Ford said. "Affordable homes, affordable groceries and affordable gas and what drives up the inflation is energy costs."
He said federal taxes on gas and other energy sources are already high enough.
"Guys, wake up (and) smell the coffee. Like, what don't you understand? Cancel this carbon tax, put it on hold, do whatever, and if you don't, the people of Canada are gonna annihilate you when the election comes up. Simple as that."
It's unlikely the prime minister will take this advice any more than he has taken it from the federal Conservatives, from Liberals in his own caucus or from businesses.
Trudeau is convinced increasing the carbon tax doesn't drive up inflation, even though the tax applies throughout the economy.
While individual Canadians, and families, get carbon tax rebates, the parliamentary budget officer has shown it doesn't cover the total cost borne by consumers.
The tomatoes we buy this time of year, grown in greenhouses, have a carbon tax applied to the natural gas used to grow them. They have the carbon tax applied on shipping them from the farm to the store.
They have the carbon tax charged on the heating, the cooling, the lighting of the store they are sold in.
We don't get a rebate on those costs, only direct costs and even then, based on averages decided by bureaucrats.
When the carbon tax is charged on the fuel excise tax and then GST is charged on top of both of those taxes, we don't get a rebate on those price increases.
A poll conducted by Leger in February for the Canadian Taxpayers'Federation found that most opposed the 23% tax hike on April 1.
What's more shocking is that the question didn't even tell people how steep the tax hike would be, they just simply asked if you supported it.
"On April 1, the federal government will again increase its carbon tax. Do you support or oppose the federal government increasing the carbon tax?" Leger asked 1,590 people across the country.
Nationally, 69% said they opposed the tax hike. In fact, in every region and every demographic group, the majority said they were opposed to this tax hike.
Not that this will stop the Liberals.
They are not only raising the carbon tax on April 1 but they are also increasing the tax on beer, wine and spirits by nearly 5% that same day and on May 1 will increase the security fee charged for air travellers by 33%.
People are hurting, the cost of living is through the roof, but Trudeau wants to make everything more expensive.
Ford is right. Voters will annihilate Trudeau and his party when we get a chance to vote again. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Costs; Voters; Carbon; Natural gas; Heating; Environmental tax; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Costs Environmental tax Tax increases

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Canada

Identifier / keyword: offering; justin; trudeau; friendly; advice; cancel

CAUGHT ON CAMERA: Ford tells Trudeau cancel the carbon tax

Full text: WATCH BELOW as Ontario Premier Doug Ford says Justin Trudeau "needs to smell the coffee" and cancel the carbon tax.

Title: CAUGHT ON CAMERA: Ford tells Trudeau cancel the carbon tax

Publication title: The Toronto Sun (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Mar 8, 2024

Section: News

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document 190 of 356

WARMINGTON: There's nothing appropriate about protesting at a synagogue or place of worship

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/warmington-theres-nothing-appropriate-about/docview/2954189662/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=WARMINGTON%3A+There%27s+nothing+appropriate+about+protesting+at+a+synagogue+or+place+of+worship&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: THORNHILL - Fights, threats, hate, intimidation and police.
Lots and lots of police. Not just York Regional Police whose jurisdiction this was in. Peel Regional Police and Toronto Police sent troops over too which included TPS's mounted unit.
Surreal to witness. The war is not just happening in Gaza but here in the GTA - all happening in a usually quiet and lawful Vaughan neighbourhood Thursday.
It was mayhem there and a glimpse into what anarchy across the country would look like. This was a powderkeg waiting to blow. While there were a few flare-ups, police managed to keep it from becoming a full explosion. But man, this was close.
This is Canada now.
This was a wild scene outside a Thornhill Synagogue Thursday - Joe Warmington photo
"The truly appalling scene permitted to unfold yesterday in Thornhill is a stain on the moral fabric of the entire country," said Richard Robertson, B'nai Brith Research and Advocacy director. "Canadians do not harass and intimidate their fellow citizens on the precipice of their houses of worship and in front of their own homes. "To do so is an affront to our national values and an abuse of our hard-earned freedoms. To travel to a Jewish neighbourhood to villainize its residents is not exercising your rights. It is an act of heinous anti-Semitism."
Here's a question for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford: What would happen if Jewish people from the GTA descended on an Islamic centre and mosque like pro-Hamas supporters did at a synagogue here Thursday?
Would there be a tweet denouncing bringing the protest to a place of worship?
It's doubtful it would ever happen. Hopefully it never does. The Jewish people I spoke with at this unfortunate event said no matter what they would never take any grievance to a mosque. That hundreds of pro-Palestinian Canadians would go to the front of Beth Avraham Yoseph of Toronto on Clark Ave. W. near Bathurst St. in Thornhill and stay all day and into the night is disturbing.
Will the police take action against this guy who targeted the BAYT Synagogue and Jewish Community
- neveragainlivepodcast@gmail.com (@neveragainlive1) March 8, 2024
It's nothing more than anti-Semitism, unlawful assembly and intimidation.
Their excuse that they are protesting a real estate seminar offering information about how to purchase a property in the Holy Land is a smokescreen. If they have a grievance about that or anything else, they can legally express their concerns outside of Vaughan's city hall or Toronto's or at Queen's Park or Parliament Hill or outside the Israel consulate or embassy.
Thornhill
- Joe Warmington (@joe_warmington) March 8, 2024
"It is unacceptable that a place of worship, or nearby schools and daycares, be targeted in this way," wrote Vaughan Mayor Steven Del Duca in a joint statement also signed by Vaughan Councillor Gila Marlow, Thornhill MP Melissa Lantsman and Thornhill MPP Laura Smith. "The right to peaceful protest is an important freedom afforded to all Canadians. But the freedom does not give people then right to incite hate, make threats, or engage in acts of violence."
There was nothing peaceful about what happened here Thursday. There were plenty of calls for driving Jews out of Israel and intifada. York Regional Police made several arrests after about four major fights broke out during the day. They indicated they will update the public Friday with the charges laid in the eight-hours of protest.
YRP, with the assistance of Toronto and Peel, deserve a lot of credit for keeping this from going completely out of control and resulting in death or injuries. The elements were there for this to be a lot worse.
Large police presence Thursday in Thornhill Thursday - Joe Warmington photo
But it's not a two sides story. This is all on the pro-Palestinian organizers who continually take the provocative action of taking their grievances to synagogues, hospitals, Jewish-owned business, schools, restaurants, daycare centres, neighbourhoods, comedy clubs, roadways and even shutting down the prime minister's gala dinner at the Art Gallery of Ontario.
They do this because political leaders like Trudeau, Ford, Mayor Olivia Chow and others don't strongly denounce it and throw the book at the leaders who organize this - some of whom are paid disrupters who have no affiliation with the Israel/Gaza war but are anti-capitalist anarchists. The prime minister, premier and Toronto mayor can learn a lesson about leadership from Del Duca, who is not just thinking about elections but thinking about basic decency.
That said, there were many peaceful protesters at this event on both sides. You always have your hot heads but you also have people who are worried about the human side of it - whether it be the horrors of Oct. 7 or the humanitarian concerns of starvation of children in Gaza who don't deserve this plight. You see people with their flags of support but remaining silent and not causing any trouble.
Many on the scene were not heckling or yelling but just representing their strong views on the slaughter of Oct. 7th - Joe Warmington photo
The police can't be blamed for any of this as much as that is an easy thing to do. Their hands are tied. Fatigued, they are facing protests every day and know if they do one thing that is perceived wrong, they will lose their careers or be a scapegoat for the politicians like former Ottawa Police chief Peter Sloly ended up being during the Freedom Convoy.
In Ottawa, the trucker protesters were benign compared to anything we are seeing here since the Oct. 7 slaughter in Israel. But the management of these protests show a double standard coming form the political leadership.
There's nothing wrong with protesting. But there is something wrong with taking that protest to a synagogue.
jwarmington@postmedia.com
WARMINGTON: Condoning anti-Semitism will lead to hate spiralling out of control
WARMINGTON: Protesters following Trudeau up ski hill is slippery slope for Canada's security

Subject: Antisemitism; Synagogues; Jewish people; Leadership; Demonstrations & protests; Prime ministers; Diplomatic & consular services; Art galleries & museums

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Art galleries & museums

Location: Gaza Strip; Canada; Israel

Identifier / keyword: thornhill; fights; threats; intimidation; police; regional

Document 191 of 356

Grit shooting blanks; Liberals at Queen's Park show ignorance on guns and justice

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/grit-shooting-blanks-liberals-at-queens-park-show/docview/2952444441/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Grit+shooting+blanks%3B+Liberals+at+Queen%27s+Park+show+ignorance+on+guns+and+justice&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you ever needed proof that Liberals don't understand gun crime, gun control or gun policy beyond using it to scare people, Stephen Blais is walking proof.
The MPP for Orleans in Ottawa stood up to try to make a point this week on guns and judges getting tough on crime and utterly failed.
The Liberals at Queen's Park have been trying to make hay out of the fact that Premier Doug Ford appointed two people who used to work for him to the panel that vets lawyers seeking judicial appointments. Imagine that, the party that won the election appointing people who agree with their philosophy to advisory roles in the government.
This is a complete non-scandal because not only does every party do this, but they should do this. You advance the agenda you were elected on by relying on those who agree with you to get things done.
No matter that the previous Ontario Liberal government did this or that the federal Liberal government that Blais supports does this, he and his party want to make this an issue.
On Wednesday though, Blais decided to go a step further and point out that Matt Bondy, Ford's former deputy chief of staff who is now chairman of the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee, is also registered to lobby for a gun manufacturer.
"How can we believe the premier's toughon-crime
STEPHEN BLAIS stance when he's asked the guy who sells the guns to appoint the judges?" Blais asked during question period.
And with that, he showed everyone that he knows absolutely nothing about guns.
Bondy is indeed a lobbyist for a gun manufacturer, a company out of Kitchener called Colt Canada that only makes and sells weapons to the Canadian military and law enforcement. In fact, Colt's only reason to exist with their facility in Kitchener is so Canada has a secure domestic supply of rifles and other small arms for the Canadian Canada. They make some fantastic firearms, but they don't sell into the retail market.
Nor are their guns found in the hands of criminals on the streets of Ottawa. No one is doing a drug deal with a C7 rifle, a C20 sniper rifle or Colt's Eagle single-shot grenade launcher.
These are products designed for and sold to the Canadian military and police services across the country. Colt produces the guns used by the guys who arrest the bad guys with guns.
But why let the truth get in the way of scaring people? Doug Ford has expressed his frustration with judges and justices of the peace giving light sentences or letting repeat violent gun offenders out on bail for years now. This isn't a new thing for him. He has been saying this since before he became premier.
But now that he has appointed two people who used to work with him, and who agree with his judicial philosophy, to the panel that screens judges, the Liberals, the NDP and much of the media are losing their collectivist little minds.
Why? It's not because Liberals don't appoint like-minded people when they're in office, it's that they do it and claim they're all being non-partisan and neutral when in reality, they aren't.
Ford is being upfront in what he wants.
"I'm proud to go out there and tell the people who are seeing violence in their homes, violence on the streets, violence in our subways that we're going to get judges that are actually going to keep these criminals in jail," Ford said in response to Blais'bizarre question.
Ask the public what they care about, it isn't lobbyists, it's keeping criminals behind bars.
That's something Blais'Liberal party has been against for years.

Subject: Violence; Judicial appointments; Firearms

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: needed; proof; liberals; understand; crime; control

Title: Grit shooting blanks; Liberals at Queen's Park show ignorance on guns and justice

Publication title: The Toronto Sun; Toronto

First page: A.5

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Mar 8, 2024

column: Opinion

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

blilley@postmedia.com

What Will It Take?

Author: Vernon, Bill

Publication info: Vernon, Bill. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/what-will-take/docview/2940991919/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+Will+It+Take%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=Vernon%2C+Bill&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Helpless, abandoned - that's my feelings, not only in cities in Ontario, but across this nation. CSIS can't/won't protect us from foreign nationals operating openly, RCMP seemingly answering to the federal Liberals, city policing paralyzed by woken/political fear. Our country is dancing to the tunes of this wokeness, vocal radical groups practicing anarchy - all enabled by those whose job description is to ensure a peaceful, orderly way of a democratic life. Gone is any semblance of that, government coverups are the norm, whether it be cancelled gas plants, document shredding, refusing to divulge information about election interference, the security of government medical facilities. In these last eight years, a seemingly endless parade of deliberate corruption.
So, we have to ask ourselves, what next, how far down is the bottom? Why is everyone silent during this collapse of law, order, absent on so many levels - and can we even hope for some relief? For sure of thing one, do not look at the unholy alliance of Trudeau/Singh, the paper tiger of Ford, the total indifferent wokeness of Chow, for any answers.
(Canadians are tolerant and our willingness to forgive government failures - at all levels - is part of who we are. But there is a different sense out there in terms of support for the Trudeau Liberals in the next election. We want change)

Letters to the Editor, March 7, 2024

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/letters-editor-march-7-2024/docview/2938606872/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: PLAY BALL!
In the 'baseball's been very good to me' unique slice of life department are Ben and Dan Shulman on their way to one day becoming the historic once-in-a-lifetime Ken Griffeys (and hockey's Gordie and Mark Howe) tandem of broadcasting? Hearty congratulations to the Shulman father and son duo. At least the Blue Jays play-by-play booth is solid.
Victor Redlick
Toronto
(Jays fans are looking forward to the season, including the broadcasting)
LIBERALS DON'T DESERVE YOUR SUPPORT
Congratulations to the Conservatives for winning the Durham byelection. What stands out for me is that the Liberals managed to get more than 7,000 votes. My question is this: How many scandals, carbon taxes, lies, coverups, international mishaps, arrogant ministers, etc. does it take to make those 7,000 or so voters angry enough to wipe the Liberal Party to oblivion? Just asking.
Rick Dwornikiewicz
Delhi, Ont.
(For the die-hard Liberals, scandals don't matter and they will always support their team)
WHAT WILL IT TAKE?
Helpless, abandoned - that's my feelings, not only in cities in Ontario, but across this nation. CSIS can't/won't protect us from foreign nationals operating openly, RCMP seemingly answering to the federal Liberals, city policing paralyzed by woke/political fear. Our country is dancing to the tunes of this wokeness, vocal radical groups practising anarchy - all enabled by those whose job description it is to ensure a peaceful, orderly way of a democratic life. Gone is any semblance of that, government coverups are the norm, whether it be cancelled gas plants, document shredding, refusing to divulge information about election interference, and the security of government medical facilities. In these last eight years, a seemingly endless parade of deliberate corruption. So, we have to ask ourselves, what's next, how far down is the bottom? Why is everyone silent during this collapse of law and order - absent on so many levels - and can we even hope for some relief? Be sure of thing one: Do not look at the unholy alliance of Trudeau/Singh, the paper tiger, Ford, or the total indifferent wokeness of Chow, for any answers.
Bill Vernon
(Canadians are tolerant and our willingness to forgive government failures - at all levels - is part of who we are. But there is a different sense out there in terms of support for the Trudeau Liberals in the next election. We want change)

Document 195 of 356

Bellwether vote?; Durham's byelection results matter very much to all parties

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bellwether-vote-durhams-byelection-results-matter/docview/2938151074/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bellwether+vote%3F%3B+Durham%27s+byelection+results+matter+very+much+to+all+parties&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Byelections don't really matter, until they do, and Monday's Durham byelection mattered.
Not due to the overall result, the Conservatives were expected to win a riding they've held for 20 years, but the size of Jamil Jivani's win, the Liberal loss and NDP collapse are noteworthy.
Despite Prime Minister Justin Trudeau campaigning for Liberal candidate Robert Rock, despite the star-studded Canada AM team of Seamus O'Regan and Marci Ien campaigning for him, he lost badly. The Liberals set expectations that even if they didn't win, they'd be competitive - they even publicly boasted they'd win.
The final results weren't even close.
Jivani, a former Toronto Sun columnist, took 57.4% of the vote, that's up from the 46.4% the Tories took when Erin O'Toole ran here in the 2021 election. The Liberals, though, took just 22.5% of the vote, while the NDP collapsed down to 10.4%.
It wasn't that long ago that the NDP was finishing second in this riding, now it's in third place. The odd part is, when the NDP collapses, at least in most of Ontario, it benefits the Liberals - but not this time.
Even if all of the NDP votes had disappeared and fallen in behind the Liberals, Jivani would have still handily won this seat for the Conservatives.
Clearly, some of that old NDP vote shifted to support Jivani.
Yes, there will be arguments that voter turnout is low in a byelection - just 27.9% of eligible voters came out - but that is always true in byelections. Yes, there will be arguments that byelections are safe protest votes against the government and the Tories held this seat already.
There are many reasons to try to discount this or any byelection result, but the reason this one matters is the 35% margin of victory for Jivani and the Conservatives, and the 22% showing for the Liberals.
The Liberal showing is proof that the polls are as bad as they seem.
Liberals representing nearby ridings like Jennifer O'Connell in Pickering-Uxbridge, Ryan Turnbull in Whitby and even Health Minister Mark Holland in Ajax must be looking at that 22% number and worrying. Holland especially knows what happens to Liberals in these ridings when there is a Conservative surge. He represented the area from 2004 until 2011 when he lost his seat until winning it back in 2015.
There is definitely a Tory surge happening and if it sustains itself until the next election, there won't be many Liberals in Ontario outside of Toronto and Ottawa. The federal Liberals won 78 of the province's 121 ridings but are projected to win just 25 seats, possibly less, in Ontario if an election were held today.
Nationally, the Conservatives have maintained a 16%-17% point lead over the Liberals for the past couple of months.
The latest Abacus poll, though, shows that outside of Quebec, the Tories have 46% voter support, the Liberals drop down to 23% and the NDP is at 22%, meaning it's effectively tied for second, while Pierre Poilievre's team holds a 23-point lead.
Durham's results are close to that for the Liberals, which should be causing concern, if not outright panic, in the PMO and at Liberal headquarters.
Liberal fortunes are falling and Conservative fortunes are rising, and, in Jamil Jivani, Poilievre and his team just picked up a star. He's a solid communicator who adds to the bench strength Poilievre needs to have to show voters that he and his party are a government in waiting.
Based on the Durham results, it's all over but the waiting. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Voters; Voter behavior

Document 196 of 356

MPs hike our taxes and stuff their own wallets

Author: Terrazzano, Ctffranco

Publication info: Terrazzano, Ctffranco. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mps-hike-our-taxes-stuff-their-own-wallets/docview/2938150681/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPs+hike+our+taxes+and+stuff+their+own+wallets&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=Terrazzano%2C+Ctffranco&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On the same day members of Parliament take more money out of your wallet, they'll be stuffing more into their own.
On April 1, the federal government is hiking carbon and alcohol taxes. That very same day, MPs will take another pay raise.
A backbench MP's salary is currently $194,600.
A minister collects $287,400, while the prime minister takes home a $389,200 annual salary.
This year's estimated pay raise will range from an extra $8,100 for a backbencher to $16,200 for PM Justin Trudeau, based on contract data published by the government.
But this year's raise is just the tip of the iceberg.
In fact, this year's raise will be MPs'fifth pay raise since the beginning of 2020.
A pandemic, lockdowns, job and business losses and their constituents not being able to afford ground beef hasn't stopped MPs from taking raises every year.
As of this April, MPs will receive an annual salary about $23,800 higher than they did pre-pandemic, while Trudeau will take home an extra $47,600.
The government could stop the upcoming pay raise like it did between 2010 and '13 in response to the recession.
But so far, the silence from all political parties on this year's pay raise has been deafening.
Politicians don't deserve raises when they make life more expensive with tax hikes.
After the April hike, the carbon tax will cost 17 cents per litre of gasoline, 21 cents per litre of diesel and 15 cents per cubic metre of natural gas.
That carbon tax will cost the average family up to $911 a year more than they get back rebates, according to the parliamentary budget officer.
While the feds are busy raising taxes, provincial governments of all political stripes have provided taxpayers with relief.
The New Democrats in Manitoba suspended their 14-cent-perlitre gas tax.
The Liberals in Newfoundland and Labrador cut their gas tax by 8 cents per litre. Conservative governments in Alberta and Ontario also provided fuel tax relief.
You could be forgiven if the upcoming MP pay raise and carbon tax hike drive you to drink. But there's more bad news: Trudeau will reach deeper into your pocket whenever you go to the liquor store or bar.
Taxes already make up more than half the price Canadians pay for alcohol.
In fact, Canadians pay five times more tax on a case of beer than our friends south of the border. In Saskatchewan, Prince Edward Island and Newfoundland and Labrador, the tax on a case of beer costs more than the total price of a case in half of American states.
And on April 1, Trudeau is hiking his federal taxes on alcohol by another 4.7%.
Even worse, Trudeau made the alcohol tax hike automatic so MPs won't even vote on it.
Last year, the feds capped their alcohol tax increase at 2%. Trudeau shouldn't have hiked the tax at all, but the smaller increase reduced the tax burden by $100 million. The least the federal government could do this year is freeze its alcohol taxes.
Politicians shouldn't be giving themselves a raise the same day they take more money from tapped-out taxpayers. Politicians shouldn't take more money from people already lined up at food banks. And they don't deserve a penny more.
All politicians from every political party should oppose April's tax and pay hike.
Terrazzano is federal director of the Canadian Taxpayers Federation

Subject: Raises; Alcohol; Members of Parliament; Politics; Carbon; Gasoline taxes; Politicians; Pandemics; Environmental tax; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Raises Gasoline taxes Environmental tax Tax increases

Location: Canada; Newfoundland & Labrador Canada

Identifier / keyword: members; parliament; money; wallet; stuffing; april

LILLEY: Durham's byelection results matter very much to all parties

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-durhams-byelection-results-matter-very/docview/2937673059/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Durham%27s+byelection+results+matter+very+much+to+all+parties&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Byelections don't really matter, until they do, and Monday's Durham byelection mattered.
Not due to the overall result, the Conservatives were expected to win a riding they've held for 20 years, but the size of Jamil Jivani's win, the Liberal loss and NDP collapse are noteworthy.
Despite Justin Trudeau campaigning for Liberal candidate Robert Rock, despite the star-studded Canada AM team of Seamus O'Regan and Marci Ien campaigning for him, he lost badly. The Liberals set expectations that even if they didn't win, they would be competitive - they even publicly boasted they would win.
The final results weren't even close.
Jivani, a former Toronto Sun columnist, took 57.4% of the vote, that's up from the 46.4% the Conservatives took when Erin O'Toole ran here in the 2021 election. The Liberals, though, took just 22.5% of the vote while the NDP collapsed down to 10.4%.
It wasn't that long ago that the NDP was finishing second in this riding, now they are in collapse at third place. The odd part is, when the NDP collapses, at least in most of Ontario, it benefits the Liberals - but not this time.
Even if all of the NDP votes had disappeared and fallen in behind the Liberals, Jivani would have still handily won this seat for the Conservatives. Clearly, some of that old NDP vote shifted to support Jivani.
Dear @JustinTrudeau,
Durham sent you a message today. Did you receive it?
Best,
Jamil Jivani
- Jamil Jivani (@jamiljivani) March 5, 2024
Yes, there will be arguments that voter turnout is low in a byelection - just 27.9% of eligible voters came out - but that is always true in byelections. Yes, there will be arguments that byelections are safe protest votes against the government and the Conservatives held this seat already.
There are many reasons to try to discount this or any byelection result but the reason this one matters is the 35% margin of victory for Jivani and the Conservatives and the 22% showing for the Liberals.
The Liberal showing is proof that the polls are as bad as they seem.
Liberals representing nearby ridings like Jennifer O'Connell in Pickering-Uxbridge, Ryan Turnbull in Whitby and even Health Minister Mark Holland in Ajax must be looking at that 22% number and worrying. Holland especially knows what happens to Liberals in these ridings when there is a Conservative surge. He represented the area from 2004 until 2011 when he lost his seat until winning it back in 2015.
There is definitely a Conservative surge happening and if it sustains itself until the next election, there won't be many Liberals in Ontario outside of Toronto and Ottawa. The federal Liberals won 78 of the province's 121 ridings but are projected to win just 25 seats, possibly less, in Ontario if an election were held today.
Nationally, the Conservatives have maintained a 16-17% point lead over the Liberals for the past couple of months. The latest Abacus poll, though, shows that outside of Quebec, the Conservatives have 46% voter support, the Liberals drop down to 23% and the NDP are at 22%, meaning they are effectively tied for second while Pierre Poilievre's team holds a 23-point lead.
Durham's results are close to that for the Liberals, which should be causing concern, if not outright panic, in the PMO and at Liberal headquarters.
'Common sense' Conservative Jamil Jivani cruises to victory in Durham byelection
Trudeau won't apologize for calling Conservative Jamil Jivani 'twofer'
Liberal fortunes are falling and Conservative fortunes are rising and in Jamil Jivani, Poilievre and his team just picked up a star. He's a solid communicator who adds to the bench strength Poilievre needs to have to show voters that he and his party are a government in waiting.
Based on the Durham results, it's all over but the waiting.
blilley@postmedia.com
Congratulations to common sense Conservative @jamiljivani on being elected as the new Member of Parliament for Durham.
Together we will axe the tax, build the homes, fix the budget, and stop the crime.
Let's #BringItHome
- Pierre Poilievre (@PierrePoilievre) March 5, 2024

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Full text: Conservative candidate Jamil Jivani celebrated his victory in the Ontario riding of Durham by thanking his allies and pouncing on his "Liberal elite" rivals, who he says are making life harder and more expensive for the working class.
Preliminary results from Elections Canada showed the former Toronto Sun columnist is poised for a seat in the House of Commons following Monday's byelection.
Jivani, a lawyer and commentator, had secured over 55 per cent of the votes with nearly all polls reporting as of late Monday evening.
Liberal candidate Robert Rock secured the second-most votes, and NDP candidate Chris Borgia placed third.
While Durham is a Conservative stronghold, it's one of the largest margins of victory the Tories have had in the riding in the past 20 years.
Dear @JustinTrudeau,
Durham sent you a message today. Did you receive it?
Best,
Jamil Jivani
- Jamil Jivani (@jamiljivani) March 5, 2024
Jivani's success comes as the Tories continue to surge in polls ahead of the governing Liberal party, and as Conservatives push a message that promises to "axe the tax, build the homes, fix the budget and stop the crime."
In his victory speech at Chuck's Roadhouse Bar & Grill in Courtice, Ont., Jivani said it's his Conservative party that will best represent the values ??of communities in the upcoming federal election, which must happen by fall of 2025. Under Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, the party will "bring change to this country," he said.
"When we talk about what's happening in the country right now, I do think it is the Liberal elites betraying the working people of this country, betraying the middle class, betraying the working class," Jivani said.
Those Liberal elites, Jivani said, include Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, the Liberal party, big banks and telecommunication companies who are "driving up the cost of everything."
Congratulations to common sense Conservative @jamiljivani on being elected as the new Member of Parliament for Durham.
Together we will axe the tax, build the homes, fix the budget, and stop the crime.
Let's #BringItHome
- Pierre Poilievre (@PierrePoilievre) March 5, 2024
He also pointed to the Ministry of Education under Ontario Premier Doug Ford. In 2022, Jivani resigned as Ontario's first community opportunities advocate after raising concerns that some of the Progressive Conservative government's COVID-19 pandemic policies were having a negative impact on marginalized youth and their parents.
Jivani also criticized other "Liberal elites" that he said include activists and academics "who undermine law enforcement and public safety."
"They're easy to spot," Jivani charged, telling his crowd of supporters to look out for people who are pushing "diversity, equity and inclusion" and "environmental, social and governance" initiatives "while hiking up the cost of living. "
"They are the people who virtue signal and engage in performance theater while your life gets harder," he said.
"And they are the people who serve the interests of a privileged few and make you feel like a bad person for expecting our country to work for you, and they are wrong."
WARMINGTON: Justin Trudeau won't apologize for calling Conservative Jamil Jivani 'twofer'
Jamil Jivani seeks federal Conservative nomination in Durham riding
The Durham byelection was held to replace former Conservative leader Erin O'Toole who served as official Opposition leader from August 2020 until February 2022, and left his seat last spring.
Durham has been a long-time conservative stronghold, with a Tory representing that riding for the last 20 years.
Congratulations to @JamilJivani & the Durham CPC team on his big byelection win. I know he will be a strong voice for Durham in Ottawa
- Erin O'Toole (@erinotoole) March 5, 2024
The restaurant was packed for Jivani's victory speech, with his supporters and staff cheering from wall to wall.
Several of his future caucus members were also there to show their support, including Ontario MP Michelle Ferreri, who represents the Peterborough_Kawartha riding, and Ontario MP Colin Carrie, who represents Oshawa.
Poilievre celebrated the victory on X, formerly known as Twitter, by congratulating Jivani online.
At the end of his 10-minute speech, someone handed Jivani a wrestling belt. He put it on his shoulder and shouted, "Let's go!"

'Common sense' Conservative Jivani cruises to victory in Durham byelection

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/common-sense-conservative-jivani-cruises-victory/docview/2937476406/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Common+sense%27+Conservative+Jivani+cruises+to+victory+in+Durham+byelection&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Durham Region is staying true blue with Jamil Jivani winning by a wide margin in Monday's federal byelection.
The region has been held by the Tories for two decades and won't be changing after voters went to the polls to elect a new member of Parliament.
The byelection was called in January after Erin O'Toole announced his retirement from politics and resigned from his seat last year. O'Toole, who was among those congratulating Jivani on social media as he pulled away in the race with a more than 30-percentage-point lead, held the riding for the Conservatives for 11 years and also led the party in the 2021 federal election.
Congratulations to @JamilJivani & the Durham CPC team on his big byelection win. I know he will be a strong voice for Durham in Ottawa
- Erin O'Toole (@erinotoole) March 5, 2024
"I know he will be a strong voice for Durham in Ottawa," O'Toole said Monday night on X.
Tory Leader Pierre Poilievre also congratulated the "common sense Conservative" on the victory at about 10 p.m.
Congratulations to common sense Conservative @jamiljivani on being elected as the new Member of Parliament for Durham.
Together we will axe the tax, build the homes, fix the budget, and stop the crime.
Let's #BringItHome
- Pierre Poilievre (@PierrePoilievre) March 5, 2024
Jivani jumped to an early lead and never looked back. As of late Monday night, he had secured 56% of the votes with 93% of the polls reporting.
His closest competitor, the Liberals' Robert Rock, was in second with 22.5%, followed by the New Democratic Party's Chris Borgia with 10.5%.
Jivani is a conservative commentator and author and penned opinion columns for the Toronto Sun before throwing his hat in the federal ring.
The 36-year-old is the founder of the Policing Literacy Initiative and Teachers Beyond the Classroom. He sat on Premier Doug Ford's council on equality of opportunity and was appointed the advocate for community opportunities for the Ford government.
WARMINGTON: Justin Trudeau won't apologize for calling Conservative Jamil Jivani 'twofer'
Jamil Jivani seeks federal Conservative nomination in Durham riding
Jivani was born in Toronto to an immigrant father from Kenya and a Scottish-Irish mother from the U.K. He attended Humber College, York University and in 2013 earned his juris doctor from Yale Law School.
The byelection comes as public polling suggests Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives hold a double-digit lead over Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals.
Durham has roughly 116,250 registered voters and includes a mix of suburban and rural areas.
O'Toole won re-election by at least nine percentage points in 2015, 2019 and 2021.

Document 201 of 356

'Common sense' Conservative Jivani cruising to victory in Durham byelection

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/common-sense-conservative-jivani-cruising-victory/docview/2937449601/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Common+sense%27+Conservative+Jivani+cruising+to+victory+in+Durham+byelection&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Durham Region is staying true blue with Jamil Jivani leading by a wide margin in Monday's federal byelection.
The region has been held by the Tories for two decades and that doesn't seem to be changing after voters went to the polls to elect a new member of Parliament.
The byelection was called in January after Erin O'Toole announced his retirement from politics and resigned from his seat last year. O'Toole, who was among those congratulating Jivani on social media as he pulled away in the race with a more than 30-percentage-point lead, held the riding for the Conservatives for 11 years and also led the party in the 2021 federal election.
Congratulations to @JamilJivani & the Durham CPC team on his big byelection win. I know he will be a strong voice for Durham in Ottawa
- Erin O'Toole (@erinotoole) March 5, 2024
"I know he will be a strong voice for Durham in Ottawa," O'Toole said Monday night on X.
Polls closed at 8:30 p.m. and Jivani jumped to an early lead, securing 13,744 votes - or about 55.7% of ballots cast - with 195 out of 225 polls reporting
Tory Leader Pierre Poilievre also congratulated the "common sense Conservative" on the victory at about 10 p.m.
Congratulations to common sense Conservative @jamiljivani on being elected as the new Member of Parliament for Durham.
Together we will axe the tax, build the homes, fix the budget, and stop the crime.
Let's #BringItHome
- Pierre Poilievre (@PierrePoilievre) March 5, 2024
His closest competitor, the Liberals' Robert Rock, was in second with 5,372 votes (22.2%), followed by the New Democratic Party's Chris Borgia with 2,612 votes (10.8%).
Jivani is a conservative commentator and author and penned opinion columns for the Toronto Sun before throwing his hat in the federal ring.
The 36-year-old is the founder of the Policing Literacy Initiative and Teachers Beyond the Classroom. He sat on Premier Doug Ford's council on equality of opportunity and was appointed the advocate for community opportunities for the Ford government.
WARMINGTON: Justin Trudeau won't apologize for calling Conservative Jamil Jivani 'twofer'
Jamil Jivani seeks federal Conservative nomination in Durham riding
Jivani was born in Toronto to an immigrant father from Kenya and a Scottish-Irish mother from the U.K. He attended Humber College, York University and in 2013 earned his juris doctor from Yale Law School.
The byelection comes as public polling suggests Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives hold a double-digit lead over Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals.
Durham has roughly 116,250 registered voters and includes a mix of suburban and rural areas.
O'Toole won re-election by at least nine percentage points in 2015, 2019 and 2021.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO

Document 202 of 356

GOLDSTEIN: The carbon tax is going up and so are emissions

Author: Goldstein, Lorrie

Publication info: Goldstein, Lorrie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/goldstein-carbon-tax-is-going-up-so-are-emissions/docview/2933942360/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=GOLDSTEIN%3A+The+carbon+tax+is+going+up+and+so+are+emissions&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Goldstein%2C+Lorrie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The futility of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's carbon tax - increasing by 23% on April 1 - was demonstrated yet again on Friday when the International Energy Agency announced global industrial greenhouse gas emissions reached a record high in 2023.
Emissions rose to 37.2 billion tonnes last year, an increase of 410 million tonnes, or 1.1%, from 36.8 billion tonnes in 2022, the previous high.
More significantly, this means the drop in global emissions in 2020 because of the world-wide recession caused by the first year of the pandemic - when emissions fell by 1.9 billion tonnes, or 5.2%, from 36.3 billion tonnes in 2019 to 34.4 billion tonnes in 2020 - was a mere blip on the radar.
Since then, emissions have increased every year, to 36.3 billion tonnes in 2021, 36.8 billion tonnes in 2022 and 37.2 billion tonnes in 2023.
They are now 8.1% higher than the were in 2020.
While the rate of annual increases in global emissions has slowed over the past decade, the United Nations' latest target to reduce emissions in 2030 by 43% compared to 2019 levels is a fantasy.
Global emissions in 2019 were 36.3 billion tonnes.
That means the UN target for 2030 is 20.7 billion tonnes of emissions, compared to 37.2 billion tonnes last year.
The idea the global economy could survive a 16.5 billion tonne cut in emissions in 2030 compared to 2023 is preposterous given the human suffering a recession of that magnitude needed to achieve it would cause.
Emissions in the first year of the pandemic, remember, fell only 1.9 billion tonnes.
Why is all this significant to Canada?
Because as parliamentary budget officer Yves Giroux has explained:
"Canada's own emissions (1.5% of the global total) are not large enough to materially impact climate change. Consequently, Canada's primary means of limiting the economic costs of climate change are through participation in a globally coordinated emissions reduction regime."
But emissions globally are going up and among the primary reasons are China, the world's largest emitter, where they rose by 565 million tonnes in 2023, and India, up 190 million tonnes.
Given the global increase in emissions of 410 million tonnes in 2023, China and India alone wiped out more than all of the reductions in the U.S. and Europe.
Canada's emissions in 2021 (the latest government data available) were 670 million tonnes, up 1.8% from 2020.
Trudeau's carbon tax will increase to $80 per tonne of emissions on April 1, up from $65 per tonne, on its way to $170 per tonne in 2030.
That will raise the price of gasoline by 17.6 cents per litre on April 1, up from 14.3 cents per litre, on its way to 37.4 cents per litre in 2030.
Wilkinson says no carbon rebates for Saskatchewan after province says it won't remit
TERRAZZANO: Feds should stop the gimmicks, scrap the carbon tax
LILLEY: Carbon tax clear loser when other Liberals won't defend it
The cost of natural gas, widely used for home heating in Canada, will increase by 15.25 cents per cubic metre, up from 12.39 cents per cubic metre, on its way to 32.4 cents per cubic metre in 2030.
It total, the cost of 22 forms of fossil fuel energy will increase on April 1. That's why the carbon tax raises the cost of almost everything, because fossil fuel energy is used to create almost all goods and services.
The Trudeau government claims 80% of households paying the carbon tax end up better off financially because of rebates.
But the PBO says when the negative impact of the carbon tax on the economy is factored in, 60% of households pay more in carbon taxes than they receive in rebates, rising to 80% in Nova Scotia in 2025, 80% in Ontario in 2026, 80% in Manitoba in 2029 and 80% in Alberta and P.E.I. in 2030.
Here are the PBO's estimated average net costs (after rebates) for households under the federal carbon tax system as of April 1, followed by the estimated net cost in 2030:
Alberta $911, $2,773; Ontario: $627, $1,820; Saskatchewan $525, $1,723; Manitoba $502, $1,490; Nova Scotia $537, $1,513; P.E.I $550, $1,521; Newfoundland and Labrador $377, $1,316.
(Quebec and B.C. have federally-approved provincial plans. New Brunswick is excluded because it joined the federal system after the PBO estimated costs. The federal carve-out on home heating oil will reduce costs in Atlantic Canada.)
The bottom line is Canada - where our emissions don't materially impact climate change - is relying on the UN plan to reduce global emissions, while they keep going up.
Given all this, the idea Canadians are going to experience less severe weather by paying Trudeau's carbon tax is absurd.

Subject: Costs; Fossil fuels; Emissions; Carbon; Decades; Pandemics; Environmental tax; Households; Climate change; Recessions

Business indexing term: Subject: Costs Environmental tax Recessions

Location: Manitoba Canada; Canada; India; China; Saskatchewan Canada; Nova Scotia Canada

Identifier / keyword: futility; prime; minister; justin; carbon; increasing

Title: GOLDSTEIN: The carbon tax is going up and so are emissions

Document 203 of 356

Mulroney a brave man with a bold vision

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mulroney-brave-man-with-bold-vision/docview/2933856517/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mulroney+a+brave+man+with+a+bold+vision&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Brian Mulroney was a courageous leader, unafraid to make tough decisions. He was a steadfast warrior on the front lines of human rights. And he was a visionary who did more than any other prime minister to make a real and lasting impact on the environment.
Mulroney died Thursday at age 84. Born to a large family in a Quebec steel town, Mulroney put himself through law school and joined the business world, but he never forgot his blue-collar roots.
First elected in a byelection in 1983, the following year he swept the Progressive Conservatives to their largest-ever majority government.
Mulroney negotiated this country's first free trade agreement with the U.S. and Mexico, over strenuous opposition from detractors. It created jobs and prosperity and formed the lasting strength of Canada's economy. Yes, he brought in the GST, an unpopular tax. Successive governments have silently thanked him for it. It was the right thing to do.
On the environment, he's best remembered for his visionary pact with the U.S. on acid rain. He also brought in the Environmental Protection Act and created eight new national parks.
He built close relationships with world leaders, especially U.S. presidents Ronald Reagan and George H.W. Bush.
And he was widely respected for standing up for human rights in South Africa and leading the push to free Nelson Mandela. Mulroney refused to be pushed around by former British prime minister Margaret Thatcher - the Iron Lady - at the 1987 Commonwealth Conference in Vancouver. He insisted the Commonwealth support sanctions against South Africa and its racist apartheid policies. Canada, he said, would be on the "right side of history" in ending apartheid.
Mandela never forgot Mulroney's principled stand. It's why Canada was the first country he visited after 27 years in prison.
Mulroney had setbacks, of course. All politicians do. They're well documented and now's not the time to rake over old foibles.
In his latter years, he became this country's elder statesman. Aspiring leaders sought his advice and mentorship.
We should remember Mulroney as a brave Canadian who changed this country for the better. We should celebrate him as a man who loved his country and did what was right, not necessarily what was popular.
May he rest in peace.

Subject: Apartheid; Environmental impact; Human rights; Prime ministers

Location: United States--US; South Africa; Canada

Identifier / keyword: brian; mulroney; courageous; leader; unafraid; tough

Title: Mulroney a brave man with a bold vision

EDITORIAL: Mulroney: A brave man with a bold vision

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/editorial-mulroney-brave-man-with-bold-vision/docview/2933820511/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=EDITORIAL%3A+Mulroney%3A+A+brave+man+with+a+bold+vision&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 205 of 356

Unsurpassed; Architect of federal Tories' '84 landslide dead at 84

Author: Riga, Andy

Publication info: Riga, Andy. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/unsurpassed-architect-federal-tories-84-landslide/docview/2933664915/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Unsurpassed%3B+Architect+of+federal+Tories%27+%2784+landslide+dead+at+84&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Riga%2C+Andy&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: MONTREAL - Brian Mulroney, whose tenure as prime minister was marked by momentous economic change and two tumultuous attempts to reform the Canadian Constitution, has died at age 84, his daughter Caroline Mulroney announced Thursday evening.
"He died peacefully, surrounded by family," Caroline Mulroney announced on X, formerly Twitter, just before 6 p.m. "We will share details of arrangements when they become available."
In his time in power from 1984 to 1993, Mulroney introduced the GST and negotiated free-trade agreements with the United States and Mexico. Contentious at the time, the GST and free trade are now mainstays of the Canadian economy.
But Mulroney, a Quebec-born anglophone embraced as family by francophone Quebecers, saw one of his biggest ambitions as PM and leader of the Progressive Conservative party - to convince his home province to endorse the Canadian Constitution - end in utter failure.
Instead of unifying the country, the efforts almost led to its destruction, fuelled by the defection of Lucien Bouchard, a minister in Mulroney's government and his onetime confidant, who almost split Quebec from the rest of Canada.
By the time he quit, Mulroney was deeply unpopular. In his aftermath, the Progressive Conservatives were left in tatters.
Born of modest means, Mulroney grew up in Baie-Comeau, Que., on the north shore of the St. Lawrence River, his father an electrician at the local paper mill, his mother a homemaker.
He took to politics naturally as a teen at St. Francis Xavier University in Nova Scotia, joining the Tory club and attending the convention that elected John Diefenbaker as party leader in 1956.
His sights already set on a political career, Mulroney went to Universite Laval in Quebec City for his law degree.
There, he connected with francophones and improved his French. Fellow student Bouchard, a bookish nationalist, became a close friend.
He took a bold stab at federal politics. Instead of starting as a backbencher, he aimed for the top, running for the Progressive Conservative leadership in 1976 against Joe Clark and Claude Wagner.
Clark won the day, but a few years later Mulroney would have his chance. Playing up his Quebec roots and business know-how, Mulroney forged a big-tent coalition, bringing together Quebec nationalists and conservatives of all stripes.
It worked like a charm. In 1984, Mulroney won one of the biggest landslides in Canadian history. His PCs took 211 of 282 seats in the House of Commons, including more than three-quarters of the seats in Quebec.
Mulroney has been praised for signing a pact with the U.S. on acid rain and for helping bring about the Montreal Protocol, a global agreement to phase out ozone-depleting substances. He also resisted calls to lift sanctions against South Africa over apartheid.
But his time in office was also marred by multiple scandals - 10 cabinet ministers left amid controversy - as well as criticism over patronage favours for Tory faithful.
Mulroney is survived by his wife Mila and his children Ben, Caroline, Mark and Nicolas.

Subject: Leadership; Francophones

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: United States--US; Canada; Quebec Canada
