Document 206 of 356

Reformer in prime, legend in own time; PM didn't just win big, he made the world better

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/reformer-prime-legend-own-time-pm-didnt-just-win/docview/2933664840/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reformer+in+prime%2C+legend+in+own+time%3B+PM+didn%27t+just+win+big%2C+he+made+the+world+better&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Brian Mulroney, Canada's 18th prime minister, who served from 1984 until 1993, died on Thursday. His daughter Caroline, a cabinet minister in the Ontario government of Doug Ford, made the announcement via social media.
"On behalf of my mother and our family, it is with great sadness we announce the passing of my father, the Right Honourable Brian Mulroney, Canada's 18th prime minister. He died peacefully, surrounded by family," she posted just before 6 p.m.
Mulroney was 84 and just three weeks away from his 85th birthday on March 20.
"He's a legend!" Mulroney's son Mark was fond of saying about his father, and he was right. This wasn't just a son looking up to his father, and anyone who has spoken to any of Mulroney's children has heard their deep admiration.
Mark's statement was on his dad's legacy, including winning back-to-back majorities for the Conservatives, something that hadn't been done since Sir. John A. Macdonald.
In 1984, Mulroney had barely been leader of the Progressive Conservatives for a year when he took on the Liberal juggernaut and won.
Mulroney and his team took 211 seats with just over 50% of the vote. No prime minister has won as many seats as Mulroney since.
On the policy front, this is the man who brought in a Canada-U.S. free trade deal that improved Canadian prosperity since it came in. He brought in the goods and services tax, replacing hidden taxes that could be manipulated, because he knew the GST was the right thing to do, even if unpopular.
If it weren't for the GST, Jean Chretien wouldn't have been able to balance the budgets the way he did after taking over. Mulroney's decision to implement the GST and to tackle problematic spending inside the federal government, including reducing the deficit from 8% of GDP to 5%, paved the way for Chretien to tackle the fiscal mess that had started under Pierre Trudeau.
Those on their own, all signature achievements, would mark a distinguished career, but Mulroney had more to offer.
He privatized Petro-Canada, Air Canada as well as de Havilland Air and Canadair. It's difficult for most Canadians to fathom the government owning a gas station, an airline and two aircraft manufacturers, but when Mulroney came to office, there were 61 Crown corporations. When he left there were just 38.
He tried, though ultimately failed, to find a way to get Quebec to sign the constitution. He first offered up the Meech Lake accord and then the Charlottetown accord.
Mulroney was about taking risks though, because the only way you can make great changes is by taking risks.
He won on free trade. He won on the GST. His risk on taming the finances paved the way for others - including Canadian taxpayers to win. As my colleague Warren Kinsella has beautifully written, Mulroney won on South Africa as well.
He led the charge against powerful world leaders including then-U.S. president Ronald Reagan and British PM Margaret Thatcher in opposing the apartheid regime in South Africa. Mulroney's push for economic sanctions and political isolation eventually won the day and true freedom for the people of that country.
Despite going against Reagan and Thatcher, Mulroney was well regarded by them and other world leaders and often sought out for advice after he left office. He was an invited speaker, and asked to give eulogies at the funerals of Reagan and his successor George H.W. Bush.
To sit and listen to Mulroney speak was a true treat. The man had a gift with words. It was like his entire body had kissed the Blarney Stone.
In 2022, I had the chance to listen to Mulroney speak at an event at a Toronto hotel ballroom. I'd covered the man before, but this was different.
Even at 82, he held the audience in the palm of his hand with his stories. He had a glint in his eye and a spring in his step and from my conversation with him, he was still paying close attention to politics.
Much has been made that he made nice statements about Justin Trudeau. Less known is that he had Pierre Poilievre over to his house for dinner and was thoroughly impressed.
Let the other outlets and writers use Mulroney's passing to go over his sins, something they will revel in. I come here to praise Mulroney, not to bury him. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Free trade; GST; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Free trade GST

Location: United States--US; South Africa; Canada

Document 207 of 356

Mulroney took big risks on big issues - and history would prove him right

Author: Kinsella, Warren

Publication info: Kinsella, Warren. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mulroney-took-big-risks-on-issues-history-would/docview/2933664819/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mulroney+took+big+risks+on+big+issues+-+and+history+would+prove+him+right&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kinsella%2C+Warren&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The biggest achievements in politics - the only achievements, really - are the ones involving risk, as in taking a risk, making a decision, making a statement, making a law that entails risk to you and your career.
Brian Mulroney took risks. I didn't work for him. In fact, I worked for Jean Chretien, his Liberal party opponent, and part of my job was to make the Mulroney government miserable.
Despite that - and when behind closed doors - Martin Brian Mulroney, PC, CC, GOQ, was a bit of a marvel to us because he took risks, because he had guts.
Case in point, South Africa. In the '80s, when Mulroney was prime minister - and presiding over two successive super-majorities - South Africa still practised apartheid. Apartheid was institutionalized racism, essentially. It was racial segregation and discrimination that had been forced on the black majority in South Africa by a white minority.
Ronald Reagan and Margaret Thatcher were Brian Mulroney's closest allies internationally. They professed to oppose apartheid, but they vociferously opposed international sanctions to bring it to an end. Thatcher called them "counterproductive."
Brian Mulroney stood up to Reagan and Thatcher, and many within his own Conservative party. In September 1986, Mulroney imposed tough sanctions on the apartheid regime and encouraged other nations to do likewise.
Said he: "I viewed apartheid with the same degree of disgust that I attached to the Nazis - the authors of the most odious offence in modern history."
Nelson Mandela thanked him for that, saying Mulroney and Canada would be forever remembered for their support.
Mulroney's other great and courageous achievement: free trade.
And, yes, we Liberals initially opposed it - or, at least, the John Turner-era Grits did (Chretien, as the country would soon see, not so much).
But Brian Mulroney saw where the world was heading - with technology ushering in an era of lightning-fast global commerce, dominated by companies all too willing to move to where they could do more business for less.
Mulroney's free trade stance was targeted by Turner during the 1988 federal election - and, for a while, it very nearly turned the tide against the Tory leader. He could have blinked, then, and backed away.
He could have reversed himself. He didn't. Mulroney persisted - and won another huge majority, and signed a comprehensive free trade deal with the United States.
He had a heart, too
There were other, less notable, parts to the man.
On the Hill, in the pre-Twitter days - when things were more civilized - all of us heard stories about Brian Mulroney's human side, like a gift of ties to Brian Tobin, his Liberal tormentor, when the MP's son was born; a call to Chretien during a health scare; quiet wishes whenever a Liberal was going through personal hardship. Not for publication, ever - but never forgotten by the recipients.
Brian Mulroney was not a great politician and prime minister because he won two big elections. He was one of the great ones because he took risks - because he took risks with things that mattered, the things that will be remembered by history.
My deepest condolences to his family, some of whom I now know and consider good friends.
Your dad was a great one. He will be missed.

Subject: Apartheid; Free trade; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Free trade

Location: South Africa

People: Mulroney, Brian

Identifier / keyword: biggest; achievements; politics; really; involving; taking

'ULTIMATE STATESMAN': Mulroney's legacy saluted as politicians mourn his death

Author: Jordan Ercit

Publication info: Jordan Ercit. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ultimate-statesman-mulroneys-legacy-saluted-as/docview/2933620418/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27ULTIMATE+STATESMAN%27%3A+Mulroney%27s+legacy+saluted+as+politicians+mourn+his+death&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Jordan+Ercit&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tributes are pouring in across the political spectrum and beyond in honour of the late Brian Mulroney.
The former prime minister, who steered the Progressive Conservatives to the biggest federal majority government in Canadian history as part of back-to-back mandates, died "peacefully, surrounded by family" on Thursday at the age of 84, his daughter Caroline Mulroney said on the social-media site X.
3/3
We will share details of arrangements when they become available.
Nous partagerons les détails des arrangements dès qu'ils seront disponibles.
- Caroline Mulroney (@C_Mulroney) February 29, 2024
"On behalf of my mother (Mila) and our family, it is with great sadness we announce the passing of my father, the Right Honourable Brian Mulroney, Canada's 18th prime minister," said Caroline Mulroney, who followed her father into politics and currently serves as Treasury Board president and francophone affairs minister in Doug Ford's Ontario PC government.
Former PM Mulroney, architect of federal Tories' landslide 1984 victory, dead at 84
KINSELLA: Brian Mulroney took risks on issues that mattered
LILLEY: Brian Mulroney's distinguished career bettered not just Canada but the world
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre offered his condolences to the Mulroney family, while honouring the "transformational change" the former prime minister ushered in during his time in office from 1984 to 1993.
"It is with great sadness that Canadians learn of the loss of one of our greatest ever statesmen, the Right Honourable Brian Mulroney. The love and prayers of all Canadians go to his beloved children, Caroline, Ben, Mark and Nicolas, and the love of his life, his best friend and...
- Pierre Poilievre (@PierrePoilievre) February 29, 2024
Poilievre said Brian Mulroney helped working-class families by unleashing free enterprise, crushing inflation, restoring fiscal sanity and concluding "one of the greatest free-trade agreements the world has ever seen," while standing "firmly" with western allies in the fight for freedom.
"He was among the first and most strident to fight against South Africa's apartheid policy and champion the cause of Nelson Mandela," Poilievre said in a statement.
He later went on to thank Mulroney for his essential role in ending this racist policy.
"I will always be thankful for his candid advice and generous mentorship to me personally. All Canadians are grateful for his immense sacrifice and the lasting legacy he leaves us all."
Brian Mulroney loved Canada. I'm devastated to learn of his passing.
He never stopped working for Canadians, and he always sought to make this country an even better place to call home. I'll never forget the insights he shared with me over the years - he was generous, tireless,...
- Justin Trudeau (@JustinTrudeau) February 29, 2024
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said he was "devastated" to learn of Mulroney's death, saying he "never stopped working for Canadians and he always sought to make this country an even better place to call home.
"As we mourn his passing and keep his family and friends in our thoughts, let us also acknowledge - and celebrate - Mr. Mulroney's role in building the modern, dynamic and prosperous country we all know today," Trudeau said.

Former Conservative prime minister Stephen Harper called Mulroney's premiership "transformational" with Canada benefitting "immensely" from the free-trade agreement and his championing of the Montreal Protocol and Canada-U.S. Acid Rain Treaty.
On behalf of our family, Laureen, Ben, Rachel, and I offer our deepest sympathies to Mila, Caroline, Ben, Mark, Nicholas, and their families.
History will record that Mr. Mulroney's premiership was transformational.
Prime Minister Mulroney fought to secure the Free Trade...
- Stephen Harper (@stephenharper) March 1, 2024
"Prime minister Mulroney stood for freedom and democracy on the world stage, in his principled opposition to apartheid in South Africa, his enduring support for Israel and his advocacy of independence for Ukraine and the other European nations long under the yoke of Soviet communism," Harper said on X.
"Canada has lost a historic figure and we mourn his passing with all his many friends and associates."
Joe Clark, Canada's 16th prime minister, said his former PC party rival was "bold, a big dreamer and an unparalleled negotiator and persuader" with an "extraordinary capacity to reconcile.
My statement on the passing of The Rt. Honourable Brian Mulroney.
- Rt. Hon. Joe Clark (@RtHonJoeClark) March 1, 2024
"Brian and I met decades ago as young partisans from small towns in distant parts of a big country, drawn by politics and public service," Clark said in a statement. "Often then we were allies, sometimes competitors and then became colleagues in and beyond national government. I am grateful for his extraordinary service and his friendship and mourn his loss."
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh said he was "saddened" to hear of Mulroney's passing while also lauding his environmental policies at the federal level.
He was also a strong opponent of Apartheid, leading the efforts to sanction South Africa by Commonwealth nations.
My deep sympathy to his family.
- Jagmeet Singh (@theJagmeetSingh) February 29, 2024
"He made an important contribution to Canada, including protecting our environment - leading the world in tackling acid rain and banning chemicals that were destroying the ozone layer," Singh said on X.
Bloc Quebecois Leader Yves-Francois Blanchet called Mulroney a "north coaster and Quebecer" who was perhaps the last prime minister to have "sincerely attempted to reconcile Quebec and Canada.
Le premier ministre Mulroney est peut-être le dernier Ã avoir tenté de réconcilier avec sincérité le Québec et le Canada. Il a été le maître-d'ouvre d'un traité de libre-échange qui n'aurait pas existé sans l'appui du Québec. Son rôle dans le respect des droits humains et la...
- Yves-F. Blanchet ???? (@yfblanchet) February 29, 2024
"His role in respecting human rights and the release of Nelson Mandela is historic," Blanchet said on X in French. "He even had the trust of (sovereigntist Quebec premier) Rene Levesque."
Premier Doug Ford said Mulroney was his role model as well as Canada's "ultimate statesman" who left behind an "unmatched legacy" in terms of free trade and helping end apartheid in South Africa.
Tonight, Canada lost its ultimate statesman.
Rest in peace, my friend. Our country will miss you.
- Doug Ford (@fordnation) February 29, 2024
"The man had a way with words like no one else," Ford said in a statement. "It didn't matter if it was a private conversation or a speech to thousands, Brian had an ability to capture a crowd and keep them captivated with his endless supply of stories. Anyone who heard him speak walked away in awe."
Governor General Mary Simon said she will remember Mulroney for his "determination in support of Indigenous rights during the constitutional negotiations in the 1980s and the Charlottetown Accord.
#GGSimon shares the following message with Canadians regarding the passing of the Right Honourable Brian Mulroney.
- Governor General of Canada (@GGCanada) March 1, 2024
"Foremost, Whit and I would like to extend our condolences to his wife Mila and to his children Caroline, Ben, Mark and Nicolas," Simon said in a statement. "We hope you will find comfort in knowing that our thoughts and those of countless Canadians are with you during this difficult time."
Bob Rae, Canada's UN ambassador and a former Ontario Liberal MP, said Mulroney was the first political leader to call when his brother David died in 1989, when he was elected as Ontario's premier in 1990, when his NDP government was defeated in 1995 and "so many other moments.
When my brother died, Brian Mulroney was the first political leader to call, as he was when I was elected Premier in 1990, when I was defeated, and at so many other moments. He was my friend, despite our differences. Love from me and Arlene @aprae to Mila and the family. Amitiés...
- Bob Rae (@BobRae48) March 1, 2024
"He was my friend, despite our differences," Rae said on X.
Even former Toronto Sun baseball writer Bob Elliott shared a heartwarming story about Mulroney, who once heard Montreal sports talk show host Ted Tevan sign off early one morning by saying, "Who is out there? Who is listening?
"The next day former prime minister Brian Mulroney called him at home to say, 'I'm listening Ted.' R. I. P. Brian Mulroney," Elliott posted on X

Document 209 of 356

Mulroney's legacy saluted as politicians mourn former PM's death

Author: Jordan Ercit

Publication info: Jordan Ercit. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mulroneys-legacy-saluted-as-politicians-mourn/docview/2933619611/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mulroney%27s+legacy+saluted+as+politicians+mourn+former+PM%27s+death&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Jordan+Ercit&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 210 of 356

LILLEY: Brian Mulroney's distinguished career bettered not just Canada but the world

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-brian-mulroneys-distinguished-career/docview/2933615351/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Brian+Mulroney%27s+distinguished+career+bettered+not+just+Canada+but+the+world&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Brian Mulroney, Canada's 18th prime minister who served from 1984 until 1993, died on Thursday.
His daughter Caroline, a cabinet minister in the Ontario government of Doug Ford, made the announcement via social media.
"On behalf of my mother and our family, it is with great sadness we announce the passing of my father, The Right Honourable Brian Mulroney, Canada's 18th Prime Minister. He died peacefully, surrounded by family," she posted just before 6 p.m.
1/3
On behalf of my mother and our family, it is with great sadness we announce the passing of my father, The Right Honourable Brian Mulroney, Canada's 18th Prime Minister. He died peacefully, surrounded by family.
- Caroline Mulroney (@C_Mulroney) February 29, 2024
Mulroney was 84 and just three weeks away from his 85th birthday on March 20.
"He's a legend!" Mulroney's son Mark was fond of saying about his father, and he was right. This wasn't just a son looking up to his father, and anyone who has spoken to any of Mulroney's children has heard their deep admiration.
Mark's statement was on his dad's legacy, including winning two back-to-back majorities for the Conservatives, something that hadn't been done since Sir. John A. Macdonald. In 1984, Mulroney had barely been leader of the Progressive Conservatives for a year when he took on the Liberal juggernaut and won.
Mulroney and his team took 211 seats with just over 50% of the vote. No prime minister has won as many seats as Mulroney since.
On the policy front, this is the man who brought in the Canada-U.S. free trade deal that has improved Canadian prosperity since it came in. He brought in the Goods and Services Tax, replacing hidden taxes that could be manipulated, because he knew the GST was the right thing to do, even if unpopular.
If it weren't for the GST, Jean Chretien wouldn't have been able to balance the budgets the way he did after taking over. Mulroney's decision to implement the GST and to tackle problematic spending inside the federal government, including reducing the deficit from 8% of GDP to 5%, paved the way for Chretien to tackle the fiscal mess that had started under Pierre Trudeau.
Former PM Mulroney, architect of federal Tory's landslide 1984 victory, dead at 84
KINSELLA: Brian Mulroney took risks on issues that mattered
Those achievements on their own, all signature achievements, would mark a distinguished career but Mulroney had more to offer.
He privatized Petro-Canada, Air Canada as well as de Haviland Air and Canadair. It's difficult for most Canadians to fathom the government owning a gas station, an airline and two aircraft manufacturers but when Mulroney came to office, there were 61 Crown Corporations. When he left there were just 38.
He tried, though ultimately failed, to find a way to get Quebec to sign the constitution. He first offered up the Meech Lake Accord and then the Charlottetown Accord.
Mulroney was about taking risks though, because the only way you can make great changes is by taking risks.
He won on free trade, he won on the GST, his risk on taming the finances paved the way for others - including Canadian taxpayers to win. As my colleague Warren Kinsella has beautifully written, Mulroney won on South Africa as well.
He led the charge against powerful world leaders including then American President Ronald Reagan and British P.M. Margaret Thatcher in opposing the apartheid regime in South Africa. Mulroney's push for economic sanction and political isolation eventually won the day and true freedom for the people of that country.
Despite going against Reagan and Thatcher, Mulroney was well-regarded by them and other world leaders and often sought out for advice after he left office. He was an invited speaker, asked to give eulogies at the funerals of Reagan and his successor George H.W. Bush.
To sit and listen to Mulroney speak was a true treat. The man had a gift with words. It was like his entire body had kissed the Blarney Stone.
In 2022, I had the chance to listen to Mulroney speak at an event at a Toronto hotel ballroom. I'd covered the man before, but this was different.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
Even at 82, he held the audience in the palm of his hand with his stories. He had a glint in his eye and a spring in his step and from my conversation with him, he was still paying close attention to politics.
Much has been made that he made nice statements about Justin Trudeau, less known is that he had Pierre Poilievre over to his house for dinner and was thoroughly impressed.
Let the other outlets and writers use Mulroney's passing to go over his sins, something they will revel in.
I come here to praise Mulroney, not to bury him.
To his wife Mila, his children Caroline, Ben, Mark and Nicholas, I offer my condolences.
May the road rise to meet the legend.
blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Free trade; GST; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Free trade GST

Location: South Africa; Canada

People: Mulroney, Brian

Identifier / keyword: brian; mulroney; prime; minister; served; until

Title: LILLEY: Brian Mulroney's distinguished career bettered not just Canada but the world

Section: Columnists

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Country of publication: Canada

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 2933615351

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-brian-mulroneys-distinguished-career/docview/2933615351/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 211 of 356

KINSELLA: Brian Mulroney took risks on issues that mattered

Author: Doucette, Chris

Publication info: Doucette, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/kinsella-brian-mulroney-took-risks-on-issues-that/docview/2933608661/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=KINSELLA%3A+Brian+Mulroney+took+risks+on+issues+that+mattered&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Doucette%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The biggest achievements in politics - the only achievements, really - are the ones involving risk.
As in, taking a risk. Making a decision, making a statement, making a law that entails risk to you and your career.
Brian Mulroney took risks.
I didn't work for him. In fact, I worked for Jean Chretien, his Liberal Party opponent. And part of my job was to make the Mulroney government miserable.
Despite that - and when behind closed doors - Martin Brian Mulroney, PC, CC, GOQ, was a bit of a marvel to us. Because he took risks. Because he had guts.
Case in point: South Africa.
In the Eighties, when Mulroney was prime minister - and presiding over two successive super-majorities - South Africa still practiced apartheid. Apartheid was institutionalized racism, essentially. It was racial segregation and discrimination that had been forced on the black majority in South Africa by a white minority.
Like father, like daughter? Brian Mulroney says parliamentary experience not necessary
SNOBELEN: Brian Mulroney left legacy of world-leading environmental initiatives
Donald Trump wrong on NAFTA deficit: Brian Mulroney
Ronald Reagan and Margaret Thatcher were Brian Mulroney's closest allies internationally. They professed to oppose apartheid - but they vociferously opposed international sanctions to bring it to an end. Thatcher called them "counterproductive."
Brian Mulroney stood up to Reagan and Thatcher - and many within his own Conservative Party. In September 1986, Mulroney imposed tough sanctions on the apartheid regime, and encouraged other nations to do likewise.
Said he: "I viewed apartheid with the same degree of disgust that I attached to the Nazis - the authors of the most odious offence in modern history."
Nelson Mandela thanked him for that, saying Mulroney, and Canada, would be forever remembered for their support.
Mulroney's other great and courageous achievement: free trade.
And, yes, we Liberals initially opposed it - or, at least, the John Turner-era Grits did (Chretien, as the country would soon see, not so much).
But Brian Mulroney saw where the world was heading - with technology ushering in an era of lightning-fast global commerce, dominated by companies all too willing to move to where they could do more business for less.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
Mulroney's free trade stance was targeted by Turner during the 1988 federal election - and, for a while, it very nearly turned the tide against the Tory leader. He could have blinked, then, and backed away. He could have reversed himself. He didn't. Mulroney persisted - and won another huge majority, and signed a comprehensive free trade deal with the United States.
There were other, less notable, parts to the man. On the Hill, in the pre-Twitter days - when things were more civilized - all of us heard stories about Brian Mulroney's human side. A gift of ties to Brian Tobin, his Liberal tormentor, when the MP's son was born.
A call to Chretien during a health scare. Quiet wishes whenever a Liberal was going through personal hardship. Not for publication, ever. But never forgotten by the recipients.
Brian Mulroney was not a great politician and prime minister because he won two big elections. He was one of the great ones because he took risks - because he took risks with things that mattered, the things that will be remembered by history.
My deepest condolences to his family, some of whom I now know and consider good friends.
Your Dad was a great one. He will be missed.

Subject: Apartheid; Free trade; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Free trade

Document 212 of 356

Former PM Mulroney, architect of federal Tory's landslide 1984 victory, dead at 84

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/former-pm-mulroney-architect-federal-torys/docview/2933611326/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+PM+Mulroney%2C+architect+of+federal+Tory%27s+landslide+1984+victory%2C+dead+at+84&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: MONTREAL - Brian Mulroney, whose tenure as prime minister was marked by momentous economic change and two tumultuous attempts to reform the Canadian Constitution, has died at age 84, his daughter Caroline Mulroney announced Thursday evening.
"He died peacefully, surrounded by family," Caroline Mulroney announced on X, formerly Twitter, just before 6 p.m. "We will share details of arrangements when they become available."
1/3
On behalf of my mother and our family, it is with great sadness we announce the passing of my father, The Right Honourable Brian Mulroney, Canada's 18th Prime Minister. He died peacefully, surrounded by family.
- Caroline Mulroney (@C_Mulroney) February 29, 2024
3/3
We will share details of arrangements when they become available.
Nous partagerons les détails des arrangements dès qu'ils seront disponibles.
- Caroline Mulroney (@C_Mulroney) February 29, 2024
During his time in power from 1984 to 1993, Mulroney introduced the GST and negotiated free-trade agreements with the United States and Mexico. Contentious at the time, the GST and free trade are now mainstays of the Canadian economy.
But Mulroney, a Quebec-born anglophone embraced as family by francophone Quebecers, saw one of his biggest ambitions as PM and leader of the Progressive Conservative party - to finally convince his home province to endorse the Canadian Constitution - end in utter failure.
Instead of unifying the country, the efforts almost led to its destruction, fuelled by the defection of Lucien Bouchard, a minister in Mulroney's government and his one-time confidant who almost split Quebec from the rest of Canada.
By the time he quit, Mulroney was deeply unpopular. In his aftermath, the Progressive Conservatives were left in tatters.
Brian and Mila Mulroney at a rally in 1986.
Born of modest means, Mulroney grew up in Baie-Comeau, Que., on the north shore of the St. Lawrence River, his father an electrician at the local paper mill, his mother a homemaker.
Outspoken and self-assured from a young age, he took to politics naturally as a teenager at St. Francis Xavier University in Nova Scotia, joining the Tory club and attending the convention that elected John Diefenbaker as party leader in 1956.
His sights already set on a political career, Mulroney went to Universite Laval in Quebec City for his law degree.
There, he connected with francophones and improved his French, which was faltering after years away from Quebec. Fellow student Bouchard, a bookish nationalist, became a close friend.It was the early 1960s and young Mulroney had a front-row seat to the ferment of the Quiet Revolution, a period when francophones started to take the levers of power in Quebec society.
At Laval, "he made himself into a truly bicultural Canadian, equally at home in French and English, equally at ease in French-Canadian and English-Canadian society," John Sawatsky wrote in his biography of Mulroney The Politics of Ambition.
Former PM Brian Mulroney recovering after prostate cancer treatment
SNOBELEN: Brian Mulroney left legacy of world-leading environmental initiatives
"Mulroney's cultural transformation was much more than a linguistic achievement. During his years at Laval, he watched the Quiet Revolution unfold and changed his outlook on Confederation. He now thought like a Quebecer. Although he still valued a strong central government with no special status for any province, Quebec's priorities had become his priorities."
Mulroney went on to become one of Quebec's top labour lawyers, helping bring labour peace to the Port of Montreal and an end to a long, bitter strike at La Presse.
But Mulroney's first taste of exposure to the general public came in 1974 when Premier Robert Bourassa asked him to serve as management representative on a commission of inquiry into violence and corruption in the construction industry. Bouchard was the commission's lawyer. It was chaired by Robert Cliche, under whom Mulroney and Bouchard had studied at Laval.
The Cliche Commission, with its sordid tales of rampant criminal activity in construction unions, made front-page news in the mid-1970s. It put the public spotlight on Mulroney. Now cast as a crime buster, he had a strong sense of media relations and ensured headline material came out in time for deadlines.
Tapping his new fame, he took a bold stab at federal politics. Instead of starting as a backbencher, he wanted to go straight to the top, running for the Progressive Conservative leadership in 1976 against Joe Clark and Claude Wagner.
Brian Mulroney attends the Progressive Conservative party convention in February 1976.
Despite Mulroney's strong showing in the first round of voting, Clark won the day.
In the ensuing years, Mulroney would bide his time as a top executive at the Iron Ore Company of Canada.
Behind the scenes, he was plotting against Clark, a wobbly leader whose minority government fell on a non-confidence motion after less than nine months in office in 1979.
Four years later, Mulroney defeated Clark to win the leadership.
Playing up his Quebec roots and business know-how, Mulroney forged a big-tent coalition, bringing together Quebec nationalists and conservatives of all stripes - fiscal, social and progressive.
It worked like a charm.
In 1984, Mulroney won one of the biggest landslides in Canadian history. His PCs took 211 of 282 seats in the House of Commons, including more than three-quarters of the seats in Quebec.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
Lucien Bouchard, who had worked on Mulroney's 1976 leadership run and later for the Yes side in the 1980 Quebec sovereignty referendum, helped devise Mulroney's Quebec strategy for the 1984 federal election.
He also wrote some of Mulroney's key speeches, including a seminal one in which the future PM promised to bring Quebec back into the constitutional fold "with honour and enthusiasm."
Quebec was left out in the cold in 1982 when the Constitution was repatriated; it was the only province not to endorse a constitutional plan negotiated by prime minister Pierre Trudeau and premiers of the nine other provinces. Premier Rene Levesque called it a betrayal, accusing other provinces and Trudeau of stabbing Quebec in the back.
Giving federalism a try, Bouchard would become Mulroney's Quebec lieutenant and environment minister.
Brian Mulroney, Lucien Bouchard and Mila Mulroney, in Chicoutimi, Que., on June 12, 1988, in the run-up to the Lac St-Jean federal by-election in which Bouchard was elected.
The ascension of Mulroney, who knew key Parti Quebecois figures well from his days at Laval and his time as a lawyer, changed the dynamic in Quebec, according to political journalist Graham Fraser.
"For the first time in their history, the Conservatives had chosen a Quebecer," Fraser wrote in P.Q.: Rene Levesque and the Parti Quebecois in Power.
"More than that, they had chosen an old-fashioned politician and a skillful conciliator. But, most important for the future shape of Canadian politics, they had chosen the first English-speaking Quebec politician acknowledged as a Quebecer by French-speaking Quebecers."
Capitalizing on that goodwill, Mulroney took it upon himself to heal the wounds created in Quebec by the 1982 repatriation.
After months of discreet talks with the provinces, he convened premiers in 1987. Ever the skilled negotiator, he managed to reach a deal to the surprise of many observers.
Under the Meech Lake Accord, Quebec would be recognized as a "distinct society." Every province would have a say in appointments to the Senate and the Supreme Court of Canada. Quebec would effectively have a veto over major constitutional changes. All provinces would play a greater role in immigration.
Quebec, under Bourassa, ratified the deal in June 1987, with other governments required to endorse it within three years.
But political circumstances and provincial governments changed. Newly elected premiers began to have reservations about the deal their predecessors had signed, concerned it would weaken Ottawa and give Quebec special status.
The accord died in June 1990 after Manitoba and Newfoundland failed to ratify it.
A June 3, 1987 photograph of, from left, Ontario Premier David Peterson, Prime Minister Brian Mulroney and Quebec Premier Robert Bourassa at the Constitutional Conference where the Meech Lake Accord was agreed to.
By then, the Mulroney-Bouchard friendship was also dead.
Bouchard had already quit the Progressive Conservative government. In trying to rescue the deal, Mulroney was "making an alliance with those who want Quebec to continue to be humiliated," Bouchard said.
Bouchard then founded the separatist Bloc Quebecois, which remains a powerful force in Quebec decades later.
Mulroney felt so betrayed by his old friend Bouchard that he privately declared that if he died and Bouchard turned up at his funeral, the service was to be stopped until Bouchard left the church, the Montreal Gazette reported in 2000.
Two years after Meech collapsed, Mulroney's second bid for a constitutional deal - the Charlottetown Accord - also ended badly.
An October 1992 referendum failed to win popular approval for the so-called Charlottetown Accord, with a set of constitutional amendments that included recognizing Quebec as a distinct society. Nationally, it was rejected by 54% of voters. In Quebec, which held its own referendum, 57% of voters said No.
Despite his constitutional failures, Mulroney remained well-respected in Quebec among politicians of all stripes.
In 2002, he became the first Canadian prime minister invested to the National Order of Quebec. At the ceremony, Parti Quebecois Premier Bernard Landry praised Mulroney's constitutional efforts.
"I often hear harsh criticism of Mulroney. That's ingratitude," Landry said. "Charlottetown and Meech were noble attempts. ... But at that moment, it was the ultimate attempt that showed that Canada was not reformable."
Mulroney has been praised for signing a pact with the United States on acid rain and for helping bring about the Montreal Protocol, a global agreement to phase out ozone-depleting substances. He also resisted calls to lift sanctions against South Africa over apartheid.
But his time in office was also marred by multiple scandals - 10 cabinet ministers left amid controversy - as well as criticism over patronage favours for Tory faithful.
Prime Minister Brian Mulroney introduces U.S. President Ronald Reagan during a ceremony at the Quebec City airport for what became known as the "Shamrock Summit" in March 1985.
Two years out of office, Mulroney was thrust back into the spotlight in what became known as the "Airbus affair."
He was accused in a government document sent to Swiss authorities of taking illicit payments for steering an Air Canada contract to the French airplane manufacturer, a charge the government couldn't back up when Mulroney sued and won a $2-million settlement in 1997.
A decade later, Mulroney testified at a public inquiry into the affair, admitting he had accepted $225,000 in cash from lobbyist Karl-Heinz Schreiber and calling it "a serious error in judgment." He denied any wrongdoing, including tax evasion or accepting kickbacks. Schreiber contended he had given Mulroney $300,000.
In his final report, inquiry commissioner Justice Jeffrey Oliphant found it had been "inappropriate" for Mulroney to have accepted "cash-stuffed envelopes" from Schreiber, to keep no records, and to disclose the payments to nobody, not even the taxman, until years later. He also slammed Mulroney for not being forthright in testimony he had given during a pre-trial hearing of his libel case about his business relationship with Schreiber.
Mulroney left politics in 1993 after two mandates, the first Tory PM to clinch back-to-back majorities since John A. Macdonald.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
But, by the end, the bloom was off the rose. Disgruntled Canadians had had enough. They were angry over the GST and free trade. And they had had it up to here with constitutional kerfuffles.
There was some solace for Mulroney. His successor, Liberal Jean Chretien, may have railed against free trade and the GST while campaigning but he kept both in place after he took office. The GST is credited with helping Chretien's government tame the federal deficit.
The first post-Mulroney election was devastating for the Progressive Conservatives. The party won only two seats.
Mulroney's coalition had fallen apart. Disgruntled Westerners saw him as just another Quebecer favouring his home province, and they hightailed it to the more conservative Reform Party. Many disillusioned nationalist Quebecers found a home with Bouchard's Bloc.
Despite Mulroney's tireless effort to get a deal done, Meech and Charlottetown cemented the view in Quebec that the rest of Canada was inflexible, setting the stage for Quebec's second referendum in 1995.
It was Mulroney's former college pal who stole the show for the Yes side.
Thanks to Bouchard's fiery speeches and relentless campaigning, sovereignists shot up in the polls as voting day drew near. In the end, Canada barely survived. The No side's margin of victory: 54,288 out of 4.7 million votes.
In 2006, the House of Commons recognized that "the Quebecois form a nation within a united Canada" in a surprise motion proposed by Conservative Prime Minister Stephen Harper.
But Quebec has still not signed the Constitution. And, stung by Meech and Charlottetown, politicians across the country - once eager to discuss changes to Canada's Constitution - remain allergic to formal constitutional talks.
Brian Mulroney and Newfoundland Premier Frank Moores at the 1976 Progressive Conservative leadership convention.
After quitting politics, Mulroney worked as a lawyer and sat on corporate boards, including that of Quebecor, the publishing and cable empire controlled by former Parti Quebecois leader Pierre Karl Peladeau.
Mulroney's party eventually dropped "progressive" from its name. In 2003, it merged with the Canadian Alliance, formerly the Reform Party.
Mulroney backed the creation of the new Conservative Party of Canada, saying a new national conservative coalition was needed to defeat the Liberals.
In September 2023, Mulroney praised the party's leader, Pierre Poilievre, saying a speech he gave at a party convention was "probably the best convention speech I have ever witnessed."
But the former PM wasn't shy about also lauding Liberal Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. In a speech in June 2023, Mulroney effusively defended Trudeau.
He said Trudeau would be remembered for his leadership on the COVID-19 pandemic and free trade and its support for Ukraine in its war with Russia.
"I have learned over the years that history is unconcerned with the trivia and the trash of rumours and gossip floating around Parliament Hill," Mulroney said. "History is only concerned with the big ticket items that have shaped the future of Canada."
Mulroney is survived by his wife, Mila, and his children, Ben, Caroline, Mark and Nicolas.
With files from Hubert Bauch, Irwin Block and Elizabeth Thompson.

Document 213 of 356

Pharmacare plan short on detail, big on cost

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pharmacare-plan-short-on-detail-big-cost/docview/2933290285/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pharmacare+plan+short+on+detail%2C+big+on+cost&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Before we fire up the confetti cannon to celebrate the federal plan for Pharmacare, we need to see some details - and a price tag.
The Alberta government has said it will opt out of the drug plan and wants the federal government to give the province its share of the money with no strings attached.
Quebec has also indicated it will opt out. Ontario wants to see more details before it endorses the plan.
Pharmacare is part of the costly supplyand-confidence agreement between New Democratic Party Leader Jagmeet Singh and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to keep the minority Liberal government afloat.
The plan is problematic on several fronts. It's not up to the federal government to tell provinces how to deliver health care. Its only obligation is to provide funding. By imposing their will on the provinces, the feds are once again challenging the underpinning of Confederation. They should stay in their lane.
Alberta Health Minister Adriana LaGrange told a news conference this week the province already has a better drug plan than the one Ottawa is proposing, including diabetes medications the feds plan to cover.
"I don't think the federal government understands that provinces do have processes in place. They're looking to provide something different, which adds bureaucracy and administrative burden on top of what we already have," she said.
In Ontario, Health Minister Sylvia Jones told the Legislature she hasn't seen details of the plan. "I've had no conversations, no feedback, no memos, nothing directly from the federal minister," she said. "We're waiting for that so I know exactly what we are looking at in terms of our responsibilities and the federal responsibilities."
Another troubling aspect to this is the cost. The parliamentary budget officer has estimated the incremental cost to federal and provincial governments combined to be $11.2 billion in 2024, increasing to $13.4 billion in 2027-28.
Russia and China are flexing their muscles.
There are wars in Gaza and Ukraine. Canada should be beefing up its military. Our NATO allies are expressing concern that, at 1.3% of the Gross Domestic Product, our contribution to the alliance lags woefully behind the 2% required of members. We're shortchanging our military at a time when we need it the most.
Pharmacare is a nice-to-have luxury.
Defence is a must-have essential.

Full text: Before we fire up the confetti cannon to celebrate the federal plan for Pharmacare, we need to see some details - and a price tag.
The Alberta government has said it will opt out of the drug plan and wants the federal government to give the province its share of the money with no strings attached. Quebec has also indicated it will opt out.
Ontario wants to see more details before it endorses the plan.
Pharmacare is part of the costly supply and confidence agreement between New Democratic Party Leader Jagmeet Singh and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to keep the minority Liberal government afloat.
The plan is problematic on several fronts. It's not up to the federal government to tell provinces how to deliver health care. Its only obligation is to provide funding. By imposing their will on the provinces, the feds are once again challenging the underpinning of Confederation. They should stay in their lane.
Alberta's Health Minister Adriana LaGrange told a news conference this week the province already has a better drug plan than the one Ottawa is proposing, including diabetes medications the feds plan to cover.
"I don't think the federal government understands that provinces do have processes in place. They're looking to provide something different, which adds bureaucracy and administrative burden on top of what we already have," she said.
In Ontario, Health Minister Sylvia Jones told the legislature she hasn't seen details of the plan. "I've had no conversations, no feedback, no memos, nothing directly from the federal minister," she said. "We're waiting for that so I know exactly what we are looking at in terms of our responsibilities and the federal responsibilities."

The Parliamentary Budget Officer has estimated the incremental cost to federal and provincial governments combined to be $11.2 billion in 2024, increasing to $13.4 billion in 2027-2028.
Russia and China are flexing their muscles. There are wars in Gaza and Ukraine. Canada should be beefing up its military. Our NATO allies are expressing concern that, at 1.3% of the Gross Domestic Product, our contribution to the alliance lags woefully behind the 2% required of members. We're shortchanging our military at a time when we need them the most.
Pharmacare is a nice-to-have luxury. Defence is a must-have essential.
Freeland pledges pharmacare deal won't jeopardize federal finances

Document 215 of 356

Zero-chill freeze; Amazingly, opposition both agitated by and supportive of PCs'tuition move

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/zero-chill-freeze-amazingly-opposition-both/docview/2932311641/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Zero-chill+freeze%3B+Amazingly%2C+opposition+both+agitated+by+and+supportive+of+PCs%27tuition+move&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The good news for parents and students: Tuition at Ontario's colleges and universities won't be going up for the next three years. The bad news for the province's universities and colleges is that they won't be getting as much money as they hoped for.
Minister Jill Dunlop stood before reporters at Queen's Park to announce no fewer than 14 times that the Ford government was investing $1.3 billion in new funding into the postsecondary education sector.
Questioned time and again about other issues, Dunlop repeated that the government was investing $1.3 billion. It was as if she thought the journalists present hadn't heard her many pronouncements on the matter previously.
The news of the investment comes after a blue-ribbon panel appointed by the Ford government suggested last November that tuition should go up, and urged the province to increase funding by $2.5 billion over three years.
Instead, the province is offering no tuition increase and just the $1.3 billion that Dunlop mentioned over and over again.
The opposition parties were quick to denounce the move as not enough.
"What has been promised today by the government comes nowhere close to what the blueribbon panel recommended was needed in our system," NDP critic Peggy Sattler said.
Yet, while denouncing the government for not following the blue-ribbon panel, Sattler was quick to endorse the tuition freeze.
"We absolutely support a tuition freeze. Students are suffering from the cost-of-living crisis the same way that everyone else in Ontario is," she said.
Liberal critic Adil Shamji made similar complaints, that the government didn't listen to its panel on funding, while also saying he supports the tuition freeze that the blue-ribbon panel opposed. That panel had in fact called for a 5% tuition hike starting this September, followed by automatic tuition hikes of 2% a year after.
That wouldn't help anyone deal with affordability issues.
"I will not apologize for continuing to freeze tuition in this province," Dunlop said. "Let me take you back to 2019, when Ontario had the highest tuition of all in Canada."
Now, three provinces have higher university tuition compared with Ontario, and seven provinces have higher college tuition.
Governing at any level is about choices, and Premier Doug Ford has decided that raising tuition rates on families right now isn't the best option. Could he have given the universities and colleges more money? Obviously, and Sattler made a solid argument that if the province is going to freeze tuition, they need to offer more subsidies.
But here we are, back to choices, and is giving colleges and universities ever-increasing amounts of money a good thing? It is also worth asking whether we need to keep every single university and college in this province, since some of them seem to be having trouble. According to the Globe and Mail, in 2022-23, seven of Ontario's 24 colleges had more international students, paying higher fees, than they had domestic students.
That included Sarnia's Lambton College with 82% international students, Northern College in Timmins with about 80%, North Bay's Canadore College at 72% and Conestoga College in Kitchener-Waterloo at 71%. This is part of a pattern that, despite NDP and Liberal claims, isn't all Doug Ford's fault. It started about a decade ago and really took off around 2016.
There is no doubt the Ford government let this get out of control, along with the Trudeau government in Ottawa, which issues the permits. Not that Dunlop would admit that on Monday - in fact, she dodged question after question, putting all the blame on Ottawa.
The reality is they are both to blame, but so are the schools that became dependent on the higher tuition paid by foreign students and now want Queen's Park to bail them out. That shouldn't happen.
Governing is about choices.
So, too, is running a major college or university.
Ford has given families what they need. He's given the colleges and universities some of what they asked for, but not all of it. It's time for those in charge of these institutions to make hard choices.
It won't be pretty, but it is necessary. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Tuition; Foreign students; Colleges & universities; Funding

Business indexing term: Subject: Colleges & universities

Identifier / keyword: parents; students; tuition; colleges; universities; going

Document 216 of 356

Rogan: ditch trudeau; 'I love Canadians, I just hate their government' , controversial podcaster says

Author: Daniell, Mark

Publication info: Daniell, Mark. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rogan-ditch-trudeau-i-love-canadians-just-hate/docview/2932311622/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rogan%3A+ditch+trudeau%3B+%27I+love+Canadians%2C+I+just+hate+their+government%27+%2C+controversial+podcaster+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.21&amp;au=Daniell%2C+Mark&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A federal election isn't scheduled to happen until October 2025, but Joe Rogan is already warning Canadians: a vote for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau will quicken the country's continued slide.
Canada's household debt is now highest in the G7, with struggling Canadians creating more than 200,000 fundraising campaigns to cover everyday expenses.
The cost of food was up last fall despite the Industry Minister's claim that prices would "stabilize" for Canadians. In Toronto, prospective home buyers with a median annual income of $91,858 will have to save for more than 25 years for a down payment to buy a house.
Trudeau has become such a polarizing leader that last month, his security detail had to step in to usher him away after he was confronted by a group of protesters.
"If he gets elected again, you guys are gluttons for punishment," Rogan said during a chat with guest Tom Green on a recent episode of his Joe Rogan Experience podcast. "I love Canadians, I just hate their government. Pierre Poilievre ... that guy makes so much more sense. He's so common sense, calling out all the nonsense that's been done under this administration, which has been so sad to watch."
Green, who moved back to Ontario recently after living in Los Angeles for 20 years, gave Rogan a glimpse of what life is like inside Canada right now amid a growing political divide.
"This is so common in Canada ... driving around everywhere in the country, in the city, everywhere, people in pickup trucks (have) 'F---Trudeau'flags ... It says 'F---Trudeau'... Canada flag on it. People are mad ... he might not get elected the next time," Green said.
"I hope he doesn't," Rogan replied. But Green said the division in Canada isn't unlike what is happening in the United States as presumptive Republican presidential nominee Donald Trump gets ready for a rematch against incumbent Joe Biden this November.
"You've got Biden as the president now ... it's the same thing, half the country hates the party in power and they want to get him out. As a proud Canadian, I wanted to (make) the distinction that Canada's like (America). Everybody's arguing about issues, important issues, it's being reinforced through these algorithms. People get mad about it ... I sometimes (think), wouldn't it be interesting if Pierre Poilievre won the next election because all of a sudden we'd have a conservative government up there. Then, let's say Biden wins down here, you have a conservative government up there, and Tucker Carlson will be going up to Canada all of a sudden talking about how great we are. It can all turn on a dime."
Rogan and Green's exchange racked up thousands of views on social media and generated plenty of comments on Reddit, with some criticizing the podcaster for his sudden embrace of Poilievre.
"I guarantee Joe hasn't seen more than 10 mins of Pierre Poilievre speaking. He probably saw a few clips of him dunking on Trudeau and started swooning," one person wrote. "Trudeau has lost the country's approval, and Poilievre made a career out of speaking for Trudeau's haters. Nothing about Poilievre's personal campaign is why Joe likes him, it's entirely because he stands as an anti-Trudeau figure."
"Why would Joe care about the PM of Canada other than wanting right wing figures in charge everywhere? ... He has an agenda now," a second added.
Rogan has been critical of Trudeau for years, largely stemming from the prime minister's handling of the trucker protest in the nation's capital in 2022.
During a special broadcast of the Joe Rogan Experience podcast that aired during UFC 297 in Toronto last month, Rogan reacted to footage of the crowd inside Scotiabank Arena chanting "F---Trudeau!" "They're yelling 'F---Trudeau'," Rogan exclaimed.
The anti-Trudeau chants came after former UFC middleweight champion Sean Strickland sparred with a reporter during a pre-fight press conference and accused them of voting for the Liberals.
"Canada, get your s---together! Come back. Come back to what you used to be," Rogan urged.
The popular podcaster then made his endorsement for Canada's next prime minister. "That Pierre Poilievre guy, however you say his name, he makes sense," Rogan said.
On an episode of the Joe Rogan Experience in December with YouTuber Derek from More Plates More Dates, Rogan said he wouldn't cross the border until there's "an overhaul of government."
"I don't go up there anymore," Rogan said. "What they've done up there, what they did with the trucker rally and what Trudeau is doing with guns and what they're trying to clamp down on censorship on the Internet.
That guy can eat s---. That place needs 100% an overhaul of government. They're sliding down that dangerous road of communism. It scares the s---out of me."
Last summer, Rogan revealed at one point he might have moved north of the border, but said Trudeau's leadership scared him off.
"Canada's f---ing falling apart. All the s---they did during COVID was just the total wrong direction," he said on an episode of his podcast featuring Post Malone.
Recent polling shows a Liberal government under Trudeau heading for a massive defeat. According to the Sun's Warren Kinsella, "a couple of models conjured up in the past week show the Conservatives are on the cusp of the largest majority in Canadian history."
But whether it is ego, blind optimism or the fanciful notion that he can win next year, Trudeau has yet to indicate he'll step aside to try and save the Liberal Party from a defeat of truly historic proportions. mdaniell@postmedia.com

Location: Canada

People: Rogan, Joe; Poilievre, Pierre

Document 217 of 356

'Must Never Be Silent'; Anti-Semitism won't win, but how is it making gains in 2024?

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/must-never-be-silent-anti-semitism-wont-win-how/docview/2932311550/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Must+Never+Be+Silent%27%3B+Anti-Semitism+won%27t+win%2C+but+how+is+it+making+gains+in+2024%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "F---Jews," says the graffiti slur on the pole on Carlton St.
"Vote Hamas," says the scrawled on the statue of iconic actor Al Waxman over the weekend in Bellevue Square.
There have been swastikas drawn all over the place as well.
This isn't 1930s Germany.
It's 2024 Toronto.
"Canada has a worsening anti-Semitism problem. The Jewish community cannot combat it alone," said Richard Robertson, research and advocacy director at B'nai Brith Canada. "As Canadians, we must learn from the lessons of the past and must never be silent in the face of intolerable hate."
If those who continue with the anti-Semitic crime spree think they are not going to get caught, they should look at Monday's news release from Toronto Police of yet another arrest in a similar case.
You just may get caught. Toronto Police alleged that about noon on Feb. 23, officers were called to King and Dufferin Sts. "The accused vandalized several mixeduse buildings by spray-painting anti-Semitic symbols on the property," police said in a statement.
It didn't take long to track down a suspect: The next day, Michael Park, 35, of Toronto, was arrested and charged with three counts of mischief under $5,000, obstructing a peace officer and failure to comply with a probation order.
Police alleged "the accused provided false identification upon arrest." Const. Sinderela Chung and Det. Pasquale Alberga said "when suspected hate-motivated offences are reported to police, the investigation could be led by a divisional investigator with the support of the hate crime unit" and that "wilful promotion of hatred and advocating genocide are hate propaganda (hate speech) offences which require the attorney general's consent to lay charges."
It's important because it could apply to the anti-Semitic graffiti on Al Waxman's King of Kensington statue in Bellevue Park.
Whoever wrote "Vote Hamas" on the actor's memorial may think they are going to get away with it, but what they don't know is there are a lot of cameras around there and police are taking anti-Semitism seriously.
On AM640's The John Oakley Show on Monday, the person behind erecting the statue, Tom Mihalik, who has run Tom's Place clothier for 60 years and was a close friend of Waxman, said Sgt. Jeff Zammit told him "don't worry" because they are all over this case and guaranteed the statue will be shined up beautifully very soon.
It's time for the city, province and country to start taking care of our statues and install the cameras and lighting necessary to ensure anybody who tries to deface them will be caught.
But don't hold your breath because our leaders have been pretty silent. As of deadline, we still haven't seen any denouncement of the Waxman statue vandalism from Mayor Olivia Chow, Premier Doug Ford or Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
But while they seem to be missing in action, full kudos to Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, Independent MP Kevin Vuong and Toronto councillor Brad Bradford. They've been loud and productive on social media, pushing back against the horrible growing scourge of anti-Semitic vandalism that runs rampant now.
All politicians need to take a stand as well, and there needs to be a less bureaucratic approach to it.
"The city takes very seriously any graffiti vandalism that depicts hate speech," the city of Toronto said in a statement. "Once city staff were made aware of the defacing of the statue on Sunday, they co-ordinated with police and took immediate action to cover the statue and understand how to safely remove the graffiti."
But the hate was still there as of early Monday evening.
"Staff have no timeline for removal right now and will maintain regular checks ... Cleaning requires a specialized contractor that handles heritage monuments in order to remove the graffiti," the city said.
My feeling is Torontonians need to take a different approach. When they see hate, they should be able to remove it immediately, with no phone call to authorities necessary.
As for those who write "F---Jews," the message is simple: F them, and see you in court. jwarmington@postmedia.com @joe_warmington

Subject: Graffiti; Antisemitism; Jewish people; Vandalism; Hate speech

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Document 218 of 356

Alberta: Get in the ring, PM; 'Punching bag' preem dares Trudeau to call election

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/alberta-get-ring-pm-punching-bag-preem-dares/docview/2932311392/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alberta%3A+Get+in+the+ring%2C+PM%3B+%27Punching+bag%27+preem+dares+Trudeau+to+call+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Danielle Smith is used to Justin Trudeau using Alberta as a punching bag, but that doesn't mean Alberta's premier isn't tired of it.
In a recent interview for the Full Comment podcast, Smith said that if Trudeau wants to campaign against Alberta to win votes, he should just call an election.
"Look, if that's where we're heading - where we're heading to an election and he's using Alberta as a punching bag to try to win votes in Eastern Canada, it's the same old story happens over and over again - then let's just do it," Smith said of an election call.
Smith has been on the receiving end of barbs from Trudeau on issues such as her gender policy and is feeling attacked by his government on plans such as electricity grid regulations and oil and gas production caps.
"I don't accept what the prime minister is saying. I know that he's trying to get people angry and worked up," Smith said of Trudeau's reaction to her gender policy.
"I think we got the right balance and I think that there's a sort of a smear that the prime minister is trying to bring forward against all parents who want to be involved in these really crucial decisions for the kids."
At the end of January, Smith announced a change to Alberta's policies on gender and transgender issues. Widely panned by the media and her opponents as an attack on the trans and greater LGBTQ community, those critics ignored key elements of her policy.
Right now, anyone in Alberta undergoing transitioning surgery is sent to Quebec, something Smith said needs to be remedied with surgical capacity and skill in Alberta. She also promised better after-surgery care in terms of hormone therapies and mental-health counselling.
Yet Smith was attacked for saying parents should be made aware of students at school changing their names or pronouns, for banning breast-removal surgery for children and for limiting the use of puberty-blocking hormone therapy in young children.
"Shame on them," Trudeau said last week in Alberta.
He claimed Smith and federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre were picking fights with trans kids.
"The question I think people should be asking is why has the right suddenly decided to attack the LGBT community," Trudeau said.
Well, the answer is they aren't attacking anyone, but are instead standing up for the right of parents to know what is happening in places like schools as the left tries to implement radical policies.
"Radical" is a word that fits the Trudeau Liberals. They are no longer a party of the centre, trying to appeal to a wide array of Canadians - they are far-left, alt-left radicals. Just look at their environmental policies, another area where Smith has had difficulty with Ottawa.
Smith took aim at Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault, whose policies have created division across the country. She pointed to the push for a net-zero power grid and oil production caps as proof.
"A leader also has to know that when their minister is going off in a direction that is harmful to the country, harmful to national unity, when they're proposing policies that are unachievable and impractical, when they're creating that kind of division, it's upon a leader to step in and say, 'Whoa, hold on a minute, there might be something that I need to do some course correction on here,'" Smith said.
Her swipe at Guilbeault and his radical nature echoed comments made last week by Ontario Premier Doug Ford during a radio interview.
"Who's running the country, Justin Trudeau or Guilbeault?" Ford asked on John Oakley's radio show.
"Right now, I don't know." Good to know that it's not just Smith whom Trudeau and his team are annoying across Canada. Truth be told, it's most premiers and governments. It's just that Alberta takes the brunt as the punching bag - to which Smith responded by calling for an election.
"Let's just have an election so that this can be resolved one way or another," Smith said.
Amen to that. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Surgery; Environmental policy; Electricity distribution; Prime ministers; Gender

Location: Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Identifier / keyword: danielle; smith; justin; trudeau; using; alberta

LILLEY: Trudeau pummelling 'punching bag' Alberta for votes, premier says

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-trudeau-pummelling-punching-bag-alberta/docview/2932289033/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Trudeau+pummelling+%27punching+bag%27+Alberta+for+votes%2C+premier+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

GUNTER: Trudeau's Alberta trip full of imaginary enemies
Poilievre against transgender women in female bathrooms, changing rooms, sports

"Who's running the country, Justin Trudeau or Guilbeault?" Ford asked on Global News Radio with John Oakley
"Right now, I don't know."
Good to know that it's not just Smith who Trudeau and his team are annoying across Canada. Truth be told, it's most premiers and governments. It's just that Alberta takes the brunt as the punching bag.
To which Smith responded by calling for an election.
"Let's just have an election so that this can be resolved one way or another," Smith said.
Amen to that.

Subject: Surgery; Environmental policy; Electricity distribution; Prime ministers; Gender; Transgender persons

Location: Canada
