Document 220 of 356

LILLEY: Ford sides with students, freezes tuition for three more years

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-ford-sides-with-students-freezes-tuition/docview/2932288652/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Ford+sides+with+students%2C+freezes+tuition+for+three+more+years&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The good news for parents and students: Tuition at Ontario's colleges and universities won't be going up for the next three years. The bad news for the province's universities and colleges is that they won't be getting as much money as they hoped for.
Minister Jill Dunlop stood before reporters at Queen's Park to announce no fewer than 14 times that the Ford government was investing $1.3 billion in new funding into the post-secondary education sector.
Questioned time and again about other issues, Dunlop repeated that the government was investing $1.3 billion. It was as if she thought the journalists present hadn't heard her many pronouncements on the matter previously.
The news of the investment comes after a blue-ribbon panel appointed by the Ford government suggested last November that tuition should go up, and urged the province to increase funding by $2.5 billion over three years. Instead, the province is offering no tuition increase and just the $1.3 billion that Dunlop mentioned over and over again.
The opposition parties were quick to denounce the move as not enough.
"What has been promised today by the government comes nowhere close to what the blue-ribbon panel recommended was needed in our system," NDP critic Peggy Sattler said.
Recommended video
Yet, while denouncing the government for not following the blue-ribbon panel, Sattler was quick to endorse the tuition freeze.
"We absolutely support a tuition freeze. Students are suffering from the cost-of-living crisis the same way that everyone else in Ontario is," she said.
Liberal critic Adil Shamji made similar complaints, that the government didn't listen to its own panel on increased funding, while also saying he supports the tuition freeze that the blue-ribbon panel opposed. That panel had in fact called for a 5% tuition hike starting this coming September, followed by automatic tuition hikes of 2% a year afterwards.
That wouldn't help anyone deal with affordability issues.
"I will not apologize for continuing to freeze tuition in this province," Dunlop said. "Let me take you back to 2019, when Ontario had the highest tuition of all in Canada."
Now, three provinces have higher university tuition compared to Ontario, while seven provinces have higher college tuition.
Governing at any level is about choices, and Premier Doug Ford has decided that raising tuition rates on families right now isn't the best option. Could he have given the universities and colleges more money? Obviously, he could have, and Sattler made a solid argument that if the province is going to freeze tuition, they need to offer more subsidies.
But here we are, back to choices, and is giving colleges and universities ever-increasing amounts of money a good thing?
It is also worth asking whether we need to keep every single university and college in this province, since some of them seem to be having trouble. According to the Globe and Mail, in 2022-23, seven of Ontario's 24 colleges had more international students, paying higher fees, than they had domestic students.
That included Sarnia's Lambton College with 82% international students, Northern College in Timmins with about 80%, North Bay's Canadore College at 72% and Conestoga College in Kitchener-Waterloo at 71%. This is part of a pattern, that despite NDP and Liberal claims, isn't all Doug Ford's fault; it started about a decade ago and really took off in around 2016.
There is no doubt that the Ford government has let this get out of control, along with the Trudeau government in Ottawa, which issues the permits. Not that Dunlop would admit that on Monday; in fact, she dodged question after question, putting all the blame on Ottawa.
The reality is they are both to blame, but so are the schools which became dependent on the higher tuition paid by foreign students and now want the government at Queen's Park to bail them out.
That shouldn't happen.
Governing is about choices; so, too, is running a major college or university.
Ford has given families what they need. He's given the colleges and universities some of what they asked for, but not all of it. It's time for those in charge of these institutions to start making hard choices.
It won't be pretty, but it is necessary.
LILLEY: Appointing judges who share your philosophy part of the system
LILLEY: Actually, Canada, or at least the government, really is broken

Document 221 of 356

WARMINGTON: Historians will look back shamefully at our ugly era of anti-Semitism

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/warmington-historians-will-look-back-shamefully/docview/2932276740/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=WARMINGTON%3A+Historians+will+look+back+shamefully+at+our+ugly+era+of+anti-Semitism&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "F*** Jews," said the graffiti slur on the pole on Carlton St.
"Vote Hamas," said the slogan scrawled on the statue of iconic actor Al Waxman over the weekend in Bellevue Square. There have been swastikas drawn all over the place, as well.
This isn't 1930s Germany. It's 2024 Toronto.
"Canada has a worsening anti-Semitism problem. The Jewish community cannot combat it alone," said Richard Robertson, research and advocacy director at B'nai Brith Canada. "As Canadians, we must learn from the lessons of the past and must never be silent in the face of intolerable hate. "
If those who continue with the anti-Semitic crime spree think they are not going to get caught, they should look at Monday's news release from Toronto Police of yet another arrest in a similar case.
You just may get caught.
Toronto Police alleged that on "Friday, Feb. 23, 2024, at approximately 12 p.m., police responded to a call for a mischief in the King St. W. and Dufferin St. area. The accused vandalized several mixed-use buildings by spray-painting anti-Semitic symbols on the property."
It didn't take long to track down a suspect.
"On Saturday, Feb. 24, 2024, Michael Park, 35, of Toronto, was arrested and charged with three counts of mischief under $5000, obstruct peace officer and fail to comply probation order."
Police alleged "the accused provided false identification upon arrest."
Const. Sinderela Chung and Det. Pasquale Alberga said "when suspected hate-motivated offences are reported to police, the investigation could be led by a divisional investigator with the support of the Hate Crime Unit (HCU)" and that "wilful promotion of hatred and advocating genocide are hate propaganda (hate speech) offences which require the Attorney General's consent to lay charges."
It's important because it could apply to the anti-Semitic graffiti attack on Al Waxman's King of Kensington statue in Bellevue Park.
Whoever wrote "Vote Hamas" on the legendary actor's memorial may think they are going to get away with it, but what they don't know is there are a lot of cameras around there and police are taking anti-Semitism seriously.
The statue of King of Kensington Al Waxman was hit with anti-Semitic graffiti this weekend and as of Sunday was still not cleaned up - Kevin Vuong photo
On AM640's The John Oakley Show on Monday, the person behind erecting the statue, Tom Mihalik, who has run Tom's Place clothier for 60 years and was a close friend of Waxman, said Sgt. Jeff Zammit told him "don't worry" because they are all over this case and guaranteed the statue will be shined up beautifully very soon.
Recommended video
It's time for the city, province and country to start taking care of our special statues and to install the cameras and lighting necessary to ensure anybody who tries to deface them will be caught.
But don't hold your breath because our leaders have been pretty silent. As of deadline, we still haven't seen any denouncement of the Waxman statue attack from Mayor Olivia Chow, Premier Doug Ford or Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
Disgusting to see this hateful graffiti targeting the statue of Al Waxman, a great Jewish Canadian.
Conservatives will continue to stand against antisemitism and all forms of hate.
- Pierre Poilievre (@PierrePoilievre) February 24, 2024
But while they seem to be missing in action, full kudos to Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, Independent MP Kevin Vuong and Toronto Councillor Brad Bradford. They've been loud and productive on social media, pushing back against the horrible growing scourge of anti-Semitic vandalism that is running rampant now.
All politicians need to take a stand, as well. And there needs to be a less bureaucratic approach to it.
"The city takes very seriously any graffiti vandalism that depicts hate speech," said the city of Toronto. "Once city staff were made aware of the defacing of the statue on Sunday, they coordinated with police and took immediate action to cover the statue and understand how to safely remove the graffiti."
What a disgrace to treat Al Waxman-The King of Kensington-this way. He was a great man who deserved better than to be victim of antisemitism. Perhaps worse is none of Canada's leaders have said a thing. But we will. Al may have died in 2001 but he's still here. Long Live the King
- Joe Warmington (@joe_warmington) February 26, 2024
But the hate was still there as of early evening on Monday.
"Staff have no timeline for removal right now and will maintain regular checks to ensure the graffiti remains covered until it can be removed safely," said the city. "Cleaning requires a specialized contractor that handles heritage monuments in order to remove the graffiti."
My feeling is Torontonians need to take a different approach. When they see hate, they should be able to remove it immediately, with no phone call to authorities necessary.
As for those who write "F***Jews," the message is simple.
F them. And see you in court.
WARMINGTON: 'King of Kensington' latest target of ugly anti-Semitic vandalism
King of Kensington memorial needs TLC Author of the article:

Document 222 of 356

As of today, it's only 1 fare

Author: Connor, Kevin

Publication info: Connor, Kevin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/as-today-only-1-fare/docview/2931866464/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=As+of+today%2C+it%27s+only+1+fare&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Connor%2C+Kevin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: TTC riders who rely on more than one transit system to get around the GTA are going to pay less starting Monday.
Commuters who pay one fare will be able to transfer between the TTC and other GTA transit systems for free.
"Customers paying with a Presto card, Presto in Google Wallet, debit or credit card (physical or one in a mobile wallet) will be able to transfer for free between the TTC, Brampton Transit, Durham Region Transit, MiWay and York Region Transit, thanks to Ontario's new one fare program," the TTC said in a news release.
"Also, customers using Presto, debit or credit to transfer between TTC and GO Transit will benefit from the one fare program. The TTC portion of the trip will be free, regardless of the direction of travel."
The move could save a rider up to $1,600 a year.
The program will be a game changer for transit riders, Premier Doug Ford said recently.
"Most importantly, it will mean massive savings for transit riders and putting money back into people's pockets where it belongs," Ford added.

Life at mount sinai; Letters to the editor

Author: Konstantinou, Al

Publication info: Konstantinou, Al. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/life-at-mount-sinai-letters-editor/docview/2931866460/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Life+at+mount+sinai%3B+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=Konstantinou%2C+Al&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford alluded to a possibility that a loved one may have been the unfortunate consequence of an ill-advised and thoughtless demonstration ("Ontario premier denounces protest," Feb. 13).
My parents were Greek Immigrants.
Just married with a baby living in a poorly heated apartment above a store on Adelaide St., the boy got sick. The father, a male chauvinist, ignored my mother's plea to visit a hospital. Out of desperation, a mother who knew her child rushed this baby to the nearest hospital - Mount Sinai.
Luck would have it that a doctor who was familiar with pneumonia was on duty. Dr. Hart, a Jewish doctor, knew right away what the problem was but also knew it was too advanced to be medically treated. Do miracles happen? Yes. Hart did what he thought no one could do and saved this boy's life. I'm alive thanks to Mount Sinai.
Today I am approaching my 83rd birthday. I have a beautiful wife and four gorgeous daughters who have given us 11 wonderful grandchildren and now six great-grandchildren.
I ask all of you protesters to just give some thought to what you're doing. The targets here are hard-working people regardless of their heritage.
We are all Canadians, we need to respect each other, that is what Canada is - people from all walks of life uniting to make this the greatest country on the face of the Earth, or we can destroy it.
(This is beautifully said. Targeting a hospital is part of a long list of reprehensible protests that have been spilling out onto our streets)

Subject: Demonstrations & protests

Identifier / keyword: alluded; possibility; loved; unfortunate; consequence; thoughtless

Document 224 of 356

'Very clear where I stand'; Appointing 'like-minded'judges just part of our political system

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/very-clear-where-i-stand-appointing-like/docview/2931866401/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Very+clear+where+I+stand%27%3B+Appointing+%27like-minded%27judges+just+part+of+our+political+system&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Can you believe the nerve of Doug Ford? He appointed people to the panel that helps select judges who agree with his philosophy on how courts should run.
Well, Ontario hasn't seen that kind of bald-faced partisanship since - well, actually, every government does this.
The Toronto Star, the official newspaper of the Liberal party, dragged this story out and filled it with outrage. They noted that two people who used to work for the Ford government have now been appointed to the committee.
University of Waterloo poli-sci professor and Ford derangement sufferer Emmett Macfarlane provided the Star with "expert" commentary.
"I'm not sure I've seen such blatant examples of patronage recently," Macfarlane told the Red Star.
Apparently, Macfarlane didn't notice all the times the Liberals appointed insiders or donors to the Judicial Appointments Advisory Council, or he didn't care because he agrees with Liberals. The same could be said of the associations representing lawyers that stepped forward to complain.
Former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne said, "intentionally tainting the judicial process is reprehensible." Current Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said, "This is entirely wrong and must be reversed."
Asked if he was OK with these appointments during a Friday news conference, Ford said he was 100% behind them.
"We got elected to get like-minded people in appointments," Ford said.
"I'm not going to appoint some NDP or some Liberal. I've made it very clear where I stand with judges."
Ford has frequently called out judges and justices of the peace for being too soft on violent offenders, especially repeat violent offenders.
"These guys are kicking in doors, putting guns to people's heads, handing over the keys and guess what, when we catch him, we find out they've been on bail eight times," Ford said, citing the worst of carjackings and auto thefts at homes around the GTA.
He's not wrong about the repeat offenders being out on bail time and again. We've documented these stories in the Toronto Sun, the police have expressed their frustration with the system, but until recently, the federal law said that bail was supposed to be the default position.
The changes brought in by the Trudeau government after years of pressure from provincial governments of all political stripes are still working their way through the system.
Ford wants to appoint judges who share his judicial philosophy and as premier, that is his right. It has been the right of every premier since our system was created. It's just that now we only hear complaints when the person up for an appointment is a conservative of any kind.
Under the Liberal governments of Kathleen Wynne and Dalton McGuinty, it was common for party donors to be appointed to the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee.
Hanny Hassan, a Liberal donor, was appointed by Dalton McGuinty and became chairman of the committee. Frank Walwyn, another regular Liberal financial supporter, made a $4,860 donation to the Ontario Liberals in 2015 while he was on the committee.
We didn't hear the Star complain about that. We didn't hear lawyers groups, which are mostly Liberal leaning, raise a fuss. That's because things were going just as they wanted them.
In saying that he has no problem appointing people who share his judicial philosophy to the panel that selects judges, Ford is at least being honest. For years, Liberal governments have set up supposedly "non-partisan" processes that always come to the same conclusion: Appoint the most Liberal judge you can find.
"That's part of democracy," Ford said.
"You voted a party in, and I'd say that no matter what parties, if the feds want to appoint Liberals up in the federal government, that's up to them. I'm appointing like-minded people that believe in what we believe in, keeping the bad guys in jail."
Imagine that, elections having consequences and people getting the kind of judicial system they voted for. The only people against that, well, they are Liberals who think they should be in charge even when they lose elections. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Committees; Appointments & personnel changes; Judicial appointments; Partisanship; Philosophy

Business indexing term: Subject: Appointments & personnel changes

People: McGuinty, Dalton; Wynne, Kathleen

Identifier / keyword: believe; nerve; appointed; people; panel; helps

Document 225 of 356

Letters to the Editor, Feb. 26, 2024

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/letters-editor-feb-26-2024/docview/2931806319/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+to+the+Editor%2C+Feb.+26%2C+2024&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: LIFE AT MOUNT SINAI
Doug Ford alluded to a possibility that a loved one may have been the unfortunate consequence of an ill-advised and thoughtless demonstration ("Ontario premier denounces protest," Feb. 13). My parents were Greek Immigrants. Just married with a baby, living in a poorly heated apartment above a store on Adelaide St., the boy got sick. The father, a male chauvinist, ignored my mother's plea to visit a hospital. Out of desperation, a mother who knew her child rushed this baby to the nearest hospital - Mount Sinai. Luck would have it that a doctor who was familiar with pneumonia was on duty. Dr. Hart, a Jewish doctor, knew right away what the problem was, but also knew it was too advanced to be medically treated. Do miracles happen? Yes. Hart did what he thought no one could do and saved this boy's life. I'm alive thanks to Mount Sinai. Today I am approaching my 83rd birthday. I have a beautiful wife and four gorgeous daughters, who have given us 11 wonderful grandchildren and now six great-grandchildren. I ask all of you protesters to just give some thought to what you're doing. The targets here are hard-working people regardless of their heritage. We are all Canadians, we need to respect each other, that is what Canada is - people from all walks of life uniting to make this the greatest country on the face of the Earth, or we can destroy it.
Al Konstantinou
(This is beautifully said. Targeting a hospital is part of a long list of reprehensible protests that have been spilling out onto our streets)
DERANGEMENT SYNDROME
Remember when Ontario was so screwed up by Bob Rae that we needed the Common Sense Revolution to get things back on track? Notwithstanding the politically incorrect but necessary work being done by Mike Harris, the asinine 'left' of the time required everyone in the room to titter and roll their eyes at the mere mention of his name. Similarly, the reader was writing a pretty good letter "Crime of ages" (Feb. 20) when for whatever reason he found it necessary to add the irrelevant phrase "like Donald Trump." He could have as easily added "Mike Harris." He tarnishes a valid point by revealing a level of derangement.
Tim Conway
Toronto
(Certain political leaders evoke that level of derangement every time)

Document 226 of 356

TTC riders using more than one GTA transit system get break, starting Monday

Author: Connor, Kevin

Publication info: Connor, Kevin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ttc-riders-using-more-than-one-gta-transit-system/docview/2931790675/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TTC+riders+using+more+than+one+GTA+transit+system+get+break%2C+starting+Monday&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Connor%2C+Kevin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: TTC riders who rely on more than one transit system to get around the GTA are going to pay less, starting Monday.
Commuters who pay one fare will be able to transfer between the TTC and other GTA transit systems for free.
"Customers paying with a PRESTO card, PRESTO in Google Wallet, debit or credit card (physical or one in a mobile wallet) will be able to transfer for free between the TTC, Brampton Transit, Durham Region Transit, MiWay and York Region Transit, thanks to Ontario's new One Fare Program," the TTC said in a release.
"Also, customers using PRESTO, debit or credit, to transfer between TTC and GO Transit will benefit from the One Fare Program. The TTC portion of the trip will be free, regardless of the direction of travel."
The move could save a rider up to $1,600 a year.
The program will be a game changer for transit riders, Premier Doug Ford said recently.
"Most importantly, it will mean massive savings for transit riders and putting money back into people's pockets where it belongs," added Ford.

Identifier / keyword: riders; transit; system; around; going; starting

Joe Rogan begs Canada to ditch Trudeau: 'Gluttons for punishment'

Author: Daniell, Mark

Publication info: Daniell, Mark. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/joe-rogan-begs-canada-ditch-trudeau-gluttons/docview/2931789336/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Joe+Rogan+begs+Canada+to+ditch+Trudeau%3A+%27Gluttons+for+punishment%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Daniell%2C+Mark&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A federal election isn't scheduled to happen until October 2025, but Joe Rogan is already warning Canadians: a vote for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau will quicken the country's continued slide.
Canada's household debt is now highest in the G7, with struggling Canadians creating more than 200,000 fundraising campaigns to cover everyday expenses
The cost of food was up last fall despite the Industry Minister's claim that prices would "stabilize" for Canadians. In Toronto, prospective home buyers with a median annual income of $91,858 will have to save for more than 25 years for a down payment to buy a house.
Trudeau has become such a polarizing leader, that last month his security detail had to step in to usher him away after he was confronted by a group of protesters.
"If he gets elected again, you guys are gluttons for punishment," Rogan said during a chat with guest Tom Green on a recent episode of his Joe Rogan Experience "I love Canadians, I just hate their government. Pierre Poilievre ... that guy makes so much more sense. He's so common sense, calling out all the nonsense that's been done under this administration, which has been so sad to watch."
"I love Canadians, I just hate their government."
Joe Rogan and Tom Green discuss how politically divided Canada is and why Pierre Poilievre is the better choice.
- Mythinformed (@MythinformedMKE) February 24, 2024
Green, who moved back to Ontario recently after living in Los Angeles for 20 years, gave Rogan a glimpse of what life is like inside Canada right now amid a growing political divide.
"This is so common in Canada ... driving around everywhere in the country, in the city, everywhere, people in pickup trucks [have] 'F*** Trudeau' flags ... It says 'F*** Trudeau.'... Canada flag on it. People are mad ... he might not get elected the next time," Green said.
Tom Green Says He Sees Lots of 'F*ck Trudeau' Flags Now That He Lives Back in Canada
"Driving around everywhere in the country, in the city, everywhere people in pickup trucks [have] 'F*ck Trudeau' flags. It says 'F*ck Trudeau.'...People are mad."@joerogan @tomgreenlive
- Chief Nerd (@TheChiefNerd) February 24, 2024
"I hope he doesn't," Rogan replied.
But Green said that the division in Canada isn't unlike what is happening in the United States as presumptive Republican presidential nominee Donald Trump gets ready for a rematch against incumbent Joe Biden this November.
"You've got Biden as the president now ... it's the same thing, half the country hates the party in power and they want to get him out. As a proud Canadian, I wanted to (make) the distinction that Canada's like (America). Everybody's arguing about issues, important issues, it's being reinforced through these algorithms. People get mad about it ... I sometimes (think), wouldn't it be interesting if Pierre Poilievre won the next election because all of a sudden we'd have a conservative government up there. Then, let's say Biden wins down here, you have a conservative government up there, and Tucker Carlson will be going up to Canada all of a sudden talking about how great we are. It can all turn on a dime."
Rogan and Green's exchange racked up thousands of views on social media and generated plenty of comments on Reddit, with some criticizing the podcaster for his sudden embrace of Poilievre.
"I guarantee Joe hasn't seen more than 10 mins of Pierre Poilievre speaking. He probably saw a few clips of him dunking on Trudeau and started swooning," one person wrote. "Trudeau has lost the country's approval, and Poilievre made a career out of speaking for Trudeau's haters. Nothing about Poilievre's personal campaign is why Joe likes him, it's entirely because he stands as an anti-Trudeau figure."
"Why would Joe care about the PM of Canada other than wanting right wing figures in charge everywhere? ... He has an agenda now," a second added.
Rogan has been critical of Trudeau for years, largely stemming from the prime minister's handling of the trucker protest in the nation's capital in 2022.
During a special broadcast of the Joe Rogan Experience podcast that aired during UFC 297 in Toronto last month, Rogan reacted to footage of the crowd inside Scotiabank Arena chanting "F*** Trudeau!"
"They're yelling 'F*** Trudeau'," Rogan exclaimed.
The anti-Trudeau chants came after former UFC middleweight champion Sean Strickland sparred with a reporter during a pre-fight press conference and accused them of voting for the Liberals.
"Canada, get your s*** together! Come back. Come back to what you used to be," Rogan urged.
The Crowd Starts Chanting "F*ck Trudeau" Before Sean Strickland's UFC 297 Fight Tonight
- Chief Nerd (@TheChiefNerd) January 21, 2024
The popular podcaster then made his endorsement for Canada's next prime minister. "That Pierre Poilievre guy, however you say his name, he makes sense," Rogan said.
On an episode of the in December with YouTuber Derek from More Plates More Dates, Rogan said he wouldn't cross the border until there's "an overhaul of government."
"I don't go up there anymore," Rogan said. "What they've done up there, what they did with the trucker rally and what Trudeau is doing with guns and what they're trying to clamp down on censorship on the Internet. That guy can eat s***. That place needs 100% an overhaul of government. They're sliding down that dangerous road of communism. It scares the s*** out of me."
Last summer, Rogan revealed at one point he might have moved north of the border, but said Trudeau's leadership scared him off.
"Canada's f***ing falling apart. All the s*** they did during COVID was just the total wrong direction," he said on an episode of his podcast featuring Post Malone.
Recent polling shows a Liberal government under Trudeau heading for a massive defeat. According to the , "a couple of models conjured up in the past week show the Conservatives are on the cusp of the largest majority in Canadian history."
But whether it is ego, blind optimism or the fanciful notion that he can win next year, Trudeau has yet to indicate he'll step aside to try and save the Liberal Party from a defeat of truly historic proportions.

Document 228 of 356

LILLEY: Appointing judges who share your philosophy part of the system

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-appointing-judges-who-share-your/docview/2931749609/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Appointing+judges+who+share+your+philosophy+part+of+the+system&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Can you believe the nerve of Doug Ford? He appointed people to the panel that helps select judges who agree with his philosophy on how courts should run.
Well, Ontario hasn't seen that kind of bald-faced partisanship since.... well, actually, every government does this.
The Toronto Star, the official newspaper of the Liberal Party, dragged this story out and filled it with outrage. They noted that two people who used to work for the Ford government have now been appointed to the committee.
University of Waterloo poli-sci professor and Ford Derangement sufferer Emmett Macfarlane provided the Star with "expert" commentary.
"I'm not sure I've seen such blatant examples of patronage recently," Macfarlane told the Red Star.
Apparently, Macfarlane didn't notice all the times the Liberals appointed insiders or donors to the Judicial Appointments Advisory Council, or he didn't care because he agrees with Liberals. The same could be said of the various associations representing lawyers which stepped forward to complain.
Former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne said, "Intentionally tainting the judicial process is reprehensible." Current Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said, "This is entirely wrong and must be reversed."
Asked if he was OK with these appointments during a Friday news conference, Ford said he was 100% behind them.
"We got elected to get like-minded people in appointments," Ford said.
"I'm not going to appoint some NDP or some Liberal. I've made it very clear where I stand with judges."
Ford has frequently called out judges and justices of the peace for being too soft of violent offenders, especially repeat violent offenders.
"These guys are kicking in doors, putting guns to people's heads, handing over the keys and guess what, when we catch him, we find out they've been on bail eight times," Ford said, citing the worst of carjackings and auto thefts at homes around the GTA.
He's not wrong about the repeat offenders being out on bail time and again. We've documented these stories in The Toronto Sun, the police have expressed their frustration with the system, but until recently, the federal law said that bail was supposed to be the default position.
Recommended video
The changes brought in by the Trudeau government after years of pressure from provincial governments of all political stripes are still working their way through the system.
Ford wants to appoint judges who share his judicial philosophy and as premier, that is his right. It has been the right of every premier since our system was created; it's just that now we only hear complaints when the person up for an appointment is a conservative of any kind.
Under the Liberal governments of Kathleen Wynne and Dalton McGuinty, it was common for party donors to be appointed to the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee.
Hanny Hassan, a Liberal donor, was appointed by Dalton McGuinty and became chair of the committee. Frank Walwyn, another regular Liberal financial supporter, made a $4,860 donation to the Ontario Liberals in 2015 while he was on the committee.
We didn't hear the Star complain about that, we didn't hear lawyers groups - which are mostly Liberal leaning - raise a fuss. That's because things were going just as they wanted them.
In saying that he has no problem appointing people who share his judicial philosophy to the panel that selects judges, Ford is at least being honest. For years, Liberal governments have set up supposedly "non-partisan" processes that always come to the same conclusion: Appoint the most Liberal judge you can find.
"That's part of democracy," Ford said.
"You voted a party in, and I'd say that no matter what parties, if the feds want to appoint Liberals up in the federal government that's up to them. I'm appointing like-minded people that believe in what we believe in - keeping the bad guys in jail."
Imagine that, elections having consequences and people getting the kind of judicial system they voted for. The only people against that, well, they are Liberals who think they should be in charge even when they lose elections.
LILLEY: Trouble at Flair Airlines exposes Trudeau government's massive incompetence
KINSELLA: It's time to go and Justin Trudeau knows it

Subject: Committees; Appointments & personnel changes; Judicial appointments; Partisanship; Philosophy

Document 229 of 356

Mired in bureaucracy; Complex legislation for homebuyers

Author: ijn, Barbara

Publication info: ijn, Barbara. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mired-bureaucracy-complex-legislation-homebuyers/docview/2931444439/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mired+in+bureaucracy%3B+Complex+legislation+for+homebuyers&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=ijn%2C+Barbara&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Many of us have had the experience where household tasks are delegated to several siblings as a group, but if no specifics are given on who does what, the buck gets passed back and forth, and tasks often don't get done.
If everyone is responsible, no one is.
Consumer protection for new home buyers in Ontario is a complex and confusing patchwork of laws, and often leads to a similar blame-game when construction defects are discovered in newly built homes.
If Ontario wants to encourage people to buy more new homes, protections have to be simplified, streamlined and subject to one authority, not several.
Most people have never heard of the government agencies Tarion and the Home Construction Regulatory Authority (HCRA), or the Ministry of Public and Business Service Delivery (PBSD), which oversees them. One would naturally look to the Consumer Protection Act to protect new home buyers, but it doesn't cover that. It oddly only pertains to timeshare purchases.
Condo and freehold homes are covered under other legislation called the New Home Warranties Plan Act, the New Home Construction Licensing Act, the Condo Act, and a complex document called the Addendum, described in 2022 by a Superior Court judge in 2022 as a "convoluted and confusingly long and obscure document."
Then there's the 40-50 page new home purchase contract itself, written by developers and their lawyers. Some contracts include so-called weasel clauses, for example saying the purchaser can't join class-action lawsuits, or can't talk about any disputes with the developer, or their contract could be cancelled and the deposit forfeited.
Even if you successfully navigate the tricky contract stage, what happens if you find a crack in your new home's basement floor, water infiltration or mould? That's when the two arms-length agencies of government, Tarion and HCRA come in.
The run-around you may get goes like this: First you contact the builder for a resolution; he's supposed to warrant the home is free of defects and built according to the Building Code. If he fails to fix the problems or denies there's a problem, you contact Tarion. They contact the builder, and give him time to respond or repair. If he refuses, Tarion can compensate you, but you have to find your own contractor to fix the issues. Some homeowners contact the municipality to see if proper inspections were done during construction, but often the municipality sends the homeowner back to Tarion, who then goes back to the builder, and the pass-thebuck goes on.
Tarion acts as a third party - they don't provide a warranty, it's the builder's warranty.
This has been confusing so the Auditor General of Ontario in 2019 instructed Tarion to remove the word warranty from its name, because it doesn't offer a warranty. A year-long judge's review of Tarion in 2017 recommended new home warranties be provided by real warranty companies regulated under the Insurance Act, as in Alberta and British Columbia.
Consumer advocates and Opposition MPPs agreed.
But free-market Premier Doug Ford ignored this recommendation, while saying he's against government running monopolies, which is what Tarion is.
Most new home warranty companies that operate in B.C. and Alberta for example, insure the builder, and then take over contact with the homeowner if there are deficiencies, and the builder hasn't taken action. There's no third party go-between, as here in Ontario.
To add to the years of confusion about Tar-i-on's role, their CEO recently stated in a branding podcast called Unpacked that Tarion is "kind of an insurance."
But the 2017 judge's review stated, "Tarion is not regulated as an insurance company," and is not "regulated as an insurance product."
Also baffling was the CEO's claim that "you don't have to buy it, everyone gets it," referring to the Tarion fee. But you do pay for the Tarion fee, it's mandatory, and passed on to the homebuyer in the purchase price.
In a subsequent episode of the podcast, two Tarion executives said purchasers need to "ask questions, ask more questions."
But with a 40-50 page contract and a complex legislative framework, how do you know what questions to ask? To put the burden on the homebuyer for consumer protection is not fair or realistic.
Another suggestion by Tarion executives was to search online reviews of builders.
They know some homeowners have been threatened with defamation suits for speaking out about problem builders. The HCRA Builder Directory, which is supposed to be a research tool for future home buyers, is of little more use than a telephone book.
The courts are also not the answer to consumer protection.
Most individuals don't have the financial resources and time off work to go through a lengthy, complex legal fight with builders or government agencies.
Each year in May, consumer advocates are reminded of the tragic suicide of Earl Shuman, who fought a 27-year battle to get his new home fixed. He was put in jail for a week for not paying his opponent's legal fees. The late journalist Christie Blatchford wrote a moving story about his epic battle in the National Post on Sept. 24, 2015, entitled "Homeowner's Kafkaesque Legal Battle with Builder and Warranty Agency."
Earl summed up the problem, which was as true then as it is now: "It's the chaos in the legislation."
Premier Ford, who likes to say he's a businessman not a politician, should end this third party go-between system which doesn't provide a warranty.
Give new home buyers the protections they pay for under a real warranty system, open to competition, as is now used in both B.C. and Alberta, and internationally.
It's up to the ministry responsible for consumer protection, the PBSD, to take action without more years of delay and more damage to consumers.
As Earl once said, "I don't blame the players, I blame the referee."
In memory of Earl Shuman, 1951-2016.
Captijn is a consumer advocate @ReformTarion

Subject: Government agencies; Legislation; Third party; Consumerism; Homeowners; Consumer protection

Business indexing term: Subject: Consumer protection

Identifier / keyword: experience; household; tasks; delegated; several; siblings

Title: Mired in bureaucracy; Complex legislation for homebuyers

Document 230 of 356

PM's Alberta trip full of imaginery enemies

Author: Gunter, Lorne

Publication info: Gunter, Lorne. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pms-alberta-trip-full-imaginery-enemies/docview/2931444207/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PM%27s+Alberta+trip+full+of+imaginery+enemies&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=Gunter%2C+Lorne&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Trudeau's trip to Alberta this week to announce $175 million in federal grants for housing starts probably did for federal credibility here (already at a low) what his 2018 Mr. Dressup Goes to Bollywood tour did for Canada-India relations.
Trudeau came without informing the provincial government. That's not exactly a legal necessity. This is, after all, his country, too. However, it is considered a courtesy for federal ministers to inform their provincial counterparts in advance.
Not telling the UCP government was deliberate.
Also, no provincial minister or MLA was invited to the housing announcement. Trudeau's lone Edmonton Liberal MP, Randy Boissonnault, was there. So too was his old friend, Mayor Amarjeet Sohi, who served in Trudeau's cabinet during its first term.
But, despite the fact that both the federal and provincial governments have largely put aside their differences to co-operate on housing affordability, there was no provincial representative in sight.
And while the P.M. had no time for a get-together with Alberta Premier Dainelle Smith, he and his staff did manage to find time for a 30-minute sit-down with "progressive" Edmonton podcaster, Ryan Jespersen.
It was the Jespersen interview that created the greatest anger among Albertans. Trudeau was at one and the same time arrogant, delusional, dismissive and insulting.
Time and again his answers betrayed his inner belief that his government's current unpopularity isn't its own fault. Poor polling has little to do with Liberal policies - carbon tax, EV mandate, inflation, housing and immigration - and everything to do with how others, notably the opposition Conservatives and online journalists, are being mean to them.
In Trudeau's mind, everything is a "comms" (communications) problem.
You can witness that thinking in last summer's cabinet shuffle, which changed no Liberal policies only the mouthpieces attempting to communicate them to voters.
And you can see it with the recent rebranding of the Climate Action Initiative Payment to the Canada Carbon Rebate. Surely the only reason 100% of Canadians aren't behind the Liberals'noble scheme is that they were too stupid to recognize what the old name was when they saw it on their bank statements.
Then Trudeau turned to insulting the intelligence of Albertans specifically.
In a patronizing tone that lasted throughout his Jespersen conversation, Trudeau said Albertans were being fooled by their own government and by online sharpies into disagreeing with his efforts to decarbonise Canada's economy.
He insisted we were being duped into not seeing how he was the true defender of our best interests, rather than the oil companies or Danielle Smith's government. For their own electoral and commercial self-interests, Trudeau claimed, these evil forces were conning people "to vote in ways that are not necessarily in their best interest."
Yet listeners had to wonder who was playing the fool? Trudeau insisted oil was rapidly becoming an unprofitable commodity, so Albertans'best chance for a prosperous future was to get behind such grandiose ideas as his net-zero power grid and his belief that pipelines are things of the past.
However, the International Energy Agency expects world oil demand to grow by a million barrels a day this year and to continue to grow until at least 2040, perhaps 2050.
In the working lifetimes of most Albertans, alternate energies will not provide the employment benefits of good old oil and natural gas. So who truly has Albertans'best interests at heart? Even while insisting he wasn't one of the "eastern bastards," Trudeau told listeners that Albertans were being manipulated by "conspiracy theorists" and pedlars of misinformation.
That's shorthand for "Hey, you hicks. I'm smarter than you." You need me and my government to guide you and regulate what you see online to keep you from harming yourselves, the planet and democracy.
Usually it's the people who see invisible plots and imaginary enemies, as Trudeau does, who need the help. lgunter@postmedia.com @sunlornegunter

Document 231 of 356

GUNTER: Trudeau's Alberta trip full of imaginary enemies

Author: Chau, Eddie

Publication info: Chau, Eddie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/gunter-trudeaus-alberta-trip-full-imaginary/docview/2931336763/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=GUNTER%3A+Trudeau%27s+Alberta+trip+full+of+imaginary+enemies&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Chau%2C+Eddie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Trudeau's trip to Alberta this week to announce $175 million in federal grants for housing starts probably did for federal credibility here (already at a low) what his 2018 Mr. Dressup Goes to Bollywood tour did for Canada-India relations.
Trudeau came without informing the provincial government. That's not exactly a legal necessity. This is, after all, his country, too. However, it is considered a courtesy for federal ministers to inform their provincial counterparts in advance.
Not telling the UCP government was deliberate.
Also, no provincial minister or MLA was invited to the housing announcement. Trudeau's lone Edmonton Liberal MP, Randy Boissonnault, was there. So too was his old friend, Mayor Amarjeet Sohi, who served in Trudeau's cabinet during its first term.
But, despite the fact that both the federal and provincial governments have largely put aside their differences to cooperate on housing affordability, there was no provincial representative in sight.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
And while the PM had no time for a get-together with Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, he and his staff did manage to find time for a 30-minute sit-down with "progressive" Edmonton podcaster, Ryan Jespersen.
It was the Jespersen interview that created the greatest anger among Albertans. Trudeau was at one and the same time arrogant, delusional, dismissive and insulting.
Time and again his answers betrayed his inner belief that his government's current unpopularity isn't its own fault. Poor polling has little to do with Liberal policies - carbon tax, EV mandate, inflation, housing and immigration - and everything to do with how others, notably the opposition Conservatives and online journalists, are being mean to them.
In Trudeau's mind, everything is a "comms" (communications) problem.
You can witness that thinking in last summer's cabinet shuffle, which changed no Liberal policies only the mouthpieces attempting to communicate them to voters. And you can see it with the recent rebranding of the Climate Action Initiative Payment to the Canada Carbon Rebate. Surely the only reason 100% of Canadians aren't behind the Liberals' noble scheme is that they were too stupid to recognize what the old name was when they saw it on their bank statements.
Then Trudeau turned to insulting the intelligence of Albertans specifically.
In a patronizing tone that lasted throughout his Jespersen conversation, Trudeau said Albertans were being fooled by their own government and by online sharpies into disagreeing with his efforts to decarbonize Canada's economy.
He insisted we were being duped into not seeing how he was the true defender of our best interests, rather than the oil companies or Danielle Smith's government. For their own electoral and commercial self-interests, Trudeau claimed, these evil forces were conning people "to vote in ways that are not necessarily in their best interest."
Yet listeners had to wonder who was playing the fool?
Trudeau insisted oil was rapidly becoming an unprofitable commodity, so Albertans' best chance for a prosperous future was to get behind such grandiose ideas as his net-zero power grid and his belief that pipelines are things of the past.
However, the International Energy Agency expects world oil demand to grow by a million barrels a day this year and to continue to grow until at least 2040, perhaps 2050.
In the working lifetimes of most Albertans, alternate energies will not provide the employment benefits of good old oil and natural gas. So who truly has Albertans' best interests at heart?
Even while insisting he wasn't one of the "eastern bastards," Trudeau told listeners that Albertans were being manipulated by "conspiracy theorists" and pedlars of misinformation.
That's shorthand for "Hey, you hicks. I'm smarter than you." You need me and my government to guide you and regulate what you see online to keep you from harming yourselves, the planet and democracy.
Usually, it's the people who see invisible plots and imaginary enemies, as Trudeau does, who need the help.

Last call for Legion; Woodbridge branch shut down over reported biker gang ties

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/last-call-legion-woodbridge-branch-shut-down-over/docview/2931240112/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Last+call+for+Legion%3B+Woodbridge+branch+shut+down+over+reported+biker+gang+ties&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Greater Toronto Area Royal Canadian Legion has been shut down due to its "overt association" with outlaw motorcycle gangs.
The decision to shutter Branch 414 - also known as the MacKenzie branch - was delivered in a letter to local branch members dated Feb. 12.
"It is with great sorrow that I inform you that the charter of the MacKenzie branch of the Royal Canadian Legion, Ontario Provincial Command has been revoked due to thebranch'sovertassociationwithoutlawmotorcyclegroups,"said the letter, obtained by the Toronto Sun and signed by Legion provincial president Derek Moore. "The overt wearing of outlaw motorcycle club colours at Legion events and premises is contrary to the Legion's Articles of Faith and disrespectful of the sacrifices made by our Veterans."
MacKenzie members can transfer to nearby Legion branches, the letter said.
Reached for comment Friday afternoon, Pam Sweeny, executive director of the Legion's Ontario Command, referred to the Feb. 12 letter.
This is the first time biker gang members have found their way onto a Legion branch's membership list, Sweeny reportedly told the CBC.
The same report said the Woodbridge branch's former president and for mertre asurer were charged last year with fraud over $5,000 and possession of property obtained by crime.
One of those charged with fraud in the case was Josie Fedele, a staffer at the time in the constituency office of King-Vaughan MPP and Minister of Education Stephen Lecce.
Lecce said at the time Fedele's employment was "immediately suspended."
According to Newmarket Today, the other person charged in the case is also known as Nick Pinto, a former PC Riding Association president, former director and treasurer of the Royal Canadian Legion Branch 414, and Vaughan council candidate in 2018 and '22.
Police alleged more than $33,000 was stolen from the branch between 2017 and '19.
There is reportedly no known link between the theft and biker-gang infiltration.
Branch 414, which had operated for 75 years, was locked up earlier this week.
It wasn't immediately clear which biker gangs were using the branch, or for how long, and for what purpose.
In its letter, the Legion's Ontario Command said it was tipped off by police that some of its members appeared to be bikers.
It wasn't clear which police agencies might be involved in the probe, but York Regional Police haven't been involved with the investigation, according to the CBC report.
The Legion opened its investigation last year and the branch's charter was suspended in October.
"We spent days, weeks and months gathering information and discussing the final decision on the MacKenzie branch and concluded that revocation was the only choice," Moore said in the letter. "This was an unprecedented situation and one which I hope we never experience again.
"I thank you all for your understanding and patience while we worked our way through this issue ... " The building and its contents will be sold, officials said, and, after debts are paid, any remaining money will "go toward veteran-centric and community programs and supports."
- Sun staff

Woodbridge Legion branch shut down due to 'overt association' with outlaw biker gangs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/woodbridge-legion-branch-shut-down-due-overt/docview/2931167298/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Woodbridge+Legion+branch+shut+down+due+to+%27overt+association%27+with+outlaw+biker+gangs&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Greater Toronto Area Royal Canadian Legion has been shut down due to its "overt association" with outlaw motorcycle gangs.
The decision to shutter Branch 414 - also known as the MacKenzie branch - was delivered in a letter to local branch members dated Feb. 12.
"It is with great sorrow that I inform you that the charter of the MacKenzie branch of the Royal Canadian Legion, Ontario Provincial Command has been revoked due to the branch's overt association with outlaw motorcycle groups," said the letter, obtained by the Sun and signed by the Legion's provincial president, Derek Moore.
"The overt wearing of outlaw motorcycle club colours at Legion events and premises is contrary to the Legion's Articles of Faith and disrespectful of the sacrifices made by our Veterans."
MacKenzie members can transfer to nearby Legion branches, the letter said.
A letter sent to members of Legion branch 414 in Woodbridge.
Reached for comment on Friday afternoon, Pam Sweeny, executive director of the Legion's Ontario Command, referred to the Feb. 12 letter.
This is the first time biker gang members have found their way onto a Legion branch's membership list, Sweeny reportedly told the CBC.
The same report said the Woodbridge branch's former president and former treasurer were charged last year with fraud over $5,000 and possession of property obtained by crime.
One of those charged with fraud in the case was Josie Fedele, a staffer at the time in the constituency office of King-Vaughan MPP and Minister of Education Stephen Lecce.
Lecce said at the time that Fedele's employment was "immediately suspended."
According to Newmarket Today, the other person charged in the case is also known as Nick Pinto, a former PC Riding Association president, former director and treasurer of the Royal Canadian Legion Branch 414, and Vaughan council candidate in 2018 and 2022.
Police alleged more than $33,000 was stolen from the branch between 2017 and 2019. There is reportedly no known link between the theft and biker-gang infiltration.
GUNTER: We will always pray for those who fought for us
Scarborough woman's poppy masks support local legion
Branch 414, which had operated for 75 years, was locked up earlier this week.
It was not immediately clear which biker gangs were using the branch, or for how long, and for what purpose.
In its letter, the Legion's Ontario Command said it was tipped off by police that some of its members appeared to be bikers.
It is not clear which police agencies might be involved in the probe, however, York Regional Police have not been involved with the investigation, according to the CBC report.
The Legion opened its investigation last year and the branch's charter was suspended in October 2023.
"We spent days, weeks and months gathering information and discussing the final decision on the MacKenzie branch and concluded that revocation was the only choice," Moore said in the letter.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
"This was an unprecedented situation and one which I hope we never experience again," Moore added. "I thank you all for your understanding and patience while we worked our way through this issue, and I take this opportunity to wish you all the best of luck in your new branch."
The building and its contents will be sold, officials said, and after debts are paid, any remaining money will "go toward veteran-centric and community programs and supports."
Branch 414 was established in 1949.
Anyone who called the Woodbridge branch on Friday would have heard a simple voicemail greeting: "Unfortunately, this branch is temporarily closed until further notice."
Legion halls have long been used as gathering spots for veterans of Canada's military, but membership has dwindled in recent years, in large part due to war veterans aging.

Document 235 of 356

CAPTIJN: New home buyers flounder in complex legislation

Author: McLeod, Cynthia

Publication info: McLeod, Cynthia. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/captijn-new-home-buyers-flounder-complex/docview/2931164167/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=CAPTIJN%3A+New+home+buyers+flounder+in+complex+legislation&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=McLeod%2C+Cynthia&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Many of us have had the experience where household tasks are delegated to several siblings as a group, but if no specifics are given on who does what, the buck gets passed back and forth, and tasks often don't get done.
If everyone is responsible, no one is.
Consumer protection for new home buyers in Ontario is a complex and confusing patchwork of laws, and often leads to a similar blame-game when construction defects are discovered in newly-built homes. If Ontario wants to encourage people to buy more new homes, protections have to be simplified, streamlined and subject to one authority, not several.
Most people have never heard of the government agencies Tarion and the Home Construction Regulatory Authority (HCRA), or the Ministry of Public and Business Service Delivery (PBSD), which oversees them. One would naturally look to the Consumer Protection Act to protect new home buyers, but it doesn't cover that. It oddly only pertains to timeshare purchases.
Condo and freehold homes are covered under other legislation called the New Home Warranties Plan Act, the New Home Construction Licensing Act, the Condo Act, and a complex document called the Addendum, described in 2022 by a Superior Court judge in 2022 as a "convoluted and confusingly long and obscure document."
CAPTIJN: Give new home buyers a voice at the policy table
CAPTIJN: Government agency has lost its way
New OREA report calls for bold action to build homes
BILD: More is needed to incentivize the construction of new rental housing supply
Then there's the 40-50 page new home purchase contract itself, written by developers and their lawyers. Some contracts include so-called weasel clauses, for example saying the purchaser can't join class-action lawsuits, or can't talk about any disputes with the developer, or their contract could be cancelled and the deposit forfeited.
Even if you successfully navigate the tricky contract stage, what happens if you find a crack in your new home's basement floor, water infiltration or mould? That's when the two arms-length agencies of government, Tarion and HCRA come in.
The run-around you may get goes like this: First you contact the the builder for a resolution; he's supposed to warrant the home is free of defects and built according to the Building Code. If he fails to fix the problems or denies there's a problem, you contact Tarion. They contact the builder, and give him time to respond or repair. If he refuses, Tarion can compensate you, but you have to find your own contractor to fix the issues. Some homeowners contact the municipality to see if proper inspections were done during construction, but often the municipality sends the homeowner back to Tarion, who then goes back to the builder, and the pass-the-buck goes on.
Tarion acts as a third party - they don't provide a warranty, it's the builder's warranty.
This has been confusing so the Auditor General of Ontario in 2019 instructed Tarion to remove the word warranty from its name, because it doesn't offer a warranty. A year-long judge's review of Tarion in 2017 recommended new home warranties be provided by real warranty companies regulated under the Insurance Act, as in Alberta and B.C.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
Consumer advocates and Opposition MPPs agreed. But free-market Premier Doug Ford ignored this recommendation, while saying he's against government running monopolies, which is what Tarion is.
Most new home warranty companies that operate in B.C. and Alberta for example, insure the builder, and then take over contact with the homeowner if there are deficiencies, and the builder hasn't taken action. There's no third party go-between, as here in Ontario.
To add to the years of confusion about Tarion's role, their CEO recently stated in a branding podcast called Unpacked that Tarion is "kind of an insurance." But the 2017 judge's review stated, "Tarion is not regulated as an insurance company," and is not "regulated as an insurance product."
Also baffling was the CEO's claim that "you don't have to buy it, everyone gets it," referring to the Tarion fee. But you do pay for the Tarion fee, it's mandatory, and passed on to the homebuyer in the purchase price.
In a subsequent episode of the podcast, two Tarion executives said purchasers need to "ask questions, ask more questions." But with a 40-50 page contract and a complex legislative framework, how do you know what questions to ask? To put the burden on the homebuyer for consumer protection is not fair or realistic.
Another suggestion by Tarion executives was to search online reviews of builders. They know some homeowners have been threatened with defamation suits for speaking out about problem builders. The HCRA Builder Directory, which is supposed to be a research tool for future home buyers, is of little more use than a telephone book.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
The courts are also not the answer to consumer protection. Most individuals don't have the financial resources and time off work to go through a lengthy, complex legal fight with builders or government agencies.
Each year in May, consumer advocates are reminded of the tragic suicide of Earl Shuman, who fought a 27-year battle to get his new home fixed. He was put in jail for a week for not paying his opponent's legal fees. The late journalist Christie Blatchford wrote a moving story about his epic battle in the National Post on Sept. 24, 2015, entitled "Homeowner's Kafkaesque Legal Battle with Builder and Warranty Agency."
Earl summed up the problem, which was as true then as it is now: "It's the chaos in the legislation."
Premier Ford, who likes to say he's a businessman not a politician, should end this third party go-between system which doesn't provide a warranty. Give new home buyers the protections they pay for under a real warranty system, open to competition, as is now used in both B.C. and Alberta, and internationally.
It's up to the ministry responsible for consumer protection, the PBSD, to take action without more years of delay and more damage to consumers.
As Earl once said, "I don't blame the players, I blame the referee."
In memory of Earl Shuman, 1951-2016.
- Barbara Captijn is a Consumer Advocate @ReformTarion

Subject: Government agencies; Legislation; Third party; Consumerism; Homeowners; Consumer protection

Business indexing term: Subject: Consumer protection

Identifier / keyword: experience; household; tasks; delegated; several; siblings

Title: CAPTIJN: New home buyers flounder in complex legislation

Publication title: The Toronto Sun (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 23, 2024

Section: Columnists

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 2931164167

Document 236 of 356

'Out to lunch'; Preem wonders where fed's head's at

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/out-lunch-preem-wonders-where-feds-heads-at/docview/2931055788/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Out+to+lunch%27%3B+Preem+wonders+where+fed%27s+head%27s+at&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford calls Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault every name in the book, mockingly referring to him as Canada's actual prime minister.
It seems the road rage between the governing federal Liberals and provincial conservatives has jumped from the street right to the airwaves.
Questioning whether it's the climate change zealots running Canada, and not Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Ford vowed no matter how powerful the environmental activists are, they stop Ontario from driving into the future.
Nor will they stop the province from building a road that will take them to precious minerals up north.
"We are going to build the road with them or without them," Ford said Thursday while appearing in studio with John Oakley on AM640.
Rare fighting words from the premier when speaking about the federal government. He also questioned if the Liberals think the province needs to go back to "horseback or bicycles."
Ford's main target was Guilbeault, who this week had to backpedal on comments suggesting Ottawa will no longer fund road building projects.
"This guy is out to lunch as far as I am concerned," Ford said. "Who's running the country, Justin Trudeau or Guilbeault?" "Right now, I don't know," he added.
It's certainly a change in tone from the complimentary language he normally reserves for his federal counterparts.
But the premier said whether it's the Hwy. 413 project or plans to build roads north to the Ring of Fire, the citizens he speaks with are all in and new Ontario Liberal party Leader Bonnie Crombie will see that come election time.
"They are going to lose," he said of the Liberals if they go down the road of stopping the billions in transportation spending. "We won every seat in that area."
However, when it comes to how he will be involved in the next federal election, Ford said focus on Ontario. can work with anybody," he said, adding even "Donald Duck."
As for federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, he said he has "met him once" and he will have to win the election himself because Ontario conservatives won't be campaigning with him.
"We will be staying out of it," Ford said.
Ford said he has more than enough work on his hands with building new homes, hiring more health care workers and growing the economy.
While the appearance on Oakley was a flashback to the days when he and his late brother, former Toronto mayor Rob Ford, would appear on the AM640 airwaves, it also offered a rare look into how the premier tries to operate as a human being.
"I try to get out of the bubble," Ford said, explaining he tries to shake off his OPP security detail and get out to where the people are in malls and box stores.
"I love hearing what they have to say," he said.
But his biggest message was aimed at the environment minister.
"This is the same guy, Guilbeault, who chains himself to trees and the CN Tower. I mean, he's an extremist," Ford said. jwarmington@postmedia.com @joe_warmington

Subject: Prime ministers; Climate change; Roads & highways

Location: Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin
