Document 237 of 356

Reading, writing and racial grievances; Divisive politics must be pushed out of the education system for good

Author: Li lley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/reading-writing-racial-grievances-divisive/docview/2931055633/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reading%2C+writing+and+racial+grievances%3B+Divisive+politics+must+be+pushed+out+of+the+education+system+for+good&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When it comes to education in Ontario, the inmates are truly running the asylum. While the Doug Ford government has spent the last several years pushing an approach that goes back to the basics and focuses on student achievement, the people running the system have other ideas.
Now boards are even pushing back against orders from the Ministry of Education to cease and desist from using divisive, radical race-based political documents.
The latest example is the document put out by the Toronto District School Board titled "Facilitating Critical Conversations, a Teaching Resource for Challenging Oppression in Toronto District School Board Classrooms."
This is the document that claimed education is "a colonial structure that centres whiteness and Eurocentricity and therefore it must be actively decolonized." It also said that education favours white males and heterosexuals and "white supremacy is a structural reality that impacts all students and must be discussed and dismantled in classrooms, schools and communities."
Nothing like dividing people along race lines to try to end racism and bring everyone together.
On Wednesday, the office of Education Minister Stephen Lecce told the TDSB that this document is divisive and unacceptable, and the board promised to withdraw the document. Yet by late Wednesday, the TDSB put out a statement saying that the document would be "temporarily removed" and edited for future use.
The entire document is problematic and the ministry has said so, but the board is determined to move forward with this radical ideology.
This isn't new. We've seen it before in previous attempts to politicize education with radical ideology, such as the 2021 changes to the math curriculum that the Ford government had to reverse.
"Mathematics has been used to normalize racism and marginalization of non-Eurocentric mathematical knowledges and a decolonial, anti-racist approach to mathematics education makes visible its historical roots and social constructions," the revised curriculum said.
"In math, let's stick with math," the premier said at the time, while saying that portion of the curriculum would be removed.
Ford's view is the same as most parents, most teachers even, but the problem is that radical activists have taken up key leadership positions in the education system, from the teacher unions to the consultant class within school boards. These are the people who push this radical racebased agenda in every aspect of the school system.
Last year, the math lead for high school teachers at the TDSB, Jason To, led a webinar that put forward the idea that saying "2 + 2 = 4" was a covert example of white supremacy. This wasn't just a Toronto issue since To was backed up by teachers from Ottawa, Manitoulin Island and elsewhere across the province.
It's not just on race. It's on gender, class, politics and any number of other issues in which far-left politics are pushed into the classroom by activist teachers, activist curriculum consultants and activist principals.
Lecce has described efforts to deal with these outbursts as akin to playing whack-a-mole - you strike one down and a new radical or collection of radicals rise up.
We can talk about racism in our classrooms, and we can discuss gender issues, class issues and even politics, but it should not be the indoctrination of students by one radical ideology, which unfortunately is what is happening time and again.
If parents are losing faith in the education system, this is part of the reason why. Parents send their children to school for an education and not an indoctrination, but the activists think otherwise.
It is parents, not the activist edu-crats, who Lecce and the rest of the Ford government should listen to. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: White supremacy; Classrooms; Ideology; Political activism; Racism; Activists; Curricula; Mathematics teachers; Indoctrination; School boards; Mathematics education; Eurocentrism; Race

Company / organization: Name: Toronto District School Board; NAICS: 611110

Identifier / keyword: comes; education; ontario; inmates; truly; running

Document 238 of 356

Choking on carbon tax; It's a clear loser when other Liberals won't defend it

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/choking-on-carbon-tax-clear-loser-when-other/docview/2931055006/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Choking+on+carbon+tax%3B+It%27s+a+clear+loser+when+other+Liberals+won%27t+defend+it&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It appears that Justin Trudeau's carbon tax is so unpopular that other Liberals won't even say if they support it.
Meanwhile, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre continues to ride high in the polls with his promise to axe the tax.
It wasn't that long ago that in certain political circles a carbon tax was seen as a no-brainer.
Now it's just a loser. Bonnie Crombie, elected as leader of the Ontario Liberals at the beginning of December, denied support for Trudeau's tax several times in what can only be seen as a disastrous first week at Queen's Park.
Crombie was asked about her position on a carbon tax at least seven times during a news conference at Queen's Park and couldn't give an answer or opinion beyond saying she'd consult. Then she went on CTV's Power Play and refused to answer questions about her position on a carbon tax at least nine times.
While at Queen's Park, she was asked about a provincial tax. On CTV, Crombie wouldn't even answer questions about the federal carbon tax.
Imagine that: The leader of the Liberal party in Canada's biggest province can't even endorse a signature policy of the federal Liberal party - the carbon tax.
There was a time when we were told you couldn't win in Canadian politics unless you had a plan to put a price on carbon with a tax, capand-trade system or some other scheme. Of course, this was never true, it was just the view of the urban Laurentian Elite who populate much of the media and chattering classes.
Doug Ford handily won Ontario's election in 2018 promising to rip up the Liberal cap-andtrade system. Jason Kenney swept to power in Alberta promising to eliminate the NDP carbon tax in that province.
There also has never been a carbon tax provincially in Saskatchewan, Manitoba, Nova Scotia, Prince Edward Now, even at the federal level, the carbon tax that Trudeau still boasts of is far from popular and Poilievre is holding onto a substantial lead in the polls with his promise to axe the tax. Polling even shows that more people think the tax hurts them financially compared to those who think it benefits them - which is the government's ongoing false message.
An Abacus Data poll of 2,199 adults taken in January showed that 43% of Canadians thought they'd save money if the carbon tax is eliminated, 20% thought they'd lose money and 37% didn't know. A further 47% said they believed that the carbon tax was a "big reason why prices for many things has risen so much." Just 26% disagreed with the idea that the carbon tax was responsible for increasing prices.
In short, the carbon tax, Trudeau's signature policy, has become a political loser.
Close to 20 years ago, a friend of mine in the polling industry said that Canadians were kind of odd on environmental issues. If the economy was going well, they'd say climate change and the environment were among the most important issues facing the country, but they were less interested in those issues as things got worse, as job losses mounted or prices rose.
That is what has happened here and the Trudeau Liberals - and their allies like Crombie in Ontario - haven't realized this and still try to cling to policies that will no longer get them votes.
In fact, holding onto the idea of a carbon tax in 2024 will only drag a political party lower. Let's hope that Trudeau and Crombie hug that carbon tax as tightly as they can. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Prices; Carbon; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax

Identifier / keyword: appears; justin; carbon; unpopular; liberals; support

Document 241 of 356

WARMINGTON: Doug Ford wonders is 'out to lunch' Steven Guilbeault running Canada?

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/warmington-doug-ford-wonders-is-out-lunch-steven/docview/2930954864/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=WARMINGTON%3A+Doug+Ford+wonders+is+%27out+to+lunch%27+Steven+Guilbeault+running+Canada%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford calls Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault every name in the book, mockingly referring to him as Canada's actual prime minister.
It seems the road rage between the governing federal Liberals and provincial conservatives has now jumped from the street right to the airwaves.
Questioning whether it's the climate change zealots running Canada, and not Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Premier Ford vowed no matter how powerful the environmental activists are, they won't stop Ontario from driving into the future.
Nor will they stop the province from building the road that will take them to precious minerals up north.
"We are going to build the road with them or without them," Ford said Thursday while appearing in studio with John Oakley on AM640.
Doug Ford quips he doesn't know who's running the country, Justin Trudeau or Steven Guilbeault#OnPoli #CDNPoli
- 640 Toronto (@am640) February 22, 2024
Rare fighting words from the Premier when speaking about the federal government. He also questioned if the Liberals think the province needs to go back to "horseback or bicycles."
Ford's main target was Guilbeault, who this week had to backpedal on comments suggesting Ottawa will no longer fund road building projects.
"This guy is out to lunch as far as I am concerned," Ford said. "Who's running the country, Justin Trudeau or Guilbeault?"
"Right now, I don't know," he added.
It's certainly a change in tone from the complimentary language he normally reserves for his federal counterparts.
But the Premier said whether it's the Hwy. 413 project or plans to build roads up to the "ring of fire" to mine the minerals needed in the future so battery plants can meet the demand as we shift to electric vehicles, the members of the public he speaks with are all in and new Ontario Liberal Party Leader Bonnie Crombie will see that come election time.
"They are going to lose," he said of the Liberals, if they go down the road of stopping the billions in transportation spending. "We won every seat in that area."
Doug Ford says Bonnie Crombie was incompetent as mayor of Mississauga#OnPoli
- 640 Toronto (@am640) February 22, 2024
However, when it comes to how he will be involved in the next federal election, Ford said he will be focusing on Ontario.
"I can work with anybody," he said, adding even "Donald Duck."
As for federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, he said he has "met him once" and he will have to win the election himself because Ontario conservatives won't be campaigning with him.
"We will be staying out of it," Ford said.
Ford said he has more than enough work on his hands with building new homes, hiring more health care workers and growing the economy.
While the appearance on Oakley was a flashback to the days when he and his late brother - former Toronto mayor Rob Ford - would appear on the AM640 airwaves, it also offered a rare look into how the Premier tries to operate as a human being.
"I try to get out of the bubble," Ford said, explaining he tries to shake off his OPP security detail and get out to where the people are in malls and box stores.
"I love hearing what they have to say," he said.
Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault to be questioned over road funding remarks: Report
Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault to be questioned over road funding remarks: Report
EDITORIAL: On the road with Steven Guilbeault
Ford said he also has continued with the Ford family legacy of trying to return constituent phones calls.
"I am doing that all the time," he said of calling back people who leave him a message.
But his biggest message was directed at the Environment Minister.
"This is the same guy, Guilbeault, who chains himself to trees and the CN Tower. I mean he's an extremist," Ford said.
But the Premier admits that extremist may just very well be running the federal government these days.
jwarmington@postmedia.com

Subject: Extremism; Prime ministers; Climate change; Roads & highways

Liberal MP Sean Fraser begins fundraising campaign, mum on leadership bid: Report

Full text: Housing Minister Sean Fraser, who may have Liberal leadership ambitions, appears to have begun a national fundraising campaign.
According to , citing Elections Canada filings, Fraser's Central Nova Federal Liberal Association held a $500-a-head fundraiser on Feb. 7 at the Calgary Petroleum Club.
The fundraiser was attended by Albertans, including former Calgary mayor Al Duerr and his wife, former Calgary councillor Evan Woolley and Calgary lawyers, developers and oil and gas executives.
Since the 2021 federal election, all but two of 481 past donors to the Central Nova Federal Liberal Association were from Nova Scotia, Quebec and Ontario, according to Elections Canada.
The Nova Scotia MP's office didn't not respond to questions Wednesday about the Calgary fundraiser.
Late last month, Fraser also did not reply when asked at a press conference if he was considering a Liberal leadership bid.

"If polling is to be believed it appears the federal Conservatives are on the march. Make no mistake about it, Pierre Poilievre if successful in the next election would be Canada's most right-leaning Prime Minister ever."
Hehr, who didn't mention Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, called Poilievre "an extremely intelligent and engaging person."

Document 243 of 356

HUDAK: Decision by Ontario Energy Board threatens housing progress

Author: Doucette, Chris

Publication info: Doucette, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hudak-decision-ontario-energy-board-threatens/docview/2929519044/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=HUDAK%3A+Decision+by+Ontario+Energy+Board+threatens+housing+progress&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Doucette%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario Government is at a make-or-break point in the housing affordability crisis.
Ontario's bold goal of building 1.5 million homes by 2031 reflects the urgency to tackle this problem with pro-growth policies. We need all hands-on deck from every level of government - but a recent decision by the Ontario Energy Board (OEB) is about to derail Ontario's housing plans.
Last December, the OEB decided to reduce the budget for Enbridge Gas by $250 million and ended Enbridge's 40-year amortization period for new natural gas connections in homes.
This method allowed consumers to pay down the cost of new natural gas connections in their home over an extended period of time.
The OEB's bad move to upend Ontario's long-standing approach to finance infrastructure like natural gas over time puts new neighbourhoods and desperately needed new homes in jeopardy.
To sustain community growth, Enbridge needs to have the funds to extend natural gas lines across the province. Under the current model, the costs of new connections are amortized over 40 years, across all users. That means more communities get connected and more homes get built.
New OREA report calls for bold action to build homes
Over half of Ontarians say housing affordability a top priority: Poll
BILD: More is needed to incentivize the construction of new rental housing supply
If the shortsighted OEB decision goes through, fewer new connections will be made and fewer homes will be built. Those that do get built will be more expensive and homebuyers will need to pay the entire hookup cost upfront, adding thousands to the price of a home.
This head-scratching overstep by the OEB will push affordability further out of reach for Ontarians, and put provincial and municipal housing targets at risk. Such one-size-fits-all policies will be particularly harmful to Ontario's smaller and northern communities, where energy infrastructure is not well-developed.
There is a dangerous knock-on effect of the OEB's bad call. In 2022, I was part of Premier Doug Ford's Housing Affordability Task Force. We recommended the province create a municipal services utility corporation model for water and wastewater. This model allows the cost of new infrastructure, like sewer lines, to be financed over time - like a mortgage - offering greater financial flexibility and keeping the price tag of utility infrastructure from reaching homeowners. This new approach alone could take up to $50,000 off the price of a new home.
In OREA's recent report, Analysis of Ontario's Efforts to Boost Housing Supply, we are calling to get this done and bring affordability closer to home.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
Sadly, the OEB decision to eliminate long-term financing for natural gas risks sidelining this valuable tool for water and sewer construction as well. The Ontario Government needs to reverse the OEB decision and ensure that additional barriers aren't created for aspiring homeowners.
OEB's role is regulation, not policymaking. The Minister of Energy is contemplating legislation to fix this mess and Ontario REALTORS® support him. The Ford Government should get it done.
As Ontario works to build 1.5 million homes by 2031, the need for natural gas will only continue to grow. To support affordable homeownership, I encourage you to reach out to your MPP and express your support for long-term financing of Ontario's natural gas lines, water, and wastewater.
If we want to create more Canadian homeowners, we should not whack them with this massive upfront bill for infrastructure that will last for generations.
- Tim Hudak is the CEO of the Ontario Real Estate Association, Canada's largest provincial real estate association

Subject: Infrastructure; Housing needs; Northern communities; Natural gas

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure

Document 244 of 356

Political mismatch; Ford benefiting from opposition leaders' weak start to session

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/political-mismatch-ford-benefiting-opposition/docview/2929296819/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Political+mismatch%3B+Ford+benefiting+from+opposition+leaders%27+weak+start+to+session&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Successful politicians who win multiple elections often do so because they face weak opposition leaders. That was true for Doug Ford before Bonnie Crombie was elected to lead the Ontario Liberals and it looks like it's still true today.
Ford has been spending a lot of time talking about Crombie lately, including dubbing her the "Queen of the Carbon Tax." It's a name Crombie has rejected, saying she hasn't spoken out in favour of the carbon tax. But, on Tuesday, she fell flat when asked about her position.
"Can you clarify your position on the carbon tax?" reporter Jessica Smith Cross, from news website The Trillium, asked Crombie.
It's a perfectly reasonable question and given all of the attention Ford has been placing on Crombie and the carbon tax, she should have been ready to answer. In some ways, she was ready as she looked down and read her carefully scripted response from the page in front of her, but Crombie's response clarified nothing. She offered a rambling attack on Ford before promising consultations.
"We are going to consult with our residents, with stakeholders, with scientists and Ontario families and we will develop a strong and a progressive plan to urgently tackle the issue of climate change," Crombie said.
"It's hard to imagine an adult these days having not formed an opinion at this point in their lives on whether carbon taxes are a good thing or not. Why are you not sharing your opinion with us, or do you actually not have one?" Smith Cross responded.
Crombie again said she'd consult and come back with a "strong and progressive plan" for fighting climate change. Pushed several more times, Crombie wouldn't say yes or no on whether she supports a carbon tax or opposes one.
This should have been simple for Crombie. She shouldn't have had to read from the page. She shouldn't have sounded unsure. She could have simply said that since Ford ripped up Ontario's cap-and-trade program that the federal carbon tax has been put in place and there is no need for a provincial one.
Instead, she walked into the trap Ford had set for her with his gimmicky legislation to require a referendum on a future carbon tax. It was a very weak first day for Crombie, who hasn't moved her party in the polls despite improving Liberal fundraising somewhat.
As for the Official Opposition, Marit Stiles of the NDP showed once again why her party isn't ever getting near power.
Stiles opened the first question period in more than two months asking the Ford government about Bill 124, the wage bill recently struck down by the courts. This is an issue that is only a vote-mover for a segment of the public service that is already voting for the NDP.
The only way the NDP can move from the opposition benches to the government side is by expanding its reach beyond unionized government workers. Yet, given the chance to ask about any other issue, it once again focused on the same issue it has been harping on for five years.
Stiles could have asked about the crisis in primary care and the shortage of family doctors, the problems in the daycare system, any number of issues affecting the lives of average Ontario residents, but she didn't.
Most days, the NDP at Queen's Park sounds like it's a lobby group for government workers and that's it.
Ford's government isn't running on all cylinders at this point. It has had missteps and many of the problems it has promised to fix, like housing affordability and supply, haven't been resolved.
What Ford has going for him is that his opponents are weak, and they both showed it on Monday. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Government employees; Carbon; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax

Identifier / keyword: successful; politicians; multiple; elections; opposition; leaders

Title: Political mismatch; Ford benefiting from opposition leaders' weak start to session

LILLEY: Ford benefitting from opposition leaders' weak start to session

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-ford-benefitting-opposition-leaders-weak/docview/2929166150/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Ford+benefitting+from+opposition+leaders%27+weak+start+to+session&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 246 of 356

Preem's gripped by Grit; Gimmicky new bills signal Ford fixation on Liberal rival

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/preems-gripped-grit-gimmicky-new-bills-signal/docview/2928717190/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Preem%27s+gripped+by+Grit%3B+Gimmicky+new+bills+signal+Ford+fixation+on+Liberal+rival&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I didn't think the Ford government would get to the point of passing gimmicky legislation so quickly, but here we are.
Often, governments pass legislation that means nothing but might excite part of their base or generate positive headlines when they are on their way out.
In the last week, the Ford PCs have promised to introduce two bills to ban future governments from taking specific actions, which means they are bills that mean absolutely nothing.
Well, they're meaningless except for trying to make things difficult politically for their new opponent, Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie.
First, Ford took great joy in standing at a Pioneer gas station in Mississauga and announcing that any future government would need to have a referendum to impose a provincial carbon tax.
Now, Ford is promising to ban all future Ontario governments from bringing in new toll roads. The 407 will, of course, be exempt.
One of the central tenets of our Westminster parliamentary system is that no government can bind the hands of a future government.
You can't pass a law telling a future government, in a future Parliament, how they must act. The government of the day can simply ignore it and pass a new law that will, of course, take precedence over the existing law.
This is why, in reality, bills requiring a balanced budget or a fixed election date are meaningless.
In 2007, the government of Conservative Prime Minister Stephen Harper passed a fixed election date law calling for votes every four years and then promptly ignored it, calling an election the next year. Harper broke that law that he passed the very next year in 2008 and won more seats than he had previously.
The federal Liberals denounced Harper's move but, of course, in 2021, just two years after the previous election, Justin Trudeau called an early election in the middle of a pandemic hoping for a majority.
Neither the actions of Trudeau nor Harper were illegal because of that central tenet: You can't bind the hands of a future Parliament. Sure, the government or the majority of MPs can accept and endorse what you have previously said or called for, but there is no requirement to do so.
Still, politicians love to try to push though such laws even though they know they are effectively meaningless. It's often a way to try to stay relevant, which at this point isn't something the Ford government needs to be doing.
Ford and his PC party won re-election in June 2022, just 20 months ago, with a bigger majority than they received from voters in Right now, they are also continuing to ride high in the public opinion polls that we can see.
The most recent Abacus Data poll, released in January, shows the Ford PCs with 38% support, the Ontario Liberals with 27% support and the NDP with 23%. The numbers remained virtually unchanged from the poll Abacus released in December just after Crombie won the Ontario Liberal leadership.
So far, Crombie hasn't moved her own party's polling numbers, but she has obviously spooked Ford, who has taken to calling her "the queen of the carbon tax."
Outside of election periods, Ford's PCs haven't spent much time talking about the NDP or their leader, Marit Stiles. As MPPs return to Queen's Park on Tuesday, Ford is focused on a party leader who doesn't even have a seat in the legislature and whose Liberals don't have official party standing.
Crombie has changed the focus in Ontario politics. Whether she can change the trajectory of her party and the province is a bigger test.
Right now, though, she has Ford's attention. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Leadership; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Environmental tax

Identifier / keyword: think; government; point; passing; gimmicky; legislation

Title: Preem's gripped by Grit; Gimmicky new bills signal Ford fixation on Liberal rival

One of the central tenets of our Westminster Parliamentary system is that no current government can bind the hands of a future government.
You can't pass a law telling a future government, in a future parliament, how they must act. The government of the day can simply ignore it and pass a new law which will, of course, take precedence over the existing law.
This is why, in reality, bills requiring a balanced budget, or a fixed election date, are meaningless.
In 2007, the government of Conservative Prime Minister Stephen Harper passed a fixed election date law calling for elections every four years and then promptly ignored it, calling an election the next year. Harper broke that law that he passed the very next year in 2008 and won more seats than he had previously.
The federal Liberals denounced Harper's move but, of course, in 2021, just two years after the previous election, Justin Trudeau called an early election in the middle of a pandemic hoping for a majority.
Neither the actions of Trudeau nor Harper were illegal because of that central tenet - you can't bind the hands of a future Parliament. Sure, the government or the majority of MPs can accept and endorse what you have previously said or called for, but there is no requirement to do so.
Still, politicians love to try to push though such laws even though they know they are effectively meaningless. It's often a way to try to stay relevant, which at this point isn't something the Ford government needs to be doing.
Ford and his PC Party won re-election in June 2022, just 20 months ago, with a bigger majority than they received from voters in 2018. Right now, they are also continuing to ride high in the public opinion polls that we can see.
The most recent Abacus Data poll, released in January, shows the Ford PCs with 38% support, the Ontario Liberals with 27% support and the NDP with 23%. The numbers remained virtually unchanged from the poll that Abacus released in December just after Crombie won the Ontario Liberal leadership.
So far, Crombie hasn't moved her own party's polling numbers, but she has obviously spooked Ford, who has taken to calling her "the queen of the carbon tax."
Outside of election periods, Ford's PCs haven't spent much time talking about the NDP or their leader, Marit Stiles. As MPPs return to Queen's Park on Tuesday, Ford is focused on a party leader who doesn't even have a seat in the legislature and whose party doesn't have official party standing.
Crombie has changed the focus in Ontario politics, whether she can change the trajectory of her party and the province is a bigger test.
Right now, though, she has Ford's attention.
blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Leadership; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Environmental tax

Identifier / keyword: think; government; point; passing; gimmicky; legislation

Title: LILLEY: Ford's gimmicky new bills sign he's focused on Bonnie Crombie

Document 248 of 356

It won't make a difference; Painful truth about Trudeau's 'rebranded' carbon tax

Author: Goldstein, Lorrie

Publication info: Goldstein, Lorrie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/wont-make-difference-painful-truth-about-trudeaus/docview/2928069916/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It+won%27t+make+a+difference%3B+Painful+truth+about+Trudeau%27s+%27rebranded%27+carbon+tax&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Goldstein%2C+Lorrie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's absurd that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and the Liberals believe that rebranding their "climate action incentive payments" as "Canada carbon rebates" is what's needed to fix their carbon tax.
What Canadians deserve is the truth about Trudeau's carbon tax, which they've never been given by the Liberals.
Consider: The Liberals say if Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre axes the carbon tax, people will lose the rebates.
In reality, if there's no carbon tax, there's no need for rebates.
The Liberals describe their carbon tax as a "price on pollution."
It's not a price on pollution.
It's a price on almost all goods and services Canadians pay for because almost all goods and services are produced using fossil fuel energy, including necessities, such as heating homes in winter.
The Liberals claim 80% of households in the eight provinces paying the carbon tax (Quebec and British Columbia have separate systems) receive more in rebates than they pay in carbon taxes.
In fact, Parliamentary Budget Officer Yves Giroux says that when the negative impact of the carbon tax on the economy is factored in, 60% of households currently pay more in carbon taxes than they receive in rebates, which will rise to 80% in Nova Scotia in 2025, 80% in Ontario in 2026, 80% in Manitoba in 2029 and 80% in Alberta and Prince Edward Island in 2030.
Here are the PBO's estimated average net costs (after rebates) for households in provinces under the federal carbon tax regime this year, with the carbon tax at $65 per tonne of emissions, followed by the estimated net cost in 2030, when it will be $170 per tonne: Alberta $710, $2,773; Ontario: $478, $1,820; Saskatchewan $410, $1723; Manitoba $386, $1490; Nova Scotia $431, $1,513; P.E.I $465, $1,521; and Newfoundland and Labrador $347, $1,316.
The PBO's calculations didn't include New Brunswick because it joined the federal carbon tax system after it did these estimates.
The federal carve-out on home heating oil will reduce the costs in Atlantic Canada to some extent, but all households will pay more when the Trudeau government hikes the carbon tax by 23% to $80 per tonne of emissions, from the current $65 per tonne, on April 1.
The Trudeau government argues that the PBO failed to factor in the economic costs of not addressing climate change.
Little impact
In fact, the PBO says, "Canada's own emissions (at 1.5% of the global total) are not large enough to materially impact climate change" and, "consequently, Canada's primary means of limiting the economic costs of climate change are through participation in a globally coordinated emissions reduction regime."
That means we're relying on countries like China - the world's largest emitter, which burns more coal than the rest of the world combined - to lower their emissions.
The idea that the increased costs faced by Canadians paying Trudeau's carbon tax up to 2030 - we don't know what happens after that - will result in savings because of less severe weather in Canada by 2030, when our emissions aren't large enough to materially impact climate change, is absurd.
The Trudeau government says its carbon tax is revenue neutral, with 90% of the money raised returned to households and 10% going to small businesses, Indigenous groups, farmers, schools, universities and municipalities.
But the carbon tax isn't revenue neutral because the Trudeau government doesn't rebate the GST households pay on top of the carbon tax.
The PBO estimates this "tax on a tax" will raise $486 million for the federal government this year, rising annually to more than $1 billion in 2030.
The Canadian Federation of Independent Business says the Trudeau government hasn't returned $2.5 billion in carbon tax revenues collected since 2019 to small businesses in Ontario, Manitoba, Saskatchewan and Alberta, despite promises to do so.
Indigenous leaders in Ontario have asked the federal court for a judicial review of the carbon tax, arguing it discriminates against First Nations by disproportionately impacting them, without appropriate financial relief.
Farmers complain that the financial relief the government has provided to defray the added costs they face because of the impact of the carbon tax on agriculture, is inadequate.
The Liberals say their carbon tax is the most effective way to lower industrial greenhouse gas emissions linked to climate change.
In reality, we don't know how effective the carbon tax is because the Trudeau government doesn't keep track of its impact on emissions.
It guesstimates it will account for up to one-third of emission reduction in 2030.
According to the latest available federal government data, Canada's emissions in 2021 went up by 1.8% to 670 million tonnes, compared to 2020.
The United States, without a national carbon tax, has been more successful than Canada at reducing emissions. lgoldstein@postmedia.com @sunlorrie

Subject: Small business; Emissions; Carbon; Decades; Judicial reviews; Environmental tax; Rebates; Cost control; Households; Climate change

Business indexing term: Subject: Small business Environmental tax Rebates Cost control

Location: Manitoba Canada; Canada; Saskatchewan Canada; Nova Scotia Canada

Document 249 of 356

Enbridge's role; Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/enbridges-role-letters-editor/docview/2928069489/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Enbridge%27s+role%3B+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Re "Doug Ford wants to raise your gas bill" (Peter Tabuns, Feb..14): I would like to respond specifically to the reference that Enbridge Gas customers are paying a "subsidy" to cover the costs of expanding natural gas infrastructure. This is simply not true and sharing misleading and inaccurate information could have damaging ramifications. Enbridge Gas abides by an existing Ontario Energy Board (OEB) regulation that protects existing natural gas customers from the cost of expanding the natural gas system and ensures costs are appropriately borne by the customers who will benefit from the new infrastructure. This regulation allows the cost to connect to the natural gas system to be spread over a defined time period, which has historically been - as approved by the OEB - up to 40 years.
Ontario is facing a housing supply and affordability crisis. The OEB's decision will increase the price of new connections by thousands of dollars, introducing an additional real barrier to affordability.
We encourage all Ontarians to become familiar with the facts and impacts of this OEB decision. The stakes are high for affordable, reliable, accessible, and sustainable energy in Ontario.
MALINI GIRIDHAR VICE PRESIDENT, BUSINESS DEVELOPMENT & REGULATORY ENBRIDGE GAS
(This is a helpful and important clarification. The OEB's decision will have expensive and long lasting consequences. What say you Premier Ford?)

Document 250 of 356

Liberals backtrack; Guilbeault shifts gears on green policies to win votes

Author: Gunter, Lorne

Publication info: Gunter, Lorne. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-backtrack-guilbeault-shifts-gears-on/docview/2928069238/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+backtrack%3B+Guilbeault+shifts+gears+on+green+policies+to+win+votes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Gunter%2C+Lorne&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal Conservatives LOVE Steven Guilbeault.
No, I haven't got that wrong. I don't mean Guilbeault is loved by his own Liberals.
According to internal polling by Pierre Poilievre's party, the federal environment minister is an enormous drain on Liberal popularity everywhere, except in Montreal and Toronto.
So, it's not surprising, in a party desperate for broader support, that Guilbeault climbed down (sort of) from his pledge to stop giving federal money to new highway construction and is now also backpedalling on his net-zero power grid rules.
No concessions yet on his EV mandate - that only electric vehicles will be available for sale in Canada by 2035 - but I'm sure that's coming.
This has been quite a week for the Trudeau Liberals'"green" agenda.
It's clear Canadians are beginning to push back against the federal government's constant haranguing and preaching on climate change and its hypocritical application of the carbon tax.
On Monday, Guilbeault told a conference of transit planners from across the country that the Liberals had decided the country's current network of highways is "adequate."
Therefore, Ottawa would no longer be handing over cash for new construction.
According to the downtown Montreal MP, Canadians should consider living in more densely packed neighbourhoods and taking transit or walking more - as if those options were a possibility outside of three or four downtown city cores.
Just consider the math behind Guilbeault's claim.
The federal government is letting in a million new Canadians each year - more if you count foreign students. Just as that is creating a housing shortage, it's quite quickly going to create a road shortage, too.
It staggers the imagination that a government that is flooding the country with newcomers could insist the current roadway system would remain adequate for more than a year or two.
Of course, by Wednesday, after Canadians had thoroughly mocked Guilbeault's proposal, the minister insisted he hadn't said what he very clearly had said in front of cameras and witnesses.
The point is, someone at Liberal HQ or the PMO was making the radical eco-minister back down, even if only a bit.
Then on Friday, Guilbeault hinted he might soften his net-zero power grid regulations, too, released just and other critics "they have been before Christmas. He told premiers heard" and he soon expected to outline as many as a dozen "major changes."
The Liberals'polling must be revealing what the Conservatives see - namely that voters outside the blinkered "progressive" bubbles enough of expensive, inconvenient, in Montreal and Toronto have had "green" pipe dreams.
Indeed, a survey of Canadians'attitudes, conducted by the very pro-Trudeau, very "green" Privy Council Office, found 70% of Canadians "strongly or somewhat agree environmentally friendly options are too expensive."
An EV might sound like a nice idea in theory, but when middle-class Canadians go to pay for one, the 40% higher sticker price dulls their enthusiasm to save the planet. to shortly announce they'll permit Expect the vote-hungry Liberals us to buy gasoline-and diesel-powered cars a bit longer, so long as we please, please, please agree to vote for them again.
They'll have to be smarter backing away from EVs than they were backing away from their carbon tax.
That reversal applied mostly to Atlantic Canada, because that reliably Liberal region was abandoning the party. But the move didn't save Liberal fortunes there, while at the same time, the obvious favouritism cost them even more support elsewhere.
However, the carbon tax controversy caused the Liberals to try to prove they weren't being inconsistent.
So, they voted down a law eliminating the carbon tax on farm fuels, which will cost Canadian farmers nearly $1 billion this year and further raise the price of groceries.
Good strategy, guys.
The Liberals, led by Trudeau and Guilbeault, are suffocating their electoral chances with their "green" obsessions. lgunter@postmedia.com @sunlornegunter

Subject: Canadians; Highway construction; Tax controversies; Carbon; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax controversies Environmental tax

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada

Enbridge Gas
(This is a helpful and important clarification. The OEB's decision will have expensive and long lasting consequences. What say you Premier Ford?)
SAME BUT DIFFERENT
In a column Feb. 15 ("Use laws to curb hate"), Warren Kinsella quotes numerous laws from the Criminal Code that he says should, or could, have been applied to the protesters at Mount Sinai Hospital. Hypocritically, I believe he actually supported Trudeau invoking the Emergencies Act to deal with Freedom Convoy protesters when there wasn't a single thing they did that couldn't have been dealt with with the very same laws he quotes in his column. Considering that a federal justice ruled that Trudeau rode roughshod over the Constitutional rights of Canadians by so doing, perhaps Warren should reconsider his position rather than displaying a blatant double standard.
Mitchell Lewis Nicholls
Brampton
(Noted)
CANADA THE FREELOADER
I don't believe that Trump's threats regarding funding of NATO by its member countries will have much effect on Canadian defence spending. Lookup a definition of 'freeloader' and you will find a reference to 'Canada.' This country's motto: 'Owe Canada. We stand on guard for free!'
Peter Birks
Mississauga
(It is embarrassing Canada isn't close to paying its fair share to ensure our national security)

GOLDSTEIN: The painful truth about Trudeau's 'rebranded' carbon tax

Author: Goldstein, Lorrie

Publication info: Goldstein, Lorrie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/goldstein-painful-truth-about-trudeaus-rebranded/docview/2928009027/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=GOLDSTEIN%3A+The+painful+truth+about+Trudeau%27s+%27rebranded%27+carbon+tax&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Goldstein%2C+Lorrie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 253 of 356

GUNTER: Liberals shifting gears on green policies to win back votes

Author: Doucette, Chris

Publication info: Doucette, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/gunter-liberals-shifting-gears-on-green-policies/docview/2927987022/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=GUNTER%3A+Liberals+shifting+gears+on+green+policies+to+win+back+votes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Doucette%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal Conservatives LOVE Steven Guilbeault.
No, I haven't got that wrong. I don't mean Guilbeault is loved by his own Liberals.
According to internal polling by Pierre Poilievre's party, the federal environment minister is an enormous drain on Liberal popularity everywhere, except in Montreal and Toronto.
So, it's not surprising, in a party desperate for broader support, that Guilbeault climbed down (sort of) from his pledge to stop giving federal money to new highway construction and is now also backpedalling on his net-zero power grid rules.
No concessions yet on his EV mandate (that only electric vehicles will be available for sale in Canada by 2035), but I am sure that's coming.
LILLEY: Trudeau government pledges no more road building!
GOLDSTEIN: Guilbeault serves up horse manure for Christmas
EDITORIAL: $200 billion spent on 'climate action' - for what?
This has been quite a week for the Trudeau Liberals' "green" agenda. It's clear Canadians are beginning to push back against the federal government's constant haranguing and preaching on climate change and its hypocritical application of the carbon tax.
On Monday, Guilbeault told a conference of transit planners from across the country that the Liberals had decided the country's current network of highways is "adequate." Therefore, Ottawa would no longer be handing over cash for new construction.
According to the downtown Montreal MP, Canadians should consider living in more densely packed neighbourhoods and taking transit or walking more (as if those options were a possibility outside of three or four downtown city cores).
Just consider the math behind Guilbeault's claim.
The federal government is letting in a million new Canadians each year (more if you count foreign students). Just as that is creating a housing shortage, it is quite quickly going to create a road shortage, too.
It staggers the imagination that a government that is flooding the country with newcomers could insist the current roadway system would remain adequate for more than a year or two.
Of course, by Wednesday, after Canadians had thoroughly mocked Guilbeault's proposal, the minister insisted he hadn't said what he very clearly had said in front of cameras and witnesses.
The point is, someone at Liberal HQ or the PMO was making the radical eco-minister back down, even if only a bit.
Then on Friday, Guilbeault hinted he might soften his net-zero power grid regulations, too, released just before Christmas. He told premiers and other critics "they have been heard" and he soon expected to outline as many as a dozen "major changes."
The Liberals' polling must be revealing what the Conservatives' is - namely that voters outside the blinkered "progressive" bubbles in Montreal and Toronto have had enough of expensive, inconvenient, "green" pipe dreams.
Indeed, a survey of Canadians' attitudes, conducted by the very pro-Trudeau, very "green" Privy Council Office, found 70% of Canadians "strongly or somewhat agree environmentally friendly options are too expensive."
An EV might sound like a nice idea in theory, but when middle-class Canadians go to pay for one, the 40% higher sticker price dulls their enthusiasm to save the planet.
Expect the vote-hungry Liberals to shortly announce they'll permit us to buy gasoline- and diesel-powered cars a bit longer, so long as we please, please, please agree to vote for them again.
They will have to be smarter backing away from EVs than they were backing away from their carbon tax. That reversal applied mostly to Atlantic Canada, because that reliably Liberal region was abandoning the party. But the move didn't save Liberal fortunes there, while at the same time, the obvious favouritism cost them even more support elsewhere.
However, the carbon tax controversy caused the Liberals to try to prove they weren't being inconsistent. So, they voted down a law eliminating the carbon tax on farm fuels, which will cost Canadian farmers nearly $1 billion this year and further raise the price of groceries.
Good strategy, guys.
The Liberals, led by Trudeau and Guilbeault, are suffocating their electoral chances with their "green" obsessions.

Subject: Canadians; Highway construction; Tax controversies; Carbon; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax controversies Environmental tax

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada

Identifier / keyword: federal; conservatives; steven; guilbeault; wrong; loved

Title: GUNTER: Liberals shifting gears on green policies to win back votes

Document 254 of 356

Anti-Semitism downplayed; CP, fed appointee defend protesters

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/anti-semitism-downplayed-cp-fed-appointee-defend/docview/2927925776/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Anti-Semitism+downplayed%3B+CP%2C+fed+appointee+defend+protesters&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The people who targeted a Jewish hospital in Toronto this week with calls for the death of Jews seem awfully concerned about how people see them.
They've been pushing back against those denouncing them with key help from Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's appointed representative on Islamophobia and a national media organization.
Amira Elghawaby, appointed just over a year ago as Canada's first special representative on combatting Islamophobia, posted to social media earlier this week that those calling out the Monday protests at Toronto's Mount Sinai Hospital were going too far.
Elghawaby said that "hospitals and medical workers must always be safe to provide important services," but then went on to defend the protests.
"Yet, also troubling and wrong is the rush to label protesters as anti-Semitic and/or terrorist sympathizers," Elghawaby said.
No, not really, because that is what these protesters have shown themselves to be since Hamas launched the war on Oct. 7.
Within hours of the brutal terrorist attacks taking place, one of the organizing groups of Monday's protests, the Palestinian Youth Movement, was organizing a rally to celebrate the attacks.
"We call upon our people in the far diaspora in Toronto to uplift our resistance and our martyrs," the group posted across social media.
A post to Instagram from Oct.
7, still visible as of this writing, celebrates the taking of "over 30 Zionist hostages" and "the fall of settlements surrounding Gaza" and "our right to resist by any means necessary."
It then asks people to rally at Nathan Phillips Square on Oct. 9 in support of these acts.
Thousands did and thousands more have since the attack.
I've attended many of these rallies and while they'll call on Israel to agree to a ceasefire, I've never heard anyone at them call for the release of hostages, for Hamas to surrender or for them to lay down arms.
There are no denunciations of Hamas'terror attacks - instead it's all about cheering on the intefadeh, the resistance, or the martyrs, which means cheering on the killing of Jews or those who kill the Jews.
Now, this group wants us to believe when their march goes along hospital row in Toronto that they were just "walking by a hospital" as the many emails flooding the inboxes of so many politicians at all levels are hearing? After leaders, including Trudeau, Premier Doug Ford and Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, denounced the actions, the groups got defensive.
"Organizers of a pro-Palestinian demonstration that passed by a Toronto hospital earlier this week are accusing the prime minister and the city's mayor of spreading misinformation and being unfair to peaceful protesters," read a story from The Canadian Press (CP), wire service published Wednesday.
Embarrassing It's a story that is utterly embarrassing for CP and should have their editors asking tough questions.
I can tell those editors that tough questions are being asked about CP and the diminishing value of its work in media companies.
How do you describe this as a march that "passed by a Toronto hospital" without adding context, or telling the truth? Mount Sinai is in an area called hospital row on University Ave.
in the middle of the city. Only the Jewish hospital was targeted, not SickKids, not Princess Margaret, not Toronto General, and yet we're supposed to take this as people simply passing by? They also didn't just pass by - protesters climbed on top of the awning to the hospital entrance, someone hoisted a Palestinian flag to the top and waved it as the mob of yobs shouted "Long live the intefadeh," while at least one of their cohort banged a military sounding beat on a snare drum.
Does that sound like walking by to you or does that sound like the purposeful targeting of not only a hospital but a Jewish hospital? It's disgusting this group would attempt to shift blame for their actions and their anti-Semitism, it's worse still that a government appointee and a national and supposedly unbiased media outlet would try to help them in their cause. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Antisemitism; Jewish people; Demonstrations & protests; Islamophobia; Xenophobia; Prime ministers; Social networks

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks

Company / organization: Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Identifier / keyword: people; targeted; je wish; hospital; toronto; calls

Document 255 of 356

Killer let loose?; Teen charged in TTC stabbing while on bail for '22 slaying

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/killer-let-loose-teen-charged-ttc-stabbing-while/docview/2927925550/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Killer+let+loose%3F%3B+Teen+charged+in+TTC+stabbing+while+on+bail+for+%2722+slaying&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a shocking development that could put her bail in jeopardy, a 14-year-old girl charged in a stabbing at the Wilson subway station Friday is one of the eight girls facing second-degree murder for the 2022 stabbing of Ken Lee.
A Toronto lawyer confirmed that the girl was freed on bail with a series of conditions after her alleged involvement in a swarming that cost Lee his life outside of a homeless shelter at the corner of Front and York Sts. on Dec. 18, 2022.
Her second-degree murder charge in that case is still working its way through the courts and has yet to go to trial.
Now, as Toronto Police hunt for an alleged accomplice to Thursday's stabbing at a TTC station during rush-hour - a 21-year-old Black man, about 5 feet 11, who was wearing all black clothing - the teenaged girl faces new charges.
"The female youth, 14, of Toronto, is charged with assault, assault with a weapon, aggravated assault (and) fail (sic) to comply (with an) undertaking," Toronto Police said in a statement released Friday.
The latest stabbing this young girl is alleged to have been involved with occurred around 5:30 p.m.
"A 52-year-old man was exiting the subway at Wilson station," police said, adding that the man "became involved in an altercation on the subway platform with a female youth and her male companion."
"During the altercation, the victim was physically assaulted and was stabbed," police added.
The male assailant ran off along Wilson Ave. toward Wilson Heights Blvd., while the teenaged girl was arrested at the scene.
The stabbing victim was taken to hospital, but police said his wounds weren't considered life-threatening.
Lee wasn't so fortunate.
His friends told the
Toronto Sun that he was heroically protecting his girlfriend, Erica, whom he was sharing a drink with on a bench outside of the Strathcona Hotel, when the swarm of eight teenage girls attacked and the 59-year-old was stabbed to death.
Of the eight girls subsequently charged with second-degree murder, three were 13, three were 14 and two were 16.
The eight accused are said to have not known each other but gathered in what is believed to be a call over social media for an alleged "wilding." The 14-year-old girl accused in the subway stabbing was one of the 13-year-year-olds granted bail after Lee's slaying. Only one of the girls charged in Lee's murder hasn't been granted bail.
Lee's family later released a statement expressing their opposition to the seven other accused girls being released on bail and their identities also being protected under the Youth Criminal Justice Act (YCJA).
"For serious crimes, these perpetrators should not have any privacy rights or bail," Lee's family said at the time. "The public should be aware of who these individuals are to protect themselves. The perpetrators must be named in order to bring forth more victims, witnesses and evidence."
The very good point the Lee family made then is front and centre now in that there was no way this victim could have known this child he encountered on the subway platform was already before the courts for a deadly stabbing.
This is yet another case of the justice system granting bail to someone charged in a violent crime only to have them accused of committing another violent crime.
And it can't be glossed over by Premier Doug Ford's justice officials.
"How is the (Youth Criminal Justice) Act protecting the public if we don't know who these perpetrators are and why they are released on bail?" the Lee family asked last year.
The slain man's family posed some excellent questions at the time - and there are many more questions now that demand answers.
It's believed the girl accused in the TTC stabbing appeared in youth court Friday and was held in custody until a bail hearing can be arranged.
The last bail hearings for these girls in the Lee case saw them yawning and snickering during the process. There is an expectation there will be a rigorous test before this accused teen is granted bail again.
If she is released on bail again, there will be plenty more to say. But to ensure she is treated fairly under the law, it's a situation where the public will just have to wait and see how it unfolds in court.
Having covered this case extensively from the beginning, along with colleagues Brad Hunter and Michele Mandel, I'd rather not end this column commenting on the girl accused in this latest violence at the TTC station or her co-accused in Lee's murder - I'd prefer to end with some words about Lee himself.
Family spokesperson Eric Shum told me previously that the Richmond Hill man, who had fallen on hard times but was rebounding, was actually a very accomplished and much-loved person who'd never hurt anybody.
"Ken was a kind soul with a heart of gold," Shum said.
In his memory, and out of respect for the latest victim of someone before the courts, there is no question that in most cases, no matter their ages, granting bail to people accused of murder has to end. jwarmington@postmedia.com @joe_warmington

Subject: Bail; Girls; Children & youth; Assaults; Violent crime; Murders & murder attempts; Homeless people; Stabbings

Identifier / keyword: shocking; development; jeopardy; charged; stabbing; wilson

Document 257 of 356

LILLEY: Trudeau appointee, Canadian Press both defending anti-Semitism

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-trudeau-appointee-canadian-press-both/docview/2927853566/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Trudeau+appointee%2C+Canadian+Press+both+defending+anti-Semitism&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The people who targeted a Jewish hospital in Toronto this week with calls for the death of Jews seem awfully concerned about how people see them.
They have been pushing back against those denouncing them with key help from Justin Trudeau's appointed representative on Islamophobia and a national media organization.
Amira Elghawaby, appointed just over a year ago as Canada's first special representative on combatting Islamophobia, posted to social media earlier this week that those calling out the Monday protests at Toronto's Mount Sinai Hospital were going too far.
Elghawaby said that "hospitals and medical workers must always be safe to provide important services," but then went on to defend the protests.
"Yet also troubling and wrong is the rush to label protesters as anti-Semitic and/or terrorist sympathizers," Elghawaby said.
No, not really, because that is what these protesters have shown themselves to be since Hamas launched the war on Oct. 7. Within hours of the brutal terrorist attacks taking place, one of the organizing groups of Monday's protests, the Palestinian Youth Movement, was organizing a rally to celebrate the attacks.
"We call upon our people in the far diaspora to in Toronto to uplift our resistance and our martyrs," the group posted across social media.
A post to Instagram from Oct. 7, still visible as of this writing, celebrates the taking of "over 30 Zionist hostages" and "the fall of settlements surrounding Gaza" and "our right to resist by any means necessary." It then asks people to rally at Nathan Phillips Square on Oct. 9 in support of these acts.
Thousands did and thousands more have since the attack. I've attended many of these rallies and while they will call on Israel to agree to a ceasefire, I've never heard anyone at them call for the release of hostages, for Hamas to surrender or for them to lay down arms. There are no denunciations of Hamas' terror attacks - instead it's all about cheering on the intifada, the resistance, or the martyrs, which means cheering on the killing of Jews or those who kill the Jews.
A screenshot from video posted to X of protesters outside Mount Sinai Hospital in Toronto on Monday, Feb. 12, 2024.
Now, this group wants us to believe that when their march goes along hospital row in Toronto that they were just "walking by a hospital" as the many emails flooding the inboxes of so many politicians at all levels are hearing? After leaders including Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Premier Doug Ford and Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow denounced the actions, the groups got defensive.
"Organizers of a pro-Palestinian demonstration that passed by a Toronto hospital earlier this week are accusing the prime minister and the city's mayor of spreading misinformation and being unfair to peaceful protesters," read a story from the Canadian Press (CP) wire service published Wednesday, emphasis added.
It's a story that is utterly embarrassing for CP and should have their editors asking tough questions. I can tell those editors that tough questions are being asked about CP and the diminishing value of their work in media companies.
How do you describe this as a march that "passed by a Toronto hospital" without adding context, or telling the truth?
Mount Sinai is in an area called hospital row on University Ave. in the middle of the city. Only the Jewish hospital was targeted, not SickKids, not Princess Margaret, not Toronto General, and yet we are supposed to take this as people simply passing by?
They also didn't just pass by - protesters climbed on top of the awning to the hospital entrance, someone hoisted a Palestinian flag to the top and waved it as the mob of yobs shouted, "Long live the intifada," while at least one of their cohort banged a military sounding beat on a snare drum. Does that sound like walking by to you or does that sound like the purposeful targeting of not only a hospital but a Jewish hospital?
It's disgusting that this group would attempt to shift blame for their actions and their anti-Semitism, it's worse still that a government appointee and a national and supposedly unbiased media outlet would try to help them in their cause.
blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Antisemitism; Jewish people; Demonstrations & protests; Islamophobia; Xenophobia; Prime ministers; Social networks

People: Trudeau, Justin

Document 258 of 356

On the road with Steven Guilbeault

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/on-road-with-steven-guilbeault/docview/2927761493/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=On+the+road+with+Steven+Guilbeault&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: To understand why many Canadians have trouble trusting the Trudeau government, look no further than Environment and Climate Change Minister Steven Guilbeault's evolving position on whether the federal government intends to continue funding roads.
On Monday, as reported by the Montreal Gazette, Guilbeault said in a speech in Montreal to a public transit conference: "Our government has made the decision to stop investing at the federal level in new road infrastructure.
"Of course, we will continue to be there for cities, provinces and territories to maintain the existing network, but there will be no more envelopes from the federal government to enlarge the road network. The analysis we have done is that the network is perfectly adequate to respond to the needs we have ... We can very well achieve our goals of economic, social and human development without more enlargement of the road network."
Responding to the political controversy this caused since this would be a major shift in federal policy that caught other levels of government by surprise - see the consternation expressed by Ontario Premier Doug Ford and Alberta Premier Danielle Smith - Guilbeault claimed Wednesday he was only talking about large, mega-projects.
"I should have been more specific," Guilbeault said. "But in that same conference, I specified that we still have funds, obviously, to maintain and enhance our road network across the country ... I was talking specifically about projects like the Troisième Lien that the CAQ government in Quebec wants to move forward with, which is something myself and many of my Quebec cabinet colleagues have said time and time again. This is not a new policy at all."
That's nice, but that's not what he said on Monday. His two statements directly contradict one another.
It recalls the political firestorm Prime Minister Justin Trudeau caused in 2017 when he said his climate change policies would eventually "phase out" Alberta's oilsands, which went down about as well as you'd expect in Alberta.
Trudeau later said he "misspoke" and "said something the way I shouldn't have said it."
The Liberals remind us of Humpty Dumpty in Lewis Carroll's Through the Looking-Glass, who scornfully explained: "When I use a word, it means just what I choose it to mean - neither more nor less."

Subject: Environmental policy; Roads & highways

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada; Quebec Canada

Identifier / keyword: understand; canadians; trouble; trusting; trudeau; government

Letters to the Editor, Feb. 16, 2024

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/letters-editor-feb-16-2024/docview/2927616233/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+to+the+Editor%2C+Feb.+16%2C+2024&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: THREE TOO MANY
Re "Bit stiff, isn't it?" (Terry Koshan, Feb. 14): One of the rules in safe driving education is to get the big picture. Clearly the NHL missed this memo in handing out five games to Morgan Rielly. Rielly did not target the head or neck with his crosscheck, but initial contact was with the well-padded and protected shoulder area, with his stick subsequently riding up. In a sport that encourages and promotes emotional play and physicality, Rielly's response to a totally hot-dog play by the Ottawa rookie was more akin to a dose of comeuppance rather than an act of retribution. The Ottawa player was not injured, nor missed any playing time. The most ludicrous statement put forth by the Safety Committee was that had Rielly aimed lower (perhaps the back or chest area) there wouldn't necessarily have been any repercussions at all. I'm not sure that Buffalo Bills' Damar Hamlin would agree with that logic! Finally, look at who the NHL is dealing with. Rielly arguably is one of the most, if not the most, revered and virtuous player in the entire league! Until very recently, he had zero penalties which is incredulous, especially for a defenceman where on many nights the refs call the most miniscule infractions. For gosh sakes, he's even married to a Virtue - retired figure skating legend Tessa Virtue. They are Canada's sweetheart couple! By all means give him a couple of games for a momentary lack of restraint, but five games - for shame!
Philip Adams
Toronto
(It was far too excessive of a punishment. But had they not done anything, there would have more anger. A couple of games would have sufficed)
CARBON TAX FUTURES
Re "Why now, Doug?" (Letters to the Editor, Feb. 15): Premier Doug Ford's allegiance to our prime minister is waning. Ford knows all too well that Justin Trudeau's popularity has hit rock bottom. It's time for him to jump ship and look after his own political career. That's why he is making future carbon tax issues subject to referendums. Democracy has become important again.
Caspar Pfenninger
Calgary, Alb.
(Does Ford know something we don't about when the next federal election is? Hopefully soon)
