Document 262 of 356

Injection of racism; In Canada, anti-drug site protester told to go back where he came from

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/injection-racism-canada-anti-drug-site-protester/docview/2926959561/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Injection+of+racism%3B+In+Canada%2C+anti-drug+site+protester+told+to+go+back+where+he+came+from&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Nothing defines the mess Canada is in with its zombie drug crisis more than an activist telling a father of Chinese descent who's opposed to a drug injection site to go back to where he came from.
So far, no tweets from Prime Minister Justin Trudeau calling out the racist behaviour.
In fact, there has been no media coverage at all.
Thanks to Opposition Leader Pierre Poilievre going to social media, perhaps that will change. And, thanks to social media, it's going to be hard to sweep this video under the rug - let alone keep it from going viral.
"You're what's wrong with Canada," a woman said to a man outside a meeting about installing one of these safe-consumption locations in his Richmond, B.C., neighbourhood. "Go back to where you came from."
The man is Canadian, and he lives in British Columbia, and he has every right to question the notion of helping drug addicts stay addicted and die.
But this woman doesn't think so. "This is not what Canada does," she said. "Canada fights for our citizens ... Go back to Hong Kong."
The crowd responded by yelling: "No drugs, no drugs." The RCMP stood there helpless as things got worse.
Trudeau, who has many tweets in support of the Asian community during this week's Lunar New Year celebrations, really should call this out the same way he called out the swastika found during the Freedom Convoy or when Conservatives were seen walking with truckers in protest.
There should be no double standard on racism.
Not only was this man discriminated against because of his ethnicity, the woman went further by saying to the largely Asian demonstration: "All of you are corrupt. The drugs come from you."
One look at the streets of Vancouver and it's clear the drugs aren't coming from the people in this crowd opposed to the drugs but from those who foster the use of them by handing out free supply and drug kits, including needles, that help people do drugs.
They create crime, hardship and despair.
We see this here in the Greater Toronto Area too. Drugs are ruining society and the approach currently employed is failing miserably.
Poilievre knows this and is vowing to change it.
"Radical activist spews racist tirade against a Chinese man trying to protect kids from a drug injection site the NDP & Liberal governments are forcing into Richmond. Help me fight against the NDP/Liberals pushing drugs on the Chinese community," he posted on his X account.
He also linked to a motion to "stop the drug injection site in Richmond" that states that "after eight years of Justin Trudeau's taxpayer-funded distribution of hard drugs has caused harm and addiction" and the "sites are grounds for criminal activity and make neighbourhoods unsafe," Conservatives call on the government to "stop handing our taxpayer funds to addicts."
But history has shown the people who believe in this "harm reduction strategy" won't do that.
On treating a fellow Canadian of Asian descent so horribly, history shows the racism charges only come to people like the Convoy truckers who are opposed to Liberal policies.
People pushing progressive policies like free drugs for addicts seem to skate. jwarmington@postmedia.com @joe_warmington

Subject: Drugs; Racism; Social networks

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks

Location: Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Document 263 of 356

Use laws to curb hate; Anti-Semitic mobs must face penalties

Author: Kinsella, Warren

Publication info: Kinsella, Warren. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/use-laws-curb-hate-anti-semitic-mobs-must-face/docview/2926957894/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Use+laws+to+curb+hate%3B+Anti-Semitic+mobs+must+face+penalties&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Kinsella%2C+Warren&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This week, a mob attacked a hospital.
Not because they support Palestine.
They did it because they hate Jews.
Mount Sinai Hospital on University Ave. isn't "Jewish," of course, any more than it is Protestant or Catholic or Muslim. This writer's sons were all born there, delivered by a former medic in the IDF who hummed along to John Cougar Mellencamp songs.
Nobody asked us about our religion while we were there. But Mount Sinai is indeed an important symbol for the 1.4% of the Canadian population who are Jews. Which is why the haters descended on the place Monday night, to promote hatred.
Like snakes, spewing venom. Mount Sinai was created 102 years ago, when some Jewish women started fundraising for a hospital because - among other things - no other Toronto hospital would allow Jews to practise medicine. Along with the lives regularly bettered or saved there, Mount Sinai delivers 7,000 babies every year.
You've likely heard about the attack on Mount Sinai Hospital because it woke up quite a few politicians from their extended slumber. Many of them had been asleep, or the political equivalent, during previous attacks on Jewish symbols in this country.
But Mount Sinai woke them up.
Was it the mob screaming for "intefadeh" - a terror campaign? Was it the masked "pro-Palestinian" types climbing scaffolding affixed to the hospital's front, waving flags, blocking peoples'access? You know: people who were there to be admitted as patients. The people there to get help in the emergency department. The people there for cancer treatment. Or to just have a baby.
Was it that? Who knows? Whatever it was, the politicians woke up. The prime minister, for once, called it "anti-Semitism," and didn't feel the need to tuck "Islamophobia" into the subclause that followed.
Ontario's premier, who has been consistently more vocal than most, called the attack on the hospital "indecent," because it was. Toronto's mayor, who previously advocated for a ceasefire - even when the anti-Israel mob was terrorizing her New Year's family skate party - also said it was anti-Semitic. Good.
So, where were the police? Good question.
There are no shortage of laws available to charge the thugs who attacked Mount Sinai Hospital this week. There is, apparently, a shortage of police.
Reportedly, there were only two on hand to deal with a parade of hundreds of thugs who were easily observed several blocks away, for many minutes, banging drums and chanting "intefadeh." Police headquarters is 850 metres away, a four-minute walk.
But the Toronto Police could only muster two officers to deal with law-breaking on a mass scale.
And, rest assured: it was all illegal. A new law, which Premier Doug Ford emphasized on Tuesday, is seen in Bill C-3, and is specifically designed to protect hospitals and the people who work in them.
The bill seeks to prevent precisely the sort of hatefest that took place on University on Monday night. If indicted, those who violate the new law can face 10 years in prison.
But that isn't the only law available to police and prosecutors for the siege of Mount Sinai. Here are some others, with the relevant sections of the Criminal Code, for officers to tuck into their wallets for future reference: n Blocking or obstructing a highway [Sec. 423(1)(g)].
* Intimidation [Sec. 423(1)]. n Causing a disturbance (Sec. 175). n Common nuisance (Sec. 180). n Interfering with transportation facilities (Sec. 248). n Breach of the peace or imminent breach (Sec. 31). n Riots (Sec. 32, 33, 64, 65, 67, 68, 69). n Unlawful assembly (Sec. 63). n Mischief (Sec. 430).
It goes without saying it's crime to simply attempt or conspire to do any of those things.
And that's not all: if one of the thugs was wearing a mask while committing an offence - and masks were in widespread use Monday night - Sec. 65 (2) of the Criminal Code makes that an offence, as well. And can also, if indicted, result in a decade behind bars.
So, there are a lot of laws available to prevent another attack on Mount Sinai Hospital, or any other hospital. Or any place or person, really.
We have the laws. In this country, we have many, many laws we can use.
What we don't have, increasingly, is the guts to use them.

Subject: Antisemitism; Jewish people

Identifier / keyword: attacked; hospital; support; palestine; mount; sinai

Title: Use laws to curb hate; Anti-Semitic mobs must face penalties

Document 264 of 356

Why now, doug?

Author: Unger, Vivian

Publication info: Unger, Vivian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-now-doug/docview/2926957845/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Full text: Re "Ford announces provincial carbon tax referendum bill" (The Canadian Press, Feb. 13): So now Doug Ford wants any future carbon tax to be subject to a referendum. Funny. He didn't seem to have a high opinion of referendums in 2020. That's when he took away London, Ont.'s ranked ballots, after Londoners voted for them in a referendum. He didn't care to listen to the voice of the people back then. I wonder what's changed? It could be that he's come around about the importance of public consultation. Or maybe he just wants to use the referendum, as so many others have, to block any change to the status quo. My money's on that second possibility.

Document 265 of 356

Clock's ticking down on Trudeau

Author: Goldstein, Lorrie

Publication info: Goldstein, Lorrie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/clocks-ticking-down-on-trudeau/docview/2926957774/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Clock%27s+ticking+down+on+Trudeau&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=Goldstein%2C+Lorrie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Time is rapidly running out for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to decide whether he's going to lead the Liberals into the next federal election.
Assuming the Liberal/NDP accord holds, it has to be held on or before Oct. 20, 2025 - 20 months away.
But Trudeau has to make a decision about his own future well before that.
If he's not going to run, he should be announcing it for the good of his party right about now.
Speculation about Trudeau's future to date has hinged on whether his confidence-and-supply agreement with NDP leader Jagmeet Singh until the fall of 2025 will hold, given Singh's frequent threats to break it - the latest on the issue of delivering pharmacare.
But the end of the accord wouldn't automatically mean a snap election.
In addition, does the NDP really want to provoke an election now, which if the polls are accurate, would result in a huge Conservative majority where the NDP would have no say? The real deadline facing Trudeau is that the longer he waits to decide on his future, the more likely that decision will be made for him by default.
That is, he will have to stay on as leader because there won't be enough time to hold a Liberal leadership race to replace him, or for a new leader to put his or her stamp on the party and convince Canadians to vote for it.
Twenty years ago, then-Progressive Conservative prime minister Brian Mulroney waited until February 1993 to announce he was resigning as party leader, prior to a federal election that had to be called by October 1993 - eight months later.
The party didn't elect its new leader - Kim Campbell - until June 1993.
In the ensuing federal election four months later on Oct. 25, 1993, the PCs were reduced from a majority government to two seats.
That marked the beginning of the end for the PC party as a viable force in federal politics and the start of a Liberal dynasty that lasted 13 years.
The eight months between Mulroney resigning and Campbell leading the party into its disastrous showing in the 1993 election wasn't enough time for the PCs to put angry voter memories of the Mulroney years behind them, or for Campbell to create her own political brand and run an effective election campaign.
Given months of recent polling indicating Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives would win a huge majority government if an election was held today, the next Liberal leader, if there is one, is going to need a lot more than eight months to campaign for the Liberal leadership, put the memory of the Trudeau years behind him, or her, and convince voters a Liberal government under their leadership would be different from the current one.
Leadership races suck up money and resources that would otherwise be used in an election, as well as creating internal divisions within the party the new leader has to be able to mend, which takes time.
Given these realities, if Trudeau is going to resign before the next election, he should be announcing it now, to give his party any realistic chance of winning.
Releasing a poll last week showing the Conservatives with a 19-point lead over the Liberals, Abacus Data CEO David Coletto said the Liberals'root problem is that "most people aren't listening anymore to Trudeau."
The question is, will they listen to any new Liberal leader? lgoldstein@postmedia.com @sunlorrie

Subject: Leadership; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Identifier / keyword: rapidly; running; prime; minister; justin; trudeau

Document 266 of 356

It's the end of the road; Enviro minister says feds done forking over cash for highways

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/end-road-enviro-minister-says-feds-done-forking/docview/2926957714/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It%27s+the+end+of+the+road%3B+Enviro+minister+says+feds+done+forking+over+cash+for+highways&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Steven Guilbeault said the quiet part out loud - the Trudeau government doesn't want any more roads.
If you think traffic is bad now, just keep this guy in power for a few more years.
Guilbeault, of course, is Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's fanatical environment minister, a former Greenpeace activist famous for getting arrested, someone I first met more than 20 years ago when he locked himself to a gas pump in Montreal to demand we all stop driving cars.
Some things never change, Guilbeault is still pushing that agenda today "Our government has made the decision to stop investing in new road infrastructure," Guilbeault told a Montreal environmental conference. "Of course, we will continue to be there for cities, provinces and territories to maintain the existing network, but there will be no more envelopes from the federal government to enlarge the road network."
Instead of giving infrastructure money to provinces and municipalities to build new roads, something the federal government has done for decades, Guilbeault says they'll only invest in what he calls "active and public transit" going forward. Translation: more bike lanes, more walking, more buses, but no more money for roads.
That's something that caught the eye of Premier Doug Ford, who despite working well with the Trudeau government, said he was shocked by Guilbeault's comments.
"I'm gobsmacked," Ford posted on X with a link to the Guilbeault story. "A federal minister said they won't invest in new roads or highways. He doesn't care that you're stuck in bumper-to-bumper traffic. I do. We're building roads and highways, with or without a cent from the feds."
He's in the middle of trying to build Hwy. 413, a new link connecting Toronto's fast-growing western suburbs. That's a project Guilbeault has expressed opposition to in the past and it's controversial with the Liberals and NDP, though popular with voters.
In the last provincial election, Ford and his PC Party won every single seat in the vicinity of the highway. This happened despite the provincial Liberals and NDP campaigning hard against the project and at least two years of almost uniformly negative media headlines where the highway was constantly described as "controversial."
Turns out, the highway isn't controversial and people like their cars, but that's something Guilbeault would know since he loves his car as well.
As we reported in 2020, Guilbeault was logging more than 3,000 km per month shortly after he was elected and appointed minister in the 2019 election. That's more driving in seven months than the 15,000 km the average Canadian drives in a year.
What makes that even more shocking is that Guilbeault had said before he was elected that he wanted to try to serve as a minister without taking the car and chauffeur.
"I never needed to have a car," Guilbeault told Quebec radio station QUB in 2019. "I don't want to make a big 'no, I'll never do that'statement, but I would try to do without. Why not? Maybe it won't work, but at least I'll try it."
A lot of those clicks on the car came from his driver shuttling him back and forth between Ottawa and Montreal despite there being a perfectly good train available.
"We can very well achieve our goals of economic, social and human development without more enlargement of the road network," he said Tuesday.
Maybe Guilbeault thinks that because Montreal, where he lives, is all built up. Other parts of the country are not, and some are growing, adding massive new populations, which will require expanding the road network.
You can't add a million people over the last couple of years as Ontario has done and not expand infrastructure, like roads. That is unless you want everyone stuck in traffic or crammed into a train while Guilbeault gets chauffeured past you. Hypocrite. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Automobiles; Infrastructure; Traffic; Decades; Roads & highways

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada

Identifier / keyword: steven; guilbeault; quiet; trudeau; government; roads

Mount Sinai Hospital, a sacred Jewish-inspired hospital which would accept any one of these pro-Hamas demonstrators if they were injured - especially the one who climbed up on the awning above the entrance waving his pro-Palestinian flag, if he were to have fallen and was hurt. GOLDSTEIN: The clock is ticking down on Trudeau

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/goldstein-clock-is-ticking-down-on-trudeau/docview/2926753970/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 269 of 356

JAY GOLDBERG: FAO report a scathing indictment of Ford's fiscal management

Author: McLeod, Cynthia

Publication info: McLeod, Cynthia. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jay-goldberg-fao-report-scathing-indictment-fords/docview/2926641430/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=JAY+GOLDBERG%3A+FAO+report+a+scathing+indictment+of+Ford%27s+fiscal+management&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=McLeod%2C+Cynthia&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's not exactly breaking news to say the Ford government is failing Ontarians when it comes to managing the province's finances, but a new report from the Financial Accountability Office shows just how bad things are.
Before exploring the report, let's set the scene.
When Ford came to power in 2018, Ontario's debt was roughly $324 billion. Government spending in former premier Kathleen Wynne's final year in office stood at $158.5 billion and the province was running a $6.7-billion deficit.
In running to replace Wynne in 2018, Ford condemned Wynne for her poor management of the province's books.
"The party is over with taxpayers' money," Ford promised, encouraging Ontarians to turn the page on 15 years of Liberal overspending and runaway deficits.
Ontarians decided to make a change and sent Ford to Queen's Park to be a bull in a china shop and put an end to the province's free spending ways.
Instead of ending the party with taxpayers' money, Ford took it over. It's like one frat house party ended and a new one, just as loud and disturbing as the first, began.
Fast forward six years later.
Ontario's books are still in the red. The spending parade is still going on. And far from being a bull in a china shop, Ford is like a purring cat, watching the province's finances crumble all around him without batting an eye.
Less than a year ago, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy told weary Ontario taxpayers he would finally balance the province's books in 2024-25.
The FAO report shows the Ford government will not meet that target. And the government isn't just one year behind schedule: Ontarians shouldn't expect a balanced budget until 2026-27.
Worse still, next year's deficit is now projected to be larger than this year's. That's right: The numbers aren't even heading in the right direction. This year's $3.7-billion budget deficit will transform into $6.2-billion deficit next year.
The FAO says the Ford government will spend a total of $201.6 billion in the fiscal year ending March 31. That's $43.1 billion larger than the spending tab Wynne racked up in 2018. In six years, Ford increased spending by $25 billion more than the rate of inflation.
This is a scathing indictment of the Ford government's fiscal management. The same people who told Ontario taxpayers the Liberals were financially irresponsible have increased spending by an average of $4.2 billion more than the rate of inflation annually since taking office.
Let's take a moment to go back to Wynne's fiscal plan, released in 2018.
Wynne's last budget projected government spending to be $182.2 billion in fiscal year 2023-24. The Ford government has overshot that target by $19.4 billion.
Some may say Wynne wouldn't have kept to her targets and a Liberal government might have increased spending more rapidly than planned as well.
That may be true. But the same people who campaigned for office to restore the health of Ontario's finances are making Wynne's plans look like austerity.
How can Ontario get out of this mess?
First, the Ford government must order a top-to-bottom review of all government spending. Spending has increased by $25 billion more than the rate of inflation since 2018. There should be plenty of frills to cut back on.
Second, Ford can start getting tough with unions. Ontario government employees already enjoy a 10.9% wage premium. Ford shouldn't sign one more bargaining deal that doesn't start to close the government employee compensation gap.
Finally, Ford can stop corporate welfare. Over the past year, Ontario has handed $30 billion in corporate welfare to two multinational auto giants, among others. That must end.
Ford must get serious. Ontarians elected a bull in a china shop. It's time for Ford to start acting like one.

Subject: Fiscal years; Government spending; Budget deficits

Business indexing term: Subject: Fiscal years Government spending Budget deficits

Identifier / keyword: exactly; breaking; government; failing; ontarians; comes

Title: JAY GOLDBERG: FAO report a scathing indictment of Ford's fiscal management

Publication title: The Toronto Sun (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 14, 2024

Section: Columnists

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Country of publication: Canada

Document 270 of 356

TABUNS: Doug Ford wants to raise your gas bill

Author: McLeod, Cynthia

Publication info: McLeod, Cynthia. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/tabuns-doug-ford-wants-raise-your-gas-bill/docview/2926561849/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TABUNS%3A+Doug+Ford+wants+to+raise+your+gas+bill&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=McLeod%2C+Cynthia&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Just before Christmas, the Ontario Energy Board (OEB) issued an important decision affecting the gas bills of nearly four million Ontario consumers.
The OEB ordered natural gas distributor Enbridge Gas to bear the costs of expanding its natural gas infrastructure, rather than imposing these costs on existing consumers.
The OEB decision acknowledged the obvious: At a time when Ontario is moving away from fossil fuels, any plan to expand natural gas infrastructure carries enormous risks - not just to the environment but to your pocketbook. And so, the OEB decided Enbridge's proposal was not in the interests of consumers.
The next day the Doug Ford government announced that it would reverse the decision and protect the interests of Enbridge. It plans to pass legislation in February that will raise energy bills across the province and make life more expensive for new homebuyers.
It all goes back to a subsidy that most gas customers don't even know they are paying. Right now, your gas bill includes a charge worth hundreds of millions of dollars each year to cover Enbridge's cost of expanding gas pipelines into new developments. On Dec. 21, Ontario's independent energy regulator decided to put a stop to this subsidy because it raises energy bills for existing gas customers and new homebuyers, while also increasing financial risks for the whole gas system.
Ending the subsidy would save gas customers more than $1 billion over four years in avoided pipeline subsidy costs, which comes to more than $300 per customer. Ending the subsidy will also encourage developers to install heat pumps in new homes, which provide much cheaper heating and cooling, instead of gas.
Ending the subsidy would be a win-win-win-win. It would lower energy bills for existing customers, lower energy bills for new homebuyers, lower carbon emissions, and avoid even more cost down the road when homes heated with natural gas convert to heat pumps.
There is, however, one loser - Enbridge Gas. It would lose millions of dollars in profits, and it is lobbying hard against the Energy Board decision.
It's no surprise that Ford's minister of Energy, Todd Smith, has announced that they will pass legislation to overturn the decision.
But gas is no longer the cheapest heating source. Investing in gas pipelines for heating is financially foolish because they will become obsolete and a massive cost to all current and future customers as we move away from gas heating.
The government's own expert electrification panel noted "growing indications that it is unlikely that the natural gas grid can be decarbonized and continue to deliver cost-effective building heat." Our neighbours, like New York State and Montreal, are prohibiting gas in new construction.
Passing legislation to reinstate a subsidy is completely out of step and risks financial disaster down the road.
The Ontario Energy Board made the right decision, based on evidence, to lower your energy bills. The Ontario government is on course to make the wrong decision, based on back-room lobbying, to raise your energy bills.
But over the years we've seen this government bend under public pressure and reverse decisions like opening parts of the Greenbelt for development.
Hopefully the evidence and the truth will prevail, the government will respect the independent decision of the Ontario Energy Board, and you will be protected from this rate increase.
- Peter Tabuns is the NDP MPP for Toronto-Danforth and is the party's critic on energy and climate action

Subject: Legislation; Energy; Customers; Costs; Subsidies; Lobbying; Natural gas

Business indexing term: Subject: Customers Costs Subsidies

Identifier / keyword: before; christmas; ontario; energy; board; issued

Title: TABUNS: Doug Ford wants to raise your gas bill

Document 271 of 356

LILLEY: Trudeau government pledges no more road building!

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-trudeau-government-pledges-no-more-road/docview/2926533362/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Trudeau+government+pledges+no+more+road+building%21&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Steven Guilbeault said the quiet part out loud - the Trudeau government doesn't want any more roads.
If you think traffic is bad now, just keep this guy in power for a few more years.
Guilbeault, of course, is Trudeau's fanatical environment minister, a former Greenpeace activist famous for getting arrested, someone I first met more than 20 years ago when he locked himself to a gas pump in Montreal to demand we all stop driving cars.
Some things never change, Guilbeault is still pushing that agenda today
"Our government has made the decision to stop investing in new road infrastructure," Guilbeault told a Montreal environmental conference
"Of course we will continue to be there for cities, provinces and territories to maintain the existing network, but there will be no more envelopes from the federal government to enlarge the road network."
Instead of giving infrastructure money to provinces and municipalities to build new roads, something the federal government has done for decades, Guilbeault says they will only invest in what he calls "active and public transit" going forward. Translation: More bike lanes, more walking, more buses but no more money for roads.
That's something that caught the eye of Ontario Premier Doug Ford, who despite working well with the Trudeau government, said he was shocked by Guilbeault's comments.
"I'm gobsmacked," Ontario Premier Doug Ford posted to X with a link to the Guilbeault story.
"A federal minister said they won't invest in new roads or highways. He doesn't care that you're stuck in bumper-to-bumper traffic. I do. We're building roads and highways, with or without a cent from the feds."
I'm gobsmacked.
A federal minister said they won't invest in new roads or highways.
He doesn't care that you're stuck in bumper to bumper traffic. I do. We're building roads and highways, with or without a cent from the feds.
- Doug Ford (@fordnation) February 13, 2024
Ford is in the middle of trying to build Highway 413, a new link connecting Toronto's fast-growing western suburbs. That's a project that Guilbeault has expressed opposition to in the past and it is controversial with the Liberals and NDP, though popular with voters.
Last provincial election, Ford and his PC Party won every single seat in the vicinity of the highway. This happened despite the provincial Liberals and NDP campaigning hard against the project and at least two years of almost uniformly negative media headlines where the highway was constantly described as "controversial."
Turns out, the highway isn't controversial and people like their cars, but that's something Guilbeault would know since he loves his car as well.
As we reported in 2020, Guilbeault was logging more than 3,000 kilometres per month shortly after he was elected and appointed minister in the 2019 election. That's more driving in seven months than the 15,000 km the average Canadian drives in a year.
What makes that even more shocking is that Guilbeault had said before he was elected that he wanted to try to serve as a minister without taking the car and chauffeur.
"I never needed to have a car," Guilbeault told Quebec radio station QUB in 2019.
"I don't want to make a big 'no, I'll never do that' statement, but I would try to do without. Why not? Maybe it won't work, but at least I'll try it."
A lot of those clicks on the car came from his driver shuttling him back and forth between Ottawa and Montreal despite there being a perfectly good train available.
"We can very well achieve our goals of economic, social and human development without more enlargement of the road network," he said Tuesday.
Maybe Guilbeault thinks that because Montreal, where he lives, is all built up. Other parts of the country are not, and some are growing, adding massive new populations which will require expanding the road network.
You can't add one million people over the last couple of years as Ontario has done and not expand infrastructure, like roads. That is unless you want everyone stuck in traffic or crammed into a train while Guilbeault gets chauffeured past you.
Hypocrite.
blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Automobiles; Infrastructure; Traffic; Decades; Roads & highways

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada

Identifier / keyword: steven; guilbeault; quiet; trudeau; government; roads

WARMINGTON: In Canada, anti-drug site protester told to go back where he came from

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/warmington-canada-anti-drug-site-protester-told/docview/2926516783/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=WARMINGTON%3A+In+Canada%2C+anti-drug+site+protester+told+to+go+back+where+he+came+from&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Nothing defines the mess Canada is in with its zombie drug crisis more than an activist telling a Chinese father against a drug injection site to go back to where he came from.
So far, no tweets from Prime Minister Justin Trudeau calling out the racist behaviour.
In fact, there's been no media coverage at all.
Thanks to Opposition Leader Pierre Poilievre going to social media, perhaps that will change. And, thanks to social media, it's going to be hard to sweep this video under the rug - let alone keep it from going viral.
Watch this progressive pro-drug activist tell a Richmond man to "go back to where you came from" because he doesn't want a drug den opened up in his community.
Nothing beats the racism of the woke class. #bcpoli #cdnpolitics
- Angelo Isidorou (@angeloisidorou) February 13, 2024
"You're what's wrong with Canada," a woman said to a man outside a meeting about installing one of these safe-consumption locations in his Richmond, B.C. neighbourhood. "Go back to where you came from."
The man is Canadian, and he lives in British Columbia, and he has every right to question the notion of helping drug addicts stay addicted and die.
But this woman doesn't think so.
"This is not what Canada does," she said. "Canada fights for our citizens ... Go back to Hong Kong."
The crowd responded by yelling, "No drugs, no drugs." The RCMP stood there helpless as things got worse.
Trudeau, who has many tweets in support of the Asian community during this week's Lunar New Year celebrations, really should call this out the same way he called out the swastika found during the Freedom Convoy or when Conservatives were seen walking with truckers in protest.
There should be no double standard on racism.
Asian Canadian stories are etched into every chapter of our history. Yesterday, as we came together in Ottawa to celebrate the Year of the Dragon, we honoured those stories - and the people behind them.
Happy Lunar New Year, everyone!
- Justin Trudeau (@JustinTrudeau) February 13, 2024
Not only was this man discriminated against because of his ethnicity, the woman went further by saying to the largely Asian demonstration: "All of you are corrupt. The drugs come from you."
One look at the streets of Vancouver and it's clear the drugs are not coming from the people in this crowd opposed to the drugs but from those who foster the use of them by handing out free supply and drug kits, including needles, that help people do drugs.
They create crime, hardship and despair.
We see this here in the Greater Toronto Area too. Drugs are ruining society and the approach currently employed is failing miserably.
Radical activist spews racist tirade against a Chinese man trying to protect kids from a drug injection site the NDP & Liberal governments are forcing into Richmond.
Help me fight against the NDP/Liberals pushing drugs on the Chinese community:
- Pierre Poilievre (@PierrePoilievre) February 13, 2024
Poilievre knows this and is vowing to change it.
"Radical activist spews racist tirade against a Chinese man trying to protect kids from a drug injection site the NDP & Liberal governments are forcing into Richmond. Help me fight against the NDP/Liberals pushing drugs on the Chinese community," he posted to his X account.
He also linked to a motion to "stop the drug injection site in Richmond" that states that "after eight years of Justin Trudeau's taxpayer-funded distribution of hard drugs has caused harm and addiction" and the "sites are grounds for criminal activity and make neighbourhoods unsafe," Conservatives call on the government to "stop handing our taxpayer funds to addicts."
Raucous end of the great drug debate at Richmond city council: Coun. Kash Heed's motion to ask Vancouver Coastal Health to consider a supervised injection/smoking site in Richmond passes late Tuesday. #richmondbc #bcpoli #cdnpoli
- Bob Mackin (@bobmackin) February 14, 2024
But history has shown the people who believe in this "harm reduction strategy" will not do that.
On treating a fellow Canadian of Asian dissent so horribly, history shows the racism charges only come to people like the Convoy truckers who are opposed to Liberal policies.
People pushing progressive policies like free drugs for addicts seem to skate.
jwarmington@postmedia.com

Subject: Drugs; Activists; Racism; Social networks

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks

Location: Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Document 273 of 356

'Absolutely terrible'; Pro-Hamas supporters surrounding Jewish hospital a hate crime

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/absolutely-terrible-pro-hamas-supporters/docview/2926319810/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Absolutely+terrible%27%3B+Pro-Hamas+supporters+surrounding+Jewish+hospital+a+hate+crime&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When you have a female Jewish doctor's car surrounded by Pro-Hamas protesters shouting "intefadeh," while blocking people at an iconic hospital with Jewish roots, it's no longer a protest but a hate crime.
The prime minister, premier, police and medical community agree.
"Interfering with the operations of a hospital is not acceptable," said Toronto Police, adding that officers are "investigating several incidents that occurred in front of Mount Sinai Hospital."
Urging "decency," Premier Doug Ford called out masked, Pro-Hamas protesters who pushed things too far Monday night in front of the "sacred" Jewish-inspired hospital. One demonstrator even scaled the front of Mount Sinai and got up on the University Ave. hospital's awning. Dr. David Jacobs posted on X about a female doctor of Jewish faith seeing her car "swarmed" with people "banging on it."
But with only two police officers present, they got away.
"I think it's absolutely terrible," said Premier Doug Ford. "Folks, get some decency. Have common sense. These hospitals are there to save lives. Mount Sinai did everything they could to save my brother's life - he was in there.
"They are an incredible hospital - along with all the hospitals across the province," he added. "They are there to help people ... do not break the law. Because that is exactly what happened, you broke the law."
Videos certainly support Ford's contention.
"As we have said before, officers use their discretion during large crowd demonstrations and even if arrests are not deemed safe to make at the time, investigations will continue and charges can be laid at a later date," said Toronto Police spokesperson Stephanie Sayer.
"TPS will be providing an increased police presence along hospital row to ensure that essential hospital services and emergency routes remain accessible" and they are "appealing to anyone who witnessed the events (Monday) evening or who experienced harassment to contact police."
There was also a counter-protest by an emergency room physician, Dr. Raghu Venugopal, who first posted on X that he was coming to the demonstration and later put up a video explaining such behaviour was inappropriate on such "hallowed" ground.
Richard Robertson, director of research and advocacy at B'nai Brith Canada noted that "the hospital was founded during a dark period in Canadian history when Jewish doctors were excluded from working at other hospitals."
He said this was "a new low for anti-Israel protesters in Canada," and added that the "sanctity of the patients, their loved ones, and those vested with the responsibility of tending to the sick, was violated." Mayor Olivia Chow, who has undergone cancer treatment at Mount Sinai, took to social media to say there's no place in Toronto for such hate.
In April 2005, Chow, then a Toronto councillor, told the CBC that she underwent surgery the previous year for thyroid cancer.
Surgeons removed her thyroid gland, and she later received radioactive iodine treatment to deal with any lingering cancer cells.
"My family has a long history with Mount Sinai. My father passed there, both my late husband (former federal NDP leader Jack Layton) and I had cancer treatments there," the municipal leader posted on X. "(Monday's) actions at Mount Sinai Hospital, a hospital founded by Toronto's Jewish community, were unacceptable. Targeting Jewish institutions is anti-Semitic and hate has no place in our city."
Councillor Brad Bradford called it "full-blown anti-Semitism" and urged Chow to "end this anti-Semitism intimidation."
Referencing a federal government law against protesting at hospitals, Ford implored people to stop.
"I don't care what you are protesting," said the premier. "You want to protest, go to City Hall, come down to Queen's Park, jump up and down, do whatever you want."
I asked a second question about the blatant anti-Semitism of this action, and what happened earlier in the evening when Aroma Cafe was vandalized once again.
"It doesn't matter if it's Islamophobia (or) anti-Semitism, folks, we have 120 nationalities here in Ontario, 200 languages are spoken," said Ford. "We pride ourselves around the world for everyone to come here and get along and live side by side, work side by side.
"We need to live peacefully together. That's my message to the people of Ontario: We live in a peaceful society. I am asking everyone, please get along. Let's work together. Get along."
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau agreed in an X post Tuesday.
"The demonstration at Mount Sinai Hospital yesterday was reprehensible. Hospitals are places for treatment and care, not protests and intimidation," said Trudeau. "I strongly condemn this display of anti-Semitism. In Toronto and across Canada, we stand with Jewish communities against this hate."
Many other leaders commented. "An embassy? Nope. A consulate? Nope. A government office? Nope," Conservative deputy leader Melissa Lantsman, the MP for Thornhill, posted on X. "The mob chose patients, their families, nurses, doctors and all those helping to save lives inside of a hospital. They chose Jews, because it's Mount Sinai Hospital. So gross."
The outrage crossed party lines. "Targeting Mount Sinai Hospital to incite intifada against Israel and the Jewish people is flagrantly anti-Semitic and undermines the health and safety of the patients and medical professionals," Liberal MP Marco Mendicino, who respresents Eglinton-Lawrence, posted on X. "The torrent of hatred against Jews and Jewish institutions must stop!" Deborah Lyons, Canada's special envoy on stamping out anti-Semitism and Holocaust denialism, wrote on X that she planned to call "Toronto's mayor and chief of police today to discuss how they will put a stop to this despicable targeting and attempted intimidation of Mount Sinai Hospital and Jews across Toronto and Canada."
Independent MP Kevin Vuong demanded to know "what action, steps" Chow was gong to take to protect health-care workers, patients and members of the Jewish community.
"Has she done the absolute bare minimum of ensuring @TorontoPolice's clear on their responsibility to protect hospitals & health-care workers per Bill C3?" he posted on X.
Based on what has happened since Hamas' Oct. 7 slaughter of innocent Israeli's, when it comes to anti-Semitism in Toronto, it will likely happen again. jwarmington@postmedia.com @joe_warmington

Subject: Cancer; Emergency medical care; Hate crimes; Jewish life & ethics; Diplomatic & consular services; Antisemitism; Hospitals; Jewish people; Thyroid gland; Criminal investigations; Demonstrations & protests; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Hospitals

Location: Canada; Israel

Company / organization: Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Identifier / keyword: female; jewish; surrounded; protesters; shouting; intefadeh

Title: 'Absolutely terrible'; Pro-Hamas supporters surrounding Jewish hospital a hate crime

Publication title: The Toronto Sun; Toronto

First page: A.4

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 14, 2024

column: Opinion

Section: News

Councillor Brad Bradford called it "full blown anti-Semitism" and urged Chow to "end this anti-Semitism intimidation."

"I don't care what you are protesting," said the premier. "You want to protest, go to City Hall, come down to Queen's Park, jump up and down, do whatever you want."
I was born at this hospital. My parents, Holocaust survivors, didn't feel comfortable at any other hospital. What's happening here is absolutely disgusting. Shame on these haters.
- Marty York (@MartyYork) February 13, 2024
I asked a second question about the blatant anti-Semitism of this action, and what happened earlier in the evening when Aroma Cafe was vandalized once again?
"It doesn't matter if it's Islamophobia, (or) anti-Semitism, folks we have 120 nationalities here in Ontario, 200 languages are spoken," said Ford. "We pride ourselves around the world for everyone to come here and get along and live side by side, work side by side.
"We need to live peacefully together. That's my message to the people of Ontario: ... We live in a peaceful society. I am asking everyone, 'Please get along. Let's work together. Get along.'"
The demonstration at Mount Sinai Hospital yesterday was reprehensible. Hospitals are places for treatment and care, not protests and intimidation.
I strongly condemn this display of antisemitism. In Toronto and across Canada, we stand with Jewish communities against this hate.
- Justin Trudeau (@JustinTrudeau) February 13, 2024
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau agreed in an X post Tuesday.
"The demonstration at Mount Sinai Hospital yesterday was reprehensible. Hospitals are places for treatment and care, not protests and intimidation," said Trudeau. "I strongly condemn this display of anti-Semitism. In Toronto and across Canada, we stand with Jewish communities against this hate."
Many other leaders commented.
An Embassy? Nope.
A Consulate? Nope.
A government office? Nope.
The mob chose patients, their families, nurses, doctors and all those helping to save lives inside of a hospital.
They chose Jews, because it's Mount Sinai Hospital.
So gross. #cdnpoli
- Melissa Lantsman (@MelissaLantsman) February 13, 2024
"An Embassy? Nope. A Consulate? Nope. A government office? Nope," Conservative deputy leader Melissa Lantsman, the MP for Thornhill, posted on X. "The mob chose patients, their families, nurses, doctors and all those helping to save lives inside of a hospital. They chose Jews, because it's Mount Sinai Hospital. So gross."
The outrage crossed party lines.
"Targeting Mount Sinai Hospital to incite intifada against Israel and the Jewish people is flagrantly anti-Semitic and undermines the health and safety of the patients and medical professionals," Liberal MP Marco Mendicino, who respresents Eglinton-Lawrence, posted on X. "The torrent of hatred against Jews and Jewish institutions must stop!"
We will be calling Toronto's Mayor and Chief of Police today to discuss how they will put a stop to this despicable targeting and attempted intimidation of Mount Sinai Hospital and Jews across Toronto and Canada. Leaders - at all levels - need to make clear that this is...
- Deborah Lyons (@DeborahLyonsSE) February 13, 2024
Deborah Lyons, Canada's special envoy on stamping out anti-Semitism and Holocaust denialism, wrote on X that she planned to call "Toronto's mayor and chief of police today to discuss how they will put a stop to this despicable targeting and attempted intimidation of Mount Sinai Hospital and Jews across Toronto and Canada."
Independent MP Kevin Vuong demanded to know "what action, steps" Chow was gong to take to protect health-care workers, patients and members of the Jewish community.
"Has she done the absolute bare minimum of ensuring @TorontoPolice's clear on their responsibility to protect hospitals & health-care workers per Bill C3?" he posted to X.
Good. What action steps will @MayorOliviaChow be taking to protect healthcare workers, patients & #Toronto's Jewish community?
Has she done the absolute bare minimum of ensuring @TorontoPolice's clear on their responsibility to protect hospitals & healthcare workers per Bill C3?
- Kevin Vuong (@KevinVuongMP) February 13, 2024
Based on what has happened since Hamas' Oct. 7 slaughter of innocent Israeli's, when it comes to anti-Semitism in Toronto, it will likely happen again.

Document 275 of 356

A price to be paid; Grapes in Rielly's corner, says no team likes to be shown up

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/price-be-paid-grapes-riellys-corner-says-no-team/docview/2925767922/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+price+to+be+paid%3B+Grapes+in+Rielly%27s+corner%2C+says+no+team+likes+to+be+shown+up&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Morgan Rielly may be pleased to learn he's got the Coach in his corner.
"I say he'll get two games," Don Cherry said Sunday on his Grapevine Podcast, with his son Tim and daughter Cindy, following the Ottawa Senators'5-3 win Saturday night over the Toronto Maple Leafs.
Time will tell what the NHL decides when they hold a Tuesday hearing for the Maple Leafs defenceman, who cross-checked showboating Senators forward Ridly Greig on Saturday night.
But Cherry said when Greig took a slapshot into the empty net to ice the win for Ottawa at the Canadian Tire Centre to rub salt into the Leafs'wounds, he broke an unwritten NHL code about showing up a team when they are down.
"I am glad Rielly was on and I'm glad he did it," said Cherry, the former coach of the Boston Bruins and longtime star of Coach's Corner on Hockey Night in Canada. "I know there is no rule in the book that says you don't do that, but you just don't do that."
Rielly certainly didn't like it.
After Greig wound up for his empty-net slapshot, Rielly gave the rookie a two-handed crosscheck that appeared to hit him on the face and head before Greig doubled over on the ice.
Cherry pointed to the unwritten rule that you don't "spike the football" in hockey. He raised the question of what was going through Greig's mind and why he did it.
Grapes, who also had Rielly's back when his playoff goal was called back against Florida last spring, has a theory.
"I think the reason he did it is because the Leaf fans were going, 'Go Leafs go'(in Ottawa) and that's why he did it."
Certainly, the arena seemed to be filled with a majority of Leafs Nation. Greig was making his point that the home team was going to win this round of the Battle of Ontario.
Rielly made his own statement. "It was a spur-of-themoment thing," Cherry said.
While he thinks NHL disciplinarian George Parros will hand out a two-game suspension, and not six games as many pundits say, he did add that Rielly "used the stick" as part of his retribution. But he added, "I think he meant to get him in the shoulder, but he got him on the back of the neck."
That said, Cherry took a little dig at Greig when he teased, "He laid there pretty good."
Ouch - times two. It's great to hear Grapes'perspective because, as Tim said, "Everybody wants to know what you think."
That's why he should be back on Hockey Night in Canada. Maybe even Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford agree - since Grapes said he got notes for his recent 90th birthday from both.
"I want to thank everybody for their kind wishes," Cherry said.
Incidentally, Grapes also picked the Kansas City Chiefs to win the Super Bowl. But it was his take on the Rielly incident that people were curious about.
My feeling was it was an illegal, dirty play that could have hurt Greig, who didn't break a rule and can score anyway he chooses. I also noted the Leafs didn't show this kind of fire in the belly when the Bruins'Brad Marchand tripped Timothy Liljegren can-openerstyle into the boards in November or last year when Florida Panthers forward Sam Bennett knocked rookie Matthew Knies out of the playoffs with a concussion.
But as Grapes has said, Leafs players and coaching staff talked about that lack of response and vowed to never let that happen gain.
Whether it's on the lacrossestyle Michigan goals, in which players put the puck on the blade of their stick and fling it into the net, or an over-the-top celebration of a goal that embarrasses the other team, Cherry says that, on the ice, the players don't like that kind of one-upmanship - and you pay a price if you decide to do something like that. jwarmington@postmedia.com @joe_warmington

Subject: Professional hockey

Location: Florida; United States--US; Canada

Identifier / keyword: morgan; rielly; pleased; learn; coach; corner

Title: A price to be paid; Grapes in Rielly's corner, says no team likes to be shown up

Document 276 of 356

Whining conservatives need to grow up

Author: Agar, Jerry

Publication info: Agar, Jerry. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/whining-conservatives-need-grow-up/docview/2925767519/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Whining+conservatives+need+to+grow+up&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=Agar%2C+Jerry&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you want to criticize Justin Trudeau, you'll have to get behind me in line.
But some of you conservatives need to grow up.
CTV News reports, "An Ontario brewery that hosted Prime Minister Justin Trudeau says it's been inundated with hundreds of hateful messages since then."
Trudeau visited the Brewer's Pantry in Bowmanville, Ont., to show support for Robert Rock, the Liberal Party candidate for the Durham federal by-election.
Brewer's Pantry posted on social media Friday: "I am not political. I am not a politician. I do not have thick skin. These are comments about my business that I am passionate about, and very proud of. Your comments and abusive phone calls hurt me."
One user wrote on Facebook: "Small business owners in Bowmanville will not forget your stance. You are not one of us."
To that person, I would say you don't represent the entire business community. Don't think so highly of yourself that you assume your hateful nature is that of everyone else.
Some of your fellow businesspeople are Liberals. Get over it.
You don't have to like Trudeau - I certainly don't - but if you are actually a business person I am surprised to learn you can survive if you will only do business with those with whom you agree.
And a note especially to those people who post such messages anonymously.
Man up. Don't be a coward. I like to think that one of the differences, as a generalization, between conservatives and liberals is that conservatives prefer to operate with the facts and liberals are overly emotional.
Too many Liberal policies come from an emotional reaction, in my view.
But in this case, when conservatives start acting like spoiled four-year-olds, it is embarrassing to the team.
For those of you writing that the business should have declined the opportunity to host the political event, put your money where your big mouth is and offer to compensate monetarily any operator that turns down Liberal business.
But I also have a message for liberals. Those of you who wrote that the attacks on the business show the "gang mentality" of the party that Pierre Poilievre is building, to quote one online fool, stop and take a look at your own team.
Liberals have never been shy about airing their hateful rhetoric. If you don't see that you are just not looking - or trying.
I am a conservative telling people claiming to be conservative that there is a mile of difference between disagreeing with or even attacking policy and partisan political action; the deliberate attempt to damage the business of a person who merely opened his doors to everyone.
The reality is that he would also welcome you.
Durhamregion.com quotes owner Chris Aucoin: "Not one of these keyboard warriors, or whatever you call them, has come into my store and said, 'Chris, you're an ass----,'nobody has come in and said that. If somebody came in and said they disagreed with what I did, I'd say, 'Yeah sure, you want a beer?'" Would you be gracious enough to accept it? I thought conservatives believed in personal responsibility.
As I said, some of you conservatives need to show some personal control and responsibility. Grow up.
Stop acting like liberals.
Agar is the 9 a.m. to noon host on NEWSTALK1010 jerry.agar@sunmedia.ca

Subject: Social networks; Breweries

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks Breweries

People: Trudeau, Justin

Identifier / keyword: criticize; justin; trudeau; behind; conservatives; reports

Local artist says he's 'p-ed off' Loblaw over shirts with lampooned logo

Author: Wilford, Denette

Publication info: Wilford, Denette. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/local-artist-says-hes-p-ed-off-loblaw-over-shirts/docview/2925744874/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Local+artist+says+he%27s+%27p-ed+off%27+Loblaw+over+shirts+with+lampooned+logo&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Wilford%2C+Denette&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For months, there have been calls to boycott grocery giants over their skyrocketing prices as Canadians grapple with the cost of living and inflation.
Loblaw, in particular, bears the brunt of the public's ire as Canadians take aim at company president Galen Weston and call out prices in their stores.
However, Loblaw may have met its match in a Toronto-based artist who is using his designs to get to the company another way.
Cristofer Lam has come up with his own version of the grocer's name - by transforming the Loblaws logo into "Roblaw$" and putting his parody merchandise on shirts, mugs and tote bags.
Orange slices and takeout pasta latest on Loblaw haters' hit list
'WHICH ONE IS THE PARODY?': Spoof Metrolinx ads seem to be 'relatable' to some
Loblaw 50% off stickers to return after public anger over discount reduction
Lam even switched up Loblaw's "Live Life Well" slogan to "Live Life Hungry" and revamped No Frills to "No Shame."
That obviously didn't sit well with Loblaw, who told Lam that his spoof merchandise is too close to their logo.
"So I've pissed off another major Canadian company," he wrote on his Facebook page
"This time it is Loblaws coming after me for IP infringement for my Roblaws shirt. This one I can't understand at all. The font isn't the same, the name is totally different and I made an entirely new logo, it just uses the same colours."
RECOMMENDED VIDEO <div class="pmYTPlayerContainer video-container" id="b6rs4im4( function() { pnLoadVideo( "videos", "b6rs4im4", "pn_video_977094", "", "", {"controls":1,"autoplay":0,"is_mobile":""} ); } )();
Lam directed interested buyers to his website, Instant Distractions, adding that he is releasing a new Doug Ford T-shirt when the legislature returns for its spring session.
Lam had previously targeted Metrolinx with his merchandise before he was forced to change his design. However, he was able to get around the issue with Metrolinx by repeatedly changing the logo, BlogTO reported.
On his site, he has a shirt that says "Metrostinx" and his take on the Rogers logo with "Robbers" below it.
A spokesperson for Loblaw told the Toronto Sun, "Like any other business, we protect our brand and trademarks from unofficial merchandise or products, particularly if the intent behind them is misleading to customers or malicious."

WARMINGTON: Don Cherry in Morgan Rielly's corner on crosscheck on Sens hot-dog Greig

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/warmington-don-cherry-morgan-riellys-corner-on/docview/2925469706/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=WARMINGTON%3A+Don+Cherry+in+Morgan+Rielly%27s+corner+on+crosscheck+on+Sens+hot-dog+Greig&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Morgan Rielly may be pleased to learn that on his crosscheck on a showboating Ottawa Senator that could get him suspended, he's got the Coach in his corner.
"I say he'll get two games," Don Cherry said Sunday on his Grapevine Podcast, with his son Tim and daughter Cindy, following the Ottawa Senators' 5-3 win Saturday night over the Toronto Maple Leafs.
Time will tell what the NHL decides when they hold a hearing for the Maple Leafs defenceman on Tuesday, but Cherry said when Ridly Greig took a slapshot into the empty net to ice the win for the Senators at the Canadian Tire Centre in the capital and rub salt into the Leafs' wounds, he broke an unwritten NHL code about showing up a team when they are down.
"I am glad Rielly was on and I'm glad he did it," said Cherry, the former coach of the Boston Bruins and longtime star of Coach's Corner on Hockey Night in Canada. "I know there is no rule in the book that says you don't do that but you just don't do that."
Morgan Rielly of the Toronto Maple Leafs stands over Ridly Greig of the Ottawa Senators after being cross checked in the head following his empty net goal at Canadian Tire Centre on Feb. 10, 2024 in Ottawa.
Rielly certainly didn't like it. After Greig wound up for empty-net slapshot, Rielly give the rookie a two-handed crosscheck that appeared to hit him on the face and head before Greig doubled over on the ice.
Cherry pointed to the unwritten rule that you don't 'spike the football' in hockey. He raised the question of what was going through Greig's mind and why he did it.
Grapes, who also had Rielly's back when his playoff goal was called back against Florida last spring, has a theory.
Here are my thoughts on taking a slapshot into an open net and more.#DonCherry #NHL #hockey #Leafs #Senators #Riely #BattleofOntario
- Don Cherry (@CoachsCornerDC) February 12, 2024
"I think the reason he did is because the Leaf fans were going 'Go Leafs Go' (in Ottawa) and that's why he did it."
Certainly the arena seemed to be filled with a majority of Leafs Nation. Grieg was making his point that the home team was going to win this round of the Battle of Ontario.
Veteran Rielly made his own statement.
"It was a spur of the moment thing," said Cherry.
A five or six game suspension for a career non offender like Morgan Rielly would border on crazy and incredulous - which is exactly how George Parros seems to operate the apparent NHL Department of Safety.
- Steve Simmons (@simmonssteve) February 11, 2024
While he thinks NHL disciplinarian George Parros will hand out a two-game suspension, and not six games as many pundits say, he did add that Rielly "used the stick" as part of his retribution though he said, "I think he meant to get him in the shoulder but he got him on the back of the neck."
That said, Cherry took a little dig at Grieg when he teased, "He laid there pretty good."
Ouch. Times two.
It's great to hear Don's perspective because as Tim said, "Everybody wants to know what you think."
That's why he should be back on Hockey Night in Canada!
Maybe even Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford agree - since Grapes said he received notes for his recent 90(th) birthday from both. "I want to thank everybody for their kind wishes," Cherry said.
Just want to thank you all you people for the birthday wishes. Much appreciated.
- Don Cherry (@CoachsCornerDC) February 7, 2024
Incidentally, Grapes also picked the Kansas City Chiefs to win the Super Bowl. But it was his take on the Rielly incident that people were curious about.
My feeling was it was an illegal, dirty play that could have hurt Grieg who didn't break a rule and can score anyway he chooses. I also noted the Leafs didn't show this kind of fire in the belly when the Bruins' Brad Marchand tripped Timothy Liljegren can opener-style into the boards in November or last year when Florida Panthers forward Sam Bennett put rookie Matthew Knies out of the playoffs with a concussion.
But as Grapes has explained - the Leafs players and coaching staff talked about that lack of response and vowed to never let that happen gain.
Whether it's on the Michigan-style lacrosse-like goals where players put the puck on the blade of their stick and fling it into the net or an over-the-top celebration of a goal that embarrasses the other team, Cherry says on the ice, the players don't like that kind of one-upmanship.
And you pay a price if you decide to do something like that. Greig paid that price. Rielly may as well in terms of games and salary missed and perhaps a fine.
If Cherry had a say in it, Rielly would be back in the lineup on Saturday, Feb. 17 against the Anaheim Ducks.
jwarmington@postmedia.com
Rielly's date with NHL player safety will likely mean hefty suspension
Leafs vow to do better in sticking together after Liljegren-Marchand incident
WARMINGTON: Nice thing about free speech is we can say this: Happy 90th birthday, Don Cherry!

Letters to the Editor, Feb. 12, 2024

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/letters-editor-feb-12-2024/docview/2925242584/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: AWAY WITH THE FOB
One of the ways to reduce the number of car thefts is to return to a regular key and discontinue the key fob. The key fob reproduces as quickly and easily as Nick Cannon. This isn't even going to be considered because the consumer is usually forced to buy another car after their original vehicle was stolen. That means the car manufacturers make more money along with the government in taxes, so Premier Doug Ford's tough guy speech the other day is really just smoke and mirrors.
Dave Watson Pickering
(The car theft epidemic the country is facing is unprecedented. Even though we commend many of our law-enforcement for shutting down these car theft rings, too many spring up to replace them. Manufacturers are going to have to do their part)
TOUGH TIME FOR THEFT
Automakers claim that, even with new security features, they cannot keep up with the surge in car thefts. Easy solution: Mandatory 10-year prison sentence for auto theft. I guarantee results.
M. Benson Brampton
(It's a start)
AN OLD APPROACH
There are too many people walking the streets, whether they are drug addicts or mentally unstable, who can harm you or your family. Just ask Prime Minister Justin Trudeau because the same person accused of threatening to kill him just allegedly threatened to do the same thing to Conservative MP Eric Duncan ("Cornwall man accused of threatening to kill PM, MP was released from custody on Feb. 1," Postmedia News, Feb. 9). We need to open the mental institutions back up to get these people off the streets to protect our families.
W. Johnson
(Something needs to be done as the current system cannot handle the situation)
PAY US BACK LIBERALS
The Canadian Taxpayers Federation recently obtained government records that indicated the Trudeau government billed taxpayers $485,000 for a Liberal cabinet retreat in Charlottetown, P.E.I., from Aug. 21-23, 2023. While many Canadians struggle to buy groceries and pay for housing, the Trudeau Liberals possess the audacity to squander almost $500,000 to wine and dine at a luxury location, billing taxpayers for their opulence. That the purpose of this luxurious gathering was to discuss the affordability and housing challenges of average Canadians, assuredly adds insult to injury. The arrogance and sense of entitlement of these greedy Liberal MPs is utterly repugnant. Canadians should be outraged and disgusted with this collection of greedy parasites, and demand that the Trudeau Liberals fully reimburse taxpayers, with interest.
Harley Whitlock Brantford
(The only way taxpayers are going to get their money back is by tossing the Trudeau Liberals after the next federal election)

Document 282 of 356

Dining on taxpayers; PM will punish Canadians with big beer levy hike

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/dining-on-taxpayers-pm-will-punish-canadians-with/docview/2924603361/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dining+on+taxpayers%3B+PM+will+punish+Canadians+with+big+beer+levy+hike&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: About 50% of all restaurants and bars in Canada are currently losing money.
And on April 1, the Trudeau Liberals are going to make it even harder to turn things around with another increase in taxes on beer, wine and spirits.
Not only that, the tax hike is once again promising to be a big one.
The annual "escalator tax" is tied to the rate of inflation and this year is set at an increase of 4.7%.
The idea of automatically increasing the tax on alcohol each year was brought about by then-finance minister Bill Morneau in 2017 when inflation was low - for the past few years, though, inflation has been out of control and adding on more taxes won't help.
The hospitality industry has yet to fully recover from the pandemic and is still dealing with higher prices for wages, food and other supplies, so the last thing it needs is another tax hike that will increase costs.
Beer Canada, the lobby group for brewers across the country, notes that Canada already has the highest beer taxes in the G7, with an average of 46% of the price you pay going to the government. Sales of beer are down 6.5% since 2019 and the sale of beer in bars and restaurants is down 20% compared to before the pandemic.
Adding on extra costs hurts the estimated 21,000 people working for breweries across the country and the many thousands more working in bars and restaurants.
Last year, after a sustained pressure campaign, the Trudeau Liberals capped the alcohol tax increase at just 2%. While I think they should stop increasing the tax every year, the least they could do is cap it, but they refuse to, and they refuse to give the struggling hospitality industry any rhyme or reason why.
Ontario has a similar annual escalator tax on beer that was brought in by the previous Liberal government. On Friday, Premier Doug Ford announced that the government would freeze the tax rate as it has done every year since coming to office in 2018.
That's what the Trudeau government should be doing, but it's too stubborn.
The Liberals should consider that they're down in the polls to the Conservatives by a whopping 15 points, according to the latest figures from Leger.
Even worse, they'are down 20 points among men, with the Tories getting 43% voter support among men compared to just 23% for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and the Liberals.
If Trudeau and his brain trust were smart, they'd have announced that they too are freezing the tax, maybe even make a big show about it ahead of the Super Bowl - an event half the country will be watching Sunday, according to Maru Public Opinion. In case people in Trudeau's office don't get the connection, lots of beer will be consumed, mostly by men, during the game.
It would be a popular move with voters, and it would help ensure the survival of jobs in the hospitality industry, in breweries big and small, in wineries and distilleries.
Instead of working with the industry and doing the right thing, the Liberals are ignoring the industry. So the industry is fighting back and once again has an advertising campaign featuring Bob and Doug McKenize telling the government to "take off" the tax hike.
Ads are airing on 16 radio stations from St. John's, N.L., to Vancouver that lean heavily toward a male audience. Just like this is a tax that consumers and the industry don't need, this is a fight the government doesn't need and it should back off from now.
Trudeau likes to say he has our backs, he could show it with this move instead of showing he's just got a hand in our back pocket as he lifts our wallet.

Subject: Taxes; Tax rates; Beer; Restaurants; Hospitality industry; Pandemics; Tax increases; Breweries

Business indexing term: Subject: Taxes Tax rates Restaurants Hospitality industry Tax increases Breweries

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: restaurants; canada; currently; losing; money; april

Title: Dining on taxpayers; PM will punish Canadians with big beer levy hike

Publication title: The Toronto Sun; Toronto

First page: A.3

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 10, 2024

column: Opinion

Section: News

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.
