Trudeau, Ford sign $3.1B health deal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-ford-sign-3-1b-health-deal/docview/2924109832/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau%2C+Ford+sign+%243.1B+health+deal&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford will announce details Friday of a $3.1-billion infusion aimed at improving health care, according to a report. The federal and Ontario leaders will meet in King City, north of Toronto, to announce the deal, the Toronto Star reported Thursday night. In exchange for the new cash, the Ford government would commit spending to establish new primary care teams aimed at boosting access to family doctors, the outlet reported. The province reportedly said it would add hundreds of new family physicians and nurse practitioners along with thousands of new nurses and personal support workers. Enrolment in health-care education programs will increase by 700, with a specific allotment for northern Ontario. Some of the money will be aimed at increasing youth mental health services and substance abuse services in rural, remote and Indigenous communities. - Sun staff

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Ontario Premier Doug Ford will announce details on Friday of a $3.1-billion infusion aimed at improving health care, according to a report.
The federal and Ontario leaders will meet in King City, north of Toronto, to announce the deal, the Toronto Star reported Thursday night.
In exchange for the new cash, the Ford government would commit spending to establish new primary care teams aimed at boosting access to family doctors, the outlet reported.
The province reportedly said it would add hundreds of new family physicians and nurse practitioners along with thousands of new nurses and personal support workers.

Enrollment in health-care education programs will increase by 700, with a specific allotment for Northern Ontario.
Some of the money will be aimed at increasing youth mental health services and substance abuse services in rural, remote and Indigenous communities, the report said, and new funding will modernize the collection of medical data.

Document 286 of 356

WARMINGTON: Canada keeps shampoo off planes but not stolen SUVs off ships

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/warmington-canada-keeps-shampoo-off-planes-not/docview/2924032242/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=WARMINGTON%3A+Canada+keeps+shampoo+off+planes+but+not+stolen+SUVs+off+ships&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You can't get a bottle of shampoo larger than 100 ml past security on a commercial flight, so how is it that 4,000-pound stolen cars, luxury SUVs or pickup trucks can be shipped across the ocean without detection?
Is it okay for media to ask Prime Minister Justin Trudeau that question? Or should we just blame Opposition Leader Pierre Poilievre for daring to do so?
We're not talking about a tube of toothpaste here or there sneaking by.
Canada has between 90,000 and 105,000 vehicles stolen each year - many of which now on roads in Europe or Africa through the stolen vehicle cartels. None of it has ever added up.
"Now is the time to be bold in developing partnerships with manufacturers, engineering experts and others to harness rapidly developing technology and enable the talent in our country to find new solutions," said @TPSMyronDemkiw at the National Auto Theft Summit in Ottawa.
- Toronto Police (@TorontoPolice) February 8, 2024
But now, finally, government called together the stakeholders to Ottawa on Thursday for the National Auto Theft Car Summit.
Better late than never.
"Vehicle theft is an epidemic in this country," Toronto Police Chief Myron Demkiw told them.
Toronto Police Chief Myron Demkiw.
Halton Regional Police Chief Steve Tanner, Peel Regional Police Chief Nish Duraiappah and OPP Commissioner Thomas Carrique have said the same.
But government has not been listening - until now.
"Toronto has experienced a dramatic spike in auto thefts over the last several years - more, by far, than any other city in Canada," Demkiw said. "In 2023, there were more than 12,000 vehicles stolen in Toronto alone."
He explained that amounts to 34 vehicles stolen every day, or one every 40 minutes.
Here at the National Summit on Auto Theft, we're working with partners from all orders of government and organizations to deliver a smart and robust plan. Together, we're working to fight against the rise of organized crime and keep Canadians safe.
- Arif Virani (@viraniarif) February 8, 2024
A lot of those vehicles are loaded into containers, transported to the Port of Montreal and then shipped abroad while our insurance rates skyrocket.
And no one in authority has any answer for this?
Government can use red-light cameras to issue fines to drivers or freeze people's bank accounts for protesting, but they seem to be stumped when it comes to combatting organized crime. The international crime players manage to stay one step ahead.
Liberals are asleep at the wheel while career car thieves and organized crime make Canada a hot spot for stolen cars.
Sign to support our common sense plan to hit the brakes on car theft:
- Pierre Poilievre (@PierrePoilievre) February 8, 2024
While police do make arrests, recover vehicles and dismantle auto theft rings, our justice system grants bail to those charged almost right away.
"We know these stolen vehicles often wind up leaving Toronto, ending up sold around the world by organized criminal groups," said Demkiw, who was joined at the conference by Deputy Chief of the Specialized Operations Command.
"Stealing cars is lucrative. Demand is incredibly high. Criminals migrate towards an easy pay day," said Demkiw. "With little skill, and few consequences, one can steal a vehicle and get cash in return."
The Chief made it very clear he's seeing a rise in carjackings and home invasions.
"I am very concerned about the escalation of violence, threats and intimidation, where all sorts of weapons and firearms are being used to steal vehicles," said Demkiw. "I am also concerned about the ability of the justice system to deal with repeat offenders."
Halton Regional Police have disrupted an auto theft network and recovered multiple stolen vehicles.
The courts and the federal government have dropped the ball. We have seen victims have their own tracking devices on vehicles they watch travel across the ocean and no one can do anything about it.
I have written about vehicles being sold offshore with Ontario licences plates still attached. Now Trudeau and his, after the fact, band-aid approach has come up with $28 million to increase security at the port.
It's a start but they need to spend that money wisely.
WARMINGTON: Police chief warns auto theft now at 'epidemic proportions'
WARMINGTON: Dude, where's my car? Stolen Ontario vehicles may be gone to Ghana but can be found online in 60 seconds
Auto theft probe, Project Stallion, trots 228 accused before courts
WARMINGTON: Nice to see victim of crime turn tables on car thief
The truth is this is not hard. The technology - from tracking to drones to registration rules that make whoever locks the container responsible for all costs of lost or stolen cargo - is available to stop all of this.
Just listen to Demkiw and Johnson, Tanner, Duraiappah and Carrique. Let them lock up the thieves. When police catch the bad guys, make them pay. Deport them if they are not Canadian.
Put modern scanning equipment at the ports or along the train and motor routes that look at every container with the same robust enthusiasm as they did trucker convoy leader Tamara Lich. Treat the real criminals the way they treat political prisoners.
Passing all of the cost onto the manufacturers and the consumers to have to put in extra tracking devices is a cop out.
Government is supposed to keep Canadians safe. This out-of-control seizing of Canadian assets by out-of-country crime elements is on the Liberal government who have not done that. It's their fault.
To fix it, take the same zero-tolerance approach that they do at airports when it comes to bottles of shampoo, perfume and nail clippers.
jwarmington@postmedia.com

Subject: Organized crime; Automobile theft; Robbery; Vehicles

Location: Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Document 287 of 356

Montreal a 'parking lot for thieves'

Author: Bruemmer, Rene

Publication info: Bruemmer, Rene. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/montreal-parking-lot-thieves/docview/2923167444/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Montreal+a+%27parking+lot+for+thieves%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Bruemmer%2C+Rene&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Standing before a backdrop of shipping containers awaiting departure in the Port of Montreal, federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre held a campaign-style news conference Tuesday pledging to increase security measures to tackle a countrywide surge in car thefts.
Flanked by posters reading "stop the crime" and "stop car theft," Poilievre said that, "after eight years of Justin Trudeau," Canadian vehicles are being stolen at historically high rates.
Many are being shipped overseas from Montreal's port, which Poilievre dubbed "a parking lot for thieves."
If elected in 2025, Poilievre promised he'd invest in 24 X-ray scanners at a cost of $55 million for the country's major federal ports in Montreal, Vancouver, Halifax and Prince Rupert, B.C., and hire a special team of 75 Canada Border Services Agency officers to run the scanners "and catch stolen cars before they leave our country."
Thirty of those agents would be placed in Montreal.
To finance the new hires, Poilievre said he'd "fire useless management consultants at CBSA."
"Common-sense Conservatives will reverse Trudeau's reckless policies that have turned our federal ports into parking lots for stolen cars," he said.
His plan came days before the federal Liberals are set to hold a national summit on combating auto theft with police, border agents and industry executives.
The Insurance Bureau of Canada says auto theft has become a national crisis, with more than 200 vehicles stolen on average every day.
In the first half of last year, auto theft jumped 31% in Ontario and 17% in Quebec.
That came after a 50% jump in both provinces from 2021 to '22.
More than 9,500 cars were stolen in Montreal in 2022, and that figure was surpassed in '23.
The problem is adding a further financial burden to cash-strapped Canadians, Poilievre said.
Car theft claims in Ontario were up 329% in the first half of 2023, totalling $700 million in losses for insurance firms in Canada.
The Insurance Bureau of Canada estimates this costs every driver in Ontario an extra $130 a year.

Subject: Automobile theft; Robbery

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada

Document 288 of 356

Scrambled eglinton; It's 2024 (!) and we still have no idea when Crosstown LRT will finally open

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/scrambled-eglinton-2024-we-still-have-no-idea/docview/2922687900/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Scrambled+eglinton%3B+It%27s+2024+%28%21%29+and+we+still+have+no+idea+when+Crosstown+LRT+will+finally+open&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Some days I think the Eglinton Crosstown extension will open before the main LRT line does. Actually, I think the Ontario line subway might open before the Crosstown.
"We are all hard at work to bring this program to a close," Metrolinx president and CEO Phil Verster said Monday.
It's the beginning of February 2024 and we are awaiting an announcement, even a hint, on when a project that was supposed to be ready in 2020 will be available to the public. The Eglinton Crosstown line, which had construction begin in 2011 when Rob Ford was mayor and Dalton McGuinty was premier, has faced more delays than most projects.
"I will not open the service up if it's not safe and reliable," Verster said.
Given what we've witnessed in Ottawa, where a locally managed project was opened early by a Liberal mayor to help Liberal MPs win re-election, that's probably a good thing. The Ottawa LRT has seen derailments, shutdowns, signal problems, doors that refused to open and, eventually, a public inquiry.
Faced with a choice between delays and the disaster Ottawa is still dealing with, we should all take delays. But delays of four years and counting seem excessive. Surely there is a middle ground between opening too early and never at all.
At the end of April 2023, faced with reporting by the Toronto Sun that the tracks were not laid properly, Verster spoke of the more than 260 instances of work not conforming to contract details and specifications.
Speaking at the time of the track not being laid to specification, Verster made it sound like it would be a short delay.
"It will have a two-month impact at least on the completion," he said.
That was April 27, 2023. Here we are 285 days later and we still have no clue when the LRT line will open.
"We'll declare an opening three months in advance of the first services running," Verster said.
When that will be is anyone's guess.
Verster said in November that system integration tests were 15% complete and now they are 28% complete. He said 23 of 40 occupancy permits had been completed, but signalling and control systems were still issues to be worked out with software and testing issues also to be addressed.
"The most important thing is we're making systematic progress," Verster said.
It's systematic progress, but it's also incredibly slow progress.
The idea that the Ontario line or the Crosstown West Extension might come online before the main Eglinton Crosstown line isn't that crazy. These projects are moving at a pace that should embarrass those in charge of the Crosstown.
This kind of ineptitude is what would get most executives fired. Verster took over at Metrolinx in 2017, having been hired by the Kathleen Wynne Liberals after a less-than-stellar performance at ScotRail, but has managed to hang onto his job.
Verster's supporters - and not all around Premier Doug Ford support the man - say that he has moved key projects forward like the Ontario line and that problems at the Crosstown date back to the early days of the Liberal government getting the basics wrong. We are too early into these projects to confirm those claims, but Verster better hope they are right or that he can retire before we see the end results.
What Toronto residents know is that the Crosstown is an unmitigated disaster and he's been the man in charge for the last eight years. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Identifier / keyword: think; eglinton; crosstown; extension; before; actually

Title: Scrambled eglinton; It's 2024 (!) and we still have no idea when Crosstown LRT will finally open

Document 290 of 356

Life of 'Hurricane Hazel' to be celebrated on Feb. 14

Author: Stevenson, Jane

Publication info: Stevenson, Jane. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/life-hurricane-hazel-be-celebrated-on-feb-14/docview/2922560598/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Life+of+%27Hurricane+Hazel%27+to+be+celebrated+on+Feb.+14&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Stevenson%2C+Jane&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Feb. 14 is "Hurricane" Hazel McCallion Day.
The life of the former Mississauga mayor, who died at her home Jan. 29, 2023 from pancreatic cancer at age 101, will be celebrated Feb. 14 - her birthday - at Vic Johnston Community Centre, 335 Church St., in Mississauga's Streetsville area.
The celebration which runs from 11:30 a.m.-2 p.m. is being spearheaded by her son Peter. Organizers are putting up 1,000 posters in store windows inviting people to attend.
"Last year Premier Doug Ford declared Feb. 14 as Hazel McCallion Day in Ontario," Peter said in a statement.
"That was not only Hazel's birthday, it was the day we laid her to rest after a state funeral. These posters and the celebrations are a chance to keep that legacy alive, even if we have lost Hazel."

Document 291 of 356

HEIMPEL: Why Bonnie Crombie is betting against Justin Trudeau

Author: McLeod, Cynthia

Publication info: McLeod, Cynthia. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heimpel-why-bonnie-crombie-is-betting-against/docview/2922543121/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=HEIMPEL%3A+Why+Bonnie+Crombie+is+betting+against+Justin+Trudeau&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=McLeod%2C+Cynthia&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau is the only Canadian politician with a long bet on the carbon tax these days.
Newfoundland and Labrador Liberal Premier Andrew Furey is staunchly against it.
Manitoba NDP Premier Wab Kinew has said his province no longer needs it.
Most interesting is new Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, who's been the subject of ongoing carbon tax attacks from the Ontario PCs. Crombie hasn't ruled it out, saying it's something she would "study further." But Crombie also has gone to lengths to say that she's never made a pro-carbon tax statement.
After years, Ontarians have lined up against the carbon tax. Fifty-five per cent of Ontarians want the carbon tax either reduced or eliminated. Sixty-one per cent of Ontarians view the tax as ineffective. This is a very different environment from the Ontario where former Liberal leader Kathleen Wynne introduced a carbon price a decade ago. And a very different set of circumstances from Justin Trudeau's when he introduced a carbon tax in his first term of office.
That having been said, climate change and the environment remain a significant concern for voters.
The question is, which voters?
Liberals and New Democrats remain the most likely partisans to support a carbon tax, by significant margins.
So, Crombie has an issue where she can risk being offside from either a substantial majority of Ontarians, or being offside from a substantial majority of her core voters. That is where 'further study' comes in.
Crombie's strategy only makes sense if you think the carbon tax will be a non-issue by the time the next provincial election happens in June 2026. That only happens if Pierre Poilievre defeats Trudeau. Poilievre's election means the end of the federal carbon tax. At that point, Crombie is betting that she can tell voters that the carbon tax is a dead issue that she has no intent of revisiting.
Progressives who believe the carbon tax is the only legitimate major policy tool for combatting climate change, in this scenario, are going to have to grin and bear it. Crombie's strategic assumption is that they'll be unwilling to line up behind the NDP over the issue of a dead tax.
If you're a progressive who cares about climate change, this looks an awful lot like Doug Ford's carbon tax plan. Do nothing, hope the issue is solved federally. For progressives, it could very easily be taken as evidence that Crombie isn't one of them.
That's one risk to this strategy. The other risk is that the 18 months between now and the anticipated start of the next federal election is a lot of time in politics. Eighteen months is a lot of time for the Ontario Liberals to be on the receiving end of Tory attacks that Crombie is pro-carbon tax without much of a communications strategy to counter it.
Can Crombie do 18 months of 'further study'? That's already a tall task.
But the unsaid part - the part her team will never admit - is that they need Trudeau to lose for it to have any hope of working at all.
- Mitch Heimpel has run parliamentary affairs for Conservative parties on Parliament Hill and at Queen's Park and is Director of Campaigns and Government Relations at Enterprise Canada.
twitter.com/mitchheimpel

Subject: Provincial elections; Voters; Carbon; Climate change; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax

People: Trudeau, Justin

Identifier / keyword: justin; trudeau; canadian; politician; carbon; newfoundland

Title: HEIMPEL: Why Bonnie Crombie is betting against Justin Trudeau

Document 292 of 356

Burn bridges for poll warmth; Trudeau puts his needs before Canada's in trashing Trump

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/burn-bridges-poll-warmth-trudeau-puts-his-needs/docview/2922312487/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Burn+bridges+for+poll+warmth%3B+Trudeau+puts+his+needs+before+Canada%27s+in+trashing+Trump&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Two top diplomats under Justin Trudeau have warned the PM that campaigning against Donald Trump is a dumb idea. Meanwhile, a major American conservative commentator has told the Toronto Sun beating up on Trump won't work out for Canada if Trump wins re-election.
For months now, Trudeau and his Liberal party have been campaigning against Donald Trump instead of Pierre Poilievre. They have made statements and made social media posts tying the two together and making Trump, and Poilievre, appear evil.
Trump currently leads by 2.1% in an average of polls, according to poll aggregator Real Clear Politics, and Biden has only had the lead in three polls released since the start of the year.
Beyond that, Trump is leading in polling for battleground states such as Michigan, Ohio, Georgia, Wisconsin, Florida, and Nevada.
"Why would you run the risk?" David McNaughton said to the Toronto Star last week.
McNaughton was Trudeau's Ontario campaign co-chairman for the 2015 election, and served as Canada's ambassador to the United States from 2016-19.
Campaigning against Trump when, given current polling, he could win, is extremely dangerous for Canada. The United States is our biggest trading partner, our friend, our ally and our protector militarily.
Trudeau and the Liberals are poking Trump in the eye when he could be the guy running the White House in less than a year.
"We need to be seen by the Americans as a trusted friend, ally, partner and, you know, right now, I don't think that feeling is as strong as it has been," McNaughton told the Star. He isn't alone in warning against the dangers of the path the governing party is on. Louise Blais served as Canada's consul general in the southeastern United States in before the 2016 election, which saw Trump voted in, and was later appointed by Trudeau as Canada's ambassador to the United Nations.
In a column for the Globe and Mail, Blais took a less political tone than McNaughton, but was still critical of the Trudeau government's approach in a very diplomatic way. She worried that the appointment of two cabinet ministers, Industry Minister Francois-Philippe Champagne and Economic Development Minister Mary Ng, to deal with the Americans would be seen as panicking over Trump's possible re-election.
"Canada's relationship with the United States is an existential one. We are reliant on the U.S. for our economy and security. And internationally, like it or not, we derive most of our influence from how the U.S. treats us. It's fundamental to how the rest of the world treats us," she wrote.
In her column, Blais recounted how in 2016, Newt Gingrich, the former Georgia congressman and Speaker of the House of Representatives, approached her about the Trump-bashing coming out of Canada.
"Be very careful, because you are picking sides and that is badly seen," Gingrich said.
Blais wisely believes that Canadian elected officials, who have to deal with whoever wins, shouldn't be participants in the American election by choosing sides. Right now, for domestic reasons, Trudeau is picking sides.
Americans are noticing what Trudeau is doing and in a soon-to-be released interview with the Sun, Glenn Beck, the American conservative talk radio host, said Trudeau was putting Canada in a dangerous spot.
"Well, it's not going to be a friendly relationship," Beck said of what would happen if Trump wins and Trudeau is still prime minister. "Donald Trump tends to remember who his friends are."
Trudeau is showing that he's not only not friends with Trump, but not friends with America if they re-elect him. That may help Trudeau win some votes domestically, but it could have horrible repercussions on our economy.
While Trudeau has no problem cozying up to actual dictators, including in Iran and China, he should shut up about Trump.
He can't, though, because he's desperate as his polling numbers fall further still. Some polls show about one-third of Canadians would vote for Trump if they could cast a ballot, meaning Trump would be more popular in Canada than Trudeau is.
Trudeau would rather put your job and economic livelihood at risk by attacking Trump so he might have a chance at saving his own job. That should tell you all you need to know about Justin Trudeau. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

LILLEY: Everyone is warning Trudeau not to campaign against Trump

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-everyone-is-warning-trudeau-not-campaign/docview/2922169809/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Everyone+is+warning+Trudeau+not+to+campaign+against+Trump&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Two top diplomats under Justin Trudeau have warned the PM that campaigning against Donald Trump is a dumb idea. Meanwhile, a major American conservative commentator has told The Toronto Sun, beating up on Trump won't work out for Canada if Trump wins re-election.
For months now, Trudeau and his Liberal Party have been campaigning against Donald Trump instead of Pierre Poilievre. They have made statements and made social media posts tying the two together and making Trump, and Poilievre, appear evil.
Trump currently leads by 2.1% in an average of polls, according to poll aggregator Real Clear Politics, and Biden has only had the lead in three polls released since the start of the year. Beyond that, Trump is currently leading in polling for battleground states such as Michigan, Ohio, Georgia, Wisconsin, Florida, and Nevada.
"Why would you run the risk?" David McNaughton said to the Toronto Star
McNaughton was Trudeau's Ontario campaign co-chair for the 2015 election, and served as Canada's ambassador to the United States from 2016-19.
Campaigning against Trump when, given current polling, he could win, is extremely dangerous for Canada. The United States is our biggest trading partner, our friend, our ally and our protector militarily.
Trudeau and the Liberals are currently poking Trump in the eye when he could be the guy running the White House in less than a year.
"We need to be seen by the Americans as a trusted friend, ally, partner and, you know, right now, I don't think that feeling is as strong as it has been," McNaughton told the Star.
He isn't alone in warning against the dangers of the current party the governing party is on. Louise Blais served as Canada's consul general in the southeastern United States in the lead-up to the 2016 election that saw Trump voted in, and was later appointed by Trudeau as Canada's ambassador to the United Nations.
In a column for the Globe and Mail, Blais took a less political tone than McNaughton, but was still critical of the Trudeau government's approach in a very diplomatic way. She worried that the appointment of two cabinet ministers, Industry Minister Francois-Philippe Champagne and Economic Development Minister Mary Ng, to deal with the Americans, would be seen as panicking over Trump's possible re-election.
Recommended video
"Canada's relationship with the United States is an existential one. We are reliant on the U.S. for our economy and security. And internationally, like it or not, we derive most of our influence from how the U.S. treats us. It's fundamental to how the rest of the world treats us," she wrote
In her column, Blais recounted how in 2016, Newt Gingrich, the former Georgia congressman and speaker of the House of Representatives, approached her about the Trump bashing coming out of Canada.
"Be very careful, because you are picking sides and that is badly seen," Gingrich said.
Blais wisely believes that Canadian elected officials, who have to deal with whoever wins, shouldn't be participants in the American election by choosing sides. Right now, for domestic reasons, Trudeau is picking sides.
Americans are noticing what Trudeau is doing and in a soon-to-be released weekend interview with the Sun, Glenn Beck, the American conservative talk radio host, new media pioneer and political commentator, said that Trudeau was putting Canada in a dangerous spot.
"Well, it's not going to be a friendly relationship," Beck said of what would happen if Trump wins and Trudeau is still prime minister. "Donald Trump tends to remember who his friends are."
Trudeau is showing that he's not only not friends with Trump, but not friends with America if they re-elect him. That may help Trudeau win some votes domestically, but it could have horrible repercussions on our economy.
While Trudeau has no problem cozying up to actual dictators, including Iran and China, he should shut up about Trump.
He can't, though, because he's desperate as his polling numbers fall further still. Some polls show about one-third of Canadians would vote for Trump if they could cast a ballot, meaning that Trump is more popular in Canada than Trudeau is.
Trudeau would rather put your job and economic livelihood at risk by attacking Trump so that he might have a chance at saving his own job. That should tell you all you need to know about Justin Trudeau.

Full text: One thing loud and clear in Canada in 2024 is that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's people are allowed to be rude and demeaning to people the government doesn't like - just as he is.
It's as if they feel they are always right and those who don't agree with them are scum.
Saying there is "no excuse" for what Ottawa residents faced from the trucker convoy, the Trudeau government is doubling down in supporting vulgar responses of residents including a senior Liberal staff member who was reportedly caught on video hurling normally below-the-belt obscenities.
"We f***ing hate you," said an at-the-time unidentified man from a balcony in Ottawa during a verbal dispute with protesters in February 2022. "The hostages are getting their f***ing revenge. Watch yourself."
The vulgarity got worse. At one point he can be heard calling a woman a "dumb f***ing c***" and a "stupid b****."
Known as "Balcony Man," the mysterious resident also gave two fingers to protesters. This video was so hot it made it to the Late Show with Stephen Colbert.
Meet Matt MacDougall aka "Balcony Guy", currently a senior policy adviser for parliamentary affairs in the office of Liberal Government House leader Steve MacKinnon.
During the convoy he worked under Mark Holland.
Surprise surprise!
- United We Stand ?? (@ChantaleBerger3) February 2, 2024
Two years later, veteran Ottawa reporter Glen McGregor broke the story claiming that the star of this viral video was a Liberal government insider: "The man is Matt MacDougall, now a senior policy adviser for parliamentary affairs in the office of Liberal Government House leader Steve MacKinnon," McGregor wrote in the National Post. "At the time of the convoy, MacDougall worked for then House leader Mark Holland, who is now health minister."
But when asked about this extreme behaviour, the government took the approach the real culprits were the truckers and not the locals pushing back on what they felt was an occupation of the capital.
The Sun reached out to MacDougall for comment but did not immediately receive a response.
Balcony Man dropped 81 f-bombs in his tirade, about one very three seconds. The clip was once included in Stephen Colbert's monologue on CBS's The Late Show.
- Glen McGregor (@glen_mcgregor) February 1, 2024
While "we will not comment on personnel," Mark Kennedy, director of communications for the Office of the Leader of the Government in the House of Commons, told the Toronto Sun, "two years ago, the residents of Ottawa were subjected to an illegal and disgraceful blockade of their streets" in which "thousands of people who lived downtown experienced unimaginable treatment by those who participated in the blockade."
WARMINGTON: Prime Minister's staffer calls people names but gets apology
WARMINGTON: Trudeau now likening opponents to 'flat Earthers'
Added Kennedy: "Trucks in the convoy honked their horns at ear-piercing levels 24 hours a day for three weeks, making it virtually impossible for residents to sleep at night. The trucks that occupied the streets pumped out toxic diesel fumes, creating an unhealthy environment."
As a fact check, the honking stopped in the first few days after a court injunction but the protest remained in place for three weeks until the government invoked the Emergencies Act - which, ironically, last week was ruled by a court to be unconstitutional.
Tamara Lich, still before the courts charged with mischief for being the alleged leader of the convoy, said after watching this video: "you never saw any of us behave like that."
Freedom Convoy 2022 organizer Tamara Lich speaks during a news conference as truckers and supporters continue to protest coronavirus disease (COVID-19) vaccine mandates in Ottawa, Ontario, Canada, Feb. 3, 2022. REUTERS/Blair Gable
That is not how the Trudeau government saw it.
"Residents who ventured out into the streets had their safety imperiled, as they were met with aggressive and intimidating protesters who hurled insults at them," said Kennedy. "We believe there is no excuse for what the people of Ottawa experienced in 2022. We supported those residents then, and we support them now."

- cheryl (@gvicequeen17) February 2, 2024

I have said it before but I don't think any of these people should be cancelled but stress that such tolerance has to go both ways for both liberals and conservatives. If the PM and his pals can scrape the bottom of the barrel with impunity, it's time to let people like fired AM 640 morning man Mike Stafford and Coach's Corner legend Don Cherry back on the air because what they said was not even close in terms of offensive.

Preem: You're going to jail; Crime so bad, Ford's pals have a bat by the bed

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/preem-youre-going-jail-crime-so-bad-fords-pals/docview/2921319294/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Preem%3A+You%27re+going+to+jail%3B+Crime+so+bad%2C+Ford%27s+pals+have+a+bat+by+the+bed&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's warning to criminals that he's "coming after" them didn't stop a group of thugs from smashing and grabbing their way through a North York mall Wednesday night.
Criminals don't care what politicians say because they know who's in charge of the streets. It's them.
Toronto Police say four masked men, described as about 5 feet 8, stormed a jewelry store in the Yorkdale Shopping Centre, near Dufferin St., around 8:45 p.m.
One of the bandits "produced a handgun and pointed it at employees" while the others "began smashing the glass of display cases with hammers," stealing jewelry and cash, before they all drove off in a newer-model grey SUV.
But don't worry, the premier has strong words for such criminals.
"We're going to catch you and you're going to jail," Ford said Wednesday. "We're going to keep you in He knows there are not enough jail cells to incarcerate every criminal and the courts are backlogged for years.
That means the boss is not Ford or Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. It's these gangs that are in charge.
They will carjack you or steal your car from your driveway, or kick down the front door of your home without fear of getting caught. Even if they are nabbed, they know they will be released from custody within a few days.
The premier knows how bad it is out there.
"I've talked to friends that are sleeping with baseball bats beside their bed because someone's going to kick the door in and put a gun to their head and say, hand over your keys," Ford said.
What kind of province have we become when the premier's friends sleep with bats? Just imagine those without such powerful friends. What are they supposed to do? The public has become sitting ducks while leaders oversee the destruction caused by drugs, poverty, gambling debts, mental health and a focus on progressive wokeism.
While Ford recommended victims of crime wait for police instead of using on a bat, he didn't mention the average emergency response time is 22 minutes.
The idea of people defending themselves with a baseball bat is interesting since just last month a 22-year-old international student working at a Peterborough corner store was slugged with one by a crook during a holdup.
In that case, the clerk managed to snatch the bat away from the robber and deliver a retaliatory blow.
The innocent kid, who is not friends with the premier, ended up charged with aggravated assault for defending himself. He has no money, no job, he's under house arrest with strict bail conditions and may have to wait two years to try to clear his name.
The criminals must be laughing their heads off. jwarmington@postmedia.com @joe_warmington

Document 297 of 356

Ottawa offers funds, not a fix; Cheques signed but no solutions on asylum seeker influx

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-offers-funds-not-fix-cheques-signed-no/docview/2920960570/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+offers+funds%2C+not+a+fix%3B+Cheques+signed+but+no+solutions+on+asylum+seeker+influx&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Marc Miller released just enough details of his new funding for asylum seekers to ensure no one is happy.
He has no solution to stop the flow of people arriving through what the government calls "irregular channels" and he isn't going to provide the money other levels of government say they are owed.
The federal immigration minister held a hastily called news conference Wednesday afternoon to announce $362 million will be shared across the country to help house thousands of asylum seekers. That is just about half of the $720 million that Toronto and Quebec wanted just for themselves.
The Legault government in Quebec sent a letter this month asking for $470 million for services provided by the province. Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow had threatened to add a 6% federal impact levy to the property tax bill if the feds don't deliver the $250 million.
That's something Miller was dismissive of when asked.
"You can call a municipal tax whatever you want, it's still a municipal tax," Miller said.
Chow's ploy to wedge the Trudeau government ruffled feathers throughout Ottawa, not just with the 24 Liberal MPs in Toronto. It was noted, according to several Liberal sources, that Chow played nice to get her deal for a new financial arrangement with Ontario Premier Doug Ford but was threatening toward them.
Whether the ploy worked isn't known quite yet because the Trudeau government is playing their own game in not releasing Toronto's number.
Miller offered up that Quebec will get $100 million from this fund, plus $50 million from another fund. But when asked about Toronto's share, he refused to say.
"Today I'm announcing Quebec.
Toronto will be announced in the coming days," Miller said. "I know what it is, I'm just not announcing it today."
Given Quebec's request for $470 million and its $100-million grant, it would be impossible for Toronto to get its $250 million from the remaining $262 million. Miller said the federal government has roughly 4,000 hotel rooms across six provinces housing 7,300 people.
According to government figures, 144,035 asylum seekers were processed by federal agencies in 2023 with 45% of them being in Quebec and 44% in Ontario.
The bulk of those in Ontario would have landed in the Toronto area, but municipalities such as Ottawa, Mississauga, Brampton, Hamilton and Niagara Falls have all taken in significant populations as well. Miller also spoke of how the federal government has been moving some asylum seekers to other provinces and paying for their hotels, which will of course eat up some of the money.
Chow had made the pitch to get $250 million from the federal government by pointing out that 43% of the city's 10,000 shelter beds are taken up by refugees on any given night. It's a number that has ballooned over the last several years to an unsustainable level.
Despite not knowing her exact allotment, Chow issued a complimentary statement late in the day.
"I'm encouraged by the announcement and look forward to working with Minister Miller on the funds for Toronto." Chow said.
Chow is required to present her updated version of the city budget on Thursday morning; whether she will be able to present a full plan with this new funding isn't clear.
One source with knowledge of the plan said the feds will count the $97 million previously pledged to Toronto as part of the total payment with just over $100 million in additional funds for this year and the promise of $40 million in 2025. In exchange, the source said, Chow will drop her threat of a federal levy.
The real problem in Miller's announcement is that he offered no plan, not even a hint of a solution, for dealing with this issue on a permanent basis. Between 2011 and 2016, the annual number of people claiming asylum ranged from 10,000 to 25,000. This year, it is more than 144,000 - up from 91,735 last year.
Miller and the rest of the Trudeau government will need to deal with this, but so far, Miller simply ignored the issue when questioned. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Document 298 of 356

On the safe side; Mayor must restore police funding to protect Torontonians

Author: Bradford, Brad

Publication info: Bradford, Brad. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/on-safe-side-mayor-must-restore-police-funding/docview/2920960393/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=On+the+safe+side%3B+Mayor+must+restore+police+funding+to+protect+Torontonians&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=Bradford%2C+Brad&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When it comes to municipal budgets, there are few things more important than our ability to ensure we have a safe city for residents.
That requires enough funding to ensure we have adequate resources for our first responders to deploy and respond when they are called upon.
Mayor Olivia Chow's proposal to cut $12.6 million from the budget - approved unanimously by the police services board - will prevent the police from hiring four classes of 90 new front-line officers to both replace retiring officers and add new ones.
Make no mistake: This cut will compromise the ability of the police to respond to Priority 1 calls, in which there is an immediate threat to life.
Today, the average Priority 1 response time is an excruciating 22 minutes. That is a direct result of not having enough frontline officers, a problem that has only gotten worse as our population growth has accelerated during the past decade.
Consider this: In 2010, we had a front-line police force of more than 5,600 officers. Today, we have around 5,100 officers - an 11% reduction in officers even as our population has grown 13%.
We need to stop the cycle of politicizing police funding and freezing budgets to ensure our city has officers when and where we need them.
While $12.6 million may not seem like a lot in the grand scheme of Chow's $17-billion budget, those dollars are directly tied to the police's ability to hire those desperately needed recruits.
If you are home during a violent break-in, you would want to know the police would be there within minutes. Right now, that's far from guaranteed.
The mayor may think she can kick this down the road and postpone hiring, but Toronto's police force is already staring down an avalanche of retirements.
Roughly 25% of officers are eligible to retire today, which puts us in a compromised position. Chow's decision to cut the budget for hiring new officers would mean retirements will outpace the ability to hire and train new officers, leaving us short-handed by next year.
Now is the best time to be hiring new front-line officers so that they can learn from the experience of these senior officers before they leave the force. We expect the best from our police, and that requires best-in-class training and mentorship, and both rely on having an adequately funded police budget.
As one of a city's core services, policing is understandably one of the largest line items, but Toronto gets good value for money. At 8.7% of our city budget, it is significantly less than in Vancouver (20.9%) or Edmonton (15.2%).
There is money available. In the new deal between Queen's Park and Toronto, $300 million was included to enhance transit safety and security. Premier Doug Ford was clear that this money should go toward more police officers on and near transit - but this budget doesn't give a nickel from that massive cheque to the police.
If the funding is not restored, Toronto's chief of police has said he would be forced to redirect traffic enforcement and neighbourhood officers to emergency response, which must always be the first priority of any police force. However, that would mean fewer officers dedicated to road safety, preventing auto theft and building relationships with communities.
It's very unusual to see Toronto's police chief speaking out so publicly about his own budget. That he would go to this extent demonstrates just how essential this funding is to deliver adequate and effective policing.
Chow has another opportunity to restore this funding when she presents the latest version of her budget Thursday. For the sake of public safety, I hope she'll restore this funding.
In a $17-billion budget, finding the $12.6 million needed to restore a core city service is a no-brainer.
For the mayor and some of her allies, cutting the police budget might be driven by ideology. But for most Torontonians, adequate funding for front-line officers is about safety and common sense.
Bradford is the Toronto councillor for Ward 19 (Beaches-East York) councillor_bradford@toronto.ca

Subject: Budgets; Funding

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets

BRADFORD: Mayor must restore police funding to protect public safety

Author: Ruryk, Zenon

Publication info: Ruryk, Zenon. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bradford-mayor-must-restore-police-funding/docview/2920691446/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=BRADFORD%3A+Mayor+must+restore+police+funding+to+protect+public+safety&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Ruryk%2C+Zenon&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: BRAD BRADFORD
When it comes to municipal budgets, there are few things more important than our ability to ensure we have a safe city for residents.
That requires enough funding to ensure that we have adequate resources for our first responders to deploy and respond when they are called upon.
Mayor Olivia Chow's current proposal to cut $12.6 million from the budget - approved unanimously by the police services board - will prevent the police from hiring four classes of 90 new frontline officers to both replace retiring officers and add new ones.
Make no mistake: This cut will compromise the ability of the police to respond to Priority 1 Calls, where there is an immediate threat to life.
Today, the average Priority 1 response time is an excruciating 22 minutes. That is a direct result of not having enough frontline officers, a problem that has only gotten worse as our population growth has accelerated during the past decade.
Consider this: In 2010, we had a frontline police force of more than 5,600 officers. Today, we have around 5,100 officers - an 11% reduction in officers even as our population has grown 13%.
Recommended video
We need to stop the cycle of politicizing police funding and freezing budgets to ensure that our city has officers when and where we need them.
While $12.6 million may not seem like a lot in the grand scheme of Chow's $17-billion budget, those dollars are directly tied to the police's ability to hire those desperately needed recruits.
If you are home during a violent break-in, you would want to know the police would be there within minutes. Right now, that's far from guaranteed.
The mayor may think she can kick this down the road and postpone hiring, but Toronto's police force is already staring down an avalanche of retirements.
Roughly 25% of officers are eligible to retire today, which puts us in a compromised position going forward. Chow's decision to cut the budget for hiring new officers would mean that retirements will outpace the ability to hire and train new officers, leaving us shorthanded by next year.
Now is the best time to be hiring new frontline officers so that they can learn from the experience of these senior officers before they leave the force. We expect the best from our police and that requires best-in-class training and mentorship, and both rely on having an adequately funded police budget.
As one of a city's core services, policing is understandably one of the largest line items. But Toronto gets good value for money. At 8.7% of our city budget, it is significantly less than in Vancouver (20.9%) or Edmonton (15.2%).
There is money available. In the new deal between Queen's Park and Toronto, $300 million was included to enhance transit safety and security. Premier Doug Ford was clear that this money should go towards more police officers on and near transit - but this budget doesn't give a nickel from that massive cheque to the police.
If the funding is not restored, Toronto's chief of police has said he would be forced to redirect traffic enforcement and neighbourhood officers to emergency response, which must always be the first priority of any police force. However, that would mean fewer officers dedicated to road safety, preventing auto theft, and building relationships with communities.
It's very unusual to see Toronto's police chief speaking out so publicly about his own budget. That he would go to this extent demonstrates just how essential this funding is to deliver adequate and effective policing.
Chow has another opportunity to restore this funding when she presents the latest version of her budget on Thursday. For the sake of public safety, I hope she'll restore this funding.
In a $17-billion budget, finding the $12.6 million needed to restore a core city service is a no-brainer.
For the mayor and some of her allies, cutting the police budget might be driven by ideology. But for most Torontonians, providing adequate funding for frontline officers is about public safety and common sense.
- Brad Bradford is the Toronto city councillor for Ward 19 (Beaches - East York). He can be reached at Councillor_Bradford@toronto.ca.

Document 302 of 356

T.O.'s houses divided; In Chow's budget, some renters equal than others

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/t-o-s-houses-divided-chows-budget-some-renters/docview/2920372984/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=T.O.%27s+houses+divided%3B+In+Chow%27s+budget%2C+some+renters+equal+than+others&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a city known for great theatre, Olivia Chow's budget show might be the best one running at the moment. It has drama, intrigue, sleight of hand and even a little comedy when you think of how ridiculous it is.
On Thursday, the mayor will lift the curtain on her "official budget," but she has been willing "Tenants will not have to pay more rent because of this budget," Chow said Tuesday. "I am lowering the multi-residential rate so that it is below 3.75%, because that is the threshold where landlords go to the tribunal and ask for more rent increase."
The property tax hike on purpose-built multi-residential buildings is usually about half of what homeowners will pay. While Chow has said that she wants to protect renters, that doesn't mean all renters will be saved from the big tax hike.
About half of the residents of Toronto are renters and the other half homeowners, but not all renters are created equal.
Politicians of all stripes and at all levels have spent the past essentially means more buildings that are 100% rental units in a standard apartment building.
Over the past 20 years, there haven't been many of these types of units built, with most new rentals coming into the system as condos rented out by individual owners or basement suites added to a home. None of those units will be spared this tax hike, just those who live in buildings made up solely of rental apartments.
That means an awful lot of renters in Toronto will be getting a rent increase due to Chow's massive tax hike.
Then we have the question about whether the tax hike will be 10.5% for homeowners or 16.5%. The official property tax hike is 9%, followed by a 1.5% hike for capital projects and then the threat of an additional 6% hike called the "federal impact levy."
That's what Chow's budget chief, Councillor Shelly Carroll, dubbed the threatened tax hike that Chow will apply if the feds don't pony up. Chow and Carroll - let's not pretend they aren't working together on this budget - threatened to add an extra 6% unless the feds handed over $250 million for the housing and other costs of asylum seekers.
Let's be clear: This is a federal responsibility, and they should pay up, but Chow's tactics have pushed a lot of Liberal noses out of joint.
It also hasn't gone unnoticed that Chow played nice with Premier Doug Ford in order to get a deal, then essentially threatened the federal Liberals, rather than working co-operatively.
There will be some money coming down the road. There was speculation it could come this week, but that is now likely off as details with jurisdictions across the country are worked out. Suffice to say, few Liberals are in a rush to help out Chow before her budget announcement Thursday.
That leaves the question: What will Chow do if the feds come up with part of the funds - say between $150 million and $200 million - but not the full cost? Will she still bring in a federal levy or eat the difference? What about the cost of lowering the tax rate for purpose-built rentals? Will she boost the tax hike for homeowners in a class warfare move, or try to look moderate? Here's a prediction. Chow will use the tools at her disposal to appear moderate, to try to show she can listen to the people. Expect the overall tax rate will be lower than 10.5%, but still too high, but enough for her to say she listened.
We might even see programs like the driveway snow clearing program saved as a sign she isn't a radical.
But don't expect a change in the police budget. On that front, Chow likes the drama and her base enjoys the show. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Document 303 of 356

Carbon tax won't save the Liberals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/carbon-tax-wont-save-liberals/docview/2920358242/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Carbon+tax+won%27t+save+the+Liberals&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Trudeau Liberals keep clinging to the idea, borne of their political desperation, that if they can just properly explain their carbon tax to Canadians, they'll flock to it in support.
Their problem is they had one chance to make a good first impression about their carbon tax and they blew it.
It started with the Liberal claim when they imposed their carbon tax in 2019 that they had found a magical tax leaving 80% of households paying it better off.
That claim was blown apart in March 2022 by independent, non-partisan Parliamentary Budget Officer Yves Giroux, who reported that after you factored in the negative impact of the carbon tax on the economy, most households - 60% - ended up worse off.
How much worse? The PBO estimated in Alberta the average net cost per household, factoring in quarterly climate action incentive rebates, will be $710 this year, rising to $2,773 in 2030.
Here are the PBO's estimated costs for the other provinces - the first figure being the estimated average household cost this year, the second in 2030: Ontario: $478, $1,820; Saskatchewan $410, $1723; Manitoba $386, $1490; Nova Scotia $431, $1,513; P.E.I. $465, $1,521; Newfoundland and Labrador $347, $1,316.
(New Brunswick joined the federal carbon tax system on July 1 last year - after the PBO provided these estimates - while Quebec and B.C. have provincial carbon pricing plans, approved by the federal government.)
The Trudeau Liberals also said paying the carbon tax would lower Canada's industrial greenhouse gas emissions but after a significant drop during the first year of the pandemic in 2020 - not because of the carbon tax but because of the global economic slowdown caused by COVID-19 - emissions rose again in 2021.
We won't know Canada's emissions for 2022 until this April because the feds always report them two years after the fact, but the Canadian Climate Institute estimates Canada's emissions went up, compared to 2021.
Finally, there's the Liberal myth that even if we were to hit their emission targets for 2030 and 2050 - we're far behind both - the weather in Canada would somehow magically improve.
In fact, as the PBO noted, Canada's emissions at 1.5% of the global total "are not large enough to materially impact climate change."

Document 304 of 356

LILLEY: Chow's budget means some renters are more equal than others

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-chows-budget-means-some-renters-are-more/docview/2920279061/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Chow%27s+budget+means+some+renters+are+more+equal+than+others&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a city known for great theatre, Olivia Chow's budget show might be the best one running at the moment. It has drama, intrigue, sleight of hand and even a little comedy when you think of how ridiculous it is.
On Thursday, Mayor Chow will lift the curtain on her "official budget," but she's been willing to tease out some aspects that will leave many taxpayers feeling like second class citizens.
"Tenants will not have to pay more rent because of this budget," Chow said on Tuesday.
"I am lowering the multi-residential rate so that it is below 3.75%, because that is the threshold where landlords go to the tribunal and ask for more rent increase."
The property tax hike on purpose built multi-residential buildings is usually about half of what homeowners will pay. And while Chow has said that she wants to protect renters, that doesn't mean all renters will be saved from the big tax hike.
About half of the residents of Toronto are renters and the other half homeowners, but not all renters are created equal.
Politicians of all stripes and at all levels have spent the last few years talking about the need for more purpose-built rental buildings in Toronto. That essentially means more buildings that are 100% rental units in a standard apartment building.
Over the last 20 years, there haven't been many of these types of units built, with most new rentals coming into the system as condos rented out by individual owners or basement suites added to an existing home. None of those units will be spared this tax hike, just those who live in buildings made up solely of rental apartments.
Which means, an awful lot of renters in Toronto will be getting a rent increase due to Chow's massive tax hike.
Then we have the question about whether the tax hike will be 10.5% for homeowners - or 16.5%. The official property tax hike is 9%, followed by a 1.5% hike for capital projects and then the threat of an additional 6% hike, called the "Federal Impact Levy."
Recommended video
That's what Chow's budget chief, Councillor Shelly Carroll, dubbed the threatened tax hike that Chow will apply if the feds don't pony up. Chow, and Carroll - let's not pretend they aren't working together on this budget - threatened to add an extra 6% unless the feds handed over $250 million for the housing and other costs of asylum seekers.
Let's be clear: This is a federal responsibility, and they should pay up. But Chow's tactics have pushed a lot of Liberal noses out of joint.
It also has not gone unnoticed that Chow played nice with Ontario Premier Doug Ford in order to get a deal, then essentially threatened the federal Liberals, rather than working cooperatively.
There will be some money coming down the road; there was speculation it could come this week, but that is now likely off as details with various jurisdictions across the country are worked out. Suffice to say, few Liberals are in a rush to help out Chow before her budget announcement on Thursday.
Which leaves the question: What will Chow do if the feds come up with part of the funds - say between $150-200 million - but not the full cost? Will she still bring in a federal levy or eat the difference?
What about the cost of lowering the tax rate for purpose-built rentals? Will she boost the tax hike for homeowners in a class warfare move, or try to look moderate?
Here's a prediction.
Chow will use the tools at her disposal to appear moderate, to try to show she can listen to the people. Expect that the overall tax rate will be lower than 10.5%, but still too high, but enough for her to say she listened.
We might even see programs like windrows, the driveway snow clearing program, saved as a sign she is not a radical.
But don't expect a change in the police budget; on that front, Chow likes the drama and her base enjoys the show.
LILLEY: Chow's gamble in fight with Trudeau may backfire
LILLEY: City budget spends like crazy while cutting police and horses

EDITORIAL: Carbon tax won't save the Liberals

Document 306 of 356

Former councillor going federal

Author: Strobel, Mike

Publication info: Strobel, Mike. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/former-councillor-going-federal/docview/2919762561/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+councillor+going+federal&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Strobel%2C+Mike&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A former city hall heavyweight is jumping to federal politics.
Karen Stintz, a former councillor, mayoral candidate and TTC chairman, will announce Monday she's seeking the Conservative nomination in Eglinton-Lawrence.
The mid-Toronto riding, where she lives, usually goes Liberal, but is considered ripe for the picking. Current MP Marco Mendicino was punted as public safety minister last July after his department transferred notorious schoolgirl killer Paul Bernardo to a medium-security prison.
"Marco's a good guy," Stintz said, "but it's a change election, and he's in the wrong party.
"It's a winnable riding and the community feels very let down by the current government" - especially a large Jewish population unhappy about the feds'stance on Israel's war with Hamas.
"I've personally been inspired by (Conservative Leader) Pierre Poilievre," Stintz said. "He gives voice to what people mean when they say they want change - affordability, managing the country, investing in the military."
Stintz left city hall after dropping out of the 2014 mayoral race. Since then, she has been CEO of Variety - the Children's Charity of Ontario, and Variety Village.

Document 307 of 356

Wiltin'in Milton?; Riding next door to Liberal leader may not be sure thing some expect

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/wiltinin-milton-riding-next-door-liberal-leader/docview/2919760268/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Wiltin%27in+Milton%3F%3B+Riding+next+door+to+Liberal+leader+may+not+be+sure+thing+some+expect&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If Parm Gill is out, does that mean Bonnie Crombie is in? Since Gill announced last week that he would resign as the PC MPP for Milton, and minister of red tape reduction in the Ford government, speculation has turned to Crombie.
Will the former Mississauga mayor and newly minted Liberal leader move the 30 km or so northwest to run in Milton? It seems like the logical thing to do since the move could get Crombie a seat in the legislature, but running in Milton may not be the wisest thing from a couple of perspectives.
First off, if Crombie were successful in winning the seat and taking the riding from the PC party, she would have a steady presence at Queen's Park, which is a good thing. But it would also tie her to the legislature, which may not be a good thing.
With the Ontario Liberal Party a shadow of its former self, the party seriously needs to reorganize and rebuild - tasks that might be easier for Crombie to accomplish away from Queen's Park.
Sure, with a seat in the legislature, Crombie could have easier access to the media covering the daily ups and downs of Queen's Park, but in truth, she has easy media access as it is. Rebuilding the infrastructure on the ground that's required to win an election is equally important to winning a seat - if not more so - for a party that wants to regain power.
The second reason Crombie may want to think twice about running is she might not win.
Milton is a swing riding that - in its current form and its previous incarnations - has gone back and forth between the Liberals and PCs provincially for years. The same is true for the riding's federal counterpart, which is held by Liberal MP Adam van Koeverden.
In the last provincial election, Liberal candidate Sameera Ali took 38% of the vote to Gill's 43%. Gill slightly outperformed his party's provincewide level of voter support - 41% - while Ali far outperformed the 24% the Liberals took across Ontario.
While there is no doubt Crombie would have a shot at winning the riding, what is best for her party? Concerns that she's not from the area and would be seen as an outsider should be ignored by anyone making this decision - van Koeverden won Milton federally in 2019 after moving from downtown Toronto. If Crombie were running in Lambton-Kent-Middlesex, that might be an issue, but not Milton.
There was a time when leaders without a seat in the legislature would effectively be given a pass by the other parties when contesting a byelection. Those days are long gone, so we shouldn't expect the PC party to go easy on Crombie if she decides to contest Milton.
In 2009, when John Tory sought to win a seat in the legislature as PC leader, the Liberals refused to go easy on him and took the riding of Haliburton-Kawartha Lakes-Brock. In 2015, newly minted PC leader Patrick Brown ran in Simcoe-North and won despite a hard-fought campaign by the Liberals.
Given that record, there is no reason for the PCs to go easy on Crombie.
The PC party has held Milton since 2018, and they won't want to give it up soon, meaning Premier Doug Ford is likely to fight hard in this byelection. Keeping it, if Crombie enters, will be a difficult task and require a strong local candidate who can go toe to toe with the new Liberal leader.
The NDP is effectively nonexistent in the riding, taking less than 10% of the vote in the last election, which makes this a PC-Liberal battle.
There are a lot of options for both parties to consider between now and when the byelection is called.

Subject: Provincial elections; Legislatures
