Document 308 of 356

Trumped-Up?; Grits'strategy to campaign on prez hopeful could backfire, poll suggests

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trumped-up-gritsstrategy-campaign-on-prez-hopeful/docview/2919760242/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trumped-Up%3F%3B+Grits%27strategy+to+campaign+on+prez+hopeful+could+backfire%2C+poll+suggests&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Trying to tie Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre to Donald Trump may not be the trump card the Trudeau Liberals think it is.
For months, the Liberals have been trying to hurt Poilievre's support among voters by connecting him to the presumptive Republican candidate. A new poll from Abacus Data shows that about 40% of voters believe Poilievre and Trump are similar, while roughly 33% find them different, with the rest saying they don't know.
More important, though, is that more Canadians believe Poilievre is the best option to deal with Trump if he wins the White House for a second time in November.
When asked, "If Donald Trump is the next U.S. president, would it be better for Canada if Justin Trudeau or Pierre Poilievre were prime minister?" a full 44% chose Poilievre, compared to just 29% picking Trudeau.
That includes 17% of current Liberal voters believing Poilievre would be the better choice and 23% of current NDP voters.
Abacus Data CEO David Coletto says the strategy of linking Poilievre and Trump could work for the Liberals, but they have their own problems.
"It may also be moot so long as people dislike Mr. Trudeau as much as they do and the desire for change is as strong as it is," Coletto said.
In fact, when asked to rate four politicians on a scale of 1 to 10, with one indicating a strong dislike, Donald Trump scored the lowest among Canadians at 3, Justin Trudeau was next at 3.6, Joe Biden was at 4.9 and Pierre Poilievre was at 5.1. That means Canadians dislike Trudeau almost as much as they dislike Trump, which is not a good sign for the Liberal government.
"The public wants change, is cranky and anxious, and is looking for an alternative to Mr. Trudeau and the Liberals," Coletto said.
His survey of voter support found 54% of Canadians believe Poilievre would make the best PM, including 21% of those who voted Liberal in 2021 and 37% of those who voted NDP. By comparison, 46% chose Trudeau as the best PM in a head-to-head matchup.
Nationally, Abacus has the Conservatives up 15 points over the Liberals - 40% support to 25% - with the NDP in third at 20%. More important, the Conservatives hold a commanding lead in every province across the country other than Quebec, where they are performing well enough to hold the 10 seats they have.
These numbers do not bode well for the Trudeau Liberals and, as a fascinating column by John Ibbitson in the Globe and Mail showed recently, things are about to get worse. If an election isn't called by April 22, the number of seats up for grabs in the next election will rise from 338 to 343.
The redistribution based on the population of the 2021 census - already out of date - means that Alberta would get three new seats, while Ontario and British Columbia would each get an additional seat. Toronto and northern Ontario each lose a seat, with the 905 suburban area around Toronto getting the new seat and the two others being shifted.
Four of the five seats are going to be difficult for the Liberals to win, with the fifth new seat being a toss-up with the Conservatives.
It's clear the Trudeau Liberals want to campaign against Donald Trump if they can. If the constant push on that front yields results for them, an election called on April 21 would put voting day in late May or early June, just as the American campaign is solidifying.
That's how desperate the Trudeau Liberals are, campaigning against a politician in another country, watching the calendar to see which dates work for them, and praying Canadians are dumb enough to believe their spin.
At this point, my money is still on Canadians being fed up with Trudeau.
Conducted Jan. 18-23, the Abacus Data poll of 2,199 Canadian adults - using a random sample of online panellists - is considered accurate within 2.1%, 19 times out of 20.

Subject: Political campaigns; Canadians; Voters

People: Trump, Donald J; Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Identifier / keyword: trying; conservative; leader; pierre; poilievre; donald

Title: Trumped-Up?; Grits'strategy to campaign on prez hopeful could backfire, poll suggests

Publication title: The Toronto Sun; Toronto

First page: A.7

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 29, 2024

Document 309 of 356

LILLEY: Poilievre best PM and best to handle Trump, says new poll

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-poilievre-best-pm-handle-trump-says-new/docview/2919487922/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Poilievre+best+PM+and+best+to+handle+Trump%2C+says+new+poll&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Trying to tie Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre to Donald Trump may not be the trump card the Trudeau Liberals think it is. For months, the Liberals have been trying to hurt Poilievre's support among voters by connecting him to the presumptive Republican candidate.
A new poll from Abacus Data shows that about 40% of voters believe that Poilievre and Trump are similar, while roughly 33% find them different, with the rest saying they don't know.
More importantly, though, is that more Canadians believe that Poilievre is the best option to deal with Trump if he wins the White House for a second time in November.
When asked, "If Donald Trump is the next US President, would it be better for Canada if Justin Trudeau or Pierre Poilievre were Prime Minister?" a full 44% chose Poilievre, compared to just 29% picking Trudeau. That includes 17% of current Liberal voters believing Poilievre would be the better choice and 23% of current NDP voters.
Abacus Data CEO David Coletto says the strategy of linking Poilievre and Trump could work for the Liberals, but they have their own problems.
"It may also be moot so long as people dislike Mr. Trudeau as much as they do and the desire for change is as strong as it is," Coletto said.
In fact, when asked to rate four politicians on a scale of one to 10 with one indicating a strong dislike, Donald Trump scored the lowest among Canadians at 3, Justin Trudeau was next at 3.6, Joe Biden was at 4.9 and Pierre Poilievre was at 5.1. That means, Canadians dislike Trudeau almost as much as they dislike Trump, which is not a good sign for the Liberal government.
"The public wants change, is cranky and anxious, and is looking for an alternative to Mr. Trudeau and the Liberals," Coletto said.
His survey of voter support found that 54% of Canadians believe Poilievre would make the best prime minister, including 21% of those who voted Liberal in 2021 and 37% of those who voted NDP. By comparison, just 46% chose Trudeau as the best PM in a head to head match-up.
Nationally, Abacus has the Conservatives up 15 points over the Liberals - 40% support to 25% - with the NDP in third place at 20%. More importantly, the Conservative hold a commanding lead in every province across the country other than Quebec, where they are performing well enough to hold the 10 seats they currently hold.
These numbers do not bode well for the Trudeau Liberals and as a fascinating column by John Ibbitson in the Globe and Mail showed recently, things are about to get worse. If an election isn't called by April 22, the number of seats up for grabs in the next election will rise from 338 to 343.
The redistribution based on the population of the 2021 census - already out of date - means that Alberta would get three new seats, while Ontario and British Columbia would each get an additional seat. In Ontario, Toronto and Northern Ontario each lose a seat, with the 905 suburban area around Toronto getting the new seat and the two others being shifted.
Recommended video
Four of the five seats are going to be difficult for the Liberals to win, with the fifth new seat being a toss-up with the Conservatives.
It's clear that the Trudeau Liberals want to campaign against Donald Trump if they can. If the constant push on that front yields results for them, an election called on April 21 would put voting day in late May or early June, just as the American campaign is solidifying.
That's how desperate the Trudeau Liberals are, campaigning against a politician in another country, watching the calendar to see which dates work for them, and praying Canadians are dumb enough to believe their spin.
At this point, my money is still on Canadians being fed up with Trudeau.
Conducted Jan. 18-23, the Abacus Data poll of 2,199 Canadian adults - using a random sample of online panellists - is considered accurate within 2.1%, 19 times out of 20.
LILLEY: Trudeau trashes Canada's reputation with our allies but at least we have Drake
LILLEY: Trudeau Liberals reaching at straws as desperation grows

Subject: Political campaigns; Canadians; Voters; Prime ministers

Document 310 of 356

Former Toronto councillor Karen Stintz looking to run as federal Conservative

Author: Ruryk, Zenon

Publication info: Ruryk, Zenon. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/former-toronto-councillor-karen-stintz-looking/docview/2919482862/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+Toronto+councillor+Karen+Stintz+looking+to+run+as+federal+Conservative&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Ruryk%2C+Zenon&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A former City Hall heavyweight is jumping to federal politics.
Karen Stintz, high-profile former councillor, mayoral candidate and TTC chair, will announce Monday she's seeking the Conservative nomination in Eglinton-Lawrence.
The mid-Toronto riding, where she lives, usually goes Liberal, but is considered ripe for the picking. Current MP Marco Mendicino was punted as public safety minister last July after his department transferred notorious schoolgirl killer Paul Bernardo to a medium-security prison.
"Marco's a good guy," Stintz said, "but it's a change election, and he's in the wrong party.
"It's a winnable riding and the community feels very let down by the current government" - especially a large Jewish population unhappy about the feds' stance on Israel's war with Hamas.
"The issues come down to a government that is leading from behind," says Stintz.
"I've personally been inspired by (Conservative Leader) Pierre Poilievre. He gives voice to what people mean when they say they want change - affordability, managing the country, investing in the military.
"It's about bringing our country back to a point of strength, when we're not seen that way anymore."
Recommended video
Stintz left City Hall after dropping out of the 2014 mayoral race, which was won by John Tory. Since then, she has been CEO of Variety - the Children's Charity of Ontario, and Variety Village, a sports complex in Scarborough geared to kids with disabilities.
"It changed my life," she says. "I now understand what it really means to be inclusive, how we need to make sure doors are open to everybody."

Identifier / keyword: former; heavyweight; jumping; federal; politics; karen

Document 311 of 356

LILLEY: Will Bonnie Crombie run in Milton? Here are the pros and the cons

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-will-bonnie-crombie-run-milton-here-are/docview/2919440089/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Will+Bonnie+Crombie+run+in+Milton%3F+Here+are+the+pros+and+the+cons&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If Parm Gill is out, does that mean Bonnie Crombie is in?
Since Gill announced last week that he would resign as the PC MPP for Milton, and minister of red tape reduction in the Ford government, speculation has turned to Crombie.
Will the former Mississauga mayor and newly minted Liberal leader move the 30 kilometres or so northwest to run in Milton?
It seems like the logical thing to do since the move could get Crombie a seat in the legislature, but running in Milton may not be the wisest thing to do from a couple of perspectives.
First off, if Crombie were successful in winning the seat and taking the riding from the PC Party, she would have a steady presence at Queen's Park, which is a good thing. But it would also tie her to the legislature, which may not be a good thing.
With the Ontario Liberal Party a shadow of its former self, the party seriously needs to reorganize and rebuild - tasks that might be easier for Crombie to accomplish away from Queen's Park.
Sure, with a seat in the legislature, Crombie could have easier access to the media covering the daily ups and downs of Queen's Park, but in truth, she has easy media access as it is. Rebuilding the infrastructure on the ground that's required to win an election is equally important to winning a seat - if not more so - for a party that wants to regain power.
The second reason that Crombie may want to think twice about running is she might not win.
Milton is a swing riding that - in its current form and its previous incarnations - has gone back and forth between the Liberals and PC's provincially for years. The same is true for the riding's federal counterpart, which is currently held by Liberal MP Adam van Koeverden.
In the last provincial election, Liberal candidate Sameera Ali took 38% of the vote to Gill's 43% of the vote. Gill slightly outperformed his party's province-wide level of voter support - 41% - while Ali far outperformed the 24% the Liberals took across Ontario.
While there is no doubt that Crombie would have a shot at winning the riding, what is best for her party? Concerns that she's not from the area and would be seen as an outsider should be ignored by anyone making this decision - van Koeverden won Milton federally in 2019 after moving there from downtown Toronto.
If Crombie were running in Lambton-Kent-Middlesex, that might be an issue, but not in Milton.
There was a time when leaders without a seat in the legislature would effectively be given a pass by the other parties when contesting a byelection. Those days are long gone, so we shouldn't expect the PC Party to go easy on Crombie if she decides to contest Milton.
The last time the leader of a major Ontario political party sought a seat in a byelection was John Tory in 2009. The Liberals refused to go easy on him, and defeated Tory in the riding of Haliburton-Kawartha Lakes-Brock.
The PC Party has held Milton since 2018, and they won't want to give it up anytime soon, meaning Premier Doug Ford is likely to fight hard in this byelection. Keeping it, if Crombie enters, will be a difficult task and require a strong local candidate who can go toe to toe with the new Liberal leader.
The NDP is effectively non-existent in the riding, taking less than 10% of the vote in the last election, which makes this a PC-Liberal battle.
There are a lot of options for both parties to consider between now and when the byelection is called.

Subject: Provincial elections; Legislatures

Identifier / keyword: bonnie; crombie; announced; resign; https; minister

Title: LILLEY: Will Bonnie Crombie run in Milton? Here are the pros and the cons

Document 312 of 356

'Beyond disappointing'; Rape crisis centre supporting pro-Hamas rally another low

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/beyond-disappointing-rape-crisis-centre/docview/2918874309/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Beyond+disappointing%27%3B+Rape+crisis+centre+supporting+pro-Hamas+rally+another+low&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Just when you thought you might have seen everything comes a Toronto rape clinic helping to organize a pro-Hamas march just months after the systemic Oct. 7 raping of women in Israel.
You read it right. Davenport for Ceasefire, Parkdale-High Park for Palestine and the Toronto Rape Crisis Centre plan to hold a "Community Art Build and Poster Making" event at Bloor St. W. and Dundas St. W. between 1 and 3:30 p.m. on Saturday to "prepare" for a pro-Palestine march on Sunday.
Calling it "family-friendly," the poster has a "free Palestine" meme and logos from the three organizations.
But the fact one of them is the Toronto Rape Crisis Centre (TRCC) is raising many concerns.
Obviously, after many testimonials from at least 10 women who were subject to sexual violence on the Black Sabbath that was Oct. 7, it's highly inappropriate for a rape centre to be involved in something like this.
But it's egregious anyway. Why would a rape crisis centre get involved in something like this? How could any Jewish woman ever seek out support there in light of this? Hamas 'proud' of acts
"It is beyond disappointing to see the Toronto Rape Crisis Centre participating in this event," community leader Ariella Kimmel said. "As a Jewish woman, I've watched for months since Oct. 7 as those around us excluded victims of sexual violence because they are Israeli. Hamas didn't just commit their atrocities, they were so proud of them, they livestreamed them."
It was all so horrifying. But months later, it's like its being celebrated.
And the whole truth on how many women were raped Oct. 7 is still unknown.
There are still many missing and kidnapped people, many were killed and can't talk, and others have not yet spoken of what they went through.
It's surprising how many people are prepared to move on from Oct. 7.
"It is alarming that the TRCC would choose to partake in an event critical of Israel, while it has chosen to remain silent and not condemn the sexual violence experienced by Israeli women during the Oct. 7 attack," said Richard Robertson, B'nai Brith's director of research and advocacy. "Silence from such organizations as the TRCC has enabled Israel's detractors to repeatedly deny the trauma of sexual violence experienced by Israeli women.
"By participating in an event with an organization that calls for a ceasefire while ignoring the Israeli hostages still being held by Hamas, including women who we know are enduring acts of acts of rape, the TRCC is sending a clear message to Jewish women, and taking away a safe space for them to come when victimized," he added.
The TRCC didn't return a request for comment.
Nor had the office of Premier Doug Ford, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau or Mayor Olivia Chow, who may all be interested in this, since this centre is a recipient of provincial funding that comes from taxpayers in their jurisdictions.
Thanks to some research from Olga Goldberg, we've learned that Toronto sends $11,500 annually to the TRCC, while the Ontario Trillium Foundation provided $24,500.
The work they do is important and no taxpayers mind seeing money earmarked for the centre.
But if any of that money is being used in a political way to support a group that killed more than 1,200 people and kidnapped up to 250 others, many say they do have a problem.
Throw in the fact there were so many women raped - and that there still are hostages - and this whole thing is gross.
While the International Court of Justice ruled Friday that it's inaccurate to call Israel defending itself against the Oct. 7 barbaric attack as genocide, it did not deal with the real culprits of that day: Hamas.
But anti-Israel sentiment around the world is one thing. It's abhorrent when it happens locally.
"The rape crisis centre should be a place any person experiencing sexual assault should feel comfortable to go," said Joanna Salit, who is a registered social worker with a master's degree in the field and understands these resources from the administration to the ground treatment level.
What Salit sees here with this event is something she has not seen before in Canada.
"Clearly, they are excluding Jewish women and Israeli women and that is appalling," she said. "Sexual assault was used as weapons of war by Hamas, which has not been acknowledged by this organization. It is really upsetting. They are supporting rapists when they are supposed to be supporting all people who are sexually assaulted."
Advocate for all Kimmel agrees.
"If you advocate for women, then that advocacy cannot be dependent on whether you agree with the government of the country from which the victim comes," she says.
It's hard to imagine anybody would want to take part in something so vile, but if this does indeed happen Saturday, Jewish women should heed the warning of what that means. jwarmington@postmedia.com @joe_warmington

Subject: Violence; Women; Sex crimes; Advocacy; Rape

Location: Israel

Company / organization: Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Document 314 of 356

Carjacking task force nabs 89 suspects, lays 554 charges, Toronto Police say

Author: Spiro Papuckoski

Publication info: Spiro Papuckoski. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/carjacking-task-force-nabs-89-suspects-lays-554/docview/2918541248/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Carjacking+task+force+nabs+89+suspects%2C+lays+554+charges%2C+Toronto+Police+say&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Spiro+Papuckoski&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Efforts to combat violent carjackings and other related crimes in the Greater Toronto Area are starting to bear fruit.
The Provincial Carjacking Joint Task Force (PCJTF), a collaboration between police forces across the GTA as well as the Ontario Provincial Police and Criminal Intelligence Service of Ontario, on Thursday announced 89 arrests and 554 charges, including carjackings and home invasions to steal vehicles.
Since Sept. 21, 2023, 109 vehicles have also been recovered by the task force.
"Our collaboration with the OPP and other services across the GTA as part of the PCJTF has proven very successful thus far," Toronto Police Supt. Steve Watts said in a statement.
"Although the number of carjackings in Toronto decreased in 2023 compared to the year before, we continue to see a concerning rise in violence related to auto thefts that the PCJTF will continue to address."
Liberals to hold auto-theft summit in February amid uptick in stolen cars sent abroad
Belleville men charged with robbery in Peel carjacking probe
Youth charged with robbery, gun offences in carjacking probe
The task force was created last year to disrupt criminal networks responsible for high-risk auto thefts, which cops say increasingly involve violence, firearms and other weapons.
Police say thieves use the stolen vehicles to either carry out other crimes or to ship them overseas to be resold. Criminals are also known to alter vehicle identification numbers (VINs) and sell the vehicles to unsuspecting buyers in Canada.
The PCJTF, which is co-led by Toronto Police and the OPP and receives funding from the Ontario government, also involves police in Peel, York, Durham and Halton regions.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO

Subject: Automobile theft; Task forces; Collaboration; Arrests; Home invasion; Robbery; Firearms

Document 315 of 356

Trudeau not doing little things to make life affordable

Author: Terrazzano, Franco

Publication info: Terrazzano, Franco. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-not-doing-little-things-make-life/docview/2918378374/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+not+doing+little+things+to+make+life+affordable&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=Terrazzano%2C+Franco&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Figuring out how to make life more affordable for Canadians shouldn't be like unravelling Einstein's theory of relativity.
If Prime Minister Justin Trudeau won't do the big things to make life more affordable, he could at least do the little things.
"We know Canadians are facing challenging times right now, people are squeezed between the cost of groceries, rents," Trudeau said during a cabinet retreat in Montreal aimed at "bringing down the cost of living."
Trudeau knows "people are squeezed" because his tax hikes are some of the things that are doing the squeezing.
Take the carbon tax. Trudeau may never scrap his carbon tax, but he could at least not raise it again on April 1.
Even after the rebates, average families will be out hundreds of dollars this year because of the higher heating bills, gas prices, inflation and the economic damage wrought by the carbon tax, according to the parliamentary budget officer.
Governments of all political stripes have paused fuel taxes to provide relief.
British Columbia's New Democrats delayed their carbon tax hike during the pandemic.
Manitoba's NDP government suspended its fuel tax.
Newfoundland and Labrador's Liberals are also providing fuel tax relief, and so are the Conservative governments in Alberta and Ontario.
The United Kingdom, Sweden, Australia, South Korea, the Netherlands, Germany, Norway, India, Ireland, Israel, Italy, New Zealand and Portugal also provided fuel tax relief.
To add insult to injury, the feds charge their sales tax on top of the carbon tax. That's right. The federal government applies its sales tax after all the per-litre taxes are added.
This tax-on-tax is costing Canadians about $500 million this year, according to the PBO.
By the end of 2030, the GST on the carbon tax alone will have cost Canadians $6.2 billion.
Ending the tax-on-tax is a simple way to save Canadians billions when fuelling up or heating their homes.
Trudeau knows taxes make it more expensive to stay warm during the winter.
Otherwise, why would he have taken the carbon tax off home heating oil for three years? That political ploy was an attempt to help Atlantic Canadians amid tanking poll numbers in this typical Liberal stronghold.
However, 97% of Canadian families use other forms of energy to heat their homes. Trudeau should extend the relief he provided to Atlantic Canadians to everyone by taking the carbon tax off all forms of home heating. That would save the average family using natural gas about $1,100 over three years.
Trudeau can also give farmers relief and ease grocery prices by making sure the original Bill C-234 becomes law this year, which would remove the carbon tax from the natural gas and propane used on farms.
The House of Commons already passed this relief twice, but it still isn't law because of shenanigans in the Senate.
The carbon tax on natural gas and propane that's used to heat barns and dry grain will cost farmers $1 billion by 2030, according to the PBO.
By making it more expensive for farmers to grow food, the carbon tax makes it more expensive for Canadians to buy food.
There's one more easy way for Trudeau to provide relief: stop his upcoming 4.7% alcohol tax hike.
Last year, the feds capped the annual increase at 2%.
Trudeau shouldn't have hiked the tax at all, but the smaller increase reduced the tax burden by $100 million. At a time when both consumers and businesses are struggling, freezing the alcohol tax is the least Trudeau could do.
The Trudeau government doesn't need an expensive getaway in Montreal to figure out how to make life more affordable. There's a simple solution - stop taking so much money from Canadians.
Terrazzano is federal director of the Canadian Taxpayers Federation

Subject: Fuel taxes; Canadians; Relief provisions; Sales taxes; Carbon; Heating; Natural gas; Environmental tax; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Fuel taxes Relief provisions Sales taxes Environmental tax Tax increases

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada

Identifier / keyword: figuring; affordable; canadians; unravelling; theory; relativity

Title: Trudeau not doing little things to make life affordable

Turn over a new leaf; Letters to the editor

Author: Seale, Andrea

Publication info: Seale, Andrea. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/turn-over-new-leaf-letters-editor/docview/2918025552/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Turn+over+a+new+leaf%3B+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=Seale%2C+Andrea&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's National Non-Smoking Week, and Ontario has a historic opportunity to reduce health-care costs and reform the tobacco industry.
Over many decades, Big Tobacco has engaged in deception and targeted marketing that has addicted youth and resulted in immense suffering, disease and premature death for so many people. The devastating impact of tobacco use in Ontario is extensive and undeniable - 1.4 million (11%) still smoke tobacco, with 16,000 tobacco-related deaths and $2.2 billion in health-care costs every year. All 10 provinces have filed lawsuits against the tobacco industry collectively seeking more than $500 billion in damages, and right now the Ontario government is negotiating a settlement with Big Tobacco. To prevent disease and save lives, it's vital that the settlement implement strict new controls on the tobacco industry, and establish substantial long-term funding for tobacco control through an independent fund. We must protect future generations. As such, the Canadian Cancer Society urges Premier Doug Ford to use this monumental opportunity to hold Big Tobacco accountable and prevent future harm to Ontarians.
CEO, CANADIAN CANCER SOCIETY (A vile habit that takes too many lives)

Letters to the Editor, Jan. 24, 2024

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/letters-editor-jan-24-2024/docview/2917959405/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: TURN A NEW LEAF
It's National Non-Smoking Week, and Ontario has a historic opportunity to reduce health-care costs and reform the tobacco industry. Over many decades, Big Tobacco has engaged in deception and targeted marketing that has addicted youth and resulted in immense suffering, disease and premature death for so many people. The devastating impact of tobacco use in Ontario is extensive and undeniable - 1.4 million (11%) still smoke tobacco, with 16,000 tobacco-related deaths and $2.2 billion in health-care costs every year. We must protect future generations. As such, the Canadian Cancer Society urges Premier Doug Ford to use this monumental opportunity to hold Big Tobacco accountable and prevent future harm to Ontarians.
Andrea Seale
CEO, Canadian Cancer Society
(A vile habit that takes too many lives)

Dundas Square, the issue that won't go away. With the erection of a bust honouring the late Lincoln Alexander at Queen's Park, the answer seems easy: Lincoln Alexander Square. A truly great man who typifies all that we can be proud of - diversity, selflessness, dignity, the list goes on. Let's put an end to this the right way.
David Woodley
Burlington
(Lincoln Alexander was first-class all the way)

Document 320 of 356

Another fine mess the feds created

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/another-fine-mess-feds-created/docview/2917705597/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Another+fine+mess+the+feds+created&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Attempting to fix yet another problem its own policies created, the Trudeau government on Monday announced a two-year cap on international students, in a system Immigration Minister Marc Miller described last week as "out of control."
That raises the question of why the government didn't act before now.
"It's not the intention of this program to have sham commerce degrees or business degrees (from colleges) that are sitting on top of a massage parlour that someone doesn't even go to and then they come into the province and drive an Uber," Miller said Monday.
If things are that bad, why was it allowed to continue for so long? In addition, how could the Trudeau government not have anticipated the problems it would create by dramatically hiking its immigration targets for permanent residents, imposing no limit on the number of international students and temporary foreign workers, and overwhelming cities with asylum seekers? It ignored warnings from its public servants two years ago that such policies would contribute to Canada's housing, affordability and health-care crises, when it announced an "ambitious" plan to boost Canada's annual immigration targets to 465,000 permanent residents in 2023, 485,000 in 2024 and 500,000 in 2025.
It's since announced another 500,000 target for 2026, compared to 272,000 when the Liberals came to power in 2015.
There are currently 900,000 international students in Canada - permits last for three years at a time - compared to 352,325 in 2015 when the Liberals came to power.
So don't expect Monday's announcement to reduce the number of international student visas this year by 35% - to 364,000 compared to 560,000 last year - with the 2025 target to be set later this year, to perform miracles.
To be fair, not all of this is on the federal government. The provinces decide which schools are authorized to accept international students, which has led to situations where some schools are degree mills churning out useless diplomas as a way to short-circuit the normal process for being accepted into Canada from abroad.
Meanwhile, many universities have been admitting foreign students - who pay higher tuition costs - to improve their bottom lines. In 2020, for example, tuition paid by international students accounted for 70% of all tuition paid in Ontario.

Subject: Foreign students; Immigration

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: attempting; another; problem; policies; created; trudeau

Title: Another fine mess the feds created

Document 321 of 356

EDITORIAL: Another fine mess the feds created

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/editorial-another-fine-mess-feds-created/docview/2917622653/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=EDITORIAL%3A+Another+fine+mess+the+feds+created&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Attempting to fix yet another problem its own policies created, the Trudeau government on Monday announced a two-year cap on international students, in a system Immigration Minister Marc Miller described last week as "out of control."
That raises the question of why the government didn't act before now.
"It's not the intention of this program to have sham commerce degrees or business degrees (from colleges) that are sitting on top of a massage parlour that someone doesn't even go to and then they come into the province and drive an Uber," Miller said Monday.
If things are that bad, why was it allowed to continue for so long?
In addition, how could the Trudeau government not have anticipated the problems it would create by dramatically hiking its immigration targets for permanent residents, imposing no limit on the number of international students and temporary foreign workers, and overwhelming cities with asylum seekers?
It ignored warnings from its public servants two years ago that such policies would contribute to Canada's housing, affordability and health-care crises, when it announced an "ambitious" plan to boost Canada's annual immigration targets to 465,000 permanent residents in 2023, 485,000 in 2024 and 500,000 in 2025.
It's since announced another 500,000 target for 2026, compared to 272,000 when the Liberals came to power in 2015.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
There are currently 900,000 international students in Canada - permits last for three years at a time - compared to 352,325 in 2015 when the Liberals came to power.
So don't expect Monday's announcement to reduce the number of international student visas this year by 35% - to 364,000 compared to 560,000 last year - with the 2025 target to be set later this year, to perform miracles.
To be fair, not all of this is on the federal government.
The provinces decide which schools are authorized to accept international students, which has led to situations where some schools are degree mills churning out useless diplomas as a way to short-circuit the normal process for being accepted into Canada from abroad.
Meanwhile, many universities have been admitting foreign students - who pay higher tuition costs - to improve their bottom lines. In 2020, for example, tuition paid by international students accounted for 70% of all tuition paid in Ontario.

Subject: Foreign students; Immigration

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: attempting; another; problem; policies; created; trudeau

Top clicks

Author: Wilford, Denette

Publication info: Wilford, Denette. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/top-clicks/docview/2917116263/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Top+clicks&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=Wilford%2C+Denette&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: PAYING FOR THEIR GAMES Pro-Palestinian protesters pushed their luck and found themselves in trouble with the law.
Despite a warning from Toronto Police that those demonstrating on the Avenue Rd. and Hwy. 401 overpass would be arrested and removed, some people showed up last Saturday and were ultimately arrested when they refused to leave.
The police were still gentle with them compared to other incidents we've seen in the past, Joe Warmington reported, though no coffee or donuts were passed to them either.
Now that police have started to push back, they need the full support of Mayor Olivia Chow, Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. FAKIH IN HOT SEAT AFTER X POST Paramount Foods was in damage control following reaction to a now-deleted tweet from its founder, Mohamad Fakih, who suggested he doesn't "need" money from someone who "supports baby killers."
Fakih's reaction on X was in response to a user who vowed to "never set foot" in one of his restaurants ever again because "terrorist He added more posts of the like, which have also been deleted, but it was too little, too late because the reaction was swift.
Fakih claims to have been "shocked" by the criticism and death threats, saying his posts were "misconstrued" and not anti-Semitic.
While he admitted that the damage was done, many online said he did it to himself. OH BABY! If you were a woman and able to get pregnant, then found out you along with four others were knocked up by the same dude, what would you do? Chop off the baby (babies?) daddy's goodies? Get into a fight with the other mamasto-be? Or make nice with the other ladies and forgive the soon-to-be-dad (times five)? If you chose the latter, then good on you because that's exactly what happened with five women in New York who all got pregnant at the same time by the same man, Denette Wilford reported.
While the Internet was shocked, the women - who joked that they were "sister wives" - all plan to get along like one big happy family and "work together" on this wild journey.
MUTANT COVID STRAIN Scientists in China have created a mutant strain of COVID-19 in a lab that has a 100% death rate for mice with human genes.
The pre-print study, published in early January on bioRxiv by researchers in Beijing, identified two SARS-CoV-2-related pangolin coronaviruses documented in 2017 and '19 - obviously before the COVID-19 outbreak - called pCoV-GD01 and GX_P2V.
The scientists focused on the latter, GX_P2V, and created a strain that infected the lungs, bones, eyes, tracheas and brains of four lab mice, and the test subjects'deaths were "surprisingly" quick, they said.
While there is the potential that the GX_P2V virus could infect humans, the researchers noted that there is no evidence that this version of the virus is in the wild.
To the best of their knowledge, at least.
However, the director of the Genetics Institute at University College London called the study "terrible" and scientifically "totally pointless."

Subject: Coronaviruses; COVID-19

Document 323 of 356

Playing into PM's hands; Canadian media outlets pile on Poilievre

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/playing-into-pms-hands-canadian-media-outlets/docview/3126972427/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Playing+into+PM%27s+hands%3B+Canadian+media+outlets+pile+on+Poilievre&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Trudeau Liberals are now playing with Quebec nationalist sentiment to try to gain votes.
There is literally no low the Trudeau Liberals won't sink to in order to try to win votes, including on issues of national unity.
On Thursday, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre took to social media to denounce the massive drop in housing starts across Quebec. It was a drop of 32% across the province but 37% in Montreal and 40% in Quebec City.
"Massive drop in construction in Quebec, while (Prime Minister Justin) Trudeau pays billions to incompetent mayors, Marchand and Plante, who block construction sites. Federal money for cities will be tied to the number of houses and apartments built when I am PM," Poilievre posted on X.
Bruno Marchand is the mayor of Quebec City and Valerie Plante is the mayor of Montreal. Neither of them have a great record on housing and both deserve to be called out. Yet despite Poilievre having called out mayors in English Canada using similar language and despite him praising the mayors of Victoriaville, Trois-Rivieres and Saguenay for increasing housing supply, his comments were portrayed as anti-Quebec.
First up was The Canadian Press, the useless, overpriced and biased wire service I've been telling my editors, bosses and CEOs to dump for close to 25 years. CP should be a straight shooter in the news business. Unlike the Toronto Sun, it isn't supposed to have a political slant, but there it was within two hours of Poilievre's post turning this into a national unity issue.
Right after CP's slanted reporting that made this about Poilievre insulting Quebec came the Liberal Party of Canada with ready-made graphics denouncing his comments. After that came the national media, quick to jump on Poilievre's statements without looking at any context, just following the Liberal pied piper.
It's really sad how biased our national media are on this and so many other fronts. Poilievre has blasted mayors across the country for failing to build housing, for standing in the way of new construction, for adding regulations that needlessly drive up costs and no one has said a thing.
As soon as he criticizes progressive mayors in Quebec, this becomes a national unity issue. We're treated to pundits who say the perfectly bilingual man who has lived on the Ontario-Quebec border for 25 years, who has sat in Parliament for 20 years, who's married to a Quebecer, doesn't understand the province and likely hates it.
Give me a break.
This is nothing more than the Liberals playing a dangerous game with national unity, inflaming Quebec nationalist tendencies needlessly to try to salvage some votes and a good part of the national media going along with it. They'll reply that I'm biased, that I'm an opinion columnist, that I regularly back conservative politicians.
All of those things are true and I'm upfront about my politics and have been for years and years.
What excuse do the supposedly neutral newsrooms of this country have for playing the Liberal game of inflaming national unity? If you're a neutral observer, an arbiter of the truth, surely you'd have seen the posts where Poilievre has called out mayors in Toronto, Vancouver, Calgary and elsewhere in his crusade to build more housing, to make it more affordable.
They ignore that though, because it doesn't fit the narrative.
This coming election will be fascinating because it won't just be the CBC, whom Poilievre has promised to defund, coming after him, it will be most of the gallery, most of the national media. The mask has been pulled back, people see these outlets for what they are, partisan and biased while not declaring it and pretending to be neutral.
Fewer people trusting the media each day isn't a good position for my industry, but it's the position my colleagues have chosen to take. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Mayors; Housing starts

Business indexing term: Subject: Housing starts

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Quebec City Quebec Canada; Canada; Quebec Canada

Identifier / keyword: trudeau; liberals; playing; quebec; nationalist; sentiment

TOP CLICKS: The week that was in viral stories

Author: Wilford, Denette

Publication info: Wilford, Denette. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/top-clicks-week-that-was-viral-stories/docview/2916892226/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TOP+CLICKS%3A+The+week+that+was+in+viral+stories&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Wilford%2C+Denette&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Toronto Sun takes you straight to the heart of the action.
Whether it's local news, provincial and national politics, or the worlds of celebrity and sports, we have you covered.
Some stories set the world on fire. And these ones are the most popular online stories from the past seven days, clicked on by Sun readers like you.
Here are our top stories:
WARMINGTON: Pro-Hamas protesters playing games with police ... then get arrested
Pro-Palestinian protesters pushed their luck and found themselves in trouble with the law.
Despite a warning from Toronto Police that those demonstrating on the Avenue Rd. and Hwy. 401 overpass would be arrested and removed, some people showed up last Saturday and were ultimately arrested when they refused to leave.
The police were still gentle with them compared to other incidents we have seen in the past, Joe Warmington reported, though no coffee or doughnuts were passed to them either.
Now that police have started to push back, they need the full support of Mayor Olivia Chow, Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
Mohamad Fakih, CEO of Paramount Fine Foods, is pictured during the pandemic in the empty dining room at his west-end location at 1585 The Queensway on Oct. 23, 2020. (Ernest Doroszuk/Toronto Sun)
WARMINGTON: Paramount Foods CEO Mohamad Fakih in hot seat after X post
Paramount Foods was in damage control following reaction to a now-deleted tweet from its founder Mohamad Fakih, who suggested he doesn't "need" money from someone who "supports baby killers."
Fakih's reaction on X was in response to a user who vowed to "never set food" in one of his restaurants ever again because "terrorist sympathizers don't deserve my business," Warmington reported.
He added more posts of the like, which have also been deleted, but it was too little, too late because the reaction was swift.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
Fakih claims to have been "shocked" by the criticism and death threats, saying his posts were "misconstrued" and not anti-Semitic.
While he acknowledged the damage was done, many online said he did it to himself.
Man making funny face while posing with five baby mamas. (lizzyashmusic/Instagram)
Man still alive after getting five women pregnant at the same time
If you were a woman and able to get pregnant, then found out you along with four others were knocked up by the same dude, what would you do?
Chop off the baby (babies?) daddy's goodies? Get into a fight with the other mamas-to-be? Or make nice with the other ladies and forgive the soon-to-be-dad (times five)?
If you chose the latter, then good on you because that's exactly what happened with five women in New York who all got pregnant at the same time by the same man, Denette Wilford reported.
While the Internet was shocked, the women - who joked that they were "sister wives" - all plan to get along like one big happy family and "work together" on this wild journey.
Group alleges ban on protests at Toronto highway overpass enforced 'selectively'
The most - and least - expensive GTA real estate markets of the year
'YES, PLEASE!': Trudeau accuses Poilievre of wanting to 'take Canada backwards'
Two Brampton homes that sold for a difference of $400,000 in 2 1/2 years. (Google Maps)
Brampton home sales offer glimpse into recent real estate market 'bloodbath'
Two homes on the same Brampton street that were up for sale two-and-a half years apart had significantly different selling prices, showing just how much of a "bloodbath" the real estate market has become, Wilford reported.
For example, three properties that recently sold were also on the market about two years ago - one was a power of sale and suffered a $265,000 loss from 2021, another sold for $250,000 less, and yet a third sold for a loss of $225,000.
That last home, which sold for $1,545,000, may sound good but in April 2022, its almost-identical next-door neighbour sold for $1,935,000. Terrifying times, but hopefully looking up?
A pre-print study from China says mice infected with a mutant COVID-19 strain had a 100% death rate. (gorodenkoff/iStock/Getty Images)
Chinese scientists create mutant COVID strain that kills mice
Scientists in China have created a mutant strain of COVID-19 in a lab that has a 100% death rate for mice with human genes.
The pre-print study, published in early January on bioRxiv by researchers in Beijing, identified two SARS-CoV-2-related pangolin coronaviruses documented in 2017 and 2019 - obviously before the COVID-19 outbreak - called pCoV-GD01 and GX_P2V.
The scientists focused on the latter, GX_P2V, and created a strain that infected the lungs, bones, eyes, tracheas and brains of four lab mice and the test subjects' deaths were "surprisingly" quick, they said.
While there is the potential that the GX_P2V virus could infect humans, the researchers noted there is no evidence that this version of the virus is in the wild. To the best of their knowledge, at least.
However, the director of the Genetics Institute at the University College London called the study "terrible" and scientifically "totally pointless."

LILLEY: Trudeau attacks Poilievre on national unity, media joins in

Document 326 of 356

LEAFS TIME MACHINE: Golly Gee, It's Howie Day in Canada!

Author: Hornby, Lance

Publication info: Hornby, Lance. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/leafs-time-machine-golly-gee-howie-day-canada/docview/2916686307/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LEAFS+TIME+MACHINE%3A+Golly+Gee%2C+It%27s+Howie+Day+in+Canada%21&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Hornby%2C+Lance&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One could spend all of Hockey Day In Canada recounting the Saturday night hijinks of Howie Meeker.
He and his tell-tale telestrator sadly left us in 2020, the oldest Maple Leaf at age 97, but Sportsnet's Hockey Day show is giving a shout-out to the Parksville, B.C., resident as part of the Vancouver-Toronto game.
"Howie was an icon, he started it all in TV hockey analysis," said Charlie Hodge, his close friend who wrote two books with Meeker, Golly Gee, It's Me and Stop It There, Back It Up!.
"It could have been anyone, but he found a way to apply (the medium) to hockey. No one had taken the size of the rink, the many parts of the game and broken it all down, in a way viewers could understand.
"You appreciated the game was more than just the puck going in the net. He was famous for his hockey schools, too. He simply loved the game.
"His personality was unique, he had a rough charm about him, but unlike Don Cherry, he never said anything rude."
Hodge describes Meeker as "a tough bugger" right to the end.
Putting his promising junior career in and around his native Kitchener, Ont., on hold, Meeker enlisted in the Canadian forces, nearly killed while training in England when a fellow soldier's grenade accidentally landed at his feet.
"I'd have killed the bastard, but they transferred him first," Meeker told the Toronto Sun at his Hockey Hall of Fame induction for the Foster Hewitt Award in 1998.
He survived his leg wounds and landed in France a year later. Returning home, he won the 1947 Calder Trophy as rookie of the year with the Leafs, beating Gordie Howe among others, thanks in part to a five-goal game.
After 185 points and four Stanley Cups up to 1951, Meeker kept playing while serving two years as Conservative Member of Parliament in Waterloo. After retiring, he coached the Leafs' farm team in Pittsburgh to the 1955 Calder Cup, spent one year behind Toronto's bench, the youngest in the league at the time, and was very briefly its general manager in 1957.
We say briefly because team exec Stafford Smythe made the mistake of shoving Meeker during a heated player personnel debate just before the season began. Meeker decked him and stormed away without working one game, replaced by Punch Imlach, whom Meeker had scouted as his potential assistant.
There followed some wilderness years for Meeker, who yearned to teach the game. In the first chapter of Stop It There! he relayed to Hodge a recurring dream of playground shinny where all his teammates were out of position despite his warnings, leaving him to defend a 5-on-1.
Meeker finally found an outlet in running instruction camps, under contract to the government of Newfoundland. He dabbled in radio broadcast work for CJON in St. John's, supplementing his income in sales and promotions for companies such as Toyota, Samsonite and Mattel Toys, but could not shake the feeling that fundamentals were being lost at both the amateur and pro level.
During a trip through Montreal in 1968 for a toy convention, he ran into Habs' TV host Ted Darling, who needed a guest analyst for a game against visiting Chicago.
"I had no idea at the time what an amazing series of events was about to unfold when I first stuck on the headset," Meeker wrote.
The squeaky-sounding sounding Meeker figured he might get only one shot, so why hold back his opinions?
"I've got the worst voice in the world, I can't remember faces and names and they wanted to make me a colour analyst?'" Meeker told the Sun. "But I'd watched other guys do it. They were scared to say anything that would cost them their jobs. They said nothing instructional, nothing critical."
Colour man Dick Irvin Jr. cringed when Meeker opened by insisting to the audience that multi-Cup winning defenceman J.C. Tremblay had messed up a play. But the next day producer Ralph Mellanby offered Meeker a regular seat.
That paired him with Dave Hodge in Toronto, in their powder blue HNIC jackets. Most fans loved Meeker's theatrics and quaint exclamations such as 'jiminy crickets' and 'gee willikers.'
"I grew up in an era where locker-room language was atrocious," Meeker told the Sun. "If I'd used that on air, I'd have been fired, so I said something else. You're like an actor. You do things to disturb people. All I cared about was it would get them interested and talking about hockey."
Howie Meeker ran as a member of the PC party.
Meeker and the telestrator, a device allowing free-hand drawings over video, became inseparable.
"I said a prayer every night to thank it for saving my butt," he said of on-air chirping the technicians to speed up, rewind, slow down or freeze the highlight. "My producer went nuts when I'd say it live and he'd get mad at the director. And the tape would always break."
Meeker didn't shy away on many issues, convinced Canada had learned too little from its near-defeat to Russia in the '72 Summit Series: "What fools we are. The Canadian know-it-all attitude toward hockey is about as stubborn and stupid as the average male's about asking for directions or reading a road map. It doesn't happen until we are really lost."
He expressed similar concerns in '98 when the country's best crashed out of the Nagano Olympics.
Meanwhile, he and wife Grace had fallen in love with B.C. in the late 1970s after taking a fishing trip while there while attending a coaching clinic. After working Canucks games for BCTV, he retired in '98, devoting time to favourite causes such as Special Olympics and guide dogs for the blind. He's a Member of The Order of Canada.
"To me, he was a Dad, a mentor, so many things and taught me a lot of life lessons," said Hodge, now a city councillor in Kelowna, B.C. "When the first book came out, we went to a lot of hotels, restaurants and bars, where he was always signing autographs, never turning away a fan of any age. He'd be the oldest person at any rink we visited, but the last guy to leave.
"'That's what it's all about kid,'" he'd tell me. "'The people.'"
ONCE A LEAF
Featuring one of the more than 1,100 skaters coaches and general managers who have played or worked in Toronto since 1917.
C Dave Tomlinson
Born: May 8, 1969 in North Vancouver, B.C.
Years with the Leafs: 1991-93
Games played: 6 (0-0-0, 4 PIM)
Sweater numbers: 14, 37
THEN
Whenever a stranger expresses surprise that Tomlinson once played for the Leafs, he's quick to boast he was picked third overall in the 1989 NHL draft.
"Then I lower my voice and say 'the supplemental draft'," the Sportsnet TV analyst for the Canucks said with a laugh. "At the time, I didn't even know what the supplemental draft was (a short-lived dispersal of older NCAA and Canadian university players, when Tomlinson had just completed his second year at Boston U).
"There was a call to our house in B.C., Mom answered and said 'it's for you, it's the Leafs.' Someone from their management was on the line, told me 'congratulations' and they sent me a bag of team T-shirts."
Tomlinson, who had 16 goals and 30 assists that college season, says he probably has Terriers teammate Joe Sacco to thank.
"The Leafs had a scout at our games watching Joe (an '87 pick) and must have noticed me. I also owe a lot to Jackie Parker, a great coach."
In the '91 national championship game, a year the 5-foot-10 Tomlinson was second to Shawn McEachern in team goals, BU lost 8-7 in triple overtime to Northern Michigan after Joe's brother, David, tied it in the last minute of regulation. BU also had Keith Tkachuk and Mark Bavis, the latter killed in a hijacked plane on 9/11 when he scouted for the L.A. Kings.
Tomlinson vividly recalled his first Leafs camp.
"Seventy guys divided into four teams, about 17 trying out at centre, eight of them on one-way contracts. At the hotel, I made the mistake of reading a newspaper and one (camp preview) said 'this Tomlinson kid has no hope.'
"Whoever wrote that likely never saw me and that was mostly the start of me playing with a chip on my shoulder."
He stayed three years in the organization, netting 64 goals over two seasons with the St. John's Leafs. He appreciated GM Cliff Fletcher calling him up twice, but sensed the Leafs had committed to Yanic Perreault and Mike Eastwood as bottom-six centres and asked for a trade at the end of 1993.
He played 36 further NHL games in Winnipeg and Florida and in '96, heard of an opening in the German League in Mannheim.
"I went for what I thought was one year and stayed 10," he said of four different club stops, taking advantage of new liberal import rules. "I wanted to be somewhere I could be a difference-maker and we won a couple of titles."
NOW
Tomlinson had two career ambitions: To play hockey and talk about hockey.
"Watching and listening to Howie Meeker had always intrigued me growing up. There's a favourite line of his I imitated when seeing him on Canucks' TV: 'that Stan Smyl, can't skate, can't shoot, but a helluva player.'
"I was fortunate enough to get a break in the business and worked hard at it."
By 2010, he'd replaced veteran Tom Larscheid on colour for Canucks radio for the local TSN station, until it lost the rights. Laid off with other staff two years later, he was eventually hired by the expansion Seattle Kraken for the same role, then lured to the Canucks' regional TV games on Sportsnet.
"This is my highest goal now and I want to keep getting better."
When not at the rink or on the road, he follows the career of his 19-year-old daughter, actress Leni Notroff-Tomlinson, and son Rocco, a 14-year-old baseball player.
FAVOURITE LEAFS MEMORY
Tomlinson can now joke about the secretive 'future considerations' involved in his trade from Toronto to Florida in July of 1993, the paltry sum of $25,000 to Toronto and the rights to Greg Smyth, who played a bit for the Leafs and St. John's.
"I figured Florida was a great place to go, a new expansion team where as a two-way centre, I'd have a real chance to play. A few days later I was going home to B.C., but my plane was diverted to Dallas. I called to tell Mom I'd be late and she said I'd just been traded to Winnipeg.
"I became another trivia question, the first player ever traded by the Panthers."
LEAFS NOTES: Mark Giordano has been around long enough not to panic over a losing streak
Auston Matthews hat trick triggers Leafs win, takes heat off coach Sheldon Keefe
LOOSE LEAFS
Wayne Simmonds will be among the former players and celebs at Winterfest on Feb. 3 at Trinity Bellwoods Park as part of the Hockey Diversity Alliance and NHL all-star weekend. "HDA has been committed to promoting diversity and inclusion in hockey since day one. By opening the event to the public, we hope to inspire a new generation of players and fans," Simmonds said ... 'Voice of the Leafs' Joe Bowen has given his blessing to a 'Holy Mackinaw' line of T-shirts from the X handle @LeafsJelly, which applies his radio play by play to televised games.
THIS WEEK IN LEAFS HISTORY
Frank Mahovlich is in the news as Auston Matthews and Mitch Marner come up on a few of his scoring records. Next Thursday will mark 63 years since he broke the single-season goal record of 38, which was held by Tod Sloan and Gaye Stewart, the Big M coming two shy of 50 ... Can you imagine a 60-minute, three-goal NHL all-star game with today's firepower in a no-hit contest? On Jan. 19, 1971, the West Division edged the East 2-1, Dave Keon the only Leaf in the game ... Saturday is the anniversary of the 1982 Darryl Sittler trade to Philadelphia.
Have a question, comment or want to see a former Leaf profiled? Drop a line to lhornby@postmedia.com.

Letters to the Editor, Jan. 18, 2023

Full text: FIRE KEEFE
Maple Leafs head coach Sheldon Keefe must be fired! It's time! It's over! After Sunday's game, he said those who have been playing well moved up and those who weren't moved down. Punishment? Really? You think, in reality, the players care what line they play on? Oh, maybe a smidgen, but that's it! They laugh, making a tonne of money for awful play. So they move six feet down the bench, big deal - it's actually more rest. What will it take for this pathetic excuse for a sports franchise to do the right thing and start by firing Keefe? Wake up, fans!
Dr. Steve Starkman Toronto
(By all accounts, they aren't going to do much to make some changes)
LEAFS NEED NEW LEADERSHIP
Quick recap of my letter from approximately five years ago. Letting Lamoriello go, keeping Dubas. Signing Tavares, Matthews, Marner and now Nylander to ridiculous contracts. From the players brought in last summer, not one is performing with any distinction to date. Nylander is a great talent when he wants to play. He should have been traded, if possible, for a No. 1 or No. 2 defenceman and maybe a couple of top draft picks. Now with a no-trade clause, you're done. We have forwards that can score, we need defence badly. Vegas and Colorado both showed you can win with Grade B goalies if you have Grade A defence. Last but not least, Keefe needs to go; he's lost the room regardless of his record, and with him, Shanahan, too.
Orest Muz Wasaga Beach
(With so much talent with such high salaries, you do have to wonder why the Leafs can't win)

D. Lammi Oakville
(The Liberals have made such a mess of so many issues, it's going to be impossible to clean it up)
CHARGE UP
With all the talk about electric vehicles, I wonder how people living in single-family residences that have no driveway but have on-street parking permits would charge their vehicles.
Jerry Hyland Newmarket
(With great difficulty)

Document 329 of 356

Notley exit will end banner days for Alberta NDP

Author: Gunter, Lorne

Publication info: Gunter, Lorne. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/notley-exit-will-end-banner-days-alberta-ndp/docview/2915819961/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Notley+exit+will+end+banner+days+for+Alberta+NDP&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=Gunter%2C+Lorne&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Now that Rachel Notley has announced her resignation as leader of the Alberta NDP, expect her party to return to its traditional distant-second status.
Not as distant as it used to be, but not within sniffing distance of government again, either.
After 10 years as leader - four of them as premier - Notley leaves her party in better shape than she found it. In the nine elections before Notley took over the provincial NDP, the party had six leaders and won an average of just five seats per campaign.
In the three elections under her leadership, the Alberta NDP won an average of 39 seats.
During some fiscal quarters, they have even out-fundraised the United Conservative Party (UCP), an achievement that never happened before Notley.
I recall during the 2015 election (the one the NDP won) an organizer telling me the party had little idea where to focus its resources because it couldn't afford the kind of detailed, internal polling the UCP and its predecessor, the Progressive Conservatives, did routinely.
Those days of by-guess-and-bygolly campaigning are gone forever for the NDP, thanks to the way Notley succeeded in getting anti-UCP voters to support her party as the sole credible alternative on the left. (The Alberta Liberals, for instance, finished eighth in the May 2023 election with just 0.2% of the vote).
Still, five years from now the Notley days are likely to be seen as the glory days for the Alberta NDP. She is their biggest asset. Indeed, outside Edmonton, Notley is the party's only asset. Without her, I predict it will be impossible for the NDP to recapture government.
There are only two ways the NDP could win a general election.
They could face a divided right split between two equally credible conservative parties. That's how they won in 2015 when the Progressive Conservatives and the Wildrose carved up the right-of-centre vote and allowed the New Democrats to waltz up the middle.
Or they could win solid majorities of seats in Edmonton and Calgary.
The first scenario seems unlikely.
The right should remain united through the next election in 2027.
That leaves the NDP's only path to power, with or without Notley, a double win in Edmonton and Calgary.
While a remote-controlled traffic cone operating under the NDP banner could retain nearly every Edmonton seat for the NDP (the massive civil service and public-sector vote in the city gives the NDP an almost insurmountable head start), Calgary remains a largely small-c, pro-business city.
The NDP came close to winning enough Calgary seats to pull off an overall win last spring, but that had as much to do with Calgarians'uncertainty about then-new UCP Leader Danielle Smith as it did with Notley's popularity.
When Notley made a rash promise at mid-campaign to gouge businesses through higher taxes, enough Calgarians swallowed their reservations about Smith to keep the UCP comfortably in power.
Now add in two factors: By 2027, Smith will have been premier for nearly five years. Unless she goes suddenly extremist, most voters will have accepted Smith is qualified. At lot of the 80,000 to 100,000 UCP voters who stayed home during the last vote will return and swamp the NDP, especially in Calgary.
And the second factor to consider is that no one the NDP is likely to select to replace Notley will have her profile, her skills or her personality.
The default vote in Calgary and the rest of the province outside Edmonton remains UCP. It takes a special opposition leader (i.e. Rachel Notley) and special circumstances (a divided right) to overcome that default.
The NDP are unlikely to go back to five or fewer seats per election, but without Notley they are just as unlikely to win power. lgunter@postmedia.com @sunlornegunter

Subject: Leadership; Voters; Elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: Canada; Calgary Alberta Canada

People: Notley, Rachel
