Document 330 of 356

No 'blood money'; Paramount Foods CEO in hot seat after X post

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-blood-money-paramount-foods-ceo-hot-seat-after/docview/2915819875/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+%27blood+money%27%3B+Paramount+Foods+CEO+in+hot+seat+after+X+post&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Paramount Foods was in damage control following reaction Monday to a now-deleted tweet from founder Mohamad Fakih, suggesting he doesn't "need" money from someone who "supports baby killers."
The member of the Order of Canada and CEO of Paramount, which operates dozens of restaurants and has an arena in Mississauga named after the company, made the comments on the social media platform X. He was responding to a poster named Eugene who wrote, "I'll never set foot in Paramount Foods ever again" and "terrorist sympathizers don't deserve my business."
A respected philanthropist and fundraiser, Fakih - who has appeared in photographs with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie - responded "we don't want your money. Please don't bring us blood money."
In an earlier X post, since deleted, he said: "we don't need your money" since "our clients are respectful families from all backgrounds ... we welcome everyone, but not the one that supports baby killers and government that (is) starving innocent civilians."
From there, Fakih said, people on X posted his picture with the tweet and created a false anti-Semitic narrative. Whatever his intention, the reaction was swift.
"With his social-media post, this Order of Canada recipient reminded many Canadians of the days when Jews were not welcome as patrons in various establishments throughout the country," said Richard Robertson, B'nai Brith Canada's manager of research. "The hurt caused by his message was amplified by his usage of the phrase 'blood money,'a term reminiscent of the classic anti-Semitic trope of blood libel."
Robertson added: "it is difficult to fathom that such a highly regarded Canadian could make such a deeply offensive comment."
While B'nai Brith did not call for Fakih's Order of Canada to be revoked, others have. In an X post, PC MPP Lisa MacLeod said Fakih "must be either removed ... or resign."
When contacted for comment, Fakih - who this week has been leading a team providing hot meals to those on the streets - countered he was "shocked" by the harsh reaction, saying his posts were "misconstrued" and never intended to be taken as many did.
Fakih, who rose from humble beginnings to enjoy great success after coming to Canada from Lebanon in his 20s, told the Toronto Sun that "for the last 100 days," he never "said anything against the Jewish community" and that he "just wants things to calm down and be fair."
He said his staff received hateful comments that scared them, and people have been giving Paramount's restaurants scathing reviews on social media.
Fakih claimed he also got "death" threats. He said he was very clear in his posts that "everyone is welcome" and the "spin" presented by those reacting to the tweets was "inaccurate."
While Fakih was uncomfortable with the negative social media attention, he was part of the public campaign to see hockey legend Don Cherry lose his spot on Hockey Night in Canada after Grapes made his "you people" comment.
On what was then called Twitter, Fakih tweeted "instead of insulting those of us who have chosen Canada as the place we want to build our lives, perhaps Don Cherry should consider matching my commitment."
Now it's Fakih who faces public scrutiny.
While he insisted it's clear his posts were not anti-Semitic, Fakih acknowledged the damage was done. Others online contended he did the damage to himself.
Fakih said his comments were directed toward one person and not intended to go beyond that. Some accept that explanation, and some do not.
That said, Fakih has been staunchly critical of Israel in the war against Hamas. On Jan. 11, he responded to an X post by commentator Avi Lewis, who was arguing that Israel had a responsibly to defend itself from terror.
Fakih's responded, "a legal absurdity to call this genocide? It's a moral absurdity to call it anything else."
When challenged that he does not have a robust track record of publicly denouncing Hamas, whose militants slaughtered 1,200 Jews and kidnapped more than 200 people on Oct. 7, he acknowledged they are considered a terrorist group and he does not support them.
Fakih recently posted surprising criticism of the Trudeau government, which he routinely supports. Trudeau even posted a video to congratulate Paramount on its 10th anniversary.
But Fakih spoke out against the Trudeau government's decision not to support the South African charges before the International Court of Justice in The Hague, alleging Israel had committed war crimes in its execution of the war in Gaza.
"I am a Liberal. This crew has lost its way," Fakih posted on X. jwarmington@postmedia.com @joe_warmington

Subject: Antisemitism; Jewish people; Social networks

Document 331 of 356

GUNTER: Notley departure will end banner days for Alberta NDP

Author: Ling, Hui

Publication info: Ling, Hui. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/gunter-notley-departure-will-end-banner-days/docview/2915705905/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=GUNTER%3A+Notley+departure+will+end+banner+days+for+Alberta+NDP&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Ling%2C+Hui&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Now that Rachel Notley has announced her resignation as leader of the Alberta NDP, expect her party to return to its traditional distant-second status.
Not as distant as it used to be, but not within sniffing distance of government again, either.
After 10 years as leader - four of them as premier - Notley leaves her party in better shape than she found it. In the nine elections before Notley took over the provincial NDP, the party had six leaders and won an average of just five seats per campaign.
In the three elections under her leadership, the Alberta NDP won an average of 39 seats.
During some fiscal quarters, they have even out-fundraised the United Conservative Party (UCP), an achievement that never happened before Notley.
I recall during the 2015 election (the one the NDP won) an organizer telling me the party had little idea where to focus its resources because it couldn't afford the kind of detailed, internal polling the UCP and its predecessor, the Progressive Conservatives, did routinely.
Those days of by-guess-and-by-golly campaigning are gone forever for the NDP, thanks to the way Notley succeeded in getting anti-UCP voters to support her party as the sole credible alternative on the left. (The Alberta Liberals, for instance, finished eighth in the May 2023 election with just 0.2% of the vote).
Still, five years from now the Notley days are likely to be seen as the glory days for the Alberta NDP. She is their biggest asset. Indeed, outside Edmonton, Notley is the party's only asset. Without her, I predict it will be impossible for the NDP to recapture government.
There are only two ways the NDP could win a general election.
They could face a divided right split between two equally credible conservative parties. That's how they won in 2015 when the Progressive Conservatives and the Wildrose carved up the right-of-centre vote and allowed the New Democrats to waltz up the middle.
Or they could win solid majorities of seats in Edmonton and Calgary.
The first scenario seems unlikely. The right should remain united through the next election in 2027.
That leaves the NDP's only path to power, with or without Notley, a double win in Edmonton and Calgary.
While a remote-controlled traffic cone operating under the NDP banner could retain nearly every Edmonton seat for the NDP (the massive civil service and public-sector vote in the city gives the NDP an almost insurmountable head start), Calgary remains a largely small-c, pro-business city.
The NDP came close to winning enough Calgary seats to pull off an overall win last spring, but that had as much to do with Calgarians' uncertainty about then-new UCP Leader Danielle Smith as it did with Notley's popularity.
When Notley made a rash promise at mid-campaign to gouge businesses through higher taxes, enough Calgarians swallowed their reservations about Smith to keep the UCP comfortably in power.
Now add in two factors: By 2027, Smith will have been premier for nearly five years. Unless she goes suddenly extremist, most voters will have accepted Smith is qualified. At lot of the 80,000 to 100,000 UCP voters who stayed home during the last vote will return and swamp the NDP, especially in Calgary.
And the second factor to consider is that no one the NDP is likely to select to replace Notley will have her profile, her skills or her personality.
The default vote in Calgary and the rest of the province outside Edmonton remains UCP. It takes a special opposition leader (i.e. Rachel Notley) and special circumstances (a divided right) to overcome that default.
The NDP are unlikely to go back to five or fewer seats per election, but without Notley they are just as unlikely to win power.

Subject: Leadership; Voters; Elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: Canada; Calgary Alberta Canada

People: Notley, Rachel

WARMINGTON: Paramount Foods CEO Mohamad Fakih in hot seat after X post

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/warmington-paramount-foods-ceo-mohamad-fakih-hot/docview/2915683116/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=WARMINGTON%3A+Paramount+Foods+CEO+Mohamad+Fakih+in+hot+seat+after+X+post&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Paramount Foods was in damage control following reaction Monday to a now-deleted tweet from founder Mohamad Fakih, suggesting he doesn't "need" money from someone who "supports baby killers."
The member of the Order of Canada and CEO of Paramount, which operates dozens of restaurants and has an arena in Mississauga named after the company, made the controversial comments in X posts. He was responding to a poster named Eugene who wrote, "I'll never set foot in Paramount Foods ever again" and "terrorist sympathizers don't deserve my business."
A well-respected philanthropist and fundraiser, Fakih - who has appeared in photographs with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie - responded "we don't want your money. Please don't bring us blood money."
These are the tweets in question. ?@mohamadfakih8? feels they have been "misconstrued" and manipulated 2b reported as something he did not say or mean. He said he was speaking about not wanting this one person's money and not ever was it intended to be about Jewish people.
- Joe Warmington (@joe_warmington) January 16, 2024
In an earlier X posting - now deleted - he said "we don't need your money" since "our clients are respectful families from all backgrounds . . . we welcome everyone, but not the one that supports baby killers and government that (is) starving innocent civilians."
From there, Fakih said, people on X posted his picture with the tweet and created a false anti-Semitic narrative. Whatever his intention, the reaction has been swift.
"With his social-media post, this Order of Canada recipient reminded many Canadians of the days when Jews were not welcome as patrons in various establishments throughout the country," said Richard Robertson, B'nai Brith Canada's manager of research. "The hurt caused by his message was amplified by his usage of the phrase 'blood money,' a term reminiscent of the classic anti-Semitic trope of 'blood libel.'"
BREAKING - Just spoke to @mohamadfakih8 on the phone and he said he's upset at the way his tweet has been depicted. Suggestions that he was saying Jews were not wanted in his restaurants are "not accurate" and were "misconstrued" by people who are trying to "spin" a narrative. He
- Joe Warmington (@joe_warmington) January 16, 2024
Robertson added, "it is difficult to fathom that such a highly regarded Canadian could make such a deeply offensive comment. Anti-Semitism from anyone, let alone public figures, should never be tolerated."
While B'nai Brith did not call for Fakih's Order of Canada to be revoked, others have.
Recommended video
In an X posting, PC MPP Lisa MacLeod said "Order of Canada recipients must be beyond reproach in upholding shared Canadian values and do so as a responsibility and a privilege" and "Mohamad Fakih must be either removed from the Order or resign."
In "Lament for a Nation" George Grant wrote "Canada was once a nation with meaning and purpose". Nearly 60 years after one of Canada's seminal political philosophy books was a best seller we are now confronted with another lament in the form of a values conflict. Thus, Order of...
- Lisa MacLeod (@MacLeodLisa) January 16, 2024
When contacted for comment, Fakih - who this week has been leading a team providing hot meals to those on the streets - countered he was "shocked" by the harsh reaction, saying his posts were "misconstrued" and never intended to be taken as many did.
Fakih, who rose from humble beginnings to enjoy great success after coming to Canada from Lebanon in his 20s, told The Toronto Sun that "for the last 100 days," he never "said anything against the Jewish community" and that he "just wants things to calm down and be fair."
- Joe Warmington (@joe_warmington) January 16, 2024
He said his staff received hateful comments that scared them, and people have been giving Paramount's restaurants scathing reviews on social media.
Fakih claimed he also received "death" threats. He said he was very clear in his posts that "everyone is welcome" and the "spin" presented by those reacting to the tweets was "inaccurate."
An amazing week! Starting with opening of a new location in Vancouver & ending by spending an evening with our PM @JustinTrudeau
- Mohamad Fakih, C.M. (@mohamadfakih8) July 22, 2017
While Fakih was not feeling comfortable with the negative social media attention, he was part of the public campaign to see Hockey legend Don Cherry lose his spot on Hockey Night in Canada after Grapes made his "you people" comment.
On what was then called Twitter, Fakih tweeted "instead of insulting those of us who have chosen Canada as the place we want to build our lives, perhaps Don Cherry should consider matching my commitment."
Instead of insulting those of us who have chosen Canada as the place we want to build our lives, perhaps Don Cherry @CoachsCornerDC should consider matching my commitment. That is the sort of action that honours the valour and the memory of our veterans. @hockeynight
- Mohamad Fakih, C.M. (@mohamadfakih8) November 10, 2019
Now it's Fakih who is facing public scrutiny.
While he insisted it's clear his posts were not anti-Semitic, Fakih acknowledged that the damage was done. Others, online, contended he did the damage to himself.
While Fakih, who has made a video in support of The Canadian Muslim Vote organization that is currently paying for a group of MPs to visit Jordan and the West Bank to meet with Palestinian refugees, has clarified that "everyone" is welcome in his restaurants.
Fakih said his comments were directed toward one person and not intended to go beyond that.
Mohamad Fakih stands as a shining example of what can be achieved if you work hard, put your community first and stand up for injustice & discrimination. Today, I join #Mississauga in congratulating @mohamadfakih8 for being recognized with the #OrderofCanada, ????'s highest honour.
- Bonnie Crombie ???? (@BonnieCrombie) December 29, 2021
Some are accepting of that explanation, and some are not.
That said, Fakih has been staunchly critical of Israel in the war against Hamas. On Jan. 11, he responded to an X post by commentator Avi Lewis, who was arguing that Israel had a responsibly to defend itself from terror.
- Joe Warmington (@joe_warmington) January 16, 2024
Fakih's responded, "a legal absurdity to call this genocide? It's a moral absurdity to call it anything else."
When challenged that he does not have a robust track record of publicly denouncing Hamas, whose militants slaughtered 1,200 Jews and kidnapped more than 200 people on Oct. 7, he acknowledged they are considered a terrorist group and he does not support them.
I am a Liberal. This crew has lost its way.#cdnpoli
- Mohamad Fakih, C.M. (@mohamadfakih8) January 13, 2024
Fakih recently posted surprising criticism of the Trudeau government he routinely supports. Trudeau has even posted a video in the past congratulating Paramount on its 10th anniversary.
But Fakih spoke out against the Trudeau government's decision not to support the South African charges before the International Court of Justice in The Hague, alleging Israel had committed war crimes in its execution of the war in Gaza.
What would your advice be to Israel in light of the
Murderous slaughter against them on Oct. 7th, the kidnappings and the thousands of rockets fired at their innocent people?
- Joe Warmington (@joe_warmington) December 31, 2023
"I am a Liberal. This crew has lost its way," Fakih posted on X.
But it was the posting, in which he highlighted "blood money" that some feeling it was suggesting exclusion. That seems to have riled up his critics the most.

Document 333 of 356

No, you're not getting all your money back

Author: Agar, Jerry

Publication info: Agar, Jerry. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-youre-not-getting-all-your-money-back/docview/2915440759/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No%2C+you%27re+not+getting+all+your+money+back&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=Agar%2C+Jerry&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Did you get money from the federal government this week? I got $183 to compensate me for the money I spent due to the carbon tax.
All in all, not a good bargain. Sure, I'd rather have the money than not. I am not giving it back. But better still would be if we didn't have the carbon tax in the first place.
All the arguments for it fail the logic test.
The stated purpose is to incent us to spend less on carbon fuels by charging more for them. That's solid economics as far as it goes. Generally as price goes up, demand goes down.
But they promised to give us the money back. Why should I buy less gasoline if the price is neutral or negligible? As such it was a failed proposition from the get-go.
Of course most people won't get all their money back. That is a lie.
The Parliamentary Budget Office, calculating the negative effect the tax has on the economy says, "Most households in Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba and Ontario will see a net loss resulting from federal carbon pricing. That is, the costs they face including the federal carbon levy, higher GST and lower incomes will exceed the Climate Action Incentive rebate they receive."
By implanting and steadily increasing the tax, the government is punishing Canadians for something we are not responsible for.
50% of the carbon emitted on the planet is from three nations combined; China, India and the USA.
Canadians account for less than two percent.
Fanatics will jump up to say, "But we are among the highest emitters per capita."
That is irrelevant. It is estimated that if we emitted nothing we would account for a drop of one quarter of one percent in worldwide emissions.
To put that in perspective let's imagine 400 cows in a closed building.
With all the methane pouring out of cows, it would get pretty close in there.
So, let's remove a cow. Anyone going to notice a difference if the remaining 99 keep on blowing? Of course not. We are the one cow, not the 99.
Ontario closed five coal burning plants to serve a green agenda. China builds two a week, so again we made no difference.
Another reason people give for adopting green policies and taxes whether a small country like ours makes a difference in emissions is that they say we have to be an example to the world.
Really? Name a country that cares what we do.
Does anyone believe a meeting ever took place in China where someone said, "Canada is closing coal plants and taxing their citizens. We better slow it down and go green, or we'll look bad to Canadian liberals."
People will call my radio show and say, "We have to do something."
No we don't. Just doing something just to be doing something is juvenile at best and really, just dumb.
To solve any problem it is necessary to do something effective, but as pointed out, there is nothing effective about the carbon tax.
The only winners are federal bureaucrats cozily employed to administer the program and send us money to try to hoodwink us into believing we are getting a good deal.
Federal employees have great benefits, none of which accrues to the rest of us, or the planet.
Agar is the 9 a.m. to noon host on NEWSTALK1010 jerry.agar@sunmedia.ca

Subject: Federal employees; Carbon; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax

Location: China

Identifier / keyword: money; federal; government; compensate; spent; carbon

Title: No, you're not getting all your money back

Publication title: The Toronto Sun; Toronto

First page: A.11

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 16, 2024

Section: Comment

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Toronto

Document 335 of 356

A new gov't staple; Expanding ServiceOntario into retail hardly cause for 'serious concerns'

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-govt-staple-expanding-serviceontario-into/docview/2914986231/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+new+gov%27t+staple%3B+Expanding+ServiceOntario+into+retail+hardly+cause+for+%27serious+concerns%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Progressives at Queen's Park sure don't seem to like progress. This week, the so-called progressive parties at Queen's Park are protesting that the Doug Ford government wants to move some ServiceOntario locations into Staples stores in southern Ontario.
The move was announced in December by a government news release, but became something for the opposition parties to start complaining about this week after a CityNews story.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles is worried that the government is working with a "U.S.-owned corporation like Staples" to deliver services. Liberal MP Adil Shamji is worried about the "corporatizing" of government services.
"This raises serious concerns about Ontarians'access to government services," NDP critic Catherine Fife said.
Really? This move to work with Staples includes about 11 locations across the province. There are close to 300 ServiceOntario locations and almost three-quarters of them are operated through private delivery.
Shamji, who ran for the Ontario Liberal leadership before endorsing Bonnie Crombie, should know this as it was his party that greatly expanded the private sector's involvement during the Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne years.
"In what world does it make sense to go to an office supply chain to renew your driver's licence or health card?" Green party Leader Mike Schreiner asked.
Maybe about as much sense as it makes to renew your driver's licence and health card at a Canadian Tire store. The last time I had to renew both of those documents, I went to the Canadian Tire on Yonge St. just north of Bloor St. in Toronto, one of the closest locations to Queen's Park, and the service was just fine.
Convenience, ease of access The people complaining about this really don't seem to know what they are talking about when it comes to service delivery. They also seem like the kind of people who would like to go back to the good old days of having to search out dingy government offices with horrible hours to access services.
Would anyone really like to go back to the days when, to access Canada Post, you had to find an official post office location and get there before 5 p.m.? Or do you enjoy the convenience of going to your local Shoppers to get stamps, mail a package or pick up that delivery you missed while you were at work? The devil will be in the details and the execution, but having visited Staples of late this is a service that will blend in perfectly with their new format.
The company is moving away from just being a place to buy office paper at a price cheaper than Dunder Mifflin and branching out into other service areas. The politicians complaining at Queen's Park would be wise to walk down University Ave. to the local Staples and see the co-working space, the coffee shop and the shipping kiosk serving FedEx, Purolator and more before dismissing the idea of a ServiceOntario kiosk.
What we should be looking for are more innovative ways to deliver services in ways that are convenient to residents. In November, the province announced a hub with the Township of Pickle Lake to deliver municipal and provincial services at the same location, similar to Ottawa City Hall, where you can access services from all three levels of government at one convenient place.
While this is being described as a "sole-sourced contract" - rarely a good move for any government - the truth is the province is looking to work with other partners as well and Staples was the first to strike a deal. Look for other changes in service delivery, including a greater ability to access services online, to come in the future.
While opposition politicians will grumble, most of us will just be thankful for the expanded access. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Political leadership

Company / organization: Name: ServiceOntario; NAICS: 926120

Identifier / keyword: progressives; progress; progressive; parties; protesting; government

Title: A new gov't staple; Expanding ServiceOntario into retail hardly cause for 'serious concerns'

Publication title: The Toronto Sun; Toronto

First page: A.7

Document 336 of 356

LILLEY: Expanding ServiceOntario into retail locations good for customers

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-expanding-serviceontario-into-retail/docview/2914891633/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Expanding+ServiceOntario+into+retail+locations+good+for+customers&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Progressives at Queen's Park sure don't seem to like progress. This week, the so-called progressive parties at Queen's Park are protesting that the Doug Ford government wants to move some ServiceOntario locations into Staples stores in southern Ontario.
The move was announced in December by a government news release, but became something for the opposition parties to start complaining about this week after a CityNews story.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles is worried that the government is working with a "U.S.-owned corporation like Staples" to deliver services. Liberal MP Adil Shamji is worried about the "corporatizing" of government services.
"This raises serious concerns about Ontarians' access to government services," NDP critic Catherine Fife said
Really?
Province to close unspecified number of ServiceOntario locations: Report
ServiceOntario expanding online options, soon to include marriage licences
This move to work with Staples includes about 11 locations across the province. There are close to 300 ServiceOntario locations and almost three-quarters of them are operated through private delivery.
Shamji, who ran for the Ontario Liberal leadership before endorsing Bonnie Crombie, should know this as it was his party that greatly expanded the private sector's involvement during the Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne years.
"In what world does it make sense to go to an office supply chain to renew your driver's licence or health card?" Green Party leader Mike Schreiner asked
Maybe about as much sense as it makes to renew your driver's licence and health card at a Canadian Tire store. The last time I had to renew both of those documents, I went to the Canadian Tire on Yonge St. just north of Bloor St. in Toronto, one of the closest locations to Queen's Park, and the service was just fine.
MOVE ABOUT CONVENIENCE, EASE OF ACCESS
The people complaining about this really don't seem to know what they are talking about when it comes to service delivery. They also seem like the kind of people who would like to go back to the good old days of having to search out dingy government offices with horrible hours to access services.
Would anyone really like to go back to the days when, to access Canada Post, you had to find an official post office location and get there before 5 p.m? Or do you enjoy the convenience of going to your local Shoppers to get stamps, mail a package or pick up that delivery that you missed while you were at work?
The devil will be in the details and the execution, but having visited Staples of late this is a service that will blend in perfectly with their new format.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
The company is moving away from just being a place to buy office paper at a price cheaper than Dunder Mifflin and branching out into other service areas. The politicians complaining at Queen's Park would be wise to walk down University Ave. to the local Staples and see the co-working space, the coffee shop and the shipping kiosk serving FedEx, Purolator and more before dismissing the idea of a ServiceOntario kiosk.
What we should be looking for are more innovative ways to deliver services in ways that are convenient to residents. In November, the province announced a hub with the Township of Pickle Lake to deliver municipal and provincial services at the same location, similar to Ottawa City Hall, where you can access services from all three levels of government at one convenient location.
While this is being described as a "sole-sourced contract" - rarely a good move for any government - the truth is the province is looking to work with other partners as well and Staples was the first to strike a deal. Look for other changes in service delivery, including a greater ability to access services online, to come in the future.
While opposition politicians will grumble, most of us will just be thankful for the expanded access.

Subject: Political leadership

Company / organization: Name: ServiceOntario; NAICS: 926120

Identifier / keyword: progressives; progress; progressive; parties; protesting; government

Document 337 of 356

They Had Their Chance; Three arrested as pro-Hamas protesters make a mockery of police restraint

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/they-had-their-chance-three-arrested-as-pro-hamas/docview/2914344063/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=They+Had+Their+Chance%3B+Three+arrested+as+pro-Hamas+protesters+make+a+mockery+of+police+restraint&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pro-Hamas protesters keep pushing their luck ... and pretty soon their luck is going to run out.
Certainly, the city's patience has. And now Toronto Police have started to push back. But they need the full support of Mayor Olivia Chow, Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
Cops also need to know that the citizens of Toronto have their backs, as well. And that support has to be clear and concise.
Neither the Avenue Rd. bridge over Hwy. 401, nor any other part of Toronto, belongs to these protesters. So if the anti-Israel bullies won't take their protest elsewhere, then they need to be taken to jail.
They are behaving like thugs, despite Toronto Police being nothing but patient with them.
Even with Chief Myron Demkiw's respectful directive that the overpass was now no longer a tolerated zone for protests, these disrespectful jerks defied the law and went out there, anyway.
One of the protesters seen in a video posted on X by Caryma Sa'd appears to be Skigh Johnson, a 25-year-old woman who was arrested by the Hate Crimes Unit in November of 2023 and charged with "mischief interfere with the enjoyment of property" for allegedly vandalizing a Starbucks in Forest Hill.
And police were still gentle with them compared to other incidents we have seen in the past. The officers braving the cold on the bridge Saturday afternoon, trying valiantly to reason with demonstrators, each deserving a commendation. They did everything possible to de-escalate the situation.
They were doing their jobs. Cops politely stood their ground against this defiant group of protesters, calmly warning them over and over to get off of the overpass - repeatedly explaining they didn't want to have to arrest anyone.
But the protesters refused to move and three of them were ultimately arrested - one charged with mischief, and the other two for obstructing police.
If this defiance keeps up, maybe Trudeau will have to do what he did to deal with the Freedom Convoy truckers and invoke the Emergencies Act? That would give police the power to arrest anybody who goes on the overpass, no questions asked.
If the current laws are not working, nor being respected, more weight may be needed to get this crisis under control.
And if pro-Hamas supporters return to the bridge on Sunday, then opposition politicians must call out the Liberal government's hypocrisy on how it went full tilt on non-violent truckers but soft on people who are supporting the slaughter of 1,200 Jews by Hamas terrorists on Oct. 7.
This can't be allowed to continue as is. The city cannot be run by terrorist supporters.
Police need backup and they need the tools to deal with an insurrectionist force that doesn't respect law and order.
The last thing police want is to see a riot on or near the bridge, but they also cannot let people get the better of the Chief.
Strong and swift action is going to have to come. A message has been sent, and no one on that side is listening. They don't seem to care. They want a showdown.
They want trouble and they are playing a game out there - talking back to police, challenging authority, even pretending to fall down.
It's all theatre. As Warren Kinsella has reported, many are nothing put paid operatives. They all should end up in court.
For now, they are trying to establish that they control the city, and they can do anything they want, anywhere they want.
Police brought out the horses to remove a guy from his own restaurant during the pandemic, so they should certainly be able to bring out the Mounted Unit to remove illegal protesters from a major bridge.
The overpass is a no-go zone for protesting and anybody who defies that is nothing but a law-breaker.
If politically correct Canada allows these disrupters to win, then Toronto - and Canada - will be lost.
The time to end the nonsense has long passed. Now is the time to show the pro-Hamas supporters who is in control of the city. jwarmington@postmedia.com @joe_warmington

Subject: Arrests; Demonstrations & protests

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Identifier / keyword: protesters; pushing; pretty; going; certainly; patience

Document 340 of 356

When new homes are needed most, rate of housing starts slips

Author: Lyall, Richard

Publication info: Lyall, Richard. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/when-new-homes-are-needed-most-rate-housing/docview/2913815950/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=When+new+homes+are+needed+most%2C+rate+of+housing+starts+slips&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=S.6&amp;au=Lyall%2C+Richard&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the final installment of the
Back to the Future trilogy, Doc Emmett Brown professed in a climactic speech to Marty McFly that, "Your future is whatever you make it, so make it a good one."
I am hopeful that we can heed the advice of the eccentric thespian due to the fact that we need to take more spirited and concerted action this year to tackle the housing supply and affordability crisis.
It will be an uphill battle, mind you. The metrics are not good. Despite substantial efforts undertaken by various levels of government, latest reports indicate the housing situation is not improving.
According to a report by RBC, only 26 per cent of Canadians can afford a single-family home right now - down from 40 per cent just a year ago. About 45 per cent of households can now afford to own a condo apartment based on their income, compared to close to 60 per cent just four years ago.
Home prices have risen due to a variety of factors, including soaring interest rates, the increasing cost of materials and labour, hefty taxes, fees, levies and development charges and slow approvals.
In Toronto, for example it takes 84 per cent of a median household income to cover housing costs.
A report by CMHC, meanwhile, notes that the annual pace of housing starts in Canada fell 22 per cent in November from a year earlier This is disturbing because it comes at a time when we need new housing the most. The rate of housing starts in Toronto and Vancouver were both down 39 per cent.
Many developers have delayed or cancelled the launch of new condo projects because of the tumult of 2023. New condo sales in the GTA were on track for around 13,000 to 14,000 sales last year.
According to Royal LePage, the aggregate price of a home in Canada will reach $843,684 in the fourth quarter of 2024, a 5.5-per-cent annual increase. At the same time, the median price of a single-family home will rise six per cent to $879,164, while the price of a condo will jump five per cent to $616,140.
This is all occurring at a time when immigration is at historic levels. Canada's population grew by more than 430,000 in the third quarter of 2023 - the fastest pace of growth in any quarter since 1957.
The situation is critical and to a degree a classic Catch 22. We need housing to support immigration. And we need immigration to support the economy and replace our aging skilled trades.
Looking into the crystal ball, builders are pessimistic about current and future selling conditions, a report by the Canadian Home Builders'Association reveals. About 40 per cent of builders in a panel said their sales traffic was down in the third quarter of 2023, and not showing any sign of recovery.
At RESCON, we have been talking about housing supply issues for many years. We have raised alarm bells about the excessive taxes, fees, levies and exorbitant development charges.
Now the chickens have come home to roost. The housing industry is not something where you can sort of flip a switch and fire it back up again. Projects that are proposed or started today could take years to finish.
Governments play a major role in righting the ship. The good news in all of this is that leaders at all three levels of government appear to be coming together to find solutions. It's a daunting task as the federal government is Liberal, the provincial is Conservative, and we have an NDP mayor in Toronto.
They need to better co-ordinate and streamline the development approvals system, as well as lower taxes, fees, levies and development charges. The feds also need to reinvest the substantial tax revenue that they receive from new home sales into infrastructure that will support more housing.
Builders should not be paying for the cost of community infrastructure. It should be shared amongst the entire tax base. New homes should also be exempt from federal and provincial sales taxes.
The crisis needs immediate, ongoing, urgent, and co-ordinated attention. The supply numbers tell the story. We must get on top of this. The residential construction industry is there to help in any way we can.
Richard Lyall is president of the Residential Construction Council of Ontario (RESCON). He has represented the building industry in Ontario since 1991. Contact him at media@rescon.com.

Document 341 of 356

Trudeau government's promises of deficit reduction ring hollow

Author: Eisen, Ben; Fuss, Jake

Publication info: Eisen, Ben; Fuss, Jake. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-governments-promises-deficit-reduction/docview/2913812189/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+government%27s+promises+of+deficit+reduction+ring+hollow&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=Eisen%2C+Ben%3BFuss%2C+Jake&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As budget season approaches, it's worth remembering that policymakers must pair promises of fiscal discipline with concrete action.
According to projections, the Trudeau government will run a $40-billion deficit this fiscal year, which is slightly larger than last year's $35.3-billion deficit, even though revenues are up almost $10 billion.
Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland has repeatedly said the era of large deficits won't last forever and promised that starting in 2026-2027 the federal deficit will be held every year to less than 1% of Gross Domestic Product (the value of all goods and services produced in the economy).
But Freeland's promises should be taken with more than a grain of salt because all governments have spotty track records when it comes to fiscal promises, particularly when they're based on spending restraint in the future. Conversely, the most credible promises are introduced with immediate action.
The most famous example in Canada's recent fiscal history came in the 1990s when Paul Martin, then the Liberal government's finance minister, vowed to slay Canada's crippling budget deficit and repair federal finances "come hell or high water." Martin's words were accompanied by immediate action in the form of the transformative 1995 federal budget that began a process of deep budget cuts and major policy reforms that immediately put the government on a path to fiscal balance. The deficit was quickly eliminated and Canada was put on a sound fiscal footing that served the country well for almost two decades.
The "hell or high water" episode shows us how governments must match strong commitments with strong actions to increase the likelihood of success. On the other hand, promises for fiscal improvements in the future - unaccompanied by any concurrent action - usually aren't worth the paper (or tweets) they're printed on.
The Trudeau government has produced many examples of incredible promises. First, on the campaign trail back in 2015, Trudeau and his team promised small and temporary deficits and to shrink the national debt burden. Since then, the prime minister and his finance ministers have never been shy about promising to improve the bottom line. However, the target dates associated with these promises came and went without much notice and the government pivoted to new plausible-sounding targets, using only words without policy change.
Again, the Trudeau government is far from alone in this tendency. In Ontario, former premier Kathleen Wynne's government repeatedly promised to shrink that province's daunting debt burden, yet failed to decisively act. Basically, it was wishful thinking.
After Ontarians voted out the Wynne government, the Conservative Doug Ford government promised to break from the Wynne approach and introduce real reforms that would slay the deficit quickly. But after a few half-hearted feints in the direction of fiscal discipline, the Ford government also failed to make good on its promises of debt reduction and as a result, Ontario remains stuck under a mountain of government debt.
Canadian history is clear - unless government promises coincide with concrete actions to immediately start shrinking budget deficits, these promises of fiscal restraint at some future date shouldn't be given much credence. Freeland's recent promises fall into this category. In the upcoming federal budget, if the Trudeau government and its cabinet want its promises to be credible, it must learn from its Liberal predecessors and the "hell or high water" moment and reduce spending to immediately begin a process of deficit reduction.
Ben Eisen and Jake Fuss are analysts at the Fraser Institute

Subject: National debt; Decades; Debt restructuring; Budget deficits

Business indexing term: Subject: National debt Debt restructuring Budget deficits

Location: Canada

EISEN AND FUSS: Trudeau government's promises of deficit reduction ring hollow

RESCON: Just as new homes are needed most, the rate of housing starts drops

Author: Slofstra, Martin

Publication info: Slofstra, Martin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/rescon-just-as-new-homes-are-needed-most-rate/docview/2913669326/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=RESCON%3A+Just+as+new+homes+are+needed+most%2C+the+rate+of+housing+starts+drops&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Slofstra%2C+Martin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 344 of 356

Trudeau's support evaporating among key demographic groups

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeaus-support-evaporating-among-key/docview/2913608010/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau%27s+support+evaporating+among+key+demographic+groups&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You have to wonder how low Justin Trudeau's support would be if it weren't for seniors. Right now, that's the best performing demographic for voter support that Trudeau's Liberals can count on and he's not even winning them.
The latest poll from Abacus Data shows Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives with a 17-point lead over Trudeau's Liberals. The Conservatives lead among every demographic group and in every region except Quebec, while the Liberals don't have a lead anywhere to be found.
Nationally, the Conservatives have the support of 41% of voters, the Liberals are in second with 24% support and the NDP in third at 18%.
It's the regional and demographic numbers that tell the real story.
In Atlantic Canada, 41% of voters in this former Liberal stronghold now say they will vote Team Blue with just 31% saying they still back the Team Red. The Red Wall has not only crumbled in Atlantic Canada - in Ontario, where the Liberals currently hold 78 seats, the Conservatives lead 41% to 29%.
In those two regions alone there would be a massive change in seat counts if an election were held today.
It doesn't really matter that the Conservatives have 57% support across Saskatchewan because they already hold every seat but the four Liberals in Manitoba and two in Alberta need to be worried about their future as Tory numbers rise.
In British Columbia, the Conservatives are taking 43% support to 19% for the Liberals and 26% for the NDP. B.C. has often been a tight three-way race over the last several election cycles but, right now, the Liberals are collapsing, and the Conservatives are benefiting.
Demographic trends are shifting Perhaps what has to be scariest for the Liberals is that their share of support among women and other demographic groups has evaporated and, in some instances, they are tied with the NDP or, even worse, in third place.
Just 25% of women say they will vote for Trudeau and the Liberals, a category they used to carry. Now, it's Poilievre taking the plurality of women voters with 37% saying they will back the Conservatives compared to 24% for the NDP.
Among those age 18-29, a demographic group the Liberals and NDP used to fight over, the Conservatives are again in the lead with 32% support compared to 28% for the NDP and 18% for the Liberals. This is likely the result of the housing affordability crisis, which Trudeau ignored for too long while Poilievre stepped forward to offer solutions.
The Conservatives have been ahead in the polls for months now, some polls giving them as high as a 19-point lead such as the Nanos poll released at the end of November. In mid-December, Abacus reported the Conservatives had dropped 5 points down to 37% support while the Liberals were up at 27%.
Many saw this as a slide in support for Poilievre, perhaps that he had peaked and was coming down. Abacus CEO David Coletto said it looks like any slide has stopped, even reversed.
"Since our last survey, the Conservatives are up 4 while the Liberals are down 3. This is a statistically significant shift in vote intentions since mid-December," Coletto said.
It's also a bad start to the year for the Liberals, who had hoped to turn things around in 2024.
This doesn't mean it's too late for them, it's just a bad sign of a continuing trend that will become harder to reverse as time goes on. As for the Conservatives, they shouldn't start popping champagne bottles just yet, we could still be a long way off from an election.
The rules to remember in politics: Polls change, voters are fickle, campaigns matter.
For now, though, it must feel pretty good to be Pierre Poilievre and pretty dismal for Justin Trudeau. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Polls & surveys; Voters

Location: Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Identifier / keyword: wonder; justin; support; seniors; right; performing

Title: Trudeau's support evaporating among key demographic groups

Publication title: The Toronto Sun; Toronto

First page: A.13

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 12, 2024

column: Opinion

Section: Comment

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place ofpublication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Document 345 of 356

LILLEY: Trudeau's support evaporating among key demographic groups

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-trudeaus-support-evaporating-among-key/docview/2913457878/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Trudeau%27s+support+evaporating+among+key+demographic+groups&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Among those age 18-29, a demographic group the Liberals and NDP used to fight over, the Conservatives are again in the lead with 32% support compared to 28% for the NDP and 18% for the Liberals. This is likely the result of the housing affordability crisis, which Trudeau ignored for too long while Poilievre stepped forward to offer solutions.
The Conservatives have been ahead in the polls for months now, some polls giving them as high as a 19-point lead such as the Nanos poll released at the end of November. In mid-December, Abacus reported the Conservatives had dropped 5 points down to 37% support while the Liberals were up at 27%.
Many saw this as a slide in support for Poilievre, perhaps that he had peaked and was coming down. Abacus CEO David Coletto said it looks like any slide has stopped, even reversed.
"Since our last survey, the Conservatives are up 4 while the Liberals are down 3. This is a statistically significant shift in vote intentions since mid-December," Coletto said.
It's also a bad start to the year for the Liberals, who had hoped to turn things around in 2024.
This doesn't mean it's too late for them, it's just a bad sign of a continuing trend that will become harder to reverse as time goes on. As for the Conservatives, they shouldn't start popping champagne bottles just yet, we could still be a long way off from an election.
The rules to remember in politics: Polls change, voters are fickle, campaigns matter.
For now, though, it must feel pretty good to be Pierre Poilievre and pretty dismal for Justin Trudeau.
blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Polls & surveys; Voters

Location: Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Document 346 of 356

Smarten up; Testing out tomorrow's tech at the 2024 CES

Author: Saltzman, Marc

Publication info: Saltzman, Marc. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/smarten-up-testing-out-tomorrows-tech-at-2024-ces/docview/2913313947/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Smarten+up%3B+Testing+out+tomorrow%27s+tech+at+the+2024+CES&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=Saltzman%2C+Marc&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I've seen the future in Las Vegas this week, and here's one thing you can bet on: Your tech will soon do a lot more thinking for you.
As expected, artificial intelligence is the pervasive theme at the 57th annual Consumer Electronics Show, which once again serves as a glimpse into the coming years.
(This is the show that first introduced the VCR in the '70s, the CD player in the '80s, HDTV in the '90s and driverless cars a decade ago.)
For 2024, it's AI everything. The explosion of generative AI software in 2023, including popular programs like ChatGPT, is now fusing with devices - added to cars, televisions, laptops, cleaning robots, and even toothbrushes - if my time at the Las Vegas Convention Center and surrounding facilities is any indication.
For a few highlights, the following is a closer look at five innovations from the big tech show.
NOTHING TO SEE HERE Garnering several gasps from onlookers, LG showed a television that's virtually transparent when turned off, so it blends into your room decor, and when turned on, you can choose from it to be opaque (like your current TV) or show animated images that seemingly float around your actual room.
Called the LG Signature OLED T, this 77-inch see-through and AI-powered smart TV is also wireless, to give you more options on where to place it. That is, connect your components, like a cable box or video game system, to the "Zero Connect Box," which is placed somewhere else in the room, and then up to 4K video and audio is wirelessly transmitted to the television.
Available in Canada sometime in 2024, this LG OLED T snagged five CES 2024 Innovation Awards.
Samsung also showed a transparent MicroLED TV that's more of a concept for future models. DRINK UP Kitchen countertops are about to get a lot more fun.
The Barsys 360 lets you create personalized cocktails with a push of a button. Consider it a robotic bartender.
Pour up to six spirits and mixes into the various compartments, and in the app, type in what you've got, such as vodka, gin, bourbon, soda water, tonic or fruit juice. With its more than 1,000 recipes (and growing), the app will then show you what you can make (or create your own recipe) and the Barsys 360 will perfectly measure it for you. Place a glass under the spout and within a couple of seconds, you're ready to sip your mixed drink.
With its modern, circular design, Barsys is fast, fun and a great conversation piece.
Unveiled at CES, it's available now for $475 US south of the border, but will come to Canada this year.
LAP IT UP Intel Core Ultra - the semiconductor company's most significant chip design upgrade in 40 years, says Intel - is adding deep artificial intelligence to dozens of 2024 laptops on display at CES.
AI on a chip is faster, more secure, and is personalized with your information stored on the computer, such as asking your laptop "Show me the email where I was promised a raise" or "Where are my wedding photos?" At the risk of getting too geeky, adding an NPU (neuro-processing unit) to the CPU (the main brains of your PC) and GPU (for rendering graphics), you can also ask your laptop to generate original content for you, whether it's a photo, song, speech or email.
Lenovo's 16-inch Yoga Pro 9i was a stunner, designed for creative types, and not only has the Intel Core Ultra chip but also a physical Lenovo AI Core Chip, too.
The Asus Zenbook Duo, on the other hand, boasts two connected screens and a removable keyboard, allowing a laptop owner to use the computer in several different configurations.
New HP Spectre laptops are also powered by AI, with what HP calls its "AI assistant Superpower," which can be customized to learn info about the user to generate personalized content.
SOUND ADVICE Among the three Sennheiser earbuds that debuted at the 2024 CES, one called the Sennheiser Momentum Sport includes a heart-rate sensor and body temperature sensor.
Optimized for fitness types, both sensors integrate with popular apps, fitness trackers and smart watches, and subscription services (including iPhone's Health app, Strava, Peloton, Garmin, Strava and Polar).
Available by April, these ruggedized Sennheiser earbuds will be rated IP-55 for water resistance, therefore ideal for sweat or if it starts to rain on your run.
Other features include the ability to hear your surroundings, also ideal for the outdoors, and up to six hours of battery life between charges and another 18 hours with the charging case.
JBL also impressed with its Live 3 Earbuds series, not just because of its powerful, clear and wellbalanced high-resolution audio, but the smart charging case with an integrated 3.68-cm LED touch display to show info such as volume level, battery life, photos and to toggle active noise cancellation.
JUST ROLL WITH IT Samsung has updated its cute Ballie home robot to include a built-in projector.
About the size of a bowling ball, this AI-powered bot will roll around your home, navigate around obstacles (thanks to lidar technology) and can roll into your room to tell you something, such as what your pets are up to.
The Full HD projector can be used to project movies against the wall or show whom you're chatting with over a video call.
Battery life is said to top three hours, and it should be available in 2024.
@marc_saltzman

Subject: Electronics industry; Artificial intelligence; Portable computers; Semiconductors

Business indexing term: Subject: Electronics industry Artificial intelligence

Location: United States--US; Canada; Las Vegas Nevada

Identifier / keyword: future; vegas; thing; thinking; expected; artificial

Document 347 of 356

Province to close unspecified number of ServiceOntario locations: Report

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/province-close-unspecified-number-serviceontario/docview/2913213217/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+to+close+unspecified+number+of+ServiceOntario+locations%3A+Report&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Doug Ford government is reportedly shuttering an unspecified number of ServiceOntario locations.
CityNews reported Wednesday that the Ontario outlets where people can renew driver's licences and health cards will be replaced by kiosks inside select Staples Canada stores.
A Dec. 7 news release announced the ServiceOntario-Staples Canada partnership, a pilot project that would see ServiceOntario kiosks open in Staples stores to "reduce the overall cost to deliver government services to the public."
That news release didn't mention any pending ServiceOntario closures, however.
ServiceOntario worker, two others charged in auto fraud investigation
ServiceOntario kiosks stay offline
It was not clear how many ServiceOntario locations would be affected; however, CityNews, citing sources, reported that some locations have been family businesses for decades and that owners were given just 70 days' notice about the closures.
Affected employees would be given opportunities to work at the new Staples Canada kiosks, according to a government spokesperson, the report said.
ServiceOntario completes 59 million transactions annually and more than 24 million transactions are completed in person at 275 ServiceOntario centres throughout the province.

Document 348 of 356

Chow's cash cow; Expect city budget to call for whopping double-digit tax hike

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/chows-cash-cow-expect-city-budget-call-whopping/docview/2912897144/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chow%27s+cash+cow%3B+Expect+city+budget+to+call+for+whopping+double-digit+tax+hike&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bill Clinton famously said, "It depends on what the meaning of the word 'is' is." This week at city hall, we are waiting on the definition of "substantial."
In the case of the tax hike proposal coming at city hall, expect a double-digit increase, along with a campaign to get more money, or taxing power, from the federal and provincial governments.
Councillor Shelley Carroll, appointed budget chief by Mayor Olivia Chow, has been telling anyone who will listen to expect a significant hike.
"I'll go so far as to say it will be substantial, because we really have reached a point where if we're having to solve our own problems, it means a substantial increase," Carroll told CBC's city hall reporter Shawn Jeffords last week.
Last year's total tax hike amounted to 7% - 5.5% on the mill rate and 1.5% on the capital levy. It was the largest property tax hike since amalgamation.
According to city staff, the average property tax bill went up by $183 in 2023 and that was just keeping up with inflation, which means current talk of a substantial tax increase should make residents nervous.
During the byelection for mayor last spring, Chow refused to say how big her tax hike would be, while opponents claimed it would be 20% or more. She did promise to push for a "luxury home tax" on any home over $3 million - something city council backed at a meeting in September - along with increasing the vacant home tax from 1% to 3%.
At September's special meeting, council also supported increasing parking fees, called for a municipal sales tax and asked city officials to study the idea of a tax on travellers through Billy Bishop airport. In addition, council voted to build 25,000 affordable homes without any plans to pay for them - save perhaps their call to start a municipal lottery.
One thing they did reject in September was a call for a core services review that would look at where the city is spending money and whether the services it is providing are still needed. Why on earth would council want to look at their existing spending to make sure it's necessary before reaching into your pocket with several tax hikes? So, what we can expect on Wednesday, based off of what we have seen so far, is a budget plan big on taxing and spending and light on retrospection.
That retrospection is needed, though, because since 2019, city spending has increased by more than 20%, which is well above the rate of inflation. The Bank of Canada calculates inflation as being 15% over that time period.
That's a sign that city spending is out of control and should be reigned in rather than simply telling residents to pay more - a lot more. Will the "significant" tax hike be the 20% councillor Brad Bradford claimed Chow would seek during the mayor's race, or the 25% former police chief Mark Saunders claimed Chow wanted? Tax hikes in that range would cost homeowners thousands more per year, which would be just fine with some residents, like the man who showed up last August during public consultations to call for a 30% tax hike. North of 7%, but hopefully well below 30% - with yet another call for the city to get new taxing powers such as the ability to levy a municipal sales tax - is what we can expect when the first budget proposal is rolled out on Wednesday.
Implementing a municipal sales tax would require provincial approval, which is unlikely. When announcing the new deal between the city and province, Premier Doug Ford said he wanted the city to deal with its own budget pressures, "not by painful cuts in services or new taxes and tolls, but through smart, effective growth."
That advice from Ford is likely to be ignored, just as he will ignore their request for a sales tax. What no city resident will be able to ignore is the big hike likely coming your way. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Luxury homes; Councils; Budgets; Sales taxes; Property taxes; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets Sales taxes Property taxes Tax increases

Identifier / keyword: clinton; famously; depends; meaning; waiting; definition

SALTZMAN: Testing tomorrow's tech today at the 2024 CES

LILLEY: Expect city's budget to call for a whopping double-digit tax hike

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-expect-citys-budget-call-whopping-double/docview/2912816953/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Expect+city%27s+budget+to+call+for+a+whopping+double-digit+tax+hike&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Will the "significant" tax hike be the 20% Councillor Brad Bradford claimed Chow would seek during the mayor's race, or the 25% former police chief Mark Saunders claimed Chow wanted?
LILLEY: Chow says city is broke but still wants to rename Dundas St.
LILLEY: Chow visits Ford at Queen's Park and says, 'Let's make a deal'
Tax hikes in that range would cost homeowners thousands more per year, which would be just fine with some residents, like the man who showed up last August during public consultations to call for a 30% tax hike.
North of 7%, but hopefully well below 30% - with yet another call for the city to get new taxing powers such as the ability to levy a municipal sales tax - is what we can expect when the first budget proposal is rolled out on Wednesday.
Implementing a municipal sales tax would require provincial approval, which is unlikely to be given. When announcing the new deal between the city and province, Premier Doug Ford said he wanted the city to deal with its own budget pressures, "not by painful cuts in services or new taxes and tolls, but through smart, effective growth."
That advice from Ford is likely to be ignored, just as he will ignore their request for a sales tax.
What no city resident will be able to ignore is the big hike coming your way without a change at City Hall.

Subject: Luxury homes; Councils; Budgets; Sales taxes; Property taxes; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets Sales taxes Property taxes Tax increases

Document 351 of 356

New name for Yonge-Dundas Square sparks 'widespread disapproval': Poll

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/new-name-yonge-dundas-square-sparks-widespread/docview/2912054476/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+name+for+Yonge-Dundas+Square+sparks+%27widespread+disapproval%27%3A+Poll&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The new name for Yonge-Dundas Square is a bit like trying to fit a square peg into a round hole, according to a new survey.
It doesn't fit.
Results from the new survey conducted by Liaison Strategies for the National Ethnic Press and Media Council of Canada confirmed the general chatter that has followed in the aftermath of city council's abrupt decision on Dec. 14 to rename Yonge-Dundas Square as Sankofa Square.
A whopping 72% of respondents disapproved of the move, while 16% said they approved and 12% weren't sure.
"There is slim support, though, for renaming Yonge-Dundas to Sankofa. Instead, there is widespread disapproval from every demographic and every Toronto borough," Liaison Strategies said in a Monday news release announcing the findings. "The name performs best downtown (17% approval), but even there 69% disapprove of the name change. Across the city, 71% disapprove of the move."
WARMINGTON: Sneaky renaming of Dundas Square to Sankofa Square is revisionist madness
WARMINGTON: If you want to erase the name Sankofa before it erases Dundas, there is a petition
Toronto council approves renaming of Yonge-Dundas Square, Dundas Station
City officials are stripping the Dundas name from some of its assets. Henry Dundas was an active politician from the 1770s to the early 1800s, when the British parliament was debating slavery abolition motions. There is a dispute surrounding Dundas's role in the propagation of the slave trade.
The city said the term "Sankofa" originates in Ghana and refers to the act of reflecting on and reclaiming teachings from the past, which enables people to move forward together.
Liaison Strategies principal David Valentin told CP24 of the poll: "I don't think we've seen numbers quite this strikingly bad for something before."
City council voted in 2021 to rename Dundas St. and its associated assets following a renewed examination of anti-Black racism in the wake of George Floyd's death.
A committee was formed to select a new name. At a council meeting last month, Councillor Chris Moise announced that after two years of consideration the committee had selected Sankofa Square as the new name.
Included in a motion to rename the square were two subway stations and a library, while a pause to rename the entire street was put forward. That project's latest price tag was estimated at $13 million.
The motion asked that the TTC enter into a deal with Toronto Metropolitan University - formerly Ryerson - to rename Dundas Station as "TMU Station." TMU has agreed to pay the entire $1.7-milllion renaming cost associated with Dundas Station if it's named after the university.
Two members of the management board that runs Yonge-Dundas Square - chair Michael Fenton and vice-chair Jan Mollenhauer - resigned after the square's renaming vote, citing concerns about a lack of public consultation.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
The Liaison Strategies poll also showed that Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow's approval rating was at 71% overall this month, but was down to 55% in Etobicoke. Some 22% disapproved of the mayor, while 7% were not sure.
Provincially, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie is seeing a small increase in popularity (1%) in Toronto.
On the national level, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's approval ratings continued to tumble. According to the poll, Trudeau's Liberals trailed the Conservatives by 10% in Etobicoke.
"Only in downtown do they have a comfortable lead as support is split between the Conservatives and the NDP," Valentin said.
The pollster surveyed a random sample of 831 Torontonians through interactive voice recording (IVR) on Jan. 2 and 3. The survey has a margin of error of plus or minus 3.39 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
