Document 352 of 356

KINSELLA: The Little Guy From Shawinigan a big success

Author: Doucette, Chris

Publication info: Doucette, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/kinsella-little-guy-shawinigan-big-success/docview/2910949996/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=KINSELLA%3A+The+Little+Guy+From+Shawinigan+a+big+success&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Doucette%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Jean Chretien is turning 90 this week. And what a life he has had.
Let me tell you one of my favorite Chretien stories, about just one afternoon in that extraordinary life.
One sunny day a few years ago, I was in Vancouver for business. Turned out Chretien was, as well. We decided to get together for lunch, down near Water Street in Gastown.
It was a nice day, so the former prime minister suggested we go for a walk. Off we went, along with the one RCMP guy assigned to him. We headed towards Waterfront Station.
People would do a double-take when they saw him, then smile, then wave to him and say, "Hey Chretien!" Things like that. Happened a lot.
KINSELLA: Tips for Conservatives to finally end Trudeau's reign
KINSELLA: Having friends across the aisle good for politicians and constituents
KINSELLA: Trudeau's woes due to overexposure
KINSELLA: Pierre Poilievre would be wise not to count out Justin Trudeau
We got to Waterfront Station, and there was a great big guy there. He was sitting on the sidewalk, looking pretty rough, and he might have been homeless. He got up and started walking rapidly towards us, his face stern.
Just when I was thinking that I would need to become a bodyshield for the former prime minister, the rough-looking guy stuck out a hand as big as a ham.
"Chretien!" He bellowed. "Thanks for keeping us out of Iraq! You did a good job! Got five bucks?"
We all laughed, of course. And Chretien, still laughing, reached into his wallet - and it wasn't one of those fancy Italian wallets, either, it looked like he got it for a fill up at Petro Canada - and took out five bucks and gave it to the guy. And then they proceeded to talk about all manner of things, like they had known each other forever.
The Right Honourable Stephen Harper (L) and The Right Honorouable Jean Chrétien (R) at the Red Carpet event for the 25th anniversary of Canada's Walk of Fame at the Metro Toronto Convention Centre on Dec. 2, 2023.
There are a million other stories like that about Joseph Jacques Jean Chretien, 18th of 19 children, 20th of Canada's 23 prime ministers. A million.
You just need to walk along any street in Canada, and I mean anywhere, and people will stop him to shake his hand or offer best wishes or ask for a selfie.
I don't know if anybody has ever done a poll on the most-loved prime minister, but if they had, I'm pretty sure my former boss would top it.
Queen Elizabeth II of England and Prime Minister of Canada Jean Chretien share a laugh during a state dinner in Toronto on Saturday, June 28, 1997.
He turns 90 this week. He still goes in to the office. He still talks on the phone with presidents and prime ministers, kings and queens.
He still offers free political advice to whoever asks for it. The recipients of the advice include unabashed fans, like one Stephen Harper, P.C. (Not so much the woke bloke, however, the one with the Chewbacca socks. That one doesn't like getting advice from people with experience. And it shows.)
At 90, Chretien still waterskis at his little place in Shawinigan, plays pickleball, goes kiteboarding - or he heads to Harvey's for a burger, a bemused Mountie in tow. And then people stop by his table to say hello, or ask for a selfie. He always stands to greet them.
Federal Liberal leader Jean Chretien cuts through a wave while waterskiing near his cottage in Lac des Piles, near Grand-Mere, Que., prior to the 1993 federal election.
That's an important distinction, I think. Chretien is beloved, in part, because people sense that he does not regard himself as better than them, or better than anyone. And he doesn't.
He didn't let the job go to his head, you see, and the statistics suggest he was pretty successful at his day job.
When he offered me the job of speechwriter in the Summer of 1990 - and, believe me, if there is a Maytag repairman equivalent in Canadian politics, it's being speechwriter to Jean Chretien - lots of friends and family told me I was crazy. He'll never become prime minister, they said. You're throwing away a promising legal career, they said.
Jean Chrétien on Nov. 1, 1989.
"Well," I'd say many times over the subsequent years, "he kind of did all right, didn't he?"
He certainly did.
Forty years in elected politics, never a defeat. Held just about every major portfolio in federal politics, never a finding of wrongdoing by him. Balanced the budget more than once, kept Canada together more than once, won three majorities in a row. Fiscally prudent, socially progressive. Gave the Shawinigan HandshakeT? to a guy who deserved it.
And, through it all, he kept his connection to the people.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
One time, we were waiting for him to arrive to start an event at a restaurant somewhere. I asked one of my fellow aides where the leader was and he laughed.
"He's in the kitchen," the aide told me. "He always enters through the kitchen, so he can talk to the staff and shake their hands."
That's Chretien. That's the little guy from Shawinigan.
Some of us who worked for him and who love him are gathering in Ottawa this week to sing happy birthday.
Ninety years: by any standard, that's a long life.
And Jean Chretien did some amazing things with that life.

Subject: Prime ministers

Location: Canada

People: Chretien, Jean; Harper, Stephen

Identifier / keyword: chretien; turning; favorite; stories; afternoon; extraordinary

Document 353 of 356

Apples to apples; Complaining about protesters not a two-way street

Author: Kinsella, Warren

Publication info: Kinsella, Warren. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/apples-complaining-about-protesters-not-two-way/docview/2910040995/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Apples+to+apples%3B+Complaining+about+protesters+not+a+two-way+street&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kinsella%2C+Warren&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sorry, partisans, but you can't have it both ways.
January 2022: Protesters start blocking Ottawa streets to make a political point. Police are ultimately used to clear them out. Some conservatives are very unhappy the police used force to do so, while some progressives are happy.
January 2024: Protesters start blocking Toronto streets to make a political point. Police haven't cleared them out using force (yet). But some conservatives are very unhappy police haven't used force (yet) - while some progressives are happy they haven't.
I know, I know. Both sides are mad at me, now. Sorry. But you can't oppose (or favour) clearing out the Ottawa road occupiers, and now take the completely opposite position with the pro-Palestine road occupiers. It's illogical, it's inconsistent, and it's unfair.
Two years ago, this writer strongly favoured the police clearing out the Ottawa occupiers, with force if necessary. As I wrote in these pages at the time: "Enough is enough. It's time to invoke the Emergencies Act ... The Act would give the government the ability to prevent more troublemakers from travelling to Ottawa to extend the siege there. It would prevent 'protesters'from blocking border crossings - including, as they did in Windsor, with children.
"It would allow the authorities to remove trucks and barricades that have been used to shut down the national capital - and cripple billions of dollars in cross-border trade. And it allows government to compensate citizens and businesses who have been victimized by the lawlessness."
And, two years later, just about every word of that also applies to the "pro-Palestine" (read: kind-of, sort-of pro-Hamas) protesters, who are now regularly blocking access to and from Toronto's 401, the busiest highway in North America. Not only are they hurting local businesses and residents - like the Ottawa occupiers did - they are using their presence to intimidate the people (mainly Jews) who live in those areas.
What's fair is fair, what's good for the goose is good for the gander and any other cliché that applies. You simply can't be OK with one group blocking roads and intimidating locals, and then oppose it when another group does it. The law, if it is to matter at all, has to be applied without fear or favour. It has to be equitable.
At just about this point in this opinion column, or course, partisans on the Left and the Right are steaming mad, and want to argue the two situations are completely, totally different.
Right about now, they're dreaming up distinctions they hope make a difference.
But they are distinctions which don't amount to a difference. In international law, blockades are considered acts of war.
In statute law, it is also illegal: In Alberta, for example, the Critical Infrastructure Defence Act properly makes it against the law for "blockades, protests or similar activities" to damage or harm essential infrastructure like roads.
And in Ontario, during the Ottawa occupation, Premier Doug Ford (rightly) applied the provincial equivalent of the Emergencies Act days before Prime Minister Justin Trudeau did so to stop blockades of roads and border crossings. Meanwhile, Canada's Criminal Code's section 423 makes blocking roads a crime punishable by five years in prison.
For centuries, the non-criminal common law has done likewise: going back to the Seventies, the Supreme Court of Canada has held that "authorities were not only entitled but duty bound, as peace officers ... the right of free access of the public to public streets."
In international law, in Canada's criminal law, in our statutory laws, in centuries of common law, it's the iron-clad rule: you are not allowed to block public roads and highways with impunity. And the police have always had the authority to arrest and detain you for doing so.
So, in case of the Ottawa occupation and the "pro-Palestine" occupations, the law must be applied consistently and fairly. If it was right and proper to arrest and detain the Ottawa occupiers who refused to leave that city's streets, it's now right and proper to arrest and detain the pro-Palestine/anti-Semitic occupiers who are refusing to do likewise on the 401's exit ramps.
The law is the law. If you apply it in one case, you need to apply it in all similar cases.
So, why haven't the police arrested and cleared out the anti-Israel mobs? For that question, I do not have an answer.

Subject: International law; Roads & highways; Criminal law

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: sorry; partisans; january; protesters; start; blocking

Document 355 of 356

Ontario NDP's spending plans ignore fiscal realities

Author: Agar, Jerry

Publication info: Agar, Jerry. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-ndps-spending-plans-ignore-fiscal/docview/2909351113/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+NDP%27s+spending+plans+ignore+fiscal+realities&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-01-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=Agar%2C+Jerry&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When the NDP says it will make life more affordable, questions have to be asked about how and for whom? Ontario NDP Leader Marit Styles released her five-part plan in the Toronto Star.
She promises to make housing more affordable, something everyone agrees is a good goal. How? "We need to bring back real rent control," she wrote.
Rent control, as with many political positions, is populist, but is it a good idea? No. It ignores the reality of the market. Prices rise because the amount demanded exceeds the amount supplied at existing prices.
Prices have a role to play, signalling when supply and demand are not in line. Price controls are artificial and ignore cost.
Price controls serve as a disincentive to developers to build and to landlords to spend on upkeep. Rent controls in New York have led to more rundown buildings, which is not helpful to low-income people.
Stiles writes, "Decades of reliance on the market alone has only made Ontario's housing crisis worse."
Is she suggesting housing problems can be solved by government? How well is that going with social housing? The worst landlord in Toronto is the City of Toronto, with $1.7 billion in backlogged social housing repairs.
Stiles'next point is to champion $10-a-day daycare, in part she says by "raising the wages of early childhood educators and other childcare staff."
It is a popular program idea among those who believe cheap daycare will allow more parents, mostly women, to work - which they say will help the economy - and those who quite frankly just want someone else to pay their bills.
Again, price has a role to play.
Artificially lowering the price while increasing the cost sets up a dynamic that will see some people luck out (or use their connections) while others will sit on long waiting lists.
How will supply match demand when almost all parents will see daycare as "affordable?" Canada already has some of the longest waiting times in the developed world for health care due to "free" universal care.
Adding dental and daycare to free or cheap services offered can only be achieved with massive tax increases, or as in health care, rationing through waiting lists, or both.
Stiles says she can make home heating more affordable. How? "By making heat pumps free."
Nothing is free. Someone pays. She will make groceries more affordable by "creating an independent consumer watchdog to prevent price gouging, investigate and fine corporate predators."
The profit margin on groceries is in the neighbourhood of 3%. So, if we reduce their profit by 33%, that will take a penny off a dollar of your grocery bill. Will you notice? Most profit for companies like Loblaw, studies show, comes from drug and finance operations.
The NDP's fifth platform is to double ODSP and OW payments to, it says, over 900,000 people.
Perhaps those programs should increase at a higher rate than the Conservatives' 5%.
Can Stiles tell us where she will find the money? Can she name any program spending she would cut in favour of ODSP and OW? Anyone can spend money. Not everyone can manage money or an economy.
Once again, the NDP is delivering populist rhetoric, yelling "gouging" and "predator," while its knowledge of business and economics is as suspect as it has always been.
Agar is the 9 a.m. to noon host on NEWSTALK1010 jerry.agar@sunmedia.ca

Subject: Price regulations; Populism; Profits; Wage & price controls; Rent control

Full text: When the NDP says it will make life more affordable, questions have to be asked about how and for whom?
Ontario NDP Leader Marit Styles released her five-part plan in the Toronto Star.
She promises to make housing more affordable, something everyone agrees is a good goal. How? "We need to bring back real rent control," she wrote.
Rent control, as with many political positions, is populist, but is it a good idea?
No. It ignores the reality of the market. Prices rise because the amount demanded exceeds the amount supplied at existing prices.

Stiles' next point is to champion $10-a-day daycare, in part, she says by "raising the wages of early childhood educators and other child-care staff."
It is a popular program idea among those who believe cheap daycare will allow more parents, mostly women, to work - which they say will help the economy - and those who quite frankly just want someone else to pay their bills.
Again, price has a role to play. Artificially lowering the price while increasing the cost sets up a dynamic that will see some people luck out (or use their connections) while others will sit on long waiting lists.
